The Springer Series in Materials Science covers the complete spectrum of mate-
rials research and technology, including fundamental principles, physical properties, 
materials theory and design. Recognizing the increasing importance of materials 
science in future device technologies, the book titles in this series reﬂect the state-of-
the-art in understanding and controlling the structure and properties of all important 
classes of materials. Dorian A. H. Hanaor

The built environment is a mirror of our values and a site of profound consequence. Every act of construction is a negotiation with the earth—an intervention in its 
ecosystems, an imprint on its material ﬂows, and an inﬂuence on human and non-
human lives. In its immense materiality, the built environment shapes our worlds, 
embedding within it our cultural, social, and ecological priorities. And so, this shaping 
is never neutral. Material selection for a project in one place always inﬂuences (and 
may come at the expense of) other environments and people. As Jane Hutton writes 
in her book: “this global material ﬂow plays out in highly differentiated ways on 
the ground: deeper quarries in one place, material improvements in another; toxic 
deposition in one place, reﬁned minerals put to use in another.”1 
The choices we make as engineers, architects, and builders are indeed imbued 
with responsibility, especially in a time of increasing environmental precarity. This 
book stands as both a reﬂection and a challenge to those who work within this critical 
terrain, offering an exploration of materials, their resources, and performances. Philosophically speaking, the body of work in this book may be interpreted as one 
that draws upon a lineage of thought that recognizes the profound contextuality and 
entanglement between the making of our buildings and the agencies involved in the 
formation of materials and products. Two masterminds, Bruno Latour’s insistence on 
a  “parliament of things ,2 ” where human and non-human actants must be recognized 
as co-constructors of reality, and Donna Haraway’s proposition of “staying with the 
trouble,3 ” which asks us to engage deeply and ethically with the complexities of our 
entangled world––are examples of such important and pivotal scholarship. These 
frameworks should be acknowledged when reading the body of work in this book 
because they resonate profoundly in asking what the role of design and science is in 
materializing our world through building. 1 Hutton, Jane Elizabeth. Reciprocal Landscapes: Tracing Materials Between New York City and 
Beyond. Routledge, 2018. 2 Latour, Bruno. Politics of nature. Harvard University Press, 2004. 3 Haraway, Donna J. “Staying with the tr

ouble: Making kin in the Chthulucene.” In Staying with 
the Trouble. Duke University Press, 2016. v

vi
Foreword
To add my stance, in the pages that follow, “sustainable materials”, as the title of 
the book implies, should not be framed as an isolated problem to be solved but as 
an ongoing dialogue—a sympoiesis—that demands innovation, humility, and a will-
ingness to embrace material variability as a critical mode of resistance to industrial 
standardization and extraction. This volume’s organization reﬂects a deliberate effort to balance historical prac-
tices, emerging innovations, and systemic analyses of material impacts. Part I, Earth-
Based Construction, is a testament to the enduring relevance of earthen materials in 
architecture. It brings forth traditional practices like cob and rammed earth, reframing 
them as active participants in a contemporary discourse that spans digital fabrication, 
structural performance, and cultural knowledge. Part II, Alternative Materials and 
Processes, includes explorations of additional nonconventional materials to challenge 
the dominance of steel and concrete. Part III, Life Cycle Impacts and Circularity of 
Materials in the Built Environment, situates material choices within their broader 
systems of production, use, and eventual decay. Here, the book takes a systems-
thinking approach, underscoring the critical importance of life cycle assessment and 
circular material ﬂows aiming to tie these methodologies to a synthetic whole. My own research has often sought to understand the role of natural materials— 
clay-soils, plant ﬁbers, and biological composites—not only as low-tech solutions but 
as expressive and embodied mediators. These materials, with their inherent variability 
and texture, are often inherently non-commodiﬁed, thus inviting an engagement with 
site-speciﬁcities of resources, labor, and craft. For example, in exploring earth-ﬁber 
composites, my intention is not merely to innovate new methods of construction but to 
highlight how these materials embody a dialogue with the environment—a dialogue 
that is based on embodied interaction with materials that are physiologically healthy 
and historically known to the body. This book aligns deeply with this ethos, calling 
for a material practice that is at once technical and ethical, precise, and open to the 
complexities of the world. The contributions gathered here illustrate a much-required in how we approach 
materials in construction—one that moves beyond the constraints of a purely STEM-
driven framework. While acknowledging the pragmatic requirements of industrial 
efﬁciency, standardization, scale, and pace, they also embrace the heterogeneity, 
porosity, and adaptability that natural and alternative materials offer. This conver-
gence requires not only different technologies and analyses but also resilient mind-
sets—one that sees material variability not as a limitation but as an inherent and 
valuable aspect of the design proce

ss. As we navigate uncertainties of accelerated digitalism and resource scarcity, the 
stakes of our work as engineers, architects, and educators have never been higher. This 
book sets forth knowledge that will be needed for the next generation of resourceful 
building, converging technology, low-tech, and site-responsive design in meaningful 
ways. It is also a reminder to design not for permanence but for adaptability, not for

Foreword
vii
extraction but for regeneration, and not for isolation but for interdependence. It is a 
privilege to introduce this work, which I believe will serve as both a provocation and 
a guide to building professionals, navigating the complexities of sustainability in an 
engineered world. Let it remind us that every act of construction is an opportunity 
to honor our shared future, one material at a time. Lola Ben-Alon 
Graduate School of Architecture, 
Planning and Preservation 
Columbia University 
New York, NY, USA

Chapter 1 
Introduction 
Dorian A. H. Hanaor 
1.1 
Introducing the Collection: Sustainable Materials 
for the Built Environment 
The built environment is the foundation of human civilization, comprising the spaces 
where we live, work, and interact. It is both a reﬂection of our cultural and techno-
logical achievements and a critical determinant of our environmental impact. As the 
global population continues to grow relentlessly, the demand for safe, comfortable, 
and resilient housing and infrastructure has never been greater. Yet, the construction 
sector, including its entire value chain, is broadly considered to be responsible for 
around 40% of global greenhouse gas emissions. The built environment therefore 
stands at a crossroads as the need to simultaneously address climate change and arti-
ﬁcial housing scarcities worldwide becomes a priority for societies. Going forward, 
the way we design, build, and maintain our structures will profoundly inﬂuence 
our ability to address climate change, resource scarcity, and biodiversity loss while 
providing citizens with access to an adequate and affordable housing within their 
countries, recognized as a fundamental human right [1]. This book, Sustainable 
Materials for the Built Environment, seeks to provide a comprehensive explo-
ration of the materials, methods, and philosophies that can guide us toward a more 
sustainable future. Intended for a diverse audience including architects, materials scientists, sustain-
ability advocates, and anyone interested in greener construction practices, this 
book brings together insights from leading thinkers and practitioners from diverse 
ﬁelds. It offers multidisciplinary perspectives on the challenges and opportunities
D. A.

H. Hanaor (B) 
Materialeyes, Materials Innovation Consulting, Berlin, Germany

2
D. A. H. Hanaor
of sustainable construction, presenting innovative solutions that balance environ-
mental stewardship, social equity, and economic viability. By examining both tradi-
tional and emerging materials, this volume aims to inspire a reimagining of the built 
environment, one that harmonizes human needs with the natural world. At the heart of this work lies a recognition that the solutions to today’s crises 
of climate change, resource depletion, and social inequity are often rooted in the 
past. Ancient building practices, reﬁned over millennia, are being revitalized through 
modern scientiﬁc rigor and digital technologies. Simultaneously, advancements in 
materials science and circular design principles are redeﬁning what it means to build 
responsibly. This underscores a central theme: sustainability thrives at the intersection 
of respect for ecological limits and bold technological experimentation. The narratives presented here go beyond the technical aspects of materials and 
their applications, inviting readers to consider the broader implications of material 
choices. Buildings are more than assemblages of matter; they are the stage upon which 
human life unfolds. By prioritizing materials that harmonize with natural systems, 
we can not only reduce carbon footprints of construction but also cultivate spaces 
that nurture well-being, cultural identity, and community resilience. The case studies 
and methodologies detailed in these pages demonstrate that sustainable construction 
materials are not a compromise but rather a means to achieve architectural beauty, 
structural integrity, and environmental stewardship. 1.2 
What Do We Seek to Sustain? Before examining methodologies for the processing and deployment of sustain-
able materials in the built environment, it is imperative to examine the foundational 
concepts of sustainability and their speciﬁc implications for construction materials. The Brundtland Commission’s deﬁnition, “Sustainable development is development 
that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future gener-
ations to meet their own needs” [2], provides a critical framework. This invites a 
deeper inquiry: What, precisely, constitutes the “needs” we seek to fulﬁl, and which 
systems or resources require preservation to ensure intergenerational equity? Deﬁning what we seek to sustain underpins goal formation, strategic prioritiza-
tion, and long-term societal planning. In an era characterized by expone

ntial tech-
nological growth, competing ideological frameworks, and fragmented policy land-
scapes, clarity of individual and collective purpose has become a scarce resource. Within neoclassical economic paradigms, the concept of “utility” has long served 
as a proxy for value, quantifying human satisfaction through metrics of production 
and consumption. However, this reductionist approach, which conﬂates well-being 
with monetary exchange, neglects the qualitative dimensions of lived experience. The 
built environment, as the physical manifestation of societal priorities, exempliﬁes this 
dissonance [3]. While humanity has honed advanced architectural and engineering 
capabilities over millennia, contemporary systems disproportionately prioritize the 
proliferation of consumer goods over the creation of digniﬁed, resilient habitats. 1
Introduction
3
This misalignment has precipitated a global housing crisis, a paradox of progress 
where unprecedented technical abilities coexist with systemic failure to address 
fundamental human needs. This imbalance has resulted in the erosion of crafts-
manship, community cohesion, and ecological stewardship in favour of transient 
economic gains. As we confront these challenges, the imperative to redeﬁne “pro-
gress” becomes inseparable from reimagining the materials, processes, and values 
that shape our built world. Central to the discourse on human well-being and identifying what we seek to 
sustain is the recognition that the primary utility derived from economic activity lies 
not in material accumulation but in the experiential dimensions of life. Experiences, 
rooted in daily existence, social interaction, and cultural participation, are profoundly 
shaped by the built environment, where individuals spend the majority of their lives. Dwellings, workplaces, and communal spaces constitute the physical substrate of 
society, where human agency intersects with ecological systems. The built environ-
ment functions as both a mediator and a manifestation of the anthroposphere, the 
aggregate of human activities, and its dynamic interplay with Earth’s spheres: the 
hydrosphere, lithosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere. Sustainable construction mate-
rials and construction practices therefore underpin our ability to sustain and improve 
human experiences on our planet. 1.3 
Materials for Sustainability 
Efforts to advance sustainable construction face a certain paradox: while the discourse 
often ﬁxates on high-tech innovation, many of the most ecologically and socially 
viable solutions derive not from novel technologies but from the reinterpretation 
of ancient building practices. Techniques such as rammed earth, stonemasonry, and 
timber framing, which have been reﬁned over millennia, offer low-carbon, culturally 
resonant alternatives to energy-intensive materials like concrete and steel. Similarly, 
agricultural by-products and bio-based composites demonstrate how circularity can 
be literally woven into th

e fabric of modern construction. The barrier to their adoption 
is seldom technological feasibility but rather systemic inertia. Unlike cutting-edge 
ﬁelds such as quantum computing or space exploration, sustainable built environ-
ments rarely demand radical scientiﬁc breakthroughs; instead, they require raising 
commercial readiness levels (CRL), addressing challenges of market structures that 
favour industrialized materials, regulatory frameworks ill-adapted to nature-based 
and vernacular practices, and ﬁnancial models prioritizing short-term returns over 
long-term resilience. This volume confronts these obstacles, examining how to align the construction 
sector with the urgent dual imperatives of addressing the global housing crisis and 
achieving net-zero targets. Through interdisciplinary analysis, it explores materials 
and methods that harmonize human needs with planetary boundaries, from revital-
ized traditional techniques to emerging digital fabrication processes. The chapters, 
authored by leading scholars and practitioners across disciplines, demonstrate that

4

H. Hanaor
sustainability in the built environment is not a question of scarcity of ideas, resources, 
or knowledge, but rather a question of prioritization. By integrating various ﬁelds 
relating to architecture and engineering and merging ancient techniques with contem-
porary tools, this work illuminates pathways to transform construction from a driver 
of ecological degradation into a catalyst for regeneration. In doing so, it reframes 
sustainability not as a technical challenge but as a collective reimagining of value— 
one that places human well-being and ecological stewardship at the centre of how 
we build. 1.4 
Themes 
The book is organized into three main thematic sections, each addressing a critical 
aspect of sustainable construction. Part 1: Earth-Based Construction 
This section explores the resurgence of earth-based materials, which have been used 
for millennia but are now being reimagined through modern technologies and scien-
tiﬁc advancements [4–8] and examines regulatory and technical hurdles that hinder 
the wide scale uptake of this sustainable construction approach [9]. Earthen materials, 
characterised by the textural triangle in terms of components of sand, silt and clay, 
can serve as the basis for wall elements subjected to mild compressive stresses. As we 
will see in the chapters of this part, structural performance of earthen materials can be 
further enhanced with the addition of ﬁbrous biomass or cementitious stabilisation 
[10, 11]. The large mass of the walls and the porosity of the earthen materials may 
provide hygroscopic temperature regulation and thermal inertia [12, 13]. Chapters in 
this section explore the life cycles of earth buildings, from traditional techniques to 
cutting-edge digital fabrication methods such as 3D printing [14, 15]. Researchers 
from around the world present insights into the thermal, mechanical, and struc-
tural properties of earthen materials, highlighting their potential to reduce carbon 
emissions, enhance energy efﬁciency, and preserve cultural heritage. The section 
also addresses the challenges of scaling up earth-based construction in contem-
porary contexts, offering recommendations for industry adoption and regulatory 
frameworks. Part 2: Alternative Materials and Processes 
Focusing on replacing conventional materials such as Portland Cement based 
concrete, and steel in the built environment, the second part of the book shifts focus 
to innovative materials and processes that offer sustainable alternatives to conven-
tional construction practices. Chapters examine the use of bio-based materials, such 
as fast-growing natural ﬁbers and hemp-lime composites as alternatives to steel 
reinforced concrete [16], as well as alternative binders including biocementation, 
geopolymers other alkali activated materials [17, 18]. In this section the opportu-
nities for circular economies, waste materials, residues and agricultural byproducts

1
Introduction
5
are discussed as pathway

s towards sustainable alternatives to versatile construc-
tion materials. By bridging the gap between traditional knowledge and modern 
innovation, this section highlights the potential of alternative materials to replace 
conventional carbon-intensive construction materials and thereby decarbonize the 
built environment while maintaining structural integrity and performance. Part 3: Life Cycle Impacts and Circularity of Materials 
The ﬁnal section of the book takes a holistic view of sustainability, focusing on the 
lifecycle impacts of construction materials and building practices, identifying oppor-
tunities for improved practices, industrial symbiosis and circularity. Chapters in this 
section present ﬁndings from life cycle assessments (LCA) of various materials, 
including engineered wood products, bio-based insulation, and recycled concrete. The section also explores strategies for reducing embodied carbon, enhancing mate-
rial reuse, and integrating circular economy principles into construction and renova-
tion practices. By examining the economic, social, and environmental dimensions of 
sustainability, this section provides actionable insights for policymakers, architects, 
and engineers seeking to create a more sustainable built environment. This book brings together diverse perspectives from leading researchers, prac-
titioners, and policymakers, offering a multidisciplinary approach to sustainable 
construction. Through their analysis of both traditional and emerging materials, the 
chapters in this book, authored by esteemed experts from around the world, paint a 
picture of optimism. They highlight the immense potential of sustainable materials 
and practices to address the most pressing challenges of our time. From rediscovering 
ancient techniques to embracing cutting-edge technologies, the solutions presented 
here demonstrate that a sustainable built environment is not only possible but also 
within our reach. By working in harmony with the natural world, we can create 
structures that enhance human experience while safeguarding the planet for future 
generations. The chapters of this book serve as a call to action and a reminder that the most 
important aspect of sustainability is the quality of life we create within the spaces 
we inhabit. It is an invitation to rethink our priorities, to align our efforts with the 
goal of fostering resilient, equitable, and environmentally responsible communities. Through its comprehensive examination of sustainable materials and practices, this 
volume aims to inspire innovation, collaboration, and a renewed commitment to 
building a better world. References

Part I 
Earth Based Construction 
From Ancient Wisdom to Modern Mastery: Earth’s Resurgence in Sustainable 
Building 
Around the globe, earth-based construction has been a major feature of the built envi-
ronment for most of human history. Even today, a signiﬁcant portion of dwellings, 
particularly in the global south, feature some element of earthen construction, 
whether it is rammed earth walls, clay-based plaster, or adobe bricks. Earthen mate-
rials, characterised by the textural triangle according to components of clay, silt, and 
sand, stand out as a logical choice for construction owing to their abundance and 
versatility. In nearly every natural environment, earthen materials can be sourced 
that provide sufﬁcient compressive strength for wall-elements in low-rise dwellings 
alongside sustainable outcomes in construction and structure use. With the industrialisation of societies and the transition away from vernacular 
construction to centralised production and distribution of building materials, earthen 
materials fell out of usage, their place being taken by mass produced masonry units 
and castable concretes; materials that offer convenience, uniformity, and scalability 
at the expense of signiﬁcant environmental footprints. As we begin to come to terms 
with the consequences of environmental degradation, climate change, and techno-
logical dominance of nature, the beauty, utility, and natural compatibility of earthen 
materials are sparking imaginations across many sectors. As housing access and 
human employment become increasingly concerning in societies around the world, 
an opportunity presents itself whereby earthen and stone construction, rather than 
being seen as manually intensive and possibly expensive labour, can be reinterpreted 
and reframed as meaningful skill-driven work that connects us to heritage, craft, and 
local materials while meeting our housing needs with a positive environmental and 
social impacts. A contrast to the screen-bound routines dominating modern life. While hands-on work is still a necessary and rewarding aspect of any construction 
endeavour, the efforts of scientists, engineers, architects, and thinkers and doers from 
across all disciplines are bringing knowledge to bear towards ﬁnding optimal ways 
to efﬁciently utilise the naturally occurring soils that surround us to create high-
value materials, structures, and communities. Vernacular earth-based construction 
is therefore an exciting an exciting material nexus point uniting past, present and

8

future—a bridge between ancestral wisdom and cutting-edge innovation, between 
local economies and global sustainability goals. This ﬁrst part of the book Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment 
explores this renaissance and brings insights from prominent practitioners and 
scholars around the world relating the many facets of earthen-construction. In the 
chapters of this part, the c

hemistry, physics, mineralogy, materials engineering, and 
construction methods relating to earth-based materials and their performance are 
examined. Further attention is given to the practical aspects of implementing earth-
based materials in the twenty-ﬁrst century in light of market requirements, circularity, 
barriers to adoption, and social and environmental impacts. Dorian A. H. Hanaor

Chapter 2 
The Life Cycles of Natural 
Buildings—From Traditional to Digital 
Lola Ben-Alon 
Abstract Material design research that looks at natural earth- and bio-based mate-
rials necessitates critical insights from a life cycle perspective. From traditional 
architecture to digital fabrication and additive manufacturing, understanding the 
environmental and social impacts is crucial for ﬁeld advancements, pedagogy, and 
novel research development. This chapter catalogues, demystiﬁes, and categorizes 
the terminology and attributes of natural materials through a life cycle approach. From the system boundaries deﬁnition to the thermal performance in the operational 
phase and the end-of-life recyclability, health, and biophilia, each building mix design 
in earth-ﬁber construction can illuminate the projected required development, and 
recommendations for the industry, driven by the need to incentivize an embodied 
approach to mandatory energy requirements. 2.1 
Impacts and the Building Share—Unveiling Raw Data 
When we talk about life cycle systems in material design research, we refer to the 
complete phases of a building—not just the use phase, but also its past iterations 
and potential future “reincarnations.” The term “life” can be misleading here; unlike 
living organisms, buildings don’t have a ﬁnite life. Instead, they transition through 
various forms—from landscapes to raw matter, to materials, to waste, and ideally, 
back to soil as nutrients. A relevant question arises: why focus on construction systems? Construction is 
the expression of dynamic energy ﬂows, from the molecular to the territorial scale, 
activating and reshaping the geography of the site. It is also the organization of 
matter. And what is a building’s matter if not energy? Matter is but captured energy. As Kiel Moe explains [15]: An object, such as a rock formation, is often thought 
as a ﬁxed, inert object or shape. However, it is actually an expression of dynamic 
energy systems and processes that range from the molecular to the territorial. Energy

of many processes are embedded or embodied in materials. Energy is what binds 
matter, what activates matter, what transfo

rms matter, and what organizes matter.” 
Construction systems are also dictated by material powers and can inﬂuence mate-
rial sources. Sod structures provide an interesting case: in the US prairie, colonial 
settlers used earth materials, weaving strong dry root systems into blocks of mud. However, instead of adopting Indigenous domed earth construction methods, the new 
settlers imposed European rectilinear designs, often resulting in structural failures 
and collapse. Material practices tied to cultural knowledge can therefore be disrupted 
by power systems, leading to erroneous imitations. While there are numerous studies documenting embodied energy and emissions 
in buildings, a true life cycle analysis of building materials should encompass the 
entire cycle, from a range of perspectives and entangled narratives: from the extrac-
tion of raw materials, land use and land grabs, through processing, regional health, 
transportation to the construction site, production, maintenance, cultural impacts, to 
the end of life via demolition, circular economy, recycling, upcycling, or reuse. A designer’s work, therefore, should encompass more than just the technical 
aspects of material selection and carbon or energy accounting—but rather engage 
in the synthesis of trade routes procurement procedures, health impacts evaluation, 
and equity analysis, all while balancing embodied carbon with the realities of trans-
portation, chemical and thermal processing, storage, and assembly lines. How can 
we better visualize the origins of materials to capture exchanges of energy, labor, 
emissions, and ideas? Critical analysis of data is an essential step to start with. In order to engaged in 
a fuller acknowledgment of life cycles, it is necessary to reinterpret primary data to 
challenge conventional narratives. Figure 2.1 underscores this by showing how the 
building sector’s carbon footprint can vary depending on how data is allocated. The 
leftmost pie chart, based on EPA data, attributes 13% of U.S. greenhouse gas (GHG) 
emissions to the building sector. When we reallocate emissions from the electric 
power sector (since it is not a ﬁnal end-use sector), the building sector’s contribution 
rises to 32%. The rightmost chart, which accounts for building materials within the 
industrial sector, pushes this ﬁgure to nearly 38%. This kind of critical inquiry into 
energy and emissions data is crucial, as it informs both funding priorities and policy 
decisions. By interrogating and reﬁning how data is presented, we can push for more 
accurate and equitable environmental policies. We must continue to interrogate institutional data critically, peeling back so to 
speak surface-level interpretations to examine the underlying raw data that shape 
our understanding of energy in relation to material systems. In Fig. 2.2, three pie 
charts illustrate the signiﬁcant, yet often overlooked, “other” category of energy 
consumption in both residential and commercial build

ings. The residential sector 
shows miscellaneous electric loads such as dishwashers, pool heaters, and spas, with 
2.50 quads of energy use attributed to “Other (Electric).” In commercial buildings, 
“Other (All Fuels)” accounts for 2.28 quads, with notable contributors including 
street lights, kitchen ventilation, and dry transformers. Together, these charts high-
light the hidden yet substantial energy demands outside of standard categories like 
heating, cooling, and lighting. Categories of ‘others’ represent a recursive echo of

Life Cycles in Material Design—From Qualitative

2.2.1 
Life Cycle Thinking 
Epistemologically, life cycle—our point of departure, is originally observed and 
acknowledged through looking at the natural world. In the biological world, every 
plant or living element has a cycle of life—from sprouting as a seed to germinating 
out to land, air, and water. Not only living beings but also living bodies, like water, have life cycles. The 
diagram in Fig. 2.3 illustrates the water cycle, beginning and ending at the ocean. From the ocean, water evaporates and condensates into clouds. Water then becomes 
precipitation that ﬂows back to the ocean or to the land, and from the land, water 
will percolate, inﬁltrate, or ﬂow back into the ocean. Fig. 2.3 The water cycle. Diagram courtesy o

f Dr. Fred Mackenzie, University of Hawai’i at Manoa

2
The Life Cycles of Natural Buildings—From Traditional to Digital
13
Emissions to air, water, and land can disturb this natural cycle by intruding on 
the movement of water throughout this system or harming the livelihood and health 
of water throughout these systems; acidiﬁcation, for instance, makes the water more 
acidic due to increased SO2 absorption within the water, as shown in Fig. 2.4. And, 
eutrophication causes a harmful algae bloom due to the excess use of fertilizers that 
run off into water bodies, thus blocking sunlight. Both interfere with marine life and 
the ecology of water. But how do we measure these impacts? Environmental Life Cycle Assessment 
(LCA) is a rigorous and systematic methodology that can sometimes feel like a 
never-ending rabbit hole. In its non-applied theoretical state, LCA is an iterative, 
endlessly looping calculation of resource and material ﬂows. The recursive nature 
of material ﬂows, with inputs branching out into further upstream processes, stem 
from the constant mining, reﬁning, and energy operations required from everything 
materials. LCA “tiers” represent these endless cycles, but since it is impossible to 
account for all possible iterations, LCA models typically halt the recursion at the 4th 
or 5th tier. LCA examines the product or building element through its various life cycle 
phases—like a biological machine—enumerating potential impacts at each stage. A 
general rule of thumb is that if a product or material requires minimal processing 
and is produced locally, we can reasonably assume that its environmental and social 
impacts will be more favorable compared to processed or imported alternatives. However, intuition alone is insufﬁcient for a thorough analysis. This is where LCA 
becomes critical, as it allows for the comparative evaluation of products, pushing 
forward policy and informed decision-making. While it is a valuable tool for tracking
Fig. 2.4 Acidiﬁcation and eutrophication of water bodies. Diagram courtesy of Dr. Fred Mackenzie, 
University of Hawai’i at Manoa

14
L. Ben-Alon
performance, LCA is not a cure-all for the built environment’s environmental chal-
lenges. Despite how scientiﬁc literature often positions it, LCA cannot be viewed as 
an “exact” science with a single deﬁnitive theory or axiom. Instead, it is a method 
that aids in understanding complexity but does not offer absolute solutions. To conduct a “proper” LCA we should follow four basic steps: (1) Deﬁne the goal, 
scope, system boundaries, and functional unit of the studied system, (2) Conduct 
an inventory analysis, or, if using software, check the inventory used “behind the 
scenes”, (3) Analyze the resulting impacts, and (4) Interpret the results. The life cycle inventory (LCI) is of utmost importance. It is the raw data used to 
create the impact assessment, and with which accuracy, or lack of, should be assumed 
for the LCA. Essentially, the LCI invo

lves investigating which raw materials go into 
the system of making a certain product or material, and, as a result, what are the 
outputs and emissions to air, land, and water. The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) 
is the conversion of LCI inventory raw data of energy, raw materials, and emissions, 
into meaningful metrics for the LCA audience. LCIA categories of such can be 
global warming potential and ozone depletion potential. Therefore, if the inventory 
is incorrect—then the impacts are incorrect and misleading. Figure 2.5 shows a 
general depiction of a building material system boundaries and the schematic inputs 
and outputs. Generally speaking, what comes out of the system—the thermal and 
emission outputs, are often harder to assess than the input energy and raw materials. 2.2.2 
System Boundaries 
To begin with, we need to establish a system for any life cycle analysis (LCA), and 
every system must have a boundary. But what exactly is a system boundary? A system 
encompasses anything we choose to study, measure, or engage within the world, 
focusing on a particular object of study while necessarily excluding others. This 
boundary delineates what is inside the system and what belongs to the ‘surroundings.’ 
It’s crucial to recognize that whenever we study something, we inevitably exclude 
many other elements. It’s impossible to analyze the entire universe with each study, 
so we must externalize or leave out certain components from our analysis. Broadly speaking, there are three types of systems: open, closed, and isolated. In 
an open system, both matter and energy can freely cross the boundary between the 
system and its surroundings. A closed system permits energy exchange but not the 
transfer of matter across its boundary. Lastly, isolated systems, which exist only as 
conceptual constructs, allow for neither matter nor energy exchange. So, where do 
buildings ﬁt into this framework? All buildings and their materials function as open 
systems. In fact, all materials in our surroundings originate from geological sources 
before they become architectural. They are inherently open systems, embodying 
inputs from the past and outputs for the future, along with unpredictable impacts and 
extensive planetary processes. The challenge becomes: how do we capture the full extent of processing required 
for a speciﬁc material system? As an example, consider the system boundary diagram

2

in Fig. 2.6, which is derived from a study of earth materials, which will be further

of the desired product—in this 
case, a wall—as well as undesirable emissions. In this study, the system boundaries

cradle” (from production back to production), “cradle to grave” (from production to 
disposal), or “cradle to gate.” The latter, depicted in Fig. 2.6, represents processes 
from the extraction of raw materials up to the point where the product is ready for 
construction—what we call “shovel ready.”

Fig. 2.6 System boundaries example for building materials
2.2.3 
Matter 
Materials can be classiﬁed into “families” or “groups,” a method often rooted in 
an engineered, mechanical view of materiality. In his seminal work on material 
selection for mechanical design, Ashby cataloged six material families: metals, 
ceramics, glasses, polymers, elastomers, and hybrids—a categorization based on 
various parameters, including atomic structure, formological behavior, and how a 
material is born into the world [2]. For example, both metals and ceramics are mined 
from the earth, but they emerge from very different geological processes. Metals 
are formed through magmatic and hydrothermal processes, resulting in a crystalline 
atomic structure, whereas ceramics are produced through sedimentation processes 
and are characterized by their rigidity and brittleness. While Ashby’s categorization offers a clear, mechanical perspective, it is very 
much a product of twentieth century material science—one that has heavily inﬂu-
enced today’s building material industry. The legacy of this engineering-focused 
approach is evident in the prevalence of transparent façade elements (from the glass

2
The Life Cycles of Natural Buildings—From Traditional to Digital
17
Fig. 2.7 Categorization of engineered system families. Image by the author 
family), steel and aluminum structures (from the metals family), luxurious vinyl 
ﬂooring (from the polymers family), and silicone sealants (from the elastomers 
family) (Fig. 2.7). But it’s precisely this narrow view of materials that we must now challenge. In the 
face of current social, environmental, and technical urgencies, we must move beyond 
such rigid categorizations. It’s time to shift focus toward naturally occurring, readily 
available, extensively variable, and minimally processed materials—material with 
shorter supply chains, reduced thermochemical processes, and healthier life cycles 
for both land and people. Figure 2.8 offers a counter-response to Ashby’s diagram. It makes a step towards adaptation to the building industry by looking at a material’s 
raw origin as its foundational attribute. Instead of focusing on properties after intense 
reﬁnement, this approach classiﬁes materials into groups like geological, plant-based, 
animal products, fungi, living bacteria, an

d micro-organisms. These categories offer 
a more expansive view of what materials are and how they can function in geogenic 
and biogenic architectural composites, a nod to Ashby’s “hybrids” but with a more 
regenerative and biophilic lens. In fact, nearly everything we reﬁne and synthesize in modern material science 
can be found in nature in a less concentrated form. Take polymers, for example. In nature, molecular compounds made of organic matter are inherently polymeric, 
aiding in plasticity and ﬂexibility, making them crucial for material design. Biopoly-
mers—those still synthesized, but from bio-based sources—are gaining attention 
in contemporary architecture and design. These biopolymers, often called bioplas-
tics, are derived from plant- and animal-based proteins and polysaccharides. But 
here lies a critical challenge: the term “bio-based” has become misleading. It often

18
L. Ben-Alon
Fig. 2.8 Categorization of natural material families. Image by the author
encompasses highly processed materials such as certain types of PVC or epoxy, 
which, although technically bio-based, are fossil fuel derivatives. To address this, I 
argue for clearer terminology—speciﬁcally, focusing on naturally occurring mate-
rials, meaning substances produced directly by living organisms without intensive 
chemical modiﬁcation. Additionally, the scale at which materials are examined can inform not only their 
performance but also the scale of their production processes. In material research, we 
often talk about three key scales: macro, mezzo, and micro. The macro scale refers to 
the structural assemblage or architectural form; the mezzo scale focuses on material 
testing, such as for rheology or fabrication parameters; and the micro scale investi-
gates the molecular or nano-level arrangements that inﬂuence the material’s behavior 
at the mezzo and macro levels. This multi-scalar approach is exempliﬁed in Fig. 2.9, 
which presents material design research conducted at the Columbia GSAPP Natural 
Materials Lab, where we enrich 3D printed earth with plant ﬁbers. The study shows 
how the ﬁbers, coated in clay paste and arranged according to the printing direction, 
behave thermally and structurally during extrusion from a 3D printer nozzle. This 
type of research highlights how materials can be understood and manipulated across 
scales, providing insights into their performance within architectural systems. 2

2.3 
Environmental Cycles: Energy, Climate, Metabolism, 
and Survivability 
When we consider the geological trade routes of building materials, we can imagine 
their histories, map their origins, and represent them in a system boundary diagram. But how do we quantify those journeys? How do we put a numbe

r on the thermo-
chemical processing, the mechanical forces exerted, the surface treatments applied, 
or the miles of transportation and intermediate storage that each material endures? How can we move from storytelling and visualization to enumeration? Take cob construction system as an example. Cob is an ancient and sustainable 
building system made from a mix of clay-rich soil, sand, straw, and water, forming a 
plastic wet-state composite that has been used for millennia in vernacular architec-
ture. While there is extensive inventory research for LCA on conventional construc-
tion assemblies, only a few studies exist for earthen materials. Cob construction has 
often been overlooked, perhaps due to its variability or perceived “low-tech” status. Yet, these materials and their life cycles deserve just as much scrutiny. The diagram 
in Fig. 2.10 presents the various constituents of a cob building system and their 
processing stages. The material ﬂows are broken down to the speciﬁc processes each 
undergoes; the straw component alone involves a series of movements from tilling and 
seeding to crop management, harvesting, and baling. The analysis includes the ﬁeld

20
L. Ben-Alon
Fig. 2.10 Life cycle inventory diagram for cob—an earth-ﬁber construction system. Image by the 
author following a previous study [6] 
preparation required, the infrastructure, the maintenance of equipment, the fertilizers 
and pesticides used, and the energy input throughout the process. Each of these stages 
adds another layer of complexity and a measurable contribution to the environmental 
footprint of cob as a construction system. In a related study, we evaluated the life cycle inventory (LCI) of straw-bale 
construction versus ﬁberglass insulation. As shown in Fig. 2.11, the processes 
for strawbale production include wheat cultivation, from tilling and seeding to 
harvesting and baling. In contrast, the ﬁberglass production process requires melting 
and reﬁning, adding binders, compressing, oven curing, cooling, and cutting. By 
diagramming alone, this exercise highlights the different levels of processing and 
thermochemical energy required for each material alternative, contributing to their 
overall environmental impact. Beyond this qualitative visual analysis, the inventory 
data was enumerated and synthesized into a life cycle impact analysis. In this way, we begin to account for the often-unseen processes that go into 
building materials. Through diagramming, we can track not just the physical mate-
rials, but the processes and entanglements they possess. Then, we move from visual-
izing material ﬂows to quantifying the true carbon or energy costs of these systems, 
shedding light on the broader ecological impacts they may have, as shown in Fig. 2.12. This transition accounts for the required LCA adherence to established regulations 
and standards, such as the ISO 14040 series, to ensure consistency and accuracy. Referring back to the LCA for cob, the inven

The visual summary of the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) results for 
embodied energy is shown in Fig. 2.14. The results are clear—earth assemblies 
outperform conventional assemblies across all impact categories for the embodied 
phase, which includes production from cradle to construction site. However, a sensitivity analysis of these results shows that even under the worst-
case scenario for the earthen assemblies—such as increased transportation distances 
or greater wall thickness—the environmental performance remained signiﬁcantly 
superior to that of conventional assemblies, as shown in Fig. 2.15. But what about the operational phase? In this study, the operational (“use”) phase 
was assessed using a hybrid methodology that combined ﬁeld data gathered through 
stakeholder surveys, computational thermal simulations, and impact assessments. Speciﬁcally, a survey involving 35 homeowners of earth buildings was conducted to 
collect data on HVAC usage and comfort patterns within actual structures built with

earth-ﬁber construction systems. The results revealed a striking trend: these earth-
ﬁber assemblies exhibit remarkable energy efﬁciency, as they require minimal space 
conditioning for cooling throughout the year, regardless of climate conditions. This 
phenomenon can be attributed to their high thermal inertia and intrinsic evaporative 
cooling properties. As illustrated in Fig. 2.16, only four out of the 35 surveyed struc-
tures (11%) utilized air conditioning (AC) for cooling purposes. The overwhelming 
majority of these buildings effectively employed passive cooling strategies, even in 
the hotter and warmer climates designated as ASHRAE climate zones 1, 2, and 3. This actual ﬁeld data shows that in reality, this is how earth-ﬁber buildings are used, 
highlighting the effectiveness of earth-ﬁber materials in providing thermal comfort 
while signiﬁcantly reducing energy consumption and thereby contributing to more 
passive architectural regimes. There are, therefore, multiple synergies possible between earthen wall construc-
tion and dynamic passive heating and cooling strategies like shading, natural venti-
lation, passive solar heating, and night insulation. For this purpose, thorough thermal 
and hygroscopic characterization is critically needed to pursue dramatically reduced 
space-heating and cooling needs, as well as passive thermal survivability in natural 
materials. The subsequent analysis continues the examination of the four earthen wall assem-
blies—cob, light straw clay, rammed earth, and insulated rammed earth—alongside 
three conventional assemblies: concrete masonry unit (CMU), insulated CMU, and 
ﬁberglass insulated wood frame. By incorporating adaptive thermal comfort ranges 
across six distinct climates, the results analyzed thermal responses with and without 
well-operated passive heating and cooling systems, alongside energy balance and 
operational impacts as part of the life cycle thinking. To further enhance our understanding, the next step involved acquiring better 
data regarding the actual thermal performance of each material through computa-
tional models and scenario simulations. This approach aimed to account for material 
porosity, thermal mass properties, and hygrothermal characteristics to fully capture 
the true and complex performance of the earth-ﬁber assemblies. The chosen climates 
represented dry, warm-hot conditions, as well as colder climates where earth mate-
rials have historically been utilized. An expected insight, shown in Fig. 2.17 illustrates

particularly pronounced in the insulated earth-ﬁber assemblies—insulated rammed

brought 50–80% of uncomfortable (overheated) hours into the adaptive comfort zone. Meanwhile, in steppe and cool-summer Mediterranean climates, the combination of 
passive heating and cooling eliminated 25–70% of uncomfortable hours. Figure 2.18 illustrates the heat ﬂux mechanisms underlying this performance, 
showcasing inherent patterns within insulated earth-ﬁber assemblies—particularly 
the light straw clay. Notably, light straw clay demonstrates superior hygroscopic 
performance, with its ability to uptake and evaporate moisture contributing to the 
thermal stability of the wall, reﬂected by the notable larger blue bars, showing the 
latent heat ﬂux in the interior of the wall assembly. This characteristic not only 
enhances thermal comfort but also potentially contributes to the relative humidity 
management and a result, the intrinsic cooling effect, of the assembly. Therefore, 
light straw clay represents a promising assembly with signiﬁcant potential to inform 
building codes concerning energy performance. 26

The results are further illustrated in Fig. 2.20, demonstrating that insulated 
rammed earth or compressed earth blocks (CEBs) require 65% less energy demand 
throughout the production and operation phases compared to insulated concrete 
masonry. Similarly, light straw clay inﬁll within a wooden frame can reduce energy 
demand by up to 53% compared to ﬁberglass insulation. These ﬁndings are even 
more striking when considering emissions, which arise not only from energy gener-
ation but also from chemical reactions during material processing and fugitive emis-
sions during quarry operations. Light straw clay outperforms all other earth-ﬁber 
and conventional assemblies across its cradle-to-grave life cycle. Its exceptional 
embodied and operational carbon performance is attributed to its low extractive 
constituent materials and the synergistic combination of thermal mass and thermal 
resistivity. This unique interplay effectively reduces both heating and cooling energy 
demand while enhancing hygroscopic performance. Building on the compelling evidence of light straw clay’s superior energy efﬁ-
ciency and minimal emissions, subsequent development of fabrication applications 
of this material in contemporary construction was pursued, with a digital ﬁber-
rich earth mixture developed to facilitate an insulative inﬁll method suitable for 3D 
printing. This approach not only draws inspiration from the traditional light straw clay 
but also embraces the potential of additive manufacturing with earth materials—a 
rapidly emerging ﬁeld that aligns with sustainable design principles. Currently devel-
oped in recent research using a “cob”-like mix design, the following results look at 
novelly developing ﬁber-rich light-straw-clay-like 3D printable recipes. Increasing 
the content of plant ﬁbers in 3D printed earth was here pursued with the goals of 
achieving higher thermal resistivity potential for code compliance, enhanced ductility 
and ﬂexural strength, and lightweight assemblies that are more viable for construction 
economy and transportation viability. Figure 2.21 summarizes a continuous research 
that was geared towards the development and material design research of 3D printed 
light earth-ﬁber mixtures with a range of plant ﬁbers from leaf, stem, and bast origins, 
including wheat straw, banana, hemp, kenaf, and sisal ﬁber. 28

2.4 
Social Cycles, Biophilia, Accessibility, and Community 
Engagement 
Social impacts and material geographies of material designs require further inves-
tigation; the environment is not just the air, ground, and water, but the people with 
whom we work and live. The metabolic ﬂow of materials is linked through economic 
forces that reﬂect the history of place. 2
The Life Cycles of Natural Buildings—From Traditional to Digital
31
Fig. 2.22 GWPnet values and the reference thicknesses for the developed 3D printable earth-ﬁber

Figure 2.23 shows the consumption of raw materials in the US over time, as 
we moved from organic biomass to industrial minerals, and the ﬂuctuations of 
construction materials in response to societal events, wars, and economic turbulences. Materials and culture, reﬂected through major events in our society are constantly 
enmeshed and interweaved. While there is a widespread consensus on the social beneﬁts of earthen and bio-
based materials, a systemic analysis that quantiﬁes these beneﬁts can shed light 
on the perceptual advantages and just encounters. From the extraction of its raw 
components to its end of life, a construction material’s production, transportation, 
use, and disposal inherently engage a variety of stakeholders at different levels: from 
workers in production facilities to designers, builders, occupants, and demolition 
contractors. The social impact of these involvements can—and should—be evaluated 
through a number of categories deﬁned by stakeholder groups and social/socio-
economic criteria, such as health and wellbeing, local employment, and workers 
conditions. Social life cycle assessment (S-LCA) is a quantitative and qualitative method that 
aims to assess the social concerns of human health, well-being, and social welfare. Similar to an environmental LCA, this S-LCA impact assessment is quantiﬁed using 
indicators across the entire life cycle of the building material while prioritizing areas 
of “hot spots” in the analysis, provided in terms of health and safety, worker condi-
tions, regional impacts, and community engagements, and regional sustainability. S-LCA is still an evolving methodology that is informed by the challenge of quanti-
fying societal attributes. Often used with a hot-spot analysis, S-LCA helps identify

32

Fig. 2.23 Consumption of raw materials in the US over time and major societal events [12]
impacts perceived ﬁrsthand by a range of stakeholders: manufacturers, designers, 
contractors, researchers, and owners. A comparative S-LCA was developed for earth, bio-based, and conventional 
(concrete and steel) materials following an online survey. Using the UNEP/SETAC 
guidelines, the survey was designed to collect social impact data from stakeholders 
in four categories: workers, occupants, local community, and society, and in response 
to eight impact categories:

health and safety, human resource management, profes-
sional development and supplier relationship, use stage responsibility, community 
development, regional environmental and human health, broader societal commit-
ments, and public interest. In order to align with the process of construction material 
production, this S-LCA also accounts for six additional categories that reﬂect the 
life cycle of building materials: extraction, production, transportation, construction, 
occupancy, and demolition/recycling. While most of the social impact categories 
affect all the life cycle stages of a material, not all apply to the occupancy phase. The distribution of the social impact categories based on the life cycle stages and 
stakeholders is shown in Fig. 2.24. The ﬁnal S-LCA results, shown in Fig. 2.25, highlight that earthen materials 
scored the highest across all social impact categories, followed by bio-based mate-
rials, particularly in health and safety, impacts on local communities, and regional 
sustainability. In contrast, concrete and steel performed the worst in most categories 
except worker conditions, where they excelled due to better professional training 
and social beneﬁts, reﬂecting the more established industries existing for these mate-
rials. These ﬁndings raise questions about the role of industrialization in reducing the 
social beneﬁts of building materials. While natural materials currently outperform

processed materials, the scores might exacerbate in the regional impact categories 
and be enhanced for workers’ conditions as the natural material industries grow. In terms of their health attributes over their use phase, natural materials were 
shown to create a biophilic effect, one that can cultivate positive evidence-based 
therapeutic effects. Speciﬁcally, when it comes to hospital and healthcare environ-
ments, natural materials like wood, bamboo, cork, and earth, were shown to affect 
occupant-related outcomes, such as increased recovery, reduced medication intake, 
and enhanced staff performance. Wood furniture was shown to reduce patients’ 
systolic blood pressure, a physical manifestation of stress [18]. Similarly, medical 
staff reported preferring 60% wooden surfaces instead of rooms with no wood or full 
wood [16]. The effects of natural materials were shown to be important for balanced 
thermoregulatory, cardiovascular, and hormonal parameters while meeting regula-
tory requirements, as summarized in Fig. 2.26. For instance, patients in rooms with 
wood paneling had 20% less stress (indicated by measured plasma cortisol levels) 
than patients in rooms with concrete walls [17]. The location and placement of natural materials across a hospital room should 
follow visual and touch surface areas to provide patients with stimuli. Therefore,

Conclusions 
The advancement and justiﬁcation of natural earth- and bio-based materials through 
the lens of life cycle assessment (LCA) unveils critical insights in material design 
research. As we navigate the intersection of traditional knowledge and new fabrica-
tion techniques, the environmental and social implications that underpin materials are

2
The Life Cycles of Natural Buildings—From Traditional to Digital
35
Fig. 2.26 Evaluation of natural versus artiﬁcial wood material. Summarized from (image by 
Maghlakelidze et al. [14], following Kokulu and Acun Ozgunler [13])
more important than ever for informed design decision-making. This chapter asks to 
catalog, demystify, and categorize the terminology and attributes of life cycle thinking 
and natural material systems. By establishing and diagramming system boundaries 
that encompass the entire life cycle—from material extraction and processing to 
operational performance and end-of-life recyclability—we can elucidate the complex 
relationships between material “recipes” so to speak and their impacts. The designer’s role, in this multifaceted analysis landscape, transcends mere tech-
nical material selection and carbon accounting. Instead, designers (and building 
professionals, in general) must engage in a synthesis of procurement procedures, 
health impact evaluations, and equity analyses, balancing embodied carbon with the 
realities of transportation and processing, thermal comfort, and the exchanges of 
labor, resources, and ideas. Both environmental life cycle assessment (LCA) and social life cycle assessment 
(S-LCA) should be further used as qualitative and quantitative exercises for a fuller 
realization of the intricate relationships between material choices, environmental 
impacts, and social dynamics in architectural practice. Speciﬁcally, for earth-ﬁber construction techniques—the study outlined in this 
chapter reveals a compelling narrative: even under adverse scenarios, such as 
increased transportation distances or adjustments in wall thickness, the LCA of 
earthen assemblies demonstrates signiﬁcantly lower embodied carbon and energy 
footprints compared to conventional materials. Further diving into operational 
phases, the coupling of thermal resistivity with thermal heat capacity was shown 
to be superior, making assemblies like insulated rammed earth and light straw 
clay particularly beneﬁcial. From a social and accessibility stand-point, aspects of 
labor practices, community health, and cultural heritage reveal that earth materials 
are perceived as advantageous by stakeholders, with th

e exception of professional

36
L. Ben-Alon
training and working conditions—a sign of reﬂection of their non-commodiﬁed 
nature and required development within the building industry. As the future of digital earth-ﬁber construction unfolds, embracing plant ﬁber and 
agrowaste materials within raw (“non-stabilized”) earth pastes offers transformative 
opportunities for environmentally sound and socially just architectural practices. By 
synthesizing insights from LCA and SLCA, we can advocate for a built environment 
that honors both ecological integrity and the spirit of people and place. As we move 
forward, this chapter invites us to contemplate and commit to an architectural ethos 
that embraces variability, complexity, and the intricate interplay between materials, 
communities, and the natural world. References

Chapter 3 
The Geographies of Sustainable 
Bio-Based Materials for Modern 
Methods of Construction 
Alejandro Veliz Reyes 
, Alexandra Carr 
, Duhan Olmez 
, 
David Avilés 
, and Mohamed Gomaa 
Abstract This chapter outlines a research agenda around material ﬂows and geogra-
phies involved in the adoption of bio-based materials by modern methods of construc-
tion (MMC). In doing so, we argue that supply chains for bio-based materials are 
not only a matter of technical speciﬁcation and procurement, but are inﬂuenced by 
broader societal, economic, and environmental forces that detach digital innovation 
from its physical, place-based manifestations. This challenge is particularly acute 
in post-industrial communities aiming to transition from localised manufacturing 
industries to knowledge- and creativity-based economies. The research is contex-
tualised within a stream of research projects, of which four are presented as illus-
trative case studies. These projects have focused on local bio-based materials and 
communities often considered peripheral to mainstream digital innovation and MMC 
discourse: automation in earthen construction, participatory housing design in a rural 
community, the development of digital infrastructures for timber manufacturing, 
and MMC housing procurement in a government-funded rural housing programme. These projects are framed by the investigation on geographies of making set forth 
by Carr and Gibson. The Chapter concludes by identifying further work opportuni-
ties for this research agenda and suggests a wider and multidisciplinary approach to 
investigating the adoption of bio-based materials in construction. A.

A. Veliz Reyes et al. 3.1 
Introduction 
Bridport, in rural south-west England, was the cordage and sailcloth capital of the 
United Kingdom (UK) from medieval times—at least from 1213 [1]—until the mid-
eighteenth century [2]. Its industrial ecosystem was inextricably linked to its natural 
and urban productive landscapes: it has a good natural environment to grow hemp 
and ﬂax, and the resulting cordage industry shaped the morphology of the town itself 
with (still visible today) long urban plots developed to house rope-making facilities 
[3] (Fig. 3.1). Bridport’s industry was recognised with a Royal Charter to avoid local 
competition that restricted the production of cordage to Bridport’s inhabitants, ‘and 
restricting hemp sales within ﬁve miles of Bridport’ [4]. The town reached national 
acclaim from its key role on the development of the maritime and naval prowess of 
the British Empire, only to later decline due to competition from imported cordage 
(Russia being a main supplier of hemp and cheaper labour from the 1550s), and the 
difﬁculties with supplying enough cordage for an ever-increasing maritime sector. A second wave of development is reported from the 1820s with the expansion of its 
port in Bridport Harbour (today called West Bay), with commercial trafﬁc coming to 
an end in the 1980s after a slow decline [5]. Despite beneﬁcial environmental, urban, 
and historical conditions for industrial growth, the town rapidly de-industrialised 
and became one of many post-industrial UK coastal towns, struggling to move away 
from manufacturing and material trade economic models and, today, showing signs of 
economic and social stagnation: problematic health outcomes [6], youth emigration 
in search for higher education and career opportunities, and low proportion of adults 
in employment, to name a few [7]. While this post-industrial decline may seem historical, processes of ‘de-
industrialisation’ still take place around the world. Rural places, typically associ-
ated to commodiﬁed aesthetics, materials, or productive systems [8], struggle to 
‘shift’ into knowledge economies and their reconversion is often seen as a matter 
of knowledge, geographic, entrepreneurial, or digital marginalisation. Works such

as McGranahan et al.’s [9] suggest an injection of entrepreneurship and creative 
professionals as a way to prompt knowledge-based economic development in rural 
spaces. Wider studies across rural Europe show encouraging signs of rural spaces as 
incubators of social innovation resulting from close-knit communities and feelings 
of marginalisation from mainstream governmental policies and industrial strategies 
[10]. However, the binarism of material and industrial production vs knowledge or 
creative economic models is challenged by Gibson et al. [11], arguing that both 
approaches are often—mistakenly—seen as opposed as a result of ‘overlapping 
conﬂations, assumptions and, in some cases, political and deﬁnitional manoeuvrings’ 
(p. 87). Today, Bridport communities are successfully resuming local hemp and ﬂax indus-
tries and beneﬁtting from those as break crops in local farms. Without a cordage 
maritime market to service, local uses of hemp include insulation bio-based material 
for local housing projects. The town has, however, lost some of the infrastructural

‘connective tissue’ that brings together its local material ﬂow ecosystem, with mate-
rial providers, farmers, housing organisations, and builders not always clustered 
around local supply chains for housing construction, lack of educational facilities to 
develop appropriate skills, and a lack of a localised productive ecosystem. Wessex 
Community Assets (WCA), a local community housing organisation, approached 
our research team at the University of Plymouth to scope the adoption of local mate-
rials in the production of community housing, while concurrently injecting modern 
methods of construction (MMC) within their supply chain. By doing so, their expec-
tation is to generate new building innovation around bio-based materials, as well as 
to grow the social infrastructures necessary to stimulate the local economy such as 
the creation of high-value skills and employment opportunities for its community—a 
vision for a new community-led economy [12] for the revitalisation of rural coastal 
towns in the UK that considers MMC for social good. In doing so, we note the shortcomings of a reductionist discourse placing digital, 
creative and industrial innovation in contrast to place-based forms of physical mate-
rial production. Instead of positioning this Chapter within this binary research space, 
we argue that digital and material ﬂows are part of more complex and critical forms 
of knowledge production [13, 14] that can result in place-based societal beneﬁts 
and impacts. In more detail, the Chapter outlines this theoretical position under the

42
A. Veliz Reyes et al. conceptual lens of these different ‘geographies of making’ [11, 15, 16]—with a partic-
ula

r focus on the geographies of low-carbon sustainable forms of material produc-
tion and digitally-enabled forms of construction, here illustrated by four concate-
nated research projects delivered between 2018–2024. This research trajectory was 
initiated with the feasibility studies “Computing craft: Manufacturing cob struc-
tures using robotically controlled 3D printing”, and “Participatory Housing Manu-
facturing”—both funded through the Engineering and Physical Science Research 
Council (EPSRC) network “Connected Everything: Accelerating Digital Manufac-
turing Research Collaboration and Innovation”. Both projects explored the use of bio-
based, low-carbon materials in the context of digital forms of architectural produc-
tion, and implemented methodologies grounded on making, hands-on prototyping, 
and material experimentation. Then, the Chapter outlines the project “100 Factories” 
funded by the UK Forestry Commission which focused on the delivery of the digital 
infrastructures required for SMEs to join the housebuilding sector, creating regional 
networks able to deliver low-carbon housing under a Design for Distributed Manu-
facturing approach. Last, the Chapter concludes with an overview of an ongoing 
investigation on regional networks of digital manufacturing in the context of the 
Rural Housing Programme delivered by the Chile Ministry of Housing and Urbanism, 
funded by the Arts and Humanities Research Council (AHRC). Methodologically, our work embodies material, disciplinary, and knowledge fric-
tions which speak to broader industrial divides—such as the difﬁculties of incor-
porating local bio-based materials into construction procurement processes, and the 
problematic divides between the creative thinking of the built environment, and its 
production [11]. Following a “research in residence” approach [17], our ‘embodied 
interventions’ [15], while providing further insight into issues of building produc-
tion, additionally shed light on how participatory and hands-on methods can generate 
broader societal beneﬁt, and how hands-on methods can provide unique lived expe-
rience insights into place-based material innovation [18] that engages with local 
communities and geographies of making (Fig. 3.2). This aligns with institutional 
expertise on “research in residence”, embedding our research teams within communi-
ties and material cultures in order to better understand the complexities of technology 
adoption, local development, and digital equity needs. Concurrently, working “in resi-
dence” allows for results that align with the needs of community groups, generating 
impact pathways and mobilising knowledge throughout a project delivery instead of 
a follow-on, later stage of the research process [19, 20]. In addition to the projects 
introduced in this chapter, this approach has been implemented in the challenge areas 
of ageing and access to heritage [21], technology adoption in design studios [22], or 
care home design [24],

to name a few. This chapter is structured as a case study report. Following a section outlining 
the theoretical position on ‘geographies of making’ by Gibson and Carr [11, 15, 16], 
we present four case study projects that embody critical approaches to intersect bio-
based material ﬂows with MMCs. As mentioned above, these include: (1) automation 
in earthen construction, (2) participatory mapping of timber and hemp material ﬂows 
in Bridport, (3) the creation of digital infrastructures needed to grow local industrial 
ecosystems of SMEs for timber manufacturing in housing projects, and (4) a case

study on the adoption of MMC in the context of a government-funded rural housing
