Theoretical Framework 
The pressing need to reduce carbon emissions has led into the discussion about how 
countries can decarbonise their economies in a time of climate crisis. In this sense, 
the agenda has been dominated by the “big discussion” around “mission-oriented” 
strategies, in which governments and economic elites would play a signiﬁcant role 
[25, 26]. On the other hand, there have been calls to re-orient the analysis of produc-
tion networks and value chains into global decarbonisation efforts in the economy 
[27]. However, those debates tend to focus on global and national scales (often, in 
the Global North), with less attention being paid to local and regional experiences of 
economic transformation [28]—or at least, how local resources could be connected 
to those global value chains by means of raw materials, labour power, or strategic 
locations. In consequence, the question of how local place-based making practices 
can ﬁnd their space in productive processes to respond to their own challenges (and at 
the same time contribute to decarbonise the economy) has been overlooked. Further-
more, it is relevant to understand how a more local scale of enquiry can bridge the 
knowledge gap between traditional making practices embedded in their landscape, 
with new technologies that could provide an opportunity to break into contempo-
rary production processes and economic beneﬁt. In this way, this chapter seeks to 
scholarly re-engage with bio-based materials in the context of local communities,

44
A. Veliz Reyes et al. and understand how the geographies of making could “come into being” [29] when 
intersected with MMC. In the context of our research, Bridport and other communities dealing with digital 
inequity have transited through different economic waves, which reﬂect the uneven 
spatial character of capitalist development in which different places are conn

ected 
to global production processes and others are disconnected depending on the ﬂows 
of capital [30]. In this sense, the Anthropocene present new challenges for research 
and development and, as Carr, et al. [31] discuss, the academic community needs to 
critically engage with the current economic processes and understand how particular 
and often devalued making practices could be most relevant to address some of 
the challenges of the Anthropocene. We identify bio-based construction and MMC 
as a relevant area to visibilise these challenge spaces—for example, MMCs have 
typically driven an agenda of optimisation, modularisation and industrial linkages 
with areas like manufacturing and computer sciences. Building upon existing global 
industrial manufacturing (such as 3D printing or robotic production) and data (such 
as building information modelling) frameworks, MMC do however struggle to scale 
within a construction industry of high fragmentation, low skill, and low technological 
adoption [32]. These systemic barriers have been recently under scrutiny in the UK 
Parliament following the enquiry “what’s gone wrong?” [33]. Back to Bridport, 
we can illustrate this struggle with the example of a relatively simple timber panel 
construction. The local community is concerned about their local environment and 
has expressed their willingness to ﬁnd ways of working with local ash timber. Ash has 
been affected by the dieback fungal disease, likely to decimate a majority of the ash 
forests across the UK [34]. Digital modes of production, such as computer-controlled 
milling, however, operate based on ﬂat sheet materials such as plywood panels— 
plywood is not produced in the UK and is mostly imported from Brazil, China, or 
Latvia to a great carbon expense. Ash timber—as any other timber!—however, bends, 
cracks, it has knots and natural warps, a deﬁned grain direction and defects from the 
dieback infection (Fig. 3.3). It’s a complex, heterogeneous material that struggles to 
ﬁt within digitally-enabled modes of production despite locally established supply 
chains, availability of local workforce, and urgent local environmental concerns. We can hypothesise, then, that the following gaps can be behind the frictions 
we have encountered between bio-based sustainable materials, and industrial MMC 
approaches:
• Disciplinary: The separation of physical making from the mainstream creative 
and knowledge-based ‘design economy’. • Economic: The separation of local supply chains and skills from global industrial 
reconﬁgurations. • Geographic: The separation of rurality and place-based research from digital 
innovation discourse and wider industrial policies. Here we identify the work of Chantel Carr and Chris Gibson as instrumental 
to understand these conﬂating gaps [11, 16]. Their conceptualisation of “geogra-
phies if making” helps us question how speciﬁcation of local materials is not only 
a matter of embodied carbon quantiﬁcation or construct

ion procurement challenges,

3
The Geographies of Sustainable Bio-Based Materials for Modern …
45
Fig. 3.3 Experimentation with local ash timber in a panel design, testing out the feasibility of local 
materials within digital forms of production. Source Carr, 2022
but a broader concatenation of social, economic, and geographic issues encom-
passing industrial policy and deliberate decisions to place creativity as an economic 
regeneration opportunity “replacing” post-manufacturing local economies, rather 
than connected to pre-existing place-based forms of physical making [11]. Only 
recently, outputs from design-driven research have attempted to establish a discus-
sion around local supply chains and their broader economic beneﬁt (for instance see 
[35]). Although rarely explored in the context of construction, this challenge area 
compounds with a commodiﬁcation of craft as a “rural” activity taking place outside 
core centres of capital and labour (e.g. cities) and thus, peripheral to policies such 
as industrial strategies (which, in the UK, comprised a “Transforming Construction” 
challenge fund), levelling-up [36], and innovation funding opportunity [37]. Last, we stress how our ‘research in residence’ approach resonates with Carr and 
Gibson’s work around ‘slow scholarship’ [15] which, although aimed at a geography 
audience, speaks of participatory methods and embeddedness as a way to further 
investigate the relationships between body and material work. We note, however, 
that beyond the understanding of labour our research has implemented hands-on 
and participatory methods to explore the frictions between low-carbon materials and 
digital forms of building production—thus, looking at the intersections of labour, 
technology, and material cultures over extended periods of time. Here we have dealt 
with a variety of forms of knowledge production and representation, including collec-
tively produced creative media, digital fabrication data and technologies, material 
studies, prototypes (including building-size prototypes) and interviews that capture 
lived experiences of a wide array of participants. 46
A. Veliz Reyes et al. 3.3 
Case Studies 
3.3.1 
Automation in Earthen Construction 
Explorations into the use of automation and digital manufacturing technologies for 
earthen construction, such as 3D printing, began around 2011 but initially remained 
at the prototypical stage due to a lack of deﬁnitive knowledge on the correlation 
between materials, machines, and craftspeople [38]. The ﬁrst signiﬁcant attempt to 
holistically examine the feasibility of incorporating automation technologies into 
traditional earth construction emerged in 2018 through an EPSRC-funded collab-
orative project between Cardiff University and the University of Plymouth. This 
project explored the potential of combining cob, a low-cost material typical from 
vernacular constructions in the south-west of England, with robotic 3D printing 
to support the local

building industry. By integrating traditional construction tech-
niques with emerging technologies, the research aimed to establish a new ethos that 
embraces vernacular knowledge and local skills for digital innovation. The develop-
ment of a robotic 3D printing cob framework followed a standard innovation process, 
progressing from concept to proof of concept and prototyping. The project had three 
primary goals: 
1. Developing a descriptive framework and situating robotic fabrication within a 
theoretical view of tectonics and craft practice. 2. Establishing empirical knowledge on material performance aspects and technical 
correlations within the man–machine workﬂow. 3. Involving prospective community members, represented by students and profes-
sionals, in hands-on explorations of digital earth construction to create a 
transitional roadmap from scientiﬁc explorations to practical application. A discussion on the aforementioned multidisciplinary goals was further devel-
oped in [13], which introduced a framework integrating nuanced craft elements and 
extended Pye and Stein’s deﬁnitions to include the issues of ‘risk’ and ‘certainty’ 
involved in the negotiation between material behaviour, technology, and traditional 
forms of making—a negotiated space of architectural production tensioned by the 
frictions between localised forms of production, and digital innovation. This frame-
work highlighted the need for multidisciplinary collaboration and suggested further 
research in knowledge representation, robotic material culture, and human–robot 
collaboration. To explore the practical and industrial aspects of 3D-printed cob, 
several studies investigated its workability, design, material processing, structural and 
thermal performance, and life cycle analysis (LCA) which included elements of mate-
rial innovation and characterisation, such as the formulation of bespoke cob mixes 
for 3D printing extrusion. Discussions with local experts and systematic testing, 
led to a revised cob mixture with increased moisture content, which facilitated 3D 
printing through overcoming issues like slow extrusion rates and inconsistency. This 
learning process concluded into developing of a unique extrusion system capable

3
The Geographies of Sustainable Bio-Based Materials for Modern …
47
Fig. 3.4 Hands-on workshops at Cardiff University on robotic 3D printing of cob in 2017 (left) 
and 2018 (right). Source Veliz Reyes et al. 2019, Gomaa 2018 (personal archive). of improving printing speed and quality [39, 40]. On the other hand, performance-
based studies showed improved thermal conductivity for 3D-printed cob walls [41], 
where the structural performance studies conﬁrmed the feasibility of 3D-printed cob 
as load-bearing walls in multi-storey houses [42]. Finally, a follow-on LCA study 
indicated favourable environmental beneﬁts [43] when compared to concrete. Methodologically, each study was integrated into a series of hands-on workshops 
involving stud

ents and professionals at the Welsh School of Architecture, Cardiff 
University (Fig. 3.4). Engaging students early in their academic careers helps create 
a continuous roadmap from learning to research and ultimately to practical appli-
cation. This hands-on experience enhances their understanding and skills while 
fostering innovation and fresh perspectives that drive the ﬁeld forward. Overall, 
this work bridges the gap between traditional earth construction and contemporary 
digital practices, offering a practical framework for both new builds and the reno-
vation of historic cob buildings. It emphasises the importance of interdisciplinary 
approaches, involving material science, design, architecture, robotics, and mechan-
ical engineering, in modern architectural design and production. Additionally, it 
contributes to robotic material culture and human–robot collaboration, advocating 
for the continuous pursuit of innovative hybrid solutions in the evolving ﬁeld of 
construction materials and methods. 3.3.2 
Participatory Design in Bridport 
This project is funded by the Arts and Humanities Research Council and focuses 
on investigating material futures for local housing in Bridport, including the imple-
mentation of participatory methods and digital technologies. A residential ﬁeldwork

48
A. Veliz Reyes et al. plan has been delivered through a collection of design interventions including pop-
up makerspaces, interviews and hands-on workshops with 63 community members 
over a year. More notably, this project supported the creation of a local “Living Lab” 
(Fig. 3.5)—a disused barn in Denhay Farm which served as a space for construction 
cocreation, material workshops, prototyping, and installation of different local mate-
rials in the context of housing prototypes and local hemp and timber supply chains. This participatory approach not only allowed for a long-term embeddedness within 
the local community [17], but more profoundly with ethical forms of investigation 
which require participation, diversity, and the inclusion of community members often 
ignored by developers of digital innovations [44]. As a result, the project helped us 
collaboratively develop an understanding of the landscape of low-carbon, bio-based 
materials for construction within a localised context through place-based practices. In 
addition to prototyping, methods such as collaborative mapping allowed for broader 
discussions around material ﬂows within the region, and how the movement of mate-
rials is a cross-sectional area of enquiry which overlaps with community concerns 
in areas such as:
• The socio-technical infrastructures necessary to adopt MMC with local and 
sustainable materials, such as skills programmes and availability of equipment. • The identiﬁcation of speciﬁc areas of intervention around housing, such as retroﬁt, 
new building development, and the compartmentalisation of existing housing 
units. • The need to replace carbon-intensive supply chains

by low-carbon alternatives 
(i.e. materials such as cement, steel, and plastic-based insulation materials). By facilitating the cocreation of a vision for a desirable future [45], local construc-
tion industry, community members were able to beneﬁt from design thinking and 
creative methods (such as the production of maps and visual media materials), 
engaging in more aspirational speculation not otherwise available through standard 
methods of public consultation such as polls. Upon completion of this research 
project, the Living Lab in Denhay Farm was utilised to deliver a skills programme, 
allowing community members to participate in the construction of the Open House, 
a community facility designed by Common Practice in Bridport (Fig. 3.6). 3.3.3 
Digital Infrastructures for Timber Innovation 
On scaling this line of research into a wider set of scenarios, this project aimed 
to develop the digital systems necessary to allow small and medium-sized enter-
prises (SMEs) to join the MMC housebuilding industry. By partnering with Open 
Systems Lab, we are particularly focused on the open-source building system Wiki-
house, consisting of volumetric prefabricated timber blocks (Fig. 3.7). By employing 
a participatory co-design methodology, the project enables the active involvement 
of industry stakeholders from the early stages, addressing both the technical and

socio-cultural challenges associated with digital adoption in the construction sector. This has included on-site collaboration with design companies, manufacturers, and 
community housing organisations. The outputs of the project (described below) are 
being trialled with local SMEs involved in the timber and manufacturing sectors: 
Woodlab Studio Ltd (Devon) and Maahee’s Way Ltd (Leicester). In terms of local 
and economic development, this project expects to allow SMEs to join a sector 
currently dominated by large-scale, capital intensive MMC companies that struggle 
to cover the UK limited housing demand. Instead of high-risk investments, a more 
distributed network of manufacturers would spread the delivery risks, and create 
employability opportunity in the SME sector—in the UK, SMEs account for 60% of 
employment, and over 50% of the total industry revenue [47]. In detail, the project is currently developing 2 outputs. A manufacturing control 
platform, which will set a series of standards and processes to set quality checks, toler-
ances in manufacturing and material speciﬁcations, and other environmental speciﬁ-
cations in order to improve the ﬁnal quality and accuracy of the building blocks. These 
will be built using the open-source database of cutting and assembly ﬁles created 
by Open Systems Lab, and comprise testing and evaluation of the control plan by 
industry partners, and the Digital Fabrication Lab at the University of Plymouth. The 
second output is the database architecture and development for a material passport 
(MP). An MP will help “track and trace” building blocks throughout their lifecycle, 
facilitating traceability in space, provenance, ownership, transport (e.g. embodied 
carbon metrics), and condition of each block, supporting the reusability of building 
elements over time (Fig. 3.8). By considering “buildings as material banks”, material 
passports have a limited implementation in the UK and recent initiatives are devel-
oping scoping activity in partnership with industry stakeholders [49]. An ongoing

Trust for implementation. 6. User support and expertise to implement MPs. 7. Policy and legal frameworks for MP implementation. 3.3.4 
MMC Adoption in a Rural Housing Programme 
Last, this ongoing project is a pilot internationalisation initiative, in partnership with 
the award-winning Chile’s Rural Housing Programme by the Ministry of Housing 
and Urbanism, the Chile Economic Development Agency, and the local Universidad 
Autonoma de Temuco and their research team expertise in local bio-based materials 
[53–55] and Wikihouse [56]. This project will prototype a small-scale innovation scenario of MMC adoption 
within a local rural ecosystem in southern Chile to help us translate our ﬁndings

52
A. Veliz Reyes et al. into an international policy context. This will include, for instance, the identiﬁ-
cation of needs for inter-stakeholder communication, and a rethinking of the role 
of innovation within industrial partners involved in the delivery of rural housing 
construction (an environment with very limited on-site “quality checks” due to acces-
sibility challenges in Chilean rural settings). Relevantly for our research trajectory, 
the project will take place in Temuco—an area of the country with high deprivation 
rates, environmental issues originated in problematic forestry practices and low-
quality housing, and conﬂict around land and land ownership with local indigenous 
mapuche communities [57]. These frame the environmental and social aspects of 
this project in similar ways our work has done in England, including references 
to sustainable forestry, regenerative landscapes, and social issues underpinning the 
decline of rural communities. As the project develops, we are working directly with local innovation stake-
holders on MMC models being used as probes to further identify challenges, 
knowledge gaps and design opportunities for MMC adoption in the local Temuco 
ecosystem. These are expected to be aligned to the goals of the Rural Housing 
Programme [58] which provides funding for either additional rooms and improve-
ments in existing properties (such as refurbishment for ageing or disabled people, or 
building performance improvements), or the creation of new productive spaces in 
existing constructions (e.g. storage, animal shelter, crafts, cheesemaking spaces, etc.). 3.4 
Conclusion 
This Chapter outlines a research trajectory that explores the intersections between 
bio-based materials and MMCs. The ultimate goal is not necessarily one of perfor-
mance optimisation, but social good—how the material

ﬂows and methods we use 
can contribute to broader societal beneﬁts and address issues of digital inequity 
and marginalisation in different social and geographic contexts. These projects are 
eminently multi-disciplinary and composed of multiple partnership arrangements, 
comprising government bodies, community organisations, technology companies, 
manufacturers, or other academic institutions. Although not the emphasis of this Chapter, we additionally note the wide disci-
plinary basis of this line of research. This helps tackle the disciplinary challenges 
identiﬁed in our theoretical framework, allowing for multiple viewpoints, types of 
knowledge, and their representations, to inﬂuence the elicitation of results through 
both quantitative and qualitative data. This includes, so far, organisations and 
researchers in our network are involved in policy, architecture, computer science, 
construction, product design, community work, digital arts, and heritage studies. By 
doing so, we propose a wider multi-disciplinary approach to the understanding of 
productive and material supply chains for sustainable construction which require 
consideration of economic, social and environmental forces inﬂuencing these new, 
physical and digital geographies of making. Here, the framework set out by Carr, 
Gibson, and other coauthors frames our enquiry by providing a conceptual lens into

3
The Geographies of Sustainable Bio-Based Materials for Modern …

geographies of making. Although their work arguably sits within an economic geog-
raphy remit, we do note the transferability of their ﬁndings into our research—with 
some differences that we have noted in the theoretical framework section. Last, we note that this is a selection of major projects, however our research is 
currently engaged in a series of initiatives that test the growth of MMC opportunities 
in communities suffering from digital inequity, including an ongoing proposal to 
test out MMC adoption by community housing organisations in a urban context, and

Chapter 4 
Are We There Yet? Progress Review 
of Digital Manufacturing for Earth 
Construction 
Mohamed Gomaa 
, Hamidreza Malekian 
, Veronica Soebarto 
, 
and Alejandro Veliz Reyes 
Abstract The construction industry is currently transforming under the dual imper-
atives of digitisation and decarbonisation. Digital Manufacturing of Earth Construc-
tion (DMEC) is emerging as a sustainable alternative to traditional building methods, 
offering solutions to environmental challenges while improving efﬁciency. This 
chapter provides a progress review of DMEC from 2021 to 2024, focusing on 
three key domains: manufacturing technologies, material and performance aspects, 
and research reliability and visibility. Serving as a “Checkpoint 2.0,” this chapter 
builds on previous review by authors, aiming to consolidate critical information to 
support the broader adoption of digital earth construction techniques. The review 
highlights signiﬁcant advancements, such as the development of hybrid systems and 
enhanced earthen mixtures. It also examines the increasing interest from industry 
and government stakeholders in scaling up DMEC for industrial applications. The 
digital earth construction framework has evolved from initial proofs-of-concept to 
full-scale prototypes, marking a signiﬁcant step toward commercial viability. M. Gomaa (B)

M. Gomaa et al. 4.1 
Introduction 
The construction industry is undergoing a profound transformation driven by two 
interrelated and critical trends: digitisation and decarbonisation. Digitisation involves 
the integration of advanced digital technologies, such as Building Information 
Modelling (BIM), 3D printing, robotics, and the Internet of Things (IoT), to enhance 
efﬁciency, accuracy, and productivity throughout construction processes. These tech-
nologies enable better design, real-time data collection, improved project manage-
ment, and more precise construction techniques. On the other hand, decarbonisation 
focuses on reducing the carbon footprint of construction activities, aiming to miti-
gate their environmental impact by adopting low-carbon materials, improving energy 
efﬁciency, and implementing sustainable design practices [1]. Together, these trends are paving the way for a more efﬁcient, sustainable, and 
environmentally responsible construction industry. As the pursuit of fully automated 
construction processes gains momentum, companies and institutions around the 
globe are rapidly advancing towards constructing full-scale structures and houses 
using digital and robotic manufacturing methods. With growing awareness of global 
warming and resource depletion, researchers and stakeholders in the building and 
construction industries are increasingly prioritising the decarbonisation of construc-
tion processes and materials. Given that the construction industry consumes nearly 
50% of the world’s raw materials, it has become imperative to reconsider conven-
tional materials and explore more sustainable alternatives. In this context, earth-based 
materials are strongly re-emerging as viable substitutes for cement-based counter-
parts, offering affordable and eco-friendly solutions for various sectors, including 
residential projects [2, 3]. Over the past decade, research on automating earth construction has made signif-
icant strides. Early prototypes and projects have demonstrated the great potential 
of earth construction as a modern and renewed method, providing numerous envi-
ronmental and economic beneﬁts. However, the transition from prototype stages 
to industrial-scale applications has been slower than anticipated due to challenges 
on multiple fronts. Additionally, despite the growing number of studies, research 
efforts around the world have remained fragmented, with a considerable lack of 
communication and collaboration between teams [4, 5]. The need for a checkpoint to review, evaluate, and reﬂect on the progress in this 
ﬁeld has become apparent. In 2022, Gomaa et al. [6] published a critical review that 
served as the ﬁrst comprehensive assessment of the major advancements in Digital 
Manufacturing of Earth Construction (DMEC), focusing on its potential and

the 
challenges of industrial-scale adoption. This study, referred to as “Checkpoint 1.0,” 
revealed that while the majority of research between 2011 and 2021 focused on 
exploring the geometrical and design aspects of DMEC, there was a lack of rigorous 
information on constructability and performance aspects, such as manufacturing 
systems, material standards, and structural and thermal performance. This deﬁciency 
in replicable data has hindered the construction industry’s adoption of new techniques

4
Are We There Yet? Progress Review of Digital Manufacturing for Earth …
59
and delayed the approval of these methods by regulatory authorities, making the goal 
of industrialising DMEC more challenging to achieve. Despite these challenges, the study reported a noticeable increase in the adoption 
rate of DMEC between 2018 and 2021, forecasting that future efforts would evolve 
to address the current challenges. The analysis of reported projects and research 
identiﬁed three key domains requiring further improvements: manufacturing tech-
nologies, performance aspects, and research quality and visibility. This chapter, 
serving as “Checkpoint 2.0,” aims to provide an updated review of the progress 
in the digital earth construction ﬁeld since 2021, building on the foundation laid by 
the previous study. The chapter explores trending research directions and improve-
ments in the aforementioned domains, ultimately enhancing the future utilisation of 
digital techniques in earth construction. 4.2 
Theoretical Framework 
This chapter serves as an extension to the critical review article by Gomaa et al. [6] in 2022, which established selection and classiﬁcation criteria for studies on 
Digital Manufacturing of Earth Construction (DMEC). Following the same criteria, 
this review is based on literature primarily sourced from ScienceDirect and Google 
Scholar, with priority given to ofﬁcially published journal articles and conference 
proceedings. The inclusion criteria for this review are as follows:
• Published between 2022 and 2024
• Focus on digitally manufactured earth constructions (DMEC)
• Emphasis on work leading to an actual or near-building scale
• Inclusion of only soil/earth-based materials
• Preference for experimental work over theoretical approaches. Search terms included combinations related to digital fabrication and earth 
construction, such as “digital earth construction” and “additive manufacturing 
of earth.” The search yielded 43 relevant works that met the inclusion criteria, 
comprising 12 studies from 2022, 17 studies from 2023, and 14 studies up to August 
2024 (Fig. 4.1). It is important to note that this book is expected to include approxi-
mately ﬁve chapters on digitising earth construction; however, these are not included 
in the 2024 count. Given the ﬁeld’s rapid development and growing academic interest, 
it is anticipated that the number of publications will have increased further by the 
time of this book’s release. The ai

m of this chapter is not to provide an exhaustive review, but rather to 
highlight key developments in the recent literature that align with the forecasted 
target domains identiﬁed in the previous critical review. It will offer an overview 
of the current trends, focusing on how close we are to achieving commercial scale 
adoption. Therefore, this chapter will be structured around the progress made in 
the target domains of improvement, with a particular emphasis on the challenges, 
potential, and future directions. 60

4.3 
Progress Precedents 
Since 2022, the literature has shown signiﬁcant progress across all target domains of 
Digital Manufacturing of Earth Construction (DMEC). There is a growing adoption 
of DMEC, indicating that ongoing efforts are leading to improved solutions for 
commercial applications. This new analysis of recent DMEC studies reveals the 
following progress ratios per each target domain:
• Manufacturing technologies: 35%
• Performance aspects: 50%
• Research quality and visibility: 15%. The ratio of the last domain, research quality, has been assessed based on the 
number of review studies that have systematically collected and evaluated other 
work on DMEC, as well as the number of newly launched initiatives and industry-led 
projects focused on digitising earth construction. From the authors’ perspective, this 
distribution indicates a healthy and balanced approach, suggesting steady progress 
towards the commercialisation of DMEC in the construction industry, potentially 
within the next 3–5 years, depending on the location. This optimism is further rein-
forced by the growing number of newly funded projects and initiatives from industrial 
stakeholders worldwide, with project timelines ranging from 18 months to 5 years. Each of the following three sections is designed to cover one target domain. Each 
section will include a brief overview of the pre-2021 state from the relevant domain 
in the previous review study [6], which serves as “Checkpoint 1.0.”. This will be 
followed by highlights from selected studies conducted after 2021, serving as the 
corresponding “Checkpoint 2.0.” This structure is intended to clearly illustrate the 
progress made in each domain over time. Studies are presented in a chronological 
order. 4
Are We There Yet? Progress Review of Digital Manufacturing for Earth …
61
4.3.1 
Manufacturing Technologies Domain 
The advancement of digital tools and machinery is pivotal for integrating earth mate-
rials into digital construction processes. Digital construction frameworks for earth 
materials primarily rely on manufacturing systems that combine the moving device 
(e.g., robotic arm) and the material depositi

on device (e.g., extruder). Up until 2021, 
most research in DMEC concentrated on additive manufacturing (AM), with other 
techniques like subtractive, formative, or hybrid methods remaining relatively unex-
plored. The majority of studies focused on wet earth methods (e.g., cob) due to their 
favourable compatibility with 3D printing processes, while rammed earth and other 
earthen techniques remained underexplored. Although notable advancements were 
made in 3D printing technologies for earth, such as those developed by producers 
like WASP and 3D Potter, challenges persisted, particularly in integrating ﬁbrous 
earth mixtures into feeding systems. Similarly, free-formed rammed earth compo-
nents continued to heavily rely on manual processes, thus not qualifying as fully 
automated. Since 2022, there have been signiﬁcant advancements in manufacturing systems 
for both wet and dry-based earth construction. New hybrid systems have been devel-
oped to deliver stronger earthen elements, and more studies have been conducted 
on automating rammed earth, wattle and daub, and sand. In addition, innovative 
approaches for implementing reinforcement and reusable light formwork have been 
introduced. For example, two studies in 2023 and 2024 [7, 8] explored the use of 
willow, a fast-renewable and locally abundant material, in novel circular material 
systems for construction. They developed a custom machine that expertly weaves 
willow ﬁbres into a continuous macro-ﬁbre, which is then threaded through a bed 
of wooden poles to create cellular voids ﬁlled with earth via pneumatic extrusion. This innovative integration of material, structure, and form sets the stage for a new 
era of sustainable construction. The “InterTwig” structure, a 4-m-tall freestanding 
demonstrator built using this system, showcases the potential of combining willow 
and earth in a seamless, eco-friendly construction process (Fig. 4.2).

Similarly, Jauk et al. [9] explored the enhancement of 3D-printed clay objects 
through ﬁlament reinforcement, which signiﬁcantly improves mechanical properties. The study involved the continuous reinforcement of clay during the printing process, 
resulting in stronger and more versatile structures. This approach offers a viable
Fig. 4.2 Left: full-scale demonstrator, right: computational structural analysis model [7]

62
M. Gomaa et al. Fig. 4.3 Additive manufacturing of ceramics using reinforced ﬁlaments [9] 
solution for creating robust architectural and construction applications, with future 
work focusing on optimising reinforcement materials and techniques for large-scale 
implementations (Fig. 4.3). On the other hand, Zhu et al. [10] developed an automated robotic system for 
constructing rammed earth walls with precision and customisable designs. Their 
methodology integrates digital design with robotic fabrication, using a parametric 
model to generate graphic patterns based on landscape painting concepts. These 
patterns are then converted into execution codes for a KUKA robotic arm, which 
extrudes earthen material in speciﬁc colour proportions. The study highlights the 
potential of digital fabrication in bridging the gap between design and construction, 
reducing the loss of design intent during the translation to physical structures. Further 
research is needed to evaluate the system’s performance in large-scale projects and 
its impact on the structural properties of the constructed walls (Fig. 4.4). Abdelaal et al. [11] presented another innovative approach that combines modular 
design with digital fabrication to enhance rammed earth construction. By using 
modular rammed earth (RE) tiles with intricate curvatures, their method allows for 
mass customisation and production, facilitating the creation of continuous free-form 
RE walls. This modular system improves efﬁciency, quality control, and reduces 
construction waste, making it a sustainable alternative to traditional rammed earth 
methods. The ﬂexibility of the tiles allows architects to create diverse and visually 
appealing structures through simple adjustments like rotation and ﬂipping. The study 
underscores the potential of modular, prefabricated RE wall sections as a scalable, 
eco-friendly solution for modern construction practices (Fig. 4.5).

Terrene 2.0, a study by Lasting et al. [12], introduces ChitoSand, a novel composite 
made from sand, chitosan biopolymer, ﬂax ﬁbres, citric acid, and burlap fabric. This combination enhances the material’s compressive and tensile properties while 
remaining biodegradable. The research employs reusable pneumatic formwork to 
support the ChitoSand composite during hardening, allowing for the creation of 
complex, optimised geometries using anticlastic shellular structures. ChitoSand 
exhibits strong mechanical properties, with improved ﬂexural strength, making it 
a viable alternative to traditional construction materials. Using polyhedral graphic 
statics, the researchers efﬁciently designed complex geometries, ensuring precise 
control over force distribution. The use of reusable pneumatic formwork further 
reduced waste and guaranteed accurate construction, with the structures maintaining 
stability over time. ChitoSand and the associated construction methods offer a 
sustainable alternative to conventional practices. This research provides a compre-
hensive roadmap for advancing sustainable construction, highlighting its potential for 
broader application. The interdisciplinary collaboration in this study underscores the 
importance of combining expertise in materials science, engineering, and architecture 
to achieve sustainable development goals Fig. 4.6.

64
M. Gomaa et al. In conclusion, the advancements in manufacturing technologies for DMEC since 
2022 have signiﬁcantly contributed to the ﬁeld’s progress. These innovations, ranging 
from hybrid systems and robotic fabrication to ﬁlament reinforcement, modular 
design, and sustainable composites, demonstrate the potential for integrating earth 
materials into modern construction processes on a commercial scale. The continued 
exploration and development of these technologies will be crucial in overcoming 
current challenges and realising the full potential of DMEC in the construction 
industry. 4.3.2 
Material and Performance Domain. Material and building performance analysis provides stakeholders with crucial infor-
mation to enhance building safety and efﬁciency for both occupants and the environ-
ment. Architectural performance encompasses a wide range of technical domains, 
including structural and thermal performance [13]. Consequently, numerous studies 
on digital earth construction have assessed mechanical, structural, thermal, and envi-
ronmental properties. Up until 2021, research into the mechanical, structural, and 
environmental properties of Digital Manufacturing of Earth Construction (DMEC) 
highlighted its potential for low-rise buildings, showcasing higher material efﬁ-
ciency and design ﬂexibility [14–17]. Large-scale prototypes of earth construc-
tions suggested that digitally manufactured earth walls could serve as load-bearing 
elements, with examples including the integration of timber systems into earth walls 
to support ﬂoors and staircases [18, 19]. However, full-scale structural testing remains 
essential to standardise these constructions and further exploration of earthen mixture 
properties is necessary. Despite the promise shown by these early studies, many relied on small-scale 
prototypes or simulations, underscoring the need for more comprehensive investiga-
tions. In particular, research has called for a deeper understanding of the optimal water 
content in earth mixtures for 3D printing, a key design parameter that can mitigate 
issues like shrinkage and cracking during hardening. Since 2022, substantial research 
has shifted towards developing revised earth mixtures that can withstand industrial 
applications, utilising rigorous and near full-scale testing conditions. These studies 
have addressed a broad spectrum of performance-related aspects, such as mechanical 
strength, thermal performance, rheology, workability, and printability. A notable contribution to this body of research is the work by Ferretti et al. [20], 
which advanced the understanding of 3D-printed earthen buildings’ material perfor-
mance. Their study examined the mechanical properties of 3D-printed wall segments 
made from an earthen mixture, focusing

on load-bearing capacities (Fig. 4.7). By 
combining soil with a rice husk-lime bio-composite, the study leveraged the silica 
content of rice husk and the bio-cementation effects of lime, resulting in compressive 
strength comparable to traditional rammed earth structures. The study also high-
lighted that good cohesion between 3D-printed layers contributed to mechanical

4
Are We There Yet? Progress Review of Digital Manufacturing for Earth …
65
Fig. 4.7 Tested 3d printed wall specimen for compressive strength [20] 
strength similar to that of hand-compacted specimens, while the inﬁll within these 
walls effectively prevented crack propagation, enhancing stability. In a similar vein, Silva et al. [21] explored the use of potato starch gel and sisal 
ﬁbres to stabilise earthen-based matrices for 3D printing. Their research aimed 
to create alternative mixtures from soil and organic materials that would improve 
printability and structural properties. Through extensive testing, including pumpa-
bility, extrudability, and compressive strength assessments, the study found that these 
natural additives signiﬁcantly enhanced the workability and mechanical strength of 
the mixtures. The ﬁndings suggest that these eco-friendly materials could offer a 
sustainable alternative to traditional construction methods. Building on that, Zavaleta 
et al. [22] investigated the use of chitosan and sisal ﬁbres to enhance the properties 
of 3D-printed earthen composites. Their research addressed the limitations of tradi-
tional earthen materials, such as poor mechanical strength and low water durability. The study’s ﬁndings revealed signiﬁcant improvements in extrusion quality and resis-
tance to water erosion, highlighting the potential of natural additives in advancing 
sustainable construction practices. Alqenaee and Memari [23] focused on conducting further veriﬁcations on 
adapting traditional cob construction techniques for modern 3D printing. Their 
research involved modifying cob mixture proportions to balance printability, build-
ability, and mechanical properties. The study provided a recommended ratio for a 
printable cob mixture and explored various printing techniques to optimise struc-
tural integrity. While the research highlights the potential of 3D-printed cob for 
small-scale construction, it also identiﬁes challenges such as shrinkage and deforma-
tion that warrant further investigation. Further expanding the exploration of earthen 
mixtures, Asaf et al. [24] developed and tested twelve sand and clay mixtures to 
assess their suitability for 3D printing. Their ﬁndings emphasised the importance 
of rheological properties, particularly the thixotropic effect, in maintaining stability 
after printing. This study underscores the critical role of particle size distribution and 
clay mineralogy in optimising the performance of 3D-printed mixtures, providing 
valuable guidelines for future construction practices. 66
M.

Gomaa et al. Rückrich et al. [25] proposed a ﬁeld-oriented methodology to ensure consistent 
and high-quality printing results. They developed three characterisation tests to assess 
the ﬂowability, cohesiveness, and deformation properties of earth mixtures. The 
research identiﬁed two potential indices, ﬂowability and green strength, as standards 
for evaluating the printability of local earth-based mixtures. These indices could 
facilitate the standardisation of industrially pre-processed materials, though further 
reﬁnement is necessary. The study by Maierdan et al. [26] took a different approach by focusing on the 
potential of xanthan gum (XG) as a biopolymer binder to improve the rheological, 
mechanical, and 3D printing properties of earth-based concrete. The research demon-
strates that XG can effectively mitigate viscosity, yield stress, and storage modulus 
over time to enhances the material’s buildability for 3D printing. The study also 
reveals that XG signiﬁcantly improves compressive strength. Despite not forming 
strong bonds with clay in the fresh state, XG may create a robust polymer matrix 
during the dehydration process, reinforcing the clay and sand particles and improving 
compressive strength. Ultimately, the study concludes that XG is a promising dual-
function agent, acting as both a binder and a rheology modiﬁer, making it suitable 
for sustainable 3DP construction applications. The methods for maximizing the ﬁbre Content in 3D-printed earth mixtures were 
also explored in a study by Carcassi et al. [27]. The research develops various earth-
ﬁbre composites using bio-based additives and agrowaste ﬁbres such as wheat straw, 
hemp, banana, and kenaf. These ﬁbres were incorporated into the mix designs at 
varying content levels, with the study achieving signiﬁcant increases in ﬁbre content 
up to 13% by weight in some cases. The key ﬁndings indicate that higher ﬁbre content 
signiﬁcantly improves the compressive strength of the materials, with an increase 
of up to 125% compared to non-ﬁbre-reinforced mixtures. Additionally, the thermal 
conductivity of the ﬁbre-reinforced mixtures was notably lower, improving the mate-
rial’s insulating properties. The study concludes that optimizing ﬁbre content in 3D-
printed earth materials can lead to more sustainable, high-performance construction 
materials, though challenges remain in balancing printability with mechanical and 
thermal performance. Lastly, Schmitz et al. [28] conducted a comprehensive study to understand the 
interrelationship between process and material parameters in rammed earth construc-
tion, with the goal of establishing a robust process for automated robotic rammed 
earth. Their research provided key recommendations for optimising compaction 
processes, which could inform the further development of robotic systems for 
rammed earth construction. Overall, these recent studies have made signiﬁcant 
advancements in the ﬁeld of 3D-printed earth construction by imp

roving material 
properties, enhancing structural integrity, and exploring innovative design strate-
gies. Research has demonstrated that integrating natural additives such as rice husk-
lime bio-composites, potato starch gel, and sisal ﬁbres can signiﬁcantly enhance the 
compressive strength, workability, and durability of earthen mixtures. Additionally, 
studies have identiﬁed optimal ratios and methods for 3D printing materials like 
cob and clay, while also developing new standards for evaluating printability and 
structural performance. Collectively, these efforts have brought 3D-printed earth

4
Are We There Yet? Progress Review of Digital Manufacturing for Earth …
67
construction closer to mainstream application, offering sustainable alternatives to 
traditional building methods and paving the way for future innovations in the ﬁeld. 4.3.3 
Research Reliability and Visibility Domain 
The adoption of Digital Manufacturing of Earth Construction (DMEC) has seen a 
noticeable increase in recent years. However, up until 2021, these efforts were still 
in their infancy compared to other well-established digital construction methods like 
3D concrete printing (3DCP). While many early experiments in DMEC claimed 
improvements in productivity, quality, and performance (thermal, structural, and 
environmental), they often lacked systematic assessment and veriﬁcation. Most 
recorded studies were student projects, proofs of concept, or commercial proto-
types, with few being published in peer-reviewed journals. This lack of deﬁnitive 
information raised concerns about the validity of the results and hindered industrial 
adoption and research replication. Additionally, research in DMEC was fragmented, 
with many groups working in isolation, leading to redundancy and slow collective 
progress. Since 2022, however, signiﬁcant efforts have been made to address these issues. As seen in the previous two domains, numerous studies have emerged to explore, 
verify, and validate the performance aspects of earthen mixtures, while also intro-
ducing improved production techniques. Additionally, several review studies have 
collectively analysed the potential and challenges of adopting DMEC on a larger 
scale [2, 29–32]. These efforts have not gone unnoticed by industry and govern-
ments. Globally, several ﬁrms and organisations are now directing their resources 
and attention toward scaling digital earth construction for industrial applications. Companies like WASP 3D in Italy and 3D Potter in the US have long been leaders in 
promoting 3D-printed earth construction. Newer entrants like Luyten 3D in Australia 
are also making signiﬁcant strides, particularly in sustainable construction. In 2023, 
Luyten 3D secured a $2.9 million AUD grant from the Australian Government’s 
CRC-P (Cooperative Research Centres Projects [33]) to develop sustainable 3D-
printed houses using local materials. Through such initiatives, Luyten 3D is helping 
to revolutionise the constructi

on industry, promoting rapid, sustainable, and scalable 
building solutions. Building on this momentum, Swinburne University of Technology 
and Luyten 3D have recently secured over A$600,000 in competitive funding from 
the Australian Research Council (ARC) Linkage Program and the Innovative Planet 
Research Institute (IPRI) in late 2024 [34]. These grants aim to comprehensively 
explore advanced engineering methodologies to enhance the industrial readiness of 
3D-printed earth construction. This reﬂects a growing institutional and governmental 
commitment to transitioning digital earth construction from experimental prototypes 
to viable industrial solutions. In the UK, the Earth Build Project (EBP) was established in 2023 to harness 3D-
printing technology for scalable, decarbonised construction across Europe [35]. EBP 
is currently focused on adapting 3D-printing techniques to suit the UK climate and

68
M. Gomaa et al. ensuring compliance with building regulations. The project has built a robust research 
network, secured Network Grant funding from the EPSRC, and formalised partner-
ships with companies like WASP and institutions such as IAAC. By bringing together 
top researchers in digital earth construction from the UK, Europe, and Australia, 
the Earth Build Project represents a signiﬁcant step forward in commercialising 
sustainable 3D-printed construction. The Earth Building Association of Australia 
(EBAA), an NGO dedicated to promoting earth construction techniques since 1981, 
has also shown a strong interest in modernising earth construction through digital 
techniques [36]. Since 2022, EBAA has sponsored workshops on 3D printing of 
cob and supported the inclusion of digital earth construction researchers in their 
annual conferences. The organisation advocates for the recognition and integration 
of earth building techniques within the broader construction industry and regulatory 
frameworks in Australia. Globally, initiatives like Mud Futures, launched in 2021 by Sushant University in 
India, have further highlighted the growing interest in earth-based construction. As 
part of the International Prize for Contemporary Architecture (IPAC) 2021–2022, 
Mud Futures explored the potential of mud as a key material in sustainable architec-
ture [37]. The year-long global symposium brought together architects, researchers, 
and practitioners to discuss how traditional mud-building techniques can be reimag-
ined and integrated with contemporary design and construction practices. Through 
workshops, lectures, and discussions, Mud Futures has inspired innovation in earth-
based construction and continues to promote the importance of sustainable building 
practices. 4.4 
Conclusion 
Over the past decade, the development of the digital earth construction framework 
has evolved from initial proof-of-concept stages to full-scale physical prototyping, 
demonstrating the signiﬁcant potential of earth-based construction. This progression 
has expa

nded the scope of digital manufacturing in construction beyond environ-
mentally harmful cement-based materials, underscoring the ecological advantages 
of earth-based alternatives. Collaborative efforts have laid a robust foundation for 
advancing digital earth construction toward industrial scalability, bridging the gap 
between traditional earth construction methods and modern digital practices. This chapter has provided a comprehensive review of the current state of digital 
manufacturing in earth construction, highlighting both the achievements and the path-
ways for further improvement. By examining diverse research threads, the chapter 
aimed to build knowledge bridges that could accelerate the adoption of digital earth 
construction techniques in modern architecture. Despite the promising advance-
ments, it is evident that additive manufacturing alone will not sufﬁce for the broader 
application of earth-based construction methods. There is a critical need to move 
beyond 3D printing prototypes designed solely for research purposes. Approaches 
such as dynamic molds, hybrid construction methods, and the industrialisation of

4
Are We There Yet? Progress Review of Digital Manufacturing for Earth …
69
machinery and processes from a market perspective are necessary to achieve practical 
implementation on a larger scale. The ﬁeld of digital manufacturing in earth construction is transitioning from the 
early to intermediate stages, marked by accumulating performance data and growing 
involvement from research teams, companies, and funding bodies. This increasing 
interest and investment are crucial for pushing the boundaries of what is possible 
in earth-based construction, paving the way for innovative, sustainable building 
practices that can be widely adopted. References

Chapter 5 
Earth-Based Materials in Contemporary 
Construction: Challenges 
and Opportunities 
Giada Giuffrida 
and Rosa Caponetto 
Abstract The growing concern regarding the worsening of environmental condi-
tions and the energy crisis has led, in recent decades, to a strong push to rethink and 
renew the construction and production sector. This need has brought the advent of 
design approaches for sustainability that are based on the circular economy paradigm, 
the reduction of resources and energy consumptions, and the minimization of climate-
changing emissions. Following these premises, some bio and geo-based materials 
such as raw earth, have received great attention by virtue of their availability, low 
economic and environmental cost, and good material performances. This contribution 
deals with framing contemporary earthen construction within the broader challenges 
of the transition path to a more sustainable built environment. Furthermore, the main 
limitations that earth construction must face today to establish itself as a valid alter-
native to conventional construction are exposed and commented on. Finally, the main 
challenges and opportunities for raw earth materials are outlined by analyzing the 
last advances in research and industrial production. 5.1 Introduction 
Building with raw earth represents an effective and sustainable solution to many of the 
current challenges related to the natural and built environments, as well as society. By 
combining tradition and innovation, this material offers signiﬁcant beneﬁts in terms 
of environmental sustainability, energy efﬁciency, health and well-being; at the same 
time, using raw earth as a building material promotes social inclusion, economic 
accessibility and cultural heritage preservation, contributing to a greener, fairer, and 
more sustainable future [1]. In light of the above, raw earth construction fully addresses the goals of recent 
international policies aimed at creating a more sustainable and healthy future for 
forthcoming generations: from the 2015 Paris Agreement to the 2030 Agenda for

in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_5 
73

74
G. Giuffrida and R. Caponetto
Sustainable Development, particularly addressing the following SDGs: SDG 3 (Good 
Health and Well-Being), SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), SDG 11 (Sustainable 
Cities and Communities), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), and 
SDG 13 (Climate Action). But how exactly does raw earth construction meet these previously mentioned 
needs? Its environmental sustainability primarily derives from its easy avail-
ability, recyclability, low-carbon processing methods, and reduced waste produc-
tion compared to other construction technologies [2]. As a renewable and abun-
dant material, raw earth alleviates the pressure on non-renewable resources and 
offers a low-carbon alternative to traditional materials like concrete and steel. Its 
hygrothermal properties help maintain stable indoor temperatures, reducing the need 
for artiﬁcial heating and cooling, and thus energy consumption [3]. Additionally, raw 
earth construction processes are less energy-intensive, contributing to a lower overall 
carbon footprint of buildings [2]. From a biodiversity perspective, raw earth construction helps mitigate biodiversity 
loss by limiting the extraction and transportation of materials over long distances and 
promoting sustainable building practices that preserve natural habitats. The quality of raw earth-built living spaces is high due to the absence of toxic 
substances, the material’s ability to regulate temperature and humidity, provide 
acoustic insulation, and create a visually pleasing and healthy environment, thereby 
promoting the psychological well-being of occupants through its naturalness and 
sustainability [4]. Socially, raw earth construction enhances economic accessibility to housing due 
to the local availability of the material, reduced transportation costs, and the potential 
for self-building. This technology also stimulates community involvement by encour-
aging active participation in self-construction, creating opportunities for learning and 
collaboration. This strengthens cultural identity by valuing traditional techniques 
(and thus preserving cultural heritage) and promoting support for the local economy 
[5]. In recent years, raw earth construction has made signiﬁcant progresses: the use 
of natural stabilizers and modern additives has improved its mechanical properties 
and durability. Additionally, the development of hybrid systems combining raw earth 
with wood, bamboo, and metal, along with the introduction of new technologies and 
machineries, has enhanced its performance, making it more suitable for contemporary 
housing needs. Technological experimentation and innovation have thus modernized 
raw earth architecture, resulting in aesthetically appealing buildings that integrate 
harmoniously with the natural environment and enhance local

resources, respecting 
the principles of ecophilia [5]. Figure 5.1 reports a state of the art of raw earth construction, with reference to its 
background, its advantages, the main construction technologies, the current limits, 
and the main challenges. These aspects will be further discussed in the following 
paragraphs. 5
