76
G. Giuffrida and R. Caponetto
5.2 Limits 
Despite the progressive increase of scientiﬁc literature and the excellent examples of 
earth-based architectures designed in recent years, raw earth struggles to establish 
itself as a material for widespread construction in industrialized countries. A number 
of recent studies have identiﬁed the main obstacles to using raw earth through liter-
ature surveys and interviews/questionnaires [6, 7]. The main limitations concern 
normative, technical, management and training aspects, but also socio-cultural and 
economic ones. In the following paragraphs, each of these issues will be scrutinized 
and analyzed, while in the next section, the latest developments and research/practice 
horizons that could help in solving the previous limitations will be exposed. 5.2.1 
Normative and Political Limits 
Lack of shared technical standards. The development and diffusion of raw earth 
construction are hindered by the lack of widely accepted guidelines and standards. Indeed, only a few nations are endowed with proper standards or normative docu-
ments for raw earth construction, and even fewer cover the wide variety of avail-
able raw earth technologies or ﬁelds of application. In an interesting contribution 
by Achenza and Agus [8], the name of the standard, the standardization body, the 
Nations, and the main contents of several raw earth standards are listed. The analyzed 
standards refer to those of Brazil, Colombia, France, Germany, India, Kenya, New 
Zealand, New Mexico, Peru, Spain, Sri Lanka, Tunisia, USA, and Zimbabwe. Other 
drafts of standards and guidelines have been produced in the meantime (e.g. the 
Californian standard for Cob construction, or the new version of the German Stan-
dards for earth blocks, masonries, mortars, panels and plasters). Reference is made 
to the main analyzed technologies and to the guidelines on soil selection proce-
dures, product expected performances, laboratory tests, design, manufacturing and 
construction aspects. Concerning the analyzed technologies, it is interesting to point 
out that compressed earth blocks (CEB) are the subject of most of the standards, 
followed by adobe, rammed earth, and, more recently, earth concrete. Nonetheless, 
the existing body of standards is limited in terms of scope, contents, and covered 
technologies. Existing standards therefore risk having a limited impact both in terms 
of scope and status of regulatory text. On the basis of the abovementioned review [8], 
the authors report an analysis of the historical and political dynamics that led Italy not 
to have a raw earth standard and consequently propose a structure for furthe

r inves-
tigations aimed at drafting a new regulation for raw earth construction (in particular 
adobe) in Italy. Lack of policies encouraging raw earth construction. Side by side to the lack of 
shared technical standards, it emerges the lack of national and international policies 
encouraging the diffusion and production of raw earth buildings and components. As 
we will see in the following pages, there are a few isolated cases where individual

5
Earth-Based Materials in Contemporary Construction: Challenges …
77
initiative has led to the creation of companies that base their assets on the production 
of raw earth building components. Several companies working on raw earth materials 
and products are present in France, Spain, Austria, Canada, Germany, Italy, United 
States, Australia, and New Zealand. Only in rare cases these enterprises have been assisted in their growth by local 
municipalities, while the launch of their activities required the cooperation of various 
partners and the use of mechanisms of national and international fundings (as UE 
ones). For instance, for the French company Cycle Terre, a cooperation of several 
parties aimed at the creation of a waste recovery chain resulting from the soils 
excavated for the construction of the new Grand Paris transport network [9]. The 
cooperative includes the Grand Paris Society, in charge of the construction of the 
transport network in Île-de-France region, but also a local municipality –in which 
two new Grand Paris stations are being built-, several research centers, a real estate 
developer, an architectural ﬁrm and a training center [9]. Rather, in most cases, it appears evident that these commercial realities have 
developed where priorly existed a raw earth technical knowledge, usually due to a 
well-spread existing earthen heritage (e.g. in France and Spain) [10], or to a relatively 
renewed earth building practice, as the one stimulated in Germany in between the two 
World Wars [11]. This is for instance the case of several French, Spanish, German 
and Austrian companies. Another major limitation of current earthen construction is the difﬁculty of inter-
twining with other supply chains in a circular economy perspective. In recent years, 
a copious scientiﬁc literature has developed on the possibilities offered by the 
stabilization of earth-based materials through the use of natural materials [12, 13], 
deriving from by-products and processing waste from agriculture [14], or from other 
production chains [15]. Despite the good technical performance of such alternative 
stabilization strategies [16], the methods and ﬂows through which the integration 
between various production chains could be achieved remain to be studied. In one 
of the authors’ latest contributions [17], the environmental convenience of stabi-
lizing rammed earth mixtures by using natural sisal ﬁbers and marble-cutting waste 
is exposed. The technical and environmental performances of these mixtures appea

r 
to be particularly competitive in relation to more conventionally stabilized mixtures 
(through the addition of sand and lime) [17]. At the same time, the authors high-
light the difﬁculties in terms of production chain, mainly due to the need to acti-
vate collaborative chains for the recovery of by-products and waste materials which 
could be repurposed in new earth-based materials, while distributing waste manage-
ment centers throughout the territories to minimize transport-related environmental 
impacts. 5.2.2 
Technical Limits 
In 2022, an interesting contribution by Fabbri et al. [18] describes the remaining 
challenges of the technical committee Rilem 274-TCE, by highlighting the main

78
G. Giuffrida and R. Caponetto
points that should be addressed in the future to explain the technical behavior of raw 
earth materials and improve it. In recent years there has been an exponential increase 
in scientiﬁc publications on the technical performances of earth-based materials [7], 
nevertheless a review of such contents is outside the scope of this brief contribution. However, it can be said that the technical limitations relate to some issues in particular, 
which are examined in the following. Soil selection and characterization for raw earth construction. Professionals and 
practitioners would like to follow easy-to-apply rules and methodologies to assess 
if a soil is suitable or not for earth construction. This approach has rarely been 
investigated in the literature (see for instance [19]). Latest researches report that 
particle size distribution is not a key parameter for predicting the strength of raw 
earth materials, while the amount and type of present clays is a factor of major 
interest [18]. Hygrothermal properties and methodologies for their assessment. Hygrothermal 
properties of earth-based materials have a direct inﬂuence on the energy performance 
of buildings and the comfort of inhabitants [20]. That is the reason why several contri-
butions have focused on the assessment of the parameters governing heat transport 
and storage, moisture storage, vapor and liquid transport (see [18] for details) in raw 
earth materials and components. As reported by Fabbri et al. [18], even if earth-based 
materials are intrinsically variable, the main issue is given by the lack of standard-
ized methodologies for the assessment of these parameters, which causes a dramatic 
dispersion of their assessed performances. Mechanical properties and structural behavior. Similarly to the previous point, 
there are no shared methodologies for the assessment of static and dynamic mechan-
ical properties of earth-based materials, and consequently, the structural behavior 
of earth buildings, especially in seismic-prone zones, needs to be further studied to 
avoid any risks related to the safety of earthen structures. In recent years, also thanks 
to the issuing of some standards for raw earth construction in seismic areas [21], 
there has been an inc

reasing awareness of the need for structural reinforcements 
for raw earth buildings. On the other hand, if it is true that raw earth is a material 
with a low tensile strength—even lower than the one of concrete-, it seems obvious 
that it should be reinforced to increase its structural performance. The experimental 
evidence (discussed by the authors in another contribution [22]) has demonstrated 
that some structural reinforcement strategies (such as those based on the use of timber 
reinforcements) are more suitable to be combined with raw earth massive walls. On 
the other hand, lightweight earth structures (such as wattle and daub ones) appear to 
be more suitable for earthen construction in seismic areas [11]. Reliability. Closely related to the previous limitation is the reliability one. Unlikely 
other construction materials, raw earth quality control during the construction process 
is usually problematic [23, 24]. There is a need to develop easy-to-apply methodolo-
gies that establish uniﬁed criteria for production, for the monitoring of in-situ drying, 
shrinkage-related issues, and quality control over time [18, 25]. 5
Earth-Based Materials in Contemporary Construction: Challenges …
79
Durability. A common criticism raised against earthen construction is its low dura-
bility over time. In 2020, Beckett et al. [26] carried out a review on the types of 
exposure and risks to which earthen buildings are subject and reported a series of 
durability tests on earth-based materials. These tests seek to assess the material’s 
resistance to direct, indirect or dripping rain, pooling water, wetting and drying 
cycles, and freeze–thaw cycles. These tests, although standardized (see for instance 
the capillary water absorption test, the accelerated erosion test, the Geelong test or 
the wire brush test) have been relatively little performed in the literature and rarely 
performed on full-scale raw earth elements. Furthermore, they are difﬁcult to carry 
out on unstabilized raw earth samples (i.e. not stabilized with chemical binders). Health and hygiene quality and control over raw materials. It is necessary to 
verify if the soil extraction site has hosted polluting activities. If the soil is to be 
mixed with others extracted elsewhere, this certainly becomes more complex. In 
this sense, some companies working with raw earth have a very stringent anal-
ysis protocol, which aims to identify any polluting agents present in the masses 
of soils arriving at the plant, for each load of material received. Some authors [6] 
have stated that it would be more appropriate to work with materials excavated on the 
construction site to more easily reconstruct their history and lower the environmental 
impacts related to transport. However, this is not always possible, due to the possible 
non-adaptability to construction purposes of the soil present on site or its insufﬁ-
cient quantity. The claimed healthiness and eco-compatibility of raw earth materials

could be a way to impose themself on more conventional building materials, but this 
requires further tests that investigate the relationship between the raw earth elements 
and the absorption capacity of bacteria or fungi [27], aldehydes, volatile organic 
compounds (VOCs) and ﬁne dusts. Furthermore, the healthiness assessment of raw 
materials (excavated soil) and secondary raw materials (which, as we already said, 
can be used for the stabilization of earth-based materials) should always be veriﬁed 
in the pre-production phase. 5.2.3 
Management and Training Limits 
Raw earth construction still has to overcome a series of management limits, which 
postulate the improvement of planning, design, construction, use and end-of-life 
phases of earth buildings. Production and integration with other supply chains. Concerning soil stabiliza-
tion for building purposes, we already discussed the potential of using waste, by-
products and secondary raw materials (obtained from other production chains). In 
this sense, it is necessary to identify clear paths for the reuse procedures of such 
materials, encouraging industrial symbiosis. 80
G. Giuffrida and R. Caponetto
Construction process. Raw earth construction processes are often too artisanal and 
unsystematized. As already pointed out in the past [6], there is a problem of coopera-
tion between parties, of exchange and reliability of information, of accountability, and 
eventually of safety. The issue of the exchange of information and communication 
concerns the various stakeholders. Services maintenance over time. Unlike other construction systems, the study of 
electrical and plumbing systems is, for raw earth construction, fundamental and must 
be carried out in a fully integrated manner at the earliest design phase. As known, the 
damaging of a water pipe can dramatically lower the mechanical resistance of earth 
masonry. Furthermore, as Morel et al. [6] point out, sometimes fewer ﬁnishing works 
are used in earthen buildings, leaving the structural walls exposed. This necessarily 
postulates the need for greater attention during the design and implementation phases. Ordinary maintenance by end-users. Closely linked to the previous point, the end-
users must be made aware of the methods of use and maintenance of raw earth walls 
and exposed raw earth facades. Lack of technical culture. Raw earth construction requires education, training, and 
speciﬁc skills for all professionals in the sector. Furthermore, to avoid the onset of 
disputes, there is a need to agree on the responsibilities and contract the mutual 
obligations of each interested party. Poor training and lack of technical culture of 
raw earth construction can lead to errors in the design and execution phases, with 
dangerous effects on the quality of earthen building components [6]. 5.2.4 
Socio-Cultural and Economic Limits 
Costs. The nascent raw earth construction market is still a niche, and although there 
are manufacturers of r

aw earth materials and products (especially premixed products 
for earth mortars and renders, but also compressed earth blocks and extruded earth 
panels), it is complex to quantify the costs of a raw earth project. If this complexity 
is, in a certain sense, limited for construction with prefabricated raw earth compo-
nents, this is even greater for on-site construction, where there is a real difﬁculty in 
quantifying the materials, rentals, and labor required. Users’ lack of conﬁdence (cultural prejudice). Alongside the other issues, there is 
the barrier represented by the social perception of raw earth construction, which is 
perhaps the most complex to overcome. There is a lack of awareness of the beneﬁts of 
earth-based technologies, not only by potential users but also by technical designers. Raw earth construction is often perceived as more expensive and time-consuming 
(also due to the lack of automated technologies), and this is particularly true in 
countries like Italy where raw earth technical culture is not widespread. The lack of 
technical information also leads to prejudices in potential customers. 5
Earth-Based Materials in Contemporary Construction: Challenges …
81
5.3 Turning Challenges into Opportunities 
5.3.1 
Transversal Standards and Wide-Ranging Policies 
About the lack of shared standards and normative documents, there is a need to 
create a common technical front aiming at underlining the similarities, rather than 
the differences, between existing raw earth standards, focusing on the normalization 
of raw earth construction (concerning its most widespread techniques). This regu-
latory advancement can only be produced by technicians specialized in raw earth 
construction who, gathered in speciﬁc technical committees, can lay the founda-
tions for the deﬁnition of shared guidelines and standards. These documents should 
relate to the characterization procedures for soils to be used in earth construction, the 
methodologies for the assessment of the expected performances of earth products 
(both in the laboratory and on site), the production and construction procedures, and 
the correct structural and hygrothermal design principles. To facilitate the develop-
ment of appropriate standards, from 2022 three RILEM Technical Committees are 
working on several aspects related to raw earth construction: the TCs PEM, MAE 
and BEC [28]. About political issues, incentive measures must be established to encourage local 
administrations to develop their territory according to the principles of ecological 
transition, encouraging the use of circular and sustainable materials. In particular, 
concerning the extracted soils and secondary raw materials (obtained from other 
production chains) to be used for raw earth construction, it is necessary to identify 
clear and unambiguous political paths that aim to simplify the procedures of adop-
tion, and possibly certiﬁcation, of such materials [6]. Furthermore, these procedures 
should r

eward the ecological choice of using natural materials with low environ-
mental impact, such as raw earth or other recycled materials, through the application 
of tax relief for all the stakeholders involved in the production and building processes. 5.3.2 
Technical Research Converges Around a Few Key 
Points 
Current scientiﬁc literature focuses on the innovation of raw earth materials and the 
improvement of their performances [13, 14], on the reﬁnement of existing construc-
tion technologies and the implementation of new ones [29], also thanks to the use of 
digital manufacturing methods [30, 31]. It is right to remember here that the technical 
solutions proposed by traditional raw earth construction can act as a “living database” 
to carry forward innovation of Architecture, Engineering and Construction sector. It 
also emerges the need to adopt a performance-based approach to raw earth materials 
and building components, which must fulﬁll speciﬁc performance targets conﬁrmed 
by clear checks [32]. 82
G. Giuffrida and R. Caponetto
As reported in previous contributions [18, 28], the new global standards on raw 
earth construction should address several items regarding:
• Material selection, analysis, and design (mainly composition and grading);
• Production and manufacturing including molds, machinery to be used, and 
reference construction techniques;
• Testing methodologies for performance assessments and quality control;
• Structural design, including aspects related to seismic-resistant design;
• Thermal design, including indication about thermal, hygrometric and moisture 
buffering performances;
• Lifetime behavior, durability, and maintenance guidelines. For many of these aspects, scientiﬁc literature and professional practice have 
already given numerous and valid answers. In many cases, these responses are based 
on the material resources and characteristics of the places, conﬁrming the validity of 
a local approach to contemporary raw earth construction, often linked to the speciﬁc 
geographical characteristics and risks connected. It is outside the scope of this chapter 
to report the latest technical achievements relating to earthen construction; in this 
regard, please refer to a recently published volume on the subject [33]. 5.3.3 
The Importance of Education and Training 
to Overcome Management Issues 
The issues concerning the exchange of information and communication could be 
solved by the involvement of builders and designers (structural and installation 
engineers) from the early stages of the architectural project. Their involvement 
could guide and frame the architectural project to the characteristics and limits 
of raw earth materials and technologies adopted, in particular as regards external 
constraints (seismic or ﬂooding risk, areas with particularly severe climates). Many 
other management issues reported above are intrinsically linked to the lack of training 
and education in the ﬁeld of raw earth construction. T

his lack affects both the design 
and management aspects of the built environment (technical culture), and the prob-
lems relating to the maintenance of systems (by technicians, workers etc.) or of raw 
earth building components (by end-users). In the authors’ opinion, technical training (aimed at professionals in the construc-
tion sector) should be acquired voluntarily (an aspect which is closely tied to socio-
cultural aspects which will be discussed in the next point). Above all, such training 
should be provided by organizations as professional associations, consulting compa-
nies or manufacturers of raw earth products, as is already the case for various 
European companies. Another opportunity to improve several aspects connected to technical culture, 
and to the execution of raw earth constructions could be provided by the use of 
digital manufacturing technologies. Indeed, innovative digital construction processes

5
Earth-Based Materials in Contemporary Construction: Challenges …
83
for earthen buildings and components include 3D printed earth, robotic rammed 
earth, and robotic sprayed earth [30], but several other techniques are being devel-
oped, always drawing from the knowledge of traditional construction techniques and 
hybridizing them with today’s technical and operative possibilities [29, 34]. Contem-
porary research on the use of digitally manufactured earth construction has begun 
to be in vogue since 2011 [31], but still has wide margins for development, both 
in the ﬁeld of in-situ digital fabrication of entire buildings or in the production of 
prefabricated components, to be assembled on site. Digital fabrication technologies 
can provide improved efﬁciency of the production process (for instance, it has been 
proved that additive manufacturing can provide a 25–60% in material reduction and 
30% time-saving as compared to traditional manufacturing techniques [31]). They 
also provide a higher level of quality in detailing, accuracy, ﬁnishing, and repro-
ducibility and increased safety during the manufacturing process [31]. An exten-
sion of the lifetime behavior of the product and improved compliance with modern 
building regulations for structural, thermal, and environmental performances are also 
expected. Lastly, digital manufacturing increases satisfaction of the contemporary 
needs of occupants, in terms of design, comfort, and well-being [30]. Ultimately, raw earth construction can beneﬁt from the push for digital transition 
that now permeates the contemporary construction sector. Methods and techniques 
for systematizing information relating to the performance of raw earth materials can 
be developed to catalog previous studies and predict (through AI-based approaches) 
mechanical, thermal, and physical performances of novel materials based on the raw 
materials inputs or design procedures used. Methods for optimizing the production 
process of raw earth materials and components can be reﬁned through life cycle ana

l-
ysis (LCA) and life cycle cost (LCC), in close collaboration with entrepreneurs oper-
ating in the sector, pursuing the objectives of economic and environmental sustain-
ability. The need to improve project management, systematize the information and 
the technical characteristics of raw earth components can beneﬁt from a BIM-based 
approach. As it happens for conventional construction, BIM tools concentrate infor-
mation and channel it with a view to interoperability between softwares and commu-
nication between all the actors involved in the various phases of the construction 
process (including the building site). In particular, the systematization of information 
related to the performances of innovative raw earth materials and components can aim 
at the creation of technical data sheets and databases that can feed the BIM libraries, 
including information about their environmental impacts, to encourage comparison 
of different “green” design options. Through these tools, it will be possible to take 
into consideration performance, environmental and economic aspects. The challenge of the twin ecological and digital transition therefore perfectly 
suits raw earth construction, which, through the digitalization of information and 
workﬂows, could raise the quality of design, execution and management of earthen 
buildings [6]. 84
G. Giuffrida and R. Caponetto
5.3.4 
A Widespread Raw Earth Culture to Overcome 
Socio-Cultural and Economics Issues 
During the last decades, several companies specialized in raw earth construction have 
been born, especially for those building sites where speciﬁc equipment and machinery 
were required (as in rammed earth construction). The cost of new earthen construction 
in Europe and in the world is still quite high, also because of its insufﬁcient use and 
spread. To reduce costs, a great opportunity can be given by automated or digital 
constructive techniques or industrialized production processes [35, 36]. Concerning the cultural prejudice, the dissemination of raw earth culture, and its 
expressive and environmental potential is fundamental for its development. Raw earth 
construction will increasingly be able to gain a foothold in contemporary construction 
only if it becomes known and recognizable. In this sense, the following initiatives 
are producing the trigger for the creation of an ever-increasing technical culture on 
raw earth construction:
• Dedicated master’s degrees
• Undergraduate university courses
• Professional training courses
• Associations and networks of professionals
• International architectural competitions
• Research projects
• Industrial research. Indeed, innovation coming from industry is also giving new inputs to the 
development of raw earth construction, according to a bottom-up approach. As previously stated [6], at the same time it is necessary to eradicate some false 
beliefs, and to do this it is essential to spread exemplary projects throughout the 
territories (the Orange

rie of Lyon in France, the Ricola Herb Center and the Swiss 
Ornithological Institute in Switzerland, the Oaxaca School in Mexico, the Amangiri 
Resort and Spa in Utah, the Schlins Workshop in Austria, are just some examples) to 
show how raw earth can be adopted to create modern, efﬁcient and environmentally 
compatible architecture and living spaces. Furthermore, it is the authors’ opinion that these “exemplary” construction sites 
should also be “exploratory”, with a view to opening up to innovative solutions and 
to the hybridization of technical and non-technical knowledge. Moreover, they could 
be conceived as “educational”, aiming at the transmission of technical knowledge 
and emphasizing the pedagogical role of the construction site, thus encouraging its 
openness to designers and operators of the construction sector, even if not directly 
involved in the work. 5
Earth-Based Materials in Contemporary Construction: Challenges …
85
5.4 Conclusions and Final Remarks 
Raw earth construction has undoubted advantages compared to conventional 
construction. However, it also presents limits, which in this contribution have been 
divided into regulatory and political, technical, management and training, socio-
cultural and economic. These limits must not be considered as insurmountable, in 
fact, the second part of the contribution presents insights into what may be the 
challenges to overcome and the opportunities to be seized. The reported insights refer to the regulations and policies that could favor the 
development of raw earth construction, the key points around which technical 
research is focusing, the importance of education and speciﬁc training for over-
coming the management issues linked to earth construction, and the need to spread 
a raw earth culture to be able to overcome socio-cultural and economic issues. The 
contribution also highlights how digital manufacturing techniques and digitalization 
in general can help in creating methods and techniques that can simplify, rationalize, 
and optimize the production and management of raw earth construction, favoring its 
adoption in contemporary Architecture, Engineering, and Construction sector. The return to raw earth construction today means returning to the principles under-
lying the origins of the history of buildings. It means taking into account the impor-
tance of raw materials present in the territories, and being aware of their limitations. It also means designing circularly, with materials that can be regenerated and reused 
at their end-of-life without harm to the environment. Finally, it means being able to 
rely on the quality of buildings in which we live, both in terms of comfort, safety 
and reliability requirements. Today, it is possible to innovate raw earth construction and make it competi-
tive, attractive, and reliable even in comparison with other sustainable construction 
technologies. To achieve this, it is necessary to overcome the aforementioned limi-
tation

s by placing the values of sustainability, reuse, circular economy, along with 
the challenges of the ecological and digital transition, at the center of future building 
and design practices. References

Chapter 6 
Cob Vernacular Architecture 
Conservation and Its Contribution 
Towards a Sustainable Built 
Environment 
Alejandro Jiménez Rios 
Abstract Cob is a low-impact building material with a minimal carbon footprint 
that doesn’t require energy-intensive manufacturing processes, uses locally available 
materials, is non-toxic and biodegradable. Moreover, cob vernacular architecture is 
often deeply rooted in local cultures and traditions. By conserving and maintaining 
these structures, cultural heritage is preserved and a connection to traditional building 
methods, which can foster a sense of identity and pride in local communities is 
maintained. In this chapter, the importance of cob vernacular heritage conservation 
and how this can contribute towards a sustainable built environment with an emphasis 
on the sustainable properties of this material is discussed. The chapter content is in 
alignment with the United Nations Sustainability Development Goals and with well 
stablished international conservation methodologies and philosophies, such as the 
Venice Charter, the Nara Document on authenticity, among others. 6.1 
Vernacular Architecture 
The word “vernacular” originates from the Latin term “vernaculus”, which means 
local, domestic or indigenous [1]. Hence, vernacular architecture is commonly known 
as folk or popular architecture [2]. This architectural style can be found throughout 
the world and is characterized by local environmental factors. Vernacular build-
ings are created using native materials by the inhabitants of a particular place to 
meet their housing needs [3]. Vernacular local adaptability has allowed for centuries 
the creation of what is presently referred to as “nature-based solutions” [4]. It is 
a true representation of the world’s cultural diversity as it is the result of complex 
societal mechanisms through the intergenerational reﬁnement of building knowledge 
and construction techniques. As clearly described by Piesik [5], vernacular skills and 
know-how are customarily passed from experienced individuals to novice learners of

Fig. 6.1 a Vernacular houses in the picturesque Irish town of Skerries, b turf houses in the

Guanajuato, Mexico 
the trade. As vernacular buildings are often constructed and inhabited by the owners 
themselves, vernacular archit

ecture has also been paradoxically deﬁned as “archi-
tecture without architects” [6]. Figure 6.1 presents some examples of vernacular 
architecture found in different countries and climates. Vernacular architecture embodies a series of economic beneﬁts. For centuries, it 
has been the engine of local economies by using local craftsmanship and materials 
to satisfy those communities’ demands for shelter. According to Santos et al. [7], 
vernacular architecture solutions bring about cost-effective solutions by enhancing 
human comfort, which ultimately results in low energy consumption. This obser-
vation is on the contrary contested by Bustan-Gaona et al. [8], who determined 
after conducting a multidisciplinary study on vernacular buildings in Equator, the 
inadequacy of light conditions inside the analyzed local study cases. Such lighting 
deﬁciency is caused by the small windows dimensions of this vernacular building 
typology. This results into higher energy consumption levels and an increased oper-
ational cost for the inhabitants. From another perspective, further economic beneﬁts 
are attached to vernacular architecture, particularly to the one built using earthen 
materials and techniques, by Zami and Lee [9]. These authors have performed a 
thorough review on the topic and present such typology as a low-cost alternative to 
cope with increased urban housing demand. Furthermore, intangible values and traditional knowledge are deeply embodied 
in vernacular architecture as it gives a unique character to a region or culture and

6
Cob Vernacular Architecture Conservation and Its Contribution …
91
helps preserve cultural heritage and traditional knowledge [7]. A recent study case 
conducted by Alnaim [10], who studied the vernacular courtyard house typology of 
Iranian houses, concluded that such building style can meet the cultural, social and 
climatic demands of local inhabitants by creating a comfortable and private house-
hold atmosphere. The loss of uniqueness character and values caused by the global 
implementation of standardized materials and construction techniques, is exposed 
for instance by Tarrad and Ahmad [11], who described Jordanian cities as being 
severely affected by this phenomena. What once was a “vibrant town with identiﬁed 
aesthetic architectural style”, is now perceived as an “anonymous and monotonous 
unit of concrete”. Sentiments of nostalgia are also evocated by Sharami and Hosseini 
[12], when they compared the declining vernacular Iranian neighborhood structure, 
known as “Mahalla”, in front of the “cruelly drawing and widening lines for the 
modernization of the urban structure”. Fortunately, universal recognition of values 
inherent to vernacular architecture and the necessity of preserving them, has been 
adopted by the United Nations (UN) [13] (see Sect. 6.3). Studying and comprehending vernacular architecture not only aids in preserving 
its historical, cultural, and architectural signiﬁcance, but it can a

lso guide stakeholders 
toward contemporary sustainable solutions [14]. Such outcomes can be achieved 
through a bioclimatic design, approach that obtains valuable insights from vernacular 
practices. As recognized by Huang et al. [15], vernacular buildings are speciﬁcally 
tailored to efﬁciently cope with local climatic and socio-cultural conditions. These 
authors emphasize the importance of empirical work to better understand and adopt 
vernacular bioclimatic design strategies, which may considerably differ from general 
tools, i.e. Mahoney Table, Olgyay Bioclimatic Chart, Givoni’s Building Bioclimatic 
Chart, etc. [16]. On another study case, Sharami and Hosseini [12] highlighted the 
positive environmental characteristics of vernacular Isfahani architecture. They have 
concluded that such typology provides optimal conditions for semi-arid and hot 
climates due to their energy efﬁcient and low-impact components, both at housing 
and urban development levels. Numerous authors have presented other examples 
of how utilizing vernacular materials, construction techniques, and typologies can 
enhance the sustainability of modern buildings [17–19]. Of paramount importance to adequately address contemporary issues, are culture 
and tradition integration with environment and nature aspects, if vernacular principles 
are to be adopted. To foster the implementation of vernacular skills and know-how, 
Santos et al. [7] have identiﬁed the need for increased awareness of the potential 
beneﬁts obtained and the value of the vernacular practice, as well as the necessity 
of providing the required ﬁnancial aid. Such measurements need to be accompanied 
by an aligned sustainable urban development strategy and adequate preservation of 
existing heritage measurements. 92
A. Jiménez Rios
6.2 
Rediscovering Cob 
For hundreds of years, humans across the globe have harnessed the earth beneath 
their feet to create shelter. Earthen architecture stands as a testament to humanity’s 
ingenuity, utilizing the abundant and accessible resources found in local environ-
ments. Today, the heritage of earthen architecture is rich and varied, encompassing 
archaeological sites, living monuments, groups of buildings, and even entire historic 
towns and cultural landscapes. The most common earthen construction classiﬁcations are based on either the 
type of method implemented (dry or wet), the structural function of the earthen 
elements (load bearing or non-load bearing, or by the type of element produced 
(monolithic, masonry, or inﬁll). Among the many methods of earthen construction, 
cob, which is classiﬁed as a wet, load bearing, monolithic technique, stands out for 
its simplicity and versatility. This chapter explores the characteristics, preparation, 
construction processes, and mechanical properties of cob, shedding light on why this 
ancient technique remains relevant and how it could be beneﬁcial in contemporary 
architecture. 6.2.1 
The Unique Characteristics of Cob

Cob is a mixture of soil, water, and natural ﬁbers such as straw. The simplicity of 
its ingredients contradicts the complexity of its behavior as a building material. The 
quality of the soil, the amount of water (main parameter determining the mixture 
consistency) [20], and the type and quantity of ﬁbers, all signiﬁcantly inﬂuence the 
ﬁnal product’s strength and durability. The natural ﬁbers added to the cob mixture 
serve multiple purposes. They improve workability by keeping the wet soil cohesive 
during mixing and placement [21], while enhancing the material’s tensile strength 
and ductility [22]. This means that cob can deform under stress without cracking 
excessively, an essential property for withstanding environmental stresses and low 
magnitude seismic events. Additionally, ﬁbers help manage soil shrinkage as it dries, 
reducing the likelihood of cracks or limiting their opening [23]. Fibers, typically between 15 and 50 cm in length, account for 1–3% by mass of 
the mixture, which shows an important variability in the characteristics of the ﬁbers 
reported by different authors. This inﬂuences not only cob mechanical properties, 
but also its thermal performance [24]. The ﬁbers decrease the material’s density, 
improving its insulating properties, although unfortunately reducing its compressive 
strength at a certain threshold. The drying of the material during the construction 
process is also accelerated by the ﬁbers. On the other hand, ﬁbers increase cob 
humidity absorption rate during operation, which is a desired characteristic that 
helps keeping healthy indoor living conditions. Moreover, the presence of ﬁbers can 
enhance resistance to erosion and frost, making cob a resilient choice for various 
climates [25]. 6
Cob Vernacular Architecture Conservation and Its Contribution …
93
Fig. 6.2 Parameters 
inﬂuencing the suitability of 
a  soil  to  be  use  d in Cob
Several components presented in Fig. 6.2 play an important role determining 
the suitability of a given soil to be used on cob. Soil type depends on its under-
lying geology, mineralogy, stratigraphy, and color, which may also be of interest 
for aesthetic purposes. A soil granulometric composition, also known as particle 
size distribution, depends on its particle’s sizes. Soil particles are classiﬁed as 
cobbles (greater than 63 mm), gravels (63–2 mm), sand (2.0–0.063 mm), silt (0.063– 
0.002 mm) or clay (smaller than 0.002 mm) [26]. Harries et al. [27] suggested the use 
of soils with a clay content between 10 and 25%, silt content between 10 and 20%, 
and sand/gravel content of about 55% to 70%. Watson and McCabe [28] proposed 
similar ranges for silts and clays, whereas they recommended having about 25–30% 
of sand and 30–40% of gravel. Clay content is crucial for soil behavior in cob because clay binds the coarser 
particles together, creating a cohesive and uniform mixture. This cohesion arises due 
to the water suction between clay particles [29]. Nevertheless,

clay content must be 
limited to no more than 30% as excessive amounts of clay would lead to considerable 
expansion and contraction, which can destabilize the structure. Soil consistency is 
determined by its plasticity and cohesion. In simple terms, “Plasticity is the ability of 
a material to be molded without fracturing, while cohesion is the ability to maintain 
that deformed shape”  [30]. Based on its water content, the state of a soil can be 
classiﬁed as liquid, plastic, semi-solid or solid, and the limits between such stages 
are referred to as Atterberg limits (see Fig. 6.3). The amount of swelling/shrinkage of a soil, also known as soil expansiveness, 
will be mainly determined by clay content and type. Montmorillonites will suffer 
bigger volumetric changes in comparison to kaolinites or illites [23]. While mixing 
of the cob constitutive components, it is also important to use the minimum amount 
of water necessary for an adequate workability as, by reducing the amount of water 
that will evaporate when cob dries, the amount of volume shrinkage and the opening 
of cracks can be controlled. Soil’s particle type, size distribution, and compaction 
degree also affect its density, property that is mainly used to compute the soil’s

94
A. Jiménez Rios
Fig. 6.3 Diagram showing 
the different soil 
classiﬁcation phases and the

limits. EMC = Equilibrium 
Moisture Content
porosity and thermal performance. Well compacted soils would be stronger than 
poorly compacted materials [25]. Finally, cob’s load bearing capacity is also inﬂuenced by the compressive strength 
of its soil. Soil strength comes from the friction forces between its particles which are 
typically assessed on function of the soil macroscopic angle of friction, φ. The shear 
strength of a soil, τ is computed as τ = σ tanφ + c, where σ , represents the soil 
effective stress, c its cohesion, and φ represents the effective macroscopic angle of 
friction. The effective stress of a soil can be calculated as σ = σ −u, where the total 
stress is represented by σ , and u denotes the pore water pressure. Thus, if pore water 
pressure increases, caused by an increase in the moisture content, the effective stress 
of the soil will decrease, consequently reducing its shear strength. These equations are 
derived assuming full soil saturation and effective stress parameters. Nevertheless, 
cob, being a manufactured unsaturated soil, presents a structural behavior not entirely 
described with such assumptions. The ﬁeld of unsaturated soil mechanics, which 
provides equations with a higher degree of complexity, needs to be consulted if a 
more reﬁned description of the macroscopic unsaturated behavior of soils is required 
[31]. 6.2.2 
Preparation of Cob and Construction Process 
Preparing the mixture for cob construction is a less industrialized process than that 
of modern materials like concrete or steel. Yet, it requires careful attention to detail. Ideally, the

soil should be sourced locally or recycled from other projects to minimize 
environmental impact. When necessary, the soil’s properties can be adjusted through 
stabilization, either by modifying its grading or by adding substances like pure sand,

6
Cob Vernacular Architecture Conservation and Its Contribution …
95
clay, or, in extreme conditions, cement or lime to make the structure stronger and 
resistant to external hazards. Traditionally, loam preparation was a labor-intensive process performed by hand 
or animal power. Today, modern machinery can expedite this process, but the funda-
mental steps remain the same. The soil is ﬁrst dried and sieved to remove large 
particles. Water is then added, and the mixture is kneaded and trampled until it 
reaches a plastic consistency. At this stage, ﬁbers are incorporated, and the mixture 
is further worked until it regains a plastic consistency that allows for its placement 
on the wall (see Fig. 6.4). Building with cob begins on a solid foundation, traditionally stone masonry, but 
often reinforced concrete is used in modern constructions which increases the envi-
ronmental impact of the whole construction. The cob mixture is placed directly on top 
of the foundation, normally in the form of small lumps that allow manual handling 
by a person, and that accumulate to form layers along the footprint of the projected 
walls. The construction grows taller layer by layer. Each layer, or lift, is about 30– 
60 cm high and requires a drying period of approximately 5–7 days before the next 
layer can be added. As each lift dries, the lateral surface of the walls is trimmed 
and smoothed to ensure evenness and prepare it for subsequent layers. This step 
is crucial for achieving a monolithic wall with no weak planes, as the layers bond 
together during the drying process (see Fig. 6.5). (a)

6.2.3 
Mechanical Properties and Structural Behavior of Cob 
Cob’s mechanical properties are primarily determined by its clay content and the 
presence of ﬁbers. These elements provide the material with its cohesive strength 
and relatively high ductility. A tabular summary of some of cob’s mechanical prop-
erties as reported in the literature is presented in Table 6.1. Moisture content also 
plays a signiﬁcant role in cob’s strength, with higher moisture after a certain optimal 
threshold leading to reduced compressive strength. Therefore, maintaining low mois-
ture content during

both construction and the building’s lifetime is essential for cob 
structural integrity. Cob also exhibits good shear strength compared to other earthen 
materials. Unlike modular earthen techniques such as adobe, cob walls are mono-
lithic, which means they lack the inherent weakness planes found in brick or block 
constructions. These properties, combined with its ability to regulate indoor humidity 
and its thermal insulating capabilities, makes cob an attractive option for sustainable 
building practices [32]. As cob is a non-standardized material, the value of the mechanical properties can 
vary considerably [39]. Keefe [20] studied the variability of cob compressive strength 
on both cube and cylinder specimens, ﬁnding that cubes compressive strength was 
20–25% higher than that of the cylinders. This variation in percentage is also common 
when the compressive strength of concrete is obtained from specimens with similar 
ratios. Furthermore, according to this author, grading, compaction, clay content and 
clay mineralogy, are all properties that can affect cob compressive strength. More

importantly, Keefe has reported that there is a negative linear relation between mois-

content, the lower the unconﬁned compressive strength of cob. This observation has

Akinkurolere et al. [41]. 6.2.4 
Numerical Modelling of Cob 
Given the numerous uncertainties in studying existing earthen structures, various 
numerical strategies often depend on experimental campaigns. These campaigns are 
essential for determining the necessary numerical input parameters, model calibra-
tion, and for validating the numerical results obtained [42]. Only a few authors have 
conducted numerical modelling studies of cob, all of them implementing the so called 
continuum homogeneous models approach [43]. One notable study by Miccoli et al. [34] utilized a ﬁnite element method macro-
modeling approach in the software DIANA. Their approach assumed plane stress, 
material homogeneity, and material continuity. By employing a total strain rotating 
crack constitutive model with a multi-linear deﬁnition of the stress–strain relation-
ship for compressive behavior, they successfully calibrated their simulations and 
replicated the shear stress-shear strain relationship observed experimentally [22]. However, the model failed to replicate the diagonal failure pattern observed in experi-
ments, likely due to small non

-symmetric conditions in the samples and testing setup. Quagliarini and Maracchini [35] also explored numerical simulations of cob, but this 
time using the ﬁnite element software MIDAS FEA. They applied a smeared-crack 
macro-modeling approach with a 2D plane stress simpliﬁcation. Their stress–strain 
curves matched experimental results up to the peak strength of the material, although 
the post-peak behavior, which is crucial for understanding the material’s energy 
dissipation capacity, was not replicated by the numerical model. The authors noted 
that the damage patterns, interpreted through principal stresses, aligned with typical 
crack patterns observed in compressive tests. Further expanding the understanding 
of cob numerical modeling, Jiménez Rios and O’Dwyer conducted a study on cob’s 
nonlinear monotonic behavior using the software tools ANSYS and ABAQUS [44]. They examined the strengths and weaknesses of various constitutive models. Their

98
A. Jiménez Rios
ﬁndings indicated that the Concrete and Concrete Damaged Plasticity models were 
the most effective at replicating cob’s nonlinear monotonic behavior in ANSYS and 
ABAQUS, respectively. 6.3 
Conservation of Earthen Vernacular Architecture 
In 2015, the General Assembly of the UN adopted the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable 
Development. This resolution aimed to transform our world through 17 interrelated 
Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) and 169 targets based on the economic, 
social, and environmental dimensions of sustainable development. In particular, 
SDG 11 focuses on making cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient 
and sustainable. Within this framework, Target 11.4 emphasizes the importance of 
protecting and safeguarding the world’s heritage (https://sdgs.un.org/goals). The UN 
Educational, Scientiﬁc and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) plays a crucial role in 
promoting cultural heritage conservation worldwide. By adopting the 1972 World 
Heritage Convention, UNESCO member states committed to identifying, protecting, 
conserving, and presenting the world’s heritage. UNESCO member states are advised on terms of conservation philosophy and 
techniques by the International Council on Monuments and Sites (ICOMOS). ICOMOS also evaluates properties inscription on the UENSCO World Heritage 
List, conducts comparative studies, and assessments on the conservation estate of 
inscribed properties. Moreover, a series of International Scientiﬁc Committees (ISC) 
have been established within ICOMOS to gather, study, and disseminate informa-
tion related to principles, techniques, and policies on conservation to enhance the 
management and protection of cultural heritage. Among the 31 ISCs, the ISC on 
Earthen Architectural Heritage (ISCEAH) and the ISC on Vernacular Architecture 
(CIAV) are particularly relevant to this chapter. As a result of the efforts done by these 
institutions, the conservation practice of earthen vernacular architecture is nowadays 
based on a set o

f principles and guidelines broadly accepted and applied by the 
international community contained within the following charters: the Venice Charter 
[45], the Nara Document on Authenticity [46], the Charter on the Built Vernacular 
Heritage [47], and the Burra Charter: the Australia ICOMOS Charter for Places of 
Cultural Signiﬁcance [48] (see Fig. 6.6). 6.3.1 
The Venice Charter 
The Venice Charter, approved in 1964, establishes the fundamental principles for 
preserving and restoring historical monuments. In this document the deﬁnition of a 
historical monument encompasses any architectural work, urban or rural setting in 
which is found evidence of a particular civilization, a signiﬁcant development, or 
a historic event. It emphasizes the use of all available sciences and techniques to

conserve monuments, both as works of art and as historical evidence. Key guidelines 
include maintaining monuments in use, preserving aesthetic and historical values, 
and documenting all interventions comprehensively. New constructions, demolitions, 
modiﬁcations or moving of all or part of a monument must be restricted and only on 
cases of absolute necessity for the safeguarding of the monument itself or justiﬁed 
by national or international interest, should they be allowed. 6.3.2 
The Nara Document on Authenticity 
Adopted in 1994, the Nara Document on Authenticity extends and complements 
the Venice Charter by addressing cultural and heritage diversity. It highlights the 
importance of authenticity in conservation, acknowledging that different cultures 
attribute value based on various criteria. Form and design, materials, and substance, 
use and function, traditions and techniques, location and setting, spirit and feeling, as 
well as other external and internal factors must be examined based on the local context 
of every cultural heritage asset to determine its authenticity. Deﬁning authenticity 
helps to “clarify and illuminate the collective memory of humanity”. The values upon 
which authenticity is based, are attributed by local cultures and their socio-cultural 
and historical contexts. Respect of local culture and its people must be of paramount 
importance for conservation professionals and other stakeholders involved in the 
conservation of the built cultural heritage environment. 6.3.3 
The Charter on the Built Vernacular Heritage 
The 1999 Charter on Built Vernacular Heritage bestow vernacular architecture with 
the representation of the culture of a community and of its ties with its surrounding 
environment. The set of built vernacular architectural assets represents the world’s 
cultural diversity and its co

nservation is promoted on this document. The Charter

100
A. Jiménez Rios
recognizes the threats posed by economic and cultural homogenization and under-
scores the need for community, government, and specialist involvement in preserving 
vernacular buildings. Moreover, this charter reinforces the principles of conservation 
stated in the Venice Charter regarding the multidisciplinary character of the inter-
ventions while highlighting the importance of craft skills and training in vernacular 
architecture conservation efforts. The Charter advocates for governments, authori-
ties, and organizations to develop educational, training, and informational initiatives 
aimed at raising public awareness about vernacular heritage, supporting commu-
nities in conserving traditional systems, materials, and skills, and promoting the 
principles of vernacular conservation. Additionally, it encourages the establishment 
of regional networks to share expertise and experiences related to vernacular heritage. In accordance with this vision, vernacular architecture is better preserved if its assets 
are treated and studied as a regional group or at settlement level. The landscape 
surrounding them as well as the intangible values attached to these architectural assets 
play an important role to better understand the way in which they are used by local 
communities, and design better and more sympathetic preservation interventions. 6.3.4 
The Burra Charter 
The Burra Charter (latest version adopted in 2013) provides guidelines for conserving 
places of cultural signiﬁcance, stressing the principle of inter-generational equity, 
which requires that places of cultural signiﬁcance to be preserved for present and 
future generations. It advises a three-tiered process that begins with understanding 
the signiﬁcance of the place, followed by the development of a conservation policy, 
and ﬁnalizes with conducting the managing of the site accordingly to such policy. This charter is deeply interlinked with the Nara document, as the signiﬁcance of a 
cultural site is highly dependent on its authenticity. 6.3.5 
Conservation Standards and Specialized Guidance 
The conservation of earthen vernacular architecture and cultural heritage requires 
in addition a multidisciplinary approach guided by international standards and prin-
ciples. By understanding and applying these frameworks, researchers and practi-
tioners can ensure the preservation of our shared heritage for future generations. Other than the ICOMOS charters, various standards and specialized guidance have 
been developed for the conservation of earthen cultural heritage. Internationally, the 
International Organization for Standardization (ISO) developed ISO 13822 Bases 
for Design of Structures—Assessment of Existing Structures [49], which addresses 
the assessment of existing structures, emphasizing their economic and cultural value. Regionally, the European Committee for Standardization (CEN) published EN 16853 
[

50], which deﬁnes the conservation process and provides guidelines on decision

6
Cob Vernacular Architecture Conservation and Its Contribution …
101
making, planning and implementation of conservation of the built cultural heritage 
environment. While specialized standards for conserving earthen structures are limited, some 
guidance documents exist speciﬁcally for cob. Recently, The Cob Research Institute 
(CRI) developed a cob construction appendix, which has been approved for inclusion 
in the International Residential for building codes in the United States [51]. Simi-
larly, the Devon Earth Building Association (DEBA) has proved that cob dwellings 
comply with United Kingdom building regulations and the suitability of cob to be 
used to build new buildings in accordance with such regulation [52]. Finally, the Getty 
Conservation Institute has also contributed guidelines on conserving earthen build-
ings, particularly through its Seismic Retroﬁtting Project (SRP) within the Earthen 
Architecture Initiative (EAI) framework (https://www.getty.edu/conservation/our_ 
projects/ﬁeld_projects/earthen/). 6.4 
Contributions of Cob Vernacular Architecture 
Conservation Towards a Sustainable Built Environment 
Rediscovering cob offers a bridge between ancient wisdom and modern sustain-
ability. Its simplicity, versatility, and environmental beneﬁts make it an attractive 
choice for contemporary construction. By understanding and applying the princi-
ples of cob building, we can create structures that are not only resilient and efﬁcient 
but also deeply connected to the natural world. Moreover, by improving our under-
standing of cob’s behavior under different conditions and reﬁning our modeling 
techniques, we can better preserve and innovate within this ancient building method, 
contributing to sustainable and resilient construction practices for the future. These 
conditions can be fulﬁlled only if the existing rich heritage on cob vernacular architec-
ture is properly preserved and the corresponding wisdom and know-how is adequately 
documented, communicated, and disseminated. Thus, in 2001 UNESCO launched 
the World Heritage Earthen Architecture Programme (WHEAP) aiming at devel-
oping policies for the conservation, revitalization, and increased awareness of earthen 
architecture. One of the notable outcomes of this project was the Inventory of World 
Heritage Earthen Architecture [53], which identiﬁed the use of earth on 150 UNESCO 
sites. The inventory also highlighted the use of cob in 29% of the sites surveyed. This indicated that cob was present in nearly 20% of the World Heritage List earthen 
architecture sites across four continents (see Fig. 6.7). Unfortunately, these invaluable assets are to a large extent vulnerable to different 
socio-economic and natural hazards, especially in front of climate change and its 
effects [55]. The deterioration or disappearance of cultural heritage represents a 
signiﬁcant loss, both economic and cultural.

Preservation efforts promote social 
cohesion, create collective identity, encourage cultural participation, and support 
economic development. They also contribute to urban planning, placemaking, and 
climate change resilience. These beneﬁts have been recognized by the UN in Target

11.4 of the SDGs which states that safeguarding cultural heritage contributes to 
making cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable [56]. Moreover, by safeguarding cultural traditions, the revitalization of local economies 
and the creation of job opportunities in rural settlements can be fostered [57]. Thus, 
endeavors to preserve the built cultural heritage environment are both required and 
desired. As highlighted by Sharami and Hosseini [12]: “It is possible to achieve sustain-
able contemporary architecture and urban design using contextual knowledge of 
vernacular architecture and combining it with new scientiﬁc achievements”. In 
that regard, the VerSus research project [54] identiﬁed principles and strategies 
inherent in vernacular architecture that can be applied to contemporary building 
projects. These principles organized around environmental, socio-cultural, and socio-
economic sustainability, and intrinsic to most types of vernacular architecture, 
demonstrate how traditional knowledge can inform modern sustainable practices. Furthermore, speciﬁc strategies were proposed to achieve the successful adoption of 
these principles as graphically presented in Fig. 6.8. 6

While Industry 4.0 h

as signiﬁcantly driven digitalization and boosted produc-
tivity, it has been found insufﬁcient to meet sustainability targets by 2030 [58]. This shortfall is due to its shortcomings addressing the environmental impacts of 
increased industrial activity and the challenges posed by automation to the human 
component of the socio-economic system. Consequently, a new paradigm based on 
three core principles: human-centrism, resilience, and sustainability, Industry 5.0, has 
emerged [59]. Within this transformative vision, social media has been recognized 
as a potential tool to foster and enhance collaboration among various stakeholders. Liang et al. [60] investigated the potential of social media to promote sustainability 
within the context of Built Cultural Heritage and Environment (BCHE) conservation, 
concluding that if utilized as a civic engagement tool, especially within the frame-
work of the Historic Urban Landscape approach [61], social media could signiﬁ-
cantly contribute to sustainability. Tourism plays a vital role in the economy of areas 
with rich BCHE, attracting visitors with its beauty, history, and cultural signiﬁcance. However, without proper management, overtourism can cause severe problems for 
both cultural heritage sites, such as anthropological degradation, and local communi-
ties, such as gentriﬁcation. Aiming at developing a sustainable tourism management 
model, de la Plata et al. [62] digitized cultural sites and presented the information 
to tourists in an engaging and accessible way, ensuring broad accessibility while 
preserving the heritage sites in Merida, Spain. Further efforts to enhance sustainability practices within the conservation of the 
BCHE have focused on participatory approaches. Bizzarri et al. [63], using a multi-
agent participatory methodology involving conservation professionals, a national 
government agency, local workers, and university students, managed to increase 
local community interest and encouraged residents of nearby villages to actively

6
Cob Vernacular Architecture Conservation and Its Contribution …
105
participate in conserving their cultural heritage. Their project, based on traditional 
skills and sustainable local materials, ensured the long-term maintenance of a case 
study site in Oman. Similarly, Ekici et al. [64] involved local communities, visitors, 
non-governmental organizations, and governmental bodies in developing policies for 
sustainable conservation practices in a rural village in Turkey. This approach, heavily 
inﬂuenced by social sustainability, effectively preserved the village’s integrity. In both 
cases, it was observed that a crucial factor in the successful implementation of any 
participatory approach was “stakeholder consciousness,” which refers to the aware-
ness achieved through the active involvement of various agents in the co-design and 
co-implementation of heritage revitalization initiatives and cultural heritage policies. Many researchers have emph

asized sustainability by examining the circularity 
and adaptability of the BCHE. Redden and Crawford’s extensive literature review 
supports the idea that historic buildings are highly sustainable and environmen-
tally efﬁcient [65]. They argued that some studies inadequately assess these valuable 
assets due to the implementation of limited methodologies that often overlook critical 
indicators like resource depletion, material waste, and pollution. Pintossi et al. [66] 
conducted a local case study in Salerno, Italy, addressing challenges posed by Adap-
tive Reuse of Cultural Heritage (ARCH) [67] through a participatory approach and 
proposed solutions for policymakers to promote ARCH for sustainable development 
based on BCHE conservation. The achievement of the UN SDGs is seriously compromised as noted in the last 
progress SDG’s report [68], and in particular Target 11.4, which aims to protect and 
safeguard the world’s heritage, as there is no data to measure Indicator 11.4.1. This 
means that progress on Target 11.4 is neither being tracked nor discussed in progress 
reports. To achieve Target 11.4, member states need to enhance collaboration, allocate 
budgets, and develop infrastructure to support cultural heritage conservation. Local 
governments should implement policies that localize the global agenda, promote 
policy initiatives, and develop infrastructure towards heritage conservation, and espe-
cially towards earthen vernacular architectures, which is particularly vulnerable and 
often neglected. Ensuring the continuous protection of earthen vernacular architecture requires a 
signiﬁcant effort which could be compensated with a more sustainable future. Key 
activities to achieve this include: the training of conservation professionals, essential 
to preserving vernacular traditions and the associated irreplaceable knowledge and 
skills, which should be taught and researched in a modern context; wide-spread 
communication and dissemination activities to raise public awareness about the 
importance of conserving remaining earthen vernacular buildings; and further devel-
opment and adoption of specialized standards for cob conservation would greatly 
help protect and preserve this rich cultural heritage. 106

Chapter 7 
Earth as a Building Material: From 
Traditional Building Techniques 
to Additive Manufacturing 
Flora Faleschini, Daniel Trento, and Mariano Angelo Zanini 
Abstract The research interest for earth-based building materials has increased 
extremely in recent years because of a tremendous rise of housing-demand world-
wide, together with the necessity of fostering environmentally friendly solutions 
within the construction industry. Nevertheless, the diffusion of earthen construc-
tion is still limited due to many reasons among which it is worth mentioning the 
poor durability of these materials (e.g., their sensitivity to moisture) and the lack of 
Codes and regulation for their design. In the meanwhile, new construction methods 
disrupt the market, among them, additive manufacturing (AM) has demonstrated its 
potential to transform the construction industry, especially through the 3D printing 
technology by extrusion. This technique has been widely studied for concrete produc-
tion but only recently has been applied for earth-based materials, being only vali-
dated under laboratory conditions with few real-scale applications. In this chapter, a 
review on earth-based materials for the building industry is presented, paying atten-
tion to the characteristics needed by these materials to be suitably applied in modern 
AM processes. Advantages, current limits and open challenges in 3D printing such 
geomaterials will be presented. Lastly, some case studies will be shown. 7.1 
Introduction 
As the world has been experiencing exponential population growth over recent years 
[1], the need for new buildings and infrastructure, especially in developing countries, 
is a highly relevant issue [2, 3]. The construction sector remains one of the key 
economic industries [4], and its impact on global economy is signiﬁcant, as about 110 
million people are employed directly in this sector [5], which corresponds to 7–10% 
of the actual workforce [4]. Furthermore, it plays an important role in determining 
the quality of life [6], nonetheless this sector is responsible for 35% of all carbon
