111

112
F. Faleschini et al. emissions and produces between 45 and 65% of the waste currently landﬁlled [7]. In 
this context, the 2021 global construction industry report highlights the absence of a 
signiﬁcant green revolution within the sector as construction industry emissions are 
projected to increase, further contributing to global warming [8, 9]. The relevance 
of sustainability in the building industry is clear and evident since stakeholders are 
increasingly aware of respecting the environment and ethical values as collective 
responsibility [10]. The implementation of sustainable construction materials and techniques signif-
icantly enhances the eco-efﬁciency of the construction sector, thereby fostering a 
more sustainable form of development. Concrete is still the most-used construction 
material and the second most-consumed substance in the world, just after water 
[11]. Considering all stages of production, cementitious concrete is responsible for 
4–8% of the world CO2 emissions [12]. Hence, the need to replace cementitious 
conglomerates with more sustainable materials to improve constructions environ-
mental footprint has led to consider earth as a viable alternative [13]. At the same 
time, developed countries are recovering and valorizing ancient construction systems, 
which involve locally available materials like earth [14]. Earth materials typically 
consist of mixtures of soil and water, often incorporating ﬁbers like straw [13]. These 
materials are commonly utilized for external walls, offering signiﬁcant thermal mass 
that enhances passive thermal design [15]. Their minimal environmental footprint 
is attributed to the low energy consumption during construction and the high recy-
clability of nearly all building materials used, which are frequently entirely natural 
and compatible [16]. In this way, earthen construction has been encouraged as an 
economically attractive solution joining with the value of vernacular architecture. In 
fact, a new sensibility is spreading, embraced by cultural heritage values, moving 
towards a functional philosophy that views architectural assets not merely for their 
historic and artistic signiﬁcance but also as representation of a traditional building art 
that requires comprehension rather than rejection, therefore, the idea of preservation 
should encompass building methods as well. Parallelly, the spread of technology and automation processes to accelerate 
industry production is hard to reconcile with traditional and old valuable manufac-
ture techniques. Over the last few decades, all economic sectors have been migrating 
progressively to digital processes to achieve higher efﬁciency. This has happened 
to the construction industry as well, which now offers greater complexity of shapes 
and methods thanks to digital manufacturing [17]. Numerous studies have

shown 
that digital/automated processes can offer signiﬁcant advantages to the construc-
tion sector, including increased design ﬂexibility and improved productivity [18]. As 
construction techniques with concrete, timber and masonry are undergoing a trans-
formation due to the integration of digital manufacturing methods like 3D printing 
and robotic fabrication, earth construction remains one of the least explored building 
types that has not been directly involved by this renovation [19, 20]. Nonetheless, 
some studies highlighted that integrating digital manufacturing with earth vernacular 
construction methods can lead signiﬁcant sustainability potentials as compared to 
other materials [15, 21]. 7
Earth as a Building Material: From Traditional Building Techniques …
113
This chapter aims to critically review the state of state-of-the-art on earth construc-
tions. The chapter primarily explores the properties of earth as a building material, 
showing the traditional techniques and the available standards. In the second part, 
additive manufacture is analyzed as an innovative construction process for earth; the 
insights include processing methods and properties, also making comparison with 
other printable construction materials. Finally, some real-scale buildings realized 
with such a technology are presented and open challenges are analyzed. 7.2 
Earth Constructions 
7.2.1 
Historical Overview 
Earth has been used as a construction material for millennia, however, there is 
no consensus about when mankind began to use this method. Some adobe-based 
dwellings discovered in Turkmenistan and dated on the period between 8000 and 
6000 BC may suggest that earth construction technique was adopted more than 
9000 years ago [22, 23]. According to another source [24], the most ancient adobe 
blocks were found along the Tigris River basin dating back to 7500 BC, while in 
Mesopotamia some evidence on the use of earth for construction purposes dates 
from the period between 5000 and 4000 BC [25]. Summarizing, it is likely that earth 
constructions appeared before 9000 years ago, with the start of early agricultural 
societies, a period comprised between 12,000 and 7000 BC [23]. However, many 
examples of ancient earth constructions have endured over the years, making it to 
now. In China, during the Tang dynasty (618–907), fortiﬁed settlements as Jiahoe, 
Gaochang and Xi’an were encircled with large, rammed earth walls, whereas the city 
of Kashgar was realized in adobe [26, 27]. The Ziggurat of Ur, erected about 2100 
BC, featured a core made of adobe bricks and was covered with ﬁred bricks held in 
place by bitumen (Fig. 7.1a). In North Africa, the Phoenicians expanded from eastern 
Mediterranean establishing settlements along the African north coast. Among them, 
their capital Carthage (now in Tunisia) was founded in 814 BC, and archeologists 
demonstrated earth walls were used here [28]. Rammed earth technology may have 
been carried to Europe by

this civilization when Phoenicians expanded in the Iberic 
Peninsula and Mediterranean islands. Rammed earth towers built by Hannibal are 
described by Roman historian Pliny, and architect Vitruvius describes rammed earth 
used in the French city of Marseilles and the Greek city of Athens being constructed 
entirely from adobe. [26]. When Islam civilization arrived in Southern Europe in 
711 AD, conﬂicts at this time led to build many rammed earth and adobe fortiﬁca-
tions [28]. The fortiﬁcations of Calatayud and Plad’Almalain in Spain, dating back 
to 884 AD, were uncovered through excavations [28]. Additionally, the Muslim 
defensive walls surrounding the historic cities of Cordoba, Seville, and Granada 
were constructed using rammed earth techniques. The Alhambra Palace in Granada 
(Fig. 7.1b), which is now a World Heritage site, was built from rammed earth around

114
F. Faleschini et al. 1238 [29]. Through time, earth has continued to be used as a construction material, 
but it was progressively replaced by the increasing use of ﬁred bricks. Earth-based 
materials were used by Native Americans in modern southern United States. The 
Pueblo peoples in contemporary New Mexico built adobe constructions that housed 
multiple families and rose several stories high. Among these structures, the Taos 
Pueblo stands out as the most famous, dating back to around 1000 AD (Fig. 7.1c). In South America, the former recorded earth bricks date back to the Moche culture, 
which developed from 100 AD [26]. The city of Cerro Blanco in Peru was the center 
of this civilization, where two adobe pyramids dedicated to the sun and the moon 
were built, named Huaca del Sol (Fig. 7.1d) and Huaca de la Luna [26]. Histor-
ical evidence has shown that earth building techniques have developed separately 
in several parts of the world. A common aspect can be observed among them, i.e. the soil and the subsoil for earth constructions were employed when timber or stone 
were not available. Indeed, if stone or timber were available, they would be preferred 
by local populations. The global signiﬁcance of earthen architecture is evident and deserves acknowl-
edgment, safeguarding, and preservation by the international community. It is worth 
mentioning that about 10% of World Heritage sites comprise earthen monuments or 
sites, being an important part of mankind’s history [34]. Today, earth is not considered a common building material, but the number of 
households living in earthen dwellings worldwide ranges between 8 and 10% [35],
Fig. 7.1 Earth-based constructions from the past: a the Ziggurat of Ur [30]; b the Alcazaba fortress 
of the Alhambra Palace in Granada [31]; c Taos Pueblo in New Mexico [32]; d Huaca del Sol in 
Peru [33]

7
Earth as a Building Material: From Traditional Building Techniques …
115
while almost 30% of the world constructions are made from earth [13]. These archi-
tectures can also be found in France, Germany and the UK that has more than 500,000 
ea

rth-based manufacts [23]. However, the importance of this construction method 
has been increasing substantially largely due to the sustainable agenda, in which the 
construction sector plays an important role. 7.2.2 
Earth Construction Techniques and Properties 
Earth architecture is a construction technique based on local materials and local 
needs, where local builders’ know-how plays an important part. These buildings are 
typically realized with a mixture of soil, water, and ﬁbers with different techniques 
according to the mixture proportions and the building process characteristics [36]. There are several methods to classify earth construction techniques for producing and 
shaping the geometries, but the most affordable is based on the distinction between 
dry and wet methods [15] (Fig. 7.2). In dry methods, the earth mixture is placed at its optimum water content (e.g., eval-
uated by Proctor test), and mechanical strength is achieved through compaction densi-
ﬁcation [15]. Rammed earth and compressed earth/sand blocks are included in this 
category, which are all load bearing [15, 36]. Instead, wet methods refer to techniques 
involving mixtures with relatively high-water content that achieve a plastic state, the 
mechanical strength is developed through densiﬁcation and shrinkage during the 
drying process until reaching an optimum moisture content. Among them, adobe, 
cob, wattle and daub, and earth plaster are the most well-known, but just adobe and 
cob have load-bearing capacity [13, 15, 36]. Typically, topsoil is not used in earth mixtures to avoid any contamination with 
organic substance (minimum excavation depth is one meter). Subsoil is usually 
preferred and mixed with water, ﬁbers and stabilizers when required. The process of making adobe bricks involves ﬁlling wooden molds with damp 
earth, which are subsequently left out in the sun to dry. When the blocks dry, shrinkage
Fig. 7.2 Classiﬁcation of load-bearing earth construction methods adapted from [12, 14, 31]

116
F. Faleschini et al. cracks may appear on the surface, for this reason straw or other natural ﬁbers are 
used to avoid this phenomenon [37] (Fig. 7.3a). Cob technique consists in building 
masonry walls layer by layer with a mixture of earth, water, and straw [16] (Fig. 7.3b). Clay is a sub ingredient of earth constructions, it can be used mixed and manufactured, 
or as it is found in nature for its excellent cohesive properties. Rammed earth consists of walls formed by compacting soil between temporary 
formworks [38] (Fig. 7.3c). Earth blocks and sand blocks on a smaller scale can be 
realized in the same way. Earth projection technique is often included in rammed 
earth category [23], it consists in compacting and stabilizing the soil to projecting it 
inside molds as for shotcrete. Earth mixtures are employed even for non-load bearing applications, among them 
wattle and daub architecture entail erecting walls using frames made of interwoven 
or lashed wooden

elements (wattles). These frames are then coated with a thick layer 
of puddled, clayey soil (daub) [38] (Fig. 7.3d). Earth plasters are made from clayey 
soils mixed with a certain amount of sand; they are appreciated for being permeable 
to water vapor [39]. Fig. 7.3 Earth-based construction methods: a Adobe bricks wall [40]; b Cob wall [41]; c Rammed 
earth [42]; d Wattle and daub [43]

7.2.3 
Current Standards 
The earthen architecture is highly variable depending on the territory where it is 
diffused, implying heterogeneity in terms of quality of the available resources, envi-
ronmental conditions, and needs of the users. Hence, the need to regulate these 
buildings in different geographical areas has risen in many countries according to the 
expected societal needs and constraints. Thus, the regulatory framework is very frag-
mented. Most documents have not the characteristics of standard, typically referring 
to speciﬁc aspects of earth constructions. Table 7.1 summarizes some of the most relevant available standards worldwide, 
the instructions are provided for: object, requirement of the product, methodology, 
quality control and anti-seismic criteria. In this context, the regulations that have 
transferred the concepts and best practices in standard tools recognized by the national 
standard bodies or equivalent authorities are considered exclusively for earth-based 
materials without the use of stabilizers. 7.3 
Additive Manufacturing for Earth-Based Materials 
7.3.1 
Additive Manufacturing as a Construction Technique 
Additive manufacturing (AM) is a production process consisting in adding thin layers 
of material consequently on top of each other [44]. It ﬁnds applications in several 
industrial ﬁelds, as it can be used with a wide variety of materials. In this context, the construction sector has been introducing digitally based tech-
nologies, e.g. additive manufacturing, to speed up the production rate, reduce the 
labor costs and afford design freedom [45–50]. Adopting AM methods in modern 
constructions provides three signiﬁcant beneﬁts [13, 51–53]. First, it reduces the 
materials/resources consumption and increases the efﬁciency, with an estimated 25– 
60% material consumption reduction and 30% time saving compared to traditional 
manufacturing techniques [45, 46, 54]. This advantage is considered of great impor-
tance for remote areas and those affected by natural disasters and war conﬂicts, where 
AM would allow the quick realization of new houses and infrastructures [49, 55]. Second, AM extends the product life, making the repair and retroﬁtting processes 
easier [13]. Third, it enhances value chains by offering shorter, and more localized 
production and supply [17, 56]. Another great potential from AM is the reduction of 
formworks use and related provisional structures during construction. Traditionally, 
formworks are used to cast concrete usin

g a subtractive process to shape the fresh 
concrete as it hardens. Their cost linked to labor time and materials impacts from 35 
to 60% of the overall concrete cost [56, 57]. In the construction industry, substantial research has been developing over the last 
few years on AM for concrete and cement products. As a result, a great development 
in 3D printing for cement-based materials has been reached [58, 59]. In terms of

mechanical properties, one of the most signiﬁcant features of 3D printed concrete is 
its anisotropy. Indeed, due to layer deposition the compressive strength in the parallel 
printing direction is higher than those of the other two directions, whereas ﬂexural 
strength in direction of stacked layers is higher than that in direction of parallel 
printing paths [58, 60]. Further, interlayer interfaces are inevitably created due to the 
layer-upon-layer deposition. In this region, increased porosity can be found, which 
affects mechanical strength, this being one of the main reasons for anisotropy [61]. Besides technical aspects, the introduction of AM in construction projects would 
change the way construction works are performed, with non-negligible social conse-
quences. Security is a major concern in the building industry because injuries and 
fatalities from construction works cause great losses to individuals and societies. In AM process, most dangerous works are performed by printers with automated 
tasks, resulting in reduced injuries [46, 56, 62]. Further, social consequences can be

7
Earth as a Building Material: From Traditional Building Techniques …
121
seen in a larger spectrum, as the printing process is highly automated, manpower 
required can be drastically reduced, and such a reduction will introduce new type 
of construction workers who need intellectual skills rather than manual ones, thus 
improving gender equality [45, 54]. On the other side, these beneﬁts highly depend on the context, size, and complexity 
of the construction project. Regarding the use of robots on-site and off-site, some 
challenges include the interaction of them with people, and the interoperability of 
more machines contemporarily, dealing with possible overlapping working areas. Some solutions have already been proposed in literature, including programming 
strategies, design of new robotic platforms, and the use of perceptional systems 
[53, 63]. 7.3.2 
3D Printing with Earth 
The integration of vernacular construction techniques with digital fabrication can 
lead to several social and sustainability advantages. However, the introduction of a 
new technology,

e.g. additive manufacturing, should maintain and represent the local 
identity, avoiding standardizing the product. When earth is employed for digital fabri-
cation, the potential of realizing sustainable constructions with vernacular knowledge 
generates responsible innovation by leveraging local industries and technologies, 
utilizing local resources [64, 65]. 7.3.2.1
Processing Aspects 
Material control in AM is one of the most challenging areas, the rheological properties 
of printable materials at the fresh state signiﬁcantly differs from those of conven-
tional ones [66, 67]. Among the developed techniques for additive manufacturing, 
extrusion-based methods are the most used and studied for construction purposes 
[68, 69]. In this kind of process, layers are extruded with a robot by pression and 
screw-driven mechanisms. The efﬁciency of this process is linked to the buildability 
of the material and the speed rate [69]. This construction process can be resumed 
into three stages: 
1. Pumping and extrusion. During this step, the mix must be ﬂuid enough to ﬂow 
through the pump and exit smoothly from the nozzle avoiding segregation, phase 
separation or blockages [70]. To ease this phase, the yield stress and viscosity 
of the material should be maintained at minimum, to limit the stresses inside the 
extruder equipment and the pressure. 2. Placement and early-stage. Here the material must maintain its shape developing 
sufﬁcient early strength to support the succeeding layers without excessive defor-
mations. To ensure such a behavior, the yield stress and viscosity should reach 
a minimum level, and this contrasts with the previous phase where lower values

122
F. Faleschini et al. of these parameters are preferred. When deposited, the material to be printed 
must show a homogeneous surface quality without visible cracks, tears, splits, or 
pores [71]. To deal with these issues, ensuring a balance between the nozzle travel 
speed and extrusion rate is essential [72, 73]. Indeed, an excessive extrusion rate 
can lead to issues such as compression stresses on the ﬁlament near the nozzle; at 
this regime, the extruded material expands around the nozzle and localized buck-
ling may occur [74]. Instead, high travel speed leads to tensile stresses inside the 
ﬁlament, and the printed ﬁlament may show tearing or cracking. Other aspects 
of great importance deal with the nozzle, in terms of dimension, shape, height, 
and movement path: all these parameters affect the printing quality. When the 
nozzle opening is excessively small, friction increases between the nozzle and the 
extruded material, which generates tangential stresses on the ﬁlaments, causing 
tears and deformations. Furtherly, a small nozzle is more prone to face blockage 
[75]. The nozzle shape also affects intercepting shock, which is typically higher 
in rectangular dies due to the presence of corners [72, 75]. 3. Hardening. This stage occurs when the material hardens and reaches

its ﬁnal 
mechanical properties. The deposited material needs to be hard enough to support 
the increasing load of consequently deposited layers. To ensure fast production and structural stability, the mixture rheology must be 
carefully optimized to satisfy the requirements of each of the above steps. For any 
printing material, a good balance between ﬂuidity for pumping and structural stability 
should be achieved by mixture optimization: this applies to earth, too. When dealing 
with such a material, it should be recalled that the strength evolution over time 
is highly non-linear. Without stabilizers, drying is the only mechanism that allows 
for strength development, and due to its lengthy this may represent a hard limit. To deal with it, fast-setting stabilizers, e.g. alginate, are often added to allow rapid 
strength evolution. According to Perrot et al. [19], earth with alginate needs 0.1 h 
to develop sufﬁcient strength to sustain the weight of 1 m wall, whereas the same 
strength without alginate is reached in more than 24 h. Apart from strength develop-
ment, elastic properties evolution is also important to avoid the structure collapse by 
buckling. Indeed, slender printed structures may collapse due to self-buckling [76], 
this phenomenon is linked to insufﬁcient elastic modulus proﬁle along the printed 
element. As for the strength, the evolution of elastic modulus can be accelerated by 
adding proper stabilizers. Anyhow, when the setting time is not enough, cold joints 
between extruded layers may appear, this indicates that the time interval between 
each successive deposit was too long to ensure good adhesion between the layers. Hence, a compromise should be attained in terms of fresh open time and buildability 
to ensure good adhesion between layers and stability. 7.3.2.2
Properties 
Earth is a unique material for AM in construction being recyclable and climate 
friendly, with minimal carbon impact and potential passive thermal performance

7
Earth as a Building Material: From Traditional Building Techniques …
123
[77]. Generally, the mechanical properties of earth-based mixtures depend on several 
factors, i.e. soil properties, moisture content, presence and quality of ﬁbers, work-
manship expertise. Compressive strength in printed earth elements is comparable to 
that of traditional ones, however the nozzle feature can affect ﬁnal properties. In the 
case of circular nozzles, some voids might remain among layers due to the circular 
cross section of the extruded material, whereas a rectangular nozzle allows fulﬁlling 
the cross-section [78]. Perrot  et  al  . [19] experienced a compressive strength of about 
1.2 MPa for earth blocks with circular nozzle, whereas the same blocks printed with 
rectangular section die exhibited compressive strength of about 1.7 MPa. Compres-
sive strength often has a close relationship with bulk density, as higher density values 
result in higher compressive strength. A relationship can also be

observed between density and conductivity, but in 
this case walls with lower density result in better thermal insulation. Gomaa et al. [79] performed thermal conductivity tests on 3D printed walls at 1:4 scale with four 
possible wall-section variations. Results demonstrate that the thermal conductivity 
of 3D oriented earth walls can reach 0.32 W/mK, which is lower than most conven-
tional brick and masonry walls. Such a good insulating performance is due to the 
incorporated inner voids in the wall-section. Concerning environmental impacts, 3D printing for monolithic constructions and 
prefabrication has lower energy consumption compared to traditional techniques 
[80]. Further, if earth is used as printing material the global warming potential is 
even lower than for concrete printing [81]. The feasibility of the use of 3D printed earth to act as load-bearing walls in low-
rise constructions was also proved by previous works [82], offering a correlation 
between 3D cross section design and the overall design of the building. 7.3.2.3
Fibers in 3D Printing of Earth-Based Materials 
Differently from earth for traditional building techniques, 3D printable mixtures 
usually have a high-water content, i.e. about 20–25%, and a reduced ﬁber content 
to reduce viscosity and ease the extrusion, thus resulting in high shrinkage during 
the drying process with possible extensive cracking [13], and low thermal properties 
[83]. However, ﬁber addition in 3D printing should be considered as a viable solution 
to improve ﬂexural strength and ductility while also increasing thermal resistivity 
and lightness, contributing to construction economy and transportation viability [84]. Looking at traditional earth constructions, ﬁber reinforcement has demonstrated to 
be effective to give stability for shape and water retention [85]. Most ﬁbers used in 
traditional earth constructions are derived from plants, and the ﬁber types may vary 
notably according to local availability and vegetation [84]. Fibers may also limit 
the lateral strains under compression behaving as a diffuse conﬁnement to increase 
the energy absorption capacity, tensile strength, ductility and toughness [86, 87]. Furtherly, the thermal resistivity increases for higher ﬁber content and length that 
may grant thermal comfort indoor [83, 84, 88]. 124
F.

Faleschini et al. Gomaa et al. [79] proposed a 3D printable earth mixture with straw at 2% by 
weight, reaching values of thermal conductivity comparable to those of traditional 
cob. Beyond the ﬁber content of 2% by weight, the authors highlighted extru-
sion problems due to clogging and increased viscosity [79]. In a second work, 
the same authors [20] optimized the mixture slightly adjusting the water content 
but maintaining a straw content at 2% by weight. Lately, Binega Yemesegen [89] 
conﬁrmed through literature review that printable earth mixtures should contain 72– 
73% subsoil, 25–26% water and 2% ﬁber by weight. However, Faleschini et al. [65] proposed several mixtures with comparable mechanical properties employing 
jute, coconut ﬁber, sisal, and goat hair at 0.5% ﬁber content by weight, optimizing 
the printability without affecting mechanical properties. Recently, Carcassi et al. [84] presented 3D printable earth-mixtures maximizing natural ﬁber content from 
commonplace “cob” with 2% ﬁbers by weight to “light ﬁber clay” containing 13% 
ﬁbers. 7.3.3 
Standards on Additive Manufacturing 
for Constructions 
Additive manufacturing has emerged as a revolutionary tool in the construction 
industry with the potential to signiﬁcantly enhance sustainability in the building 
industry and speed up construction processes [90]. Although this technology holds 
huge potential in the future of construction industry, the number of codes and stan-
dards considering the use of 3D printing in construction is currently few. For 3D 
printing to be widely adopted in construction, there should be compliance with 
existing codes and standards, which are designed for traditional building methods. This highlights the importance of developing new codes for 3D printing to aid in 
transforming traditional building materials into printable ones. This transformation 
is crucial for moving from formwork-based technologies to more automated additive 
manufacturing approaches [91]. Future standards for 3D printing in construction should address several key aspects 
to ensure its efﬁcient application. These include guidelines for geometric complexity, 
ensuring the safety and reliability of complex structures enabled by 3D printing. Stan-
dards should specify the printed object size, minimum number of layers, and proce-
dures for compressive and ﬂexural strength tests for mechanical properties testing. Additionally, guidelines for integrating steel bar reinforcement within printed layers 
are necessary to enhance structural load-carrying capacity, reliability and durability. Finally, speciﬁcations for nozzle size and particles dimension are crucial, as they 
signiﬁcantly impact the quality and properties of 3D printed structures [92]. As the 
ﬁeld of AM gained the attraction of researchers and continues to grow, these standards 
will likely be updated and expanded to keep pace with technological advancements 
and industry needs. 7
Earth as a Building Material: From T

raditional Building Techniques …
125
Some efforts to establish standards for 3D printing in constructions are underway, 
with notable initiatives such as those by the ﬁrm Winsun. They worked with China’s 
national construction department to update traditional building codes to adapt stan-
dards to 3D printing technologies [93]. Recently, ISO/ASTM 52939:2023 was 
published, this document sets the requirements for additively manufactured structural 
and infrastructure elements to ensure safety and efﬁciency in 3D printing construc-
tion industry. The purpose of this document is to outline the essential requirements 
for the production and delivery of high-quality additively manufactured structures, 
whether for residential or infrastructure purposes, in the construction sector. Indeed, 
important steps are deﬁned which will be controlled and monitored to ensure high 
quality printed constructions. However, this document is not technology-, or material-
speciﬁc, and consequently more detailed regulations should be applied depending 
on the adopted approach. Nonetheless, printed elements must be approved by a 
locally certiﬁed engineer and comply with both local and regional speciﬁcations and 
requirements. Further, ISO/ASTM 52900 and ISO/ASTM 52950:2021 are about 
general principles in AM, the former focuses on fundamentals and vocabulary, the 
latter shows an overview of data processing. 7.3.4 
Real-Scale Examples of 3D Printed Constructions 
After the development of contour-crafting [94], many 3D-printed large-scale struc-
tures were built [95]. Most of these printed buildings are made from concrete [96], but 
there are examples of earth-based 3D-printed houses which will be discussed in this 
section. In 2017 a 20 m2 pavilion was printed in 15 days at an international construc-
tion fair to show the combination of robotic construction and natural material [97]. Some key limits identiﬁed in this project regarded the maximum 3D printing height, 
here limited to 20 cm/day due to slow hardening of adobe, and also to the connec-
tions among printed walls and the other building components, such as foundation 
piles, window framing, roof structures, etc. [97]. WASP (World’s Advanced Saving 
Project) is an Italian company which is one of the pioneering companies in onsite 
3D printing with earthen materials and renowned for its innovations in 3D printing 
technology. One of their ﬁrst project is GAIA [98], a 30 m2 house made of locally 
available material soil (30% sand, 30% clay, and 40% silt), natural waste (25% rice 
husk, 40% straw-chopped rice), and 10% hydraulic lime showcasing the potential of 
3D printing in sustainable constructions (Fig. 7.4a). The other one is TECLA [99], 
printed with Crane, i.e. WASP’s large-scale 3D printer, in 2021. TECLA was formed 
of two connected dome-shaped volumes with an outer wall comprising 350 stacked 
layers of 3D-printed clay with a thickness of 12 mm (Fig. 7.4b). WASP in collabo-
ration with IAAC (Institute fo

r Advanced Architecture of Catalonia) also printed a 
wall portion 40 cm thick. The mix for this wall is the mixture of clay and rice ﬁbers 
which was used in the previous projects by WASP. The process of printing this wall 
took 40 h [100]. 126
F. Faleschini et al.

Fig. 7.4 Real-scale examples of 3D printed constructions: a GAIA prototype [98]; TECLA 
prototype [99]; Casa Covida [101]; TOVA prototype [102] 
Another example of 3D printed house is Casa Covida (Fig. 7.4c) which is in 
Colorado’s San Luis Valley. The name of the house underscores the project response 
to the COVID-19 pandemic and its impact on how people live and design spaces. This building is made of a mixture of sand, silt, clay, water, and straw, all are locally 
available. This house comprises 3 separate spaces which are for co-habitation during 
the time of pandemic [101]. Finally, TOVA (Fig. 7.4d) is the ﬁrst 3D printed house in Spain which is made of 
locally available material soil, aloe, egg white and enzymes [102]. 7.3.5 
Future Developments and Open Challenges 
Although AM in the building industry leads to several advantages, its use and 
advancement are still in their beginning and face numerous obstacles. Typically, printed surfaces are rougher than conventional cast surfaces, especially 
when a circular-section nozzle is employed. Hence, such a manufacturing process 
may provide structures which do not ﬁt with designers’ and end users’ requirements, 
as per the current technology development. Nevertheless, some stakeholders appre-
ciate the printed surface texture, especially for earth-based materials, as it gives a 
raw appearance that matches with soil. Another issue is related to the compatibility of raw materials for printing. The 
constituents, especially the soil, need to fulﬁll certain requirements to be compatible

7
Earth as a Building Material: From Traditional Building Techniques …
127
with AM in terms of printability and buildability. The greatest advantage of AM 
with earth-mixtures lies in the use of local soil, especially when the building site is in 
remote areas. Although, if the local soil is not suitable for printing, the transportation 
required to bring the desired material would increase the costs and jeopardize the 
economic and environmental beneﬁts of choosing 3D printing. The durability and 
effective use of local soils in the aforementioned projects is a topic that need to be 
still fully understood. Further, other challenges are still unsolved to implement AM with most current 
construction site layouts. The printer adaptability to work at different levels even with 
nonlinear geometry also arises issues related to the transportation and the location 
of the equipment in the building site [56]. Additionally, a continuous feeding system 
is required including material preparation, mixing, and delivery to the nozzle [103]. Hence, new construction plans should be selected, as most printing activities work in 
continuous at almost constant rate, whereas discrete activities typically distinguish 
traditional building techniques [104]. Due to brittleness and discontinuity between layers, strength-related issues arise 
when printing load-bearing elements [105]. Printed structures are expected to be 
anisotrop

ic due to the printing path which creates linearity at the same level, but 
discontinuity between subsequent layers. Further, voids may appear between ﬁla-
ments [106], creating a weaker interface where cracks are more likely to appear. Hence, the structural capacity of the hardened element strongly depends on the effec-
tiveness of the printing process. If a good bond between layers is created, the ﬁnal 
element is more prone to behave as a structural element. A good balance between 
mixture open time, machine speed rate and hardening are required, but according 
to some authors [45], the current technology might not be suitable for use in large-
scale models or buildings due to the stability and strength limitations of printed goods 
made with existing printing materials. Hardening is also a matter of paramount impor-
tance for earth-based printed materials, as it is highly inﬂuenced by environmental 
conditions, such as temperature and relative humidity. Similar to structural capacity, durability is also affected by pores volume and 
distribution. Referring to results carried out on 3D printed concrete, Zhang et al. [107] employed X-ray computed tomography (X-CT) to analyze the distribution of 
voids and the difference in the macro-behavior between 3D mix and traditionally cast 
concrete. It was highlighted that the voids in printed concrete are well-interconnected 
or even continuous within the ﬁlament, which could lead higher weaknesses based on 
freeze–thaw tests, deeper water penetration according to rapid chloride ion migration 
tests, and continuous enhancement in the early stages based on sulphate attack tests 
[107]. Similarly, interfacial defects may have an important effect on the carbonation 
depth and permeability of printed materials. Sun et al. [108] experienced higher 
chloride penetration depth and larger carbonation depth along the ﬁlament interfaces 
than that in the matrix, but the chloride ion penetration and the carbonation depth 
gradually decreases in the gravity direction. No speciﬁc indications are provided 
for earth materials, but similar considerations may apply regarding the inﬂuence 
of increased local porosity and defects at ﬁlament borders on material durability. 128
F. Faleschini et al. Furthermore, earth shrinkage may also be increased due to the higher drying speed 
in 3D printing process than in ordinary casting procedures. 7.4 
Concluding Remarks 
This chapter has reviewed the current state-of-the-art regarding the use of earth in 
construction, speciﬁcally providing some insights regarding its use as a printable 
material. Despite some real-scale applications were already realized in some well-
known projects, the current research is still limited, and many open issues need to 
be investigated. Mostly, the current research gaps are in common with concrete 3D 
printing: technological limits, normative barriers and materials optimization are the 
most important aspects on which the research needs to focus an

d advance signif-
icantly. In terms of speciﬁc problems of earth materials, instead, durability and 
shrinkage aspects are worth mentioning. 3D printing of earth and soils may represent one of the most sustainable alter-
natives to current construction methods in the next future, as it adopts natural and 
locally-available materials, with minimum environmental impacts. It can even work 
synergistically with other traditional construction methods. This makes 3D printing 
of earth very attractive also for those regions in the world that are less accessible to the 
concrete market, paving the way for a soft-transition to an automatized construction 
industry that valorizes ancient construction materials and methods in new valuable 
processes. References

Chapter 8 
Clay-Based Plasters: Back to the Future 
Paulina Faria 
Abstract The use of clay-based mortars is almost as old as mankind and they 
continue to be applied nowadays, namely indoors as plasters. That is because the 
environmental and technological advantages of clay-based plastering mortars clearly 
surpass their barriers and drawbacks. Selected aspects of the formulation, applica-
tion, protection and use of clay-based plasters are presented and discussed here based 
on recent research. Ways of further increasing its environmental and technological 
performance are highlighted, with a view towards facilitating a broader use. 8.1 
Introduction 
Most probably, the ﬁrst building product Mankind produced was a mortar, made by 
mixing local earth with water. It was used to ﬁll the space between branches to build 
shelter and protect from rain, wind and sun. With time, requirements concerning 
buildings increased and, much later, mortars started to be used to layer masonry 
units and coat externally (by renders) and internally (by plasters) [1] different types 
of building walls and ceilings. Renders and plasters can be applied on the substrate 
in one or more layer and their main function is to protect the masonry or monolithic 
walls and ceilings they coat. As before, the ﬁrst mortar used to plaster walls and ceilings was clay-based as it 
could be made with a local, available, unexpensive material: earth, excavated from 
the local soil, composed by different fraction and types of clay, silt and sand, after the 
removal of coarser particles. To produce the plastering mortar, the earth was mixed 
with water. Therefore, in clay plasters (also called earth plasters), the sand acts as 
aggregate, the silt as ﬁller and the clay as binder. In terms of materials, the binder is the main change in comparison to other common 
plastering mortars, known by the name of their binder, namely cement plasters, lime 
plasters and gypsum plasters: while the others are produced after thermal treatment, 
the clay is used as it is in nature. P. Faria (B) 
Department of Civil Engineering, CERIS, NOVA University Lisbon, Caparica, Portugal 
e-mail: paulina.faria@fct.unl.pt 
© The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025 
D. A. H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series 
in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_8 
135

136
P. Faria
(a)
(b) 
Fig. 8.1 Old wall coated with a clay plaster with ﬁbres, ﬁnished with a limewash, partially removed 
(a) and new wall coated with another clay plaster, produced with a different earth and without 
supplementary ﬁnishing (b) 
Therefore, for millennia, people produced and applied clay plasters as they were 
the more affordable and available. Frequently the plasters were limewashed not 
to show they were clay plasters, and also to provide a whitish surface and mor

e 
luminous indoor space, together with some durability to an eventual contact with 
water (Fig. 8.1a). Nowadays, clay plasters are technically recognized mainly due to the contribu-
tion to comfort they can provide but also to their low environmental impact and very 
positive life cycle assessment [2, 3]. Therefore, some people concerned with the envi-
ronment and healthy buildings require them for their buildings. Others also require 
this type of plasters because the natural building products are nowadays fashionable. Mainly in the latter case, it is often a request that the clay plasters are not painted 
(Fig. 8.1b). Therefore, it is important to know the potentiality of clay-based plasters and how 
they can fulﬁl buildings and users´ requirements. For that, the formulation of clay 
plasters is important. 8.2 
Clay Plasters Formulation and Main Requirements 
Clay plasters are commonly produced with one layer of a clay-based mortar (also 
called earth mortar), made by mixing a sieved earth, composed by clay, silt and sand 
particles, eventually with supplementary sand or other aggregate, with water. In turn, 
clay-based mortars can be produced locally, with local earth, or sold pre-dosed. In 
this latter case they are commonly produced with an earth local to the producing 
company, the earth and eventually additions (supplementary sand or similar size 
aggregate, natural ﬁbres) are dosed by precise mass, the dry materials are packed 
and transported to a commercial company or directly to the building site. In the 
pre-dosed clay plasters producing company, the formulation can be better controlled 
than when the composition is formulated in situ. In big working sites with many

8
Clay-Based Plasters: Back to the Future
137
square meters to be plastered, some producing companies have movable equipment 
and can also prepare the pre-dosed mortars on site. In the building site, both the local 
materials used to formulate the mortar or the pre-dosed materials are mixed with 
water and applied, manually or by mechanical projection. The projection equipment 
used for clay-based plasters is similar to the one used to apply gypsum plasters. Commonly, they are regularized with a 2 m ruler and then the surface is ﬁnished with 
a trowel or a sponge. Each earth is composed by several different types and fractions of clay, silt, 
sand and coarser aggregates. As mentioned before, the clay fraction acts as binder 
providing important properties to the plaster [4, 5], namely controlled drying 
shrinkage, good adhesion to the wall, good cohesion between the particles, adequate 
mechanical performance, but also good hygroscopicity and a naturally pigmented 
colour (Fig. 8.2). The clay fraction of an earth is commonly composed by one main type of clay 
minerals, while other clay minerals can also co-exist. In literature clay plasters with 
the kaolinitic [4, 6] and illitic [4, 7] clay minerals are common; with montmorilonitic 
clay minerals it is not recommended b

ecause of high shrinkage [4]. Additional and very important properties are also being studied on clay plasters, 
such as performance in case of ﬁre [8, 9] and their ability to capture pollutants, and 
release them when ventilation is provided [10–12], further contributing to safety and 
indoor air quality. There are no speciﬁc international standards for clay plastering mortars. The 
European standard for rendering and plastering mortars is the EN 998-1 [1], that
Fig. 8.2 Samples of clay plasters with diversity of colours

138
P. Faria
deﬁnes 0.4 N/mm2 as the minimum compressive strength these mortars should have. This standard refers to the EN 1015 (composed by several parts) standards to deﬁne 
how to produce and test, for different properties, plaster samples. However, the mortar 
production, curing and some test methods are much more appropriated for cement-
based mortars and not so much for clay-based ones. However, since 2013 there is 
a German national standard speciﬁc for clay plastering mortars: DIN 18947 [13], 
deﬁning speciﬁc requirements, labelling and test methods, such as the adsorption 
and the dry abrasion tests, while other methods are based on some EN 1015 parts, 
such as the mortars production in a laboratory [14], ﬂow table consistence [15], 
bulk density of the fresh [16] and hardened [17] mortars, ﬂexural and compressive 
strengths [18] and adhesion to a substrate [19]. To produce clay-based plastering mortars, the earth is crushed to roughly disag-
gregate the different fractions and sieved to remove the coarser aggregates, bigger 
than sand. In case the volumetric proportions clay-silt-sand in the raw earth are 
considered adequate, that means that the clay plastering mortar are adhesive and 
does not crack after drying when coating a substrate. If the earth is too clayish, to 
avoid drying shrinkage and cracking when applied on a wall, supplementary sand 
or other aggregate with similar particle size distribution or natural ﬁbres should be 
added, before mixing with the water content necessary to provide workability [20]. For that, the volume of added aggregate or ﬁbres should be optimized: increased 
added volumes of extra aggregate or/and ﬁbres are tested on small plaster samples 
on a substrate, and in general the minimum volume that reduces visible shrinkage is 
adopted (Fig. 8.3). The reason why the volume of additional aggregate to include in the mortar formu-
lation is commonly minimized is because it reduces the adhesion to the substrate and 
also because frequently it is a sand that has to be speciﬁcally extracted, while the earth 
used to produce the mortar can be a local construction and demolition waste. There-
fore, that optimizes the plaster performance and minimizes environmental impact 
and costs. However, some recycled aggregates with similar to sand particle size can 
be used instead of natural sand, as well as ﬁbres. Natural ﬁbres, resulting from agriculture wastes, can also be added to the clay 
plaste

ring mortar formulation. Depending on the type (for instance thin like typha 
ﬁbres, or thicker as oat straw), size and volume of ﬁbres added, different properties 
can be changed [21], namely drying shrinkage, density, ﬂexural and compressive 
strength. After application on a wall or ceiling, the plastering mortars just dries, without any 
chemical reaction. Particularly when the plastering mortars contains plant ﬁbres, the 
initial drying should have good ventilation; otherwise, biological colonization can 
occur during the initial drying as it takes long. The clay minerals and the hardening just by drying allows clay plasters to have 
a high hygroscopicity when compared to other plasters and other indoor coatings 
[22, 23], acting as passive moisture buffers. Furthermore, they are reversible, easily 
repairable with a humid sponge and reusable just by mixing the dry mortar again 
with water. However, contact with water used indoors or, by accident, entering by an 
open window when it rains may damage the clay plasters. 8
Clay-Based Plasters: Back to the Future
139
Fig. 8.3 From down to up, examples of testing increased volume and type of aggregate added to 
two different clay mortars, to deﬁne the minimum content that controls cracking
While the contribution of vegetal ﬁbres and other biomass can be important to 
increase the hygroscopicity of other plasters, namely gypsum-based [24, 25], the 
inﬂuence they may have on the highly hygroscopic clay plasters is not signiﬁcant. However, the addition of vegetal ﬁbres can also contribute to decrease the mortars 
life cycle analysis, due to the contribution of the plant to CO2 sequestration, and also 
to provide a more natural aesthetics, as ﬁbres are seen in the surface of unpainted 
clay plasters (Fig. 8.1b). To provide some durability when in contact with water, when that is a requirement 
for the plaster, different architectural protections and ﬁnishings can be design and 
applied on speciﬁc areas, to avoid being a barrier to the use of clay plasters [26]. In 
general, the contact of plasters with water only occurs by accident (cleaning with 
water, a window left open when it rains), except when they are applied close to taps 
and showers. Of course, on those areas speciﬁc protection, formulation or, more 
commonly, a water resistant coating should be applied, such as ceramic tiling. Reactive additions can be added in the mortar formulation, although that may turn 
the plaster not reversible and reusable; only recyclable. However, both the ﬁnishings 
and the reactive additions [27] should not reduce signiﬁcatively the moisture buffer 
capacity of the clay plasters. 140
P. Faria
8.3 
Clay Plasters Reactive Additions 
The main reason to add reactive addition to clay plasters formulation is to provide 
some durability when in contact with water. However, plasters, applied indoors, may 
not need that type of durability. Therefore, eventual additions should not increase 
too much the environmental imp

act of the clay plasters, keep their high adsorption 
and the reversibility/reusability of the mortars. In different parts of the world vernacular additions were added to clay plasters, and 
particularly to clay-based renders, to provide that durability. An example is the addi-
tion of cow dung, that includes small ﬁbres together with compounds resulting from 
the animal digestion [28–30]. This addition does not implicate bad smell or toxicity 
because it is added after drying. Furthermore, it allows reducing the supplementary 
sand or other aggregates added because it also contains natural ﬁbres, already cut 
by the animal. Also, it does not reduce the previously mentioned advantages of clay 
plasters: adsorption, low embodied energy and reusability. Finally, it improves the 
resistance when in contact with water for mortars with different types of clays and 
cow dung type and preparation. However, the intensity of the effect may change with 
the type of earth and processing of the cow dung [31, 32]. Another vernacular example 
is the addition of cactus mucilage and many other natural materials, namely biopoly-
mers [33] and linseed oil [26]. Many other materials are being tested [34] and some 
can be agro-industry wastes. Therefore, the biostabilization is nowadays an important 
line of research, supported by the work of RILEM Technical Committee 318—BEC, 
Bio-stabilised earth-based construction: performance-approach for better resilience 
[35]. The main advantages of these bio-based additions are that they do not signiﬁcantly 
reduce the moisture buffer capacity of the plasters and the reversibility and reusability 
of the mortars, while they may not increase signiﬁcantly its embodied energy and, 
therefore, environmental impact. Another advantage is that in general they do not 
change much the natural colour of the clay plaster. Another type of reactive additions are mineral binders. Remembering that the 
main function of plasters is to protect the substrates where they are applied, physic-
mechanical and chemical compatibility is required. Therefore, although clay-based 
plasters should fulﬁl requirements deﬁned in the EN 998-1 [1], namely a minimal 
compressive strength of 0.4 N/mm2, high strength and low water vapour permeability 
mortars are not required. As for other plasters, on corners or other areas more prone 
to mechanical impacts, protections can be applied [26]. Furthermore, past literature 
has shown the durable use of air lime and gypsum-based binders in historic clay-
based building elements [36, 37]. So, these are the more appropriate binders when the 
choice is to add a binder to a clay plastering mortar, and other mineral binders, such 
as cement, should be avoided. To avoid increasing too much the embodied energy of 
binder-added clay plasters, recycled lime and gypsum may be used. However, although the resistance to water increases, adding low content of air 
lime may decrease the mechanical performance of the clay mortars

[6, 38], and 
adding a high content turns the mortar into a lime-based mortar and no longer a

8
Clay-Based Plasters: Back to the Future
141
Fig. 8.4 From right to left, 
slight colour change of a 
dark chocolate coloured clay 
plaster, depending on 
supplementary sand added, 
and impacting colour 
changes when adding low 
contents of air lime and 
natural hydraulic lime 
clay-based one. Furthermore, the addition of a low content of air lime promotes a 
higher reduction of moisture buffering capacity in comparison to the same addition 
of hemihydrate gypsum, while the latter promotes a slight increase in mechanical 
properties [26]. Therefore, it seems that, if the choice is to add a mineral binder, 
hemihydrate gypsum may be the best choice [39]. However, the addition of both 
binders turns the clay plaster no longer reversible and reusable, as it turns into an 
artiﬁcial stone, only recyclable. Furthermore, adding mineral binders to naturally 
coloured earth will most probably signiﬁcantly change the plaster natural colour, 
which should also be taken in consideration before the decision (Fig. 8.4). 8.4 
Clay Plasters Surface Protection 
Another alternative to increase durability of clay plasters, not only to water, is to apply 
a surface ﬁnishing or protective treatment. These systems can be applied by different 
ways, such as by brush, roller sponge or spray. Surface ﬁnishings can be varnish 
or paint systems. However, these systems may signiﬁcantly reduce the moisture 
buffering capacity of the clay plasters [40]. In the case of varnishes, they do not change 
the colour but the texture (Fig. 8.5); in the case of common paints or limewash, they 
also change the colour. The advantage of the limewash, in comparison to common 
paints, is that the moisture buffering reduction is very low [26]. Clay-based paints 
can also be applied. They are produced with coloured clay with very low particle 
size [41]. Surface protective treatments can be obtained by the application of different mate-
rials, also by brush, roller or spray, namely some of the ones that can be added in the 
formulation of the clay plasters, such as linseed oil or cactus mucilage, and others 
such as beeswax, cellulose water or other bio-based products [42, 43]. Some do not

142
P. Faria
Fig. 8.5 Decorative clay 
plaster with a varnish 
ﬁnishing in a toilet of a 
restaurant in Lisbon, 
Portugal (Credits Vera 
Schmidberger)
form a surface ﬁlm, impregnating the external millimetres of the plasters and consoli-
dating the surface; others, such as beeswax, form a ﬁlm and reduce more signiﬁcantly 
the moisture buffer capacity. Some slightly change the colour while with others the 
colour change is more visible. The effect on both water adsorption and damage by 
water dripping also change with different treatments. The same with the increase of 
environmental impact, although in general is not highly affected when using natural 
materials. 8.5 
Final Remarks 
Although earth as a buildin

g material has many applications, one of the oldest is to 
produce clay-based mortars. The structural use of earthen walls has many advantages 
but some limitations, namely in high rise buildings due to the high mass of those 
walls. However, the use of earth to produce plastering mortars does not seem to have 
limitations, as those plasters can be applied on many different walls. Those walls 
can be made with vernacular, traditional and innovative materials and products, 
such as masonries with adobe, compressed earth blocks, ceramic bricks, concrete 
blocks and straw bales, monolithic rammed earth and cob walls, and even 3D printed 
walls. Therefore, clay plasters are compatible both with new, vernacular and historic 
building walls, and can be both applied as ﬁrst plaster and as renovation plaster. They 
can even be applied as sacriﬁcial plasters to protect archaeological structures. The advantages of clay plasters are broad. In past times the main issue was to have 
availability and low cost of the material to produce the plastering mortars. Nowadays

8
Clay-Based Plasters: Back to the Future
143
many other requirements are added to the previous. The low environmental impact 
can be kept, once the addition of mineral binders with high embodied energy is not 
recommended nor needed. As plasters, applied indoors, do not have contact with 
rain, the clay plasters can be left visible without any treatment or protection. Alter-
natively, some protective ﬁnishing can be applied on the surface, or low content of 
low embodied energy materials can be added to the formulation. Bio-based materials, 
namely resulting from agro-industrial wastes, can be a possibility. The choices should 
depend on the requirements deﬁned for the plasters on different areas of different 
buildings. In general, it is important that the surface protection or the additions to 
the formulation do not reduce the moisture buffering capacity, which in turn may 
also provide some pollutants capture, and a signiﬁcative contribution to comfort and 
environmental indoor quality, the reversibility, facilitating small repairs and changes 
in texture if required, and the reusability of the mortar at the end of its life cycle. References

Chapter 9 
Rammed Earth in Modern Construction: 
Physical and Mechanical Properties 
Fernando Ávila, Esther Puertas, Álvaro Blanca-Hoyos, and Rafael Gallego 
Abstract Rammed earth is a traditional construction technique widely used all 
over the world, and which is currently experiencing a resurgence of attention as 
an eco-friendly building solution. Nevertheless, the general absence of national and 
international standards based on the structural comprehension of such constructions 
poses a challenge for architects and builders looking to incorporate this technique 
into modern building projects. In this context, the present chapter develops a thor-
ough analysis of the state-of-the-art of the knowledge regarding the mechanical and 
physical properties of rammed earth, modern testing techniques applied to this tradi-
tional material and main results, values of consensus, and uncertainties. However, 
the mechanical properties of unstabilized rammed earth are sometimes not enough to 
meet the requirements of existing building codes, so stabilization and reinforcement 
techniques are applied to enhance its mechanical behavior. The second part of the 
chapter focuses, therefore, on the stabilizing and reinforcing techniques for rammed 
earth. 9.1 
Introduction and Historical Background 
Rammed earth (RE) traditional construction technique is experiencing a signiﬁ-
cant revival as a sustainable and eco-friendly building solution. Used historically 
across several cultures and geographic regions, this construction method involves 
compacting layers of moist earth between temporary formwork to create walls. Today, as the construction industry increasingly prioritizes environmental sustain-
ability, RE is gaining renewed interest for its minimal environmental impact, use of 
local materials, and potential for reducing the carbon footprint of buildings. F. Ávila (B) 
Department of Continuum Mechanics and Theory of Structures, Polytechnic University of 
Valencia, Valencia, Spain 
e-mail: favila@mes.upv.es 
E. Puertas · Á. Blanca-Hoyos · R. Gallego 
Department of Structural Mechanics and Hydraulic Engineering, University of Granada, Granada, 
Spain 
© The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025 
D. A. H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series 
in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_9 
147

148
F. Ávila et al. From ancient times, humans have employed earth as a construction material. Its low cost, versatility, and favorable mechanical and insulating properties have 
made it an excellent choice throughout history [1, 2]. Cultures across the globe have 
devised various building techniques using earth as the primary material, adapting 
these metho

ds to local conditions and improving construction practices. Many exam-
ples of earth-built structures from different civilizations have survived to the present 
day. The earliest examples of earth architecture preserved today can be found in the 
Near and Middle East. Some of the oldest structures have been discovered in the 
Turkestan Region (Kazakhstan), where archaeological sites dating from 8000 to 
6000 BCE reveal houses made with adobe [3]. Regarding rammed earth, some of 
the oldest examples in the same zone are the building foundations found in the area 
corresponding to the ancient Assyrian civilization, dating back to around 5000 BCE 
[1]. In the African continent we can also ﬁnd some of the oldest examples of earthen 
constructions. In Upper and Middle Egypt, some earth block constructions have been 
estimated to be over 4000 years old. Notable examples, such as the fortiﬁcation of 
Medinet Habu and the Temple of Ramses II at Gourna, still stand today. In Sub-
Saharan Africa, numerous examples of centuries-old earth construction techniques 
are present and still in use, sustained by the oral transmission of knowledge. The 
widespread use of earthen construction across the African continent is primarily due 
to its excellent thermal performance, which keeps interiors cool during the day and 
warm at night, as well as its low cost and high potential for material reuse [4, 5]. In Europe, with some examples of earth construction (earth-ﬁlled timber-framed 
houses and wattle-and-daub building technique) date from the Bronze Age, but the 
greatest expansion of rammed earth construction was between the 15th and the nine-
teenth centuries, mainly in Central Europe [1]. Earlier examples (built from between 
the thirteenth and sixteenth centuries) are found in Southern Spain, including some 
UNESCO heritage sites like the Alhambra of Granada (Fig. 9.1a, the Royal Alcazars 
of Seville, or the Desmochada Tower, part of the Almohad enclosure of the city of 
Caceres [6]. The case of America, particularly Central and South America, is unique due to 
its historical context. Pre-Columbian America had a rich tradition of earth construc-
tion, which later blended signiﬁcantly with techniques introduced by the Spaniards 
after 1492 [8]. Notable pre-Hispanic examples include the Sacred City of Caral-
Supe in Peru (3000 - 1800 BCE), the North Platform of Monte Albán in Mexico 
(ﬁrst century BE) or the burial chambers of Tierradentro in Colombia (sixth - tenth 
century). Numerous historic urban centers in Latin America, such as Potosí (Bolivia), 
Quito (Ecuador), Antigua Guatemala (Guatemala), Cartagena de Indias (Colombia), 
Valparaíso (Chile), Puebla (Mexico) or Old Havana (Cuba), showcase a mixture of 
indigenous and Spanish earth construction techniques, featuring notable adobe and 
rammed earth structures [6, 9]. Today, earthen construction is gaining renewed attention due to two fundamental 
characteristics. Firstly, its reduced cost makes it an ideal

solution for countries and 
regions with limited resources, and secondly, its low environmental impact aligns

with the imperative shift towards more sustainable construction practices. These 
factors position earthen construction as a crucial ally in achieving the UN Sustainable 
Development Goals [10] and as a compelling alternative to conventional building 
materials, thus promoting environmental sustainability in the construction industry. In fact, both earth construction in general and rammed earth construction in particular 
have seen increasing relevance in contemporary building practices, with numerous 
modern constructions employing these techniques in recent years (Fig. 9.1b). However, and despite its beneﬁts, the widespread adoption of RE in contemporary 
construction faces substantial challenges. One of the primary obstacles is the lack of 
comprehensive national and international standards tailored to the structural prop-
erties and performance of rammed earth constructions. This gap in standardization 
presents difﬁculties for designers and builders who seek to integrate this traditional 
technique into modern projects while ensuring compliance with current building 
codes and regulations. The present chapter aims to address this gap by providing an in-depth analysis 
of the current knowledge on the mechanical and physical properties of rammed 
earth. It reviews modern testing techniques applied to this material, highlighting key 
results, consensus values, and existing uncertainties. Understanding these properties 
is crucial for developing reliable structural assessments and for advancing the appli-
cation of rammed earth in contemporary buildings. There are several cases, however, 
where unstabilized rammed earth (URE) does not meet the mechanical performance 
requirements set by the existing building codes. To overcome this limitation, stabi-
lization and reinforcement techniques are employed to enhance its strength and dura-
bility [11]. This topic is addressed in the second part of this chapter, evaluating the 
existing techniques and their beneﬁts and drawbacks. 150
F.

Ávila et al. 9.2 
Characterization of the Material Properties 
The resurgence of interest in rammed earth as a sustainable building material in 
recent years, has prompted extensive research into its mechanical and physical prop-
erties. The mechanical behavior of RE, including its compressive, tensile, and shear 
strengths, is critical for evaluating its performance in modern construction. By exam-
ining the results from existing literature, this section aims to establish consensus 
values while addressing the uncertainties that still persist in the ﬁeld. Beyond its mechanical properties, the section also explores the thermal and 
acoustic insulation capabilities of rammed earth, which are crucial for ensuring user 
comfort in buildings. The durability and degradation of RE are also discussed, empha-
sizing the importance of protecting these structures from environmental factors to 
prolong their lifespan. 9.2.1 
Compressive Behavior 
Rammed earth structural elements are typically designed to work primarily under 
compression. Consequently, their unconﬁned compressive strength (UCS) has 
become the most common parameter for deﬁning their mechanical performance, 
similarly to other quasi-brittle building materials, such as concrete. The procedure 
commonly used to obtain the UCS of rammed earth materials involves conducting 
uniaxial compression tests. In the absence of speciﬁc standards for testing the UCS 
of rammed earth samples, researchers have adapted ASTM standards like D1633 
[12] or D2166 [13], designed for the evaluation of the compressive strength of soil– 
cement cylinders and cohesive soil, respectively, or proposed alternative procedures 
based on existing standards for cement mortars and masonry design rules [14–16]. Although the variability in UCS results for RE in the literature is partly due to the 
material inherent heterogeneity, establishing a standardized test procedure would be 
essential for making results from different studies truly comparable. It is widely recognized that UCS of RE is affected by the geometrical charac-
teristics of the specimens (size and shape) and the manufacturing procedures and 
conditions (moisture content and compaction energy). However, the exact relation-
ship between these parameters and the mechanical properties of rammed earth is 
still not fully known. Previous studies [15] have observed signiﬁcant variations in 
the compressive strength of RE samples with different shapes and sizes made from 
the same material. In this case, the reduction in the UCS for the larger samples was 
attributed to variations in the material grading, but other authors [17] evaluating spec-
imens of different scales have also found that UCS for smaller samples was higher 
than for larger ones. This underscores the need to approach the results of compres-
sion strength tests on conventional (small) RE specimens with caution, as they may 
not directly correspond to the mechanical capacity of full-scale walls. 9
Ramme

d Earth in Modern Construction: Physical and Mechanical …
151
Regarding the shape of the samples, studies in the literature have reported 
substantial differences in UCS results for prismatic versus cylindrical samples [15, 
18]. One possible explanation is that the friction between the earth and the form-
work during ramming is greater in prismatic specimens, particularly at the corners, 
allowing cylindrical specimens to be more effectively compacted and therefore 
exhibit better mechanical performance. Additionally, differences in load distribu-
tion patterns between prismatic and cylindrical specimens may also contribute to 
the observed variations in UCS results [19]. The procedures and results for some of 
the main studies regarding the compressive behavior of URE using prismatic and 
cylindrical samples are reported in Tables 9.1 and 9.2, respectively. Another key aspect for the compressive strength of RE materials is the compaction 
energy applied to the soil during manufacturing, in fact, it is well-known that 
increasing the material density tends to result in higher UCS. However, it is very 
uncommon that existing studies measure or report the manufacturing compaction 
energy. In this regard, a recent study [16] proposed a new manufacturing method-
ology based on the widely used Proctor test, including the use of a standardized 
Proctor mold and the control of the compaction energy. The results showed a very 
signiﬁcant reduction of the dispersion of the UCS of the samples (coefﬁcient of 
variation lower than 2%) with respect to other studies in literature about RE. Table 9.1 Results from uniaxial compression tests of URE cylindric samples in literature

Regarding the elastic modulus (E) of the RE materials, the dispersion in the results 
is even more evident, with values from less than 100 MPa to more than 1000 MPa. This considerable variability is related to the intrinsic heterogeneity of the materials, 
but also to the signiﬁcant differences in the manufacturing and testing procedures, as 
observed for the UCS. Additionally, the studies in literature show differing deﬁnitions 
of elastic modulus that contribute to this dispersion. Some authors [23, 24] propose 
the calculation of a secant stiffness modulus in the elastic region of the compressive 
stress–strain curve of the material, following the expression indicated in the standard 
ASTM C469 [39]: 
E = (σ2 − σ1)/(ε2 − ε 1)
(9.1)
where ε1 is a longitudinal strain equal to 5 × 10–5 , σ1 is the stress corresponding 
to the longitudinal strain ε1, σ2 is the stress corresponding to 40% of the ultimate 
load and ε2 is the strain produced by σ2. As  Eq  . (9.1) was designed for concrete 
samples, other authors have proposed modiﬁcation to adapt this expression to the 
elastic behavior of RE, considering σ1 equal to 35% maximum stress and σ2 equal 
to 75% of the maximum stress, with ε1 and ε2 as their corresponding strains [32]. However, this is not the only option to address the stiffness of RE, and other 
authors prefer using the tangent modulus, deﬁned as the slope of the stress–strain 
curve in its elastic area [21, 25, 40, 41]. In addition, some studies [28, 42] calculate 
the secant modulus as the ratio between the maximum stress and its corresponding

strain, and in one investigation [43] a cyclic triaxial test was used to deﬁne the elastic 
modulus. There is a general agreement that there is a relationship between the compressive 
strength of RE and its elastic modulus, but the exact correlation between these two 
parameters is still unclear. The Australian Earth Building Handbook [44] recom-
mends the use of an elastic modulus of 500 MPa for all RE materials, but according 
to the results in literature, this value seems to be too high for URE samples. Another 
well-known standard, NZS 4297 [45], indicates that E can be calculated as 300·fc ,

fc

where UCS1 is the lowest UCS value of the series and CVUCS is the coefﬁcient of 
variation of the UCS series. Beyond these recommendations, as it can be observed 
in Fig. 9.2, the majority of the existing studies about URE obtain an E-value lower 
than 400 times the compressive strength and, in many cases, the elastic modulus is 
under 200 MPa regardless of the UCS of the material. 9.2.2 
Tensile and Shear Behavior 
Rammed earth elements are generally designed to work

mostly under compressive 
loads, as they are known to have very low tensile and shear strength. However, these

154
F. Ávila et al. two parameters have proven to be essential to deﬁne (and prevent) failure of RE 
structures subjected to extreme loads, such as an earthquake [30, 46]. According to the results in literature [24, 30, 37], the tensile strength of RE is equal 
to ca. 10% of the compressive strength of the material. This relationship, obtained by 
means of pull-off and Brazilian tests, results into approximate tensile strength values 
between 0.10 and 0.35 MPa, in agreement with other studies in literature [19]. Similar values, with some few exceptions, are obtained for the shear strength, as 
shown in Table 9.3. Although some authors [23, 47] have performed direct shear 
tests to characterize the shear strength of RE, the most common procedure is to 
calculate it indirectly carrying out diagonal compression tests (DCT) [16, 26, 36, 
37] on relatively large samples, in most cases following the procedure stablished in 
ASTM E519 [48] for masonry assemblies. The shear strength of RE samples, highly inﬂuenced by its moisture content [51, 
52], is directly related to the cohesion and friction angle of the material. Applying 
the Mohr–Coulomb theory, these parameters can be obtained in laboratory through 
shear box tests and triaxial tests [49, 53], or calibrated through numerical models 
[16, 30, 37, 38]. As it can be observed in Table 9.3, values of cohesion are typically 
in the range between 50 and 200 kPa, while the friction angle has values from 37° 
to 50°, excluding some very few exceptions. Table 9.3 Characterization of the shear behavior of URE in literature
