9.2.3 
Hygrothermal and Acoustic Properties 
Ensuring the thermal and acoustic comfort inside a building is essential in any archi-
tectural project. These factors are related to the general conﬁguration and situation 
of the building, but also to insulating properties of the materials used. The main parameters to measure the thermal properties are the thermal conduc-
tivity and the thermal resistance. In t

he case of unstabilized rammed earth, it has been 
observed that the compacted material has a thermal conductivity ranging from 1.0 to 
1.4 W/(m K) [7, 19], which is very similar to that of brick masonry and better than 
the one measured in concrete or stone. The value measured for RE, nevertheless, is 
highly dependent on the material density and moisture content [1, 7, 54, 55]. Not only the conductivity of the material itself should be considered, however, but 
also the thickness of the structural element. For this purpose, the thermal resistance is 
deﬁned as the ratio between the thickness of the element and its thermal conductivity. Therefore, the thermal resistance of a 50-cm thick wall would be of around 0.4 m2K/ 
W, quite far from the minimum value of 1.3 m2K/W indicated in some earth building 
standards [44]. This issue could be addressed by adding certain polymers or phase-
change materials to the soil, or by installing thermal insulating panels attached to the 
walls, similar to how conventional walls are commonly insulated [56–58]. Additionally, RE walls have the capacity to buffer temperature variations inside the 
building, due to their thermal inertia, reducing thermal amplitude by more than 70% 
[57, 59, 60]. This property, together with the ability of RE to balance air humidity 
(signiﬁcantly reducing the relative humidity ﬂuctuations) [1, 60, 61], has a very 
positive impact on the thermal comfort. Acoustic insulation, on the other hand, is commonly measured evaluating the 
decibel reduction provided by a certain structural element, so it depends on the 
material and the thickness of the element. This acoustic insulating capacity is typi-
cally represented by the sound reduction index [62] or the sound transmission class 
(STC) [63, 64]. Most studies regarding URE show values of both parameters of about 
57 dB [19], proving a good acoustic performance of the material (for reference, a 
STC of 60 dB means that ampliﬁed sounds are hardly audible [65]). In the absence 
of direct tests to measure the sound reduction index, an approximate value can be 
obtained using the expression indicated in BS 8233 [66]: 
R = 21.65 log10(ρ · t) − 2 .3
(9.3)
where R is the sound reduction index in decibels, ρ is the material density in kg/m3 
and t is the thickness of the wall in meters. In addition, rammed earth, due to its porosity, can reduce sound reverberation 
inside the building [67, 68], although further studies about this topic would be 
required to correctly assess this property. 156
F. Ávila et al. 9.2.4 
Durability and Degradation 
The sensitivity of rammed earth to the weathering action is a relevant matter of 
concern for designers, building companies and users. Unprotected RE walls are 
potentially vulnerable to rain and wind erosion, according to the degradation observed 
in several existing structures and the laboratory tests carried out in different studies. These tests include accelerated erosion and wire brush tests [40, 69–71]

, performed 
according to ASTM D559 [72] (designed for compacted soil–cement samples), water 
erosion tests [73] (according to NZS 4298 [74]), and rainfall simulation and drip tests 
[75]. Based on the results of these studies, cement stabilization can help reduce erosion 
damage to RE materials. However, it is generally recommended to externally protect 
RE elements, whether stabilized or not, using waterprooﬁng paints and plasters. Additionally, reducing direct exposure to rain and wind through the use of sloping 
roof systems is advised [75–77]. External protection and stabilization are also useful to enhance the durability of 
RE under aggressive environments. A signiﬁcant reduction of URE mechanical prop-
erties has been observed when exposed to sulfate, alkaline and acid environments, 
which can be minimized if cement is added as a stabilizer to the mixture [76, 78]. However, some precautions should be taken when using cement to avoid secondary 
alkali-aggregate reactions and sulfate induced swelling induced by the presence of 
the stabilizer [69]. Another factor that can lead to the degradation of rammed earth materials is the 
exposure to dry–wet cycles and frost-defrost cycles. Although further research on 
this topic is still needed, some studies [73] suggest that the inclusion of cement 
and the presence of gravel in the soil can signiﬁcantly enhance the ability of RE to 
withstand frost-defrost cycles without substantial degradation. 9.3 
Stabilization and Reinforcement 
When employing the RE technique in new constructions, its mechanical performance 
frequently does not meet the standards deﬁned by building codes. To enhance these 
mechanical properties, as well as improve its thermal and acoustic behavior or dura-
bility, various additives can be added to the soil, resulting in what is known as stabi-
lized rammed earth (SRE). These stabilizers improve the performance of rammed 
earth through physical and chemical interactions with the soil particles and water 
present in the mixture. Some of these additives, such as lime or natural ﬁbers, have 
been used since ancient times, while others, like cement, sub-products from other 
industries (e.g. coal combustion residues), artiﬁcial ﬁbers, and advanced materials, 
have been introduced more recently but are having a high impact today. The use of stabilizers in rammed earth is becoming increasingly common, signif-
icantly enhancing its mechanical properties and allowing for its application in a

9
Rammed Earth in Modern Construction: Physical and Mechanical …
157
wider range of construction projects. However, if additives are used systematically 
and without careful consideration of the speciﬁc requirements that URE cannot fulﬁll, 
there is a risk that RE constructions could lose some of their most valuable charac-
teristics that make this construction technique particularly attractive: low cost and 
minimal environmental impact. 9.3.1 
Additives and Stabilizers 
Additives and stabilizer

s can be classiﬁed based on whether their use in the stabi-
lization of RE is through chemical reactions (such as cement, lime, and gypsum) or, 
in contrast, based on their shapes (natural and synthetic ﬁbers). 9.3.1.1
Cement, Lime and Other Chemical Stabilizers 
These stabilizers are used to chemically enhance the mechanical properties of RE and 
strengthen its matrix. The most commonly used stabilizer is Portland cement, as it 
allows to achieve the highest compressive strength and durability [11, 31]. Soils with 
a lower percentage of clay are more suitable for cement stabilization since higher 
clay contents are more likely to exhibit more signiﬁcant shrinkage [52]. Additionally, 
there are other requirements that the soils used for cement stabilization should meet, 
such as not containing organic material. According to several studies, the unconﬁned compressive strength improves as a 
higher percentage of cement is added, as does the tensile strength [11, 24]. Further-
more, durability parameters such as erosion resistance are also improved [76, 78]. Another advantage, in terms of technical and legal certainty, is that cement stabi-
lization is considered in most modern rammed earth standards in countries such as 
Australia, New Zealand, and the United States, making it easier for designers and 
builders to use cement in RE constructions. However, despite its mechanical advantages and the improved durability provided, 
cement stabilization could compromise the sustainability beneﬁts of RE. The prox-
imity of resources (potential use of local soils) and the positive impact on local 
economies are affected by using cement as a stabilizer. Cement stabilization also 
impacts the environmental cost of RE by increasing the global warming potential 
over 100 years by 70%, while increasing by 35% the acidiﬁcation potential of land 
and water compared to soil without cement stabilization [69]. The low cost of this 
building material could also be compromised, as, for example, 10% cement-stabilized 
RE cost per unit weight can be between two and four times the one of URE [22, 23]. Hence, the use of natural stabilizers has gained growing interest with the aim of 
improving the mechanical and durability properties of RE without compromising its 
environmental and economic beneﬁts. Among these, lime stabilization is the most 
commonly used, due to its historically use to stabilize earthen materials in general 
and RE in particular, both as an additive to the mixture or as an external protection

158
F. Ávila et al. [32]. Historical heritage sites made of lime-stabilized rammed earth (LSRE) can be 
found in several regions, such as the Alhambra of Granada (Spain), the Fujian Tulou 
(China) or numerous traditional houses in Southern Portugal or France [23, 79–82]. Lime stabilization results from the hydration reaction of lime, which increases the 
concentration of Ca2 + and OH − [31, 32]. This reaction improves the mechanical 
performance and durability by

reducing the porosity of RE, which is closely related 
to the UCS [83]. Lime is not only used as part of the soil and water mixture, but it 
is also sometimes applied at the wall surface to reduce the effects of rain and wind 
erosion. However, using cement or lime as surface plasters can affect the hydric 
behavior of RE, causing disadvantages related to increased porosity when applied 
after the initial weight stabilization of RE instead of being part of the initial mixture 
[80]. To reduce the footprint and environmental costs of cement and increase the bene-
ﬁts of lime stabilization, the combination of these two stabilizers with subproducts 
such as ﬂy or bottom ash, recycled concrete aggregates or brick waste material has 
been studied by some authors [41, 61, 84]. The use of these materials combined 
with cement can provide similar improvements, halving the percentage of cement 
needed to achieve the mechanical requirements established by building standards 
[61]. However, the stabilization of RE materials always increase their environmental 
impact, in terms of carbon emissions and embodied energy, among others, as shown 
in Table 9.4. Table 9.4 Carbon emissions and embodied energy per cubic meter of RE

9.3.1.2
Natural and Synthetic Fibers 
To enhance the mechanical properties of rammed earth, particularly those where it 
fails to meet construction standards, such as tensile and shear strength, additives 
that enhance the material properties through their physical shape, rather than their 
chemical properties, are often used. These additives are typically presented in the 
form of ﬁbers, and their use for RE enhancement is more accurately referred to 
as reinforcement (or additivation) rather than stabilization, although the term “ﬁber 
stabilization” is not rare in literature [11, 42]. Fiber reinforcement can be performed by adding short pieces of ﬁber directly to 
the earth mixture, in which case the ﬁbers act as an additive, or by using ﬁbers in the 
form of fabric for internal or external structural reinforcement [87–89]. This section 
focuses on ﬁbers used as an additive to the RE mixture, while internal or external 
reinforcement using ﬁbers will be covered in the following section. Fibers used as additives in RE mixtures can be classiﬁed as natural or synthetic 
based on their origin. Examples of natural ﬁ

bers include cereal straw, palm tree ﬁbers, 
leaf ﬁbers, and sheep wool [11, 90]. Recent research has also explored the use of 
synthetic ﬁbers, such as waste tire ﬁbers, ﬁberglass and diverse plastic ﬁbers [21, 
24]. While synthetic ﬁbers offer better control over dimensions and characteristics, 
natural ﬁbers maintain the low environmental cost of RE, which is one of its primary 
advantages. Further research is steel needed to properly evaluate the effect of the 
diverse types of natural and synthetic ﬁbers on the mechanical performance of RE 
materials. 9.3.2 
Reinforcement Techniques 
The use of ﬁbers as short pieces can impact the workability of rammed earth in 
terms of compactability, especially when a high percentage of ﬁbers is added. Addi-
tionally, mixing the material with ﬁbers can be challenging due to their presence. To address these issues, ﬁber reinforcement has also been explored as internal or 
external reinforcement. In these cases, instead of individual pieces, a grid of ﬁbers 
is placed internally and externally to enhance the tensile and ﬂexural strength of RE. As an alternative to fabrics, bars made from various materials have been used to 
reinforce RE. The main aim of these bars is to enhance the ﬂexural strength of RE 
and are sometimes employed to reinforce critical points such as corners, joints, or 
wall openings. Similar to ﬁbers, bars can be made of natural materials (e.g. wood 
and bamboo) or from more modern materials like steel [91], which have been the 
subject of recent studies investigating their impact on RE. 160
F. Ávila et al. 9.3.2.1
Internal Reinforcement 
When the ﬁber grid or fabric is placed between layers of rammed earth (Fig. 9.3a), 
it is considered internal ﬁber reinforcement. This fabric is embedded within the RE 
matrix to enhance ﬂexural strength and strain energy dissipation. Variables such as 
the diameter of the ﬁbers and the spacing between them are crucial for assessing 
ﬂexural strength [88]. However, similar to using short ﬁber pieces, the presence of interlayer fabric can 
affect the compactability of the material. Another signiﬁcant challenge with this rein-
forcement technique is the limited knowledge about the effect of a fabric in the inter-
layer on the isotropy of the RE element. Flexural strength tests of internally reinforced 
RE have shown that the layer above the fabric could be expelled with crack propaga-
tion [88]. Further research is needed to understand potential negative effects, partic-
ularly on compressive strength, to avoid compromising some mechanical properties 
while enhancing others. Given that RE is often compared to concrete as a quasi-brittle material, reinforcing 
RE walls with bars (Fig. 9.3b) to improve ﬂexural strength is a logical research focus. However, including bars in RE presents concerns about ensuring a proper and uniform 
compaction to the mixture [93]. The inclusion of bars can hinder the compaction of 
the material nearby due to the difﬁculty in

using the rammer close to the bars, as 
vibration techniques used for concrete are obviously not applicable for RE. Hence, 
the bond performance between the earth mixture and the bars is a critical factor, as 
the enhancement of ﬂexural and tensile strength depends on the effective interaction 
between these elements. This kind of internal reinforcement technique can be implemented with steel 
bars, like conventional reinforced concrete, which may include ribs for better grip. Alternatively, natural wooden or bamboo bars can be used with the aim of maintain the 
ecological beneﬁts of RE and avoid some steel-related issues such as bar corrosion. (a)
(b) 
Fig. 9.3 RE with internal reinforcement: specimen with interlayer synthetic fabric [88]  (a)  and  
representation of a wall with internal insulation and steel bars [92]  (b )

9
Rammed Earth in Modern Construction: Physical and Mechanical …
161
9.3.2.2
External Reinforcement 
To avoid issues resulting from poor compaction and increasing the anisotropy of RE 
elements caused by internal reinforcement with bars or fabrics, recent research has 
explored alternative external reinforcement techniques. These methods involving the 
application of external reinforcements are often carried out after the RE elements 
have reached their maximum strength [80, 88], frequently with the aim of increasing 
the structural performance of an existing building that does not meet the current 
standards or that has been damaged (for instance due to a seismic event). While internal reinforcement techniques can compromise the workability and 
compactability of the mixture, external reinforcement techniques face the challenge 
of ensuring that the RE structure and the reinforcement work effectively together. After the RE wall has been built, the reinforcement should be applied with materials 
and procedures that ensure strong adhesion to the RE surface, making it work as a 
single composite structure. This is particularly relevant for external fabric reinforcements (Fig. 9.4), which 
can be made from natural or synthetic materials, as it happened with the ﬁbers used in 
internal reinforcement. The selection and application of the material used to bond the 
fabric to the RE surface presents its own challenges, as differences in moisture content 
between the RE and the added material could affect the hygroscopic characteristics 
of the earthen structure. Alternatively, wire mesh can be used in a similar way to 
fabric, typically placed at both sides of the RE element and attached to the it by wire 
connectors linking both sides [36, 94]. Despite these challenges, external reinforcement with fabric has proven useful 
according to several studies, particularly for reinforcing heritage buildings made of 
rammed earth to improve their seismic performance and prevent collapses in previ-
ously damaged areas [89]. Unlike internal reinforcement, which must be incorporated 
during construction, external reinforcement allows for the

enhancement of critical 
structural points with superﬁcial treatments, preventing collapse mechanisms such 
as wall overturning, which are common in historical buildings subjected to seismic 
forces [89, 91]. (a)
(b) 
Fig. 9.4 Jute fabric (a) and application as an external reinforcement for a RE arch (b)  [87]

162
F. Ávila et al. Other solutions to enhance their structural and seismic performance of RE struc-
tures are the inclusion of vertical rigid elements (columns) and horizontal conﬁning 
elements. Steel and concrete columns can be incorporated to the RE structure during 
construction to increase the loading capacity and the seismic behavior of the assembly 
[95]. Cast-in-situ or precast reinforced concrete columns can be also combined with 
horizontal reinforcements (concrete tie beams or wire ties) to increase energy dissi-
pation and bearing capacity [96]. Concrete bond beams, commonly placed at the top 
or half-height of walls, have proven useful to prevent wall overturning and reduce 
out-of-plane drift, enhancing the seismic behavior of RE buildings. Another reinforcement method involves installing external wooden elements to act 
as a conﬁning structure for the rammed earth building. These reinforcements help the 
walls work monolithically, minimize maximum displacements during seismic events, 
and enhance the energy dissipation capacity of the structure [79, 94]. The wooden 
elements should be positioned on both sides of the wall and securely connected 
with steel rods passing through [94]. This technique is especially beneﬁcial for the 
rehabilitation of existing RE buildings. Acknowledgements This research is part of the projectEvaluacióndelavulnerabilidadestructural

Chapter 10 
Raw Rammed Earth Construction 
in Contemporary Contexts 
Joseph Dahmen, Carene Umubyeyi, Karissa Wenger, and John Ochsendorf 
Abstract Replacing energy-intensive building materials with low carbon sustain-
able alternatives is a key aspect of addressing global climate change. The built envi-
ronment produces approximately 37% of anthropogenic carbon emissions (Building 
Materials and the Climate in Constructing a New Future, United Nations Environ-
ment Programme and Yale Center for Ecosystems + Architecture, 2024). A signif-
icant share of these emissions is due to fossil fuels consumed to manufacture and 
distribute building materials. The need for low carbon materials has led to a renewed 
interest in traditional earthen construction methods. These inherently circular mate-
rials provided humans with shelters for thousands for years prior to the emergence of 
energy-intensive materials. Recent estimates indicate that approximately 8–10% of 
the global population live in some form of earthen housing (Marsh and Kulshreshtha 
in Build Res Inf 50:485, 2022), which range from provisional shelters to architec-
turally signiﬁcant buildings. Rammed earth is one form of earth construction, in 
which soil is compacted in layers into formwork to produce monolithic walls. Tradi-
tional rammed earth construction practices relied on naturally-occurring clays in soil 
for adhesion, a method known as “unstabilized” or “raw” rammed earth. This method 
was used to construct buildings that have lasted for hundreds of years. Cement stabi-
lizers are often added to rammed earth to adapt its performance to contemporary 
standards, a method known as “stabilized rammed earth.” Although stabilizers can

in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_10 
167

168
J. Dahmen et al. improve strength and durability, they do so at an environmental cost. This chapter 
discusses the environmental advantages and durability considerations governing the 
use of raw rammed earth construction in contemporary architectural contexts. 10.1 
Earthen Construction Overview 
Buildings account for as much as 37% of global anthropogenic greenhouse gas 
(GHG) emissions [1]. A signiﬁcant subset of these emissions, known as embodied 
energy, is due to manufacturing building materials and construction activity. Embodied energy accounts for 8–10% of global GHG emissions. Replacing energy 
intensive construction materials with environmentally sustainable low-carbon alter-
natives will be a key part of achieving ambitious GHG reduction targets laid out 
by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) and others. Traditional 
building practices such as earthen construction offer valuable insights in this regard. Earthen dwellings have provided comfortable shelter to humans for thousands of 
years, long before the widespread availability of non-renewable fossil fuels and 
energy-intensive supply chains. The legacy of this approach is still evident in the 
approximately 650–700 million people, approximately 9% of the global population, 
who live in some form of earthen housing [2]. Earthen buildings range from simple 
shelters to architecturally signiﬁcant buildings represented on the World Heritage 
List, such as the famous Alhambra in Granada, Spain shown in Fig. 10.1. [3, 4]. Rammed earth construction, in which monolithic walls are produced by compacting 
multiple layers of loose soil into formwork, is among the oldest earth construc-
tion technologies, extending back approximately 10,000 years [5, 6]. Traditionally, 
builders selected soils that contained sufﬁcient amounts of naturally-occurring clays 
to provide the necessary adhesion of soil particles required for the technique [7]. More recently, ordinary Portland cement (hereafter referred to as ‘cement’) and 
other chemical binders are added to soil mixtures to provide higher design strengths 
and ensure predictable performance. However, these techniques add to the overall 
environmental impacts of rammed earth. The urgent need for low embodied energy 
materials to reduce climate change impacts gives traditional raw earth techniques 
ne

w relevance in contemporary contexts. This chapter explores the use of rammed 
earth as a low-impact material in contemporary architecture. 10.2 
Stabilized Versus Raw Rammed Earth 
A growing number of contemporary builders have adopted rammed earth and 
compressed earth block construction in Europe, Australia, New Zealand and North 
America [8], responding to the demand for sustainable construction materials and 
methods. Contemporary architectural design using rammed earth (Figs. 10.2, 10.3)  is  
documented by [9, 10]. These efforts are supported by a growing body of knowledge

10
Raw Rammed Earth Construction in Contemporary Contexts
169
Fig. 10.1 Unstabilized rammed earth has been used to construct buildings such as the Alhambra 
in Spain that have lasted for hundreds of years. Photo by Jebulon, Image available for use under 
the CC BY 4.0 license: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alhambra#/media/File:Dawn_Charles_V_P 
alace_Alhambra_Granada_Andalus
about rammed earth construction methods and performance. Normative guidelines 
for rammed earth applications in contemporary construction contexts include [8, 11, 
12]. Technical guidance is offered by [13–17]. Rammed earth methods and materials 
have been the topic of international conferences convened in the past decade [18]. Commercial activities and academic research point to the potential of rammed earth 
as a sustainable construction method for contemporary contexts, highlighting the 
beneﬁcial effects of a minimally processed material whose thermal mass can reduce 
heating and cooling loads when designed appropriately. However, the majority of the 
building codes and technical advice guiding the use of rammed earth in industrialized 
regions mandate the incorporation of cement stabilizers, driven by a desire to bring 
its structural performance and durability into compliance with contemporary expec-
tations. Research shows that adding cement stabilizers to rammed earth increases 
its compressive strength [19]. Stabilization may also reduce initial surface erosion 
due to precipitation [20], although compressive strength does not correlate precisely 
with erosion resistance. Rather, erosion resistance is a factor of material consolida-
tion due to compaction [7]. What is clear is that the incorporation of stabilizers drives 
up the embodied energy of rammed earth substantially, raising questions about its 
appropriateness as a material for sustainable architecture. Embodied energy (EE) refers to the total amount of energy required to produce a 
product, including raw material acquisition, manufacturing, transportation and instal-
lation. As a measure of the environmental impact of materials, it correlates closely to

170
J. Dahmen et al.

Fig. 10.2 Contemporary design using rammed earth—windhover Contemplative centre at Stanford 
university in Palo Alto, California (2015). Architect: Aidlin darling design. Builder: rammed earth 
works. Photo © Matthew Millman. Used with permission
CO2 emissions. Each kilogram of cement requires approximately 4.5 MJ of energy 
per ton to produce [21], releasing approximately 0.9 kg of CO2 into the atmosphere 
[22]. Cement stabilizers account for the greatest share of the EE of stabilized rammed 
earth due to the energy content of cement, which substantially outweighs other energy 
inputs [23]. Both the EE and climate impacts of stabilized rammed earth vary linearly 
with stabilizer use [24], which inﬂuences overall environmental more than the energy 
required to compact soils [19, 24, 25]. The incorporation of cement stabilizers in 
rammed earth reduces its environmental beneﬁts, but by how much? 10.3 
Assessing the Environmental Costs of Stabilization 
Nine research projects characterizing the EE of rammed earth construction are 
summarized in Table 10.1. The different studies have been converted into MJ/m3 
of ﬁnished rammed earth for the purpose of comparison. Based on these results, a 
table of likely ranges of EE for stabilized and raw (unstabilized) rammed earth have 
been derived. Unlike building products manufactured in highly controlled facili-
ties, rammed earth is typically constructed in situ, making consistent comparisons 
across projects challenging due to the differences in construction practices across 
individual building sites. However, the available data can be used to estimate the

embodied and operating impacts over the lifetime of the building, from raw material

limited in scope to what is known as a “cradle to gate” approach. This approach 
accounts for all environmental impacts incurred up to the point that the product 
leaves the manufacturing facility, corresponding to stages A1–A3 below. Because 
rammed earth is produced at the building site in most (though not all) cases, cradle to 
gate accounting of rammed earth ends with the material installed at the building site. The different life cycle stages from material acquisition to construction, are outlined

• A2: Transportation of raw material to the manufacturing facility 
Energy required to transport soil, amendments and stabilizers to the building

None if the walls are produced in situ; can be a factor for rammed earth 
prefabricated offsite. Since most rammed earth is produced at the building site, cradle to gate studies 
comparing rammed earth to other materials such as site-cast concrete must include 
the energy required by these materials at stages A4 (transport to construction site) 
and A5 (assembly and installation). 10.3.1 
Method of Comparison 
The results of the nine research projects consulted were converted to EE per 
cubic meter [MJ/m3] where necessary to enable clear comparison. The following 
assumptions were used when converting:
• Density of RE assumed to be 1900 kg/m3 except where otherwise noted
• Density of concrete is assumed to be 2400 kg/m3
• Energy has been converted to primary energy 
The results have been listed in Table 10.1. Cement stabilized variants are listed at 
the top. Lime stabilized blends, which are ﬁred at a lower temperature than cement 
and require less energy to produce, are listed in the middle. Results for raw rammed 
earth are listed at the bottom. The research source and date of the research results 
are provided. Within each group, EE results are arranged from low to high. The life cycle assessment framework requires a functional unit, deﬁned as a quan-
tity of a product required to provide a speciﬁc service, in order to compare building 
materials [26]. Thermal mass is a common rationale for specifying rammed earth 
in buildings, so a functional unit of 1 m2 of wall of delivering a delivering a heat

176
J. Dahmen et al. capacity of 500 kJ/K is used to enable comparison. Rammed earth has a speciﬁc 
heat capacity of approximately 850 J/kg K [27], comparable to that of concrete. One 
square meter of rammed earth at a thickness of 300 mm provides 500 kJ/K of heat 
capacity, compared to a concrete wall 250 mm thick providing the same performance 
due to its slightly greater density. In practice however,

raw rammed earth is often 
constructed at a thickness of 450 mm to provide larger bearing area due to its lower 
compressive strength. This produces more thermal mass than is required for many 
buildings. Accordingly, Table 10.1 thus compares the EE/m2 of wall area of a 300 mm 
thick stabilized rammed earth wall to a 450 mm thick raw rammed earth wall. Both 
of these walls are compared to a 250 mm thick concrete wall for reference. The 
EE of the concrete wall is provided using data taken from cradle-to-gate life cycle 
analysis data (stages A1–A3) provided by [21], with 15% additional EE reported to 
account for energy incurred during stages A4 (transporting ready-mix concrete to 
the building site), and A5 (setting formwork, lifting, placing materials, and stripping 
forms once the concrete has cured) [28]. Reinforcing steel has not been considered 
in the rammed earth or reference walls. Five studies provide information on the EE of raw rammed earth construction. Disregarding the values reported at either extreme, which were due to manual produc-
tion at the low end [23], and unusually high transportation of soils at the high end 
[27], suggests a range of approximately 125–1200 MJ/M3 for raw rammed earth. In 
contrast, the seven studies reporting EE for stabilized rammed earth range between 
400 and 1577 MJ/m3. The lowest results in this range were reported where manual 
labour was used for excavation, mixing, placement and compaction. However, these 
ﬁndings are less relevant to industrialized regions, where these activities are generally 
accomplished with the assistance of machinery powered by electricity or distillate 
fuels. For industrialized regions, it is likely that stabilized rammed earth ranges 
between 1200 and 1600 MJ/m3. Table 10.2 provides aggregated ranges for stabilized and raw rammed earth. The 
ranges were determined by a qualitative analysis of the data reported in Table 10.1, 
and assumes mechanical extraction, transportation, processing and ramming. The 
wall thicknesses are the same as those indicated in Table 10.1, and include a concrete 
reference case for comparison. Whether rammed earth can be expected to land at the 
higher or lower end of the range provided depends on particular soil requirements, 
whether soil amendments such as additional sand or clay are required, and whether 
stabilizers are required. The aggregated ranges in Table 10.2 assume that the soil 
is supplied at the building site, with excavation energy considered except in the 
most minimal case, where it is assumed the soil used is provided by the foundation 
excavation, and can thus be considered an externality of that process. The ﬁnal column of Table 10.2 compares the impacts of different types of rammed 
earth walls with foundations included. Rammed earth is typically constructed on a 
continuous concrete foundation at the same thickness as the wall above to protect it 
from moisture below grade. This requires large quantities of concrete belo

w grade, 
particularly in the case of raw rammed earth walls, which are nearly twice the thick-
ness of concrete walls, and often thicker than stabilized rammed earth walls. The 
impacts of the concrete used in foundations are often overlooked when assessing

10
Raw Rammed Earth Construction in Contemporary Contexts
177
the impacts of rammed earth. The ﬁnal column thus assumes a rammed earth wall 
constructed to a height of 3 m above grade, resting on a concrete foundation of 
the same thickness as the wall above, constructed to a depth of 1 m below grade. Reinforcing steel has not been included in the analysis of either the wall or the 
foundation. Limitations 
The data reported above has several important limitations. EE is only one of six major 
impacts measured in an LCA study, which include impacts to human health, resource 
depletion, and other categories. Limiting the scope to EE may leave out important 
aspects of comparison. For example, while EE correlates closely with CO2 equivalent 
emissions in most cases, it does not account for the CO2 emissions caused during 
the production of cement due to the chemical reaction that occurs during calcination. This reaction, which releases approximately half of the CO2 attributable to cement, is 
not reﬂected in the EE totals provided. This would signiﬁcantly increase the climate 
impacts of cement stabilized rammed earth. It should also be noted that the cradle to 
gate approach employed is an effective means of limiting the scope of investigation, 
but it does not account for a number of potential beneﬁts of rammed earth during the 
operational and end of life phases of the building. These include potential reductions 
to operating energy consumption during the occupied phase of the building, indoor air 
quality beneﬁts caused by hygrothermal regulation, and ease of recycling and reuse 
during building demolition. One ﬁnal consideration is that in practice, rammed earth 
builders sometimes add more stabilizers than required by mix design speciﬁcations 
to ensure that design strengths are met. Most mix designs are provided by weight, 
which is often converted to volume on the building site using approximate means. The 
risk of structural failure leads builders to err on the side of excessive use of cement 
stabilizers. Interviews conducted with North American builders suggested that actual 
rammed earth stabilization rates of 12–14% were not uncommon there, exceeding the 
intended speciﬁcation by as much as 40–50% [29]. Overuse of stabilizers, whether 
intentional or not, can result in a stabilized rammed earth wall whose EE signiﬁcantly 
exceeds that of a concrete wall serving a comparable purpose. 10.4 
Strength of Raw Earth Construction 
The impacts of cement stabilizers indicated in Tables 10.2 and 10.3, and the existence 
of many historic rammed earth buildings constructed without them, raises the ques-
tion of how these traditional techniques could be adapted to contemporary contexts. Raw r

ammed earth construction requires special considerations, which starts with 
the selection of the soil, and more speciﬁcally, the clay constituent. Broadly, clays 
can be categorized as either expansive (typically montmorillonite) or nonexpansive 
(kaolinite or illite) [30]. Expansive clays have properties that enable them to incor-
porate water molecules in their microscopic structure, resulting in visible volumetric 
changes at the macroscopic scale [31]. The higher the content of expansive clays

present in the wall, the more shrinkage and swelling can be expected in the earthen 
material. Shrinkage cracks also facilitate water inﬁltration into earthen walls during 
subsequent wetting events [32]. To avoid excessive deterioration due to expansion 
and shrinkage, contemporary raw earth construction guidelines stipulate that only 
non-expansive clays be used as binders in the construction of rammed earth walls 
and that the overall clay content falls within a range of 15–30% by weight [8, 11]. The strength of raw rammed earth is a function of the following interrelated 
factors: type and percentage of clay, the soil type and gradation, optimal moisture 
content, and compaction energy applied. Soil is a highly variable resource, and a full 
discussion is beyond the scope of this chapter. However, numerous references exist 
that outline the use of natural soils to produce raw rammed earth walls, such as [8, 11, 
13, 33]. Geotechnical evaluation of site soils is often advisable when developing mix 
designs and determining optimal moisture content, as well as creating samples for 
testing. With proper selection of soils and adequate compaction at optimal moisture 
levels, raw earth can be expected to achieved unconﬁned compressive strengths 
of approximately 1–2 MPa [34]. As compared to 2–9 MPa for stabilized rammed 
earth, depending on the amount of cement stabilization and compaction density 
[35]. The tensile strength of unreinforced rammed earth (whether stabilized or not) 
is approximately 10% of the compressive strength [34] which can be considered 
negligible. The strength of raw earth is derived from bonds between clay platelets. The 
ingress of moisture weakens these bonds, with clays reverting to plastic behaviour. This makes it essential to protect structural walls constructed of raw rammed earth 
from moisture due to precipitation or rising damp to retain design strengths [36]. It is clear from the data indicated above that the compressive strength of raw 
rammed earth is low by the standards of most conventional construction materials. However, the relatively low strength of raw rammed earth walls can be accommo-
dated

by designing thick walls with conservative slenderness ratios, which reduce 
compressive loading per unit area to acceptable levels. Tensile forces should be 
avoided altogether. In some cases, it may be advisable to provide a load bearing 
frame using a higher strength material in conjunction with rammed earth walls, as 
was done in the commercial building documented in [27]. Although the strength 
of unreinforced rammed earth is low, with careful design it is possible to use it 
effectively for a range of applications (Figs. 10.4 and 10.5). 10

10.5 
Durability of Raw Rammed Earth Walls 
The susceptibility of raw earth walls to weathering presents special challenges for 
contemporary construction. A full discussion of the factors involved can be found in 
[36]. The durability of a building material is a measure of its resistance to deterioration 
over time [37]. Raw earth walls are susceptible to weathering and deterioration due 
to environmental factors even when non-expansive clays are used as binders. As 
mentioned above, moisture ingress has an adverse effect on the bonds between clay 
platelets, causing a loss of compressive strength. The loss of mechanical strength also 
causes erosion at the wall face. This can be due to water inﬁltration and evaporation, 
surface erosion caused by rainfall, wind or snow, and damage caused by repeated 
freeze–thaw cycles [32], or rising damp due to capillary action from below. At the 
material scale, suction is an important element in the strength development of raw 
rammed earth [6]. As soils dry and suction increases, strength and internal cohesion 
between soil particles also increases. However, this increase has limits. Toll and 
Ong [38] have demonstrated that strength contributions from suction in a sandy clay 
decrease as the level of saturation increases. A typical exterior rammed earth wall in 
temperate climates has an estimated moisture content of 3–10% due to the relative

180
J. Dahmen et al. Fig. 10.5 Unreinforced earth in contemporary architecture: Atelier AHA Männedorf, Switzerland, 
(2022). Architecture: atelier.krecl GmbH: Lehm Ton Erde Baukunst GmbH Martin Rauch. Photo 
© Bruno Helbling. Used with permission
humidity absorbed. Since cohesion decreases in earthen materials with increases in 
internal water content, connections can be made between environmental conditions 
resulting in prolonged moisture ingress and consequent weathering and deterioration. The evaporation of water can also cause deterioration in rammed earth walls. As water 
evaporates from the soil, it carries dissolved ions with it, which migrate towards the 
wall surface and form salt crystals. These salt crystals reduce cohesion between soil 
particles and

can consequently lead to weathering [39]. Entry of water into earthen walls is primarily caused by incident rainfall and 
capillary suction from the surrounding ground. In addition to water penetration, the 
angle and speed at which droplets impact a wall’s surface and the formation of 
continuous streams of water can create pitting or localized regions of deep erosion 
[40]. This is particularly common when water accumulates and drips down from roof 
overhangs and other higher surfaces [41]. Careful detailing can reduce exposure to 
these deterioration mechanisms, therefore reducing the durability demands placed on 
the earthen materials themselves. The short-and long-term durability of raw rammed 
earth walls can be evaluated by employing laboratory and in-situ testing. 10
Raw Rammed Earth Construction in Contemporary Contexts
181
10.6 
Laboratory Durability Assessments 
Laboratory tests exist to assess the durability of earthen materials. These include 
the wire brush test which consists of a cyclical procedure of water immersion, oven 
drying, and ﬁrm brushing of soil–cement earthen samples to assess material loss due 
to repeated wetting and drying [42]. Also common is the accelerated erosion test, 
which involves spraying a specimen sample with a jet of water at a speciﬁed pressure 
and determining the maximum erosion depth and extent of moisture penetration in 
the material [13, 37]. Richards [43] used this test to investigate earthen heritage 
deterioration at an archeological site in northwest China. The drip test, developed by 
Yttrup [46] attempts to simulate rain droplets on an earthen surface by releasing water 
droplets from a wet cloth wick onto an inclined specimen [40]. These tests, which 
reproduce the effects of environmental conditions in a controlled laboratory setting, 
can provide some insight into comparative durability performance of different soils. Although the tests outlined above may be helpful comparative assessments, their 
accuracy in predicting long term durability is the subject of debate. Research by 
[20, 40, 44, 45] indicates that laboratory testing often exaggerates the intensity of 
environmental factors responsible for deterioration, resulting in overly conserva-
tive performance criteria. Furthermore, the combined effects of multiple climatic 
conditions are difﬁcult to simulate accurately in the laboratory, making the tests less 
effective in forecasting long-term performance [45]. These challenges demonstrate 
the value of full-scale experimental studies of rammed earth structures subjected to 
actual climatic conditions over multiple years or decades. 10.7 
Long-Term Durability Assessments 
Several long-term durability studies have been conducted on structures utilizing 
earthen materials to date. Guettala et al. [45] carried out a 4-year study to test the dura-
bility of eight small walls constructed using stabilized earthen bricks in the region of 
Biskra, Algeria. Prior to constructing the walls, br

ick specimens containing the same 
soil mix were subjected to laboratory tests, including an ASTM D559 wire brush test, 
a spray test, and a freeze–thaw test. The design of the experiment made it possible 
to compare the performance predicted by laboratory tests to actual observed weath-
ering in the ﬁeld. Results conﬁrmed that the outcomes of accelerated weathering tests 
conducted in the laboratory were signiﬁcantly more severe than observed weathering 
produced by the climatic conditions of Biskra after the 4-year test period. Similar 
results were found in a study by [46] where the erosion rates of raw compressed 
soil blocks subjected to a laboratory spray test were compared to the erosion rate 
of a test wall constructed using the same soil blocks and exposed to natural rain 
precipitation for three years. The erosion rate was quantiﬁed by comparing volume 
of soil removed from the specimens to the measured volume of water impinged, both

182
J. Dahmen et al. in the laboratory and in the ﬁeld. Results showed the quantiﬁed ﬁeld erosion rate 
was approximately one third that of the laboratory rate suggesting that relying only 
on laboratory testing would result in overly conservative guidelines. Two research projects in investigate the long term weathering of rammed earth 
walls, providing a more accurate quantiﬁcation of the erosion of raw rammed earth 
walls in actual climatic conditions. Bui et al. [20] conducted a study on the dura-
bility of rammed earth wall specimens exposed to 20 years of natural weathering in 
France. The specimens were constructed on a concrete foundation and covered by an 
asbestos cement roof to protect them from water penetration. Two types of soil mixes 
were used, with lime stabilization added in several specimens. Erosion was quanti-
ﬁed using stereo-photogrammetry, a method that involves estimating distances using 
photographic images taken from various locations. Results showed that raw rammed 
earth walls achieved a maximum erosion of approximately 18–21 mm over the course 
of the study, which amounts to a rate of approximately 1 mm/year [20] Similar rates 
of erosion are reported in a 16 year study of erosion mechanisms of a free standing 
raw rammed earth test wall constructed on the campus of the Massachusetts Institute 
of Technology in the United States in 2005 [36] (Fig. 10.6). The research documents 
the erosion of the 450 mm thick wall and identiﬁes the environmental factors respon-
sible for the observed deterioration. While erosion levels are highly dependent on 
localized environmental factors, results show that regions of the test wall with high 
levels of environmental exposure experienced an erosion rate of 3.5 mm/year, regions 
with medium exposure have an erosion rate of 1.5 mm/year, and regions with low 
exposure have an erosion rate of 0.5 mm/year, as reported in Table 10.3. The results 
presented are for a test wall with a steel cap overhanging the wall by 3.5 cm on each 
side. These valu

es can be considered conservative, since they rely on erosion rates 
for a wall with minimal protection from precipitation that is exposed to weathering 
on both sides. Fig. 10.6 Free standing unstabilized rammed earth test wall constructed on the campus of 
the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in 2005 [36]. Photo by Joseph Dahmen. Used with 
permission. 10
Raw Rammed Earth Construction in Contemporary Contexts
183
The durability studies mentioned above are useful in quantifying the weathering of 
raw earthen walls constructed with at least minimal protection from vertical precipi-
tation. Without protection from vertical precipitation, raw rammed earth will deterio-
rate more rapidly. In contrast, providing ample roof overhangs will signiﬁcantly slow 
down erosion due to precipitation. Historic rammed earth structures provide ample 
evidence that when adequately protected, raw rammed earth can last for hundreds of 
years, suggesting that this low impact material could be used to replace more carbon 
intensive construction materials with signiﬁcant environmental beneﬁt. 10.8 
Conclusion 
There is a growing interest in rammed earth construction as a response to the climate 
crisis. Embodied energy of building materials currently accounts for 8–10% of 
global GHG emissions. Replacing energy intensive conventional construction mate-
rials with environmentally sustainable low-carbon alternatives will be a key part 
of achieving ambitious greenhouse gas reduction targets laid out by the IPCC and 
others. However, most rammed earth in industrialized regions relies on chemical 
stabilizing agents that adversely affect its environmental beneﬁts. In contrast, raw 
rammed earth, which relies on clays occurring naturally in soil for adhesion, has 
been used for millennia to construct comfortable buildings that can last for hundreds 
of years. The following guidelines may be useful when specifying raw rammed earth 
as an ecologically sound material for contemporary contexts:
• Minimize or eliminate the use of cement stabilizers
• Minimize the transportation of soils, preferably within 50 km. • Use pier and beam foundations to minimize the use of continuous thick concrete 
footings below grade. • Plan for erosion of 1–2 mm/year in temperate climates, which can be reduced or 
eliminated through detailing that protects walls from precipitation. Unstabilized rammed earth is an age-old material that has new relevance for 
sustainable construction. Soil is a nearly ubiquitous resource that requires minimal 
processing to function as a safe and effective construction material, provided that 
its unique characteristics with respect to strength and weathering are accommodated 
through careful design and detailing. References

Chapter 11 
New Technologies for Earth 
Construction: 3D Printing, a Reality 
or a Mirage? Ana Mafalda Matos, Paula Milheiro-Oliveira, and Humberto Varum 
Abstract The construction industry is on the brink of a revolutionary transforma-
tion with the advent of new technologies designed to address the pressing demands 
of sustainability, efﬁciency, and affordability. Among these innovations, 3D printing 
using earth materials stands out as a particularly intriguing development. This tech-
nique, which leverages locally sourced soil, proposes a paradigm shift from tradi-
tional construction methods. However, as with any emerging technology, it prompts 
a critical question: is 3D printing for earth construction a tangible solution to be 
adopted, or is it merely a futuristic vision that remains out of reach? This discus-
sion explores the current state of 3D printing with earth materials, examining its 
practical applications, technological advancements, and the challenges that must be 
overcome to determine whether it is a present-day reality or an elusive mirage. This 
study speciﬁcally sought to identify the key mixture variables that impact the prop-
erties of a local and Portuguese stabilised earth, potentially suitable for 3D printing, 
and to determine the appropriate ranges for further investigation. To achieve this, 
a  23 central composite design (CCD) approach was employed to develop statistical 
models capable of describing fresh properties such as slump and other engineering 
characteristics like mechanical performance, resistivity, and mass loss. As such, a 
central composite design (CCD) was followed to ﬁnd statistical models capable of 
describing those key stabilised earth mixtures properties in function of mixture input 
parameters: water to solids volume ratio (Vw/Vp); superplasticiser to cement weight 
ratio (Sp/p); cement to soil weight ratio (c/s). List of Abbreviations

11.1 
Introduction 
Earth (soils) is a raw natural building material as old as humanity, with records 
from the prehistoric period and worldwide use, which gives it a universal character. Earth was used in the construction of not only houses but also the most valuable and 
important monuments and architectural ensembles in the world. Even today, the earth 
is widely used as the main building material in regions such as Africa (particularly 
in the North), where this material does not compete economically. The tradition 
still exists in Central and Latin America and Asia, especially outside the large cities. Thus, a high percentage of the world’s population, estimated to be two billion people, 
lives or works in earthen constructions among 150 countries [1, 2]; however, with 
high variability around the world. As such, the Earth materials provides economic, 
ecological, and social beneﬁts, including health and comfort, due to their natural 
properties. The thick layers of earth elements possess high thermal inertia, which 
helps regulate relative humidity through their porous structure and clay content, 
contributing to excellent indoor air quality. Traditional techniques based on raw earth are vernacular because they use locally 
available materials on the construction site or nearby. Earth construction techniques 
are varied, from rammed earth, adobe, and cob, depending on the identity of cultures

11
New Technologies for Earth Construction: 3D Printing, a Reality …
189
and places, societal contexts and times in which they were integrated. For this reason, 
Earth Construction techniques usually present marked variations from region to 
region, and there may even be signiﬁcant differences between buildings located 
in the same areas [3]. The construction methods were based on empirical know-how 
and passed from generation to generation without any scientiﬁc tool or analysis. Those earth construction features are incompatible with the current building mate-
rials sector, particularly in Western economies, which today is based on standards,

quality control, performance and modelling. Besides, the vernacular earth construc-
tion method is slow, time-consuming and require a large and very skilled workforce 
[4]. Other construction methods employing materials such as reinforced concrete, 
timber, or masonry have advanced, while Earth construction upgrades were almost 
non-existent. Therefore, the scientiﬁc community needs to reinvent Earth Construc-
tion and revisit its potential to meet the Agenda 2030 challenges by strengthening 
conﬁdence in Earth materials, adapting it to current technologies, and unlocking its 
potential to construction stakeholders. Studying new construction methods for earth 
construction is crucial for advancing these practices, enhancing their viability, and 
addressing contemporary environmental, economic, and social challenges. One of the front’s action to upgrade earth construction techniques has been based 
on cement-based materials technology, namely the earth materials production and 
casting using the similar methods employed in concrete. Some examples can be 
seen in the following references [5–11], as concrete earth, poured Earth, ﬂowable 
Earth, and self-compacting earth, among others. This approach has demonstrated 
that plasticisers and superplasticisers commonly used in cement-based materials, 
such as polycarboxylate ethers, are compatible with Earth materials. Additionally, 
stabilising raw earth with hydraulic binders—such as lime (10–15% by soil mass), 
Portland cement, and/or blended mixtures (typically 5–10% by soil mass), or calcium 
sulfoaluminate cement—enhances the workability of Earth materials by reducing 
viscosity [5, 11–15]. It also facilitates quicker setting, improves mechanical strength, 
and increases water resistance and, consequently, durability. The rise of digital fabrication techniques, like 3D printing of cementitious mate-
rials (3DPC), has shown that advanced digital and automated production processes 
can offer signiﬁcant advantages, including creating freeform architectural designs 
without the need for formwork [21]. Numerous institutions, universities, and compa-
nies around the globe have presented digitally fabricated prototypes of structural 
elements, furniture, and full-scale demonstration buildings [22–26]. Nevertheless, 
these projects predominantly rely on cement-based materials. Combining natural or based on natural materials, such as earth, with the emerging 
digital production processes enables the integration of cutting-edge technology with 
the vernacular construction as grounds for contemporary digital innovation. In fact, 
a major driver was the 3D printing of a full-size earth-based structure in Italy by 
WASP [24], unveiling the potential of earth construction, and expanding the scope 
of 3D printing beyond cement-based materials. Very last research works of Gomaa 
et al. [22], Veliz et al. [23], Alhumayani et al. [24] showed the potential positive 
environmental beneﬁts on the 3D printing of earth-b

ased materials might have due

190
A. M. Matos et al. to the reduction of the carbon footprint related with materials extraction and process, 
transportation, and the waste generation. The advancement of a 3D-printable framework for Earth or Earth-based materials 
remains limited, with current experiments and applications still in their early devel-
opmental stages [22, 25–29]. Most of the research focuses on the design possibilities 
of 3D Printing for Earth construction. There is a need to start and provide scientiﬁc 
information rationally for the design of earth printable materials, considering typical 
soil variability and different types of soils, the fabrication machines employed, and 
construction workﬂow and processes, among others. From the material’s point of view, several issues must be stressed out. Alqenaeea 
and Memari [22] focused on 3D printable cob mixtures design. They found that 
49% clay, 24% water, 15% sand, 10% lime, and 1.5% straws achieved printability 
and buildability simultaneously and adequate mechanical properties for low-rise 
houses. Thus, mixture design study and small changes from traditional cob mix ratios 
provided a 3D printable cob mixture version manufactured using similar concrete 
mixer, pumped system, and printed by a computer-controlled industrial robotic arm 
[22]. Silva et al. [30] analysed several earth mixtures for 3D printing: raw earth mixes, 
ﬁbre-reinforced earth mixtures and stabilised (with Portland cement) ﬁbre-reinforced 
earth mixtures. The study on fresh and hardening properties, using a rheometer, 
revealed that earth mixed with 24.3% of water (wt. %) reinforced with 1% (wt. %) 
of sisal ﬁbre and stabilised with 13% (wt. %) of PC was suitable for 3D printing. Perrot et al. [27] employed a biopolymer, alginate, to reach earth printable mixture 
by improving green strength. Two earth composites with 45% water content were 
studied, and 0% and 3% of alginate. Both mixtures were printed. However, the 
construction ratio was higher with alginate due to the fast increase in green strength. Compressive tests in printed samples with the circular cross-section showed similar 
results for both earth mixes with and without alginate, around 1.20 MPa, typical 
values for cob. 11.2 
Research Signiﬁcance 
As the construction industry faces increasing pressure to adopt more sustainable 
and efﬁcient practices, the potential of integrating advanced technologies with tradi-
tional building methods has garnered signiﬁcant attention. Among these innova-
tions, 3D printing has emerged and historically rooted in techniques like adobe, 
cob, and rammed earth, earth construction can be renowned for its environmental 
beneﬁts and cultural heritage. The advent of 3D printing offers the possibility of 
enhancing these age-old methods with precision, speed, and versatility. However, 
lacks to provide scientiﬁc information on the constructability aspect, such as a rational 
basis for the design of new and advanced Ear

th-based materials, considering typical 
soils variability and different types of soils, the fabrication machines employed, and 
construction workﬂow and processes. 11
New Technologies for Earth Construction: 3D Printing, a Reality …
191
The study focuses on stabilising Portuguese soil by incorporating low dosages of 
cement. This approach aims to enhance the bonding between soil particles, thereby 
improving the mechanical strength and durability of the material. By optimising 
these properties, the research seeks to soil-based material suitable for 3D printing 
applications, balancing structural performance with sustainability. One of the ﬁrst barriers is the mix design of earth- or earth-based materials that 
can be printable. The present study aimed to study a stabilised Portuguese soil for 
future application in 3D printing construction method. To develop statistically signif-
icant models for explaining the printability-related properties of earth-based mixtures 
and other engineering characteristics, a statistical factorial design approach was 
employed. This approach analysed how the input parameters of the mixtures inﬂu-
enced the outcomes. The mix proportions were determined using three key ratios 
(factors): the water-to-soil volume ratio (Vw/Vs), the superplasticiser-to-cement 
weight ratio (Sp/p), and the cement-to-soil weight ratio (c/s). The response variables 
included slump, mass loss, electrical resistivity, and mechanical strength. Regres-
sion models were developed for each response variable, highlighting the signiﬁcant 
inﬂuence of speciﬁc factors. 11.3 
Materials and Methods 
11.3.1 
Raw Materials 
A Portuguese soil from South region was used in the current research study. The 
raw soil particles presented some gravel and lumps, which seem not appropriate for 
actual printing technology [26]. Therefore, the raw soil particles were sieved through 
sieve of 1 mm. The soil fraction passed in the sieve was used in the current study, and 
the fraction retained was rejected, as seen in Fig. 11.1. The Particle size distribution 
(PSD) of soil (passed in a sieve 1 mm) was performed according to ASTM E 100 and 
ASTM D422 by sieving and hydrometer methods, respectively, and it is depicted in 
Fig. 11.2. As can be perceived, this soil consisted of 12% clay, 8% silt and 80% sand. A scanning electron microscopic available at University of Porto [31], FEI 
QUANTA 400 FEG ESEM with integrated spectroscopic characterisation with EDS 
(Energy Dispersive x-ray Spectroscopy), allows access to soil particle morphology, 
as depicted in Fig. 11.3. As can be seen, soil particles present roughly similar dimen-
sions (corroborating PSD analysis) with particles mostly with round edges, which 
can positively affect the workability of the composite. Besides, the EDS analysis 
presented mainly silicium and aluminium, typically found in soils (Fig. 11.3c). The soil stabilisation was performed using Port (CEM I 42.5 R, complying with 
EN 197–1) and limest

one ﬁller according to EN 13139 and EN 12620. The main 
chemical, physical and mechanical properties of cementitious materials are high-
lighted in Table 11.1. A polycarboxylate ether superplasticiser was used to ensure

Mix Design and Design of Experiments 
The stabilised earth mix proportions design was based on the following three factors 
(mixture input parameters): water to soil volume ratio (Vw/Vs); superplasticiser to 
cement weight ratio (Sp/c); cement to soil mass ratio (c/s). Using the fundamental 
equation of formulation: 
Vs + Vc + Vlf + Va + Vsp + Vv = 1.0 m3 
(11.1) 
where, Vs is the volume of soil (earth), V is the volume of cement, Va is the volume 
of water, Vsp is the volume of superplasticiser, and Vv is the estimated void volume. The soil-to-mortar ratio volume was kept constant in all mixtures at 0.530. Consid-
ering the adopted values of Vs/Vs, Sp/c, c/s (see Table 11.3) and the raw materials 
density presented in Sect. 11.3.1, it is possible determine the quantity (kg/m3)  of  
each constituent material. A Central Composite Design (CCD) was chosen to explore the possibility of 
developing statistically signiﬁcant models that correlate the printability and other 
engineering properties of stabilised earth with the input parameters of the mixture 
previously described. The 23 Central Composite Design (CCD) included 8 factorial 
points (Fi), supplemented by 6 axial points (CCi) and 4 central runs. The inﬂuence 
of each factor -Vw/Vs, Sp/c, and c/s-was assessed at ﬁve levels: − α, − 1, 0, + 
1and + α, as detailed in Table 11.2. To ensure the design’s rotatability, the value of 
α was calculated as nF1/4, where nF is the number of factorial combinations of the 
design, equal to 8 in this study. Accordingly, − α, − 1, 0, + 1and + α were set to 
− 1.682 and + 1.682, respectively. Table 11.2 provides the correspondence between 
coded and absolute values of the input mixture parameters, while Table 11.3 displays 
the combinations of the three factors across all mixtures. The interval range for the 
design variables of stabilised Earth mixtures, as shown in Table 11.2, was determined 
based on preliminary tests, which veriﬁed the minimum workability and stability for 
both the most viscous (F1) and the most ﬂuid (F8) stabilised Earth mixtures. Several output properties were used as response models, namely, Slump, mass loss 
control between 7 and 28 days (M

ass loss_7d, Mass loss_21d, Mass loss_28d, respec-
tively), electrical resistivity evolution between 7 and 28 days (Resist_7d, Resist_21d, 
Resist _28d, respectively) and ﬂexural and compressive strength at 28 days (F and 
Rc, respectively). The following section brieﬂy describes the test methods employed. 194

11.3.3 
Response Properties 
The stabilised earth mixtures (presented in Sect. 11.3.2) were produced using a mixer 
in accordance with EN 196–1, which operates at 62.5 and 125 rpm. The ﬂow diameter of fresh mixes was measured using a ﬂow table in compliance 
with EN 12,350–5 (refer to Fig. 11.4). For the slump ﬂow test, the fresh stabilised 
Earth mixtures were placed into a brass cone in two layers, with each layer compacted 
by tamping 25 times using a specialised rod. The excess mortar was levelled off using 
the straight edge of a trowel. The cone was then removed, and the table was dropped 
25 times from a height of 12.5 mm within 15 s. Two perpendicular measurements of 
the spread ﬂow diameter were recorded, and the average value was used to determine 
the slump ﬂow. Three prismatic specimens, with dimensions 40 × 40 × 160 mm3 (see Fig. 11.5), 
for each mixture and used for both mass control and electrical resistivity (after 
demoulding, 7, 21 and 28 days) and mechanical strength (at 28 days). The specimens 
were demoulded after 48 h of the cast, and at this moment, the mass was accessed 
and used as reference mass of the following measurement at 7, 21 and 28 days. Afterwards, demoulding the specimens were kept in a controlled environment (T = 
20 ± 2ºC and HR = 50 ± 5%) until each testing time. The electrical resistivity of the specimens was measured by the two-electrode 
method on prismatic specimens with two stainless steel meshes embedded at both 
extremities at 7, 21 and 28 days of curing. This method is a non-destructive test usually 
employed in cement-based materials [32–34] as measure of the hydration process, 
microstructure evolution and durability, according to their ability to carry electric 
charge [35]. Electrical resistivity is affected by the mixture design (such as water 
content, volume of voids, cement type, among others [36]) and curing conditions 
(such as temperature and moisture environment). Since specimens were exposed to 
the same conditions (T = 20 ± 2°C and HR = 50 ± 5%) [37], the hydration process 
of stabilised earth specimens was not compromised by the curing conditions, so the 
resistivity was only inﬂuenced by mixture design, and it is possible to compare results 
between mixtures. The electrical resistivity was calcul

ated using Eq. 11.1, following 
Ohm’s Law, to determine the relationship between the intensity of the current and

which the current passes (m2), and L is the length between electrodes (m). At the age of 28 days, after the mass loss and electrical resistivity measurements, 
the mechanical strength was evaluated according to EN 196–1. 11.3.4

the other way, an increase in C = c/s increases GWP. This occurs mainly because the

The carbon dioxide index (CI) translates the relation between the CO2 emission 
per each MPa of compressive strength. As such, the higher the CI, the higher the 
CO2 for lower strength. In this case, CI increases with the increase of A = Vw/ 
Vs and decreases with the increase of C = c/s, but the factor Vw/Vs has a higher 
inﬂuence, see Fig. 11.9b. It is evident that Vw/Vs and c/s are the most signiﬁcant 
factors affecting environmental performance analysis. It can be also concluded that 
CI is higher for soils compared to concrete [32, 42]. 11.4.2

As aforementioned in Sect. 3.2, a 2-level 3-factor central composite design with 6 
axial runs and 4 central points has been used. The full quadratic model (See Eq. 11.1) 
was estimated based on CCD; however, some terms of Eq. 11.1 may not be signiﬁcant:

A. M. Matos et al. The state-of-the-art statistical procedure was applied to the modelling of the 
response, involving all steps of the process except validation with additional runs [43]. The steps include ANOVA testing, the diagnosis of residuals, checking for

outliers 
and leverage points and analysing determination coefﬁcients and model comparison 
statistics, as well as lack of ﬁt. A detailed description of the aforementioned proce-
dure can be found in a previous publication of the authors authors [44] or dedicated 
bibliography [43]. Some points are discussed as follows here. Statistical modelling of the response surface for the 9 response variables was 
performed based on the DoE data. Table 11.7 exhibits the estimates of the model coef-
ﬁcients in coded values, as well as the associated values obtained for the ﬁtting statis-
tics R2 and Adjusted R2. The Coded coefﬁcient estimates presented in Table 11.7, 
are an interesting way of analysis since they indicate the relative effect of the mixture 
parameter, Vw/Vp, Sp/p and c/s, on each response variable. Higher values indicate 
the stronger inﬂuence of the mixture parameter in the response variable. A positive 
coefﬁcient means that the response (or transformed response in the case of Resist,7d) 
variable will increase if the given mixture parameter increases and vice-versa. The two most relevant contributions of input factors are shown in bold face, and 
the most relevant of the two is also underlined. Non-signiﬁcant contributions that 
are kept in the model equation are ﬂagged with the word NS in upersript. Even 
though these contributions are non-signiﬁcant, they are preserved in order to keep 
the hierarchy of the models and other statistical properties in general in the statistical 
modelling procedure (see [43]). Analysis of Table 11.7 enables the conclusion that, with the exception of early 
age mass loss (7 days) and late resistivity (28 days), Vw/Vs is the main factor 
contributing to the response of the material. The mixture parameter sp/c only signif-
icantly contributes to resistivity at 28 days and is the second most important contri-
bution to this response, the interaction with c/s being the most signiﬁcant term in the 
response model. The ratio c/s comes second in the scale of the most signiﬁcant contri-
butions to the mechanical strength, ﬂexural and compressive. Signiﬁcant quadratic 
effects of Vw/Vs also exist on Slump and mass loss at all ages. In the case of early 
mass loss (7 days), this quadratic effect is even the most signiﬁcant. The signiﬁcant 
contribution of Vw/Vs to the responses is negative except for Slump, meaning that 
they will signiﬁcantly decrease with an increase in Vw/Vs. On the contrary, Slump will signiﬁcantly increase with an increase in Vw/Vs. The inﬂuence of sp/c and its interaction with c/s on resistivity at 28 days is positive, 
meaning that late resistivity is increased as a response to an increase in Vw/Vs. The 
interaction between sp/c and c/s mostly inﬂuences late resistivity. Figures 11.8, 11.9, 
11.10, 11.11, 11.12 show the contour plots of the surfaces that represent the response 
as a function of the factors considered in the study, keeping the less important factor 
value ﬁxed. The mixture pa

rameter Vw/Vs clearly inﬂuences all variable responses, with the 
exception of early mass loss and late resistivity, and is key to ﬁnding good formu-
lations for both fresh state properties and mechanical response. This is expectable 
because it translates the water volume in the mixture, as the higher is Vw/Vs, the 
higher the water content, which decreases mechanical strength. On the other way,

11.4.3 
Material Optimisation Possibilities 
Based on the obtained models, optimisation can be conducted to ﬁnd the optimal 
earth mix ratio region using the desirability function of Design Expert software. An 
example of an optimisation process is indicated in Table 11.8, and the importance

208
A. M.

Matos et al. weights for all goals were set equal. The potential stabilised Earth mixtures must 
present a viscosity and deformability adequate for extrudability and buildability. A 
Slump range of 140–150 mm was simulated to satisfy those requirements. Those 
constraints resulted in a region belonging to the design space represented in yellow 
in Fig. 11.14. Founded solutions for optimised mixes can be found in this region 
with desirability equal to 100%. As can be perceived, solutions were found for Vw/ 
Vs between 0.75 and 0.76 and for all c/s ranges of CCD. The optimisation can also be performed to ﬁnd the best multi-performance 
targeting other engineering properties, namely, low cracking risk at early ages and 
higher compressive strength. In order to ﬁt those performance requirements, earth 
mixtures proportions were optimised to satisfy the optimisation constraints indicated 
in the second column of Table 11.8. Table 11.8 Optimisation process

The Mass loss_28d was minimised to accomplish low cracking risk, and Compres-
sive strength results at 28 days were maximised. The desirability function revealed 
that stabilised earth with mixture parameters Vw/Vs = 0.751; Sp/p = 1.100%; c/ 
s =  =  0.117 was the best solution. It must be stressed that, considering several 
performance requirements in both fresh state properties, mass loss and compressive 
strength, no solutions could be found with desirability of 100%. This is because there 
are no stabilised earth mixtures with a mass loss lower than 11% in the current CCD. Thus, additional studies are needed in a wider range to optimise mass loss reduction 
and increase mechanical strength simultaneously . 11.5 
Conclusions 
Studying new construction methods for earth construction is essential for 
addressing contemporary challenges related to environmental sustainability, 
economic constraints, cultural preservation, and technological innovation. This research was a kick-off for an innovative technique for earth construction. The study focuses on stabilising Portuguese soil to explore its viability for extru-
sion 3D printing by addressing workability, structural integrity, and environmental 
performance. This study aimed to investigate stabilised earth and select combinations and ranges 
of powder materials (cement + LF + soil), water and superplasticiser dosage, and 
understand how to reach potential printable earth mixture. Speciﬁcally, this study 
aimed to investigate which

key mixture variable inﬂuences stabilised earth properties 
with adequate performance for printing and which are the appropriate ranges for 
deeper research. For this purpose, a 23CCD was followed to ﬁnd statistical models 
capable of describing fresh properties (slump) and other engineering properties, 
such as mechanical behaviour, resistivity and mass loss. The main conclusions are 
the following:
• The mixture parameter Vw 
Vs was the main effect in most of the output properties 
evaluated (Slump, late mass loss, early resistivity and mechanical strength);
• The mixture parameter sp 
c and its interaction with c/s were the main contributions 
to late resistivity;
• The second most inﬂuencing factor contributing to mechanical strength is c 
s . This work stands as a preliminary investigation of the mixture parameters that 
most control fresh state as well as mechanical properties of stabilised soil materials. Modelling the slump response is particularly challenging. There is no doubt that 
one is facing a nonlinear model and the ﬁtting shown in the present work dismissed 
one mixture as being a regression outlier. The lack of ﬁt obtained is non-signiﬁcant. However, more research would be advised, either replicating some of the points in 
the CCD or by, expanding the CCD to consider a 3rd order model or adding new 
factors. 210
A.

M. Matos et al. In summary, this study established preliminary guidelines for designing mixtures 
and evaluating potential stabilised earth materials suitable for 3D printing, utilising 
locally sourced raw materials and standard production and testing techniques. Future 
research should explore innovative construction methods for earthen materials,
