Part II 
Alternative Construction Materials 
and Processes 
From Waste to Resilience: Innovating Beyond Industrial Materials 
The enormous carbon footprint of established construction materials and the need 
for greater improvements in the lifecycle outcomes of the construction value chain in 
light of global challenges of climate change, environmental degradation, and housing 
scarcity have motivated a rethinking of the way structures are built and the materials 
we use in them. The material landscape is ever changing. The abundant old-growth hardwood 
forests replete with oak, elm, cedars, and chestnut trees that once provided us with 
a beautiful palette with which to create comfortable built environments, have been 
depleted and are no longer available to meet our burgeoning needs. The industrial 
materials and processes that we have come to rely on to create huge swaths of built-up 
landscapes rely on brute-force manipulation of matter at planet-threatening scales. These factors have motivated the search for new ways to identify and utilise alter-
native materials and processes to create successful structures and communities. In 
particular, innovators from diverse backgrounds have found ways to utilise waste 
p

roducts from industry, agriculture, and mass consumption as an asset in the produc-
tion of sustainable materials, helping transition the built environment from our largest 
waste stream source to a vanguard of circular economies. The chapter in this second part of the book Sustainable Materials for the Built Envi-
ronment showcases inspiring work by discipline-bridging thinkers and practitioners, 
showing just how much can be achieved by identifying opportunities for circu-
larity, looking for natural alternatives to industrial materials, and bringing together 
diverse elements to produce sustainable composite materials and compound struc-
tural elements that serve to enhance lifecycle outcomes across elements of the built 
environment. Addressing modern demands, for load-bearing structures, retroﬁtting, energy-
efﬁcient insulation, resilient pavements or otherwise, requires more than revisiting 
historical practices. It demands audacity: leveraging AI to optimize material reuse, 
drawing inspiration from Indigenous land stewardship, or rethinking supply chains to 
bridge sectors like forestry and tech manufacturing. The projects proﬁled in this part 
of the book exemplify this ethos, demonstrating how cross-industry collaboration

214
Part II: Alternative Construction Materials and Processes
and systemic thinking can turn constraints into catalysts. By prioritizing synergies 
over silos, these innovators chart a path toward a built environment that thrives within 
planetary boundaries, proving sustainability is not a limitation but a launchpad for 
ingenuity. Dorian A. H. Hanaor

Chapter 12 
Using Marine Sediments to Improve 
the Mechanical Behaviour of Earthen 
Materials 
Miakonirainy Vololontsoa Razaﬁtrimo, Louise Lemesre, Simon Guihéneuf, 
Damien Rangeard, and Arnaud Perrot 
Abstract Earth construction is emerging as a sustainable and local solution for 
reducing the environmental impact of the construction industry. Numerous research 
initiatives are currently revitalising this traditional construction material, gathering 
many experts. Earth is a mix of particles of various sizes, from clay and silt to sand 
and gravel. These materials can be used for structural or non-structural purposes, with 
compressive strengths ranging from 1 to 10 MPa. They also offer superior indoor 
hygrometric regulation properties. However, their water sensitivity poses durability 
issues. Recently, low-tech stabilisation solutions have emerged to enhance water 
resistance. Simultaneously, Europe dredges 50 million cubic meters of marine sedi-
ments annually, with France contributing signiﬁcantly. The traditional solution of 
sea disposal raises environmental concerns, leading to stricter regulations favouring 
sediment valorisation. Like earth, sediments are mainly a mix of mineral particles but 
may also contain other pollutants. Although these sediments present technical and 
environmental challenges, their use reduces the need for natural resource extraction

. Various solutions have been tested, including their application in earth construction. This proposed work evaluates the potential of incorporating sediments into earthen 
materials, examining the effects of salt and the possibility of stabilization with locally 
sourced seaweed extracts. M.

M. V. Razaﬁtrimo et al. 12.1 
Introdution 
Earth construction is emerging as a sustainable and local solution for reducing the 
environmental impact of the construction industry [1]. Many research initiatives are 
currently very dynamic and gather lot of experts to revive this vernacular construc-
tion material. The International Union of Laboratories and Experts in Construction, 
known as RILEM have launched three technical committees in 2022 on earthen 
construction [2] to follow-up the ﬁrst earth construction technical committee dedi-
cated to earthen construction in the twenty-ﬁrst century. In France, a national project 
called “PN terre crue” bears witness to this, as does the rise of numerous projects in 
this ﬁeld. Earthen materials including components of clay, silt, sand and gravel, can be used 
either as structural or non-structural construction materials and can exhibit compres-
sive strengths ranging from 1 to 10 MPa [3]. This material is also known to exhibit 
superior properties in terms of indoor hygric regulation [4, 5]. The major drawback 
of this construction material is its water sensitivity which can lead to durability and 
robustness issues. Nevertheless, some vernacularly inspired and low-tech stabilisa-
tion solutions have recently emerged to provide sufﬁcient water resistance regarding 
national standards [6–9]. At the same time, in Europe, 50 million cubic meters of marine sediments are 
dredged each year in France [10–12]. The accumulation of sediments on the seabed 
and in harbours leads to obstructions that hamper marine activities and makes 
dredging operations mandatory. Dumping marine sediments at sea is the easiest way 
to manage the problem and has been considered the traditional solution. However, 
the disruption of seabed ecosystems arising from the deposit of sediments on the wild 
sea ﬂoor is raising environmental concerns [13–15], leading to stricter national regu-
lation favouring sediment valorisation [16]. Therefore, port authorities are looking 
for sustainable solutions regarding sediments that cannot be disposed at sea [17] and 
are challenged to develop strategies to manage the volume of sediments extracted 
from the dredging operations. This situation offers an opportunity to valorise these sediments while limiting the 
extraction of traditional natural resources. However, marine sediments can present 
technical, environmental and regulatory challenges. The presence of salt or pollutants 
from human activities can impact the valorisation of sediments in different applica-
tions. A literature review shows that many solutions have already been envisaged and 
tested with or without prior treatments such as supplementary cementitious materials 
[18, 19], ﬁred clay bricks [20], ﬁlling material [21], artiﬁcial aggregates [12], pave-
ment base mat

erials [22] or even substrates for plant growth [23]. The possibility of 
using sediments in earthen construction have been limited to adobe bricks [20, 24] 
with only one case of incorporating sediments originated from harbours. The proposed work assesses the potential of using sediments as a potential addition 
in earthen materials whatever the consistency of the material and the construction 
process. It proposes to address these challenges by ﬁrst combining a sediment with 
two types of available earthen resources using a speciﬁc mix-design methodology

12
Using Marine Sediments to Improve the Mechanical Behaviour …
217
[25, 26], analysing the effect of salt and testing the possibility of stabilisation with 
locally-sourced seaweed extracts. 12.2 
Materials and Methods 
12.2.1 
Materials: Sediments, Soil and Sands 
To improve the distribution of earth particle size distribution, as demonstrated in the 
study by Perrot et al. [26], two reference earthen materials were designed from two 
local clayey and ﬁne soils. The ﬁrst was based on a quarry by-product originated 
from a granite rock quarry in Brittany. This by-product originates from a Breton 
granite quarry, composed of crushed rocks and by-products of crushing. It shows a 
grain size distribution ranging from 0 to 4 mm. This type of sand is widely used in 
the construction ﬁeld, particularly in ready-mix concrete to reduce the use of natural 
sands. The second reference soil is a mix of 20% in dry mass fraction of ﬁne-grained 
clayey soil (0–80 µm), 30% of ﬁne sand (63–200 µm), and 50% standardized sand 
(noted 0–4 mm). The sand is added to broaden the grain size distribution, and ensure 
high dry densities as suggested by [25], various sands of different grain sizes are 
added, including ﬁne sand (0–200 µm) and standardized sand (0–4 mm). The studied 
sediments were collected from the Lorient harbour during an annual dredging oper-
ation. The sediment was then stored at ambient temperature and relative humidity 
for drying. In total, six different materials were tested, and the particle size distributions (wet 
sieving for the coarse fractions and laser diffraction granulometry for the fraction of 
particles below 63 µm) were provided in Fig. 12.1. Geotechnical parameters such as 
Methylene Blue value, Atterberg limits (measured using the fall cone method) for 
the sediments and the clayey soil and organic content are provided in Table 12.1 and 
were determined following different methods of the French standard NF EN ISO 
17892. It can be noted that the sediment and the clayey soil are relatively close in 
terms of clay activity (plasticity index and methylene blue value) and particle size 
distribution. However, the sediment contains signiﬁcant amount of sodium chloride 
and organic matter. 12.2.2 
Materials: Algae Extract 
In order to stabilise the earthen materials and provide water resistance to these mate-
rials, four species of invasive marine algae from Brittany were studied in t

his article: 
Solieria Polysaccharide collected in Saint Gildas de Rhuys (latitude: 47° 30 0 N, 
longitude: 2° 49 60 W, France), Ulva Polysaccharide collected in Sarzeau (latitude:

the form of precipitated polysaccharides collected in Saint Gildas de Rhuys (latitude: 
47° 30 0 N, longitude: 2° 49 60 W, France). In this study, the stabilisation solutions have been limited to sea-based products 
in order to adopt a circular economy approach enabled by the valorisation of local 
resources, coherently anchored in the territory of Southern Brittany. 12.2.3 
Methods: Sample Preparation 
Prismatic test specimens with dimensions of 40 × 40 × 160 mm3 are fabricated 
for different formulations (Sediment + ﬁrst reference soil and sediments + second 
reference soil). The targeted water content is the one that gives the mixture a plastic 
consistency, slightly above the plastic limit (wp) in order to obtain a shapeable adobes-
like consistency. Six water contents were tested for each soils in order to ﬁnd the

12
Using Marine Sediments to Improve the Mechanical Behaviour …
219
consistency that provides the highest dry density and compressive strength, in the 
same spirit of Proctor-like tests used in geotechnical engineering [26]. The casting is done manually by the operator (without the use of additional 
mechanical tools) in a 40 × 40 × 160 mm3 mould. After moulding, the moulds 
are placed in a chamber with a stabilized atmosphere (20 °C and a relative humidity 
of 50%) for at least 3 days. Then, the specimens are demoulded and dried in an 
oven at 60 °C until their masses stabilise. The drying period at room temperature 
helps limit the water content gradient in the sample and the possible appearance of 
differential shrinkage. Once drying is complete, the dry mass as well as the dimensions (length, width, 
and height) of each specimen are measured to determine the dry density (kg/m3). Three specimens are made with different compositions of the studied soils 
containing the sediment. For the present study, mixtures comprising 30%, 40%, and 
50% sediment will be mixed respectively with 70%, 60%, and 50% of both reference 
soils. Samples made only with the reference soils are also prepared for comparison 
purposes. 12.2.4 
Methods: Mechanical Testing 
Once the specimens are considered dry (mass stabilization), they are broken in half 
by ﬂexion. The half-specimens (40 × 40 × 80 mm3) will be tested in compression 
using a 300 kN loading machine equipped with a 50 kN load sensor. The compression 
tests are carried out at a constant loading velocity of 0.1 mm/s. The apparatus consists 
of a ﬁxed

plate, a movable plate, a drive mechanism with uniform movement at a 
controlled speed, and a force and deformation i ndicator. The ends of the specimens must be parallel and perpendicular to the direction of 
the applied force. They are placed between the two plates of the press and properly 
centered. Two steel shims are used on the two ﬂat faces of the specimens. The sample 
compressive strength is computed from the maximum recorded force divided by the 
sample initial section (perpendicular to the loading direction). 12.3 
Results and Discussions 
12.3.1 
Compressive Strength of Earthen Materials 
Containing Sediments 
In order to ﬁnd the best water content to prepare moulded samples, the dry densities of 
the samples were plotted in function of the initial water content for the two reference 
soils. Examples of curves for an addition of 40% sediment for both reference soils are 
plotted in Fig. 12.2. It is worth noting that each curve shows a maximum dry density

220
M. V.

Fig. 12.2 Investigating the water content effect of the material compactibility: dry density versus 
initial water content for the two materials with a sediment content of 40%. The best water content 
is the one providing the maximal dry density 
value for a speciﬁc water content value. Such speciﬁc water content allows the best 
compromise which provides the smallest porosity within the materials matrix. At dry 
state, the porosity originates from the initial water that evaporates during drying and 
the entrapped air which is close to 0 for the highest water content and may increase 
as the initial material gets drier and becomes hard to mould. The determination of 
the best water content for a speciﬁc process is mandatory for the optimization of the 
process in order to obtain the highest dry densities. It is observed from Fig. 12.2 that the optimal water content is higher for the 
clayey soil (reference soil 2) than for the crushed sand (reference soil 1) because of 
the higher clay activity (and hence higher water demand) of the reference soil 2. This 
leads to smaller dry density for the materials formulated with this reference soil. The compressive strengths of the different earthen materials are plotted in 
Fig. 12.3. It is worth noting that it has not been possible to obtain samples made 
only with reference soil 1 due to a lack of cohesion in the sample. Therefore, the 
unconﬁned compressive strength of this material can be considered equal to 0. It 
can be explained by the very low clay activity of the crushed sand as displayed in 
Table 12.1. It is also obvious that the compressive strength of the second reference 
soil is only about a quarter of the strength of the same soil combined with different 
proportions of sediments even if the obtained dry densities are slightly decreased. Figure 12.3 also seems to indicate that adding sediment can drastically increase the 
compressive strength of earthen materials. For reference soil 2, the sample exhibiting 
the highest compressive strength is a sediment content of 50% which is the highest 
tested dosage (6 MPa) while for reference soil 1, the highest compressive strength is 
obtained at the highest reached dry density for a sediment content of 30% (5.3 MPa). Ardant et al. [3] explain that the compressive strength of earthen materials depends 
on both the dry density of the sample which describes the sample porosity and the 
clay activity that can be described using the Methylene Blue Value (MBV). They 
have shown that a master curve linking the ratio between compressive strength at

12
Using Marine Sediments to Improve the Mechanical Behaviour …
221
Fig. 12.3 Compressive strength versus dry density for the tested mix-design (Reference soils + 
various sediment contents)
dry state and MBV to the dry density of earthen materials exists and can be used to 
predict the strength of a sample. Such observation can explain the different results 
obtained with reference soils 1 and 2 as the sedimen

ts increase the clay activity and 
also change the dry density of the samples in different ways. It is interesting to use the Ardant et al. model to study the effect of sediments on 
the mechanical behaviour of earthen materials. Therefore, the compressive strength 
to MBV ratio is plotted against dry density for the different tested mix designs 
(reference soils 1 and 2 at different sediment dosages) along with the prediction of 
Ardant’s model in Fig. 12.4. The observation of Fig. 12.4 clearly highlights the beneﬁcial effects of sediment 
on the compressive strength of the earthen materials. It can be seen that the samples 
without sediment show compressive strengths that align with Ardant’s model and 
behave as standard earthen materials. However, it is also clear that at a constant dry 
density, the compressive strength increases with the sediment contents. In Fig. 12.4, 
it is remarkable that each dot corresponding to samples having the same sediment 
content aligns and seems to be located on a curve almost parallel to the Ardant predic-
tion curve. Moreover, the highest curve is the one corresponding to the highest tested 
sediment content (50%). Such results can be explained by the impurities contained 
in the sediment: organic matter and salt that can 1/stabilize the earthen matrix and 
2/crystalize into solid particles. It is interesting to note that these compounds, often 
considered to be pollutants hindering the valorisation, here provide a signiﬁcant rein-
forcement effect on dried hardened materials. Further research needs to be carried 
out to isolate and to quantify the effect of salts and organic matter on the mechanical 
behaviour of sediment-added earthen materials. 222
M.

Fig. 12.4 Compressive strength/MBV versus dry density for the tested mix-design (Reference soils 
+ various sediment contents)—comparison with the prediction of the Ardant model
12.3.2 
Stabilization of Earthen Materials Containing 
Sediments with Algae Extracts 
Water resistance tests have been performed on samples made of reference soil 2 with 
an addition of 40% sediment. The test consists in immersing the sample in tap water 
for 20 min, removing the sample and sealing it in a bag for 24 h before measuring 
the compressive strength. This test is inspired by the former French standard NF XP 
P 13–901 and is known to induce a severe solicitation on earthen materials. The measured compressive strength of a given unstabilised sample is equal to 0 
has the material without algae extract has been destroyed by the immersion period. However, it is interesting to note that the addition of the different extracts from 
invasive and locally-sourced algae have provided water resistance to the sediment-
added earthen samples as the material shows mechanical strength ranging from 1.2 
to 1.8 MPa depending on the algae extract type (Fig. 12.5). These promising results show how the advantageous valorisation of locally-
sourced waste such as marine dredge sediments and invasive algae can become a 
sustainable material for modern construction. 12
Using Marine Sediments to Improve the Mechanical Behaviour …
223
Fig. 12.5 Compressive strength of sediment-added earthen materials (reference soil 2 with a 
sediment content of 40%)
12.4 
Conclusions 
In conclusion, the use of sediment in earthen materials presents a promising avenue 
for sustainable and environmentally friendly building practices. The management 
of dredged marine sediments in Europe, particularly in France, presents an environ-
mental challenge and an opportunity. The shift from traditional sea dumping to valori-
sation aligns with stricter environmental regulations and the need for sustainable 
solutions. This study demonstrates the potential for incorporating marine sediments 
into earthen construction materials, offering a dual beneﬁt of resource efﬁciency and 
environmental conservation. The speciﬁc mix-design methodology developed in this 
work, combined with the use of locally-sourced seaweed extracts for stabilisation, 
provides a viable pathway for using sediments and earthen construction. Moreover, 
the study also demonstrates that the addition of sediment in earthen materials can 
enhance the compressive strength of the sample while seaweed extracts provide 
water resistance. Valorisation of marine-based waste can become a chance for earth 
construction. Overall, the integration of marine sediments into earthen construction not only 
contributes to sustainable construction practices but also promotes the responsible 
management of dredged materials. Future research and practical applications should 
continue to explore and reﬁne these methods, ensuring that earthen construction can 
fully

realize its potential as a sustainable building solution. Quantiﬁcation of the 
effect of salts and organic matter still needs to be done and the robustness of these 
strengthening and stabilizing solutions must be evaluated in relation to the resources 
variabilities. 224

Chapter 13 
Approaches for the Use of Rapidly 
Growing Willow Rods in Earth-Based 
Construction 
Rebekka Volk, Simon Stefﬂ, Moritz Dörstelmann, and Elena Boerman 
Abstract This chapter investigates construction applications of natural ﬁbres and 
fast-growing plant-based materials, which can be processed into load-bearing compo-
nents using specially tailored digital manufacturing technology. The sourcing and 
production of the developed sustainable and recyclable freeform lightweight struc-
tures based on willow and earth are described in detail and material samples are 
shown. At the end-of-life, the components can be disjoined and the composite mate-
rials in the components can be fully separated mechanically for composting, recycling 
or incineration. Moreover, the developed structures were prototyped, implemented 
and tested in full-scale research demonstrators at the 2023 German National Garden 
Show in Mannheim (BUGA23). With this contribution we show the feasibility of 
such novel robotic construction methods of biogenic materials in structural applica-
tions for the ﬁrst time. The envisioned application potential lies particularly in the 
new construction of residential buildings, hotels, ofﬁce buildings and buildings with 
similar uses. R. Volk (B)

R. Volk et al. 13.1 
Introduction 
The European Union is approximately 43.9% covered by forest and other wooded 
areas. Finland and Sweden have the largest share of this, whereas Germany ranges 
in the middle with a forested area of 30–45% [1]. At the same time, Germany was 
the largest roundwood producer in the EU in 2020 and shared the leading position in 
sawn timber production with Sweden [2]. Although wood is a renewable resource, 
with a rotation period of 30–140 years, it is not an unlimited resource. Despite high 
and overall increasin

g production ﬁgures, domestic wood quantities are not sufﬁcient 
to meet European demand, which is why the material is imported into the EU from 
all over the world [2]. According to a WWF study, global wood consumption in 2020 was between 
4.3 and 5.0 billion cubic meters, far exceeding the globally sustainably cultivated 
amount of around 3.0 billion cubic meters. The consumption trend here is increasing, 
although the risk limit of 4.2 billion cubic meters, which entails signiﬁcant sustain-
ability restrictions, has already been exceeded [3]. Moreover, timber is frequently 
utilized in engineered wood products which contain glues and adhesives. These 
high-performance materials are energy-intense to produce and are created from a 
combination of natural and fossil resources. Furthermore, energy recovery is the 
sole end-of-life option for these products (see Fig. 13.1). Thus, other approaches are required either to reduce the demand or to develop 
other renewable materials for construction purposes in order to meet the demand. For this, willow as a fast-growing natural raw material seems suitable to replace 
wood that is not inﬁnitely available or other construction materials that have a high 
environmental impact. Fig. 13.1 “Circle” of wood. Copyright KIT Professorship of Sustainable Construction

13
Approaches for the Use of Rapidly Growing Willow Rods …
229
Willow is a pioneer plant settling which colonize areas that are poor in nutri-
ents and water and can quickly regenerate after strong environmental inﬂuences 
such as ﬂooding. Their cultivation therefore does not (directly) compete with other 
agricultural products, which further underlines their importance for sustainable use 
of resources. Due to its rapid growth and short rotation periods of up to 30 years, 
willow is now mainly cultivated for energy production purposes [4] and harvested 
in 1–5 years short rotation cycles, depending on the intended use [5]. Construction 
purposes are not considered yet. Moreover, when willow is used in building compo-
nents, the aim is either to reclaim it from the component and use it in other wooden 
application as a ﬁlling material or biodegrade it after it reaches the end of utilization 
cycle as a construction/product material. Clay is a traditional construction material with high local availability [6]. It can 
be recycled inﬁnitely [7] and can be processed and manufactured as a secondary 
building material in the same quality as the primary material [8]. To ensure optimal 
and efﬁcient recyclability of the clay mixture, leaching processes can be used to 
separate mineral and plant additives. Alternatively, the organic fraction can remain 
in the material mixture (see Fig. 13.2). A combination of both willow and clay is envisioned for innovative construction 
elements, that are less energy and emission-intense in production than conventional 
construction elements from concrete, faster regrowing than wood, potentially locally 
sourced, not har

mful to nature, enabling rewetting of agricultural land and are fully 
recyclable. To achieve this, the research projects ReGrow [9], WillowWeave and 
ReSidence [10] developed a holistic experimental and digital construction approach 
within an integrative co-design process in order to produce such a construction 
component of a willow-clay composite on a 1:1 scale. Fig. 13.2 “Circle” of clay. Copyright KIT Professorship of Sustainable Construction

230
R. Volk et al. 13.2 
Method 
The aim was to develop circular construction methods based on regional materials and 
to create functionally integrated lightweight structures for use in construction. The 
design principles should make modular combination of the resulting components 
possible and special attention was paid to concepts for the subsequent use of the 
structures. Central elements that contribute to this goal are the following three design 
and manufacturing processes:
• Bespoke computational design and fabrication solutions to enable free-form 
wound lightweight structures based on the combination of willow and clay as 
natural sustainable materials. • Robotic fabrication methodology for implementing weaved willow tree branches. • Lightweight wood construction. The aim was to investigate construction applications of fast-growing plant-based 
materials, which can be processed into load-bearing components using specially 
tailored digital manufacturing technology. For this, the whole life cycle of the 
building component was designed (see Fig. 13.3) for ﬂexibility and circularity. When 
designing buildings and their components, maintaining material cycles through the 
detachability of materials and their joining techniques play a crucial role in order 
to be able to continue using the resources as well as maintaining their values and 
material quality as much as possible. Thus, the separability and recyclability of the 
used material were a critical aspect. Fig. 13.3 Life cycle of willow-clay-composite from sourcing, production, use to end-of-life and a 
new cycle

13
Approaches for the Use of Rapidly Growing Willow Rods …
231
Moreover, the resulting lightweight structures were assessed with respect to 
environmental impact categories, e.g. greenhouse gas emissions, and compared to 
conventional constructions of the same load bearing capacity and showed promising 
results. The harvested willow rods (1. and 2.) are made ﬂexible for weaving in a dampening 
or watering process. Then, they are coupled and additively fabricated by a custom 
machine to baskets (3.) that are then ﬁlled with moist earth. Finally, the prefabricated 
building components (4.) are drying for several weeks for hardening. The basket 
weaving and components can be built in any shape required for loadbearing structures 
(5.). For installation, the prefabricated elements are joined onsite with a crane and 
can be easily disjoined (6.). At the end-of-life, earth and willow can be separated 
mechanically, where the biogenic

share can be utilized as biobased ﬁlling material 
in other applications, or alternatively composted or incinerated and the earth can be 
directly recycled. The development was driven forward by interdisciplinary co-design workshops 
and supported by bespoke manufacturing technology for the production of the 
components. To achieve this, a comprehensive fabrication system was developed, 
starting with a two-axis machine capable of extruding and depositing continuous 
willow-jute composite strands made from spliced willow branches using additive 
techniques. Controlling the machine’s extrusion, movement, and deposition path 
proves challenging due to factors such as the ﬂexibility and varying thickness of 
willow branches, as well as the requirement for tensioning in the ﬁnal compo-
nents. Moreover, challenges due to low local availability of willows (unexpected 
high market demands), or variability of biogenic raw materials had to be faced and 
mastered. 13.3 
Solutions 
On the one hand, a bespoke design-to-manufacturing process [11] for the use of 
fast-growing willow tree branches was expanded and adapted in order to implement 
lightweight structures in the future construction industry not only in an material-
efﬁcient manner, but also on the basis of renewable raw materials. On the other hand, 
an approach to digital fabrication for structural applications was developed. This 
includes both the development of a new type of construction and design repertoire 
and the implementation of corresponding machine technologies for the production 
of load-bearing components. Both design ideas are combined in a complementary 
manner to create a modular concept for producing functionally integrated building 
components from renewable raw materials. Figure 13.4 shows the necessary pre-processing of the willow rods to make them 
useable for robotic weaving processes. It includes planting and harvesting, damping 
or watering and the production of endless strains for the robotic weaving process. During the fabrication of the building components, a stabilisation by metal forms 
is provided which can be reused afterwards. Moreover, engineering wood products

232
R. Volk et al.

Fig. 13.4 “Circle” of willow. Copyright KIT Professorship of Sustainable Construction 
are used to stabilize the whole component and to enable the clay ﬁlling. Then, the 
willow baskets are connected to each other and the demonstrator is manually ﬁlled 
with the clay building material including light biogenic reinforcement aggregates. Finally, the composite elements are dried for several weeks and then transported and 
erected with a crane onsite using steel ring screws, that can be detached after the 
modules reach their ﬁnal position. After use, a full recyclability of the elements is envisioned. The clay can be 
recycled and reprocessed to secondary building materials, but for the willow rods, 
after their recovery from the component one option is a post-treated utilization as 
organic ﬁlling material to keep the biomass in the technosphere. When reaching 
the last use cascade level, the end-of-life biological compositing turns willow into 
organic material and returns it to biosphere. The engineered wood products’ recycling 
depends on the availability of local material recovery plants (crushing, sorting), the 
demand for this material as well as the used adhesives which would often lead to 
energy recovery as the most likely end-of-life scenario. As part of the research, two sustainable and recyclable lightweight structures 
were developed and produced, erected and tested with full-scale research demon-
strators at the 2023 German National Garden Show “Bundesgartenschau BUGA23” 
in Mannheim (see Fig. 13.5). Moreover, the willow-clay building demonstrator was 
expanded to investigate the research focus on local energy generation and microcli-
mate adaptations. Information events, visitor surveys and an international symposium 
took place at the Federal Garden Show to give a large audience an insight into the 
research topic of sustainable and digital construction. 13

13.4 
Conclusions 
Thanks to the current state of the art, robotic freeform winding can already be used to 
produce topology-optimized and low-waste lightweight structures based on natural 
and energetically low processed raw materials. This composite development repre-
sents a further step towards the sustainable construction of tomorrow. Future appli-
cations in residential buildings, hotels, ofﬁce buildings and similar usages are envi-
sioned and can cover a considerable share of new construction demand. By further

234
R. Volk et al. developing the process technology to meet the special requirements of reinforce-
ment made of biogenic sources like natural willow tree branches, sustainable load-
bearing structures should be produced on a larger scale, something that has not been 
technically feasible to date. In addition, regionally available, rapidly renewable raw 
materials are

being made usable for scaled applications. For this, the sustainable 
sourcing of willow and earth needs to be addressed with respect to an industrial scale 
production, since both willow short rotation coppice production facilities as well as 
clay mines need to be ventured. However, this is necessary support the transition to a 
circular economy in the construction sector and further reduce the CO2 footprint of 
lightweight structures. At the same time, state-of-the-art digital design and manufac-
turing processes are being used, which guarantees high material efﬁciency and leads 
to a high potential for the future of the construction industry in terms of economic 
utilization. References

Chapter 14 
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives 
in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High 
Performance and Sustainability 
Dorian A. H. Hanaor 
Abstract The addition of carbon nanomaterials at low concentrations has been 
shown to produce signiﬁcant improvements to the strength and hardness of concrete. Nanostructured carbon based materials including graphene nanoparticles, graphene 
oxide and carbon nanotubes have been applied in various concrete types with inter-
esting results, presenting diverse opportunities for the reduction of cement content 
and the improved performance and sustainability outcomes of cemented materials. Here we examine the mechanisms through which carbon nanomaterials enhance 
the performance of concrete and discuss the challenges and opportunities involved 
in the future development of these advanced materials. In particular the value of 
carbon nanomaterial modiﬁed concrete towards greater sustainability in the built 
environment is scrutinized. Graphene oxide appears to be the most widely used and 
effective method to improve cement performance in terms of strength and density 
of hydration products. The improvement of cement performance is found to occur 
through enhanced hydration kinetics and denser hydration products. However, the 
high environmental impact of their production and the lack of a clear framework to 
guide the implementation of such carbon nanomaterials as cement additives remain 
as obstacles towards the achievement of improved sustainability outcomes. 14.1 
Introduction 
Interactions of carbon with materials in the built environment underpin many 
concepts towards greater sustainability in materials production and usage including 
cement carbonation and the use of biomass as carbon-sinks in structures. Most carbon 
atoms in materials in the built environment are either in the form carbonate phases
D. A.

H. Hanaor (B) 
Faculty of Science and Engineering, Southern Cross University, East Lismore, NSW 2480, 
Australia 
e-mail: dorian@materialeyes.net 
URL: https://www.materialeyes.net

© The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025 
D. A. H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series 
in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_14 
237

238
D. A. H. Hanaor
in masonry and concrete or as part of large organic macromolecules in polymers 
and wood. Carbon nanomaterials, and carbon ﬁbres may seem at ﬁrst glance not 
to be signiﬁcantly relevant in the built environment. Carbon materials, that is those 
consisting primarily of carbon bonded to other carbon atoms, involve carbon–carbon 
bonds in either sp2 or sp3 hybridization. In sp3 hybridization a tetrahedral bonding 
is present, while sp2 bonding results in a hexagonal lattice. Carbon in its sp2 bonded form is rather ubiquitous in nature, occurring in bulk 
form as graphite and readily forming small particles (including nanoparticles) in 
soot and ash. However, it has only been since the 1990s that carbon nanomaterials 
(CNMs) based on sp2 hybridized hexagonal lattices of carbon, including fullerenes, 
carbon nanotubes (technically a type of fullerene) and graphene have truly emerged 
as a highly promising class of materials. The emergence of carbon nanomaterials 
has taken place largely as the result of advances in the synthesis and characterisation 
of nanomaterials. Our ability to produce highly pure forms of carbon nanomaterials 
though physical and chemical processes has evolved hand-in-hand with our ability 
to characterise using various spectroscopic techniques, the structure and quality of 
these materials. Methods have ﬂourished over the last three decades for the production and 
characterisation of controlled carbon nanostructures including
• Highly ordered nanomaterials of sp2 bonded carbon: Fullerenes (so-called Buck-
yballs) having distinct numbers of carbon atoms in a closed sphere; single wall 
carbon nanotubes (SWCNT), in their zigzag, armchair or intermediate chiralities; 
and single-sheet graphene layers which still require meticulous care in creating, 
often on the surface of a single-crystal copper. • Moderately-ordered nanomaterials or those made of nanobuilding blocks of 
predominant sp2 carbon: Carbon-nano onions consisting of numerous shells; 
multi-wall carbon nanotubes in varying degrees of defectiveness (in the form of 
sp3 bonding), length and shell number, carbon nanoﬁbers; defective or multi-sheet 
graphenes, such as graphene-oxide, reduced graphene-oxide (rGO) and graphene 
nanoparticles (which are more-appropriately considered graphite nanoparticle). Additionally, the largely disordered carbon black, which often is in the form of 
agglomerated nanoparticles comprising between 50 and 70% sp2 bonded carbon 
can be consi

dered a nanomaterial, and indeed is widely used as a ﬁller in various 
polymers and is widely used to impart durability in tyres. The evolution of synthesis technologies has resulted in a growing market and a 
general downward trend in the cost of carbon nanomaterials, inspiring their gradual 
uptake in a range of niche applications. The market for multi-wall nanotubes and 
vapour grown carbon nanoﬁbers has grown signiﬁcantly as these have been taken 
up in applications of high performance composites and energy storage. Nevertheless 
the volume of sp2 carbon nanomaterials applied in industry is quite limited and near-
pristine nanotubes and fullerenes are mostly conﬁned to research and beyond the 
laboratory, their real-world use remains very limited. The gap between the potential performance of nanomaterials and their uptake in 
real-world applications is nowhere starker than in the case of graphene. Since the

14
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High …
239
early 2000s the popular science and engineering media has been awash with reports 
of outstanding mechanical, electrical and thermal performance achievable in single-
layer sp2 bonded carbon and the astonishing engineering applications that will soon 
be enabled by this new wonder-material. And yet, the heralded graphene revolu-
tion has been slow to arrive. The challenges in harnessing the impressive theoretical 
properties of graphene arise from the difﬁculties in producing and applying this 
material in its single-layer and defect-free form. These obstacles have not prevented 
chemical suppliers from proﬁting from the sale a broad range of materials under 
the name “Graphene”. However these are invariably in the form of aggregated parti-
cles consisting of multi-layer stacked graphene, and generally offer none of the 
performance characteristics of single sheet graphene. Oddly enough, one area of application where graphene is already ﬁnding effec-
tive application, is in the improvement of concrete. It may seem counterintuitive that 
one of the ﬁrst successful applications of a graphene carbon nanomaterials is as an 
additive to concrete–generally the most widely used and among the cheapest engi-
neering materials. However upon closer inspection, it is evident that this application 
of graphene is not as bizarre as it may seem at ﬁrst glance. Particularly small addi-
tions of graphene (graphene oxide most commonly) have been shown to result in 
profound changes to the behaviour of concrete, with implications towards pathways 
to using this material more sustainably. In particular the use of nanomaterial addi-
tives in concrete, are attractive as a method towards reducing the fraction of cement 
necessary to achieve required performance in structural use, allowing reductions to 
the carbon footprint of concrete structures. 14.2 
Materials and Applications 
14.2.1 
Carbon Fibres 
The earliest use of carbon materials as reinforcement in concrete involved carbon

ﬁbres. Carbon Fibre Reinforced Concrete (CFRC) was ﬁrst reported in 1972, not long 
after the mass production of PAN and Pitch based carbon ﬁbres became commonplace 
[1, 2]. Carbon ﬁbre reinforcement may be in the form of short or continuous ﬁlaments. The rationale for reinforcement with carbon ﬁbres, despite their generally higher cost, 
is their high stiffness and tensile strength. Values of ﬂexural and compressive strength 
are enhanced by around 50% compared with unreinforced, concrete with additions 
of just a few percentages of carbon ﬁbres, indicating mechanical performance levels 
comparable with steel reinforced concrete, with only a small fraction of the overall 
weight and ﬁbre volume [3]. Additional value comes from their corrosion resistance, 
making them useful in situations where steel reinforcement may be prone to corro-
sion, such as in thin proﬁles or near marine environments, although the oxidation of 
carbon ﬁbres is also a problem that is not negligible. Most carbon ﬁbres are derived 
from polymers or other petroleum derived precursors (Commonly PAN). The energy

240
D.

H. Hanaor
used to produce carbon ﬁbres and their fossil based precursors means that in terms of 
LCA outcomes, for a functional unit of 1 cubic metre of concrete, CFRC generally 
has a higher environmental footprint (in terms of Global Warming Potential, GWP) 
when compared with conventional steel reinforced concrete [4]. However this func-
tional unit may be misleading, as the volume of ﬁbres and concrete used varies 
with application requirements. Nevertheless a survey of the ﬁeld shows that signif-
icant sustainability dividends are unlikely to be reached through the replacement of 
steel with carbon ﬁbre in concrete. Nanomaterials however open up new avenues to 
exploit unique carbon structures to impart superior performance in concrete poten-
tially allowing for a signiﬁcant reduction in required cement fractions, with the 
addition of small fraction of nanoscale additives [5]. 14.2.2 
Graphene Oxide 
Among the various carbon nanomaterials that may be used to improve the perfor-
mance of concrete, graphene oxide (GO) stands out in terms of the feasibility of its 
application and the promise it offers towards effective enhancement in mechanical 
and physical properties of the materials. Graphene oxide is less complex to produce than pristine single sheet graphene, 
and due to the functional groups present on the surfaces of graphene oxide this 
material is polar and thus tends to disperse well in water. The aqueous solubility of 
GO is key to its effective use as a cement additive. In many nanocomposite systems 
the aggregation of nanoparticles hinders the fulﬁlment of their potential, as they 
tend to clump together to reduce their surface energy, making GO a highly attractive 
candidate. Graphene oxide is mainly produced from graphite through the use of oxidation and 
exfoliation agents. The Hummer’s method is probably the most widely known, with 
variations of this method being used to produce GO towards research and real-world 
use. The production of GO through oxidation and exfoliation is relatively scalable, 
although the use of aggressive oxidation agents gives this material a considerable 
lifecycle impact, limiting sustainability outcomes that are achieved in such materials 
[6]. The functional groups present on the surface of graphene oxide may include 
hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxyls. The presence of these groups enables the afore-
mentioned aqueous solubility and eliminates the need for organic solvents, reducing 
toxicity concerns and simplifying integration into concrete mixes [7]. Graphene oxide has been extensively researched as an additive in cement pastes, 
mortars and concrete since at least 2012 [8–10]. It is routinely found that the addition 
of graphene oxide to cement pastes at levels in the order of magnitude of 0.05 
wt% produces enhancements to compressive strength typically around 30–60% and 
similar improvements to ﬂexural strength [11, 12]. In concrete and mortar, similar low 
levels of addition produce slightly mil

der improvements relative to cement pastes, but 
the trend and the signiﬁcant enhancements remain clear. Reports have further shown 
that when attention is given to the oxidation conditions of GO sheets, a doubling

14
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High …
241
of ﬂexural and compressive strength can be achieved [8]. Alongside a dramatically 
increased strength, small amounts of GO, as low as 0.02 wt%, produce a marked 
reduction in slump, indicating reduced workability, owing to the interactions of water 
molecules with the high level of surfaces brought into play by the GO additive [12]. This has implications for optimising the water ratio in GO enhanced concrete. The use 
of GO additives also reduces the permeability of concrete, through the formation of 
denser hydration product. This limits the ingress chloride and other ions and provides 
advantages in durability and corrosion resistance for steel reinforcement [13, 14]. 14.2.3 
Terminological Ambiguities and Material Realities 
The integration of graphene into concrete represents an active and promising direc-
tion in material science, offering pathways to enhanced strength, durability, and 
sustainability. However, a critical challenge lies in the ambiguity surrounding the 
term “graphene” itself. While graphene oxide (GO) is widely studied for its compat-
ibility with cementitious matrices, graphene’s application in concrete is not limited 
to GO. The broader landscape is muddled by inconsistent terminology, where mate-
rials labelled as “graphene” or “graphene nanoparticles” often deviate signiﬁcantly 
from the idealized monolayer carbon structure. As discussed, Graphene, in its IUPAC deﬁnition, refers to a single atomic layer 
of sp2 bonded carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. Yet, in practice, mate-
rials marketed as graphene frequently consist of multilayer graphite particles (often 
exceeding 10 layers) or disordered carbon structures, such as turbostratic carbon, 
which lacks the crystalline order of true graphene [15, 16]. This inconsistency 
stems from variations in synthesis methods, commercial labelling practices, and 
a lack of standardized deﬁnitions across industries [17]. For instance, “few-layer 
graphene” may describe stacks of 2–10 layers, while “graphene nanoplatelets” often 
comprise thicker, irregular graphite fragments [18, 19]. Such discrepancies compli-
cate efforts to isolate graphene’s speciﬁc contributions to concrete performance, as 
interactions between the additive and the cement will depend heavily on layer count, 
edge structure, and defect density, and particularly dispersion. The ambiguity in material classiﬁcation poses signiﬁcant challenges for 
researchers. Studies reporting “graphene-enhanced concrete” may, in reality, involve 
graphite derivatives or carbon additives with vastly different behaviors. For example, 
turbostratic carbon, characterized by misaligned graphene layers, exhibits distinct 
interfacial in

teractions with cement hydrates compared to well-ordered GO. This 
variability undermines cross-study comparisons and obscures the mechanisms by 
which carbon nanomaterials inﬂuence concrete properties. Despite these hurdles, 
several investigations have explored non-GO graphene forms. Multilayer graphene particles have been shown to improve compressive strength 
in cement pastes and concrete by ﬁlling nano-pores and bridging microcracks [18, 
20–22]. Graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) are commercially available and have been 
explored as cement additives. While, as mentioned, the terminology of GNP may be

242
D. A. H. Hanaor
applied inconsistently, the LCA of GNPs remains quite favourable with equivalent 
emissions as low as 0.17 kg CO2 resulting from the production of 1kg of GNPs [23]. This is in stark contrast to GO, which may have an equivalent CO2 footprint over 1 
tonne per kg of graphene oxide produced [24]. Such LCA outcomes are supportive 
of GNP usage. In comparison with the use of GO, the addition of GNPs to concrete 
tends to produce a more mild effect on compressive strength, in the order 10–20% 
and even a weaker enhancement of ﬂexural strength, in the order of 0–10% [19–22]. Reports of graphene modiﬁed cements and mortars exhibiting greater enhance-
ment of mechanical properties, in the order of doubling compressive strength, have 
been reported for the use of Graphene Nanoparticles (a term often used in the liter-
ature to describe widely differing materials) produced by chemical treatment with 
strong acids, followed by thermal shock and exfoliation [25]. It is however likely 
that many of the graphene nanoparticles reported in concrete are in fact more appro-
priately considered to be GO, due to the oxidising chemical treatments involved in 
their processing. 14.2.4 
Carbon Nanotubes 
Towards the enhancement of concrete, graphene oxide would appear to be the most 
promising and useful among carbon nanostructures. The ease of dispersion and the 
relatively facile fabrication of GO creates versatile avenues towards the modiﬁcation 
of concrete to extract higher levels of performance. It is therefore not surprising that 
commercialisation efforts focus on the implementation of GO in concrete. However, 
the use of ﬁbres, wires and elongated bars is a sensible approach towards the enhance-
ment of ﬂexural strength and ductility in concrete, and it is the use of such ﬁbres that 
constitutes the most common approach towards the fabrication of reinforced concrete 
materials, and the use of carbon ﬁbres (not nanoscale) is among the most attractive 
options towards the substitution of steel in concrete materials. We can therefore 
expect carbon nanotubes, a highly interesting 1D-nanostructure with outstanding 
axial properties, to be of interest towards their implementation in advanced and 
sustainable concrete formulations. Various carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and nanoﬁbers have been proposed as rein-
forcing phases in concrete. The logic behind

such applications is clear, the high 
tensile strength of carbon nanotubes and nanoﬁbres, and the formation of dense 
hydration products and carbonate phases at interfaces with the CNTs means that 
good strengthening effects should be possible in such composites [26]. In these 
applications, the general trend is the use multi-wall nanotubes or carbon nanoﬁbers as 
alternative short-ﬁbre reinforcing phases, as single wall nanotubes are prohibitively 
expensive. As with graphene, the key to the successful use of CNTs lies in their 
successful dispersion and appropriate surface functionalisation, with certain studies 
showing that the addition of CNTs may in fact decrease the mechanical performance 
of cements [26]. Commonly the observed CNT driven enhancement of compres-
sive strength in hardened cement pastes is relatively mild and highly dependent on

14
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High …
243
the chemical and physical methods used to disperse the nanoscale phase [27–29]. Enhancement is regarded to occur mainly through crack bridging. Concretes made with CNT additives would generally fall under the category of 
High-Performance Fibre Reinforced Cementitious Composites (HPFRCCs). These 
are a subclass of Engineered Cementitious Composites (ECCs, also called bend-
able concrete). ECCs are high-performance, ﬁbre-reinforced materials designed for 
exceptional ductility and crack control, achieving tensile strain capacities hundreds 
of times greater than unreinforced concrete [30]. In architecture, their durability and 
self-healing properties reduce maintenance needs and extend structural lifespans, 
aligning with sustainable goals by minimizing material waste and embodied carbon. The inclusion of CNTs in ECCs may enable thinner, lighter and more durable struc-
tures, reducing material use and prolonging service life. ECCs may beneﬁt from the 
addition of CNTs not only because of enhanced mechanical performance but also 
chemical resistance, to acid and sulphate attack, which are particularly important in 
this type of material [31]. 14.3 
Mechanisms 
The integration of carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and graphene oxide 
(GO), into cementitious matrices has emerged as a transformative strategy for 
enhancing concrete performance. These materials exert their inﬂuence at the 
nanoscale, primarily by modulating the microstructure of hydrated cement phases 
during setting and hardening. A critical mechanism involves the morphological alter-
ation of calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H) binding phase in concrete. Studies indi-
cate that graphene and GO act as nucleation templates, promoting the formation 
of densely packed, interlocked C–S–H structures with “ﬂower-like” morphologies 
(Fig. 14.1)  [8, 12]. These conﬁgurations enhance mechanical strength and durability 
by reducing porosity and improving interfacial bonding between hydration products 
and aggregate surfaces [32]. The nucleation of acicular, ﬂower like o

r interlocking C–S–H hydration products 
is likely only part of the story. Carbon nanomaterials, including both graphene, GO 
and CNTs, enhance hydration kinetics of cement, forming denser hydration prod-
ucts surrounding the dispersed carbon nanoparticles and more extensive reactions of 
hydraulic phases [33–35]. A further important mechanism that has been discussed 
is the formation of interlocking carbonate phases including calcite and aragonite at 
interfaces with the cement gel [36, 37]. While not as widely reported as the C–S–H 
ﬂower mechanism, the decomposition of graphene oxide into CO and CO2 in the 
alkaline medium of the cement gel, and the carbonation of the surrounding hydrated 
phases of CH, ettringite and C–S–H to form needle-like CaCO3 is likely an impor-
tant factor in the often highly enhanced performance in C nanomaterial modiﬁed 
concretes [33, 37]. 244
D.

A. H. Hanaor
Fig. 14.1 Schematic 
illustration of graphene 
nucleation of ﬂower-like 
C–S–H phases (generated 
using DALL E 3)
Further performance gains arise from synergistic interactions of graphene, 
graphene oxide or carbon nanotubes with supplementary nanoparticles. The combi-
nation of pozzolanic silica fume and graphene oxide stands out as an attractive 
combination of additives [38–41]. Silica fume helps create further C–S–H phases 
through reaction with hydroxide materials, which boost the strengthening effect 
brought about by the addition of graphene and GO. It has been widely shown that 
silica fume (nanoscale amorphous SiO2) ampliﬁes nucleation efﬁciency by helping 
disperse graphene and providing additional reactive sites for interconnected C–S–H 
growth [39, 40, 42]. Similar effects may be seen also with carbon nanotubes, whereby 
silica fume enhances dispersion and turns an otherwise mild enhancement by CNTs 
into a more signiﬁcant strengthening effect in cementitious materials of various kinds 
[38]. Based on reported mechanisms for strength enhancement by carbon nanomaterials 
it is logical to expect that pozzolanic additives in general would provide synergy in 
developing further strength, through the formation and interlocking of greater levels 
of C–S–H, through the pozzolanic interactions. As GO is known to enhance hydra-
tion kinetics and extent of reactions, it is unsurprising that pozzolanic reactions 
and formations of further strength giving hydration products further beneﬁt from 
the addition of graphene oxide, which acts as a catalyst for pozzolanic activity of 
additives. Further to silica fume, the addition of GO further works in synergy with 
nano-alumina (Al2O3)  [43, 44], calcined clay [45], and Fly-ash [42, 46] to contribute 
to denser and stronger hydration products in cement. These synergistic effects high-
light pathways towards extracting further utility from supplementary cementitious 
materials (SCMs), and reducing the need for clinker in concrete formulations. 14
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High …
245
Towards Commercialisation and Sustainable Built Environments 
It has been demonstrated that the optimised addition of GO can produce economi-
cally enhanced concrete with higher levels of strength/cost ratio achieved [12], and 
it is likely that with a decreasing cost of GO production, these levels can be even 
further improved. Graphene enhanced concrete is mostly implemented in pilot stage 
and early commercialisation stage projects and is yet to become a mainstay feature 
of the construction landscape. The economic production potential of GO has inspired 
numerous commercial ventures into graphene enhanced concrete, making this one 
of the ﬁrst sectors to see widespread uptake of graphene nanomaterials. A key draw-
card driving the uptake of graphene enhanced concrete is the increased density, and 
the consequent improved resistance to corrosion, chloride ingress and fre

eze–thaw 
cycles. Several key barriers to commercial uptake of carbon nanomaterial enhanced 
concrete are clearly identiﬁable. Firstly, the construction industry is well known 
as a highly conservative market, exhibiting great inertia and slow adoption rates of 
new materials, due to well-entrenched supply chains and rigid regulatory frame-
works. Secondly, the high costs of graphene oxide additives means that there are few 
use-case scenarios where its inclusion makes signiﬁcant economic sense to include 
this additive and thirdly, there remain technical challenges to using graphene or 
GO effectively, particularly relating to the dispersion of additives and prediction of 
long-term performance. The innovation and commercialisation scene relating to the development and 
implantation of carbon nanomaterials in concrete is notably active. Venture arms of 
large construction materials producers and investor-funded startup companies are 
seeking to capitalise on the potential for new and improved materials that deliver 
superior sustainability outcomes and new markets for advanced materials. Currently 
most ventures provide carbon nanomaterials, mainly graphene oxide, as admixtures 
to be included in concrete mixes. Some selected ventures from around the world are 
outlined in Table 14.1. This list is by no means exhaustive, and the dynamic nature 
of this sector means that new ventures are constantly emerging and disappearing in 
the scene. 14.4 
Towards Sustainable Built Environments 
A key motivating factor driving the development of carbon nanomaterials for the 
enhancement of cementitious materials is the urgent need to reduce the enormous 
carbon footprint of the concrete sector, which is widely evaluated as being responsible 
for ~ 8% of GHG emissions. In this context the improvement of cement performance 
through the use of nanomaterials can reduce the environmental impact of the concrete 
industry through three main enabling pathways as outlined here. 246

1. Enabling the use of less concrete 
As we have seen, the integration of carbon nanomaterial additives such as GO and 
CNTs in concrete, together with other additives such as silica fume, nano-alumina or 
others, brings about signiﬁcant improvements in compressive and ﬂexural strengths. This, along with the improved density of hydration products and consequent better-
ment of corrosion resistance in steel reinforced concrete brought about by such addi-
tives can enable the design of slimmer, more material-efﬁcient structural elements. The improvement in material performance may thus allow engineers to reduce cross-
sectional dimensions in beams, columns, and slabs without compromising struc-
tural integrity, thereby lowering material consumption in typical applications [5, 
50]. Typical reported enhancements to compressive and ﬂexural strengths are in the 
region 30–50%, for cement pastes, mortars and concretes. However, a systematic

14
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High …
247
approach to determine the extent to which these enhancements can be translated to 
reductions in materials usage remains to be developed. Industry adoption will depend 
not only on cost reductions in nanomaterial synthesis but also updated building codes 
and regulatory requirement to reﬂect these advanced material properties. 2. Enabling concrete with a smaller cement fraction 
Another approach towards realising the beneﬁts of nanomaterial additives in more 
sustainable concrete construction is to use a smaller cement fraction to achieve

required mechanical performances in cemented materials, namely concrete. Again, 
the relationship is quite complex, and it remains challenging to translate reported 
mechanical performance enhancements to cement-savings in applied concrete. Based 
on standard mix designs, it would seem that the addition of small amounts of graphene 
oxide in the order of 0.02 to 0.05 wt% to concrete, may result in M25 concrete mixes 
exhibiting strengths comparable to typical M40 mixes [51, 52], which would imply a 
cement saving in the order of ~ 100 kg per cubic metre of concrete. Assuming ~ 1kg of 
CO2 is emitted per kg of cement produced, achieving net carbon reductions through 
the use of GO to reduce the cement fraction in mix designs, assuming around 700g of 
GO is required per cubic metre of concrete, requires the GO to have embodied carbon 
below ~ 140 kg CO2 equivalent per kg GO produced for any net CO2 reductions to 
be realistic. As some reports put the embodied carbon of GO as high as 4800 kg 
CO2e equivalent per kg GO [24, 53], it is relatively clear that the use of GO towards 
improving the embodied carbon of concrete necessitates synthesis approaches that 
are more environmentally friendly than the common Hummer’s method. A more 
detailed LCA of this approach is necessary, and it is important to identify pathways 
that produce useful strength enhancements at particularly low additive levels. 3. Enabling higher performance from alternative cement chemistries 
Carbon nanomaterial additives, while mainly studied in the context of Portland 
Cement (OPC) are also of value towards extracting higher levels of performance from 
alternative cementitious materials, and are particular interesting towards the modi-
ﬁcation of low-carbon cements. As we have already seen, synergy between carbon 
nanomaterials and other SCMs are widely studied, including enhancing the effect of 
pozzolanic additives such as ﬂy ash, silica fume and nano-alumina. This approach 
sets the stage for greater versatility in the design of optimised cement mixtures. The 
use of GO, GNPs or CNTs has also been shown to affect the performance of more 
sustainable cement alternatives including geopolymers and natural hydraulic lime 
(NHL). Finding pathways to apply these additives in greener cements sets the stage 
to broadening the industry uptake of alkali-activated materials, carbonating cements 
and other binders not reliant on traditional clinkers. Outlook 
While a large volume of research has conﬁrmed the outstanding ability of small levels 
of carbon nanomaterial additives to impart signiﬁcant improvement to the mechan-
ical and physical attributes of cementitious materials, translating these improvements 
to better and more sustainable built environments remains challenging. In particular,

248
D.

H. Hanaor
achieving net improvements to sustainability outcomes through the use of these mate-
rials necessitates progress in our ability to sustainably produce them, and implement 
them. Sustainable Methods to Produce Additives 
Lifecycle assessments (LCAs) have been conducted to scrutinize a range of graphene 
and graphene oxide materials. These differ in feedstock, processing, output and by-
products. Towards use in concrete, it is evident that GO is of predominant interest, and 
the reported carbon dioxide equivalent of this material in terms of global warming 
potential varies widely from 10 to 1000s kg CO2 equivalent per kg of produced carbon 
nanomaterial. It is clear that a long path remains to be trodden before we can reliably 
produce these materials in industrial quantitates in a sustainable manner. There are 
many factors to consider in developing more sustainable approaches to produce 
graphene related materials and carbon nanotubes. A wide range of waste and bio-
based feedstock have been considered towards the production of graphene materials 
including lignin, waste tyres and agricultural waste. [24, 54, 55]. Carbon nanotubes 
are mostly produced from hydrocarbon precursors, but these too can be derived from 
waste feedstocks [56]. The establishment of scalable methods to produce carbon 
nanomaterials from sustainable feedstocks with low environmental impact are clearly 
needed in order for them to be of relevance in materials for the built environment, and 
various research efforts, under the umbrella of the EU’s Graphene ﬂagship project, 
are increasingly aligned with this [57]. Implementing Carbon Nanomaterials Towards Sustainable Built Environments 
While the addition of very low levels of carbon nanomaterials has been widely 
demonstrated to enhance the strength and performance of cement pastes, mortars, 
and concretes, signiﬁcant challenges must be addressed before these innovations can 
be effectively translated into real-world, sustainable concrete solutions. One approach 
that stands out towards the effective use of graphene oxide in the enhancement of 
concrete, is its implementation as a surface treatment agent. This my facilitate the 
nucleation of dense and hard hydration products on the outer surfaces of concrete, 
where they are most needed, while reducing the overall amount of nanomaterial 
consumed [14, 58]. The interplay between various carbon nanomaterials, cement 
types, aggregates, and supplementary additives creates a highly complex, multidi-
mensional optimization problem that is difﬁcult to navigate. Currently, there is no 
established industry framework or systematic methodology to guide the integration of 
carbon nanomaterials with low-carbon cements, supplementary cementitious mate-
rials (SCMs) such as silica fume or ﬂy ash, recycled aggregates, and waste-based 
additives. Developing such a framework is essential to create optimized concrete 
mixes that not only meet structural performance requirements

but also minimize 
environmental impact. Bridging this gap will require interdisciplinary collaboration, 
advanced computational modelling, and rigorous lifecycle assessments to ensure 
that the promise of nanomaterials can be realized in a way that aligns with global 
sustainability goals. 14
Carbon Nanomaterial Additives in Concrete as a Pathway Towards High …
249
14.5 
Concluding Remarks 
The integration of carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene, graphene oxide and 
carbon nanotubes, into concrete represents an astonishingly effective way to extract 
higher performance from concrete, offering unparalleled improvements in mechan-
ical strength, durability, and material efﬁciency. By enabling slimmer structural 
designs, reducing cement demand, and enhancing alternative binders like geopoly-
mers, these nanomaterials may be able to play a role in addressing the urgent need 
to decarbonize an industry responsible for around 8% of global CO2 emissions. However, their commercial viability hinges on overcoming entrenched industry 
inertia, prohibitive production costs, and unresolved technical challenges relating 
to the sustainable production and implementation of these materials. Collaborative 
efforts among researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders are critical to stan-
dardize material deﬁnitions, incentivize green production pathways, and update regu-
latory frameworks. As pilot projects and startups demonstrate early successes, the 
future of nano-carbon enhanced concrete lies in harmonizing its theoretical promise 
with lifecycle sustainability, ensuring it evolves from a laboratory marvel into a 
useful material in producing the sustainable concrete needed so badly in our built 
environments. References

Chapter 15 
Advantages and Challenges of 3D 
Geopolymer-Based Concrete 
in Construction 
Lateef N. Assi, Ali Alsalman, Rahman S. Kareem, and Kealy Carter 
Abstract Rapid urbanization and industrialization have created signiﬁcant chal-
lenges in the construction industry, including labor shortages, resource consump-
tion, and safety concerns. Traditional concrete production, which relies heavily on 
Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC), is highly energy-intensive and a major contrib-
utor to carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions, exacerbating climate change. Recently, the 
integration of three-dimensional printing (3DP) with geopolymer-based concrete 
has emerged as a promising solution for sustainable construction. This innovative 
approach offers economic, environmental, and productivity beneﬁts over conven-
tional methods. Studies have shown that 3D Geopolymer-Based Concrete signiﬁ-
cantly reduces construction costs by lowering labor and material expenses, primarily 
due to the elimination of formwork. The cost of manufacturing prefabricated bath-
room units using 3DP is notably lower compared to traditional precast methods. Additionally, 3DP technology minimizes material waste, reduces construction time, 
and decreases labor costs, resulting in an overall more efﬁcient and cost-effective 
process. Environmental assessments indicate that 3DP concrete construction has a 
better environmental performance than conventional methods, with notable reduc-
tions in CO2 emissions and resource consumption. The use of geopolymer-based 
concrete further enhances these beneﬁts by reducing the reliance on energy-intensive 
cement. Overall, the adoption of 3DP and geopolymer-based concrete in construction 
presents a viable path toward addressing the challenges posed by urbanization and 
industrialization, offering substantial improvements in cost efﬁciency, environmental 
impact, and productivity. L.

Introduction 
3D printed geopolymer-based concrete (3DPGC) combines the beneﬁts of 
geopolymer concrete with the advanced construction potential of 3D printing tech-
nology. This innovative approach has acquired attention due to its sustainability, 
proﬁciency, and ability to address challenges in developed construction. Geopolymer 
concrete is an eco-friendly alternative to conventional Portland cement concrete 
with 0% of Portland cement. It is made using industrial by-products like ﬂy ash, 
metakaolin, and slag, which are activated using alkali solutions; mostly a combi-
nation of sodium silicate and sodium hydroxide. The use of geopolymer concrete 
results in several beneﬁts such as lowering CO2 emissions, and embodied energy, 
enhancing durability, and improving the mechanical properties of concrete including 
compressive and ﬂexural strengths [1, 2]. 3D printing in construction utilizes additive 
manufacturing techniques to layer geopolymer concrete directly into desired shapes, 
eliminating the need for molds. This integration offers design ﬂexibility; complex 
structures can be built with ease and material efﬁciency; reduced waste during 
construction, and labor reduction; automation reduces dependency on manual labor. This study investigates the advantages of 3DPGC, for instance, its cost-effectiveness, 
environmental beneﬁts, and material competence, while discussing practical limita-
tions, including interlayer bonding, anisotropy, and scalability. The ﬁndings highlight 
the potential of this transformative methodology in changing construction practices, 
emphasizing the need for further technical and regulatory developments. 15.2 
Advantages of 3D Geopolymer-Based Concrete 
The combination of 3DPC and geopolymer concrete can have several advantages. They are summarized in the next sections. 15.2.1 
Economic Beneﬁts 
The economic beneﬁts of 3DGPC are substantial, driven mainly by the reduction in 
labor and material costs. Conventional construction techniques depend primarily on 
formwork, which accounts for a signiﬁcant portion of the costs in labor and mate-
rials. On the other hand, the 3D printing process eliminates the need for formwork. Typically, the labor cost accounts for more than half of the cost of conventional 
construction. The cost of materials and equipment accounts for about 20% [3]. On 
the other hand, the material cost for 3DPC is about half of the total cost, while the 
labor cost is reduced to about 35% and the equipment cost is about 20% [4]. 3DPC 
does not require formwork as used for conventional construction; the use of formwork 
increases the costs of labor, materials, and equipment. Figure 15.1 shows the cost

unnecessary in the fabrication of walls using 3DPC. As a result of reducing material 
wasted and no over-engineering, the cost of 3DPC may be lower than conventional 
concrete. The use of 3DPC reduces 30–60% of construction wastes, 50–80% of labor 
costs, and 50–70% of production time [6]. Due to the rapid development of the 3DP, 
the equipment cost of 3DP technology will be lower than that of the equipment 
cost of traditional construction when 3DP technology achieves its large-scale imple-
mentation [3]. Overall, the use of 3DP in construction relatively reduces the cost of 
construction compared to using traditional construction. 15.2.2 
Environmental Beneﬁts 
3DPGC addresses essential ecological interests by reducing carbon emissions and 
supporting resource effectiveness. Contrasting conventional concrete, which relies 
on energy-intensive OPC, geopolymer concrete uses industrial by-products such as

Productivity and Time Efﬁciency 
The 3D printing process improves efﬁciency by reducing construction time and 
improving workﬂow productivity. Automated procedures reduce human error and 
streamline operations, reducing the duration of construction projects by up to 70% [5]. Figure 15.3 displays the working time and total weight of a prefabricated bathroom

exhibits an overall improvement of 48.1% in productivity when three-dimensional
