Conclusions of Beneﬁts 
3DPGC presents an innovational advancement in sustainable construction, offering 
signiﬁcant environmental, economic, and productivity advantages over conventional 
practices. Its use of industrial by-products, such as ﬂy ash and slag, drastically 
reduces CO2 emissions compared to OPC, while its precision in material deposi-
tion minimizes construction waste by 30–60%. Enhanced Life Cycle Assessment 
(LCA) metrics highlight its superior environmental performance, though challenges 
related to alkali activators persist. Economically, 3D printing eliminates the need for 
formwork and reduces labor costs by 50–80%, material costs by 50%, and production 
time by 50–70%. Moreover, productivity improvements include a 48.1% increase in 
efﬁciency for prefabricated components and a 26.2% reduction in structural weight, 
streamlining operations, and material handling. By addressing labor shortages and 
improving safety throu

Assi et al. its ability to signiﬁcantly cut costs, reduce waste, and deliver innovative architec-
tural designs, 3D-printed geopolymer concrete stands as a pivotal technology for 
advancing sustainable, efﬁcient, and eco-friendly construction practices. 15.3 
Technical Foundations 
The structural performance of 3DPGC elements is a crucial area of interest nowa-
days due to the potential enhancement of this technology in the construction industry 
and due to the ecological beneﬁts of geopolymer concrete compared to conven-
tional concrete. The major keys of this technology regarding structural performance 
are printability, fresh mechanical properties, hardened mechanical properties, and 
durability. 15.3.1 
Printability and Fresh Properties of 3DPGC 
Printability of the 3DPGC is associated with the recognized properties of layer-
by-layer construction without the need for formwork. Generally, the printability of 
3DPC refers to the extrusion of the concrete mixture easily through a nozzle and 
accumulated layer by layer while maintaining the shape and structural integrity of 
the pored elements (Le et al., 2012). The printability of 3DPC may include extrud-
ability, buildability, and rheological characteristics. Extrudability of 3DPGC is the 
ease with which the concrete mixture can be extruded successfully without blocking. Studies have shown that there are several factors affecting the extrudability of 3DPGC 
such as rheological properties, particle size distribution, binder content, alkaline acti-
vating solution, and curing conditions [11, 12]. The extrusion process of any type of 
3DPCs is required to be homogeneous without segregation or cracking [13, 14]; this 
process is signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced the in case of 3DPGC due to the high viscosity of 
the activation solution. Based on the extrusion method of printed concrete, the fresh 
properties inﬂuence the printability of the pored elements. Typically, there are two 
common methods of the extrusion of 3D-printed concrete; screw and ram extrusions. Table 15.1 states the comparison between them [15–17]. On the other hand, build-
ability is the ability of the concrete to maintain its shape and support without failure 
or build up the desired element with tolerable deformation [18, 19]. In other words, 
buildability refers to the capability of the concrete to sustain its weight in addition to 
the weight of the concrete from the above layers without collapsing. Commonly, the 
thickness of a single layer of printable concrete is small; typically ranging from 1 to 
10 cm to minimize the effect of the preliminary gravity stress [19]. Accordingly, the 
shear stress yields by gravity must be lower than the yield stress of cementitious mate-
rials to preserve the shape of the printed concrete. Hence it is essential to control the 
thickness of layers to prevent the risk of collapsing due to gravity. Studies have shown 
that there are several types of failure of 3DPC of multiple layers; yield failure of

increases, the probability of failure of the bottom layer due to compression stress 
is higher [20]. Fresh properties of 3DPGC are critical for guaranteeing the quality 
and structural performance of the printed concrete elements. Rheology is the typical 
term used in the literature to describe fresh properties, which can be deﬁned as the 
process of assessment of the viscosity, elasticity, and plasticity of cementitious mate-
rials under shear stress [21]. It was found that the rheological properties of 3DPGC 
were inﬂuenced signiﬁcantly by the composition of the alkaline activating solution. It can be shown in Fig. 15.4 that the silicate-to-sodium ratio in the alkaline solution 
(Si/Na) ratio of the activator affected the rheological properties of the geopolymer; 
the yield stress and structural rebuilding ability enhanced with the reduction of the 
Si/Na ratio [22]. Reference [23] investigated the open time of 3DPGC which refers 
to the period during which the freshly mixed concrete stays workable. They stated 
that mixtures with potassium-based activators had shorter open time compared to the 
mixtures with sodium-based activating solutions. 15.3.2 
Mechanical Properties 
The mechanical characteristics of 3DPGC are signiﬁcant as they affect directly 
its practical applications in the construction industry. These properties include 
compressive, tensile, and ﬂexural strengths, elastic modulus, and durability. The 
early compressive strength is required to be developed shortly after the concrete has 
been printed which allows the structure to support loads. In addition, the capacity

260
L.

Fig. 15.4 Relationship 
between yield stress and Si/ 
Na ratio
of the fresh concrete layers to adhere to each other and produce a cohesive structure 
is crucial for the integrity of the printed elements since they are directly related to 
the interlayer bond performance of the concrete [24]. Therefore, the development 
of these properties before the setting time requires more consideration compared to 
conventional concrete since this technology utilizes no formwork and depends on the 
strength of layer-by-layer construction; the early-age mechanical properties affect 
the buildability of the printed elements [15]. Reference [25] conducted a study on the 
mechanical properties of 3DPGC and they found the loading direction of the tested 
elements affected the compressive and ﬂexural strength of the printed concrete. It was 
determined that 3DPGC samples exhibit greater compressive strength when loaded 
in the y direction compared to the x and z directions (Fig. 15.5). This behavior can be 
understood by comparing it to the micro-mechanics of composite materials. When 
loaded perpendicular to the layers, the load is directly transferred and distributed 
uniformly across the cross-section. However, when loaded in the transverse direc-
tion, the layers tend to slip or separate from each other, resulting in lower strength 
in the specimen. A typical value of the compressive of 3DPGC was reported in the 
range of 20–60 MPa depending on the mixture design and curing regimen. For tensile 
and ﬂexural strengths, the values are in the range of 2 MPa to 4 MPa and 3 MPa to 10 
MPa, respectively. However, elastic modulus values ranging from 10 to 30 GPa were 
reported in the literature [26, 27]. The ﬂexural strength of printed concrete varies 
with testing orientation and can be higher than the mold-cast samples [15]. Previous 
studies have shown that the compressive strength of 3DPC can be higher when the 
application of the load is parallel to the printed layers. On the other hand, the tensile 
strength is lower when the load is perpendicular to the layers due to the weaker 
interlayer cohesiveness. Moreover, the ﬂexural strength can vary substantially based 
on the loading directions that are relative to the orientation of the printed layers 
[17]. Overall, the anisotropy of 3DPC can be affected by several aspects such as the

printing process and devices, mixture proportions, and ambient conditions such as 
humidity and temperature. The major concerns of 3DPC regarding structural perfor-
mance are the interlayer bond strength and anisotropy. The interlayer bond strength 
is inﬂuenced by several factors; which include the interval time, the speed of the 
printing head, and the height of the print nozzle, which have signiﬁcant impacts on 
the interface bond strength of 3DPC [15]. The anisotropy of 3DPGC describes the 
var

iation of the mechanical properties of the concrete based on the layer-to-layer 
construction process. In contrast to conventional concrete, which is typically cast 
to construct a uniform material, 3DPC demonstrates distinct characteristics along 
different directions because of how each layer is placed and bonds with the previous 
one. 15.3.3 
Mixture Proportions 
To ensure the performance of 3DPGC, the mixture proportions require signiﬁcant 
consideration. Mixture proportions include ﬂy ash, ground granulated blast furnace 
slag, metakaolin, or a combination of them as the base materials for geopolymer 
concrete. An alkaline activating solution is required to initiate the polymeriza-
tion process. High-range water-reducing admixtures (HRWRA) are fundamental to 
enhancing ﬂowability and reducing water content while maintaining the desired 
workability. To control the viscosity of the concrete, viscosity modiﬁer admixtures 
are essential; typically, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) is used. Aggregate 
is also required to increase the stiffness of the concrete; normally conventional sand, 
and recycled concrete sand, and manufactured with a well-graded particle size distri-
bution to optimize packing density and rheology. On the other hand, coarse aggregate 
normally is excluded to ensure nozzle compatibility and avoid blocking [28, 29]. 262
L.

Assi et al. 15.3.4 
Reinforcement 
Due to the brittle failure modes of 3DPGC, which result from the low tensile-
to-compressive strength ratio, the incorporation of reinforcement is necessary to 
modify the structural performance of the printed concrete. However, incorporating 
reinforcement continuously into 3DPGC is challenging due to the layer-by-layer 
printing process [24]. Conventional steel reinforcement may be incorporated into 
3DPC either during or after printing. On the other hand, ﬁber reinforcements (i.e. steel, polypropylene, glass, carbon) can be added to the mixture design as in ﬁber-
reinforced concrete to enhance the ductility and reduce the propagation of cracks; 
it was found that steel ﬁber is not suitable for 3PDC because the bulging ﬁbers can 
obstruct the adhesion between interface layers [11]. The typical ﬁber content ranges 
from 0.5–2% by volume fraction of the mixture. The integration of mesh or grid 
materials within the layers of 3DPC concrete can also increase the tensile strength 
of the concrete and ensure a uniform distribution of the applied loads. Moreover, 
employing post-tensioning practices in 3DPC concrete structures could improve 
their load-bearing capability and mitigate cracks [30–33]. 15.3.5 
Summary of Technical Challenges 
Rheological properties, including yield stress and viscosity, are fundamental for 
asserting the shape of printed layers while accepting smooth bumps. However, 
achieving an optimum balance is challenging, as the high viscosity of geopolymer 
mixtures frequently indicates difﬁculties in extrusion and ﬂow. Studies have shown 
that the Si/Na ratio; which is a critical ratio in the alkaline solution to ensure the 
performance of geopolymer concrete, signiﬁcantly inﬂuences rheological proper-
ties; lower ratios enhance yield stress and structural rebuilding ability. Adjustments 
to rheology must also account for temperature and humidity variations, which can 
impact material behavior during printing. The use of high-viscosity alkali solution 
for geopolymer concrete and ﬁne aggregates poses challenges in maintaining homo-
geneity and avoiding blockages. Screw-based extrusion systems have been shown 
to mitigate some of these issues by providing continuous mixing during the extru-
sion process, nevertheless, this requires accurate control of particle size distribution 
and binder content of the designed mixtures. The open time of 3DPGC, which is 
the interval during which the mixture stays workable, is an important challenge. Potassium-based alkali solutions tend to decrease the open time compared to sodium-
based ones, leading to quicker settings and decreased workability during printing. This limitation limits the timeframe for achieving strong interlayer bonding and 
homogenous layer deposition. Buildability, which is the ability of the 3D-printed 
concrete to sustain the weight of sequential layers without collapsing, is a signiﬁcant 
concern for 3DPGC. Studies suggest that inadequa

te yield stress in fresh conditions

15
Advantages and Challenges of 3D Geopolymer-Based Concrete …
263
may lead to geometric alterations or collapse during the printing process. Addition-
ally, maintaining a uniform mixture during the extrusion process is necessary for 
inhibiting segregation and guaranteeing consistent mechanical properties. However, 
the incorporation of high-viscosity activation solution and ﬁne aggregates aggra-
vates segregation risks, speciﬁcally during persistent printing periods or under vari-
able environmental conditions. The inclusion of viscosity-modifying admixtures can 
improve the stability of fresh geopolymer concrete. Conversely, excessive use of such 
admixture can increase the mixture’s viscosity, complicating extrusion and print-
ability. Reinforcement incorporation, especially ﬁbers is another challenge that can 
affect the nozzle compatibility, uniform distribution of the ﬁbers, interference with 
layer bonding, and cost. 15.4 
Reliability Process and Limitations 
The reliability process of 3DPGC in general is still questionable since the application 
of this technology is contemporary and not standardized in codes and speciﬁcations. However, studies have proven the reliability of this technology such as accuracy 
and customization, reducing waste of materials, and the speed of the construction 
process. 3D printing techniques enable precise and sophisticated designs that are 
challenging to attain with conventional construction methods. The layer-by-layer 
methodology reduces waste compared to traditional poring procedures. In addition, 
structures can be printed more rapidly than they can be built using conventional 
approaches that involve formwork [30, 33]. However, studies have also stated the 
limitations of this technology. The printing procedure might lead to weaker interlayer 
bonds, yielding anisotropic mechanical properties and material inconsistencies since 
the rheological and setting characteristics of the concrete mixture are challenging. Other limitations can be the cost and the Size constraints; the preliminary investment 
for 3D printing equipment is substantial although these costs can be balanced out 
over time by savings in labor and materials. Difﬁculties also arise when attempting to 
expand 3D printing technology for large-scale construction because of restrictions 
in equipment size and logistical concerns [34, 35]. The durability of the 3DPGC 
still needs to be investigated entirely, such as the freezing and thawing cycles and 
exposure to deicing salt and sulfate attacks. Another limitation of 3DPGC is that 
alkali-activated concrete could have short open times and require careful handling 
to avoid early setting. 15.5 
Regulatory Challenges 
The regulatory challenges facing 3DPGC involve the development of new codes and 
standards that focus on these types of structures to achieve the desired safety and 
structural performance. This involves developing the aspects regarding rheol

ogy and

264
L. N. Assi et al. workability, load-bearing capacity, type of reinforcement, and long-term service-
ability under different environmental conditions. Quality control and certiﬁcation of 
the materials are also required. Testing of the materials utilized in 3D printing proce-
dures to satisfy established standards for strength, durability, and ecological impact 
is signiﬁcant. Therefore; guaranteeing reliable material quality and performance for 
geopolymer concrete is essential for regulatory compliance [36]. Considering the 
possible risks related to material handling, equipment operation, and worker safety 
is another point that needs to be considered regarding the regulations of 3DPGC. In addition, environmental inﬂuences such as sources of materials, management of 
wastes (i.e., use of by-product materials), and CO2 emissions generated from printing 
technology should be considered. However, geopolymer concrete has proven to be 
eco-friendly compared to conventional concrete; less energy is required and less CO2 
is released into the environment [1]. In short, these regulatory challenges emphasize 
that there is a need for cooperation between the industry sector, regulatory organiza-
tions, and research institutions such as universities to develop reliable schemes for 
the utilization of this innovative technology. 15.6 
Conclusions 
The integration of 3D printing technology with geopolymer-based concrete offers 
a transformative approach to addressing the challenges of modern construction. By 
leveraging the environmental, economic, and productivity beneﬁts of this innova-
tive technology, the construction industry can reduce its reliance on traditional, 
resource-intensive methods. The reduction in CO2 emissions, construction waste, 
and material usage underpins the sustainability of 3D geopolymer concrete, while 
its ability to lower costs and improve construction efﬁciency highlights its economic 
viability. Despite these advantages, challenges such as ensuring structural integrity, 
addressing anisotropy, and developing standardized regulatory frameworks remain 
critical areas for further research and development. Advances in reinforcement tech-
niques, optimization of mixture designs, and the reﬁnement of printing processes will 
be instrumental in overcoming these obstacles. Furthermore, collaboration among 
industry stakeholders, regulatory bodies, and researchers will be essential to estab-
lishing comprehensive guidelines and promoting widespread adoption. In conclu-
sion, 3D geopolymer-based concrete stands at the forefront of sustainable construc-
tion technologies, offering a promising path toward a more efﬁcient, environmentally 
conscious, and innovative construction industry. References

Chapter 16 
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel 
in Concrete Reinforcement 
Jake Cardillo, Sean Blackley, Lex Hartnett, Matthew Horan, Scott Rayburg, 
and Melissa Neave 
Abstract Due to environmental and global warming concerns, sustainable alterna-
tives to traditional materials in the building industry are rapidly gaining in popularity. Historically, the construction industry has relied heavily on steel reinforcement in 
concrete, however the emissions associated with the production of steel are large, 
and steel production will need to be drastically reduced (or green steel technologies 
will need to become commercially available) to meet net zero goals. One way to 
reduce steel use in construction is to replace steel reinforcement in concrete with 
sustainable alternatives. While a number of concrete reinforcement alternatives have 
been proposed and researched, no study has explored which of these are most suit-
able as replacements for steel across a range of structural, cost and environmental 
indicators. This study aims to investigate and compare traditional steel reinforce-
ment with emerging sustainable reinforcement types used in concrete, including 
recycled plastic reinforcement, carbon ﬁbre reinforcement, and natural reinforce-
ment types (bamboo and basalt). To accomplish this, a series of nested systematic 
literature reviews were conducted on each of these reinforcement types with a focus 
on strength, cracking control, cost, and carbon emissions. The results of this study 
show that all of the alternatives to steel for reinforcement have potential applications 
in industry. However, they all lack the ﬂexural strength of steel, and so are unsuited 
to situations where ﬂexural loads are limiting. In addition, carbon ﬁbre was shown 
to be an unsustainable alternative to steel, as its manufacture requires higher carbon 
emissions than steel, while recycled plastic and natural reinforcement options were 
shown to be similar or more sustainable than steel. Finally, the study identiﬁed a key 
weakness in the existing literature, in that

there are few studies that directly compare 
different reinforcement types against one another using the same sample character-
istics. If alternative reinforcements are to gain popularity, this issue will need to be

what applications they are most suited to. 16.1 
Introduction 
Global warming, resulting from anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions, has seen 
increases in atmospheric temperatures that are driving numerous global responses, 
including rising sea levels and more frequent natural disasters [1]. Although many 
human activities contribute to these increasing emissions, steel manufacturing has 
been identiﬁed as the most energy consuming and CO2 emitting industry in the 
world, contributing to more than 7% of global annual carbon emissions [2, 3]. Steel 
has multiple uses but over half of global steel production is consumed by building and 
infrastructure, with one of its most common applications being to reinforce concrete. Indeed, since the mid-nineteenth century, carbon intensive steel has been paired with 
concrete to form fundamental structural members that are high in both strength 
and durability [4] and that have become one of the most highly regarded construc-
tion materials currently utilised [5]. As steel usage in construction is projected to 
increase in coming years [6, 7], the construction industry needs to consider utilising 
more sustainable building materials to reduce its carbon footprint, with considera-
tions of alternatives to steel for reinforcement in concrete representing an important 
component of this. Steel reinforcement is an iron-carbon alloy hot rolled into bars, 6–50 mm in 
diameter [8]. Since its adoption in the mid-nineteenth century, carbon steel has 
become the most common material for reinforcing concrete structures. The high 
tensile strength and ductility, enhanced constructability, economic availability, and 
thermal expansion properties allow for its synergistic relationship with reinforced 
concrete, a composite construction material unbounded in its shape and size [9]. Despite its desirable properties and widespread use, steel reinforcement is a product 
of the iron and steel industry, the largest energy consuming and carbon dioxide 
emitting manufacturing sector in the world [10] and steel reinforcement produc-
tion processes contribute a signiﬁcant portion of the embodied carbon emissions 
of construction projects [11]. A potential beneﬁt of steel reinforcement is that it is 
highly recyclable, a property which provides an opportunity to utilise more efﬁcient 
production technologies. Unfortunately, the time-lag of scrap availability restricts 
the feasibility for greener (recycled) production methods being able to meet the 
world’s growing demand for steel reinforcement and there remains a reliance on 
virgin (primary) sourced materials [12]. This means that the construction industry 
needs to go beyond steel recycling and needs to do more

than wait for the develop-
ment of net-zero carbon “green steel” [13] if it is going to address the immediate 
issues surrounding its current high carbon emissions. Identiﬁcation of the high levels of carbon dioxide (CO2) being emitted by the steel 
manufacturing industry has led to investigations into potential sustainable concrete 
reinforcement alternatives [14]. Studies into these alternatives indicate they can be

16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
269
grouped into three categories: recycled plastic, recycled carbon ﬁbres and natural 
materials [15–20]. Whilst these studies have revealed capacity for each of these 
alternatives to provide reinforcement to concrete, there are yet to be any investiga-
tions that compare the performance of these sustainable reinforcement alternatives 
to not just traditional steel, but between each other to determine the most appropriate 
technology for the construction industry to adopt. Thus, the aim of this study is to 
redress this limitation in the existing literature and to provide clarity for an industry 
that is wanting to shift to more sustainable practices. Identifying sustainable alternatives for concrete reinforcement is not a new 
concept. Research into the development of recycled plastics for reinforcing concrete 
dates to the 1960s [21], while carbon ﬁbre reinforced polymers (CFRPs), devel-
oped using recycled carbon ﬁbres, began being utilised for concrete reinforcement 
in the early 1970s [22]. Even more recently, the availability of certain recycled mate-
rials has prompted further research into the possibility of natural reinforcements for 
concrete that include basalt, bamboo and other plant ﬁbres. However, the literature 
indicates that the performances of these sustainable concrete reinforcement alterna-
tives show considerable variation. For example, studies indicate that the use of plastics 
and carbon ﬁbres in concrete reinforcement enhances the mechanical characteristics 
of plain concrete, such as compressive, tensile, ﬂexural, and impact strengths, and 
provides increased resistance to cracking [23, 24]. Likewise, natural reinforcements 
such bamboo provide a tensile strength that is almost identical to steel [25]. However, 
there are limitations to most of the alternatives to using steel for concrete reinforce-
ment that suggest they are suitable for use only as non-structural members [20]. Thus, 
although there are possible alternatives to steel for concrete reinforcement there is 
also a need to consider the extent to which they can legitimately replace the current 
product. Several types of plastic have been used in the construction industry to reinforce 
concrete, with different applications reﬂecting their inherent properties. For example, 
Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP) rods or sheets are resistant to corrosion 
and are therefore often used in environments where traditional steel reinforcement 
would corrode (e.g., bridges or marine decks) [2

6]. Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer 
(CFRP), on the other hand, has a high strength-to-weight ratio and is used to wrap 
concrete columns and beams, increasing their load capacity and seismic resistance 
[27]. Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) and High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE) provide 
lightweight alternatives to using a coarse aggregate in concrete mixtures, potentially 
reducing pressure on the sourcing of natural materials [28]. These plastic-based 
reinforcements enhance the overall performance and longevity of concrete struc-
tures while reducing maintenance costs and improving resistance to environmental 
stressors. Using plastic as reinforcement in concrete offers several beneﬁts beyond its 
capacity to reduce the need for emissions intensive steel. Recycled plastic can be 
cost-effective and adaptable [29] and its redeployment for concrete reinforcement 
decreases the volume of plastic material that is sent to landﬁll [30–32]. These beneﬁts 
indicate that reprocessed plastics have several beneﬁts that make them appealing and

270
J. Cardillo et al. may result in them being implemented more frequently as reinforcement in concrete 
in the future [33]. Of the plastics mentioned above, GFRP, PVC and HDPE can all be readily sourced 
from recycled (post consumer and industrial) materials. CFRP, however, is less likely 
to incorporate recycled plastic because the thermosetting materials used in their 
production create permanent chemical bonds that are often not recoverable after use. Currently, only 2% of carbon ﬁbre (CF) products are recycled and reused each year 
in the UK [34], with the remaining being sent to landﬁll or incineration factories. Providing a market for decommissioned CF waste products represents an environ-
mentally friendly approach that is also cost effective and energy efﬁcient [35] and 
these products have some potential to work as alternatives for steel reinforcement in 
concrete. Indeed, several studies have proven that the addition of CF and CFRPs can 
greatly enhance the mechanical properties of plain concrete [1, 19] and point to the 
potential for CFRPs to provide reinforcement for concrete. However, ongoing work 
is required to develop systems that will more effectively enable the plastic matrix in 
CFRPs to be recycled. In addition to using recycled plastic and CF products for concrete reinforcement, 
several naturally occurring elements are also available. This approach of natural 
concrete reinforcement employs a variety of natural materials such as different 
woods, stone, and plants. Making use of these materials will advance the construction 
industry’s sustainable development agenda while also lowering waste production, 
conserving resources, and fostering a greener society. However, more research is 
required to ensure these materials are able to survive the intense strain of the struc-
tures they need to be used in. There are several natural materials that have been used 
for concrete reinforcement. The most comm

on is bamboo, which has strong ﬁbres 
that exhibit an impressive compressive strength, which is approximately twice that 
of concrete. Furthermore, its tensile strength is almost identical to that of steel [25]. Basalt, on the other hand, is a naturally occurring volcanic rock that is has extraordi-
nary chemical and thermal stabilities and its production generates no harmful gases 
or waste residue [36]. Finally, plant ﬁbres obtained from various sources, including 
jute, hemp, ﬂax, cotton, and sisal, are extremely cost effective while exhibiting a 
large range of mechanical, sustainable, thermal, and physical properties [37]. Thus, 
the use of natural products to reinforce concrete has immense potential to help the 
construction industry reduce its carbon emissions into the future. This research presents an in-depth analysis of existing literature examining the 
possibilities of using alternative sustainable materials in concrete as a viable replace-
ment for steel. The study will consider three dimensions for each concrete reinforce-
ment material: (1) its structural performance, (2) its environmental performance, 
and (3) its cost. Providing information about these dimensions will enable more 
informed decision making for those in the construction industry who are seeking 
more sustainable alternatives to their building practices. 16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
271
16.2 
Methods 
To achieve the research aims, this study utilizes a series of nested systematic liter-
ature reviews that explore current research into concrete reinforcement. In total 
four systematic literature reviews were conducted (Fig. 16.1). These investigated 
the existing literature examining (1) traditional steel, (2) recycled plastic, (3) recy-
cled carbon ﬁbre and (4) natural materials. For each reinforcement type, studies that 
investigate structural performance, environmental performance, cost and suitability 
for different applications were included. This provided a comprehensive analysis 
of existing knowledge and research into the use of various materials for concrete 
reinforcement, while enabling the identiﬁcation of possible gaps in the literature that 
might warrant further consideration [38]. The four systematic literature reviews were undertaken using a series of keywords 
entered into the large, multidisciplinary database known as Scopus. As Table 16.1 
indicates, these keywords began with a high-level search term (“reinforcement AND 
concrete”) that identiﬁed nearly 61,000 documents. These were subsequently honed 
down using increasingly focused search terms that resulted in 85, 68, 75 and 63 papers 
that considered traditional steel, recycled plastic, recycled carbon ﬁbres and natural 
materials, respectively. Each of these 291 papers was then examined to consider 
how the concrete reinforcement materials performed in terms of strength, cracking 
control, cost and carbon emissions. Fig. 16.1 Systematic literature review inclu

sions and exclusion criteria. Numbers indicate the 
document count for each criteria group

Concrete, in general, is extremely weak in tension [39] and thus, reinforcement 
members are required to adequately withstand the forces under which it is regu-
larly placed. There are several aspects to strength, all of which are important when 
evaluating the impacts of using concrete reinforcement. In this study, split tensile, 
compressive and ﬂexural strengths were all considered. The split tensile strength 
represents the ability of a reinforcement member to resist splitting under vertical 
loading [40] whereas compressive strength represents the ability of the member to 
withstand being compressed when placed under pressure. A main consideration when 
investigating compressive strength is that although concrete is generally good at with-
standing compression forces this is highly dependent on the mix design adopted [41]. To minimise potential inconsistencies in this parameter, a large selection of studies 
had to be considered. Finally, ﬂexural strength refers to the ability of the member to 
resist bending. All infrastructure design must take cracking control into consideration and there-
fore this was deemed an important parameter in this study. Concrete is the most 
used building material in world [42] but it is prone to cracking, which can impact 
the long-term effectiveness of the poured concrete. Thus, ability of the four rein-
forcement member types to limit crack widths was speciﬁcally considered, most 
commonly through comparisons of results from bending tests, which are used to 
evaluate the ﬂexural behaviour of materials. The application of a load to the testing 
material (i.e., reinforced concrete) results in the cracking of the material which repre-
sents its initial failure point under bending stress. As the test continues, the applied 
load increases until reaching the ultimate load, which signiﬁes the maximum load the 
material can withstand before complete failure. Crack widths resulting from these 
tests are closely monitored, offering insights into the material’s ductility and ability 
to absorb stress. Although this is a widely reported testing method, it should be noted 
that conditions between studies can vary in terms of beam sizing, and the number and 
spacing of stirrups used in the test, which can impact on ﬁnal performa

nce. These 
discrepancies will be reported when and if they occur. 16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
273
A primary consideration of any construction project is economic cost and this is 
a major driver for the selection of construction materials. Different reinforcement 
materials have the potential to improve the economic performance of reinforced 
concrete technology and, in doing so, reduce the total construction costs of projects. To understand the total economic costs of the alternative concrete reinforcements, 
raw material and reinforced concrete element costs were analysed separately. Whereas strength, cracking control and cost dimensions of concrete reinforce-
ment materials are of immediate interest to the construction industry, the control of 
carbon emissions is driven more by external pressures to reduce atmospheric pollu-
tion. Reporting about this parameter is now commonly expected and for this study 
estimates of the volume of carbon emitted by the different reinforcement materials 
are presented in terms of kg CO2/kg. 16.3 
Results 
An analysis of existing literature was undertaken to determine the performance of 
sustainable materials as concrete reinforcement. The alternative materials included 
recycled plastics, carbon ﬁbres and natural materials, with black carbon steel rein-
forcement as the control. In total, 180 articles were reviewed to assess the perfor-
mance of the reinforcement materials across four key criteria, that included: (1) 
strength; (2) cracking control; (3) cost analysis; and (4) carbon emissions. Of 
these 180 articles, 65 analysed strength performance, 58 tested cracking control, 
25 presented cost data and 32 analysed the carbon emissions of the alternative 
reinforcements. 16.3.1 
Strength Performance of Alterantive Reinforcements 
To guarantee adequate performance of concrete members, testing techniques have 
been developed to assess the tensile, compressive, and ﬂexural strengths of the various 
materials [43]. Such testing mechanisms are pivotal in deriving suitable applications 
for a material and outlining any possible limitations or defects [44]. To successfully 
replace carbon intensive steel in concrete reinforcement, alternative reinforcements 
must exhibit similar strength characteristics. 16.3.1.1
Split Tensile Strength 
The split tensile strength of steel and the alternative concrete reinforcement mate-
rials as determined by the systematic literature review are presented in Fig. 16.2. In the context of this study, split tensile strength indicates the ability of concrete 
to resist cracking and shrinking. Findings are returned for both plain concrete and

274
J. Cardillo et al. Fig. 16.2 Split tensile 
strength analysis of materials 
used to reinforce concrete 
for concrete that has been reinforced with the various products considered in this 
study. The results show that natural reinforcement materials, speciﬁcally bamboo, 
exhibit the best median value for ten

sile strength. Indeed, under splitting tensile loads 
bamboo has a median tensile strength of 4.77 MPa, which is an improvement of 54% 
over the results for plain concrete. Other natural products also performed strongly 
in this category. For example, although there was some variation in responses for 
the highly ductile basalt ﬁbre reinforced concrete, on average this product showed 
improvements of up to 30% over plain concrete. Plant ﬁbres like hemp, sisal, jute, and 
ﬂax were collectively shown to improve plain concrete splitting resistance by up to 
25%. Traditional steel returned a median split tensile strength of 4.20 MPa followed 
by recycled plastic at 3.60 MPa and carbon ﬁbres at 3.46 MPa. All of the reinforcing 
materials represent an improvement over plain concrete, which has a median result 
of 3.11 MPa. These results show that split tensile loading is a non-limiting factor for 
alternative materials as all alternatives improved the split tensile strength of concrete 
to some degree. 16.3.1.2
Compressive Strength 
The compressive strength of concrete and its various reinforcement materials is 
presented in Fig. 16.3. Compressive strength indicates a material’s ability to with-
stand being crushed or squashed when placed beneath a weight. This parameter is 
important for load bearing elements such as columns and walls. As was the case with 
the split tensile loading results, bamboo exhibited the largest increase in strength 
over the bare concrete, with compression improved by up to 52% (median value of 
59.45 MPa). The next best performing natural materials were basalt ﬁbres, improving 
compressive results by 10%, and natural plant ﬁbres, which increased the compres-
sive strength by 3–6%. Carbon ﬁbres demonstrated a 46% increase in compressive

behaviour with a median of 56.92 MPa. In contrast, steel only increased compressive 
strength by 2% (39.93 MPa), while the recycled plastic actually reduced compres-
sive strength. Indeed, the recycled plastic had a median compressive strength value of 
32.91 MPa, which represents a 16% reduction in strength compared to plain concrete. 16.3.1.3
Flexural Strength 
The ﬂexural strength of concrete and its reinforcement materials is presented in 
Fig. 16.4. Understanding ﬂexural strength, which measures the ability of a material 
to withstand bending, is critical for the design of beams and slabs. The median ﬂexural 
strength for steel reinforced beams was an impressive 39.90 MPa in comparison to 
plain concrete median results of 4.50 MPa in ﬂexural capacity. All of the alternative 
materials improved the ﬂexural loading of basic concrete, although to a much lower 
extent than steel. Of these, recycled plastic improved the ﬂexural strength by up 
to 24% (5.60 MPa), carbon ﬁbres the ﬂexural strength by up 28% (5.80 MPa) and 
bamboo reinforcement imp

roved ﬂexural strength by 30% (5.90 MPa). Lastly, basalt 
ﬁbres also increased the ﬂexural strength of concrete with improvements of up to 
30%, while natural ﬁbres such as hemp, sisal, jute, and ﬂax increased the ﬂexural 
strength of the concrete by only 3–10%. 276

16.3.2 
Cracking Control 
Cracking control results can be separated into ﬁrst crack load (Fig. 16.5) and ultimate 
crack load (Fig. 16.6). Each bar in Fig. 16.5 and Fig. 16.6 represents a different study 
examined in the systematic literature review (i.e., 16 papers in total). The results 
reveal that steel has the best ﬁrst crack load of any reinforcement type (average = 
47.64 kN). Indeed, all of the alternative reinforcement materials averaged below 
50% of the value obtained for steel, with bamboo averaging 22.13 kN, carbon ﬁbre 
averaging 17.03 kN, and recycled plastic materials averaging 21.13 kN. A similar 
pattern is observed for ultimate load with steel performing best (average= 107.84 kN) 
followed by carbon ﬁbre (average =79.3 kN), plastic materials (average = 69.38 kN), 
and bamboo (average = 44.7 kN). 16.3.3 
Cost Analysis 
The data for the economic costs of the raw materials and the reinforced concrete 
elements are presented in Figs. 16.7 and 16.8, respectively. In both cases, costs are 
given relative to steel, which is set as 1.0 on each graph. For raw materials, the 
median costs relative to the cost of steel are 0.13, 1.84 and 0.35 for plastic, carbon 
ﬁbre and natural material alternatives, respectively. Similar results can be seen for 
the reinforced concrete element costs, which relatively to steel are 0.60, 1.37 and 
0.64 for plastic, carbon ﬁbre and natural material alternatives, respectively. 16

16.3.4 
Carbon Emissions 
Carbon emissions provide some evidence of variations in the environmental perfor-
mances of the different materials used to reinforce concrete. Some papers within 
the systematic literature review provided information about this parameter and these 
results are presented in Table 16.1. The research indicates that the production of 
virgin (primary) and recycled (secondary) steel generates an average of 2.31 and 
0.40 kg CO2/kg, respectively. The carbon ﬁbre and plastic alternatives to steel have 
considerably higher carbon emissions for both virgin and recycled products. Even 
considering that the research revealed a range of results, these products are consis-
tently more carbon intensive than steel. In contrast, both the bamboo and basalt ﬁbre 
alternatives recorded lower carbon emissions than either virgin or recycled steel. These ﬁndings also indicate that the production of recycled steel is a viable alterna-
tive for reducing carbon emissions, although the often limited availability of supply 
is a factor that reduces its widespread use. 16.4

Discussion 
The ongoing production of steel for use within reinforced concrete structures is 
accelerating the detrimental impacts of global warming. As such, the adoption of 
alternative sustainable reinforcements presents an opportunity to promote sustain-
ability within the construction industry and negate the impending consequences of 
climate change that are resulting from it. This study aims to determine the potential 
of recycled plastics, recycled carbon ﬁbre and natural materials as sustainable and 
viable alternatives to using steel for reinforcing concrete. This study investigates this 
according to four key criteria, namely: (1) strength; (2) cracking control; (3) cost; 
and (4) carbon emissions. Comparisons are made between reinforcement material 
types and between the criteria to provide an interpretation of which material the 
construction industry should be considering. 16.4.1 
Strength Analysis 
Currently, no studies provide a succinct analysis of the ability of alternative reinforce-
ments to improve the strength parameters of concrete. This investigation provides 
a summary of different tests performed across multiple studies using a variety of 
techniques and materials. The results of this study showed that all alternatives offer 
similar performance to steel in reinforcing concrete members when subject to split-
ting and compressive forces. However, the superiority of steel is evident in the ﬂexural 
testing results with continuous steel bars able to withstand ﬂexural loads up to eight 
time larger than the other reinforcement options. 280
J. Cardillo et al. The split tensile strength of a reinforcement member is heavily affected by the 
bond between the reinforcing materials and the concrete [45]. Natural materials, 
speciﬁcally bamboo, displayed impressive results in split tensile testing scenarios that 
were similar to the performances exhibited by steel. The unique anatomical structure 
of bamboo can help explain this result as staggered arrangements of vascular bundles 
effectively distribute the stresses imposed [40]. Furthermore, the ﬁbre composi-
tion of bamboo is oriented in a longitudinal direction meaning applied loads in the 
same direction allow higher resistance to splitting forces. Also, the ﬁbre density of 
bamboo allows great stiffness in small volumes of members [46]. Lastly, bamboo has 
great bonding capabilities to concrete which helps prevent members from splitting 
apart and results in the impressive splitting resistance [47]. Meanwhile, basalt and 
plant ﬁbre reinforced concrete also represent versatile and sustainable reinforcement 
options when under splitting loads with both of these improving the properties of 
plain concrete. This suggests there is a potential target market for natural reinforcing 
materials in countries where steel is a difﬁcult or expensive to procure. Possible 
regions for sustainable infrastructure that utilise local plant and rock ﬁbres without 
hindering performance may be c

ountries like China, India, and Bangladesh [48]. Similar to bamboo in split tensile loading results, recycled plastic provides an 
improvement over plain concrete strength with a median that is only slightly below 
that returned for steel. However, recycled plastic displays ductile properties that allow 
visual deformation prior to failure, which is an important serviceability factor [49]. However, plastic is a cheap material that can be sourced for landﬁlls and existing 
waste streams. This could provide an opportunity for the construction industry to 
reduce pollution by diverting these materials into repurposed applications through the 
reinforcing of concrete to be used under certain circumstances. For example, plastic 
ﬁbre reinforcement could lead to a reduction of steel reinforcement requirements in 
members subject to splitting forces such as bicycle lanes and footpaths [50]. Lastly, 
carbon ﬁbre reinforcement materials returned the lowest split tensile median of all of 
the possible alternatives to steel. This can be associated with the material properties 
of carbon ﬁbres which can be brittle in failure [51]. In addition to this, single strip 
carbon ﬁbres generally have a weaker bond to concrete which prevents effective 
transfer mechanisms of stress through the member in tension [52]. The compressive strength values largely depend on the mix design of the concrete 
being used. For this study, testing results were taken at their 28-day strength value. Also, for compressive strength results it was assumed that the water to cement (w/c) 
ratio and type of aggregate were kept relatively consistent across studies to minimize 
discrepancies. Overall, the median value of plain concrete compressive strength was 
set to 40 MPa which is typically used in structural members like footings, industrial 
ﬂooring and retaining walls [53]. Plastic ﬁbres were shown not to increase the median 
value of concrete under compression for this strength value. However, there may be 
a limitation in this assumption that plastics have an adverse effect on compressive 
results due to the mix designs and ﬁbre inclusion percentages explored in the liter-
ature. For example, [45] tested the strength of 30 MPa concrete with polypropylene 
(plastic) ﬁbres and showed that the compressive strength was increased by about 9%. Similarly, [54] analyzed compressive results of the same strength grade (30 MPa)
