with polypropylene ﬁbres and found that compressive strength increased by up to 
23%. However, when the strength grade of the concrete was increased to 50 MPa, 
plastic ﬁbres were found to decrease the compressive strength of the concrete. Carb

on ﬁbres were shown to have a positive impact on the strength of compressed 
concrete due to its ductile nature under loading conditions. Similarly, the natural 
reinforcement options also displayed relative increases in compressive capacity with 
bamboo being the best performing natural material in concrete reinforcement. Carbon 
ﬁbre, bamboo and basalt all had similar performance in terms of compressive strength 
improvement, meaning all offer similar beneﬁts relative to plain concrete. An issue that can arise with ﬁbre additions to concrete is that the workability 
of concrete mixes lessen as the volume percentage of ﬁbres is increased. Higher 
contents of ﬁbre within cement may result in poorer distribution and decreased level 
of compaction leading to a lessened compressive strength [55]. Table 16.2 shows 
how the optimal percentage of included ﬁbres by volume seems to be 1%, (which 
maximises compressive strength) with values above or below 1% resulting in reduc-
tions in compressive strength. Ultimately, to best determine whether plastic and 
other ﬁbres improve or reduce the compressive strength of concrete, a wider variety 
of studies will need to be conducted, testing across more potential concrete strength 
and ﬁbre inclusion percentages (Table 16.2). Flexural strength is important for long spanning members such as concrete beams, 
columns, pipes, or culverts [56]. Based on the results from 70 studies, poor ﬂexural 
strength outcomes for all concrete reinforcement alternatives is the main limitation 
for the replacement of steel as reinforcement for structural concrete. However, this 
does not mean that there is no place for such alternatives. There are instances where 
the ﬂexural strength of a member is not critical, and in these circumstances recycled 
plastic, carbon ﬁbres and natural materials could be used [57]. These could include 
secondary beams and/or footings although further research would need to validate 
the utility of steel replacements in these instances. 16.4.2 
Cracking Control 
The cracking control of the sustainable alternatives to steel for concrete reinforce-
ment plays a key role in determining the application of each material. For bamboo, 
several important considerations arise when analysing its performance in concrete. For example, [58] found that bamboo culms in their natural state can withstand large

282
J. Cardillo et al. loads. Meanwhile, [59] found that treated bamboo delivered even better results. These ﬁndings indicate that the treatment process signiﬁcantly improves bamboo’s 
load-bearing capacity. [60] and [61] further reinforced these ﬁndings, revealing that 
bamboo evidences consistent average ﬁrst crack loads, although there were variations 
in deﬂection between the specimens, suggesting there are some potential differences 
in structural integrity. These results have important signiﬁcance for the construction 
sector, suggesting that bamboo may be an effective replacement for steel in certain 
s

cenarios, such as the small-scale construction of slabs, particularly in areas where 
bamboo is prevalent. The ﬁndings of this study suggest that bamboo, especially when 
treated, holds promise as a sustainable reinforcement material for concrete. Carbon ﬁbre reinforced polymers (CFRP) tend to perform well in terms of 
cracking control, especially for ultimate load. These results were consistent across 
studies. The signiﬁcance of these ﬁndings lies in the promising application of carbon 
ﬁbre rebar in enhancing the structural integrity of concrete. With consistent and 
robust performances demonstrated across studies, the research highlights this mate-
rial’s potential to modernise construction practices, offering a lighter yet stronger 
alternative to traditional steel reinforcement. While there are areas for future explo-
ration, the data indicates a potential future for using carbon ﬁbre on its own, but also 
including it as a hybrid material with steel. There are a wide variety of recycled materials that have been explored in the 
literature. For most of these, only limited observations have been made. For example, 
[62] were able to demonstrate the exceptional potential of synthetic waste wire, which 
exhibited a high ﬁrst crack average. These cracking results suggest the feasibility 
of synthetic waste wire as a strong reinforcement material, challenging traditional 
opinions about using recycled materials. Meanwhile, [63] and [64] explored the utility 
of glass in different forms (rebar and ﬁbre), with glass rebar outperforming glass ﬁbres 
in terms of cracking control. These results suggest that glass, as a recycled material, 
might offer an environmentally friendly alternative to traditional steel reinforcement. For plastics, cracking control results are mixed, ranging from second best after steel, 
to amongst the lowest cracking control. This obviously depended on the nature of 
the product being considered. The best performing material in terms of cracking control was steel, although 
the range of outcomes was again quite large between studies. These variations arise 
from differing test conditions, such as variations in the number or reinforcing bars or 
specimen size. The signiﬁcance of these ﬁndings lies in reafﬁrming the critical role 
steel plays in construction. Because of these results, steel reinforcement continues 
to be a reliable choice for construction, offering relatively consistent and generally 
high-performance outcomes. 16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
283
16.4.3 
Cost Analysis 
Existing literature provides limited discussion on the economic performance of 
concrete reinforcement alternatives. It is also very difﬁcult to compare costs of rein-
forcement across studies given differences in material densities, quantity of reinforce-
ment material, units of costing, and availability of material in varying locations. This 
objective of this study to incorporate numerous sources of data and com

pare the mate-
rial and element costs of the various reinforcement materials provides industry profes-
sionals insight into the actual costings of recycled plastics, recycled carbon ﬁbres 
and natural materials in comparison to the industry standard of steel reinforcement. When considering economic costs, traditional black carbon steel reinforcement 
was used as the control in this study. The cost of black carbon steel reinforcement is 
relatively stable in the market given its use as the traditional material for inclusion 
in reinforced concrete. Material costs are heavily dependent on the grade of steel 
with prices ranging from USD $660 [65] to $1361 [66] per tonne. The most common 
steel reinforcement grade is the 500N (Type 60) steel costing on average USD $1065 
per tonne. The results of this study show that steel reinforcement is generally more 
expensive than recycled plastic and natural alternatives but less costly than carbon 
ﬁbre reinforcements. However, the use of black steel reinforcement engenders greater 
costs during the construction period relative to carbon ﬁbre reinforcement due to its 
high density requiring higher labour costs and larger construction equipment to place 
the reinforcement [67]. The susceptibility of black steel reinforcement to corrosion 
also requires ongoing maintenance costs and greater life cycle costs in comparison 
to carbon ﬁbre reinforcement [66]. This is considered one of the greatest limitations 
of steel reinforcement often requiring ongoing inspection and maintenance activities 
with a reduced design life resulting in more frequent demolition and reconstruc-
tion when compared to the use of alternative non-corrosive reinforcements such as 
carbon ﬁbres and recycled plastics [68]. Black steel’s susceptibility to corrosion also 
necessitates more stringent design requirements, often resulting in the inclusion of 
larger sections and superﬂuous reinforcement designs to meet the design life for the 
steel reinforced concrete structures [69, 70]. Despite these limitations, black steel 
reinforcement remains the most utilised concrete reinforcement in the world and is 
widely available across the construction industry. In comparison to steel, recycled plastic reinforcement proved to be the least costly 
alternative of those analysed in this study, with an average price of USD $3273 
per m3. This compares to steel ﬁbres which cost on average USD $9263 per m3. The signiﬁcant price difference makes recycled plastic an attractive alternative to 
steel and, not surprisingly, its use has grown signiﬁcantly in recent years [71]. The 
density of plastic ﬁbres is also beneﬁcial to its mass per cost efﬁciency in comparison 
to dense steel ﬁbres. Plastic ﬁbres have densities ranging from 900 to 1800 kg/m3, 
which is signiﬁcantly less than steel ﬁbres that have a density of 7860 kg/m3. As such, 
the use of plastic ﬁbres requires less mass of reinforcement material per volume of 
concrete and saves additional transp

ortation costs in comparison to steel due to the 
reduced weight of the concrete mix [72]. However, the use of plastic ﬁbres to reinforce

284
J. Cardillo et al. concrete also requires increased costs associated with the use of superplasticizers, a 
concrete additive required to maintain the workability of the concrete and which can 
cost up to 10 times the price of a standard cement binder [72, 73]. Signiﬁcantly, recycled plastic reinforcement is only appropriate for use as concrete 
reinforcement ﬁbres and not in continuous reinforcement bars and, as such, all 
comparisons in this study are made to steel ﬁbre reinforcements. Plastic ﬁbres proved 
to be on average 39% cheaper than steel ﬁbres when utilised for reinforced concrete 
pavements, such as for concrete footpaths and roads. In comparison to non-reinforced 
concrete pavements, pavements reinforced by plastic ﬁbres were on average 5–15% 
cheaper due to the reduced thickness (10–20%) of the pavements. In contrast, the 
signiﬁcant cost of steel ﬁbres increased the cost of reinforced pavements by 8–20%, 
even considering a 32–35% average reduction in the pavement thickness due to its 
structural properties [72–74]. As such, the cost efﬁciency of recycled plastic in ﬁbres 
to reinforce concrete pavements is clearly a cost-effective alternative in comparison 
to steel. The cost of carbon ﬁbre for concrete reinforcement varies widely depending on 
the type of ﬁbre used, the manufacturing process utilised and the capacity of the 
manufacturing operation. The use of carbon ﬁbre reinforced bars proved to be the 
most expensive reinforcement analysed in this study, costing a median of 223% above 
the price of steel reinforced bars. This high cost is due to the larger material costs 
associated with producing continuous carbon ﬁbre reinforcement bars. The manu-
facturing of carbon ﬁbre reinforcement is a complex process requiring specialised 
equipment and expertise. It is considered more labour-intensive and technologically 
advanced than steel production resulting in greater production costs. Given its use as a 
high-performance material with an impressive strength-to-weight ratio, carbon ﬁbre 
reinforcement bars tend to be used in more specialist applications where cost consid-
erations are secondary to performance. This lower economy of scale in comparison 
to steel reinforcement further accounts for the higher material costs of carbon ﬁbres. Despite their high production costs, carbon ﬁbre bars are lighter than steel reinforce-
ment bars, allowing for greater prefabrication, reduced transport costs and reduced 
placement costs [69]. As such, the use of carbon ﬁbre reinforcement generally entails 
reduced construction costs in comparison to steel reinforcement [65, 75]. Overall, 
carbon ﬁbre reinforcement is not considered a cost-effective alternative to steel rein-
forcement in most applications. Its signiﬁcant upfront costs generally make carbon 
ﬁbre reinforcement too costly in comparison to

other alternatives. However, given 
the large upfront costs, this material may be considered an economic option for use in 
large-scale reinforced concrete structures that have a design life in excess of 50 years. In comparison to carbon ﬁbre reinforcement, natural materials such as bamboo, 
basalt and various plant ﬁbres are highly cost effective in comparison to steel rein-
forcement. The median price of these materials was 35% lower than the cost of steel. Similarly, the cost of reinforced concrete elements utilising natural reinforcements 
were an average of 36% lower than the cost of steel. Natural materials have appli-
cations in both reinforcement ﬁbres and continuous reinforcing bars and bamboo 
and basalt are key natural materials utilised to reinforce continuous concrete bars. Bamboo particularly shows impressive economic savings with a cost ratio of 0.63

16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
285
in comparison to steel. Applications such as its use in low-cost earthquake-resistant 
buildings has the potential to reduce total building costs by 5.5%, with the cost of 
reinforced concrete stiffening members, such as sloof and ring beams, reduced by 
up to 38% in comparison to a steel reinforced alternative [76]. As such, the use of 
bamboo has signiﬁcant potential for employment in earthquake prone areas such as 
Indonesia where stiffening members are often ignored due to the high price of steel 
reinforcement. In contrast, basalt ﬁbre reinforced polymer bars showed a 200% cost 
increase in comparison to the cost of steel. This is mainly due to its relatively recent 
adoption in concrete reinforcement (reﬂecting a low economy of scale) and limited 
availability [77]. Natural reinforcement ﬁbres were the best performing natural material with a 
median cost ratio of 0.24 relative to steel. However, this result is based on a very 
limited literature. [78] suggest these materials are available in abundance at reduced 
prices, while acknowledging the price of natural ﬁbre reinforcement varies greatly 
across geographic regions. Another factor that helps keep costs down for natural 
reinforcement materials is that some natural ﬁbres, such as cellulose, do not impede 
the slump and workability of concrete in comparison to other ﬁbre alternatives. This 
reduces the need for expensive superplasticizer additives [79]. 16.4.4 
Carbon Emissions 
For a concrete reinforcement material to be considered sustainable, having low CO2 
emissions relative to traditional virgin steel is essential. In response to global warming 
and to reduce the carbon footprint of the construction industry, research has been 
conducted into the use of recycled, as opposed to virgin, steel for reinforcing concrete 
[80]. The unique ability of steel to retain its metallurgic properties over repeated recy-
cling, establishes it as the world’s most recyclable material and assures its consistent 
quality [81]. Some of the largest beneﬁts of recycling steel

can be seen in the reduc-
tion of energy consumption throughout the production process, which also directly 
corresponds to lower carbon emissions. A study by [82], showed that for every 1 kg 
of steel scrap recycled at end of life, 1.5 kg CO2 equivalent emissions could be 
saved by voiding virgin production processes. These results were supported by other 
studies, including those by [83] and [84] who found that a saving of 1.6 kg CO2/kg 
was observed in steel recycling processes when compared to virgin production. Although recycled steel is preferred to virgin steel in terms of its environmental 
performance, certain recycled plastics can provide even lower emissions than recy-
cled steel, while having the additional beneﬁt of removing that plastic (at least 
temporarily) from the waste stream. The computation of average material carbon 
emissions from the literature reviewed in this study revealed that recycled plastic was 
comparable to virgin steel in terms of its associated carbon emissions. This suggests 
this is a potentially viable material to replace virgin steel and has the added beneﬁt 
of removing plastic from the waste stream. Although recycled steel is preferred to 
virgin steel in terms of its environmental performance, certain recycled plastics can

286
J. Cardillo et al. provide even lower emissions than recycled steel, while having the additional beneﬁt 
of removing that plastic (at least temporarily) from the waste stream. The computa-
tion of average material carbon emissions from the literature reviewed in this study 
revealed that recycled plastic was comparable to virgin steel in terms of its associated 
carbon emissions. This suggests this is a potentially viable material to replace virgin 
steel and has the added beneﬁt of removing plastic from the waste stream. However, 
unlike steel, recycled plastic can only be used once before becoming unﬁt for further 
use in the construction industry. This is because recycling plastic into ﬁbres cannot 
be reversed [85] and indicates that plastic ﬁbres are likely to end up in landﬁll at its 
end of life. This clearly limits the potential for plastic to be used as a meaningfully 
sustainable alternative to virgin steel. In addition to recycled plastics, an investigation into the sustainable performance 
of recycled carbon ﬁbre (CF) was also undertaken. Unlike steel, the concept of 
using recycled CF in the construction industry is relatively new, with the demand 
for this material increasing over the last decade [86]. Studies performed by [87] 
and [88] established that virgin production of CF created emissions of between 
22 and 25 kg CO2/kg produced. This ﬁgure was signiﬁcantly reduced to between 
3.73 and 4.65 kg CO2/kg produced when utilising mechanical recycling [87, 88] 
and resulting in a reduction in carbon emissions of 82.2%. Despite these signiﬁcant 
results, recycled CF emissions were still much higher than those for virgin steel, 
suggesting it is not a viable alternati

ve to steel in terms of its impact on greenhouse 
gas emissions. Products such as bamboo and basalt do not have existing recycling systems and, 
as a consequence, this study could not investigate emissions associated with recy-
cling these materials. As such, this study only considers the virgin production of 
these materials. Bamboo only grows in certain climates and requires speciﬁc soil 
conditions, which means it is not an easily accessible material for all locations [89]. This means that bamboo ﬁbres that have travelled long distances will have higher 
CO2 emissions in their production process. Producing bamboo ﬁbres can emit as 
little as 0.13 kg CO2/kg, but this ﬁgure will substantially increase if transportation 
distances are high [90]. Despite this, bamboo ﬁbres that have been locally sourced 
and produced seem to be one of the most environmentally friendly reinforcement 
alternatives. An additional reinforcement option that has been widely researched is 
basalt ﬁbres. There aren’t many locations where basalt cannot be found, making it 
a widely accessible reinforcement option when produced in ﬁbrous form [91]. [92] 
and [93] examined the life cycle analysis of basalt ﬁbres and found that the emis-
sions associated with production varied between 0.43 and 0.49 kg CO2/kg. Although 
the energy requirements to produce basalt ﬁbres are considerably higher than that 
of bamboo ﬁbres, the CO2 emissions are comparable and choosing either of these 
natural reinforcements would result in signiﬁcantly lower emissions than virgin steel 
and comparable or lower emissions than recycled steel [91]. 16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
287
16.4.5 
Synthesis 
The detrimental contributions of steel production to global warming are challenging 
the construction industry to consider the use of alternatives to it. The examination of 
three alternative concrete reinforcement materials, namely recycled plastics, carbon 
ﬁbres and natural alternatives, presents an opportunity to produce a more sustain-
able built environment, although it also highlights limitations for each material in 
terms of its the potential to replace the consistently high performing traditional steel 
reinforcement. This section provides a synthesis of the ﬁndings for these proposed 
materials. Recycled plastic ﬁbres, both PVC and HDPE, show the lowest strength perfor-
mance of the reinforcement materials analysed, proving to be considerably weak 
in some parameters in comparison to steel reinforcement. The ﬂexural strength of 
recycled plastic ﬁbre reinforcement was signiﬁcantly less than that of steel, which is 
a major limitation on the application of plastic reinforcement for concrete structures. The materials performance through cracking control tests proved more promising, 
although it is still inferior in comparison to structural steel reinforcement. Recy-
cled plastic did prove to be the most cost-effective reinforcement material, being 
signiﬁcantly ch

eaper than steel. Finally, in terms of its sustainability credentials, 
recycled plastic reinforcements outperformed steel with regards to CO2 emissions 
during their production, while simultaneously having the beneﬁt of removing plastic 
from the waste stream. The production of virgin plastics, however, is not a sustain-
able alternative to steel reinforcement as its emissions are much higher than that 
of virgin steel. Overall, recycled plastic reinforcement should be considered as a 
sustainable and cost-effective alternative to steel reinforcement in lighter loading 
applications such as footpath pavements and bicycle lanes, with additional strength 
and serviceability tests required prior to each application. In comparison to plastics, recycled carbon ﬁbres showed little promise as a truly 
sustainable reinforcement alternative. This was due to the carbon emissions of their 
production, more than doubling the carbon emissions of virgin steel reinforcement 
production. Carbon ﬁbres did show strong strength characteristics for split tensile 
and compressive strengths, but similarly to plastics, lacked the ﬂexural performance 
of steel reinforcement. The brittle nature of carbon ﬁbres also resulted in the greatest 
cracking of the materials tested, a well-documented limitation of the material given 
its poor ductility. Carbon ﬁbre also proved to be the costliest material analysed, a 
signiﬁcant setback for its application in a cost centric construction industry. Similarly, to recycled plastics, natural materials were also found to be effective as 
sustainable materials in concrete reinforcement. The use of bamboo outperformed 
the characteristics of steel in both split tensile and compressive strength, with basalt 
also producing competitive results. Again, steel proved to be superior with regards 
to the ﬂexural strength performance, clearly outperforming bamboo and basalt, both 
of which showed greater ﬂexural strengths in comparison to recycled plastics and 
carbon ﬁbre reinforcements. Natural ﬁbres however showed the least ﬂexural strength

288
J. Cardillo et al. of all the three alternative materials, perhaps the greatest limitation in their applica-
tion in reinforced concrete members. The cracking resistance of bamboo was again 
inferior to steel, raising concerns on its ability to satisfy the serviceability require-
ments of reinforced concrete members. Despite this, the cost performance of bamboo, 
basalt and other natural ﬁbres was signiﬁcantly more attractive than steel reinforce-
ment, a substantial beneﬁt to increase its supply chains and application within the 
industry. Natural materials were proven to be the most sustainable alternative mate-
rial studied, showing the least embodied energy and carbon emissions when utilised 
as concrete reinforcement, with the majority of their energy inputs and carbon emis-
sions associated with their supply chains. Overall, natural materials have excep-
tional potential as an alternative to st

eel reinforcement, with bamboo showing the 
most promising results with regards to its strength contributions, cracking durability, 
economic performance and reduced embodied energy and carbon emissions. 16.4.6 
Recommendations 
Despite some of the limitations of the materials discussed throughout this study the 
results show a clear beneﬁt when adopting alternative reinforcements such as recycled 
plastics and natural materials. The results are particularly encouraging across the 
economic and sustainability parameters aligning with the interests of construction 
professionals and the aims and objectives of this study. However, this study indicates 
the need for more work on this topic, particularly surrounding the strength and 
cracking control performance of the sustainable reinforcement materials to increase 
conﬁdence in their application. Recycled plastics and natural materials were proven 
to show inferior strength and cracking characteristics in comparison to carbon steel 
reinforcement. As such, it is not feasible to suggest they are superior alternatives 
to safely provide the required reinforcement in all concrete structures and further 
investigation is required to determine the limits of their possible application. Existing 
literature shows strong potential in reinforced pavement applications however, further 
research should be undertaken to broaden their potential use with strong reference 
to the economic and sustainable beneﬁts demonstrated through this study. Further research should also be focused on developing new recycling methods 
for materials such as plastics, steel, and carbon ﬁbres to improve the embodied 
energy and carbon emissions of their production. The construction industry needs to 
continuously ﬁnd ways to improve the sustainability of its supply chain and as such, 
should seek for the continuous improvement of recycling processes to reduce overall 
energy inputs and carbon emissions when utilising recycled materials as concrete 
reinforcement. Finally, the use of hybrid reinforced structures should be investigated for its poten-
tial to provide immediate sustainability improvements in the construction industry. The ability to combine the structural and durability characteristics of steel, with the 
sustainable and economic beneﬁts of recycled plastics and natural materials presents 
potentially viable options that could help to negate the exponential growth of steel

16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
289
reinforcements. As such, for the successful application of sustainable reinforcement 
it is necessary for future studies to build upon the ﬁndings of this research and fully 
understand the capabilities of sustainable materials for concrete reinforcement. 16.4.7 
Research Limitations 
The capacity to identify which materials represent the preferable alternative for rein-
forcing concrete was limited by the lack of uniformity between the studies that were 
investigated. This is espec

ially evident in the wide ranges of concrete mixes tested, 
which often resulted in vast differences in results between studies, particularly in 
terms of strength. To improve the capacity of companies to compare data about 
different forms of concrete reinforcement, standard measurements of these materials 
are required. In addition, the measurement of split tensile strength in natural elements like 
bamboo must consider the variability of the material, with a requirement that the 
temperature at the time of testing be controlled [94]. The moisture content must 
also be consistent amongst samples and the species and age of the plant must be 
controlled to obtain reliable data for comparisons to typical homogenous steel [95]. Unfortunately, these factors are rarely controlled for or reported on in the literature, 
once again limiting the capacity to undertake a systematic analysis of the behaviour 
of bamboo. Similarly, the measurement of the strength of plastic and carbon ﬁbres 
must also be considered and the material quality needs to be reported as the purity of 
recycled materials can lessen strength capacities [49]. Lastly, due to a lack of speciﬁc 
standardization across the industry no single reinforcement conﬁguration was able 
to be studied. Essentially allowing for a range of variable factors through irregularity 
in reinforcement sizing, orientation, quality, volume percentage and composition. Despite the promising results, limitations exist within the study of cracking. Testing conditions often varied between studies, such as whether stirrups were used 
or the number and spacing of the stirrups, can inﬂuence results and give values 
outside the expected ranges. This introduces variability, affecting the comparability 
of results. Furthermore, the number of reinforcing bars also differs between tests. Although similar results were found using mostly two bottom reinforcing bars, other 
tests utilised one or three. Ultimately, this study has highlighted a lack of consis-
tency across all concrete reinforcement types in terms of how the materials were 
treated, the concrete mixes used and even the testing setups. Until research addresses 
these issues, it remains unclear exactly how these alternatives will perform under 
standardised conditions. Finally, a lack of data on materials like carbon ﬁbre, bamboo and basalt make it 
difﬁcult to estimate accurately estimate CO2 emissions associated with their produc-
tion processes. For emissions, transportation was a major contributor to the varying 
results seen in this study and this is challenging to fully account for. Longer distances 
travelled by materials will results in higher CO2 emissions, but it is difﬁcult to directly

290
J. Cardillo et al. compare these results because they are relative to the site of application of the mate-
rial [96]. Finally, the production or recycling method utilised was the biggest factor 
in energy requirements observed during the production process. Some re

cycling 
methods, such as pyrolysis, will use up to 30 times more energy [97] than simpler 
recycling methods, such as mechanical recycling, leading to much higher estimated 
CO2 emissions. This complicates the comparison of emissions beneﬁts derived from 
using alternative materials for concrete reinforcement. 16.5 
Conclusion 
The ongoing production of carbon intensive steel reinforcement for use in rein-
forced concrete technology continues to exacerbate the detrimental impacts of global 
warming. There is a growing need for the application of alternative materials to 
provide the necessary structural reinforcement in concrete structures with potential 
to reduce the consequential embodied energy inputs and carbon dioxide emissions 
associated with the production of steel reinforcement. The use of recycled plastic, 
recycled carbon ﬁbre and natural alternatives may provide an opportunity to negate 
the increasing CO2 emissions resulting from the increasing use of reinforced concrete 
by the construction industry. However, to date a direct comparison of the potential 
of these different materials has not been undertaken. This study aimed to determine the suitability of recycled plastics, recycled carbon 
ﬁbre, and natural materials as sustainable reinforcements to provide viable alter-
natives to the use of steel in reinforced concrete structures. A systematic literature 
review was undertaken on these alternative reinforcement materials with respect to 
their: (1) strength; (2) cracking control; (3) cost; and (4) carbon emissions. For an 
alternative reinforcement material to be deemed a viable alternative to steel, they must 
meet stringent strength requirements to ensure the continued safety of reinforced 
concrete technology. This study analysed the strength of reinforcement through a 
review of the split tensile, compressive, and ﬂexural strengths of the materials, all 
of which represent integral performance criteria in the selection of reinforcement 
materials. The ﬂexural strength of steel reinforcement was shown to be unmatched 
by the alternative materials, immediately placing limitations on the use of the other 
materials within structural applications, particularly for concrete members subject to 
large ﬂexural loads. Alternative materials did however outperform steel across split 
tensile and compressive strength tests, validating their application as a viable rein-
forcement alternative where ﬂexural loads are not limiting. Concrete reinforcement is 
also crucial to limit the propagation of cracks throughout concrete structures. While 
it is reasonable to expect that cracking will occur in concrete, effective reinforce-
ment designs limit the onset and widths of cracking, ensuring reinforced concrete 
members meet both strength and serviceability requirements. The study found steel 
to be the dominant material with the largest ﬁrst cracking load. Although recycled 
plastics, recycled carbon ﬁbre and natural materials showed inferi

or performance in

16
Sustainable Alternatives to Steel in Concrete Reinforcement
291
comparison to steel reinforcement, they performed well enough to suggest that they 
could still be suitable for certain applications. The alternative materials showed greater promise in their economic performances, 
a major consideration in the selection of construction materials. Recycled plastics 
and natural materials signiﬁcantly outperformed steel across material costing and 
reinforced concrete element costing. In contrast, recycled carbon ﬁbres exhibited 
increased economic costs across both categories. To ensure each material met the 
aims and objectives of this study a sustainability analysis was also undertaken to 
determine carbon emissions associated with the production of each material. Recy-
cled plastic ﬁbres and natural reinforcements indicated signiﬁcant improvements 
over the sustainability of steel, whereas recycled carbon ﬁbres showed an increase 
in carbon emissions in comparison to steel. As such, recycled carbon ﬁbres are not 
considered a sustainable alternative given they do not meet the aims of this study. This research investigated multiple concrete reinforcement alternatives across 
key performance criteria, accentuating the need to consider sustainable concrete 
reinforcement and its application within a greener construction industry. The study 
reveals there is a place for recycled plastics and natural material in concrete rein-
forcement and encourages industry standards and engineers to pursue the speciﬁc 
application of these materials to ensure a greener future. However, much research 
is still needed, especially with respect to standardised testing conditions across 
materials, to determine accurate behaviours for all reinforcement materials under 
similar conditions and to identify the best applications for alternative reinforcement 
materials. References

Chapter 17 
Lightweight Alkali-Activated Materials: 
Sustainable Route to Passively 
Decarbonize the Built Environment 
Rui M. Novais and João A. Labrincha 
Abstract Over recent years, alkali-activated materials (AAMs) have been regarded 
as potentially lower carbon footprint alternatives to Portland cement binders, with 
a role to play towards the decarbonization of the built environment. The use of 
low density AAMs seem particularly promising extending the application range 
of these novel binders to other high added value applications including their use 
as thermal and acoustic barriers, moisture regulators, and thermal energy storage 
materials. These additional functionalities have the potential to further contribute 
to the reduction of energy consumption and the improvement of living conditions. Herein, the most relevant and recent research focusing the use of lightweight AAMs 
in the built environment is summarised and critically discussed. 17.1 
Introduction 
The building sector is one of the main contributors to global ﬁnal energy consump-
tion (30%) and energy derived emissions (26%) [1], this being particularly relevant 
under the EU climate-neutrality strategy by 2050. The sector is currently underper-
forming, and the operational emission must fall by ca. 50% to get on track with 
the net zero emission scenario by 2030. Urgent actions to decarbonize the building 
stock and realign with the forecasted emissions path are imperative. The adoption of 
energy efﬁciency measures can play a signiﬁcant role in the decarbonization scenario. Amongst the various approaches the use of thermal insulating materials is particu-
larly attractive as they mitigate the energy losses inside buildings, therefore reducing 
the need for heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems [2, 3]. This 
is critical as heating and colling needs account for a signiﬁcant share of the energy 
used in buildings [4]. Conventional thermal insulating materials encompass inor-
ganic (e.g., rock and glass wool) and organic materials (e.g., expanded polystyrene

and polyurethane foams). The former are gradually being replaced due to their high 
embodied energy, while the latter are known to present poor thermal stability and 
potential safety hazards [5]. In addition, in the era of unprecedented use of resources 
[6, 7], with still not fully perceived consequences on the environment and human 
health [8], other parameters including the embodied energy, the carbon footprint and 
the recyclability of the materials are becoming hugely important [9, 10]. Lightweight 
alkali activated materials (AAMs) emerge as an excellent alternative considering both 
technical (e.g., high temperature stability, low thermal conductivity, non-ﬂammable) 
and environmental reasons (e.g., green and low cost technology enabling the use of a 
wide range of industrial wastes as precursors) [11–13]. Their dense counterparts have 
been mainly considered as a possible eco-friendlier alternative to Portland cement 
[14, 15] provided that the mixture design is property considered [16]. Nevertheless, 
lightweight AAMs present additional features suggesting their use in less explored, 
but promising, applications including non-structural building materials (e.g., thermal 
[17–19] and acoustic panels [20–22], moisture regulators [23–25]; ﬁre resistant mate-
rials [26–28], EMI shielding [29, 30] and thermal energy storage panels [31–33]), 
pH regulators [34–36], adsorbents envisioned for wastewater treatment [37, 38] and 
catalysts or catalytic supports [39–41]. As a result, the interest in such materials has 
increased dramatically in recent years as demonstrated by the data shown in Fig. 17.1. These novel applications might be a key driver to foster the use of this technology 
(alkali activation). It should be noted that the survey was performed by using the 
keywork “geopolymer” and not “alkali activated material” due to the much higher 
number of articles using this terminology. The differences between both systems 
are beyond the scope of the present chapter, the distinction can be found in relevant 
literature [42–44]. Herein, the term AAM will be employed, geopolymers being 
considered subsets of AAMs. The connection between the identiﬁed 383 articles focusing “lightweight geopoly-
mers” and the dominant keywords was then evaluated using the VOS viewer soft-
ware [45]. A co-occurrence map is presented in Fig. 17.2. The map was generated by 
deﬁning the minimum number of occurrences as ten. From the initial 2630 keywords, 
only 91 met the deﬁned criterion. Disregarding the expected keywords associated 
with the nature of the binders (e.g., “inorganic polymers”, “geopolymers”, “geopoly-
mer”), the most frequently used keyword is “compressive strength”, followed by “ﬂy 
ash”, “thermal conductivity”, “light weight concrete” and “lightweight geopolymer”. Interestingly, the ninth most frequently used term is “aggregates”, and this is asso-
ciated with one of the strategies used to produce lightweight AAMs (as de

tailed in

17.2 
Different Strategies to Produce Lightweight 
Alkali-Activated Materials 
One of the ﬁrst investigations regarding the production of lightweight AAMs was 
reported by Le-ping et al. in 2010 using aluminium powder as a pore-forming agent 
[46]. Bodies with porosity ranging from 40 to 83% and compressive strength compre-
hended between 6.0 and 13.7 MPa were produced. A similar approach was then

300
R.

M. Novais and J. A. Labrincha
Fig. 17.2 Co-occurrence map of the keywords reported in literature for studies dealing with 
lightweight geopolymers. The size of the label is related to the keyword frequency, while the 
connection lines indicate the link strength with other keywords
reported by Prud’homme et al. [47] but in this case in situ foaming was achieved 
through the incorporation of silica fume in the slurries, generating dihydrogen and 
leading to the production of highly porous bodies. In the same year a pioneering 
approach was reported by Okada et al. [48] involving the production of composites 
containing PLA ﬁbres by extrusion. The production of porous AAMs was achieved 
after extracting the polymer ﬁbres using alkali treatment and/or by thermal decom-
position. Since then, a large number of articles has been published and various tech-
niques employed. In this section, the most common routes to produce lightweight 
AAMs will be brieﬂy described, the pros and cons of each technique will be high-
lighted. It should be noted that the emphasis is attributed to protocols envisioning 
the production of materials for the built environment. Detailed descriptions of the 
existing protocols can be found in recent review papers [49, 50]. The two most commons, and mature, methods that have been employed in liter-
ature to produce lightweight AAMs are by physical and chemical foaming or by 
incorporating lightweight and/or porous aggregates. In the physical foaming (also 
known as mechanical foaming) gas bubbles are introduced in the slurry and then 
vigorously mixed to produce bodies containing a large number of voids [21, 51], the 
mixing speed being a key parameter to control the ﬁnal porosity of the specimens 
[50]. The chemical route involves the use of small amounts of pore forming agents 
that trigger chemical reactions and generate gas bubbles. Common agents include 
ﬁne metallic powders (e.g., Si, Al, Zn) and hydrogen peroxide [11]. Figure 17.3

17
Lightweight Alkali-Activated Materials: Sustainable Route to Passively …
301
presents a schematic representation of the chemical and mechanical foaming [52]. The ﬁgure also illustrates the role a surfactant, typically employed to stabilize the 
liquid–gas interface (e.g., reduce the surface tension) and ensure a more homoge-
neous pore size distribution. Recent studies have shown that the amount and nature 
of the surfactant can play a signiﬁcant role on the type and pore size distribution of 
the produced pores [53–55]. The physical and chemical foaming approaches enable the production of highly 
porous specimens with properties (e.g., mechanical, thermal and acoustic) that can be 
tailored according to the envisioned application. However, the production of bodies 
with homogeneous pore size distribution by these methods is challenging as porosity 
is dependent on several parameters including the nature of the precursors (e.g., reac-
tivity/dissolution rate that affect the viscosity and the

setting time of the pastes) [56], 
the mixing speed [57], the type and amount of foaming agent [57–59], but also on 
the geometry of the mould (e.g., the height and volume affect the expansion of the 
slurry in the chemical route) [60–62]. This said, a precise control of the synthesis

conditions is feasible and bodies with fairly homogenous pore size distribution can 
be produced [63, 64]. An alternative strategy to decrease the density of AAMs is by the incorporation 
of porous and/or lightweight aggregates. This approach has long been established 
for Portland cement-based composites [65–68], and their AAM counterparts are 
produced by a similar manner. Typically, the solid precursors and the activators are 
initially mixed for a speciﬁc time until a homogenous slurry is produced. After-
wards, the aggregates are added to the paste, mixed, and then the paste is poured 
into moulds [69, 70]. Other studies follow a different protocol, in which the aggre-
gates are initially mixed with the solid precursors and only then the water and the 
alkali activator is added to the solid materials (e.g., aggregates and raw materials) 
[69, 71]. This methodology is straightforward compared to the previous avenue (e.g., 
physical or chemical foaming), allowing a precise control of the properties of the 
composites simply by controlling the recipe of the binder (e.g., liquid-to-solid ratio) 
and the nature and volume of the aggregates [20, 72, 73]. The aggregates can be 
divided according to their origin in two classes: (i) natural or (ii) synthetic. The 
former corresponding to materials found in nature that can be used without signif-
icant processing steps (e.g., cork, rice husk, pumice, tuff, scoria), while synthetic 
aggregates require heat treatments for thermal expansion (e.g., expanded vermi-
culite, expanded perlite, lightweight expanded clay aggregates) or a fully manufac-
tured product (e.g., geopolymer aggregates, polymer-derived foams). Figure 17.4 
presents digital photographs, and in some cases SEM micrographs, of both natural 
and synthetic aggregates. The aggregates included in Fig. 17.4 show a highly porous 
microstructure responsible for the low density of such aggregates and endowing low 
thermal conductivity to the composites when incorporated into the AAM matrix. The 
volume of pores in the aggregates, as well as their distribution and interconnectivity 
will ultimately impact the properties of the composites. For example, cork is known 
to contain up to 200 million cells/cm3 [74], very different from the microstructure of 
rubber derived from the recycling of end-of-life tires (also known as crumb rubber) 
where a smooth and ﬂat surface structure without visual porosity has been reported 
[75]. As a result, the bulk density of cork is frequently comprehended between ca. 100–130 kg/m3 [76, 77], while the density of crumb rubber ranges between ca. 400–540 kg/m3 [78, 79]. Other materials including polymers foams (e.g., expanded 
polystyrene (EPS) [19]), but also expanded perlite [28], expanded vermiculite [73] 
and lightweight expanded clay aggregates (LECA®)  [80] are also frequently used, in 
some cases exhibiting much lower densities (e.g., EPS—10 to 20 kg/m3 [81]). Never-
theless, the polymer foams are de

rived from fossil fuels and their high embodied 
energy and carbon footprint raise strong concerns regarding their environmental and 
social sustainability, even if the cost is very attractive for industrial applications 
[82]. For example, EPS has an embodied energy of 88.6 MJ/kg and an embodied 
carbon of 2.55 kgCO2/kg [83]. As a comparison, cork, a natural a fully renew-
able resource, shows ca. 22 times lower embodied energy (4.0 MJ/kg) and 13 times 
lower embodied carbon (0.19 kgCO2/kg) [83]. As for the mentioned synthetic aggre-
gates their expansion requires very high temperatures (e.g., expanded vermiculite 
(800–1000 ºC) [84], expanded perlite (700–1200 ºC) [85], expanded glass aggregate

17
Lightweight Alkali-Activated Materials: Sustainable Route to Passively …
303
(900–1300 ºC) [86]) which again result in high embodied energy and carbon (e.g., 
expanded perlite—10.0 MJ/kg and 2.55 kgCO2/kg) [83]. As a result, there is a need 
to develop new synthetic aggregates that show lower embodied energies and reduced 
carbon footprint. Alkali activation can also be employed to synthesize lightweight 
aggregates [87–89]. Conventional methods include sintering [88] and cold bonding 
[89], the latter being desirable from an environmental perspective. In a recent work, 
a simple and scalable procedure (e.g., suspension-solidiﬁcation) was used to fabri-
cate highly porous mm-size spheres by the alkali activation of red mud (challenging 
industrial waste derived from the Bayer process [90]) [28]. The spheres showed a 
geometric density of 570 kg/m3, enabling their use as lightweight aggregate. A digital 
photograph and a representative SEM micrograph is shown in Fig. 4d. The use of lightweight aggregates also raises technical challenges including the 
higher water-to-binder ratio required for synthesising the AAM composites, which 
subsequently reduce their mechanical performance [79]. The expected increase in 
the viscosity of the slurries when rising the volume of aggregate in the formulations 
might lead to the partial destruction of the aggregates, this being particularly relevant 
when dealing with fragile aggregates such as expanded perlite [28]. Another critical 
parameter that can strongly impact the properties of the composites is the possible 
formation of a interfacial transition zone (ITZ) [91], even if this feature seems much 
more problematic in Portland cement composites [92]. Other techniques including 
the use of sacriﬁcial ﬁllers [48, 93], freeze casting [94, 95], additive manufacturing 
[96], microwave irradiation [97, 98], can also be used to produce lightweight AAMs. Nevertheless, and despite their merits they will not be considered in this chapter as 
their synthesis is more complex (e.g. sacriﬁcial ﬁllers, freeze casting) or the tech-
nology is at an embryonic stage (e.g., additive manufacturing, microwave irradi-
ation), factors that hinder their mass production (at least in the current scenario) 
at a competitive cost compared

to the chemical foaming or the use of lightweight 
aggregates. 17.3 
Thermal and Acoustic Barriers 
The prospects of using AAMs in the built environment as low thermal conductivity 
materials alternative to the conventional fossil fuel-derived foams is explained by 
a multitude of potential beneﬁts including high thermal stability, non-ﬂammability 
and green synthesis. Since the original works in the ﬁeld [46–48], the impact of 
key inﬂuential parameters such as nature and amount of the foaming agent [64, 99] 
or the aggregate [20, 25, 75] has been deeply studied. In most cases, an increase 
on the amount of foaming agent or aggregate induces a reduction on the density 
of the AAMs. Nevertheless, in the former there is an upper limit after which the 
foam is unstable and collapses [100], while in the latter the maximum amount of the 
aggregate is controlled by the drop on the workability of the samples [78] but might 
be surpassed by modifying the recipe of the binder (e.g., higher liquid phase) or by 
adding superplasticizers [52]. Table 17.1 summarises literature results on lightweight

lowest thermal conductivity value reported in each study was included. As can be seen both strategies enable the production of ultra lightweight materials. Interestingly, the lowest value reported in the table corresponds to the work of Duan 
et al. [101] where hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was used to produce a foamed paste 
and then polystyrene beads were used as lightweight aggregate. The combination of 
chemical foaming with the use of a lightweight aggregate lead to the production of 
specimens with ultra-low density (100 kg/m3). The idea behind the study is particu-
larly attractive even if in this case a polymer foam has been used in very high amounts, 
this strategy deviating from the environmental sustainability paradigm envisioned for 
future building materials. A similar approach was reported in [125] but using cork as 
a lightweight aggregate and aluminium powder as foaming agent. Surprisingly, the 
density of the cork foams was slightly higher than those seen in the foams prepared 
using the same amount of foaming agent but without adding cork, and this shows that 
the presence of the aggregate disrupts the formation of gas bubbles and hinders their 
entrapping in the mixture. As mentioned previously, the chemical foaming method 
is challenging and the combination with lightweight aggregates raises complexity 
to the system. Nevertheless, this might be an interesting avenue to produce bodies 
with very low thermal conductivity. The results included in Table 17.1 also show the 
feasibility of producing AAMs combining suitable mechanical performance with 
low thermal conductivity values. For example in [105] bodies showing compressive 
strength of 10 MPa and thermal conductivity of 110 mW/m K were produced using 
expanded glass combined with an air entraining agent. An additional advantage of the AAMs compared to polymer foams is their much 
higher thermal stability. Alves et al. evaluated the thermal

stability of AAM compos-
ites prepared using various aggregates, including EPS, cork, crumb rubber, expanded 
vermiculite and LECA® [75]. The composites containing expanded vermiculite and 
LECA® showed remarkable thermal stability up to 600 ºC (for 2 h), while the other 
composites showed lower thermal stability due to the organic nature of the aggregates. Recently, the ﬁre resistance of composites containing alkali activated red mud spheres 
was evaluated according to the ISO-834 test [28]. The spheres-containing composite 
maintained their structural integrity, showing no visible cracks and remaining unbent 
during the ﬁre test (maximum temperature 1050 ºC). 306

Besides the low thermal conductivity and high thermal stability that

can be 
observed in lightweight AAMs, these materials can also present other key features 
including sound absorption properties. This is particularly relevant as noise pollu-
tion is now a key global public health concern [126, 127]. However, the number of 
studies addressing the use of AAMs as acoustic barriers in building applications is 
much lower than those considering their use as low thermal conductivity materials as 
highlighted in our recent review [11]. One of the ﬁrst investigation on the topic was 
reported in 2014 by Hung et al. [128]. The mechanical foaming route (e.g., incorpo-
ration of air bubbles produced by a foam generating tank) was employed to fabricate 
porous AAMs. The impact of the density of the foams on the sound absorption coefﬁ-
cient (α) was evaluated, results showing that lower densities signiﬁcantly increase the 
α across the studied frequency range (100–4000 Hz). The lightest specimen (0.4 g/ 
cm3)  shows  a  α ranging from 0.5 (100 Hz) to 0.9 (ca. 4000 Hz). Other studies follow, 
and in 2015 Zhang et al. [17] used a similar protocol (e.g., mechanical foaming)

however a very different behaviour was observed. The sound absorption coefﬁcient 
of the specimens has very high in the low frequency range (below 150 Hz), being 
low (α < 0.3) beyond this frequency range and up to 1600 Hz. Chemical foaming 
was employed by Papa et al. [61] to produce silica-fume based foams. The samples 
showed two absorption peaks: one between 500 to 2500 Hz, and the second between 
4000 to 5000 Hz. Interestingly, the studied samples, comprising the use of silica fume 
and/or metakaolin and different activating solutions (sodium or potassium based), 
showed very different behaviour suggesting that the properties of the AAMs can be 
tuned by modifying the formulations possibly due to the modiﬁcation the porosity 
and tortuosity of the foams. The sample produced using metakaolin and potassium 
silicate solution reached an impressive α = 0.9 (1210 Hz). AAMs prepared by adding light aggregates can also exhibit very interesting sound 
absorption coefﬁcient [75, 80]. As an example, cork-containing AAM composites 
prepared with 85 vol% of black expanded cork show α = 1.0 (ca. 2100 Hz) [20]. The same group also showed the viability of tuning the sound absorption coefﬁcient 
of the AAM composites simply by adjusting the volume and size of the cork (e.g., 
expanded cork granules) as demonstrated in Fig. 17.6, showing the feasibility to tune 
the samples according to the frequency of the noise pollution source [25]. Other recent approaches include the synthesis of composites containing silica 
aerogel, material know to present extremely low thermal conductivity (12–18 mW/ 
m  K  ) [129]. The composites were prepared using H2O2 coupled with two surfactants 
(e.g., sodium dodecyl sulphate and triton X 100) and with 10 or 20 vol% of silica 
aerogel particles (four different particle sizes were used: 2–40 μm, 100–700 μm, 
100–120 μm and 700–4000 μm). Figure 17.7 presents the acoustic absorption of 
the studied samples. Most of the samples enriched with aerogel particles showed 
enhanced sound absorption coefﬁcient compared with the reference composition 
(foamed AAM without silica aerogel) and this was attributed to the higher open

17.4 
Moisture Regulators 
Besides the thermal and acoustic properties there are other important characteristics 
that should be considered when designing future building materials. A key feature to 
deploy novel materials is there multifunctionally, i.e. a material that can fulﬁl several 
tasks simultaneously. As mentioned in the introduction, one strategy that can mitigate 
the energy consumption of buildings is using thermal insulators, however, a down-
side of this option is that highly insulated walls pose risk of condensation and mois-
ture related issues [130] known to affect human health [131]. The air quality inside 
buildings is currently a major source of concern, particularly in the post-pandemic 
scenario (Covid-19) [132] as literature suggest links between the humidity level and 
the spread of the virus [130]. In this context, a very desirable property of building 
materials is their ability to passible adjust the humidity levels [11]. The moisture 
buffer value (MBV) is a key performance indicator quantifying the capacity of the 
given material to absorb and release moisture upon daily ﬂuctuations of humidity 
[133]. Common hygroscopic materials include wood and cellulose insulation [134], 
but modiﬁed mortars can also be used to adjust the humidity level inside buildings 
particularly when doped with superabsorbent polymers [135] or porous aggregates 
(e.g., perlite, zeolite) [136]. Studies evaluating the feasibility of lightweight AAMs 
to act as moisture regulators are limited, the few exceptions include the pioneer 
work of Rossi et al. [24] where AAM mortars were produced using construction and 
demolition wastes as aggregate and hydrogen peroxide as foaming agent. The MBV 
of the non-foamed mortar (0.8 g/m2 Δ%RH) was substantially increased to 5.6 g/ 
m2 Δ%RH in the sample prepared with higher amount of foaming agent. Despite 
the promising result, the mass evolution of the mortars upon cycling followed an 
increasing trend which might suggest a moderate saturation tendency. A much lower 
MBV (1.64 g/m2 Δ%RH) was reported for black expanded cork-containing AAMs 
[20]. Nevertheless, the composition containing 87.5 vol% of cork showed a very 
stable moisture sorption and desorption behaviour throughout the studied cycles, as 
can be seen in Fig. 17.8. A follow-up study by the same authors evaluated the impact of the cork granules 
size (expanded cork was used) on the composites MBV [25]. The highest MBV 
reached 2.22 g/m2 Δ%RH for the composition containing 80 vol.% of cork gran-
ules with particle sizes comprehended between 0.5 and 2.5 mm, and this ranks this 
composite in the top classiﬁcation ascribed in the Nordtest protocol (MBV > 2.0 g/ 
m2 Δ%RH, “Excellent”) [137]. It s

hould be noted that besides the excellent ability to 
regulate the moisture level, this composite also exhibited low thermal conductivity 
(79 mW/m K), and high sound absorption ability (α = 0.65 at ca. 2000 Hz), demon-
strating the potential of this route (alkali activation) for producing multifunctional 
building m aterials. Another study evaluated the impact of the sodium hydroxide/sodium silicate ratio 
on the hygric properties of porous AAMs [138], reported MBV values compre-
hended between 4.58 and 5.20 g/m2 Δ%RH. The MBV initially increased when 
rising the sodium silicate content up to a speciﬁc threshold and then decreased. This

phenomenon was attributed to changes in the pore network (size and connectivity) of 
the produced AAMs. Recently, the same group intended to correlate the paste chem-
istry (initial water content and alkalinity) with the hygrothermal performance of 
AAM monoliths [23]. Results show that the water content does not play a signiﬁcant 
role in the samples ability to adsorb and release humidity, at least in the conditions 
reported in the study, MBV values ranging from 4.93 to 5.14 g/m2 Δ%RH. On the 
contrary, the molarity of the activating solution does play a role in the sample’s mois-
ture buffering ability, even if no clear tendency has been observed: 10 M (4.65 g/m2 
Δ%RH), 12 M (5.65 g/m2 Δ%RH) and 14 M (4.79 g/m2 Δ%RH). A different approach to measure the hygroscopic properties of porous AAMs has 
been reported in [139]. The authors stated that the Nordtest protocol, and others 
including the ISO 2425, merely quantify the effects of cyclic humidity ﬂuctuations 
on the material mass. To surpass this limitation the authors have collected data for one 
year to deﬁne the principal climatic periods. Two main periods of relative humidity 
were identiﬁed: low relative humidity (35–45%) and high relative humidity (75– 
85%). The hygroscopic performance of the AAMs was then evaluated as follows: (i) 
the samples were exposed to the laboratory environment after saturation in moisture 
(e.g., water immersion for 24 h); and (ii) dried at 100 ºC for 24 h, followed by exposi-
tion to laboratory environment. In each cycle (saturation and drying) the mass of the 
samples was monitored every hour for the ﬁrst 12 h, and then every 24 h until reaching 
200 h. The role of nano and mesoporosity in the samples (promoted by tuning the 
SiO2/Al2O3 and the amount of hydrogen peroxide) on the moisture buffering capacity 
was also studied. Not surprisingly, the samples showed higher moisture saturation in 
the high relative humidity period reaching 27.5% for the samples foamed with 0.9 
vol.% of H2O2 and with SiO2/Al2O3 = 2.45. At this regime (RH = 75–85%) the SiO2/ 
Al2O3 ratio in the samples also plays a slight role on the moisture saturation of the 
materials, increasing from 22.33 to 24.10% as the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio rises from 1.93 to

17
Lightweight Alkali-Activated Materials: Sustainable Route to Passively …
313
2.71 (for samples foamed with 0.6 vol.% of H2O2). After the drying step the moisture 
accumulation was evaluated, results showing low accumulation in the low humidity 
regime (ranging from 5 to 7%). In the high humidity regime, the samples show a 
higher ability to accumulate moisture (values ranging

from 8 to 13%). Interestingly, 
the accumulation is dependent on the volume of millipores. The proper combina-
tion of gel pores (nano and meso) with millipores (induced by the foaming agent) 
allows a very efﬁcient sorption and desorption behaviour. According to the authors 
the millipores play an important role in aeration by limiting the moisture condensa-
tion potential, while facilitating desorption [139]. These are interesting ﬁndings that 
suggest the feasibility of tuning the AAMs hygroscopic capacity by adjusting the 
formulation and design materials with hierarchical porosity. Nevertheless, additional 
studies are suggested as the conditions imposed to the samples (e.g., water soaking 
and drying at 100 ºC) deviate from real life conditions. 17.5 
Thermal Energy Storage Materials 
The use of thermal energy storage (TES) materials in the built environment might 
be a key strategy to tackle the energy consumption of the sector. TES systems can 
be divided in three classes considering the type of TES material: sensible, latent and 
chemical heat storage systems, the pros and cons of each method are detailed in 
[140]. The implementation of such systems in buildings might be performed using 
passive and active solutions [141]. Considering the built environment one of the 
most promising methods is to store latent heat in phase change materials (PCMs) 
[142]. PCMs can store and release thermal energy upon indoor temperature swings 
reducing the peak temperatures and improving the thermal comfort for inhabitants 
[141]. The incorporation of PCMs in Portland cement matrix has been widely reported 
as a strategy to enhance the concrete heat storage capacity [143, 144], while this 
avenue is much less explored in AAMs [145, 146]. Moreover, the vast majority of 
studies has explored PCM incorporation in dense AAMs envisioned for structural 
applications [31, 32], possibly due to the high thermal conductivity requirement for 
efﬁcient heat exchange. A recent example reports the incorporation of PCMs in 3D 
printed AAMs [147]. Nevertheless, herein the emphasis is given to lightweight AAMs 
as a solution to decarbonize the built environment, their dense counterparts being 
beyond the scope of this chapter. The number of studies addressing the production of 
lightweight PCM-containing AAMs is scarce, virtually all existing literature covering 
the impregnation of porous aggregates with PCMs (e.g., waste aerated concrete block 
[148], lightweight expanded clay aggregate [149, 150]) prior to their addition to the 
AAM slurry. The encapsulation of the PCM in the host aggregate must be ensured 
and this usually requires the use of a coating material (e.g., polyester resin [149], 
alkali activated paste [150], expanded graphite [151], hydrophobic expanded perlite 
[152] and polyurethane foam [153]) adding complexity to the system. While this is 
an interesting approach, some studies address only the efﬁciency of the impregnation

314
R. M.

A. Labrincha
process [150], while others evaluate the impact of the PCM content on the physical 
properties of the AAMs (e.g., compressive strength and thermal conductivity) [149], 
neglecting the actual TES capacity of the produced materials. In [153], differential 
scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to measure the TES properties of the PCM 
(encapsulated in a polyurethane foam) containing AAM composite. Not surprisingly, 
the sample containing the highest amount of PCM (20 wt%) showed the highest latent 
heat of fusion and crystallization, 6.4 and 6.2 J/g, respectively. The thermal stability 
of this composite over time was evaluated by performing twenty DSC heating/cooling 
measurements, results showing no impact on the thermal properties over time. While 
DSC measurements can provide relevant information (e.g., speciﬁc heat, melting 
temperature and enthalpy) more robust tests to characterise the TES performance 
of building materials are advisable. Hassan et al. [154] built small size test cells 
40 × 40 × 40 cm. An 80 W silicon heating mat was used to supply heat to the setup. The samples were placed at a predeﬁned distance from the heat source (220 mm) and 
then the temperature in the front and the back surface of the samples was measured 
during one heating cycle. The PCM-containing composites showed lower maximum 
temperature in both the front and back surfaces. A similar protocol was used in [148]. While such tests provide information regarding the TES capacity of the materials it 
deviates from the real-life scenario where daily ﬂuctuations of temperature are to be 
expected. Ramakrishnan et al. [152] constructed cubic test cells (30 × 30 × 30 cm) 
intended to ensure one-dimensional heat transfer process through the samples under 
study. Five surfaces were isolated with EPS, the remaining surface corresponding to 
the test panel. The test cells operated in a cyclic temperature variation to represent a 
summer diurnal temperature proﬁle, two heating and cooling cycles were imposed. Results showed a reduction of 1.85 ºC and 3.76 ºC in the maximum temperature 
of the test cells in the 15 and 30 wt% PCM composites compared to the reference 
composition, respectively. Nevertheless, very similar values were observed in the 
minimum temperature of the cells but no explanation for this feature was given. An innovative approach to produce multifunctional lightweight AAMs with TES 
capacity has been reported by Gonçalves et al. [33], the production of bodies 
combining a highly porous top-layer with a dense bottom-layer containing PCMs as 
illustrated by the digital images included in Fig. 17.9. The idea behind the approach is that the top porous layer provides thermal insu-
lation and moisture regulation ability, while the PCM-containing layer ensures TES. Indeed, the composite containing 30 wt% of PCM in the bottom-layer had a thermal 
conductivity of 172 mW/m K and a MBV of 2.71 g/m2 Δ%RH. The TES capacity 
was evaluated by performing therma

l cycles under realistic temperature and humidity 
conditions by placing small size tests cells (30 × 30 × 30 cm) in a climatic chamber. Ten heating/cooling cycles were then imposed at a constant relative humidity (60%). Figure 17.10 presents the temperature evolution inside the test cells within the ﬁrst 
ﬁve cycles for the reference composition (0 wt% PCM) and the PCM-containing 
specimens. A decrease in the temperature swing was observed in the PCM-coated 
cells, and an increase in the PCM content in the samples decreased the temperature 
ﬂuctuation. The lowest temperature swing (13 ºC) being observed for the 30 wt% 
PCM, this being 5 ºC lower than the reference (18 ºC). The impact of the heating/

17
Lightweight Alkali-Activated Materials: Sustainable Route to Passively …
315
Fig. 17.9 Digital pictures of the bi-layered AAM specimens illustrating the porous top-layer and 
dense bottom layer: a cylindrical specimens (12 cm diameter and 2 cm thickness) and b cubic 
specimens (4 cm). Figure reprinted from [33] with permission from Elsevier (ID: 5840130777397)
cooling rate on the TES was also explored, results showing that under the studied 
conditions a minor impact was observed as in all cases fusion and solidiﬁcation of 
the PCM occurred. Despite the promising results reported in existing literature on the topic, there 
is a knowledge gap regarding the long-term TES performance of PCM-containing 
AAMs under realistic conditions. Additional and deeper investigations are required 
to validate the use of these materials in the built environment. 17.6 
Conclusions and Future Trends 
The use of lightweight AAMs in the built environment emerges as a very promising 
avenue to decarbonize the sector. Results reported to date demonstrate the possi-
bility of producing materials with multifunctional behaviour including thermal and 
acoustic insulation, moisture regulation ability, and in some cases ﬁre resistance. The 
multifunctionally exhibited by these materials combined with their green synthesis 
protocol sets them apart from conventional thermal insulating materials. Despite the 
unique combination of properties of these novel materials, their industrial deployment 
is hindered by the lack of standards regarding synthesis protocols and formulations, 
the latter being particularly sensitive as the raw materials will exert a major role on 
the physical properties of such materials. Future research should also address the use 
of alternative waste-based activators as these reagents are currently the main bottle-
neck when considering the embodied energy and carbon of AAMs. Regardless of 
the synthesis protocol and the composition of the mixture, the environmental, social 
and economic sustainability of any of the proposed solutions requires in-deep life 
cycle assessments (LCA) and life cycle cost analysis (LCCA). This is expected to 
be a hot topic in the near future. 316
R. M. Novais and J. A.

Labrincha

Lightweight Alkali-Activated Materials: Sustainable Route to Passively …
317
The potential of lightweight AAMs to act as moisture regulators has yet to be 
fully explored, despite the relevance of this property in indoor air quality and inhabi-
tants’ health conditions. It should also be noted that existing protocols to measure the 
moisture buffering capacity of building materials present signiﬁcant shortcomings, 
robust approaches must include deeper characterizations under various, but realistic, 
scenarios (e.g., impact of humidity, temperature) and study the long-term perfor-
mance of the materials. Investigations focusing the optimization of the AAM pore 
size distribution to maximize the performance of these materials are suggested. The TES capacity of lightweight AAMs remains virtually unexplored as a signif-
icant effort has been devoted to the physical characterization (e.g., compressive 
strength, density, thermal conductivity) of the PCM-containing AAMs, rather than 
on their TES capacity. A major bottleneck of existing literature is that the TES 
evaluation is performed in unrealistic conditions, signiﬁcant steps must be taken to 
evaluate the TES capacity under realistic temperature ﬂuctuations mimicking their 
real-life operation. In a nutshell, additional studies to demonstrate the viability of 
these materials in energy storage applications are of the utmost importance. Acknowledgements This work was developed within the scope of the project CICECO-Aveiro

Chapter 18 
Incorporating Residues for Sustainable 
Road Pavement Construction: 
An Analysis of Asphalt Mixtures 
with Dolomite, Limestone, Fly Ash, 
and Red Mud 
Mayara S. Siverio Lima, Liseane P. Thives, Arturs Riekstins, 
Remo Merijs-Meri, and Viktors Haritonovs 
Abstract The pursuit of sustainability within the construction industry has led 
to innovative approaches in materials engineering, particularly in road pavement 
construction. This paper explores the utilization of industrial by-products and mineral 
ﬁllers—speciﬁcally red mud (RM), limestone (LS), dolomite (DL), and ﬂy ash 
(FA)—as substitutes for conventional ﬁllers in asphalt mixtures. Various propor-
tions of these ﬁllers were incorporated into asphalt, following the Superpave mix 
design guidelines. The selection of ﬁller percentages was strategically aligned with 
both the theoretical framework of optimal ﬁller-to-asphalt ratios and regulatory limits 
on ﬁller content. This approach takes into account the potential for large-scale appli-
cation in countries like Brazil, a major producer of Red Mud. A comprehensive 
materials characterization has been carried out, including physical, chemical, and 
performance properties. The asphalt mixtures mechanical performance was evaluated
