H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series 
in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_18 
327

328
M. S. Siverio Lima et al. through a series of tests designed to assess their suitability for road pavement appli-
cations. These tests included the Semi-Circular Bending test (SCB), Thermal Stress 
Restrained Specimen Test (TSRST), and Wheel Tracking test (WTT), providing 
insights into the mixtures’ crack resistance, thermal cracking resistance, and rutting 
resistance, respectively. The ﬁndings reveal that the incorporation of Red Mud and 
Fly Ash enhances the adhesion properties between bitumen and aggregate, with 
notable improvements in bitumen coverage. The mixture with Fly Ash demanded 
higher amounts of bitumen, which has led to higher permanent deformation results. On the other hand, mixtures utilizing Dolomite and Limestone powders exhibited 
superior performance in the WTT, suggesting their potential applicability in high-
trafﬁc roads. The TSRST results underscored Red Mud’s satisfactory performance, 
attributed to its granulometry and the stiffness it given to the mixture. 18.1 
Introduction 
Flexible pavement is extensively used in road infrastructure globally, and is selected 
to provide rolling comfort. In the UK, 95% [1] of paved roads are ﬂexible pavements; 
in the US, 94% [2, 3]; and in Brazil, 98% [4], underscoring its signiﬁcance. The pave-
ment structure comprises layers like surface, base, and sub-base over the subgrade 
to support trafﬁc loads and climate [5]. Hot mix asphalt (HMA) is the predominant 
surface layer material, typically made of ﬁne and coarse aggregates, ﬁller, and asphalt 
mixed at temperatures above 150 °C. Asphalt, the primary component of HMA, is 
mostly derived from petroleum crude oil reﬁning, though it occurs naturally in some 
places and is graded for speciﬁc engineering properties [6]. Asphalt’s waterproof and inert nature makes it valuable for various applications 
involving waste reuse or recycling [7]. Asphalt mixtures are among the most recy-
cled construction materials. Its reused in new pavements, reduces the need for 
virgin materials, contributing to a circular economy [8, 9]. Reducing the carbon 
footprint of asphalt pavements requires understanding the impacts generated over 
the entire pavement life cycle, employing low-carbon materials, and extending its 
lifetime [10]. The pavement material enhance its capacity in bearing the structure’s load. Addi-
tives like Portland cement, lime, and ﬂy ash are used to improve strength and perfor-
mance. The ﬁller, consisting of ﬁne particles, is crucial for HMA’s behavior and 
mechanical performance, and are classiﬁed into the following categories: (i) mineral 
powders obtained through screening and crushing of rocks, like limestone, granite 
and dolomite; (ii) industrial products or by-products, for instance, hydrated lime, 
cement, and ﬂy ash, and (iii) ashes or ﬁne particles derived fr

om waste materials, as 
steel slag powder, as rice husk ash and glass powder [11]. These materials enhance the stability of the mixture and improve the adhesion 
between the asphalt and aggregate interface [12]. Previous studies have conﬁrmed 
that the inclusion of ﬁllers, both mineral and by-product materials, can signiﬁcantly

18
Incorporating Residues for Sustainable Road Pavement Construction …
329
improve the moisture resistance, mechanical performance, and durability of HMA 
and mastics [11, 13–17]. Al-Bayati et al. [18] observed that the addition of ﬁllers in asphalt mixtures 
impacts two primary aspects: mastic properties and consequently asphalt mixture’s 
mechanical performance. Studies have shown that optimal ﬁller concentrations result 
in stronger mixtures due to their ability to promote better aggregate-asphalt cohe-
siveness, minimize oxidative aging, improve moisture damage resistance, increase 
modulus stiffness, and enhance mechanical properties such as fatigue resistance and 
rutting performance [19–22]. Regarding the effects on asphalt and mastic, ﬁller addition signiﬁcantly stiffens 
the mixture [22, 23]. Diab and Enieb [24] investigated the mechanisms of mineral 
ﬁllers in asphalt at both the mixture and mastic scales. They concluded that the 
type and amount of ﬁller strongly inﬂuence the mechanical properties of asphalt 
mixtures, and the physical properties of mastics are highly dependent on the ﬁller type 
and concentration. The ﬁller-asphalt interaction signiﬁcantly affects asphalt mastics, 
increasing their rigidity even under varying temperatures and load frequencies [25]. Researchers have examined the suitability of industrial waste and recycled mate-
rials in pavement construction worldwide during the last decades. One prevalent 
method for employing circular economy practices in pavement production is to use 
waste materials as a substitute for non-renewable resources or raw materials [26]. Yaro et al. [27] asserted that using waste materials and inserting new recycling tech-
niques in asphalt pavement is critical. Materials derived from industrial activities, 
such as ﬂy ash from coal power plants, slag from steel manufacture, or red mud 
from aluminium production, can likewise be utilized as alternative materials that 
contribute to the sustainability of the road area. Although the effects of mineral ﬁllers on the properties of asphalt mixtures are 
continuously investigated, the potential of reusing industrial waste materials as ﬁllers, 
such as red mud and ﬂy ash, remains underexplored. This is particularly signiﬁcant 
considering the potential beneﬁts associated with their reuse. As industrial waste 
generation increases worldwide, these materials present a promising alternative for 
use in various infrastructure projects [28, 29]. Wong et al. [30] emphasized that substituting solid waste products for conventional 
ﬁllers can reduce the demand for raw materials. However, it is crucial to assess the 
potential

impacts, such as the leaching of chemical compounds, to minimize negative 
environmental outcomes. Evaluating these factors is essential to ensure the safe and 
sustainable use of industrial waste materials in construction. According to the United States Geological Survey [31], bauxite ore is a crucial 
raw material for many industries, including chemical, metallurgical, and construc-
tion sectors. Brazil ranks as the fourth-largest global producer of bauxite, with an 
annual output of approximately 33 million metric tons. The primary bauxite deposits 
are located in the northern region of the country, with the State of Pará being the 
leading producer. Brazilian mining companies face signiﬁcant environmental and 
sustainability challenges in managing and reusing red mud. Red mud is a by-product of reﬁning bauxite ore to produce alumina for the 
aluminium industry, primarily through the Bayer process. This process involves

330
M.

S. Siverio Lima et al. reacting bauxite with sodium hydroxide under high temperature and pressure condi-
tions. For every ton of alumina produced, around 1.5–1.6 tonnes of red mud are 
generated [32]. According to the International Aluminium Institute, the amount of 
red mud produced per ton of alumina can range from 0.55 to 2.21 tonnes [33]. Due to its variable composition, red mud is often considered a hazardous mate-
rial. However, its potential as a valuable resource merits further exploration [34]. Numerous studies have investigated the use of red mud as a ﬁller in asphalt mixtures 
[34–37]. Nevertheless, additional analysis is necessary to fully address and satisfy 
environmental concerns. In countries with extensive construction activities, such as China, the demand for 
raw construction materials, like ﬁllers to produce asphalt mixtures, is exceptionally 
high. Utilizing by-products like red mud not only addresses this demand but also 
offers signiﬁcant cost savings in construction. Moreover, it contributes to sustain-
ability by repurposing industrial waste and reducing the reliance on traditional raw 
materials [35]. Choudhary et al. [38] compared the performance of asphalt mixtures using stone 
dust and red mud as ﬁllers. Their ﬁndings indicated that the red mud mixture exhibited 
higher Marshall stability, as well as improved resistance to rutting and cracking. This 
enhanced performance was attributed to the characteristics of red mud, such as its 
ﬁneness and porosity, which facilitate uniform ﬁller distribution and greater asphalt 
absorption. Wu et al. [37] incorporated red mud into asphalt and compared it with the standard 
asphalt base. They found that the penetration of the red mud-asphalt blend was 
lower, the softening point was signiﬁcantly improved, and ductility was reduced. Additionally, Ou et al. [39] demonstrated that using red mud instead of limestone as 
a ﬁller enhanced the fatigue resistance of asphalt mastics. Another valuable waste material is ﬂy ash, a by-product of burning pulverized 
coal in thermal power plants. Over the years, researchers have explored numerous 
ways to reuse ﬂy ash across various sectors, including as a ﬁller material, to reduce 
environmental burdens and enhance economic beneﬁts. Globally, coal combustion 
for power generation produces more than 500 million tons of ﬂy ash annually, yet 
only 25–30% is reused and reintegrated into the production chain [40]. Fly ash 
primarily consists of silica, aluminium, and iron oxide. Studies have shown that 
using ﬂy ash as a ﬁller in asphalt mixtures improves their mechanical properties 
[41]. Giri and Priyadarshini [42] concluded that ﬂy ash not only enhances asphalt 
mixture performance but also has the potential to replace conventional ﬁller materials 
in asphalt pavement construction. To comprehensively evaluate the performance of asphalt mixtures containing 
various by-products, this study conducted a detailed laboratory assessment. The focus 
was

on analysing the characteristics and mechanical properties of asphalt mixtures 
using different ﬁllers, including red mud, dolomite, limestone, and ﬂy ash. The tests 
were conducted at the Latvian State Road Laboratory in Riga. The primary objective 
was to assess the effectiveness of red mud as a ﬁller and to evaluate its impact when 
used together with other ﬁllers. 18

Aggregates 
For the production of asphalt mixtures, granitic aggregates with nominal maximum 
sizes of ¾ (19.0 mm) and 3/8 (9.5 mm) were sourced from a quarry in Latvia. The 
characterization of these aggregates was conducted using the following tests: Los 
Angeles abrasion (EN 1097-2) [43], density (EN 1097-6) [43], ﬂakiness index (EN 
933-3) [44], and granulometry (EN 933-1) [45]. The Los Angeles abrasion test results were 10.4% for the ¾ 
aggregates and 
11.36% for the 3/8 
aggregates. These values are signiﬁcantly lower than the 
threshold of 25% speciﬁed by EN 1097-2 [43], indicating that the aggregates are 
well-suited for use under high and heavy trafﬁc conditions. The measured density for both the ¾ and 3/8 aggregates was 2.78 g/cm3. The  
ﬂakiness index, determined according to the EN 933-3 standard [45], was 14, which 
complies with Latvia’s requirements (<20). The granulometry results are detailed in 
Sect. 18.2.5,  Fig  . 18.3. 18.2.2

Fillers 
Four different ﬁller materials were used in this study: red mud (RM), limestone (LS), 
dolomite (DL), and ﬂy ash (FA). The RM was sourced from northern Brazil, while 
the LS, DL, and FA were obtained from Latvia. An extensive and comprehensive 
analysis was conducted to characterize these ﬁllers through various laboratory tests. Prior to testing, the ﬁller samples were dried in a laboratory oven at 110 ºC. The particle size distributions of the ﬁllers were determined using Laser Diffrac-
tion (LD) analysis with the Analysette 22 NanoTec device, which has a detection 
range of 0.1–1000 mm. The granulometric curves are presented in Fig. 18.1. The ASTM D242 standard [53] speciﬁes that ﬁller materials should pass through 
sieves No. 16 (1.18 mm), No. 30 (600 μm), No. 50 (300 μm), and No. 200 (75 μm) 
with the requirements of 100%, 97%–100%, 95%–100%, and 70%–100% passi

ng, 
respectively. Figure 18.1 illustrates that all ﬁllers met the speciﬁcation requirements 
for granulometry except for ﬂy ash (FA), with only 54% passing through the No. 200 
sieve. The ﬁller densities were measured following the EN 1097-7 standard [43], with 
results presented in Table X. The speciﬁc surface areas of the ﬁllers were determined 
using the BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) method. The test results (Table 18.2) indi-
cated that red mud from Brazil exhibited the highest speciﬁc surface area (8.3 m2/g), 
while limestone showed the lowest (2.5 m2/g). According to Géber and Gömze [54], 
the presence of clay minerals accounts for the high speciﬁc surface area observed in 
red mud. Similarly, Zhang et al. [29] found that red mud had a signiﬁcantly higher 
speciﬁc surface area compared to other mineral ﬁllers. The authors concluded that this

difference in speciﬁc surface area enhances red mud’s adsorption to asphalt compo-
nents, positively inﬂuencing the mechanical properties of asphalt mastic, particularly 
in terms of improved resistance to permanent deformation. The void characteristics of ﬁllers inﬂuence asphalt absorption, which was evalu-
ated in this study using the Rigden Voids (RV) method according to the EN 1097-4 
standard. Harris and Stuart (1995) stated that the fractional voids in dry compaction 
correlate well with the stiffening effect of ﬁller on asphalt, and the RV result is a 
tool for selecting a ﬁller. The results (Table 18.2) showed that RM (44.8%) and FA 
(42.4%) had higher RV values compared to LS (32.6%) and DL (30.9%), which were 
similar. Romeo et al. [55] afﬁrmed that RV is a valuable indicator of the stiffening 
effect of ﬁllers on asphalt. According to Zhang et al. [56], ﬁllers with elevated RV 
can absorb and ﬁx more free asphalt, thereby enhancing the friction effect among 
ﬁller particles. The chemical composition of the ﬁllers was evaluated using X-ray ﬂuorescence 
(XRF) through the fusion method, an accurate technique for analysing powder 
samples. This method mitigates heterogeneity caused by grain size and mineralog-
ical effects, ensuring consistent results. The fusion process homogenizes the material 
properties by vitriﬁcation, allowing for an expanded calibration range using either 
synthetic calibration curves with reagents or calibration to varied materials Yamada 
[57]. The tests were performed using a benchtop wavelength-dispersive X-ray ﬂuores-
cence (WDXRF) spectrometer, speciﬁcally the Supermini model. The procedure, 
as described by Yamada [57], involves mixing more than two selected reagents to 
create a fusion bead

, followed by material calibration. The ﬁller samples were fused 
using a benchtop high-frequency fusion machine (Bead Sampler) at 1200 °C. The 
spectrometer, equipped with an end-window Pd-target 200 W X-ray tube, operated at 
50 kV–4.0 mA, with a measurement area of 30 mm in diameter. Table 18.3 presents 
the chemical composition of each ﬁller analysed in the study. Table 18.3 reveals the primary chemical compounds of the ﬁllers analysed. Lime-
stone (LS) predominantly consists of calcium oxide (CaO), while ﬂy ash (FA) has 
elevated percentages of silicon dioxide (SiO2) and CaO. Red mud (RM) is mainly 
composed of Fe2O3, Al2O3, and SiO2, which together account for over 80% of its 
total weight. Additionally, RM contains a signiﬁcant amount of Na2O (11.31%), 
contributing to its alkaline character and enhancing asphalt adhesion. Studies have 
conﬁrmed that higher percentages of Na2O in red mud result in higher pH values 
compared to other mineral ﬁllers [28, 29]. 334

The presence of silicon dioxide (SiO2), particularly in FA and RM, can nega-
tively impact adhesion with asphalt, leading to lower moisture resistance in mixtures 
[58]. However, compounds such as SiO2,  Al2O3, and Fe2O3, which are abundant in 
RM, have been shown to signiﬁcantly affect the stiffness of asphalt mastics, thereby 
improving rutting resistance [59]. 18.2.4 
Mastics 
Seven types of mastics were produced, each containing 40% ﬁller by volume mixed 
with bitumen. To evaluate the inﬂuence of RM, different proportions and combina-
tions were created. These included: 40% red mud (40RM), 40% limestone (40LS), 
40% dolomite (40DL), 40% ﬂy ash (40FA), a mixture of 20% red mud and 20% lime-
stone (20RM20LS), a mixture of 20% red mud and 20% dolomite (20RM20DL), 
and a mixture of 20% red mud and 20% ﬂy ash (20RM20FA). The mastics were prepared by incorporating a speciﬁc amount of each ﬁller or 
ﬁller combination into the bitumen. Initially, the bitumen PEN 50/70 and the ﬁllers 
were heated to 155 °C and 160 °C, respectively, following the EN 12697-35 standard 
[60]. For the bitumen PEN 50/70, the compaction temperature was set at 150 °C, 
while the mixing temperature was maintained within 20 °C above the compaction 
temperature, with aggregates and ﬁllers heated to the mixing temperature ± 5°C. The ﬁllers were then gradually added to the bitumen and manually mixed using a 
glass rod for approximately 3–4 min to ensure homogeneous mastics. The prepared 
mastics were stored at room temperature for subsequent laboratory tests. 18

shown  in  Tab  le 18.4, adding ﬁller to the bitumen increased the softening point while 
decreasing the penetration values of the mastics. FA was the ﬁller that resulted in the 
highest softening point value. Conversely, the inclusion of RM with other ﬁllers also 
elevated the softening point of the mastics. The Fraass breaking point was reduced for 
all the mastics. As expected, the mastics became stiffer after aging, and the softening 
point increased. The elastic recovery test was performed according to the standard EN 13398 [51], 
and a storage stability test was conducted following EN 13399 [61]. The adhesion of mastic/aggregate was evaluated us

[60]. In this study, the boiling water stripping method was adapted without using

3 h; (ii) after drying, the aggregates were heated at 160 °C for 3 h; (iii) the mastics 
were heated at 155 °C for 3 h. The bowl used for mixing was also heated with the

Expressed as the degree of bitumen coverage in percentage 
To perform the tests, 510 g of aggregates were used per mix. The amount of 
mastics added depended on the ﬁller type, ranging from 16 to 20 g. Aggregates and 
mastics were mixed by hand until the aggregates were fully coated. After mixing, the 
coated aggregates were spread on aluminium foil to cool down for 12 h at 20 ± 5  °C. Thirty aggregate particles were meticulously chosen, ensuring complete coverage, 
to enhance the accuracy of the process. These particles were placed in supporting 
netting w ith hooks. A beaker containing 700 mL of water was placed on an electric stove, regulated to 
maintain the water at 100 °C. When the water reached boiling point, the supporting 
netting with aggregates was inserted for 30 min. Subsequently, the particles were 
visually evaluated by two different individuals separately; each person determined 
the coating percentage for each aggregate particle based on the guidance established 
in the EN 12697-11 standard [60]. An average of the individual evaluations was 
calculated to express the results (Table 18.5). The results (Table 18.5) showed that LS and DL mastics did not promote adequate 
aggregate adhesion. Adding RM to the limestone and dolomite improved the adhe-
sion, while mastics composed only of RM and FA were enough to promote adhesion 
to the aggregates. In this way, it was necessary to incorporate an anti-stripping addi-
tive for mixtures composed with the following mastics

: 40LS, 40DL, 20RM20LS 
and 20RM20DL. Figure 18.2 illustrates the Thermal Susceptibility Index (TSI) of mastics, a param-
eter derived from penetration and softening point tests. TSI quantiﬁes asphalt’s 
consistency sensitivity to temperature variations, typically ranging from − 1.5 to 
+ 0.7 for Brazilian bitumen standards [53, 62]. A higher TSI value indicates lower 
thermal susceptibility, meaning the asphalt is less sensitive to temperature changes. 18.2.5

64], which set a maximum ﬁller content limit of 12%, the formulation adhered to 
this constraint, considering Brazil’s signiﬁcant global production of red mud (RM). 18

18.2.6 
Mixing 
Initially, the mineral material, ﬁller, and bitumen are divided into appropriate portions 
based on the speciﬁc mix recipe. The size of these portions is determined by the mix 
design requirements. The mixing temperature is dependent on the bitumen class; for 
road bitumen B 50/70, it is set at 150 °C, with a maximum allowable temperature of 
170 °C. To prepare the materials, the portions of mineral material and ﬁller are placed 
in an oven at 155 °C for a minimum of 8 h before mixing, ensuring they reach the 
required temperature and stabilize. Bitumen portions are also heated in an oven at 
155 °C for 3–5 h. Both the mineral material/ﬁller and bitumen are heated 5° above 
the mixing temperature to compensate for heat loss and maintain optimal viscosity 
during mixing. Prior to mixing, the mixing container is preheated to 150 °C. Once the mineral 
material and ﬁller have sufﬁciently aged in the ovens, they are transferred into the 
mixing container. The bitumen (mastic) is immediately poured over the mineral 
material, and stirring commences promptly. The mixing duration is set at 4 min. After mixing, the asphalt concrete mixture is portioned as needed for subsequent 
tests or applications. This process ensures uniformity and proper preparation of the 
asphalt mixture according to established standards. 18.2.7 
Performance Tests 
18.2.7.1
Semi Circular Bending Test (SCB) 
The SCB test was employed to evaluate the fracture toughness of asphalt mixtures, 
providing crucial insights into crack propagation under load. This test is instrumental 
in designing mixtures to enhance crack resistance, particularly against thermal and 
fatigue stresses. The SCB test adhered to the standard EN 12697-44. 18
Incorporating Residues for Sustainable Road Pavement Construction …
339
For each asphalt mixture, a single slab was compacted, and a core with dimensions 
of 150 ± 1 × 80 mm was extracted. Two specimens were prepared from each core. These specimens were then shaped to appropriate dimensions (diameter 150 ± 1 
mm, height 74 ± 1 mm, thickness 50 ± 3 mm), with a notch (width 0.40 ± 0.2 mm, 
depth 10.0 ± 1.0 mm) cut into the middle of the ﬂat edge. Prior to testing, specimens were conditioned in a climatic chamber at 0°C for 4 h. Subsequently, they were subjected to three-point bending at a constant deformation 
speed of 5.0 ± 0.2 mm/min. During testing, force (kN) and displacement (mm) values 
were continuously recorded. The fracture toughness (N/mm^3/2) of the specimens was calculated using 
Eqs. (18.1–18.2), where factors such as specimen height and notch depth inﬂuence

18.2.7.2
Thermal Stress Restricted Specimen Test (TSRST) 
The TSRST was employed to assess the thermal cracking resistance of asphalt 
mixtures, focusing on determining the minimum temperature at which asphalt can 
resist cracking. The test methodology adhered to the standard EN 12697-33 [60]. Initially, asphalt slabs were compacted using a roller compactor. From each 
mixture, two prismatic samples measuring 160 × 40 × 40 mm were cut longitu-
dinally from the slab, following the guidelines speciﬁed in EN 12697-46 [60] where 
the longitudinal axis of the specimens must be orthogonal. Subsequently, each sample was securely glued to two aluminium plates and left 
to dry completely. The specimens were then positioned in a ﬁxed frame within a 
thermostatic chamber. According to the standard protocol, the test commenced at 
20 °C, and the temperature was decreased at a rate of 10 °C per hour, precisely 
following these parameters throughout the test duration. At the conclusion of the test, failure stress and failure temperature were deter-
mined, providing essential data on the asphalt mixture’s ability to withstand thermal 
stresses without cracking. This approach ensures standardized testing conditions and 
reliable evaluation of thermal cracking resistance in asphalt materials. 340
M.

Siverio Lima et al. 18.2.7.3
Wheel Tracking Test 
The wheel tracking test was employed to assess the rutting resistance of asphalt 
mixtures in accordance with standard EN 12697-22 [60]. Each mixture underwent 
testing using a single slab compacted by a roller compactor to dimensions of 320 × 
260 × 47 mm, as speciﬁed in EN 12697-33 [60]. The test procedure followed method B, where a small device tracks a wheel across 
the sample continuously for either 20,000 crossings or until a rut depth of 20 mm 
is attained. Testing was conducted in ambient air conditions at a temperature of 
60 °C, replicating conditions comparable to those experienced by truck tires under 
real-world conditions. During the test, the load applied to the sample by the wheel was designed to simu-
late the stress exerted by a truck tire. The depth of the wheel track was measured 
using LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) displacement indicators, 
with measurements recorded every 5 cycles throughout the test duration. At the conclusion of the test, the tendency of rut depth accumulation provided 
critical data on the asphalt mixture’s ability to resist permanent deformation caused by 
repeated loading cycles, thereby informing decisions related to material performance 
and suitability for road construction applications. 18.3 
Results and Discussion 
18.3.1 
Semi Circular Bending Results 
The SCB results (Fig. 18.4) provide insights into the fracture toughness and resistance 
to cracking of asphalt mixtures, revealing distinct performances inﬂuenced by the 
type of ﬁller used. The DL exhibited notable strength, achieving a resistance value of 
43.0 N/mm3/2, indicating its suitability for demanding applications. The FA showed 
slightly lower resistance at 38.4 N/mm3/2, yet still contributed signiﬁcantly to fracture 
resistance. The LS demonstrated competitive performance with a resistance value of 40.4 
N/mm3/2, comparable to conventional ﬁllers. Red mud (RM) exhibited a resistance 
of 39.3 N/mm3/2, highlighting its potential as a sustainable ﬁller option. Mixtures 
combining RM-DL and RM-FA showed resistance values of 39.0 N/mm3/2 and 
38.8 N/mm3/2, respectively, suggesting viable alternatives with substantial cracking 
resistance. The combination of RM with LS yielded a resistance of 39.4 N/mm 3/2, surpassing 
that of RM alone, indicating a promising balance between mechanical performance 
and sustainability in road construction. It is important to note that the observed differences among these mixtures are 
statistically marginal, requiring further rigorous statistical analysis to conclusively

M. S. Siverio Lima et al. • Red Mud with Dolomite: This mixture shows the highest resistance to thermal 
stress, withstanding a stress of over 6.1 MPa at a temperature nearing – 31 °C. This 
suggests that the mixture has a high tolerance to cold-induced tension, which could 
make it suitable for environments experiencing extreme cold weather conditions. • Red Mud: The pure RM mixture resisted to a stress of approximately 5.9 MPa at a 
temperature just below – 28 °C, eventually suggesting that the DL could optimize 
the performance of asphalt mixtures produced with RM at very low temperatures. • Red Mud with Limestone: A combination of RM and LS resisted around 5.8 
MPa at temperatures approaching – 28 °C. • Red Mud with Fly Ash: Exhibiting a stress of about 5.6 MPa at roughly – 26 °C, 
this mixture ranks slightly lower in thermal stress resistance compared to the other 
Red Mud mixtures. However, it still maintains a considerable level of resistance, 
underscoring the viability of utilizing a combination of industrial by-products in 
asphalt mixtures. • Fly Ash: The mixture with Fly Ash as the sole ﬁller shows resilience to thermal 
stress at a higher temperature, close to – 22 °C, with a stress resistance near 5.4 
MPa. Although its performance is commendable, it suggests that FA may be less 
suited to extremely cold climates compared to the other ﬁllers tested. • Dolomite and Limestone: Both mixtures appear to have the lowest resistance to 
thermal stress among the evaluated materials, with DL exhibiting slightly better 
performance than LS. They withstood stress levels in the vicinity of 5.3 MPa at 
temperatures around – 22 °C and – 24 °C, respectively. Hence, the results suggest that the RM promotes thermal cracking resistance 
at lower temperatures, which could be beneﬁcial for paving in regions with cold 
climates. Conversely, conventional ﬁllers like DL and LS alone, while still resisting 
thermal stress, may be more suitable for environments where the temperatures do 
not fall as low. 18.3.3 
Wheel Tracking Test 
In evaluating the rutting resistance of asphalt mixtures, the WTT serves as a crit-
ical assessment, closely representing the effect of continuous vehicular trafﬁc on 
pavement structures. The empirical

data from laboratory testing presents an instruc-
tive comparison among various asphalt mixtures enhanced with different ﬁllers. Figure 18.6 shows the rutting resistance of the diverse asphalt mixtures tested:
In the WTT, the best results were shown by the mixtures that used classic ﬁllers— 
DL and LS powders. Both mixtures presented very similar results, and according to 
the Latvian road speciﬁcations could even be applied to main roads with the high 
trafﬁc loads, where the WTS (Wheel Track Formation Speed) requirement is 0.1 
mm/1000 cycles. Either the reference mixture with LS as the one produced with DL show good 
resistance with minimal rut depth after extensive wheel crossings, suggesting longer

Hypothesis 
These ﬁndings suggest that while the performance of mixtures with solely industrial 
by-products as ﬁllers might be slightly inferior to conventional ﬁllers, they present 
a potential alternative with acceptable mechanical properties for road pavement 
construction. (a) Wheel Tracking Test: 
• Dosage: The results are primarily inﬂuenced by the amount of asphalt used in 
each mixture. Mixtures with higher percentages (5% vol.) of asphalt, such as 
FA, RM + FA, RM + DL and RM + LS have automatically higher suscepti-
bility to temperature and, therefore, are more prone to deformation. The least 
amount of bitumen presented at the mixture with 40% LS is potentially the 
main reason for its best mechanical performance, in comparison with other
mixtures. • Granulometry Inﬂuence: The granulometry of the ﬁllers also impacts their 
resistance to permanent deformation. Mixtures with dolomite and limestone 
showed the best performance, l

ikely due to their relatively continuous, ﬁne, 
and stable granulometry (with minimal variations). This allows the mastic to 
be better mixed and ensures that the binder coats all grains more effectively. The mixture with red mud showed the worst WTT result—0.28 mm/1000

18
Incorporating Residues for Sustainable Road Pavement Construction …
345
cycle. This is probably explained by the granulometry of the red mud. Unlike 
classic ﬁllers, this powder contains more particles that are relatively coarser. In this way, potentially contributing to the overall adhesion of the mixture. (b) Thermal Stress Restrained Specimen Test (TSRST): 
• Anti-aging Agents: Some studies [65–67] show that certain elements, such 
as Fe2O3,  Al2O3, and SiO2, are directly associated with anti-aging proper-
ties due to their ability to enhance the mechanical, thermal, and oxidation 
resistance of asphalt. Therefore, the presence of these compounds in RM and 
FA can explain the positive results observed in aging resistance and adhe-
sion properties. Studies suggest [36, 68–70] that the red mud’s alkalinity and 
hydrophobicity promote a strong bond with asphalt due to its acidic nature, 
offering a satisfactory resistance against moisture damage and consequently, 
beneﬁting the low temperature performance of mastics with red mud in its 
composition. • Contribution of Other Fillers: Meanwhile, the presence of CaO and MgO in 
dolomite (DL) and limestone (LS) contributes to the rigidity and mechanical 
stability of the asphalt. The MgO presented in dolomite’s composition, has an 
afﬁnity to water, and promotes a volumetric expansion reaction, which allows 
more water to come inside the mastic and remove the binder ﬁlm from the 
aggregate. Thus, the adhesive and consequently low temperature properties 
of the dolomite are strongly dependent on its hydrophilic characteristics. These results are supported by adhesion and anti-aging analyses conducted 
in a previously published article [36]. (c) Semi-Circular Bending (SCB) Test: 
• Sample Size: Due to the limited number of samples tested for each mixture, 
it is not possible to statistically conﬁrm signiﬁcant differences in the results 
among the various asphalt mixtures. 18.5 
Conclusions 
This study demonstrates that incorporating industrial by-products, such as red mud 
and ﬂy ash, into asphalt concrete mixtures can achieve performance levels compa-
rable to traditional ﬁllers like dolomite and limestone. Even though it is not possible 
to statistically conﬁrm signiﬁcant differences between results due to the number of 
samples, the Semi-Circular Bending (SCB) test results revealed that dolomite exhib-
ited the highest resistance to cracking, followed by limestone and mixtures containing 
red mud, suggesting their potential for high-performance applications. The Thermal Stress Restrained Specimen Test (TSRST) ﬁndings highlighted the 
superior thermal stress resistance of red mud mixtures, particularly when combined 
with dolomite,

Siverio Lima et al. anti-aging agents compounds in red mud and ﬂy ash, such as Fe2O3,  Al2O3, and 
SiO2, potentially contributed to their thermal stability . The Wheel Tracking Test results showed that mixtures with dolomite and lime-
stone have a good rutting resistance, meeting high-trafﬁc road requirements. On the 
other hand, red mud and ﬂy ash mixtures showed slightly lower rutting resistance, 
which would be probably viable for lower-trafﬁc applications. These ﬁndings under-
score the feasibility of using industrial by-products in asphalt mixtures, promoting 
sustainability in road construction. To conﬁrm the potential use of alternative materials, such as red mud and ﬂy ash, in 
road construction, it is essential to conduct comprehensive environmental tests. These 
tests should ensure that any hazardous components are effectively encapsulated by 
the bitumen ﬁlm, thereby mitigating environmental risks. This would provide a safe 
and durable alternative for road construction. References

Chapter 19 
Sugarcane Enzymes in Construction: 
Sustainable Alternatives for Earth 
Material Stabilization 
Ana Cervilla-Maldonado, Francisco Martín-Villegas, 
Ignacio Valverde-Palacios, and Raquel Maria Fuentes-García 
Abstract Most contemporary construction systems rely heavily on the use of steel 
and concrete as their primary building material, with natural materials being less 
frequently employed. Earth or soil is a key natural material that is readily accessible, 
and which was once of great importance as a structural material. This material is 
well-known and is employed in the construction of simpler architectural structures, 
primarily in developing countries. However, this material is currently in disuse as 
a structural material in Europe. The use of earthen constructions does not neces-
sarily imply a certain social level, as this material has been employed successfully 
in the construction of simple houses as well as palaces, walls and churches. Earthen 
construction is often used with stabilizing binders including lime and cement. The 
use of such additives enhances the structural integrity of so-called stabilized-earths 
or stabilized soils. The objective of this research is to demonstrate how the optimized 
use of binding additives, including ecological enzymes, lime, clay and cement can 
improve the mechanical performance of stabilized soils. The physical–mechanical 
properties of stabilized soils examined here, including uniaxial compression and 
longitudinal strain, are evaluated in accordance with the European Standard EN 
ISO 17892-7:2019. The average compressive strength values obtained are between 
1.0 MPa and 2.3 MPa for soils stabilized lime and organic enzymes with clay addi-
tives, respectively. These values compare favorably with compressed earth blocks 
and adobe blocks [1]. On the other hand, the values of the Modulus of Elasticity,

Introduction 
Construction materials are considered environmental resources that have been 
obtained to be processed to shape the buildings around us. These materials undergo, 
to a greater or lesser extent, a series of natural processes that help us to better under-
stand how they will be used, as well as their behaviour throughout their useful life. Over time, the methods of extraction and treatment of these materials have evolved, 
enhancing their inherent qualities and facilitating their adaptation to the diverse 
requirements to which they are subjected [2]. The Industrial Revolution marked a turning point in history, during which new 
materials such as concrete and steel emerged. These new materials have gradually 
supplanted soil as a historical construction material. Nevertheless, despite the signif-
icant advancements these materials have brought to the construction sector, they 
remain among the most polluting materials globally. Consequently, it is imperative 
to take all possible measures to minimise this damage [3–8]. One such measure is 
the return of earth to the construction scenario, as it can contribute signiﬁcantly to 
sustainable development, and to the fulﬁlment of the Sustainable Development Goals 
(SDGs) of the UN. Furthermore, in order to provide the soil with resistance at an early stage, 
white cements of low/medium strength are often used at low levels to provide 
improved robustness while maintaining a certain permeability to gases [9]. Histori-
cally, numerous binders, such as lime, have been employed with considerable success 
in conjunction with soil. This can be observed in numerous rammed earth construc-
tions that were built in Granada, encompassing both residential and palatial ediﬁces, 
as well as defensive structures such as walls and ramparts [9]. In recent years, interest has grown in the development of more sustainable alter-
natives to Portland cement based binders, with a particular focus on those with lower 
energy consumption [10]. In the ﬁeld of road construction, enzymatic stabilisers have 
gained considerable popularity due to their simplicity of application and effective-
ness in stabilization [11–14]. These stabilisers are environmentally benign and free 
from toxic substances, which has led to their being regarded as a potentially valuable 
solution for soil stabilisation by numerous studies. The ﬁndings of this study have the potential to inform and enhance the utilisation 
of enzyme-enhanced soil as a construction material. Consequently, the principal 
objectives of this research are to characterize and compare the mechanical properties 
of soil with varying dosages of ecological enzymes derived from sugar cane,

clay, 
lime and cement. Furthermore, the use of strain gauges enables the determination of 
the Poisson Ratio and the Dynamic Modulus of Elasticity, among other parameters. 19
Sugarcane Enzymes in Construction: Sustainable Alternatives for Earth …
353
19.2 
Materials and Methods 
19.2.1 
Materials 
19.2.1.1
Alhambra Formation Soil 
The soil layers or horizons deﬁned by Perez-Acosta [15] are indicated in Fig. 19.1. These comprise the A horizon (the shallowest layer) to the D horizon (the deepest 
layer). The colour of Horizon A is darker due to the presence of decomposed organic 
matter near the surface. The accumulation layer, or Horizon B, is the layer in which 
minerals transported by water accumulate. Consequently, this layer contains a signif-
icant quantity of clay. Horizon C is formed on top of the parent rock and is composed 
of fragments of the parent rock. The deepest layer, Horizon D, is known as the source 
rock. The base material for this investigation is the Alhambra Formation soil 
(Fig. 19.2a), which was extracted in the north-eastern part of Granada, southern 
Spain. For the purposes of this investigation, the B horizon of the edaphic soil, 
mixed with the top of the C horizon, was used. Fig. 19.1 Detail of 
Alhambra Formation 
horizons

354
A. Cervilla-Maldonado et al. Fig. 19.2 Detail of the materials used for the research 
In order to avoid contamination of the B horizon with organic remains, the A layer 
or horizon was ﬁrst removed for the extraction of the soil samples. The samples were 
stored in 20 kg bags and transported to the laboratory, where they were subjected to 
a preliminary cleaning and drying treatment, prior to carrying out the tests. 19.2.1.2
Organic Sugar Cane Enzymes 
The organic enzymes utilized, designated as COMPAT-TO® (Fig. 19.2b), are manu-
factured by the company Nanosystems. The mixture of organic enzymes acts as a 
catalyst, facilitating the binding action on the plastic particles of cohesive soils. These 
enzymes have been employed in previous research with the objective of identifying 
a solution that would facilitate the compaction, conservation and protection of roads 
or soils against wear and deformation. The properties and applications have been 
interpreted from the technical data sheets provided by the manufacturer. Given the high clay content of the selected horizon of the Alhambra Formation 
soil, which is indicative of a cohesive soil, it may be reasonably anticipated that the 
mechanical behaviour of the material will be improved. It is therefore postulated that 
the soil reinforced with the enzymatic additives derived from sugar cane will exhibit 
enhanced mechanical resistance values and a reduction in deformation. 19.2.1.3
Micronized Beige Clay 
Micronized beige clay (Fig. 19.2c) was employed in conjunction with the ecolog-
ical enzymes derived from sugar cane to introduce additional ﬁnes to the mixture 
and improve the mechanical performance of the material. The dosage of clay used

varies between 4 and 8%, in order to deﬁne the optimal amount that combines most 
effectively with the organic enzymes and the Alhambra Formation Soil. The mineralogical composition of the micronized beige clay is principally consti-
tuted of quartz (43%), kaolinite (9%), calcite (7%) and plagioclase (5%), in addition 
to lesser quantities of other components, including k-feldspar, hematite and mont-
morillonite, which account for less than 5% of the total analysis. The sample was 
provided by the company Argiles Colades. 19
Sugarcane Enzymes in Construction: Sustainable Alternatives for Earth …
355
19.2.1.4
Hydrated Lime CL 90 S 
In this study, the type of hydrated lime CL 90 S (Fig. 19.2d), produced by the company 
Ancasa, has been employed. Hydrated calcium lime 90 powder is an aerial lime that 
is primarily composed of CaO (quicklime) and/or Ca(OH)2 (calcium hydroxide), 
with no hydraulic or pozzolanic additives [16]. 19.2.1.5
Cement 
The cement employed was of the white cement BL II/A-L 42.5 R [17] (Fig. 19.2e). No mechanical tests were conducted, as the requisite information was available in the 
form of a quality certiﬁcate from the supplier. Moreover, the quantity of cement used 
in this research in relation to the weight of the soil is notably low. This is because its 
role as a binder is only temporary until the main binder, lime, begins to carbonate, 
providing strength. 19.2.2 
Methods 
19.2.2.1
Conformation of Compressed Earth Cylinders (CEC) 
Once the previous cleaning treatment of the extracted and oven-dried soil has been 
carried out, the manufacture of test specimens is carried out by compacting them to 
normal proctor density and with an optimum moisture content of 8.5% established 
in previous research [9] for soil of the same nature and granulometry. Cylindrical samples of compacted soil with dimensions 100 × 120 mm were 
made up of Alhambra Formation soil (AF soil) without additives, and other samples 
mixed with speciﬁc additives. It should be noted that all the samples were composed 
of a base of 2500 g of AF soil, to which different dosages of ecological enzymes and 
clay were added (7.5 ml of enzymes and 4%, 6% and 8% of clay for each sample), 
as deﬁned in Table 19.1. In the case of the samples that were combined with lime, 
the lime contents used were 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10% of the weight of the soil. Finally, for 
the samples added with lime and cement, the dosage percentages were 8% lime and 
1 and 2% cement, calculated with respect to the weight of the soil. The CECs were manufactured in accordance with the speciﬁcations set forth in 
Standard UNE 103500:1994 [18] and allowed to reach a constant weight through a 
drying process. The required drying time is deﬁned as a period of between 30 and 
45 days, with a temperature of 20–24 °C and humidity of 30–40%, contingent upon 
the speciﬁc conditions of the laboratory environment. 356

19.2.2.2
Measurement of Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity 
Once the drying process of the samples has been completed, the propagation speed 
of the longitudinal wave impulses is determined in accordance with the requirements 
set out in the UNE-EN 12504-4 Standard. In this way, the propagation time of the 
wave through the test specimen is obtained. The wave propagation speed through 
the sample is calculated using Eq. 19.1, where the distance between sensors (corre-
sponding to the length of the test specimen, 120 mm) and the time t of transmission 
of the wave between sensors (s) are used to obtain the longitudinal wave propagation 
speed (m/s). v = d 
t 
(19.1) 
19.2.2.3
Compression Test, Modulus of Elasticity and Poisson Ratio 
Subsequently, in accordance with the provisions of the UNE-EN ISO 17892-7:2019 
standard, the compressive strength of the samples is carried out. The test entails 
applying a constant force of 1 mm per minute until the specimen breaks. The force 
is applied through the plates of the 10-tonne capacity multi-test press manufactured 
by Controls S.A. Furthermore, some specimens have been instrumented with strain 
gauges connected to the HBM QuantumX data acquisition equipment, with directions 
parallel and perpendicular to the axial load, to obtain the Poisson ratio (ν) and the 
Modulus of Elasticity (E, 0). The Modulus of Elasticity is then calculated using Eq. 19.2, based on the recorded 
values from the gauges. In this equation, the parameters x and y represent the intervals 
within which both the stress (σx, σ y) and the longitudinal or transverse deformation 
(ɛ) are calculated prior to reaching the maximum value of rupture. Stress (σ)  is  
deﬁned as the internal resistance that a material presents to an applied external force. Deformation (ɛ) has a non-dimensional value that represents the change in the length

19

of a material with respect to its initial length. Changes in the length of the samples 
are usually minimal and strain gauges with a gauge factor of 2.11 ± 1.0% were 
employed to measure them. The gauge type used was 50/120 CLY41-3L-0.5M from 
HBM. E = σ x% − σ y %
εx% − εy% 
(19.2) 
In order to calculate the Poisson ratio (ν) according to Eq. 19.3, it is necessary to 
determine the ratio between the longitudinal and transverse deformation. Poisson’s coefﬁcient (ν) is the measure of the relationship between transverse and 
longitudinal strains, deﬁned as the elongation that occurs when a force is applied in 
the longitudinal direction as opposed to the strain in the transverse direction. This 
coefﬁcient is calculated using the same interval values as those employed to calc

ulate 
the Modulus of Elasticity. ν =

19.2.2.4
Analysis with the Inverted Plate Microscope 
A detailed microscopic analysis was conducted to compare the samples of CECs that 
achieved the highest compressive strength results and exhibited the best deformation 
behaviour with a reference sample of CEC without additives. For this purpose, an 
inverted plate microscope (Nikon Epiphot 200) with polarised light was employed. Following the removal of the breakage cones from the selected samples, they 
were subsequently embedded in resin. A vacuum machine (Struers CitoVac) was 
employed to distribute the Epoﬁx resin evenly throughout the sample and to ﬁll all 
the pores. After a 24 h period of resin curing, the samples were cut and polished. The samples were polished with diamond discs (MD-Piano 220, MD-Allegro and 
MD-Dac). The duration of polishing was determined individually until the samples 
were correctly visualised in the microscope, with the objective of eliminating the 
marks of the cutting process. 358
A.

Cervilla-Maldonado et al. 19.3 
Experimental Results 
19.3.1 
Relationships of Compression Strength Versus Time 
and Longitudinal Strain 
The load capacity of each specimen is studied until reaching its breaking point. In 
addition, the relationship between compressive strength and longitudinal strain is 
studied, obtained using a strain gauge placed in the longitudinal direction of the 
applied load. This allows the behaviour and deformation of the specimen to be 
analysed over time. The mean compressive strength of the reference samples with FA soil (without 
additives) is 1.9 MPa. A linear behaviour is also observed from 0.5 MPa, up to the 
points of maximum compressive strength of each sample, at which point the sample 
passes into a plastic state and the load begins to decrease caused by the collapse of 
the material. It can be observed that the deformation of the samples is linear as the 
compressive force is applied and increases (Fig. 19.3). Figures 19.4 and 19.5 present the results of tests conducted on samples composed 
of 7.5 ml of ecological enzymes derived from sugar cane and micronized clay, with 
a percentage of 4% and 6%, respectively. The objective of this study was to analyse 
the behaviour of the material, AF soil, when lime was replaced by a more ecolog-
ical binder such as the enzymes used. The results were positive, with the highest 
compressive strength achieved by the sample composed of 7.5 ml of enzymes and 
4% of micronized clay, which exhibited an average compressive strength value of 
2.3 MPa. The samples containing 8% hydrated lime by weight of soil (Fig. 19.6)  showed  
higher compressive strength values compared to the rest of the soil and hydrated 
lime dosages. Conversely, the deformations in tension demonstrate a more linear 
behaviour. This sample reaches the highest tension and exhibits the best linearity in 
the elastic state until breakage. The samples containing 8% hydrated lime and 1% cement in relation to the weight 
of the soil (Fig. 19.7) exhibited higher compressive strength values than the other 
dosages studied, which contained 2% cement. Fig. 19.3 a Compression stress-time curve of the reference CECs without additives. b Stress–strain 
curve of the reference CECs without additives

Physical–Mechanical Parameters Derived 
from Destructive and Non-destructive Tests 
This section presents the physical–mechanical parameters of the samples following 
the ultrasonic pulse propagation velocity tests and the simple compression rupture 
tests. Table 19.2 shows the mean value of the results obtained for each typology. As can be seen in Table 19.2, the mean values of compressive strength, transverse 
and longitudinal elastic modulus, Poisson ratio, density and dynamic modulus of 
elasticity calculated from the ultrasonic pulse propagation time are shown. The CECs exhibiting the highest compressive strength are those reinforced with 
7.5 ml of ecological enzymes derived from sugar cane and 4% of micronized 
clay. Additionally, these specimens demonstrate the highest densities in compar-
ison to those with additives. Furthermore, a correlation is observed between the 
average values of compressive strength and the densities obtained in this typology. Conversely, the propagation velocity of the ultrasonic wave is inversely proportional 
to the proportion of clay added to the mixture. About the analysis of the elastic 
moduli and Poisson’s ratio, the average values are generally improved compared to 
the specimens formed with soil only (Table 19.2). Regarding the analysis of the dynamic elasticity modulus, it can be observed that 
the values are lower in the lime and cement samples, since they require a longer time 
for the wave to pass through them due to the low percentage of binder. On the other 
hand, the samples containing ecological enzymes and clay present a higher value of 
elasticity modulus, since the propagation of the ultrasonic wave through the material 
is faster, showing that a more homogeneous and more compact mixture is achieved. 19

19.3.3 
Microstructural Analysis 
Finally, the porosity, the bond between the matrix and the natural aggregates, as 
well as the effect of the additives on the microscopic level in the mixture were anal-
ysed. In order to conduct this analysis, the sample containing 7.5 ml of ecological 
enzymes and 4% micronized clay was compared with the sample that had been 
added with 8% hydrated lime CL 90 S. These two samples were then compared 
with the sample without additives. The enzyme-reinforced sample was selected for 
analysis because it had the highest compressive strength of all the samples. In addi-
tion, it is essential to compare it with the sample reinforced with 8% hydrated lime, 
since it has the highest compressive strength of its type and the one that presents 
the most favourable deformations. In addition, the microscopic effects of replacing 
lime with ecological enzymes derived from sugar cane and micronized clay will be 
investigated. Figure 19.8 shows the details of the resin-embedded breakage cone of 
the analysed samples. At the macroscopic level, the aggregate particles, which are 
predominantly elongated and oval, are dispersed within the soil matrix. Furthermore, 
the heterogeneity of the aggregates is observed, with different tonality depending on 
their nature and composition. For instance, in the FA soil sample (Fig. 19.8a), the 
whitish natural stones are quartz, dolomite, or calcite, as the dominant mineralog-
ical elements. In contrast, the grey or brown natural stones are phyllosilicates, as 
indicated by reference studies (Valverde-Palacios et al., 2022). At the sample added 
with enzymes and clay (Fig. 19.8b), areas with more golden or yellow staining are 
additionally observed. In contrast, in the lime-reinforced sample (Fig. 19.8c), white 
crystallisations of heterogeneous sizes are randomly distributed in the sample. 19
Sugarcane Enzymes in Construction: Sustainable Alternatives for Earth …
363
Fig. 19.8 Detail of the subsamples. All the samples were bedded in epoxy resin and polished, on 
a scale of 1:100 
Microstructure of the CEC added with 7.5 ml of organic enzymes from sugar 
cane and 4% micronized clay 
The microstructure of the interfacial zone is contingent upon a few factors, including 
the type of soil, the size and composition of the aggregates, as well as t

he moisture 
content during the dosing process. In this case, the sample exhibits areas with height-
ened golden hues, which accentuate the brownish-pink hue characteristic of FA soil 
(Fig. 19.11). Low porosity is evident in the sample added with enzymes and clay and is quanti-
ﬁed at 0.58% (Fig. 19.12). The discontinuity between the natural stones, matrix, and 
additives is minimal and is mainly marked by irregular shapes (42%). The porosity 
of the sample is isolated and randomly distributed in the matrix, corresponding to 
occluded air (Fig. 19.9a, c). Furthermore, the formation of air pores during the mixing 
and compaction process, which become trapped beneath the natural aggregates with 
an irregular surface area exceeding 1 mm2, has also been observed in previous studies, 
in that case, in concrete samples with recycled aggregates [19]. The accumulation of water at the perimeter of the surface of the aggregates contin-
uing in the matrix indicates a point of weakness between the interfacial matrix-
aggregate bonding. This zone is deﬁned as a porous region surrounding the aggregate 
with a maximum thickness of 23 μm (Fig. 19.9b). Microstructure of the CEC added with 8% hydrated lime CL 90 S 
The most representative feature of the sample is the presence of bright white crystals, 
which correspond to the crystallisation of the hydrated lime was added to the sample. These crystallisations are distributed throughout the sample and present diameters 
below 20 μm in the case of the smaller crystals and reaching 155 μm in the case of 
the larger crystals (Figs. 19.10 and 19.11). As shown in Fig. 19.12, this sample exhibits a higher porosity (1.51%) than the 
samples reinforced with enzymes and clay, as well as the sample without additives. 364
A. Cervilla-Maldonado et al. Fig. 19.9 Analysis of images of the sample of CEC modiﬁed with 7.5 ml of organic enzymes from 
sugar cane and 4% micronized clay
Fig. 19.10 Analysis of images of the sample of CEC modiﬁed with 8% hydrated lime CL 90 S
Nevertheless, the porosity remains relatively low, resulting in a minimal discontinuity 
between natural stones and the matrix (Fig. 19.10). This discontinuity is predomi-
nantly marked by elliptical (69%) and circular (17%) shapes, which correspond to 
occluded air produced during the mixing process (Fig. 19.13). On the other hand, the proportion of elongated pores (3%), which correspond to 
cracks, is notably lower than that observed in the samples reinforced with enzymes

Conclusions 
This research focuses on analysing the results obtained from each dosage of test 
specimens studied, with special emphasis on the use of ecological enzymes from 
sugar cane and clays as a substitute for hydrated lime. In order to ascertain the 
material with the optimal performance, a series of tests were conducted, including 
ultrasonic pulse propagation velocity and compression strength tests. Considering 
these tests, the ﬁndings of this study can be summarised as follows:
• The CECs added with 75 ml of enzymes and 4% of micronized clay exhibited the 
highest compressive strength (2.3 MPa) when compared to the remaining types. It is important to note that these specimens also exhibit greater deformations, as 
they lack the addition of lime or cement. • The addition of CECs to a hydrated lime mixture (CL 90 S) with a percentage of 
8% results in the achievement of the requisite properties for the material. Among 
the samples containing lime, those with 8% exhibit the highest resistance and 
demonstrate the most favourable behaviour against deformations. • The favourable deformation behaviour of CECs combined with 8% hydrated lime 
can be attributed to the microscopic analysis of this specimen, which revealed 
a low ratio of elongated cracks or microcracks. In this specimen, 3% of elon-
gated pores were observed, in comparison to 15% in the CEC without additives. This is attributed to the stability of this typology, which is a consequence of the 
carbonation processes of the lime. In addition, microscopic analysis has demonstrated a correlation between the 
porosity of the specimens, their compressive strength and deformation. This indicates 
that specimens with a higher compressive strength exhibited a lower porosity, as 
observed in the case of the specimens that reached a higher compressive strength: 
CEC added with 75 ml of enzymes and 4% of micronized clay. This is due to the 
fact that the presence of ﬁnes (clays) and the binding effect of the enzymes result in 
a more effective compaction of the specimens. This research demonstrates the impact of additives, including organic enzymes 
derived from sugar cane, clays, lime, and cement, on the physical and mechanical 
properties of CECs. It is also important to note that the soil, with or without additives, 
exhibits very satisfactory physic mechanical properties. 19

Chapter 20 
Photocatalytic, Antimicrobial 
and Conductive Functional 
Nanomaterial-Doped Cement 
Composites for Sustainable Construction 
Agnieszka Ślosarczyk and Łukasz Klapiszewski 
Abstract Due to the high carbon footprint of building materials, including those 
based on cementitious binders, high durability and very often new functionalities are 
expected. This chapter provides an overview of recent research on the role of nanoma-
terials in shaping new properties of cementitious composites, including self-cleaning, 
antimicrobial and self-sensing properties. Important parameters in the design of these 
solutions are the nature and physicochemical properties of the nanoadditives, their 
proper dispersion in cement composites and the shaping of new material properties 
in terms of their durability. 20.1 
Introduction 
It will soon be 25 years since the Roadmap for Nanotechnology in Construction 
(RoNaC) was drawn up. It envisaged certain developments in nanotechnology in the 
construction industry, as shown schematically in Fig. 20.1. An important aspect of this 
issue has been the understanding of the mechanisms of nanoscience, i.e. the modelling 
of the structure of materials at the nanoscale using bottom-up or top-down methods, 
and the application of the resulting nanomaterials in construction. These directions 
have enabled the gradual development of materials nanotechnologies, including the 
nanocoating of building materials with special functions, the modiﬁcation of struc-
tural materials such as steel, wood, concrete and glass with nanoparticles, or the 
design of materials with super-insulating properties such as silica aerogel. However,

for the ﬁrst decade or so, mainly due to the size of the construction sector and socio-
economic factors, these developments were not as spectacular as in other industries 
such as medicine, agriculture or electronics [1–3]. This trend has been changing over the last few years and has been particularly 
noticeable in the last three to f

our years. The move towards nanotechnology-based 
solutions is being driven by a number of factors, the most important of which is 
the drive to reduce carbon dioxide emissions and move towards a near zero carbon 
economy. This trend in all areas of business and life has also led the construction 
industry to look for low-carbon material solutions with improved performance and 
durability to ensure a long service life of building materials. The increased perfor-
mance and durability of materials also contributes to lower maintenance costs for 
building infrastructure and lower deferred repair costs. This also has the effect of 
postponing the problem of recycling and reusing these materials at the end of their 
service life [4, 5]. Figure 20.2, based on publications from the last three years, 
provides a summary of the most frequently used keywords reﬂecting the topics of 
articles on nanomaterial modiﬁcations of cementitious composites. Three groups of 
relationships can be observed between nanoparticles and self-cleaning and antibacte-
rial properties. The same relationships can be seen regarding biocompatibility. These 
areas are mainly related to metallic nanoparticles, of which silver nanoparticles and 
zinc oxide have been the most studied in the last few years. Slightly less pronounced is the third record, sensing, which is mainly associated 
with carbon nanostructures. This is an issue that has emerged in the last few years 
and is gaining in popularity with smart concretes, which make it possible to monitor 
the gradual degradation at the nano and micro levels. Nevertheless, it is worth noting
