in recent years indicates a widespread interest in nanomodiﬁcation of cementitious 
composites to obtain speciﬁc new functionalities, which could lead to their increasing 
importance in the construction sector in the near future [6]. The main objective of this chapter is to present the latest trends in nanomodiﬁed 
cement composites. The ﬁrst section presents the inﬂuence of nanomaterials on 
the physical, mechanical and durability properties of cement composites, while the 
following sections focus on the new functionalities that cement composites acquire 
as a result of nanomodiﬁcation. The mechanisms of action and the most commonly 
used nanostructures for self-cleaning, antibacterial and self-sensing properties of 
engineered cementitious

composites are presented. 20.2 
Nanomaterials and Its Inﬂuence on Mechanical 
and Durability Performance 
Nanostructures are classiﬁed according to the number of dimensions in the nano: 
0-dimensional 0D (all dimensions in the nano, e.g. nanoparticles of silver, copper, 
silica, alumina or titanium dioxide), 1-dimensional 1D (1 dimension in the nano, e.g. metallic nanorods, carbon nanotubes and nanoﬁbers), 2-dimensional 2D (2 dimen-
sions in the nano, e.g. metallic nanoshells or graphene, graphene oxide) and bulk 
nanostructures, which are bulk materials with nanoscale features such as grains or 
pores, an example of which is silica aerogel. All of these types of nanostructures 
can be used in cementitious composites to inﬂuence various material properties, 
ranging from mechanical, photocatalytic, antimicrobial and self-sensing properties 
to improved performance and durability [7]. The improvement of mechanical performance has been the main and initial direc-
tion for the use of nanoadditives in cementitious composites. The use of nanos-
tructures, which allow the modiﬁcation of the cementitious binder at the nanoscale, 
instead of the widely used microadditives, has given spectacular results in improving 
the strength of the cement matrix and, consequently, the durability of the composite. The most widely studied nanoadditive is nanosilica. Compared to microsilica, 
nanosilica has a larger BET surface area and smaller size, corresponding to the 
size of the C–S–H crystallites. Nanosilica is therefore ten times more effective 
than microsilica [8, 9]. The effect of nanosilica in cement composites is multi-
faceted. Firstly, due to its nanometric dimensions, nanosilica provides additional 
active centers that act as growth sites for the silicate phases of the cement binder and 
accelerate the hydration of the cement. This is the so-called ﬁlling or nucleation effect, 
which results in a thickening of the microstructure of the cement composite, a reﬁne-
ment of the porous structure, an increase in adhesion to the aggregate and an improve-
ment in both the mechanical and durability parameters of the cement composite. In 
addition, due to its pozzolanic properties, nanosilica can react with calcium hydroxide

20
Photocatalytic, Antimicrobial and Conductive Functional …
373
to form additional C–S–H phases, resulting in signiﬁcant increases, particularly in 
compressive strength. The effect of nanosilica can be seen in the early stages of 
strength development. The positive effect of nanosilica can be seen at low levels, up 
to 3% by weight of the cement; typically nanosilica in powder form with a size of 
5–25 nm has been used [8, 10]. Similar effects of accelerating the hydration of the 
cementitious binder, thickening the microstructure and improving the mechanical 
performance of the cement composite can be achieved by using other nanoadditives. However, these are often inert additives, i.e. they do not participate in pozzolanic 
reactions like

nano- or microsilica. Their action is mainly physical, according to 
the ﬁlling mechanism or the so-called seeding effect. In studies into nanoadditives in 
concrete, metallic nanoxides, a portmanteau of nano-oxide referring to nanoparticles 
of metal oxides (Fe2O3, NiO, TiO2), nanoscale calcium carbonates, or carbon nanos-
tructures (carbon nanotubes and nanoﬁbers, graphene, graphene oxide) have been 
most commonly used [7, 11–15]. The effects of nanosilica with a diameter of 15 and 
50 nm, iron nanoxide with a diameter of 50 nm and nickel nanoxide with a particle 
diameter of 15 nm at a level of 1 wt% on the properties of cement mortars have 
been studied by Zhang et al. [16]. They showed that the nanoadditives accelerate the 
hydration of the cement binder due to their higher BET surface area and accelerate 
the initial and ﬁnal setting of the cement binder. They also show an effect on early 
compressive strength values after 3 days of setting, increasing them by an average of 
about 20% compared to mortars without nanomodiﬁers. Final compressive strengths 
after 28 days of setting were also higher, by an average of 10%. The small diameter 
nanosilica was the most effective, followed by the 50 nm diameter nanosilica, and the 
nickel and iron nanoxides were slightly less effective [16]. In addition to improving 
compressive strength, the metallic nanoparticles also had a positive effect on tensile 
and ﬂexural strength [9, 17, 18]. Recently, metallic nanoxides such as ZnO or CuO 
have been used in cementitious composites, which can delay the setting process of 
the cement binder during the ﬁrst days of curing. In the long term, however, the 
presence of these nanoparticles contributes to the improvement of both ﬂexural and 
compressive strength, through the nucleation effect. The increase in the C–S–H phase 
on these nanostructures leads to a reduction in the porosity of the cement matrix and 
a reduction in the size of the calcium hydroxide crystallites, which contributes to 
better adhesion of the cement matrix to the aggregate and consequently to greater 
durability of the whole system [19–21]. Spectacular improvements in the mechanical performance of cementitious 
composites can be achieved by introducing 1D and 2D carbon-based nanomate-
rials in the form of single or multi-walled carbon nanotubes (single-walled carbon 
nanotubes SWCNTs and multi-walled carbon nanotubes MWCNTs, respectively) 
and graphene and graphene oxide nanostructures. Carbon nanotubes are character-
ized by very good mechanical performance, with tensile strength ranging from 50 to 
500 GPa for SWCNTs and 10–60 GPa for MWCNTs. Young’s modulus values are 1 
TPa and 0.3–1.0 TPa for SWCNTs and MWCNTs respectively. In addition, their high 
aspect ratio of over 1000 means that the carbon nanotubes not only accelerate the 
hydration process through the nucleation effect, but also act as nano-reinforcement, 
bridging the cracks that form in the cement matrix. In this way, the

introduction of

374
A. Ślosarczyk and Ł. Klapiszewski
even small amounts of nanotubes, at a level of 0.1% by weight, leads to an improve-
ment in compressive strength of up to 30% and in tensile and ﬂexural strength of 
up to 50% [22–24]. Graphene, which has a modulus of elasticity of 1000 GPa, a 
tensile strength of around 130 GPa and a high aspect ratio of over 6000, also has 
a crack-inhibiting effect at the nanometer level. However, it has a very high BET 
surface area of around 2600 m2/g, which, like carbon nanotubes, makes it very difﬁ-
cult to disperse in a cement matrix. More easily dispersed is graphene oxide, which, 
due to the way it is obtained (oxidation, puriﬁcation and exfoliation of graphite), has 
hydrophilic oxygen-rich functional groups built into the structure that allow electro-
static repulsion. These groups also provide additional active centers on which the 
C–S–H phase begins to grow and a strong bond is formed. Due to oxidation, graphene 
oxide has lower parameters than graphene; these are: Young’s modulus 23–42 GPa, 
tensile strength around 0.13 GPa, aspect ratio over 1500 and BET surface area 700– 
1500 m2/g. Nevertheless, even at low levels of 0.02–0.05 wt%, it is able to bridge 
repeated cracks and accelerate the hydration of the cementitious binder, reducing the 
overall porosity of the system by about 13% and improving the compressive strength 
by 15–33% and the ﬂexural strength by 41–59% of the cementitious composite 
[25, 26]. The increase in material strength and, in particular, the thickening of the 
microstructure and reduction in the porosity of the cement matrix in nanomodiﬁed 
cement composites results in increased resistance to external factors. Nanomodiﬁ-
cation of materials has been shown to signiﬁcantly reduce water sorption, carbonate 
and chloride penetration, sulfate attack and improve corrosion of steel reinforcement 
[27, 28]. The main disadvantage of all nanomaterials, from nanosilica to carbon nanos-
tructures, is their large BET surface area, which gives them a high afﬁnity for water, 
leading to signiﬁcant limitations in the workability of the cement matrix and the 
tendency of nanoadditives to aggregate and agglomerate. The uneven distribution 
of additives very often contributes to the deterioration of mechanical properties 
and special properties, including photocatalytic and antibacterial properties and, in 
the case of conductive materials, an increase in the percolation threshold and the 
need for increased use of carbon nanostructures. Therefore, an important element 
of nanoengineered cementitious composites is the uniform dispersion of nanoad-
ditives, which can be achieved by different techniques: mechanical or ultrasonic 
mixing, with or without the addition of water-reduced admixtures, and, in the case 
of carbon-based materials, it is possible to use surface modiﬁcation by functional-
ization with COOH and OH groups [29–32]. Another more sophisticated method is 
the use of core–sh

ell structures, in which a hydrophobic nanoxide is coated with a 
hydrophilic one to improve its dispersion in the cement matrix. Examples of such 
solutions are Fe2O3@SiO2 or Fe3O4@SiO2 systems [33, 34]. Good dispersion can 
also be achieved with hybrid systems. In their work, Klapiszewska et al. used lignin 
as a nanoparticle carrier to achieve a satisfactory dispersion of zinc nanoxide and 
ZnO-SiO2 bicomponent systems [35]. It is worth noting that the efﬁcient disper-
sion of nanostructures in cementitious composites results in the possibility of using

20
Photocatalytic, Antimicrobial and Conductive Functional …
375
smaller amounts of nano additives, which in turn leads to lower composite manufac-
turing costs, increased durability and more efﬁcient use of special functionalities of 
these materials, such as antibacterial, self-cleaning or self-sensing properties. 20.3 
Self-cleaning Properties of Cement Composites 
The most commonly used metal nanoxide in engineered cement composites with self-
cleaning properties is titanium(IV) oxide. This is mainly due to the photocatalytic 
properties of the oxide in the presence of solar radiation; the band gap energy for 
titania in the most effective crystalline form of anatase is 3.2 eV, while for the 
rutile phase equals 3.0 eV. Other semiconducting materials that can be used in these 
solutions include zinc oxide, which has similar photocatalytic properties to titania, 
and ferric oxide, which has slightly lower photocatalytic properties (band gap energy 
is 2.2 eV). In addition, titanium dioxide exhibits superhydrophilic properties and is 
known to be inexpensive, non-toxic and chemically stable, making it compatible 
with most building materials, including cement composites. These properties enable 
titanium dioxide to be used in building materials, particularly in self-cleaning surfaces 
and in the reduction of organic and inorganic pollutants in the air, especially nitrogen 
and sulfur oxides and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [36–38]. In addition, its 
optical properties mean that titanium dioxide is used in rooﬁng and road systems 
in the form of passive cooling coatings that can reduce the surface temperature of 
materials by several degrees. The future use of such solutions in large cities could 
contribute to reducing the negative effect of urban heat islands [39, 40]. In turn, 
the antibacterial properties resulting from the photocatalytic properties of titanium 
dioxide, combined with its self-cleaning properties, make titanium dioxide suitable 
for use in organic and mineral coatings on heritage buildings or in public facilities 
(hospitals, schools). The use of such coatings counteracts biological corrosion of 
building materials and prolongs the life of buildings [41–43]. Figure 20.3a shows potential directions of application for materials with self-
cleaning properties, while Fig. 20.3b shows a mechanism for the self-cleaning action 
of semiconducting materials. Semiconducting ox

ides, including titanium dioxide, are characterized by a speciﬁc 
electronic structure that includes an electron-ﬁlled valence band and an empty 
conduction band. As a result of the absorption of a quantum of radiation corre-
sponding to the energy difference between the bands, an electron is excited and 
moves from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving a positively charged 
empty hole in the valence layer. The resulting electrons and holes can migrate to the 
surface of the photocatalyst and react with the donors and acceptors adsorbed on the 
oxide surface. In this way, positively charged holes can oxidize water or hydroxyl 
groups adsorbed on the surface to form reactive hydroxyl radicals. At the same time, 
the electrons excited to the conduction band react with the oxygen adsorbed on the 
TiO2 surface and reduction to the superoxide radical anion takes place. This anion can 
then react with the hydronium cation to form a hydroperoxyl radical and cause further

376
A. Ślosarczyk and Ł. Klapiszewski
Fig. 20.3 Potential directions of application for materials with self-cleaning properties (a)  and  a  
mechanism for the cleaning action of semiconducting materials (b),  based  on [44]
reduction to H2O2. The resulting reactive oxygen species (ROS) are involved in the 
oxidation of organic compounds to fully mineralized forms. The greatest contribu-
tion to the reduction of nitrogen and sulfur oxides is made by reactive hydroxyl and 
superoxide anion radicals, which can oxidize the oxides to form nitric acid (V) and 
sulfuric acid (VI), which are easily removed from the surface of the material [44, 45]. In self-cleaning applications, commercial P25 is most commonly used as a mixture 
of rutile and anatase, suggesting that its properties are sufﬁcient for the cement matrix 
to achieve photocatalytic properties. However, studies indicate that there are several 
factors that inﬂuence the photocatalytic performance of titania nanoparticles. Of 
the many factors, the most important are the proper dispersion of the nanoxide, 
with the anatase form being more effective than the rutile form, and the size of 
the nanoparticles and their BET surface area. Smaller particle sizes and larger BET 
surface areas are more effective and mean that high photocatalytic efﬁciencies in 
cement composites can be achieved with lower levels of nanoadditive, below 1.5 
wt% [46, 47]. 20
Photocatalytic, Antimicrobial and Conductive Functional …
377
Titanium nanoxide can be incorporated into the cement matrix in two ways, either 
as a surface impregnation layer or as an admixture to mortar or concrete. In the ﬁrst 
case, the nanoxide is usually mixed with organic substances such as silanes and 
mechanically applied to the surface of the cement composite and/or other building 
materials, most commonly stone, ceramics or wood. In the second case, nanoxide 
is added to mortars and concrete mixtures during the mixing process, usually in 
quantities of less than 5 wt%.

Both methods have their drawbacks. In the case of 
silane coatings, it is their short durability, while in the case of TiO2 admixture to 
cementitious composites, it is the loss of photocatalytic properties due to adsorption 
of the C–S–H phase onto the oxide grains and impaired access of UV radiation to 
the oxide surface, which often blocks the photocatalytic reaction [48–50]. Another 
drawback is the small range of visible light in which titanium dioxide is activated. Therefore, work has been done in recent years to increase the photocatalytic efﬁciency 
of titanium dioxide in the visible light range by enhancing its action with other 
semiconducting oxides such as ZnO, Fe2O3,  SiO2 or by modifying noble metals 
with Ag, Au, Cu or elements such as C, S or N [51–55]. 20.4 
Antimicrobial Properties of Cement Composites 
Cement composites are exposed to many external factors, the most common of which 
are physical hazards (temperature effects, cyclic mechanical loading, erosion) or 
chemical hazards (carbonization, salt and soft water, nitrogen and sulfur oxides in 
the urban atmosphere). All these external factors contribute to the progressive disin-
tegration of the cement binder, an increase in its porosity and a consequent loss of 
durability and performance [56]. An additional factor that can signiﬁcantly accelerate 
these processes is the presence of micro-organisms (algae, mosses, fungi, bacteria), 
which affect the durability of surfaces based on cement binders, particularly in struc-
tures exposed to conditions of variable humidity. The growth of microorganisms 
on the surface of cementitious composites, initially forming a thin ﬁlm and over 
time numerous colonies, affects their visual assessment, while the presence of toxic 
substances secreted by the microorganisms causes acidiﬁcation of the environment. The gradual leaching of cementitious binder components leads to a reduction in pH 
and degradation of the cementitious composite [57–60]. One way to counteract this phenomenon is to modify cement composites with 
nanoadditives with antimicrobial properties. Most commonly, silver and copper 
nanoparticles or, increasingly, titanium, zinc, calcium, aluminum or magnesium 
oxides are used in solutions for the construction industry [61–67]. These nanoxides 
are incorporated into cementitious composites in two ways: either by using silane 
dispersions of the nanoadditives on the surface, or by mixing them into the mortar 
or concrete mix before setting in small quantities of less than 5 wt% [68–70]. These 
solutions are most commonly used in heritage buildings, but due to their potential 
fungicidal and bactericidal properties, they can also be used in public buildings, 
hospitals and food storage areas [71–74]. 378
A. Ślosarczyk and Ł. Klapiszewski
The mechanism of microbial cell destruction and the effects of microbial inac-
tivation by selected nanostructures are shown in Fig. 20.4. The types of nanos-
tructures mentioned above are divid

ed into three types that determine their interac-
tion with microorganisms, namely pure nanoparticles (e.g. Ag, Cu), nanoxides with 
photocatalytic properties (e.g. ZnO, TiO2) and other nanoxides (e.g. Al2O3,  MgO). The action of the nanoparticles in the ﬁrst group and the nanoxides in the third 
group is mainly based on their mechanical interaction with the cell wall. Thanks to 
their nanometric size, Ag and Cu, as well as the Al3+,  Mg2+,  Zn2+ cations released 
from the oxides, are able to disrupt the cell membrane of the micro-organism and, 
thus introduced into the structure, are able to react with the cell’s DNA and enzymes, 
leading to their deactivation. Nanoparticles also have the ability to accumulate in 
the structure of the cell membrane, thus preventing the replication of the cell’s DNA 
code. In the case of nanoxides with photocatalytic properties, the disruption of the 
cell membrane occurs through the action of reactive oxygen species released by 
the photocatalytic reaction. ROS have a tendency to disrupt the cell membrane and 
penetrate the cell structure, leading to the deactivation of DNA, proteins and enzymes, 
resulting in a decrease in cellular metabolic stability and reduced ATP levels [75, 76]. Table 20.1 shows nanoparticles and nanoxides and their inhibitory effects on 
selected micro-organisms in cementitious composites. Nanostructures have been 
shown to have a beneﬁcial effect on both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, 
fungi and algae, although the efﬁcacy depends on several factors, including the type 
and size of nanoparticles, morphology and dispersion in the cement matrix. Nanopar-
ticles are the most effective in inhibiting bacteria. Fungi and algae are more difﬁcult 
to deactivate [30, 77–79]. In this regard, it is worth noting that in the future, when 
designing composites with antimicrobial properties, attention should be paid to the 
selective selection of the right nanoadditives in order to use the most effective nanos-
tructures for deactivating a given microorganism. This will result in slower microbial 
immunization against the selected nanoadditives and better microbial resistance of 
the cementitious composite over a longer service life. 20.5 
Self-sensing Properties of Cement 
The durability of cementitious composites, their longevity and, therefore, their overall 
life cycle, depends to a large extent on the maintenance and repair phase of the mate-
rial during its service life. The more durable and resistant the material, the longer it can 
be used and the repair phase can be postponed. As a result, recycling and reusing into 
new, fully functional products, which is the basis of the circular economy, is delayed. This is why the maintenance and condition monitoring phase of building components 
and structures is so important [88, 89]. In recent years, the idea of smart concrete 
has been developed to monitor the condition of structures. Smart concrete is an engi-
neered material based on high

performance, low emissivity cementitious composites 
that incorporate nanostructures with conductive properties into their structure [90]. Such structures can also include nanomaterials that tend to self-heal or self-adjust

[91–93]. However, self-monitoring of the structure is possible by introducing carbon 
materials into the structure of the cement matrix to form a conductive network that 
responds to changes in the resistivity and degradation of the concrete. This makes it 
possible to predict the condition of the concrete structure and apply appropriate repair 
in time to prevent permanent, irreversible material degradation [90]. Figure 20.5 illus-
trates the idea of smart concrete, with an indication of the development directions of 
the material solution and the positive pro-environmental effects that can be expected 
in the future due to the introduction of carbon nanostructures in the cement matrix. The design of high performance cementitious composites with self-sensing proper-
ties, through the use of low carbon binders and a more durable structure, will enable

a reduction in CO2 emissions to the atmosphere and a reduction in the consumption 
of natural raw materials, as well as timely repairs due to their self-monitoring ability. This in turn will result in a longer service life of the material and lower repair and 
maintenance costs for the structure. Fig. 20.5 Components of smart concrete (a)

A. Ślosarczyk and Ł. Klapiszewski
The possibility of using carbon materials to monitor the condition and degrada-
tion of the cement matrix by studying the conductivity of the cement matrix (and 
indeed changes in its resistivity) was introduced in the early 1990s [94]. Initially, 
the main conductive materials used were short carbon ﬁbers, steel ﬁbers or carbon 
black, added to the cement matrix in quantities of less than 0.5% by weight [95– 
97]. Today, as a result of the intensive development of different allotropic varieties 
of carbon and the development of relatively cheap ways to obtain carbon nanos-
tructures, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanoﬁbers, graphene and graphene oxide are 
proposed in smart concrete solutions [98–102]. Thanks to their nanometric size, the 
percolation threshold, i.e. the ability of carbon nanostructures to form a conductive 
network, is achieved at low levels, very often below 0.15 wt% [103]. The  main  draw-
back of introducing carbon nanostructures, which are characterized by a high aspect 
ratio and a very high BET surface area, is their proper dispersion in the cement matrix. Improved dispersion of carbon nanostructures is achieved by the use of superplas-
ticisers, ultrasonic mixing and surface modiﬁcation, mainly in acids, sulfuric and 
nitric acids [104]. 20.6 
Conclusion 
The chapter presents trends and results of research on the use of nanostructures 
in cementitious composites over the last few years. The literature review shows a 
growing interest in the use of nanoadditives for the development of so-called smart 
structures, including cementitious composites with self-cleaning, antimicrobial and 
self-sensing properties. The use of such coatings, façade elements, small-scale infras-
tructure and structures capable of self-monitoring and self-repair are potential direc-
tions for the development of cementitious composites in the near future. In addition, 
nanostructures help to improve the quality and durability of the material solutions 
presented and to extend the life of these materials, which has a direct impact on the 
sustainability of the construction industry. However, the performance of such solu-
tions depends on several factors, the most important of which are the proper disper-
sion of the nanoadditives to ensure the lowest possible dosage while maintaining the 
desired effects, and the selective, directional incorporation of the nanoadditive for 
the desired effect. The rational design of cementitious composites with nanoadditives 
and the dissemination of their positive effects among engineers and architects will 
certainly inﬂuence a wider and safer application in practice in the future. Acknowledgements This work was supported by the National Science Center Poland under 
research project no. 2022/45/B/ST8/02288. 20

Part III 
Lifecycles and Sustainability of Materials 
in the Built Environment 
The sustainability of materials production and their implementation in the built envi-
ronment is a complex topic to grapple with and there is no single right way to conduct 
an LCA for a system or material. Implementing more sustainable approaches towards 
materials cannot be done on gut-feeling alone and requires meaningful insights into 
the real-world consequences of procuring and implementing the alternative materials 
discussed in previous chapters of this book. Design for sustainability in the built 
environment requires depth and breadth of knowledge. Understanding the thermal, 
mechanical, and longevity performance of earth- and bio-based materials and gaining 
insights into the complexity of processing and applying these materials at the large 
scales encountered in the construction industry is instrumental towards bringing 
about positive change in this conservative sector. The chapters in this part of the book Sustainable Materials for the Built Environ-
ment confront the complexity of material lifecycles, interrogating the metrics, and 
methodologies that deﬁne “sustainable” practices in construction. From extraction to 
demolition, each link in the construction value chain including processing, transporta-
tion, application, maintenance, and end-of-life, brings about cascading consequences 
for carbon budgets, biodiversity, and human well-being. Sustainability is regarded 
here not as a static destination but as a dynamic interface between society and nature. Designing more sustainable practices in the built environment requires methodical 
data-driven analysis, transparent accountability, and a willingness to dig deeper under 
the surface, reveal new information, and confront trade-offs. Scrutinies of materials 
performance across multiple indicators are presented here alongside approaches to 
conducting LCAs and an examination of frameworks required to foment sustain-
able practices, revealing new insights into the design and selection of sustainable 
materials. Through case studies, materials reviews, and the examination of LCA frame-
works, this part of the book reveals how innovators are redeﬁning lifecycle thinking. Importantly, these studies demonstrate that using materials to create more sustain-
able built environments requires cross-disciplinary collaboration. Architects may 
seek to partner with ecologists or agriculturists to design buildings using sustainably

390
Part III: Lifecycles and Sustainability of Materials in the Built …
sourced bio-based materials in a way that sequesters carbon and supports biodiver-
sity. Engineers may work alongside sociologists or geologiests to ensure low-carbon 
materials align with cultural preferences,

needs, and local availability of materials. Professionals in policy, data-science, and systems engineering meanwhile promote 
standardized LCA frameworks that help create transparency and catalyse effective 
decision making processes throughout the value chain. The chapters in Part III showcase the multi-faceted and interconnected nature of 
materials and their use in construction, inspiring new ways of designing for sustain-
ability. These chapters show that sustainability is not a constraint but a catalyst for 
creativity. By embracing complexity, confronting trade-offs, and prioritizing systems 
over silos, the built environment can evolve from a driver of planetary degradation 
to a pillar of regeneration. The path forward is neither simple nor singular, but as the 
following pages demonstrate, it is rich with possibility. Dorian A.

H. Hanaor

Chapter 21 
Engineered Wood Products 
(EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency 
and Environmental Performance 
Maximilian Pramreiter, Johannes Konnerth, and Paul Mayencourt 
Abstract Today, the construction sector has access to a vast array of resources, 
ranging from non-renewable materials like steel and concrete to natural materials 
like stone and timber. These materials are either used alone or in combination as 
part of composites. Driven by the need to reduce the environmental impact of the 
built environment, Engineered Wood Products (EWPs) like Glued Laminated Timber 
(GLT) or Cross Laminated Timber (CLT) have gained increasing interest in the 
construction sector in recent years. This chapter explores the resource efﬁciency 
and environmental performance of these EWPs, by examining established literature 
on production processes, material yields, and life cycle assessments. The chapter 
highlights the role of different wood species, processing methods, and mechanical as 
well as environmental performances. It also discusses circularity and potential areas 
for future research to enhance the overall sustainability of the wood value chain. This 
comparative approach aims to support material selection decisions and foster more 
responsible use of forest resources in the built environment. M. Pramreiter (B) · J. Konnerth

Renewable Materials, University of Natural Resources and Life Sciences Vienna, Austria (BOKU

M. Pramreiter et al. 21.1 Introduction 
21.1.1 
Is There Enough Wood for Construction? The built environment fulﬁls a multitude of functions in our modern society. While 
in the Stone Age it primarily served as a shelter and mankind spent most of its time in 
nature, today we spend a large part of our day in or around man-made structures. Simi-
larly, the materials we use to create these environments have signiﬁcantly changed 
and expanded throughout the course of history. Today, the construction sector has 
access to a vast array of resources, ranging from non-renewable materials like steel 
and concrete to natural materials like stones and timber. These materials are either 
used alone or in combination in the form of composites and are either load-bearing 
or non-load bearing. One of these natural materials, wood,

is currently experiencing 
a renaissance in construction. This renaissance is fuelled by the need to create a sustainable living space for 
our society while at the same time tackling climate change [1, 2]. Wood as building 
material offers not only the capability to build high rise and large volume structures 
but it also sequesters carbon while being used as material. It is therefore no surprise 
that a race for the tallest wooden skyscraper has ensued in recent years, with height 
records sometimes being broken only by one metre to the previous champion (e.g. the Ascent Tower in the USA at 87 m, followed by the Mjøstårnet Building in 
Norway at 85 m, the HoHo Tower in Austria at 84 m as seen in Fig. 21.1b and 
the Sara Kulturhus Centre in Sweden at 75 m). It will not stop here, future projects 
like the “Rocket & Tigerli” in Switzerland (100 m, targeted completion in 2027) or 
the “C6” in Australia (planned with 183 m) in Australia are already in the pipeline. Considering humans’ past, it is not the ﬁrst time that wood is considered to be an 
excellent building material. Recent discoveries by Barham et al. [3] assume that 
wood was used as structural material already 476,000 years ago (see Fig. 21.1a). However, compared to this distant past, the current material need for construction 
is on a totally different level [4] and when society talks about (partially) substituting 
steel and concrete with wood, the following question arises: “Is there enough wood 
for construction and how should we build with wood to preserve our forests?” 
21.1.2 
The Global Wood Value Chain 
In an attempt to answer this question, it is necessary to take a look at the globally 
available woody biomass from forests, the current areas of use and the potential 
demand for building products in the future. 21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
393
Fig. 21.1 Wood as oldest building material and current shooting star in architecture and design. a Earliest evidence of structural use of wood in archaeological records, dating back at least 
476,000 years (adopted with permission from [3], copyright Prof. Larry Barham)). b HoHo in 
Vienna, high-rise ﬂagship project using mass timber, completed in 2021 (with permission from 
KiTO photography https://www.kito.at/, copyright by Michael Baumgartner/KiTO)
21.1.2.1 
Current Material Flows and Areas of Use 
According to Bais et al. [5] roughly 158 megatons of carbon [MtC] of the annu-
ally extracted woody biomass (1936 MtC) end up in wood-based products (part of 
HWP  in  Fi  g. 21.2). This represents roughly 13% of the annually socioeconomically 
used biomass of the world’s forests (1219 MtC, “Used extraction” plus “Recovered 
paper”), with the remaining 87% divided between wood energy use (48%), paper 
and paperboards (22%), losses (8%), wood waste (6%) and poles as well as posts 
among other niche products (3%). Based on these current wood carbon ﬂows (see Fig. 21.2), a signiﬁcant utilisation 
potential for wood in

construction can be identiﬁed, as almost 56% of the annually 
used forest biomass (1050 MtC) is used as fuel wood. A portion of these resources 
could be used for construction products with higher added value and longer carbon 
sequestration periods, while remaining available for further material use after re-use 
or recycling [8]. The material can then still be used as fuel at the end of its use 
cycles. However, it should be noted that there are considerable regional differences. For example, looking at the ﬁndings of Mantau [9] for wood ﬂows in Europe (EU 
27), only 36% of the annually used forest biomass is directly put into fuel wood. In addition, there are still available reserves in our forests that could be sustain-
ably harvested for the production of building materials. According to Bais et al.

Fig. 21.2 Global wood carbon ﬂows [MtC/yr] from net primary production (NPPactual)  to  ﬁnal  
consumption in 2010 (adopted with permission from [5], copyright by Anna Liza Bais). TBA 
(total biomass appropriation), HANPPharv (human appropriated net primary production), SOC 
(soil organic carbon) and HWP (harvested wood products). To estimate [m3/yr] divide numbers by 
0.5 (carbon to wood ratio [6]) and then by density of assumed wood species mix (e.g. 0.650 t/m3 
average density according to [7])
roughly 30% of the annual net primary production (currently 4% of 49,200 MtC/yr, 
globally) could be harvested without impairing the health of the forests. However, 
regional differences must also be considered here, as parts of the world have already 
exceeded this threshold [5]. However, in the European Union (EU 27) for example, 
only 75.7% of the annually available forest biomass is utilised [9]. A similar poten-
tial was recently reported by the European Commission [10]. In addition, the avail-
ability and accessibility of forest area for wood supply is also essential to mobi-
lize these potential reserves in the future [11, 12]. Considering accessibility, it is 
worth mentioning that countries with large cities not necessarily also have access to 
vast amounts of forest resources (see Fig. 21.3). Therefore, the utilisation of wood 
might not be the best solution everywhere on the planet yet, due to increasing trans-
port distances and potential exploitation of foreign forest resources without proper 
regulations and adequate forest management practices. To sum up the supply side for wood in construction it can be said, that there is 
still potential for raw materials from the world’s forests and not mentioned so far, 
forest plantations. However, sustainable forest management practices and accurate 
resource monitoring will be an absolute necessity in order to prevent overuse in the 
future [15]. 21.1.2.2 
Potential Demand for Construction 
Taking a look on the demand side, projecting future growth for wood in construction 
is even more complex than the available forest resources. The various methods of 
collecting data on the current consumption and ﬂows of building materials are already 
a much-researched topic [16]. However, future shares of different materials and their 
demand are often not even considered at this stage. Furthermore, it is also difﬁcult

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
395
Fig. 21.3 The world’s largest cities and global forest cover in 2020. (Created with ArcGIS online, 
copyright by ESRI [13, 14])
to provide a global perspective, as regional customs, policies and regulations play an 
important role when it comes to the demand side of the construction sector [17–21]. Nevertheless, a possible approach estimating future demand for wood in construction 
was recently provided by Churkina et al. [1]. They investigated different transition 
scenarios (“business as usual”, “10% timber”, “50% timber” and “90% t

imber”) to 
provide a sustainable living space for the additional 2.3 billion urban residences 
until 2050 by incrementally substituting a steel- and concrete-based construction 
method with a timber-based building system. According to their ﬁndings, the addi-
tional wood demand could range from 20 MtC/yr in the “10% timber” scenario up 
to 1360 MtC/yr in the “90% timber” scenario, which would outstrip the current 
total roundwood supply reported by the FAO [22, 23]. This wide range of potential 
demand shows the high uncertainties linked to the different visions for the future 
of the construction sector and underlines the unpredictability that the forest-based 
industries have to deal with. Especially considering the wide range of EWPs and 
possible building systems they are used for. However, besides a singular focus on 
mass timber construction as assumed by Churkina et al. [1], a wide range of possibly 
more resource-efﬁcient systems or combinations of them could be employed to create 
the future living space. For example, timber frame construction stands out in partic-
ular as a possible complementary construction method with potentially lower raw 
material consumption compared to systems based only on mass timber. Paired with the push to also transition the energy sector to a more sustainable 
mix of energy sources it becomes clear that in order to prepare for this future market 
conditions the resource efﬁciency and environmental performance of processes and 
products needs to be part of the discussion. This will help provide the necessary 
baseline in order to support policymakers with the sustainably distribution of the 
available forest-based raw materials. 396
M. Pramreiter et al. 21.1.3 
Scope of This Chapter 
This chapter aims to present currently available Engineered Wood Products (EWPs) 
for structural applications (see Fig. 21.4) and summarizes the current state of the art in 
regard to their resource efﬁciencies as well as production processes (Sect. 21.2). The 
overview is complemented by a concise discussion of their mechanical (Sect. 21.3) 
and environmental (Sect. 21.4) performance. These aspects of EWPs have recently been reviewed in depth by Pramreiter 
et al. [24]. This publication serves as the foundation for this chapter and is supple-
mented by a more detailed look into the by-products, sometimes referred to as 
residues or scrap, during the production processes. Furthermore, six additional EWPs 
are added to the comparison. It should be mentioned that a detailed description of the 
different products and production steps has been published multiple times up to this 
day and can be found most recently in Niemz et al. [25] and also in Dunky et al. [26], 
Ansell [27], Walker [28], Wagenführ [29], Williamson [30] and the Forest Products 
Laboratory [31]. These references serve as a baseline for the depicted production 
steps in Sect. 21.2. It should be noted here that the chapter aims to present the products without the

goal of promoting one over the other. The comparisons should rather give insight 
into the variety of available products and provide base-values for material selection. It is therefore not the aim of the authors to rank the available products. Fig. 21.4 Overview of established engineered wood products (EWP) for structural applications 
based on the used intermediary (1Lumber or Sawn timber, 2Peeled veneers, 3Strands, 4Scrim-mats, 
5Particles, 6Fibres). a Typically beam-shaped. b Typically panel-shaped. (Not to scale, drawings 
created by Frederic Lam)

21.2 Resource Efﬁciency 
The ﬁrst section of this chapter gives insight into the utilized raw materials and the 
resource efﬁciency from the ﬁrst breakdown (the ﬁrst disintegration of roundwood 
into intermediaries) to the ﬁnal material yield of the ﬁnished EWP. Furthermore, it 
presents the most common occurrences of by-products during manufacturing. The 
statements are taken from the literature (see Sect. 21.1.3) and expert interviews in 
the ﬁeld. 21.2.1 
Deﬁnition of Resource Efﬁciency 
Resource (process) efﬁciency, also referred to as material or product yield can be 
calculated based on the following equation: 
Resource efﬁciency or material yield 
= Intermediaries or material output
Roundwoodor intermediary input × 100[%] 
(21.1)
It expresses the ratio between the output of intermediaries or ﬁnal material and 
the input of roundwood or intermediaries. The remaining % to 100% is typically 
by-product which still can be used in other wood processing streams and end up 
in different materials. This is an important merit of the wood processing industry. Basically, nothing of the input raw material is wasted in a classical sense but rather 
transferred to other process streams of typically lower added value compared to 
structural building products [32]. Furthermore, there are few additional factors that 
need to be considered when using this metric to compare products and their efﬁciency 
in utilizing raw material. In regards to ﬁrst roundwood breakdown into different intermediaries, the 
measuring principle (volume or weight), the measuring standard (different log scales) 
as well as the consideration of the bark content dictates how one unit of roundwood 
input is actually calculated. This can lead to signiﬁcant differences in reported vs. actual roundwood input and signiﬁcantly inﬂuences the reported process efﬁcien-
cies [33, 34]. According to a recent study by the Food and Agriculture Organization 
of the United Nations (FAO) [35] different national log scales can cause a devia-
tion from true log volume as high as 32.3% (8.4% on average). Furthermore, when 
converting 1000 Board Feet (bd. ft.), the traditional unit for sawmill processes in 
North America, the resulting volume in m3 can range from 3.87 m3 up to 17.23 m3 
depending on the log scale and small end diameter [35]. Additionally, the subsequent processing of intermediaries

into ﬁnished EWPs, the 
unit (weight or volume) in which the throughput is monitored needs to be considered 
as well. While lumber-(which can also be referred to as sawn timber) and veneer-
based processes are usually controlled only using volume (e.g. m3 or bd. ft.), the

398
M. Pramreiter et al. strand-, particle- and ﬁbre-based processes are monitored using mostly weight (e.g. bone-dry tons also referred to as ATRO) and to some extend also volume. This differ-
ence is especially critical when considering the different drying steps to achieve a 
target moisture content and the compaction (i.e. densiﬁcation) during the pressing 
steps. The change in moisture content leads to a change in dimension (volume 
shrinkage, values for volume shrinkage of selected wood species can be found in 
literature [31, 36]) and weight (water extraction), while the compaction further inﬂu-
ences the dimension (reduced cross-section). Both steps inﬂuence the actual density 
[kg/m3] of the material, which is the driving parameter when converting output mate-
rial into different units. The accurate determination of these factors is therefore key 
when comparing these materials. 21.2.2 
Raw Material 
The predominantly used raw material for the production of EWPs consists of different 
assortments of roundwood (typically without bark) from forests and, in the case of 
Particle Boards (PB) and to some extent Fibre Boards (FB), industry by-products (e.g. chips and sawdust) as well as recovered wood from consumers [25]. Depending on the 
required properties of the ﬁnal product and the technological processing conditions, 
relevant quality factors of the utilised roundwood include: 
• Wood species (coniferous or deciduous, also referred to as softwood or hardwood, 
see  also  Fi  g. 21.5a) 
• Dimension (mainly diameter, length and volume)
Fig. 21.5 Overview of global roundwood used for the production of engineered wood products 
(EWP). a Typical tree morphology of coniferous and deciduous trees (adopted with permission, 
copyright by Andreas Tockner, Institute of Forest Growth, University of Natural Resources and Life 
Sciences, Vienna). b Development of global total roundwood supply from 1961 to 2021 as reported 
by the FAO [22] and amount of industrially used roundwood (incl. the volume of sawlogs & veneer 
logs) for the production of EWPs

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
399
• Growth characteristics (the shape and the quality of the roundwood) 
• Biological condition of the tree (is the wood in a healthy condition, e.g. fungal 
decay or other forms of biological attack). For the sake of simplicity, the deﬁnitions laid out by the FAO [37] for different raw 
materials of the forest-based value chain will be used in the subsequent comparison 
of EWPs. More speciﬁcally: 
• “Roundwood” (roundwood that is removed from the forest), 
• “Wood fuel” (roundwood that will be used for the purposes of power production). • “Industrial roundwood” (all roundwood r

emoved for utilisation, except wood 
fuel). • “Sawlogs and veneer logs” (roundwood that will be sawn lengthways or used for 
the production of peeled or sliced veneers). The terms “sawlogs” and “veneer 
logs” will also be used on their own. Furthermore, general terms and deﬁnitions for roundwood can be found in ISO 
24294 [38] and EN 844 [39], while the measurement of dimensions and volume 
calculation is standardised in ISO 13059 [40] and further described for Europe in EN 
1309-2 [41]. Deﬁnitions in regards to growth characteristics (quality) can be found 
in ISO 24294 as well as in EN 1927 [42–44] for coniferous and in EN 1316 [45, 
46] for deciduous species. Deﬁnitions concerning the biological condition of the 
tree are standardised in EN 1309-3 [47]. The previously mentioned quality aspects 
are to a certain extend already assessed in the forest, before the tree is harvested 
in order to decide on the target process and potential part of the industry sector the 
roundwood will be sold to. In that regard it needs to be mentioned that in some 
instances the market condition may favour certain process streams over others even 
if the roundwood would fulﬁl higher quality requirements. In some cases, this can 
go so far that individual production sites procure all the available roundwood in a 
region without pre-assessing the quality of the acquired material. According to the FAO [22, 37], the global roundwood supply has almost constantly 
increased since 1961 (the earliest recording) from 2.5 × 109 m3 to 4.0 × 109 m3 in 
2021 (see Fig. 21.5b). Similarly, the volume of industrial roundwood has increased 
from 1.0 × 109 m3 in 1961 up to 2.1 × 109 m3 in 2021. About 1.2 × 109 m3 of 
“sawlogs & veneer logs” were harvested in 2021, which accounts for 57% of the total 
industrial roundwood supply and represents a slight decrease compared to the share 
of 63% (0.6 × 109 m3) in 1961. This decrease in relative importance is partly caused 
by the development of particle-based EWPs like Laminated Strand Lumber (LSL) 
and Oriented Strand Board (OSB) in the late 1960s and the subsequent installation of 
production capacities in Europe and North America from the 1980s onwards, which 
led to the substitution of PLY and other EWPs in some regions [28]. However, from a 
structural perspective, sawn timber-based EWPs are still dominating the wood-based 
construction sector. This starts with strength-graded and sometimes ﬁnger-jointed 
timber for timber-frame construction at the lower end of added value, all the way up 
to prefabricated Cross-Laminated Timber (CLT) wall- and ﬂoor-elements, currently 
offering the highest added value for the wood industry. CLT has seen signiﬁcant

400
M. Pramreiter et al. increases in market share in Europe since 2010 [48, 49] and is currently fuelling a hype 
of so-called mass timber construction in North America which started in 2018 [50]. The roundwood used for these products predominantly comprises of coniferous wood 
species l

However, according 
to recent studies by Hanewinkel et al. [51] and Thuiller et al. [52] climate change 
will also impact the species mix of forests in the future, with an expected increasing 
share of climate-resilient deciduous trees at the cost of currently dominating conifers. This trend is already visible in the supply of industrial roundwood (see Fig. 21.5b), 
as the share of coniferous wood has decreased from 74% in 1961 to 58% in 2021, 
while the share of deciduous wood has increased from 26 to 42%. However, this past 
shift is probably caused by an increase in industrial roundwood used in the pulp and 
paper industry as well as other industrial roundwood used for poles, posts, fencing, 
pitprops, shingles and shakes, wood wool, tanning, distillation, among others. In these 
areas the share of deciduous species has continuously increased while the supply of 
conifers has decreased [22]. Nevertheless, this shifting species mix will lead to an 
increase of, currently considered low-quality, non-sawable assortments. This is due 
to the fact, that deciduous trees grow more heterogenous compared to conifers, with a 
higher share of branches and lower share of high-quality stem wood (see Fig. 21.5a) 
[11, 53]. This shift will require a more diverse combination of processes in order 
to efﬁciently cover the potential demand for structural EWPs [2]. It also inﬂuences 
other technological factors like adhesive technology, mechanical performance and 
dimensional stability. 21.2.3 
First Breakdown and Intermediaries 
As mentioned in the previous section, the predominant raw material source for 
EWPs is roundwood (without bark) from coniferous or deciduous tree stems (see 
also Fig. 21.5). An exception to this is recovered wood in the PB production. Simi-
larly, by-products from other ﬁrst breakdown processes like wood chips, sawdust or 
ﬁnes can be seen as raw material for the PB and in the case of wood chips also for 
the FB production. Figure 21.6 summarizes the breakdown steps leading to different EWPs with their 
intermediaries in a simpliﬁed way. The raw material is put through ﬁrst breakdown by 
either sawmilling, veneer peeling, stranding, scrimming or chipping. In some cases, 
a secondary breakdown will occur if the required size of the intermediary needs to 
be further reduced. This can include for example clipping for PSL, ﬂaking for PB 
or reﬁning for FB production. Steps like planing, ﬁnger-jointing or sanding should 
not be considered as part of a secondary breakdown, as it does not change the size 
of the material in a signiﬁcant way. A concise discussion of the typical sizes of the 
different intermediaries and the dimensional freedom of the different EWPs can be 
found in Sect. 21.2.4. 21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
401
Fig. 21.6 Simpliﬁed summary of utilized raw material, ﬁrst and secondary breakdown, intermedi-
aries and ﬁnished EWPs (based on [25, 37]) 
This ﬁrst roundwood breakdown is one of the most domin

ating factors when it 
comes to overall resource efﬁciency. The ﬁrst breakdown gives the basic dimen-
sion of the intermediary and dictates a certain range of resource efﬁciency due to 
the underlying technology. Currently, only the sawmilling and veneer peeling (also 
slicing, for non-structural applications) processes create an intermediary that is also 
sold as its own product. The production of the strand-, particle- and ﬁbre-based 
EWPs is normally a fully ﬁnished process and the intermediaries are usually not 
marketed. This is due to the fact that the sawmill and veneer industry has a very long 
tradition and has historically become more and more integrated by implementing 
additional value-adding processes over the years. In comparison, the other indus-
tries have mainly developed from the need to either utilize a certain raw material or 
substitute an existing product (e.g. OSB as substitute for PLY). For this reason, there 
is a lot of literature on the resource efﬁciency of lumber and veneer production and 
relatively little on the ﬁrst breakdown of the other EWPs. Figure 21.7 summarizes the values found in literature on the process efﬁciency 
of sawmilling and veneer peeling and the subsequent process steps to arrive at either 
dried and planed lumber or dried and clipped veneer sheets. A detailed list of literature 
dealing with the process efﬁciency of ﬁrst-round wood breakdown can be found in 
the appendix of Pramreiter et al. [24].

Fig. 21.7 Process efﬁciency [%] of the ﬁrst roundwood breakdown and subsequent by-products 
until the ﬁnal intermediary material. The base input material is roundwood (without bark), with the 
input unit being either cubic volume or weight. (Based on [24]) 
21.2.3.1 
Sawmilling 
The average total green lumber yield related to sawlogs under bark ranges from 25% 
up to 74% with an average of 52%. In general, sawmilling efﬁciency increased due to 
improved process technologies over the past decade’s [54–57]. Nevertheless, various 
factors inﬂuencing sawmilling efﬁciency are summarized in Table 21.1. Market characteristics (e.g. price of lumber vs. price of by-products) inﬂuence 
sawmilling efﬁciency by conscious decisions against or in favour of an improved 
lumber yield [58, 59]. In contrast, mill, roundwood, process and lumber characteris-
tics are predominantly discussed to improve lumber yield. Roundwood characteris-
tics, diameter, length, taper, curvature and local decay are the most important factors 
inﬂuencing process efﬁciency. Based on ﬁndings in the literature [33, 58, 63–65] 
lumber yield increases with increasing roundwood diameter, while yield generally 
decreases with increasing length, taper and curvature. To compensate for differing 
roundwood shapes and sizes, process characteristics like sawing patterns (opening 
faces) [33, 58, 68, 73], as well as rotation and positioning [74, 75] of the roundwood 
are usually a direct answer to raw material characteristics to maximize yield on a 
log-to-log basis. Additionally, general equipment properties like machine set-up [61, 
63] and sawing kerf width [33, 58, 63] along with maintenance schedule [58]  also  
inﬂuence the process efﬁciency. However, the ongoing improvements in 3D X-ray 
scanner technology have enabled the industry to obtain information about the internal

structure of the raw material and have led to a shift towards value-focused processing 
rather than yield-based [84]. In order the discuss the subsequent processing steps of green lumber into dried & 
planed lumber, Fig. 21.8 gives an overview of typical sawmilling opera

tions and 
indicates where by-products occur. Literature reports an average total dried & planed lumber yield from 9% up to 
60% with an average of 46%. The lowest reported dried & planned lumber yield 
of 9% [85] is based on an assumed distribution of process efﬁciencies and very 
likely reﬂects only a theoretical worst-case scenario. Similar to round wood volume,
Fig. 21.8 Typical operations and by-products during sawmilling. Sawn timber can also be replaced 
with lumber

404
M. Pramreiter et al. the methods of measuring lumber volume inﬂuence the reported process efﬁciency. Most commonly, actual dimensions of lumber are used to determine the cubic volume 
(length × width × height). One exception can be found in North America, where 
nominal dimensions are used to calculate board feet (bd. ft.) of lumber [33, 35, 54]. One bd. ft. is deﬁned as a 1-inch (2.54 cm) thick and 12 by 12 in. (30.48 cm) long 
board, which would lead to 1 m3 being 424 bd. ft. [33]. However, as these nominal 
sizes sometimes ignore dimensional changes due to drying and planing of the lumber 
actual volume could be as low as 0.66 m3 [35]. Besides these variabilities, drying 
(6% average drying loss [35, 59, 64, 78, 85–90]), planing (13% average planning 
loss [35, 59, 64, 78, 85–93] and sorting [59, 78–83] signiﬁcantly decreases the ﬁnal 
intermediary yield of the sawmilling process (also see Fig. 21.8). 21.2.3.2 
Veneer Peeling 
The total green veneer yield ranges from 40% up to 82% with an average of 72%, 
showing lower variability compared to sawmilling (see Fig. 21.7). Similarly, techno-
logical advancements increased veneer recovery over the last decade signiﬁcantly. The chipless peeling of veneers leads to a generally higher material yield compared 
to lumber from sawmilling and similarly veneer yield increases with increasing small 
end diameter. However, this requires uniform raw material of preferably high diam-
eter and high quality. In addition, the machine conﬁguration also plays an important 
role. Currently, spindleless lathe technology is the state of the art, enabling the log 
to be peeled down to a residual diameter of about 25 mm. In comparison, older 
literature discusses suitable core diameters to be around 80 mm and above, also 
depending on the potential utilisation of the peeler cores for other products (e.g. poles for fencing) [33]. After peeling, green veneers are usually clipped and dried to 
target moisture content as depicted in Fig. 21.9. Fig. 21.9 Typical operations and by-products during veneer peeling

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
405
As depicted in Fig. 21.7 total yield of veneer sheets dried ranges from 38% up 
to 77% with an average of 57% showing higher variability (Fig. 21.3) compared to 
green veneers. As with the sawing process, there are also different ways in which the 
veneer volume is determined. The cubic volume is either measured based on actual 
dimensions (length×width× thickness) or based on the surf

ace area on an equivalent 
thickness basis [35]. For example, a veneer with 1.25 m2 and a 1 mm thickness would 
equal 0.00125 m3, including a small plus or minus thickness tolerance [33]. As both 
methods are based on actual dimensions the variability in reported veneer yield 
should be negligible. More importantly, the loss due to clipping is reported [33, 64, 
94, 95] to be the most severe factor in decreasing veneer yield. According to ﬁndings 
by Woodﬁn [94] as well as Fahey and Woodﬁn [64] roughly 20% of material is turned 
into by-products, whereby the clipped volume (mainly cracked and defective areas) 
is signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by the applied clipping strategy [96]. Final veneer drying 
further reduces the overall yield, with the target moisture content ranging from as 
low as 2% up to 15% [28]. The lower target moisture content is usually dictated 
by the target size [33] and possible bonding conditions for subsequent Laminated 
Veneer Lumber (LVL) and Plywood (PLY) production [26]. 21.2.3.3 
Inﬂuence of Wood Species on the First Breakdown 
Based on Fi g.21.7, the inﬂuence of wood type (coniferous vs. deciduous) is negligible 
for the ﬁrst roundwood breakdown. This underlines the greater inﬂuence of the 
principal technology on the level of achievable resource efﬁciency as compared to 
the processed wood species. Considering sawmilling and veneer peeling, a key factor 
in process efﬁciency [58, 97] is the optimization of mills to process either coniferous 
or deciduous roundwood. This emphasizes the importance of a plannable and steady 
supply of comparable roundwood assortments for these industries. Currently, the 
smaller share of deciduous wood species processed by the industry (see Fig. 21.5)  is  
either subjected to a stricter degree of pre-sorting compared to softwoods, with the 
non-sawable and non-peelable assortments being either used for wood fuel or pulp 
and paper, or is directly felled for these low-added value sectors [22]. Therefore, the 
attainable roundwood quality of deciduous trees will be a key issue in the future [98, 
99] and could signiﬁcantly reduce the process efﬁciency of sawmilling [71]  as  well  
as veneer peeling [97]. Furthermore, a closer look at other process-related factors 
like productivity, energy demand, necessary pre-treatment and resulting intermediary 
quality is needed to get a full view of the effect of the changing raw material situation. 21.2.4 
Typical Sizes of Intermediaries and Finished EWP 
At this point, the typical sizes of the intermediaries and ﬁnished EWPs will be 
presented brieﬂy in order to provide an overview of the achievable dimensions. To 
utilise different EWPs in construction, a certain dimensional freedom is required

406
M. Pramreiter et al. to support an array of different building types and designs, while also ensuring the 
necessary structural safety. Furthermore, the high degree of prefabrication in the 
wood-based construction industry also requires a certain ﬂexibili

ty when it comes 
to logistics. Based on currently available literature and building projects, Table 21.2 
summarizes the typical sizes of the used intermediaries (sawn timber, peeled veneers, 
strands, scrim-mats, particles and ﬁbres) and indicates the typical dimensional ranges 
for the ﬁnished EWPs. Looking at the typically beam-shaped EWPs, Glued Laminated Timber (GLT) 
currently offers the highest ﬂexibility in regards to the size of the ﬁnal members. It 
ranges from standardized dimensions up to singles beams for timber engineering, 
not only covering great spans but also 3D-shaped constructions [121, 122]. Today, 
the construction industry can access a wide knowledge-base in regards to design 
and manufacturing but also in the ﬁeld of connectors and logistics (on and off the 
construction site) when it comes to GLT. LVL, Parallel Strand Lumber (PSL) and 
Laminated Strand Lumber (LSL) on the other hand are predominantly used in stan-
dardised lengths. However, LVL recently gained interest for timber engineering due to 
the higher mechanical performance at smaller dimensions [123], while LSL and PSL 
are mainly used in North America with standardised dimensions. SCR is currently 
not produced on an industrial scale and it has been largely ignored since the last push 
to bring a product onto the market in 2003 [118]. However, the scrimber process has 
received renewed attention in recent years due to its ability to process small-diameter 
and low-quality roundwood [124]. Considering the panel-shaped EWPs, CLT is currently generating the most atten-
tion in the industry as well as research, which has signiﬁcantly increased its dimen-
sional ﬂexibility while also offering multiple degrees of pre-fabrication. PLY, OSB, 
PB and FB are still mainly used with standardized dimensions and rather as one 
component of a construction element. However, companies started producing PLY 
[111] and OSB [116] as single load bearing element with bigger dimensions and 
design ﬂexibility in the recent past. DLT and NLT are currently only manufactured 
to a limited extent with standardised dimensions [104, 105, 125]. However, they 
have a long history as structural EWP and offer an adhesive-free alternative to the 
established EWPs and are comparatively easy to manufacture and recycle. 21.2.5 
Further Processing and Finished EWPs 
After the ﬁrst and secondary roundwood breakdown the intermediaries can be either 
stored for later use or directly processed into the ﬁnished EWPs. Figure 21.10 
summarizes the values found in literature on the material yield of different EWPs. 21
