21.2.5.1 
Inﬂuence of Wood Species on Material Yield of EWPs 
Depending on the EWP (see Fig. 21.10) no or too little literature is available to 
provide a general statement regarding the inﬂuence of wood species on the ﬁnal 
material yield. Taking a look at the lumber-based products GLT and CLT, there was 
no literature on the processing losses of GLT made from deciduous species and the 
literature for CLT is primarily based on the same numerical simulation of low-grade 
yellow-poplar roundwood [76, 136] reporting a lower yield compared to conifers. In 
addition, the losses through pre-sorting and strength grading of the input lumber are 
reported to be signiﬁcantly higher for deciduous [59, 78] than for coniferous species 
[79, 80]. In regards to the veneer-based EWPs, a similar statement can be made, 
with no literature available on deciduous LVL and little literature on PLY [35, 127], 
showing values signiﬁcantly lower compared to conifers. In addition, total LVL and 
PLY includes literature [33, 55, 64, 137, 138] reporting a mixture of both coniferous 
as well as deciduous species, exhibiting a higher variability in yield compared to 
mere conifers. This indicates that the higher inhomogeneity of deciduous trees also 
inﬂuences the range of achievable material yields of LVL and PLY. In contrast to the 
lumber and veneer-based EWPs, Fig. 21.10 shows a different picture for OSB, with 
deciduous

OSB having a higher material yield compared to OSB made from conifers. 21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
409
In that regard, results from Feng and Knudson [139] show a signiﬁcantly higher share 
of ﬁnes from green pine (15.4%) compared to green aspen (4.1%). This indicates, that 
the production of deciduous OSB could result in a generally higher material yield. Nevertheless, aspen can be considered a low-density deciduous species [140] with 
technological properties similar to conifers [141]. Therefore, this statement should 
not be transferred to other deciduous species like oak or beech. In general, most of the reported values either stem from a process that was opti-
mized for the utilized tree species or are the result of an optimization to utilise a new 
species. This wide array of investigations underlines the necessity to approach poten-
tial changes in the raw material basket with an open eye in regards to the potential 
processing technologies. 21.2.5.2 
Glued Laminated Timber (GLT) and Cross Laminated 
Timber (CLT) 
Taking a look at GLT and CLT, comparable efﬁciencies can be observed, with total 
GLT yield (GLT dimensional or custom in Fig. 21.11) ranging from 7% up to 53% 
and an average of 39% and CLT (CLT master-panel in Fig. 21.11) achieving an 
average yield of 37% ranging from 7–53%. Overall, the further processing losses of 
GLT and CLT are similar within different literature sources. This is a result of both 
EWPs having similar processing steps and by-products during manufacturing (see 
Fig. 21.11). For total GLT [142–145], the average losses during production amount to about 
16% in a range of 12–18% based on timber input. In CLT production [85, 93, 145– 
150], roughly 16% of input material is transformed into by-products, ranging from
Fig. 21.11 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of GLT and CLT

410
M. Pramreiter et al. 12% up to 20%. From this, it can be argued that the main reason for the high variability 
in yield can be found in the base efﬁciency of the input timber. In both cases the main 
steps causing additional losses are lumber trimming and ﬁnger jointing, planing of 
the lamellas and joinery 1 in the case of CLT and cosmetics & sanding in the case 
of GLT [27, 102]. However, it is important to note that especially planer shavings, 
which are a big loss factor [57, 85], are probably counted double in some cases for 
GLT as well as CLT as the process may also be started with rough-sawn lumber 
[147]. For CLT, this is countered by the fact that the losses caused by the joinery 2 
(e.g. cut-outs for windows and doors) are not yet included. Depending on the end-
use of the panel this could further bring down the ﬁnal yield signiﬁcantly. While 
ﬂoor panels do not require many cut-outs, outer and inner walls require openings 
for windows or doors, with the total area of these openings mainly driven by design 
aspects as well as building physics (lighting and ventilation) [151,

152]. 21.2.5.3 
Dowel Laminated Timber (DLT) and Nail Laminated Timber 
(NLT) 
Currently, no publicly available literature deals with the resource efﬁciency of DLT 
or NLT. Both products are manufactured from dimensional lumber and instead of an 
adhesive either wooden dowels (DLT) or nails (NLT) are used to form the ﬁnished 
EWP (see Fig. 21.4). Depending on the producer the typical processing operations 
(Fig. 21.12) start, similar to GLT and CLT, with dried sawn timber which is in some 
cases ﬁnger jointed before a planing and proﬁling step to create the ﬁnal raw lamella. Therefore, similar by-products to GLT and CLT occur. In the case of DLT, the lamellas 
are then laid up to either a beam or a panel (similar to CLT) and holes are drilled for 
subsequent dowel insertion. This creates further wood shavings as a by-product. In 
the case of NLT, the lamellas are directly laid up and nailed together in a repeating 
pattern. The resulting beam or panel is then further processed in a joiner. However, in 
the case of NLT, the options for subsequent joining are limited due to the nails within 
the material. The ﬁnal EWP can also move through a ﬁnal cosmetics and sanding 
step before being either stored or sold directly. Based on these similarities with GLT and CLT it can be assumed that the overall 
material yield of DLT and NLT also falls in the same range. More speciﬁcally, 
the material yield tends to be higher compared to CLT and GLT due to the lower 
requirements on the surface quality of the lamellae. Another potential advantage of 
DLT and NLT lies in the potential recyclability of beams and panels at the end of 
their life-cycle due the absence of hard to dissolve adhesive bonds. 21.2.5.4 
Laminated Veneer Lumber (LVL) and Plywood (PLY) 
Taking a look at the veneer-based products, LVL and PLY achieve higher average 
yields compared to the lumber-based EWPs. Total LVL yield ranges from 27 to 72% 
with an average of 52% and an average further processing loss of 9% [137, 153,

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
411
Fig. 21.12 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of DLT and NLT
154]. Total PLY typically achieves an average yield of 52%, in the range of 33–62% 
according to literature. As depicted in Fig. 21.13, the losses from the input green 
veneers are caused by waste veneer, lay-up scrap, panel trimming and sanding as 
well as material testing [33, 64, 137, 138, 153], with improved material utilization 
of roughly 15% compared to the lumber-based EWPs. The higher maximum yield 
of LVL compared to PLY could be explained by fewer cross-cuts and fewer surfaces 
for sanding as usually thicker billets are produced [155]. Fig. 21.13 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of LVL and PLY

412
M. Pramreiter et al. Fig. 21.14 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of PSL 
21.2.5.5 
Parallel Strand Lumber (PSL) 
PSL reaches an average material yield of 64%, ranging fro

m 62 to 65%. Within 
the group of veneer-based products, it can be seen as a special case of EWP. In 
comparison to LVL and PLY, the production of PSL includes a secondary breakdown 
of the veneers into veneer clips (see Fig. 21.14). This enables a higher material yield 
compared to LVL and PLY, as veneer pieces that are not a full-width veneer sheet 
(roundups, ﬁshtail, etc.) can be utilized [156]. The resulting clips are continuously 
feed into a hot press to form an endless beam, which is then formatted to the ﬁnal 
dimensions. 21.2.5.6 
Laminated Strand Lumber (LSL) 
The material yield of LSL ranges from 55% up to 76%, with an average of 69%. The lower yield compared to OSB (see next section) is due to the unidirectional 
structure over the entire thickness of the material, demanding generally bigger (see 
Sect. 21.2.4) and more uniform strands, with high aspect ratios [156]. Therefore, part 
of the ﬁner fractions which are usually put in the middle layer of OSB cannot be used 
for the homogeneous build-up of LSL beams. Besides these differences, the main 
drivers for additional material losses (see Fig. 21.15) are formatting and sanding of 
the ﬁnished panels and beams [27, 138, 157]. In general, the log stranding process does not require high-quality, mature logs 
and is geared towards small-diameter roundwood of lower quality [27]. Therefore, 
the process allows for a bigger range in roundwood quality and supports more log 
diameters and shapes. The factors inﬂuencing strand yield partly differ from the 
previous two ﬁrst disintegration processes. Increasing log moisture content [139] 
and ambient temperature [158] signiﬁcantly decreases the produced ﬁnes in stranding 
and therefore increases strand yield. In regards to machine set-up, the knife angle is 
reported [158] to be an important factor inﬂuencing the share of ﬁnes. In addition,

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
413
Fig. 21.15 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of LSL
the positioning of the logs against the rotating knife using of clamps or pushers partly 
dictates the strand geometry and therefore also inﬂuences the strand yield [28]. The 
ﬁnes are normally separated from production and are typically used for the provision 
of energy as they reduce the strength of the ﬁnal board and increase the amount of 
adhesive required. Furthermore, strands that do not fulﬁl the required sizes are also 
used for energy, but can also be used for PB. The ﬁnal target moisture content of the strands is in the range of 2–5% [27, 28] 
usually dried from above ﬁbre saturation [159]. As mentioned previously, this could 
further affect the ﬁnal material yield depending on the reported input and output 
unit. This is especially critical when comparing the different processes, as volumetric 
shrinkage is usually considered a loss in volume-based lumber and veneer production. However, as LSL and OSB production is commonly monitored using (dry) weight, 
this

could lead to inaccuracies when comparing the processes. 21.2.5.7 
Oriented Strand Board (OSB) 
The average material yield of OSB is 83% and ranges from 66 to 96%. Similar to 
LSL, the main drivers for by-products besides the ﬁnes from stranding industrial 
roundwood are formatting and sanding of the ﬁnished panels (see Fig. 21.16). OSB 
is typically made of three layers, two unidirectionally oriented surface layers and 
one randomly or perpendicularly oriented middle layer. This was inspired by the 
orientation of plywood and combines two stiffness-providing surface layers and a

414
M. Pramreiter et al. Fig. 21.16 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of OSB 
middle layer which gives additional dimensional stability while also improving shear 
stiffness. This allows for a bigger range of strand sizes to be used in the production 
compared to LSL, which explains the higher resource efﬁciency compared to LSL. 21.2.5.8 
Scrimber (SCR) 
The average material yield of SCR is 86%, ranging from 84 to 90%. These values 
are partly based on the company that originally produced SCR under the product 
name TimTek® [160]. Based on the available literature, by-products occur during, 
the formatting and ﬁnishing of the ﬁnal panels (see Fig. 21.17). Additionally, it 
should be mentioned that it can be assumed that there is a certain share of unusable 
intermediaries (ﬁnes after scrimming and after drying). However, this is not discussed 
in the available literature. As of 2024, SCR is the only EWP discussed here that is not produced on an indus-
trial scale. Nevertheless, the process of splitting small-diameter logs has recently 
gained renewed interest and should therefore also be mentioned here. Especially as 
it could be one viable way to process the potentially increasing share of non-sawable 
and- peelable assortments in the future. Furthermore, it could also be used to add 
value to currently undervalued assortments like branches and crown tops, which were 
recently found to offer similar technological potential to stem wood [161]. 21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
415
Fig. 21.17 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of SCR
21.2.5.9 
Particle Board (PB) 
In comparison to the other processes, the production of PB can be seen as having 
the most ﬂexible input raw material and offers a common utilisation strategy for 
by-products from sawmilling but also from stranding and possibly also from veneer 
peeling. The average material yield for PB is 76% and ranges from 49% up to 87%. The lower end of this spectrum should be taken with a grain of salt as this low 
value of material yield is not typical for an industrial-scale facility. However, the 
reported numbers stem from a facility which utilizes mainly low-quality raw mate-
rials. According to the study [129], the raw material includes branches and wood 
from garden pruning, poplar and eucalyptus roundwood, other low-quality deciduous 
roundw

ood, forest ﬁrewood and industrial by-products, underlining the raw material 
ﬂexibility of this process. Overall, the production of PB leads to by-products during 
screening and ﬁnishing of the ﬁnal panel (see Fig. 21.18). 21.2.5.10 
Fibre Board (FB) 
Completing this comparison of different EWPs, the average material yield for FB 
is 89%, ranging from 75% up to 98%. This also represents the highest poten-
tially achievable resource efﬁciency of the presented products. However, this, in 
turn, requires the highest degree of disintegration of roundwood in order to obtain 
single wood ﬁbres or ﬁbre-bundles as intermediaries. This is typically achieved by 
combining two major breakdown processes, chipping of roundwood and subsequent 
reﬁning of the obtained chips into ﬁbres (see Fig. 21.19). While this has the potential to yield a 100% useable intermediary material for 
FB production, it also requires the highest total primary energy input of all the 
processes. With this, the environmental performance, typically evaluated by Life 
Cycle Assessment (LCA), as well as the mechanical performance needs to be brought 
into the comparison, as resource efﬁciency alone does not provide the complete

416
M. Pramreiter et al. Fig. 21.18 Typical operations and by-products during the manufacturing of PB
picture in regards to sustainability of wood-based building materials in a broader 
sense. 21.3 Mechanical Performance 
The architectural freedom [162] and design phase [163] of a building demands 
a certain mechanical capacity of the suitable construction materials [164]. There-
fore, the potential mechanical performance of the different EWPs is another impor-
tant aspect. In addition to the strength, stiffness and long-term behaviour already 
discussed in Pramreiter et al. [24], the speciﬁc modulus of elasticity (spec. E0,mean) 
[GPa/kg/m3] in the main load direction is compared with the material yield in 
Fig. 21.20. It should become clear that the lower material yield of the lumber-(GLT, CLT, 
DLT and NLT) and veneer-based (LVL, PLY and PSL) EWPs, can be balanced by the 
higher attainable stiffness per kg EWP in contrast to the particle-based products [165, 
166]. However, this only covers the serviceability side and one load case (bending) of

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …

M. Pramreiter et al. the design process. Therefore, it is highly recommended to also consider section four 
in Pramreiter et al. [24] for a more complete picture of the mechanical performance. 21.4 Environmental Performance 
The evaluation of the environmental performance of materials and products is a 
very complex topic including different possible approaches and indicators [167]. Therefore, the following LCA indicators [168, 169] (funct

ional unit is “1 m3 of 
the respective EWP)” were selected to provide an overview of the environmental 
performance of the established EWPs: 
• Released carbon 
• Stored carbon 
• Net carbon storage potential (difference between “Stored carbon” and “Released 
carbon”) 
• Global warming potential (GWP) 
• Total primary energy. 21.4.1 
Selected LCA Indicators 
Considering remarks by Oneil et al. [170] it needs to be mentioned that the presented 
values in this section are subject to a certain degree of uncertainty for two main 
reasons. First, the values include two allocation principles (economic and mass allo-
cation), with economic allocation usually leading to a higher environmental impact of 
the higher-value product [170]. This signiﬁcantly inﬂuences the outcome, as market 
prices of the main and co-products can ﬂuctuate greatly over time. Second, the number 
of values found in the literature varied greatly between the different products. In the 
case of NLT, PSL and SCR no literature could be found and for DLT only values from 
one environmental product declaration (EPD) are currently available [171]. A similar 
issue is present in regards to LSL, with only one available publication [157]. This 
is especially critical when different production regions (e.g. transport distances and 
energy mixes) and actuality of the data (e.g. age of the facility) are considered. This 
underlines the need to study the environmental performance of EWPs in more detail, 
broadening the investigated regions and raw materials. Furthermore, the underlying 
LCAs need to be kept up to date much more regularly, as factors like environmental 
regulations, energy provision and efﬁciency, data quality and availability are subject 
to more frequent changes than, for example, mechanical performance and can signif-
icantly change the outcome of the assessment. Nevertheless, Table 21.4 summarizes 
the values found in the literature for the discussed products. Overall, one m3 of EWP (cradle-to-gate) offers a net carbon storage potential 
of 705 kg CO2 equiv. (192 kg C), by temporarily storing 1019 kg CO2 equiv. (277 kg C) and releasing 314 kg CO2 equiv. (85 kg C) into the air during production

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
419
(including provision of raw materials). These numbers are in line with assumptions 
made by Churkina et al. [1], which underlines the potential of EWPs to decarbonise 
the building sector. More speciﬁcally, PB (889 kg CO2 equiv.), GLT (827 kg CO2 
equiv.) and DLT (823 kg CO2 equiv.) offer the highest potential of the available 
EWPs, followed by LVL (781 kg CO2 equiv.), LSL (751 kg CO2 equiv.) and CLT 
(739 kg CO2 equiv.). FB, OSB and PLY complete the available literature with 671 kg 
CO2 equiv., 576 kg CO2 equiv. and 533 kg CO2 equiv. respectively. Currently, there 
are no values available for PSL and SCR. For material selection, the material with 
the highest net carbon storage potential should be selected. In regards to GWP

, the established EWPs, except for FB, show a rather homoge-
nous GWP with an average of 232 kg CO2 equiv. per m3 material. According to the 
available literature [131, 172], the high GWP of FB (487 kg CO2 equiv.) is caused 
during the manufacturing stage (A3). They don’t give a speciﬁc cause for this in the 
discussion, but it is safe to say that the heat required for the drying and (hot) pressing 
steps as well as the necessary adhesive play a key role. This is also supported by the 
total primary energy demand (see Table 21.4). While this value may seem very high, 
it corresponds to the general trend that the GWP increases with decreasing size of 
the intermediary. In contrary to net carbon storage potential, the goal ought to be 
to choose the EWP with the lowest GWP [kg CO2 equiv.]. It should be noted here 
that GWP is one of the most discussed and accepted indicators in LCA, yet for a 
full picture, especially when materials from different processes are compared, all the 
evaluated LCA indicators need to be considered. A closer look at the total primary energy demand [MJ] in Table 21.4 shows a 
signiﬁcant difference on two levels. First, the provision of raw material has a signif-
icant inﬂuence on the reported total primary energy. On average, the raw material 
supply and transport stages, in LCA are typically referred to as A1 and A2 [168, 
169], cause 49% (4037 MJ of 8178 MJ) of the total primary energy demand in 
EWPs. For the lumber-based products (GLT, CLT and DLT), these stages represent 
66% (3320 MJ of 4997 MJ) of the total primary energy demand. In the veneer-
(LVL and PLY) and strand-based products (OSB), they constitute 51% (3885 MJ of 
7577 MJ) and 45% (3614 MJ of 8114 MJ) respectively and they account for 50% 
(3585 MJ of 7118 MJ) in PB and 28% (5540 MJ of 20,043 MJ) in FB. Second, the 
total primary energy demand for the manufacturing stage, in LCA typically referred 
to as A3, signiﬁcantly increases with decreasing size of the intermediary. This is a 
well-known correlation in wood technology [25] and can be explained by the fact 
that the disintegration of roundwood into ever smaller intermediaries and the subse-
quent re-assembly to composites typically causes greater expenditure of energy and 
requires more energy-intensive auxiliary materials (e.g. synthetic adhesives and hot 
pressing instead of pressing at room temperature). It also explains the decreasing 
share of total primary energy demand for the provision of raw materials. 420
M. Pramreiter et al. 21.4.2 
Speciﬁc Environmental Performance 
Considering the typical functional unit of “1 m3 EWP” and the different densities of 
the EWPs, a comparison based on a speciﬁc environmental performance similar to 
the speciﬁc mechanical performance is also of interest. Therefore, Fig. 21.21 shows 
the speciﬁc net carbon storage potential (Fig. 21.21a), speciﬁc GWP (Fig. 21.21b) 
and speciﬁc total primary energy demand (Fig. 21.21c) in relation to the respective 
material yie

ld from Sect. 21.2. It needs to be mentioned that the density was taken 
from Table 21.3 instead of the respective LCA literature. This was done due to the 
fact, that not all references on LCA had a clear description of the corresponding 
density of the ﬁnal product. Taking a look at Fig. 21.21, an inﬂuence of the intermediary size is still visible, 
with smaller intermediaries leading to a higher environmental impact. LSL and FB 
seem to also have a particularly high total primary energy demand compared to the 
other EWPs. In regards to LSL, this high demand is caused by the log storage in 
ponds to heat the logs before processing and the subsequent stranding as well as 
the drying process. Furthermore, the typical panel thickness of 75 mm and above 
requires the use of a steam injection press in order to maintain suitable cycle times. However, as noted earlier this is only based on one publication [157]. Considering 
FB, the high energy demand is caused by the reﬁning and drying process, which 
requires signiﬁcant amounts of energy [126, 131]. 21.4.3 
A Three-Dimensional Comparison of Established 
EWPs 
Together with the previous comparisons, it should become even clearer now that 
there is no such thing as one ideal EWP but rather a balance of factors that needs to 
be achieved. Therefore, the end of this section presents a three-dimensional compar-
ison of established EWPs (see Fig. 21.22). This comparison serves as blueprint for a 
material selection process which incorporates the discussed material yield, mechan-
ical performance as well as net carbon storage potential. For future investigations it 
should be extended with other mechanical properties, LCA indicators as well as not 
yet discussed indicators (e.g. production costs or recyclability) in mind. Especially, 
aspects like recyclability and different cost factors might further support a more 
balanced material selection. While it might be difﬁcult to discuss the cost factor 
using reliable values from industry (not commonly accessible), the aspect of recy-
clability or circularity has gained signiﬁcant interest in recent years. Therefore, the 
last section of this chapter will give a brief comment on the circularity aspects of the 
discussed EWPs. 21

21.5 A Comment on Circularity 
Circularity in engineering and architecture has become an important consideration 
for sustainable design in recent years [173]. Beyond the initial embodied emissions 
in new products, an increased life span and future reuse of building materials and 
components can greatly increase the carbon and emission efﬁciency of new prod-
ucts [174]. Building designs or material systems conceived for an end-of-life scenario 
with planned deconstruction or reuse (DfD—Design for Deconstruction) have greater 
chances of being reused or recycled in the future. The selection of EWPs for a new

424
M. Pramreiter et al. building design has a large inﬂuence on the future resource, process and environ-
mental efﬁciency of their reuse or recycling, as they all have different potentiasl for 
reuse of the raw materials they are made from [175]. Ideally, a building is designed for a longer life cycle than typically provided by 
building codes (50–100 years) in order to extend the carbon storage and best utilize 
the spent carbon and emissions for the construction of the building. Next, adaptive 
reuse of the building can further extend its life cycle, by bringing upgrades that 
can serve its new performance requirements (su

ch as structural integrity or thermal 
performance) and accommodate for the new use of the space. If the building has to be 
taken down, building materials or components can be reused or recycled to feed into 
new buildings. Otherwise, the product’s intermediary can be reused in new EWPs as 
salvaged intermediary (e.g. salvaged lumber). As shown in the previous sections, the selection of suitable EWPs balances a few 
parameters, such as structural versus environmental performance and material yield. Circularity and reuse of EWPs are currently less quantiﬁable and hard to include 
in the analysis but predictions are emerging from current research and references 
from the past [176, 177]. EWPs made with current adhesive technology might have 
a lower potential for efﬁcient reuse of the intermediary when compared to EWPs 
assembled with mechanical fasteners like DLT or NLT. Denailing NLT panels or 
removing dowels from DLT panels allows for a simple recovery of the product’s 
lumber. Glued products, if not reused as a whole component, will need to be resawn 
and resurfaced for the production of new EWPs, bringing them back into the same 
process ﬂow with associated processing losses. Furthermore, information about the 
durability of the performance of glue over many building life cycles has not been 
demonstrated. Historical buildings built out of wood demonstrates the durability of 
the wood ﬁber itself, as a long-term structural material, like the Hōryū-ji temple in 
Nara, Japan that has been in use for the last 1300 years. These predictions don’t consider future developments in adhesives and disas-
sembly technologies. However, given the increase appeal for wood-based construc-
tion such as mass timber around the world in its current technological state, these 
considerations should be weighted in to increase the long-term resources efﬁciency 
of EWPs. 21.6 Concluding Remarks 
The widespread application of Engineered Wood Products (EWPs) for not only small 
residential but also medium to high rise buildings will be key in order to decarbonize 
the global construction sector. The recent completion of ever higher building projects 
(see Fig. 21.23) utilizing selected EWPs as one of the main structural components has 
already shown the advantages of wood-based products along with existing materials 
like steel and concrete. However, the potential demand for built environments in the future could put 
increasing pressure on the world’s forests and may jeopardise the sustainable supply

21
Engineered Wood Products (EWP)—Resource Efﬁciency …
425
Fig. 21.23 Past, present and potential future importance of common material categories used in 
different disciplines of engineering (adapted with permission from [7], copyright by Elsevier). *Engineered Wood Products as structural components
of sufﬁcient raw materials. Therefore, the construction industry needs to consider the 
full variety of EWPs and incorporate aspects like resource efﬁciency, environmental

and mechanical performance as well as circularity into their decision process. This 
chapter summarized this information and shined a light on the currently occurring 
by-products during production and provided base-values for material selection, not 
only in construction, but also for other potential applications in the future. It is 
important to emphasise here that the comparisons should not be seen as a ranking, 
but rather a demonstration of a potential material-ﬁrst approach to wood as a raw 
material and how the by-products of one EWP can be and partly already are the 
raw material for another. While there are certain aspects of the wood-based value 
chain that need critical discussion and further research, the authors ﬁrmly believe 
that the shift towards a climate-resilient society requires the sustainably harvested 
resources of our forests, which are then carefully handled and utilized in long-lasting 
products. After a century characterised by mainly non-renewable building materials 
(see Fig. 21.23), the twenty-ﬁrst century seems to mark the beginning of the age of 
wood-based building products. Acknowledgements The results presented in this chapter are part of the research project

Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity 
of Bio-Based Insulation Materials: 
An Overview for the Construction Sector 
Rosa Agliata, Nouman Khadim, and Elena Lucchi 
Abstract The growing interest in low-energy and sustainable architecture has driven 
a demand for buildings with minimized environmental footprints. Within this context, 
building insulation materials serve as key players in curbing operational energy usage 
within the construction sector. However, their production and use have been identiﬁed 
as signiﬁcant contributors to carbon emissions and overall environmental impacts. Notably, the embodied carbon in insulation products varies signiﬁcantly, ranging 
from materials derived from plastics (e.g., EPS, XPS) or mineral ﬁbers (e.g., glass 
wool, rockwool) to those based on biological components. Remarkably, the latter 
often exhibit a carbon-negative balance, presenting a promising solution in the quest 
for sustainable construction materials. Despite this inherent advantage, the global 
utilization of bio-based insulating materials still hovers below 10%. Wood ﬁber mate-
rials account for nearly 60% of this usage, followed by cellulose ﬁbers constituting 
approximately 30%, leaving the remaining 10% to encompass all other bio-based 
insulation products. This chapter aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of the 
characteristics of both traditional and innovative bio-based insulation materials to 
understand: (i) how bio-based insulation materials behave in terms of circularity and 
sustainability; (ii) what CE–LCA considerations are essential for material selection; 
and (iii) how to make a trade-off between circularity, LCA and physical properties 
(energy use, insulation, acoustics). R. Agliata (B) 
Dipartimento di Ingegneria, Università della Campania “Vanvitelli”, Aversa (CE), Italy 
e-mail: rosa.agliata@unicampania.it 
N. Khadim 
Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands 
e-mail: n.khadim@vu.nl 
E. Lucchi 
Università di Pavia, Pavia, Italy

R. Agliata et al. 22.1 
Introduction 
Thermal insulation materials play a crucial role in enhancing the sustainability 
of buildings. By improving energy efﬁciency, reducing environmental impact, and 
promoting healthier indoor environments, these materials contribute signiﬁcantly to 
sustainable building and energy efﬁciency practices. They reduce the heat transfer 
through building envelopes (e.g., walls, roofs, and ﬂoors), lowering the energy 
consumption for heating and cooling. Besides, they maintain stable indoor temper-
atures, enhancing occupant comfort and reducing thermal ﬂuctuations. Also, proper 
insulation reduces the risk of condensation of building structures, preventing mold 
growth and associated health issues. Many insulation materials also offer sound-
prooﬁng beneﬁts for reducing noise pollution from outside and between indoor 
environments. However, their production and use have been identiﬁed as signiﬁ-
cant contributors to carbon emissions and overall environmental impacts. Manu-
facturing insulation typically involves energy-intensive processes, which often rely 
on fossil fuels, leading to substantial greenhouse gas emissions. The extraction and 
processing of raw materials, such as petrochemicals for foam insulation or minerals 
for ﬁberglass, also have environmental repercussions, including habitat destruction 
and resource depletion. Furthermore, some insulation materials, like foamed plastics, 
release potent greenhouse gases during production and disposal. This dual nature of insulation underscores the importance of sustainable produc-
tion practices and the selection of eco-friendly materials to minimize negative impacts 
while maximizing energy savings. Adopting a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)-based 
approach for the selection of the most appropriate insulation materials can help 
highlighting the carbon footprint of the latter throughout their life stages, including 
production, use, and disposal phases. Besides, focusing on the circularity aspect of 
the material and the component, permits to identify opportunities for recycling and 
reusing materials, reducing waste, and conserving resources. This philosophy encour-
ages the development of insulation products designed for longevity and end-of-life 
recovery, ultimately supporting a more sustainable and resilient built environment. A thermal insulating material is characterized by the reduced ability to conduct 
heat ﬂows. This property is quantiﬁed for homogeneous materials by “thermal 
conductivity”  (λ), which represents the material’s intrinsic ability to conduct heat. For non-homogeneous materials, “thermal conductance” (C) is used, which accounts 
for the material’s overall capacity to transmit heat, considering its composite nature. Conventionally, thermal insulation materials have a λ value below

0.065 W/mK. They 
can be broadly classiﬁed into organic and inorganic, based on their chemical compo-
sition and origin. Inorganic insulation materials do not contain carbon-hydrogen 
bonds and are composed of mineral or metallic elements. They can be divided into 
mineral-based (e.g., rockwool, ﬁberglass, slag wool, cellular glass), and aerogel (e.g., 
silica and polymer aerogel) [1]. Conversely, 
organic 
materials 
are 
primarily 
composed 
of 
carbon-based 
compounds. They can be further divided into petrochemical-derived and bio-based 
materials. Synthetic materials are derived from petroleum or natural gas through

22
Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity of Bio-Based Insulation …
435
chemical processes and are usually in the form of polymers. Examples are expanded 
polystyrene (EPS), extruded polystyrene (XPS), polyurethane (PUR), phenolic foam, 
polyisocyanurate foam (PIR). According to the EU Commission (2020), bio-based 
materials «[…] are fully or partially made from biological resources, rather than 
fossil raw materials»  [2]. Bio-based materials are derived from renewable substances 
of natural origin, either vegetable or animal [3]. These products offer sustainable 
alternatives to traditional, petroleum-based items across a wide range of industries, 
including packaging, agriculture, construction, personal care, and textiles [4]. They 
are often considered more sustainable and environmentally friendly compared to 
other categories. Below, the chemical composition, manufacturing processes, and 
performance of these materials are described to better understand their applicability 
in buildings. 22.2 
Bio-Based Thermal Insulation Materials 
Bio-based thermal insulation materials are fully or partially made from substances 
derived from living organisms and biological resources [4]. These materials can be 
broadly classiﬁed into categories based on their origin and processing methods:
• Plant-based materials that derived from crops like cork, wood, natural textile ﬁbers 
(e.g., hemp, kenaf, coconut, ﬂax, corn, jute, etc.), and cellulose ﬁber. • Animal-based materials that sourced from animal products such as sheep wool, 
horse mane, and feathers. Most bio-based insulation materials are ﬁbrous due to the natural ﬁber content in 
plants or animal wool. Historically, some of these materials were the most widely used 
in the construction sector. Recently, there has been a renewed interest in these mate-
rials to develop more adaptable and sustainable products. In general, their production 
and installation processes are not harmful to the environment or human health [5]. Furthermore, they are typically recyclable and biodegradable, which signiﬁcantly 
contributes to sustainable construction practices. These properties help reduce the 
carbon footprint of buildings and support the circular economy by enabling the reuse 
of materials. Additionally, their natural composition often results in lower emissions 
of volatile organic compounds (V

OCs), improving indoor air quality and promoting 
healthier living environments. Below, the properties of the various bio-based insulation materials are described, 
divided into:
• Plant-based materials: 
– Cork (Sect. 22.2.1). – Wood ﬁber (Sect. 22.2.2). – Cellulose ﬁber (Sect. 22.2.3). – Natural textile ﬁbers (Sect. 22.2.4). 436
R. Agliata et al. • Animal-based materials: 
– Sheep wool (Sect. 22.2.5). 22.2.1 
Cork 
Cork is a vegetable-based material composed primarily of suberin, a hydrophobic 
substance, along with lignin, polysaccharides, and various extractives. It is harvested 
from the bark of cork oak trees, processed into granules, and then compressed to 
form blocks or sheets without the need for synthetic adhesives. Thermal insulation 
materials are divided into:
• Natural (or pure) cork. • Expanded cork. Natural cork (Fig. 22.1) is obtained from the processing of the bark of the cork 
oak, which grows in a Mediterranean climate. The bark can be harvested once every 
ten years, respecting the natural reproduction cycles of the plant. The bark is removed 
from the tree, stripped of its outermost layer, and then crushed. This yields a product 
in coarse granules, which are ground and left to age for approximately one year. Subsequently, they are boiled to soften and disinfect them from any parasites. The 
thickness of natural cork insulation panels is typically between 0.2 and 0.10 m. It is 
available on the market as panels, strips, loose material, and sheets. Natural cork is 
distinguished by its discreet insulation properties, excellent thermal storage perfor-
mance, hygiene, impermeability, durability, non-combustibility, and recyclability 
[5]. Expanded cork is obtained by expanding cork granules in an autoclave with high-
temperature steam (350 °C). The heat causes a 20–30% increase in volume and results 
in the release of resin (suberin), which serves as the natural binder of the conglom-
erate. Subsequently, the material is subjected to compression, thereby enhancing its 
compactness. In addition to its thermal and acoustic insulation properties, expanded 
cork has high resistance to chemical and biological agents, is elastic, mold-resistant, 
rot-resistant, and stable over time. It is a breathable material, permeable to vapor, 
hydrophobic, and waterproof. Fig. 22.1 a Natural cork; b expanded cork. Photo Authors

437
The characteristics of the material are reported considering composition, fabri-
cation process, building applications, performance of density (ρ), λ-value, speciﬁc 
heat capacity (c), and moisture vapor transmission rate (μ) (Table 22.1). 22.2.2 
Wood Fiber 
Wood ﬁber is composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, with small amounts 
of extractives and minerals. It is produced by breaking down wood into ﬁbers through 
mechanical or chemical processes, then pressing into mats or boards with the help 
of natural or synthetic binders. Thermal ins

ulation ﬁbers are divided into:
• Wood ﬁber, classiﬁed according to their density in rigid and ﬂexible. • Mineralized wood panels. Wood ﬁber derives from the processing waste of resinous wood from sawmills. The waste is then subjected to a series of thermomechanical processes, including 
cutting, grinding and ﬁbrillation. Thermal insulating wood ﬁbers (Fig. 22.2a) are 
categorized as rigid or ﬂexible, depending on their wood density. In both cases, 
wood is combined with water and additives (e.g., aluminum sulfate, boron salts) 
to enhance ﬁre resistance and protection from pests, insects, and mold. Otherwise, 
wood could be treated with hydrophobic solutions (e.g., bitumen, latex, or beeswax) 
to improve impermeability and water resistance. The high speciﬁc heat of wood 
ensures high thermal capacity and performance. The ﬁbrous nature of wood provides 
good acoustic resistance, with sound absorption values dependent on ﬁber size. The 
open-pore structure allows the passage of water vapor, and breathability. The material 
possesses good mechanical resistance, dimensional stability, and damage resistance. However, it is susceptible to attacks from rodents, insects, parasites, and ﬁre [6]. Mineralized wood panels (Fig. 22.2b) are manufactured using timber that has been 
cultivated rapidly and waste materials derived from the wood processing industry. The shredded ﬁbers are impregnated with magnesite, Portland cement, or other 
mineral binders, which renders them compact and rigid, more resistant to biolog-
ical attack and ﬁre. It has high mechanical, chemical, biological, and ﬁre resistance 
performance, dimensional stability, and durability. Thermal performance is reduce 
compared to wood ﬁber, but it has similar thermal capacity, and higher damping 
of temperature ﬂuctuations. The material’s acoustic performance is ensured by the 
presence of voids. The characteristics of the material are reported below (Table 22.2). 22.2.3 
Cellulose Fiber 
Cellulose ﬁber (Fig. 22.3) primarily consists of cellulose, a complex carbohydrate or 
polysaccharide. It derives from the processing of newspapers and magazines, which 
are ground and ﬁbrillated to facilitate recycling. 438

The ﬂakes are subjected to boron salts to enhance ﬁre and biological resistance. The material is breathable, hygroscopic, and durable, as well as has elastic properties, 
minimal deformation, and high resistance to compression and biological agents. Its 
hygroscopic properties permit it to absorb more than 15% of its weight in water vapor. Nevertheless, prolonged contact with water results in the deterioration of cellulose, 
leading to rot and degradation. Thermal and acoustic performance are high thanks 
to the presence of air and ﬁbers oriented in all directions. Furthermore, it has good 
thermal phase shift and attenuation characteristics. Nevertheless, its ﬁre resistance 
is diminished, necessitating the application of ﬁre-retardant surface ﬁnishes. The 
material is available in bulk form or as semi-rigid panels, which are produced by 
weaving cellulose with polyester or jute ﬁbers. Rigid panels are created through the 
hot processing of cellulose with binders and support ﬁbers. The characteristics of the 
material are reported below (Table 22.3). 22.2.4 
Natural Textile Fibers 
Natural textile ﬁbers are a complex of products cropped-by secondary materials left 
after the primary agricultural products that are processed into insulation materials. These ﬁbers are harvested, cleaned, and processed into ﬁbers through mechanical 
means, and then spun and formed into insulation batts or loose-ﬁll. Thermal insulation 
ﬁbers are divided into:
• Hemp ﬁber
• Kenaf ﬁber
• Flax (or linen) ﬁber
• Coconut ﬁber
• Corn ﬁber
• Jute ﬁber
• Rice ﬁber
• Mixed textiles ﬁber. Hemp ﬁber (Fig. 22.

4a) derives from the processing of the hemp plant, which is 
cultivated in temperate climates to produce oilseeds, textiles, and fodder. The ﬁber

is subjected to chemical treatments to improve the ﬁre resistance, and addition of 
adhesive polyester ﬁbers, and potato starch to enhance strength and cohesion of 
felts and panels. The material has good thermal and acoustic insulation, thermal 
inertia, breathability, hygroscopicity, and resistance to mold and biological agents 
[7]. Nevertheless, it has reduced compressive strength especially for wall or ceiling 
applications that requires reinforcement systems to guarantee mechanical stability 
[5]. Like hemp, Kenaf ﬁber (Fig. 22.4b) derives from the processing of a speciﬁc plant 
belonging to the hemp family, cultivated in temperate climates to produce sacks. It has 
good thermal and acoustic insulation properties, high thermal inertia, breathability, 
hygroscopicity, mechanical strength, dimensional stability and resistance to mold and 
other biological agents. Additional additives are required to enhance its ﬁre-resistant 
properties. Flax ﬁber (Fig. 22.4c) derives from the processing of a plant from the Linaceae 
family that is cultivated to produce oilseeds and textiles. Insulating materials are 
produced from ﬁbers discarded by the textile industry with the addition of polyester 
ﬁbers to e

nhance their strength. The material is low degradable, resistant to biological 
attack, stable, and durable. Additional additives are required to improve ﬁre resis-
tance. It has good hygroscopic properties, although it becomes susceptible to decay 
with prolonged contact with water. Furthermore, it has good thermal and acoustic 
insulation properties, as well as thermal inertia [8]. Coconut ﬁber (Fig. 22.4d) is obtained from the fruit of the coconut palm, cultivated 
in tropical climates to produce textile ﬁbers for furnishings and mats. It has good dura-
bility, mechanical resistance to compression, friction, and biological attack, thermal 
and acoustic performance, breathability, and thermal inertia. It is ﬁre-sensitive but 
burns very slowly. Corn ﬁber derives from the processing of maize kernels. It has good thermal and 
acoustic performance, breathability, biodegradability as well as low toxic emissions, 
and smoke emission during combustion. 442

Jute ﬁber is obtained from the processing of jute, a plant cultivated in South-

breathability, acoustic performances, electrostatically neutrality, and recyclability 
[6]. Rice husk insulation is produced from the agricultural waste of rice hulls. It

to prevent degradation [8]. The characteristics of these materials are reported below 
(Table 22.4). 22.2.5 
Sheep Wool 
Sheep wool (Fig. 22.5) consists mainly of keratin, a ﬁbrous structural protein, with 
minor amounts of lipids and other organic materials. It is obtained from the shearing 
of the animal’s ﬂeece that is treated with natural soaps and sodium carbonate to 
remove lanolin and impurities, and then carded into ﬁbers. These ﬁbers are treated with non-toxic agents, such as insecticides or boron salts, 
to enhance ﬁre resistance and prevent pest infestation and

moth, before being formed 
into insulation batts or rolls. Furthermore. the addition of hemp and polyester ﬁbers 
can enhance the rigidity of the material. Sheep wool has medium thermal and acoustic 
performances, thermal capacity, hygroscopicity, and durability. although it can be 
susceptible to attacks by moths and parasites. The characteristics of the material are 
reported below (Table 22.5). 444

22.3 
Environmental Impacts and Carbon Footprints 
of Insulation Materials 
Environmental impact means «[…] any change to the environment wholly or partially 
resulting from a product during its life cycle»  [8] while Life cycle means «[…] 
the consecutive and interlinked stages of a product from raw material use to ﬁnal 
disposal»  [9]. 22.3.1 
Cork 
Cork is derived from renewable materials, speciﬁcally the bark of cork oak trees, 
which are limited in availability and conﬁned to speciﬁc geographic regions. Addi-
tionally, cork trees have very long regeneration times, requiring several years for 
the bark to be harvested sustainably. Despite these constraints, the production and 
processing phases of cork have relatively low environmental impacts. The primary 
environmental concern in its life cycle is related to the transportation phase, which can 
generate signiﬁcant emissions due to the distances involved in moving the raw and 
ﬁnished products. The use of synthetic adhesives may result in the emission of VOCs. Upon reaching the end of its useful life, the material can be reused or composted 
by simple grinding of the ﬁnished panels. The use of synthetic adhesives in cork 
products can lead to the emission of VOCs, which may affect indoor air quality. However, advancements in eco-friendly adhesives are helping to mitigate this issue. At the end of its useful life, cork materials can be reused or composted, promoting a 
circular economy. Simple grinding of ﬁnished panels allows the material to be repur-
posed for other applications or composted, returning valuable nutrients to

the soil. Overall, cork’s life cycle analysis highlights its potential as a sustainable material, 
with beneﬁts such as low production impacts and recyclability. Addressing trans-
portation emissions and VOCs from adhesives can further enhance its environmental 
proﬁle. 454

Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity of Bio-Based Insulation …

22.3.2 
Wood Fiber 
Fiber-based wood insulation, derived from natural wood ﬁbers, offers excellent 
sustainability credentials. Wood ﬁber has a positive energy balance, with reduced 
energy consumption during production, installation, and recycling phases. However, 
the environmental impact of transportation from production areas to application 
sites must be evaluated, particularly for long-distance transport, as it can diminish 
the material’s eco-compatibility. Pollutant emissions during production are primarily 
linked to additives, which are used in minimal concentrations. Upon reaching the end 
of its useful life, the material can be recycled and reintroduced into the processing 
cycle. Alternatively, if it has not been treated with bitumen or adhesives, it can be 
composted. Mineralized wood ﬁber, although originating from natural wood ﬁbers, 
undergoes a mineralization process involving the infusion of minerals or binders. The presence of binders increases emissions during combustion as well as poses 
challenges for reuse. One potential reuse option involves crushing the panels for use 
as inert material in concrete construction, such as lightweight subﬂoors, composite 
slabs, and prefabricated elements. Both ﬁber-based and mineralized wood insulation 
offer sustainable solutions for thermal insulation in buildings, with ﬁber-based vari-
ants characterized by better biodegradability, and reuse.

Efforts to optimize trans-
portation logistics and reduce emissions from adhesives and binders can further 
enhance their environmental proﬁles. 22.3.3 
Cellulose Fiber 
The environmental balance of cellulose ﬁber is positive, particularly for ﬂakes derived 
from recycled material. This results in decreased energy consumption across the 
production, transportation, installation, and recycling stages. Upon reaching the end 
of its useful life, the material can be reintroduced into the processing cycle or disposed 
of in waste-to-energy plants. During production, pollutant emissions are primarily 
attributed to the additives used, which are present in minimal concentrations. In 
contrast, panels require greater energy expenditure during processing and may result 
in emissions associated with additives and binders. Similarly, recycling is a consid-
erably more complex and costly process. Thus, while its production and recycling 
phases boast minimal environmental impacts, attention must be directed towards 
optimizing processing methods and recycling techniques for panels to align with 
stringent eco-standards. 456
R. Agliata et al. 22.3.4 
Natural Textile Fibers 
The environmental balance of hemp is positive, as it is a renewable raw material with 
a relatively short growth cycle and is easily adaptable to areas with very different 
climates. If cultivated correctly. hemp does not require the use of fertilizers, chemical 
additives, or herbicides. thereby negating any potential ecological risks. Conversely, 
it is capable of soil remediation and improvement. The primary source of pollutant 
emissions is the production of synthetic ﬁbers used as mechanical support. The 
environmental balance of kenaf is positive, as it is a renewable raw material with a 
relatively short growth cycle and is widely distributed across European territories. The cultivation of kenaf does not require the use of fertilizers or pesticides, which 
has the beneﬁcial effect of improving soil quality. The problem is the high water 
required for irrigation, particularly during the initial stages of germination. Pollutant 
emissions result from the production of synthetic ﬁbers used as mechanical support. Linen is a renewable raw material with a positive environmental balance. It has a 
short growth cycle. It is widely spread across European territories, and its cultivation 
does not require the use of fertilizers, herbicides, or pesticides. The emissions that 
result from the production of synthetic ﬁbers used as mechanical support are those 
that are inherent to the manufacturing process. The environmental balance of coconut 
ﬁber is inﬂuenced by transportation, which heavily impacts emissions and the ﬁnal 
energy consumption of the material. 22.3.5 
Sheep Wool 
Sheep wool is a renewable material that is widely available in areas where sheep are 
raised. The quantity of material obtained from shearing exceeds the demand of the 
textile market. The production and processing sta

ges have a minimal environmental 
impact, particularly in the case of loose wool. The production of mixed panels is 
associated with a higher environmental cost. Once the material has reached the end 
of its life cycle, it can be recycled; however, stabilizing treatments must be repeated. Mixed materials must be sent to landﬁlls and waste-to-energy plants. A synthesis of environmental performance of different bio-based insulation 
materials is reported below (Table 22.6). 22.4 
Circular Principles for Insulation Materials 
Circular economy is an emerging concept that contrasts with the traditional take-
make-dispose pattern of consumption by emphasizing closed material loops (take-
make-reuse) through regeneration, reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, and recy-
cling. While there are various deﬁnitions of circularity, it can be broadly deﬁned as

«(…) an economic system that is based on business models which replace the ‘end-
of-life’ concept with reducing, alternatively reusing, recycling and recovering mate-
rials in production/distribution and consumption processes, thus operating at the 
micro level (products, companies, consumers), meso level (eco-industrial parks) and 
macro level (city, region, nation and beyond), with the aim to accomplish sustainable 
development, which implies creating environmental quality, economic prosperity and 
social equity, to the beneﬁt of current and future generations» [10]. Circular design, 
also known as Design for Circularity (DfC), translates the principles of the circ

ular 
economy into practical application. Given the massive environmental footprint and 
high energy demand of the buildings and construction industry, this sector is at 
the forefront of the transition to circularity. Construction practitioners are actively 
incorporating circular thinking into building materials, components, and structures. Insulation materials are a prime example. They signiﬁcantly inﬂuence a building’s 
energy consumption during operation, which is often the largest source of carbon

458
R. Agliata et al. emissions. Additionally, many insulation materials have a high environmental impact 
during their production and disposal stages. Research on 503 houses found that insu-
lation material production is responsible for 26.1% of total carbon emissions, second 
largest contributor after concrete production [11]. Thus, implementing circularity in 
insulation materials is vital. This section brieﬂy discusses the ﬁve main circularity 
principles presented by the Ellen MacArthur Foundation (EMF) and their application 
to insulation materials. 22.4.1 
Design Out Waste [Output Side] 
Design phase is known as the most effective phase to eradicate waste and implement 
circular principles. Waste is eliminated when materials and components are designed 
for reuse through disassembly and refurbishment, a concept known as “closing the 
material loops”. These loops are categorized into biological and technical loops. Biological nutrients are non-toxic and can be easily composted. Bio-based insula-
tion materials like cork, wood, and cellulose ﬁbers are preferred because they can 
be returned to the natural environment through non-toxic and restorative loops. This 
generates a closed-loop system where the materials decompose and become nutri-
ents for new growth. However, some bio-based materials may contain contaminants, 
such as adhesives, that hinder biodegradability. To address this, binder-less insula-
tion materials using coconut husk and bagasse have been developed [12]. Similarly, 
technical nutrients include polymers and composite materials, should be designed 
for disassembly and reuse. They should consume minimum energy for reuse/recycle 
and retain optimal quality at the end of their lifecycle. 22.4.2 
Build Resilience Through Diversity 
Insulation materials should be designed for longevity and resilience, adapting to 
external shocks and efﬁciently meeting ever-changing requirements throughout their 
lifecycle. This practice is known as “slowing down the loops”. Buildings are typi-
cally designed to last 50–60 years, but they can often endure for up to 100 years. Given such a long lifespan, the purpose and function of buildings continually evolve, 
making functional requirements hard to predict. To avoid excessive waste and repair 
during renovations, insulation systems should be designed to last longer and adapt to 
future needs. However, achieving this may require trade-offs between various mate-
rial properties, such as balanci

ng high porosity, which enhances insulation but reduces 
mechanical strength. Durable insulation materials with high moisture resistance and 
good UV stability are preferred. Additionally, modular designs for insulation systems 
facilitate future modiﬁcations and easy repairs. 22
Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity of Bio-Based Insulation …
459
22.4.3 
Optimize Energy Use 
As discussed in Sect. 2.2, materials fabrication process can be energy intensive. Therefore, materials that require less energy for manufacture, repair and reuse should 
be preferred. Table 22.6 provides a comparison of embodied energy and renew-
able capability of various materials. The renewable energy sources should be used 
in manufacturing, construction, disassembly phase of the materials to reduce the 
embodied energy. Similarly, the use phase of buildings often accounts for the highest 
energy consumption and carbon emissions. Insulation materials play a crucial role 
here. Favor materials with high insulation efﬁciency, as they can signiﬁcantly reduce 
the overall energy demand of a building, lowering its environmental impacts. LCA 
can be valuable tools for selecting the most suitable materials. These assessments 
consider the entire life cycle of a product and identify options with the lowest overall 
energy consumption. Insulation materials made from cork, sheep wool and cellulose 
are known for having lower embodied energy compared to traditional options. 22.4.4 
Thinking in System 
A building functions as a complex system, with each element affecting and being 
affected by others. This necessitates a holistic design approach that considers insu-
lation materials not just in isolation, but in relation to the whole building, environ-
ment, and social context. Buildings are viewed as “material banks”, with components 
having varying lifespans and maintenance cycles. Brand divided the building into 
“Six Layers framework” (site, structure, skin, space plan, services, and stuff) [13]. Insulation materials play important role in skin, structure and space plan. Thermal or 
acoustic insulation is usually provided in roofs, elevations and external walls, these 
elements form the skin of the building. Partition walls (space plan) may also have 
insulation materials. In few cases, insulation may be integrated into structural compo-
nents such as insulated concrete forms. Since every layer has its own function and 
lifespan, circular design principles become critical. A ‘system thinking’ is required. Designers need to consider how insulation interacts with other parts (walls, roofs), 
how easy it is to access and remove, how to assemble and disassemble it for future 
reuse or recycling, and ﬁnally, how to repair or replace it with minimal damage to 
surrounding elements. By making insulation easy to access through modular design, 
repairs become more circular, minimizing harm to connected parts. 460
R.

Agliata et al. 22.4.5 
Waste is Food [Input Side] 
In theoretical terms, waste is non-existent in a fully circular system. On input side, 
the waste should be fed back into system as ‘feedstock’ instead of virgin raw mate-
rial. This waste input can come from the same process or even from entirely different 
industries. The bio-based insulation materials discussed in Sect. 2.2 exemplify this 
principle. They utilize agricultural waste (corn stalks, wheat straw, etc.), paper waste 
(newspapers), and wood industry residuals to create insulation materials. This elim-
inates the need for end-of-life waste treatment in ﬁrst lifecycle and virgin mate-
rials extraction in following lifecycle. Technical waste from textiles, rubber, and 
plastics can also be upcycled into thermal insulation materials [12]. In circularity 
certain strategies are preferred over others. For instance, upcycling that prioritizes 
reusing materials in their original form or for a higher-quality purpose, is favored 
over traditional recycling which can lead to material degradation. In this regard, ‘R-
Imperatives’ also known as ‘R-Ladder’ and’10 R framework’ provides a hierarchy 
of various approaches that can be used to implement circularity principles. As shown 
in Table 22.7, Refuse (R0) is the most preferred option, while Recovery (R9) is the 
least desired. 22.5 
LCA and Circularity Indicators (CI) for Insulation 
Materials 
Implementing circularity and sustainability principles in construction such as insula-
tion materials is a complex process that requires several considerations and transfor-
mations in the usual practices. The evolving nature of circular economy and blurriness

22
Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity of Bio-Based Insulation …
461
regarding its conceptual foundation adds another layer of difﬁculty. Tracking progress 
towards circularity and sustainability goals can be challenging. However, assessment 
methods such LCA and circularity indicators offer objective ways to measure the 
circular and environmental performance of the materials. These methods provide a 
common language to communicate between stakeholders and allow the comparison 
between different alternatives. The analysis can help identify the environmental and 
resou

rce intensive processes that can be optimized to improve the overall perfor-
mance of the material. Additionally, they provide a holistic lifecycle perspective of 
the material loops which is vital aspect of multicycle circular economy. This section 
explores how LCA and circularity indicators can be applied to evaluate bio-based 
insulation materials. 22.5.1 
Circularity Indicators 
Circularity indicators are assessment methods that transform the circular principles 
into measurable values. Indicator developed for buildings are known as building 
circularity indicators. Indicators can be nano (material), micro (building), meso 
(industrial symbiosis) and macro (city and region) level. Among the available indi-
cators for building and materials, the Whole Building Circularity Indicator (WBCI) 
stands out as a multi-level, multi-cycle innovative framework. It synthesizes existing 
information and offers a lifecycle perspective to track circularity performance. Based 
on quantitative material, design, and building data, the WBCI provides circularity 
ratings on a scale from 0 (fully linear) to 1 (fully circular) for materials, compo-
nents/elements, systems, and the whole-building. While it compares material and 
component alternatives, it also sheds light on how choice made at material level can 
signiﬁcantly inﬂuence the building’s circularity performance [14]. Research high-
lights that the supply chain level circularity of insulation materials is quite low, for 
instance it stands at 1.5% in Swiss residential building sector. The main reason for 
low circularity score is the difﬁculty to deconstruct and collect insulation material 
as a single trough. The insulation materials are generally glued (chemical connec-
tions) to the construction elements that hinders the fast and residual free recovery. Furthermore, dedicated recycling facilities are scarce, currently limited to EPS, stone 
wool, glass wool, and wood ﬁberboards [15]. In this regard, WBCI is a guiding tool to 
improve the circularity performance of insulation materials. Building on the WBCI’s 
mathematical and theoretical framework, Table 22.8 outlines key factors inﬂuencing 
circularity performance of inﬂuence materials. It explains these factors, provides 
relevant examples, and explains their impact on overall circularity scores. The appli-
cation of material circularity indicator on individual biobased insulation materials, 
such as ﬂax, reveals their potential to enhance the circularity value of façades or 
walls. Flax insulation exhibits a low linear ﬂow value and a high material circularity 
value due to its high biobased content and ease of biodegradability at the end of its 
life. However, this impact becomes negative or insigniﬁcant when seen as an element 
(i.e., wall, roof), due to disassembly and other technical concerns [16]. 462

Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity of Bio-Based Insulation …
463
22.5.2 
Lifecycle Assessment for Biobased Insulation 
Materials 
As discussed earlier, some insulation materials demonstrably reduce building opera-
tional energy use, while others, such as bio-based materials, appear environmentally 
beneﬁcial due to their lower manufacturing and disposal emissions. To choose the 
most suitable material for a speciﬁc case, it is essential to analyze the material from 
a holistic perspective. LCA bridges this gap by considering emissions across the 
entire product lifecycle, including raw material extraction, manufacturing, opera-
tional energy use in buildings, and end-of-life deconstruction, waste disposal, and 
recycling. Similar to the circularity indicator, LCA assigns values to different mate-
rial options. These values and ratings enable the comparison of the environmental 
performance of materials and the identiﬁcation of areas for improvement. Bio-based materials store CO2, helping to reduce climate impact, while non-
renewable materials like EPS contribute to an immediate increase in global warming 
potential (GWP) due to their energy-intensive production processes. Bio-based mate-
rials reduce the burden on depleting resources, have higher speciﬁc heat capacity, 
and passively contribute to the indoor environment. In cont

rast, non-renewable mate-
rials are cost-effective, easy to install, and highly resistant to moisture. Conducting a 
comparative LCA among various bio-based materials, as well as a cross-comparison 
between non-renewable and bio-based materials, is vital to understanding their envi-
ronmental performance. However, the comparison of LCA studies is only possible 
if analyses are conducted under same conditions such as methodological choices 
and geological locations. LCA results are sensitive to many factors, such as (but 
not limited to) goal and scope of study, functional unit, system boundaries, selected 
impact assessment method and database [17]. Table 22.9 compares the environmental impacts of four common bio-based insu-
lation materials under the same conditions. Wood ﬁber contributes the least to global 
warming, producing only 2.72 kg CO2 eq. per m2 of external wall insulation, which 
is crucial given the current climate crisis. In contrast, ﬂax has a high impact on global 
warming, generating 13.62 kg CO2 eq. for the same application. Overall, insulation 
materials made from miscanthus perform best in the other three impact categories. Similarly, bio-based materials particularly wood ﬁber and miscanthus produce signif-
icantly less lifecycle impacts across several impact categories compared to EPS and 
stone wool insulation materials [16]. Biobased insulation material ﬂax performs 
similar to stone wool in terms of insulation properties. Netherlands’s NIBE platform 
[18] classiﬁed ﬂax insulation as ‘best’ choice in terms of environmental performance, 
whereas stone wool is considered as ‘bad’ choice (class 6c) (Table 22.9). Another important consideration for bio-based insulation materials is that 
achieving the same thermal resistance may require a thicker layer compared to tradi-
tional materials. Figure 22.6 provides a comparison between different bio-based and 
nonrenewable insulation materials for 100 m2 with a thermal resistance of (5 m2K)/ 
W, based on research conducted on 503 residential houses. XPS has signiﬁcantly 
higher carbon emissions compared to bio-based alternatives. Straw bale and hemp

performance is recommended to select the most optimal solution. Combining LCA 
and WBCI, along with various design scenarios, can help objectively make these 
trade-offs (Fig. 22.6). 22.6 
Conclusion 
This chapter elucidate the environmental properties of natural and bio-based insula-
tion materials, shading light on the environmental beneﬁts, as well as possible short-
comings, deriving from their application in the building industry. These materials

22
Life Cycle Assessment and Circularity of Bio-Based Insulation …
465
often present a carbon-negative balance compared to traditional plastic or mineral-
based insulations, showing a signiﬁcant potential in advancing sustainability and 
circularity. Despite their advantages, bio-based insulations currently represent less 
than 10% of the global market. However, a thorough evaluation of the environmental pros and cons should rely on 
quantitative analyses, using tools like LCA and Circularity Indicators, which provide 
a robust framework for evaluating the environmental performance and sustainability 
of insulation materials. These tools help identify opportunities for improvement in 
material selection, usage, and end-of-life management, promoting a more holistic and 
sustainable approach to building design and construction. This analysis wanted to 
underscore the importance of adopting sustainable practices and advancing research 
in bio-based insulation materials to achieve a balanced integration of environmental, 
economic, and social considerations in the built environment. References

Chapter 23 
Analysis of Building Foundation 
Reconstruction: A Holistic Assessment 
of Its Economic, Social, 
and Environmental Impacts 
Antonio Ferreira-Sánchez, Mª Desirée Alba-Rodríguez, 
Valeriano Lucas-Ruiz, and Madelyn Marrero 
Abstract The issue regarding rehabilitation versus building demolition remains an 
open debate. In order to make decisions in a sustainable way, the social, economic, 
and environmental dimensions all need to be considered. Although rehabilitation is 
not always the most economical solution, it can, in most cases, offer other bene-
ﬁts beyond solely contributing towards environmental sustainability, including the 
reduction of project execution times, protection of communities, and the restriction 
of urban sprawl. A holistic assessment of these factors is therefore essential. In the 
present work, a multi-family building in Seville, Spain, constructed in 1950 and 
containing forty social housing dwellings, suffered an accident related to soil subsi-
dence. Consequently, prompt decisions had to be made in order to rehabilitate the 
property and return the occupants to their dwellings within a year. The building also 
needed a signiﬁcant repair to its foundations. In the present work, a methodology 
is deﬁned to rapidly assess the economic, social, and environmental sustainability 
of various options of reconstruction work. Four solutions for the reconstruction of 
the foundations are studied, which give rise to a methodology for the assessment of 
similar scenarios of repair work. A. Ferreira-Sánchez
