A. Ferreira-Sánchez et al. 23.1 
Introduction 
Approximately 50% of the buildings in Europe are over 50 years old. In comparison 
to contemporary buildings, these are more vulnerable to catastrophic events, such 
as ﬂoods, earthquakes, and accidents, since their construction predates today’s more 
restrictive construction codes [1]. When a building needs to be repaired under emer-

gency circumstances, the results of the work, the techniques, and the organisation 
used rarely become available for public scrutiny. This is due not only to the affected 
stakeholders’ avoidance of negative publicity, but also to the related responsibilities 
and to a general lack of technical knowledge in the background of professionals to 
deal with the details and complexities of the various techniques available [2]. Another 
aspect that highlights the need for deeper knowledge regarding buildings affected by 
an emergency repair is derived from the urgency with which it must be undertaken. This kind of intervention has an insufﬁcient number of previous studies available 
and hence offers only a limited range of possibilities, and emergency repairs tend 
to involve insufﬁcient time to ﬁnd the optimal solution at a certain technical and 
economic level [3]. Although there are examples of emergency retroﬁts related to 
certain infrastructures [4], their solutions cannot be suitable for every type of building 
repair. It is necessary to increase the publication of case studies, where the origin of 
the damage, the technical solutions, the results, and the management processes are 
fully reported [5]. Recent, simulations [6] and studies [7] have determined the feasi-
bility of methodologies using micropiling as a solution for unstable soil. However, 
economic, environmental, and social aspects must also be considered if a sustainable 
solution is to be found. Reference [8] revealed that building environmental analysis 
largely centres on new construction and fails to include the productivity, efﬁciency, 
and short- and long-term consequences of the building rehabilitation work. More-
over, it has become necessary to include the environmental aspect since building 
rehabilitation is not solely an economic issue. In fact, due to the multifaceted complexity of repairing damaged buildings, a clear 
methodology is necessary to successfully achieve the goals of the intervention [9]. A 
method to prioritise the actions to be taken during the rehabilitation is also needed, 
in order to help in the work planning and to decide on the best solutions to be imple-
mented during execution. Nowadays, limitations on the work plan that include only 
the technical and economic aspects are becoming increasingly common. The envi-
ronmental performance of building retroﬁts is currently promoted by local, regional, 
and national governments, and will become mandatory in the near future. The Euro-
pean Union encourages stakeholders to use the common framework LEVEL(s) to 
improve building resilience against climate change, among other major objectives 
[10]. To maximise the positive effects of environmental building interventions, a 
wider approach is required that considers the urban context and provides in-depth 
knowledge regarding the environmental impact related to the work. These consti-
tute key factors in the creation of the strategic measures to be implemente

d [11]. 23
Analysis of Building Foundation Reconstruction: A Holistic …
469
Certain studies [12] even show that, in comparison with new buildings, higher efﬁ-
ciency standards can be achieved when a life-cycle approach is considered during 
the rehabilitation. In the present work, the relationship between environmental, social, and economic 
factors is analysed. All three factors constitute major aspects of the decision-making 
process and are proposed while considering the improvements necessary to swiftly 
render a building habitable subsequent to an accident [13]. In order to consider 
as many dimensions as possible in this complicated process, a model is proposed 
that, starting from the project budget perspective, includes the environmental [14], 
economic (project bill of quantities and budget), and social (health and safety on the 
construction site) [15] assessment of the foundation underpinning. For the economic evaluation, the work breakdown system (WBS) of the Andalusia 
Construction Cost Database, [16] is employed. The WBS can improve the general 
knowledge by including environmental impact assessment and by allowing an 
ecological comparison in terms of the carbon footprint (CF) [17], the ecological 
footprint (EF) [18], and the water footprint (WF) [8, 19]. Social aspects are also relevant since the reconstruction of a building after a 
disaster carries huge implications for the tenants, and these are also taken into 
consideration [3]. When a damaged building is under major structural risk and imme-
diate intervention is required, then multiple technical factors and unknown elements 
become involved, which can inﬂuence the ﬁnal decision [20]. Many social indicators 
have been employed by researchers, such as occupants comfort, building manage-
ment, accessibility, etc. The social dimension of sustainable development is difﬁcult 
to assess mainly due to the subjective aspects in its assessment. Another social aspect 
involves the construction work itself [14] The breakdown system of construction cost 
databases can be useful from the perspective of the health & safety risks associated 
with the foundation repair [21]. Four solutions are considered for the foundation repair work: foundation 
underpinning, reinforced grouting, cement injection, and injections of expandable 
polyurethane resin. The generation of information on expertise and tools may help 
technicians in other, similar situations. The present work focuses on the case study of 
40 dwellings damaged during an excavation that was taking place on an adjoining lot 
and caused the evacuation of all families from said dwellings. This resulted in tech-
nical and social problems that needed immediate action. Techniques of psychological 
ﬁrst aid for social management are presented. Indirect aspects of the repair work are 
also assessed using environmental indicators, cost assessment, and occupational-risk 
evaluation of the project. Lastly, a model of action is proposed for the

restoration of 
buildings under emergency conditions that takes into consideration social, ecological, 
and economic aspects. In order to deﬁne the methodology, four reparation projects are deﬁned, one for 
each solution, using the WBS of ACCD. On this basis, the proposed model assesses 
the feasibility of the repair work of the building foundation, which would otherwise 
be facing a demolition, by means of a global vision that incorporates environmental, 
social, and economic evaluation. Furthermore, the risks on the construction site are 
also considered through the identiﬁcation of health & safety risks. 470
A. Ferreira-Sánchez et al. 23.2 
Literature Review 
23.2.1 
Environmental Assessment 
For the environmental assessment of construction projects, identiﬁcation is made of 
the carbon emissions and embodied energy (EE) as the most commonly evaluated 
indicators in the review conducted by [22]. However, other indicators stand out in the 
assessment of buildings, such as the ecological footprint (EF) and the water footprint 
(WF), which are normally referred to as part of the footprint family. The term footprint 
family was coined for the ﬁrst time by [23] and [24]. Reference [25] consider that 
this term encompasses the three most widely studied footprints (CF, EF, and WF) 
due to the intuition of the term footprint. According to these studies, the family of 
footprints can be considered as an indicator that gathers other indicators, in an effort 
to obtain basic information from each of the footprints, with special emphasis on 
comparative aspects [26]. The success of these footprints is due, ﬁrstly, to the fact 
that the results they produce are understandable to non-scientiﬁc members of society, 
and secondly, to their ease of application in environmental policies and decision-
making [27, 28]. It is necessary, however, to advance in their standardisation, since 
research combining multiple footprints remains limited in contrast to single-footprint 
studies [29]. A good association of these indicators is shown in the evaluation of 
construction and demolition waste (CDW) in the building life cycle [30], and [31] 
employ said indicators in the assessment of urbanisation projects. Moreover, the 
complete building life cycle has also been analysed by simultaneously employing 
these indicators [14]. The CF indicator constitutes the most widely used indicator from the footprint 
family in the assessment of buildings. Reviews related to the use of the CF [32] 
demonstrate its importance in the sector. Other studies strive to establish scales of 
CO2 emission intervals in construction processes [33–35]. The ArDiTec group works 
on the calculation of the CF in buildings [28, 36] and has developed the evaluation 
tool OERCO2 [37, 38]. Another interesting indicator is that of the EF, which was introduced by [39], 
who measured the EF of humanity and compared it with the carrying capacity of the 
planet. The EF is the amount of land that would be re

quired to provide the resources 
(grain, feed, ﬁrewood, ﬁsh, and urban land) and absorb the emissions (CO2)  of  
humanity. Moreover, the ArDiTec research group has successfully adapted the EF 
indicator to the assessment of construction impacts [40, 41]. Their analysis includes 
the rehabilitation of buildings [13] and the retroﬁtting and usage of buildings [8]. The WF considers yet another important asset, that of the water consumption of 
human processes. Construction materials consume water in their extraction, manu-
facture, waste management, and reuse. Reference [42] study direct and indirect water 
consumption in the life cycle of residential buildings and show that the indirect water 
consumption is greater than the direct household consumption of water. The WF of 
buildings can be analysed from a global perspective [43] either through an input– 
output analysis of total consumption in the country or via models that analyse the

23
Analysis of Building Foundation Reconstruction: A Holistic …
471
components in construction projects [44–46]. It is also worth mentioning the research 
of the ArDiTec group where they incorporate the WF indicator into the assessment 
of urbanisation projects in which both direct and indirect water consumption are 
evaluated [47, 48]. 23.2.2 
Economic Assessment 
For the cost evaluation of construction projects, different regions have developed clas-
siﬁcation methodologies of construction-related activities. These have a long tradi-
tion in the sector, such as MasterFormat [49], CI/SfB [50], the Standard Method of 
Measurement of Civil Engineering [51], Uniformat [52], and Uniclass [53]. In Spain, 
the classiﬁcation systems by the Institute of Construction Technology of Catalonia 
[54] and Generador de Precios [55] appear among those most commonly employed. Over the last three decades, in the south of Spain, the regional government has deﬁned 
the Andalusian Construction Cost Database (ACCD), which has become mandatory 
in public bidding [56]. 23.2.3 
Social Assessment 
Direct Impact 
There are social impacts by the damaged building that affect tenants directly. Mate-
rial loss and evacuation generate stress, and tenants’ mistrust can arise due to the 
complexity of the technical solutions or to lack of protocol knowledge established 
for the intervention [57]. This factor can directly inﬂuence the choice of the ﬁnal 
solution to be applied due to the desire to reduce the duration of the project. The effects of a severe structural accident in a building are similar to those caused 
by earthquakes, ﬂoods, and conﬂicts. The health of the population can be seriously 
affected if left untreated. In order to prevent such effects, the World Health Organi-
zation has developed “Psychological First Aid (PFA)” [58], whose main objective 
is to provide a human response in support of those suffering and those in need [59]. The conceptual and methodological simplicity of this technique has demonstrated 
its effectiveness a

nd can be applied by non-specialists [60]. It can be combined with 
the technical aspects of the repair work of the damaged building [57] from an inte-
gral point of view, in a “Model of action in buildings against damage and accidents 
with social repercussions” [57] where clear commitment and a deﬁned time limit can 
generate a favourable attitude of the families affected. Situations of post-traumatic 
stress are prevented and, through the participation of the occupants, a favourable 
climate can be created both for the execution of the repair work and for its reception. 472
A. Ferreira-Sánchez et al. Indirect Impact: Health & Safety in the Construction 
The construction sector presents one of the highest accident rates in the European 
Union (EU) and accounted for 20% of the fatal accidents occurring at work in 2020 
[61]. Furthermore, the types of injuries appear to remain constant over time [62]. The cause-effect of accidents must be recorded in order to propose solutions and 
interventions towards the reduction and prevention of accidents [63], and the miti-
gation of risks needs to be delivered at various levels and by different actors [64]. Tools to assess and control H&S risks have been developed over the years, such as 
the Storybuilder Tool [65], the Risk Assessment Model [66], the Safety, Health, and 
Environmental Checklist for Contractors in the Netherlands [63], the Construction 
Site Risk Assessment Tool (CONSRAT) [67], and other similar instruments [68]. However, these are independent of other tools already in place at the construction 
site regarding cost control, which require additional expertise and/or personnel. In 
the present work, a methodology is proposed for the control and deﬁnition of risks 
in a systematic way, and this is linked to the economic and environmental control of 
the project. 23.3 
Materials and Methods 
23.3.1 
Economic Assessment 
For the generation of the inventory of resources of the life cycle, the use of construc-
tion cost databases and their WBS systems are proposed. The regional database, 
ACCD, [56] is employed, which has a pyramidal classiﬁcation. It follows the cost 
structure established by the Public Procurement Law and its implementing regula-
tions [69], and divides the budget into chapters that group together basic costs (BC), 
auxiliary costs (AC), and unit costs (UC). Basic costs refer to construction mate-
rials, machinery, and labour, while the AC are combinations of the BC that have 
no entity of a ﬁnished work (such as mortars, concrete, work team, etc.). The UC 
are elements that represent ﬁnished elements on the work site such a cubic metre of 
soil excavated for the foundation, a square metre of terrazzo ﬂoor polished and in 
place, and a window set into the façade. The pyramidal structure continues with the 
chapters of work that classify the stages of the construction of the buildings and their 
corresponding tasks, for example, Chap. 1 corresponds to the clearing of the plot a

nd 
to small demolitions, if necessary. Chapter 2 groups earthwork tasks, while Chap. 3 
covers the construction of the foundation. The subsequent chapters of the budget 
include structure, brickwork, and installations. With the measurement of work units 
or UC, the amount of each speciﬁc activity is then obtained, and, by aggregation, the 
total budget of the work is attained. The pyramidal structure enables the creation of 
a database that combines both economic and environmental information (Fig. 23.1). 23

23.3.2 
Environmental Assessment 
Labour 
Of all the footprints only the EF indicator includes the impact of workers through 
their food and generation of municipal solid waste (MSW). The workers’ food is 
considered their energy source and is obtained from Eq. 1 in Fig. 23.2. as typical 
adult food intake consisting of meat, ﬁsh, cereals, and water [70]. Food also has an 
energy footprint, due to its transformations. The footprints according to the food source (meat: EF of pastures; ﬁsh: EF of 
productive sea; cereals: EF of crops) has a natural productivity and equivalence factor 
between actual productive hectares (ha) and global hectares (gha). The equivalence 
factors are 2.51 for crops, 0.46 for pastures, 1.26 for forests, 0.37 for sea, and direct 
occupation is 2.51 gha/ha [71]. The equivalence factors used [72] are implicit in 
the calculation of EF (gha/person/year) of each country. In Spain, footprints in gha/ 
person/year are 1.45, 0.27, 0.41, and 0.49 in 10–3 gha for crops, pastures, ﬁsheries, 
and fossil fuels, respectively. It has been considered that breakfast and lunch (taking 
place on-site) represent approximately 60% of the daily food intake of an adult [41]. Equation 3 of Fig. 23.2 is utilised to calculate the impact of municipal solid waste 
(MSW). An average generation coefﬁcient per worker and the emission factor of its 
subsequent treatment are used [14]. The impact of the workers mobility to the work 
site is excluded as per UNE-EN 15978 [73]. Construction Materials 
The determination of the impact of construction materials follows the methodology 
deﬁned by [74]. The ﬁrst step is to perform a conversion of the original unit of 
measurement of each basic cost (m3,  m2, m, t, thousands of units of bricks, etc.) 
into m3, and its density is obtained from the Catalogue of Constructive Solutions 
of the Technical Building Code and the Basic Document of Structural Safety of the 
Technical Building Code DB-SE AE [1], and hence the weight of each element is 
attained. This enables the environmental coefﬁcients per kg to be applied as obtained 
from the LCA database, Ecoinvent LCA [75], which is recognised as being one of the

474

energy (EE), and the ecological (EF), carbon (CF), and water footprints (WF)
most compl

ete databases at European level [76] and known for its integration with 
the Simapro LCA software [77]. The Life Cycle Inventory of materials using the 
IPCC 100a methodology is employed to obtain CO2 emissions of building materials. This methodology isolates CO2 and other GHG emissions from the LCI inventory. 23

Equation 4 in Fig. 23.2 is applied to obtain the EF, and Eq. 6 is employed to obtain 
the remaining indicators. The work is similar to that conducted for the calculation of the CF with the 
SOFIAS tool [78], which uses data either from environmental product declarations, 
OpenDAP, or from the BEDEC platform [79], developed by the Institute of Construc-
tion Technology of Catalonia [80], see Fig. 23.2 where the integration of indicators 
in the budget of the work is shown in a schematic way. The Cmi is obtained by means 
of the family of materials, measured in weight per ground ﬂoor area, by which its 
environmental impact is multiplied to obtain IUMAT. In order to evaluate the A4 stage of the life cycle [73], an analysis of the transport 
of the material is also conducted, thereby establishing approximations of distance 
travelled, as in the previous section on machinery. The average diesel consumption 
(26 L/100 km) and its emissions (2.62E−03 tCO2/litre) are used. The water consump-
tion in its production is 1.26 m3/litre of diesel and its embodied energy is 57.7 MJ/ 
litre. The capacity of the trucks and travel distance is 2000 and 24,000 kg, and 20 and 
250 km, for the transport of concrete and for other materials, respectively [81]. As 
an example, in Table 23.1, basic costs are analysed, and their costs, transformation 
coefﬁcients and associated environmental impacts are presented. Machinery 
The construction machinery impact is calculated using the engine power and the on-
site hours of use, while the energy consumed is determined in kWh, which will be 
transformed into CO2 emissions [81]. The calculation is CDW transport machinery. To obtain fuel consumption, the “Machinery Manual” prepared by SEOPAN [82] 
is used, where the technical data of different models and types of machines on the 
market are collected. From the power of the engine, the litres of fuel consumed are 
calculated, (Eq. 9 in Fig. 23.2) and the amount of CO2 generated per litre is applied 
[83]. The WF per litre of fuel is also obtained from Ecoinvent, and the CF, WF (Eq. 11 
in Fig. 23.2), and EF (Eq. 11 in Fig. 23.2) are calculated. The electrical machinery used on-site, depending on the engine power and the use 
time, is measured in kWh [81]. The emissions of the production of one kWh by the 
Spanish electricity system is employed [84], as GHG gases, measured through the 
global warming potential (GWP) of the various gases emitted into the atmosphere, 
and through weighting the tons and transforming them into equivalent tons of CO2. For the WF of the electrical machines, the WF associated with

the Spanish energy 
mix is used. The EF is calculated using Eq. 9 in Fig. 23.2 and, for the remaining 
indicators, Eq. 12 is applied. 23.3.3

illustrated in Fig. 23.3.

The steps are the following:
1. Problem boundaries: collective and individual protection and the delimitation of 
spaces, in compliance with Spanish law regarding organisational and ergonomic 
activities necessary to prevent and/or minimise risks [85, 86]. 2. Collection of building characteristics, and its bill of quantities is expressed in the 
work breakdown system of the ACCD. 3. Risk indicator: the “Simpliﬁed accident risk assessment system” published by 
the INSST in the Prevention Technical Note 330 is employed [87]. For ergonomic 
impacts, either the “Evaluation of working conditions: postural load” is assessed 
or the REBA method (Rapid Entire Body Assessment) [88, 89], published by the 
INSST in NTP 601. 4. Work procedures: analysis of each work unit, and of its procedures and/or tasks. 5. Risk assessment: accidents and ergonomic risks of each procedure previously 
deﬁned are assessed and aggregated into a single indicator. 23

(DL: 10 for very deﬁcient, 6 for deﬁcient, 2 for improbable, and 0 for acceptable), 
the exposure level (EL: 4 for continuous, 3 for frequent, 2 for occasional, and 1 for 
sporadic), and the consequence level (CL: 100 for catastrophic or fatal, 60 for very

Case Study 
A case study is analysed of the foundation repair of forty apartments located in a 
building in Seville (Spain) which had been constructed in the 1950s. The building was 
damaged by a soil movement produced by nearby construction work; the foundation 
d

isplacement damaged the masonry of the loadbearing walls. It was necessary to 
evacuate the tenants quickly due to the safety risk. The building presented cracks 
and deformations that needed major repair work [3], see Fig. 23.5. For the emergency foundation underpinning, four techniques are considered: foun-
dation underpinning; cement injection; injections of expandable polyurethane resin; 
and increasing load transmission area of the foundation footings. The four solutions are brieﬂy described:
• Foundation underpinning: 232 IRS (Repetitive and Selective Injection) 27 m-long 
micropiles (actual solution), in order to reach the resistant substrate of the ground, 
located at 25 m below the soil surface, and to transmit the mechanical tensions 
into solid clay located at that level. These IRS micropiles also enable selective 
cement injections to be made in selected soil levels. • Soil cement injection: 300 kg of cement per metre perforated via 360 injections 
located at each metre of foundation have been considered. Using this solution 
to increase the soil compaction due to the pressure of the mortar injection. The 
mortar considered has a water/cement ratio of 0.4 using a cement, EN 197-1-
CEM I 42,5 R, with a compression resistance of 42.5 MPa. Its maximum depth

it’s length, was selected. The diameter was 140 mm and reinforced with a 60.3 mm

and the most commonly solution available, were placed along the foundation every

foundation underpinning, cement injection, injections of expandable polyurethane 
and increasing load transmission area of the foundation footings. Technically, there 
are several differences. The main difference is the depth of the action: in micropiling 
it is necessary to drill up to a depth of 25 m, while for reinforced grouting it is only 
necessary to reach the section of soil that needs to be improved (in this case the 
soil layer that is 6 m deep under the foundation). In the micropiling, a connection 
between the micropile and the existing foundation is needed, see Fig. 23.7; while 
in the reinforced grouting, the existing foundations remain untouched and the soil 
underneath is treated, see Fig. 23.8. 23.5 
Results and Discussion 
23.5.1 
Economic and Technical Comparison 
The external side of the building foundation is underpinned with 58 micropiles, of 
27 m each in length, resulting in 1566 m in total and a cost of 898,890.78e. In  a  
second hypothetical scenario, the reinforced grouting needs 241 m under the founda-
tion, with 97 grouting points (2 of which are placed every 2–2.5 m) with between 4.5

484
A. Ferreira-Sánchez et al. Fig. 23.7 Reinforced grouting [3]
and 6 m distance between each perforation and 1018.5 m in total length. Each unitary 
cost is described in Table 23.4. The total cost in this case would be 511,117.66e. In the case of using injections of expandable polyurethane resin, the cost would be 
544,883e for 240 points of injection. If the traditional solution were adopted for 
increasing the foundation support area, the cost would be 324,166e. Although the 
cost of micropiles is indeed the highest of all the scenarios, the direct transmission 
of load to a resistant soil layer ensures the success of the solution. Moreover, since 
this underpinning solution is widely used in construction, it is available for use in 
emergency situations; in contrast, grout injections, a less known system, despite 
presenting a more affordable solution, need not only a deeper soil characterisation, 
but also studies for the design of an optimal solution in terms of effective pressures, 
deep injections, and materials used. The use of injections of expandable polyure

thane 
resin is a solution that requires no heavy machinery or auxiliary systems (but cannot 
reach deeper soil layers), which makes it a quick way of dealing with minor subsi-
dence of foundations but, on the other hand, the use of polymers has yet to be proven 
as a durable solution and the effects of its expansions need rigorous control, which 
increases its risks in emergency applications. The traditional solution, based on incre-
menting the foundation support area, uses less machinery but much more manual 
labour and, for this reason, is the solution with the longest execution time. Moreover,

due to the need for a connection to the existing foundation, there is also a technical 
risk of failure and the involvement of complex planning. As a result, a foundation recovery solution must be determined not only by means 
of the economic assessment, but using an approach that takes into consideration all 
the aspects (solution availability, execution time, and complexity) that contribute 
towards the success of the solution. The micropiling solution is the most expensive, at approximately 40% more than 
the others, and the traditional reconstruction is the cheapest. However, from the 
technical evaluation of the latter method, it is the most complex to perform and is 
less reliable in correctly transmitting the loads between the newly built sections and 
the original foundation. 23.5.2 
Environmental Comparison 
For comparison with work by other authors, the impacts are calculated per ground 
ﬂoor area of the building, 3504.00 m2. The CF value of construction of new buildings 
varies signiﬁcantly: from 0.128 to 1.35 tCO2/m2, as calculated by others [35, 91, 92]. This wide range can be due to the different levels of complexity of the projects under 
study. In the present work, the repair work can also be expressed in terms of ground 
ﬂoor area. The CF of micropiling is 0.163 kgCO2eq/m2, while mortar injection is 
0.251 kgCO2eq/m2, polyurethane injection is 0.0359 kgCO2eq/m2, and traditional 
restructuring is 0.0629 kgCO2eq/m2. The CF of the repair work is signiﬁcantly high: 
even higher than those found in the construction of complete buildings. Comparing 
the solutions, the CF impact of mortar injection is substantially greater than in the 
other solutions (see Fig. 23.9), which is due to the large amount of mortar needed 
in order to complete just one metre of micropiling, since part of the material is 
dispersed in the soil and needs 300 kg of cement for every metre in depth thereof, 
see Table 23.4. 23

Fig. 23.9 Footprints and cost comparison 
The EF determined by other authors is in terms of ground ﬂoor area of the construc-
tion of new buildings, at 0.6 gha/m2 [93], and 0.05 gha/m2 [94]. In the present 
work, the EF of micropiling is 7.87E−02gha/m2, mortar injection is 1.20E−01 
gha/m2, polyurethane injection is 1.57E−02 gha/m2, and traditional r

estructuring 
is 3.12E−02 gha/m2. The results also show that the impact of the repair work has a 
high EF as compared to new construction. The EF follows a similar trend because the 
carbon footprint of construction materials is transformed into forest footprint, and 
is more signiﬁcant than the impact from machinery or the workforce. If the impact 
of the materials is separated from other EF categories, see Fig. 23.10, then the tradi-
tional solution leaves the greatest footprint in each category. This is largely due to 
the traditional solution being more labour-intensive and the footprints from workers 
food is higher in each category. The machinery has a greater impact during injections

than during micropiling, injections of expandable polyurethane resin (polyurethane 
injection), and the increasing of transmission area (traditional restructuring). The WF of micropiling is 0.86 m3/m2, that of mortar injection is 1.16 m3/m2, 
polyurethane injection is 2.11 m3/m2, and traditional restructuring is 1.11 m3/m2. The WF of the whole construction of warehouses is 6–12 m3/m2 [95]. The results of 
other authors of the construction of dwellings stand at 20.80 m3/m2 [46], 20.83 m3/ 
m2 [96], and 26.6 m3/m2 [45]. This shows the high impact that repair work exerts in 
comparison to that of the construction of a new building. From among the suggested 
solutions, the solution of polyurethane injections has the highest WF impact. This is 
caused by the injection of expansive resins for consolidation of the ground located 
under the existing foundation. The procedure is water-intensive: 7380 m3 of water 
per metre per column. 23.5.3 
Comparison of the Social Indicator 
In Fig. 23.11, the risk assessment is presented per unit cost and construction solu-
tion. From these results, it is clear that traditional underpinning is the technique that 
presents the greatest risk in its execution, with 110 risk level points. The highest 
accumulation of risk for this solution corresponds to unit cost 03RZK90010 (Foun-
dation underpinning) with 87 points, due to both the numerous procedures necessary 
for its execution, with a total of 26 procedures (compared to the average of 9 proce-
dures required to execute the other constructive solutions), and the risk involved in

23
Analysis of Building Foundation Reconstruction: A Holistic …
491
executing most of them, especially the PrPe03RZK007 procedure (Gallery excava-
tion under foundation) which stands out with 17 points, see Table 23.5. The elevated 
risk of this procedure is due to the risk of burial of the operator who conducts the 
gallery excavation. The comparison of the results of the risk assessment broken down 
into procedures can be consulted in Table 23.5. The sub

sequent solution that stands out for its level of risk is the solution solved 
with micropiling with 67 points, since it requires a greater number of procedures 
for its execution. In contrast, the unit cost with the highest level of risk is located in 
the solution with mortar injection, whose unit cost of 03WSW00001 (Consolidated 
ground column with pressurised cement slurry injections) by itself accumulates 41 
risk level points, see Fig. 23.11. This elevated risk is due to the fact that all of its 
procedures present a high level of risk, between 10 and 11 points each (see scoring 
of the procedures in Table 23.5). Fig. 23.11 Comparison of risk assessment results: in terms of unit prices and complete construction

23.6 
Discussion 
The results obtained show that the elements that most signiﬁcantly affect the ecolog-
ical impact are the construction materials due to its embodied resources, although 
machinery fuel consumption and workforce are also major factors. The EF is the 
only indicator which takes into account the impact of labour. From the economic perspective, the micropile foundation exerts the highest impact 
of all the solutions evaluated, whose social indicator is also high, following on the tail 
of the traditional reconstruction of the foundation. The traditional solution involves 
an elevated risk of the worker being buried by soil displacement during the repair 
work. Strategies that reduce the EE of construction materials can reduce CF and EF 
signiﬁcantly. From a technical point of view, the results obtained conclude that, by using IRS 
micropiles, under emergency conditions, the underpinning of the foundations on soft 
soils improves the soil characteristics (also using grout injections) and transfers the 
loads to resistant soil layers. From its economic dimension, the traditional solution remains the cheapest. The 
environmental results conclude that despite being the most economical, its environ-
mental cost is higher than that of polyurethane injections due to an intense use of 
labour. Finally, in this case study, polyurethane injections are considered the optimal 
solution when all three aspects are considered: social, environmental, and economic. 23.7 
Conclusion 
The foundation rehabilitation of a 40-dwelling building, having been subject to 
an accident, is studied from a social, economic, and environmental perspective. The project budget information, more speciﬁcally the work breakdown structure 
facilitates the evaluation of all impacts. For the ﬁrst time, the footprint family indicator

s are employed to analyse a foun-
dation repair in order to identify the materials, machinery, and labour that exert high 
environmental impact. The indicators are sensitive to the differences between the 
four solutions studied herein. The application of the proposed procedure has proved 
to be effective to address technical, economic, social, and environmental aspects 
simultaneously, when a work breakdown system is applied. From the solutions evaluated, the cheapest solution is the traditional restructuring. Its environmental indicators are also on the lower side, but its social indicator is the 
highest due to the risk of suffocation during the excavation. The micropilling is the 
most expensive and its social indicator is also high. Also, it CF is the second highest. But is the most commonly employed due to technical aspects and availability of the 
equipment. The underpinning of the foundations on soft soils improves the soil char-
acteristics and transfers the loads to resistant soil layers. For a better understanding,

498
A.

Ferreira-Sánchez et al. interrelations between variables and indicators need to be established in a uniﬁed 
indicator. In future research, a database of repair work and its classiﬁcation is to be deﬁned 
in order to assess all activities taking place in any building reconstruction. To this end, 
social, economic, and environmental impacts must accompany each construction unit 
selected in the project. References

Chapter 24 
LCA Analysis and Strategic Scenarios 
for the Reduction of Gas Emissions 
from Concrete Constructions: A Case 
Study 
Federica Cucchiella, Chiara Marchionni, and Marianna Rotilio 
Abstract The construction industry plays a key role in future sustainability strate-
gies, as it is responsible for 39% of global carbon dioxide emissions. To counteract the 
impact generated by the construction sector, it is necessary to direct efforts to make 
more informed design choices. Although many studies have shown that dry construc-
tion systems are able to reduce emissions during the entire life cycle compared to 
traditional on-site construction, there are circumstances in which the latter must 
necessarily be used. In the present research, after having identiﬁed a gap in the liter-
ature between studies that investigate how to reduce the environmental impact of 
such constructions, the Life Cycle Assessment analysis of a case study is proposed. In particular, the case of a multi-stored building which uses the cast-in-situ rein-
forced concrete construction system as an executive choice is analyzed. For the LCA 
analysis the SimaPro software was used and the data obtained lead to identifying the 
impact categories in the respective pre-use, use and end-of-life phases, through the 
ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint (H) v.1.05 method, to determining which of these is more 
impactful and worthy of attention. The study therefore proposes strategic scenarios 
to mitigate polluting emissions, particularly in the phase of greatest impact, i.e. the 
pre-use phase. F. Cucchiella 
Department of Industrial and Information Engineering and Economics, University of L’Aquila, 
L’Aquila, Italy 
C. Marchionni (B) · M. Rotilio 
Department of Civil, Construction-Architectural and Environmental Engineering, University of 
L’Aquila, L’Aquila, Italy

F. Cucchiella et al. 24.1 Introduction 
The construction sector is responsible for 40% of global energy consumption [1], 
causing approximately 39% of global greenhouse gas emissions (GHG), 12% of 
global drinking water use: for these reasons it is considered the most responsible 
for the changes climate [2–4]. Regarding these, the construction sector is also called 
upon to reduce the quantity of construction and demolition waste, which represents 
33.5% of that produced in Europe [5]. Achieving the decarbonization objectives of 
the Paris Agreement (COP21) on climate change, i.e. zero net carbon emissions in 
the construction sector by 2050, requires that strong attention be paid to the construc-
tion front by adopting development concepts capable of taking natural resources into 
account, from the early stages of the project a

nd the surrounding environment. The 
current critical environmental issue requires the construction sector, therefore, to 
identify design solutions capable of reducing the consumption of resources and the 
production of waste with the assumption of the time horizon of the entire life cycle, 
starting from the consideration of energy and matter ﬂows [6]. In fact, while the 
linear economy focuses on the limited life span of resources without considering the 
products end of life [7], the circular economy (CE) promotes a sustainable built envi-
ronment with increased efﬁciency for construction resources and waste minimization 
at construction stage and end of life [8]. Reference [9] assert as this is why the CE 
has attracted increased attention as one of the most powerful moves towards sustain-
ability. However [10] underlines as the concept of CE is still relatively new in the 
building construction industry (BCI): [11, 12] study as the CE concept for building 
construction and demolition waste (BCDW) emerged in 2014. Núñez-Cacho et al. [13] show as CE is regarded as an important alternative to the linear economy that 
deﬁne unsustainable: the need for a shift from a linear approach to a CE clarify the 
recent attention given to it, also determined by the most recent European directives 
[14, 15], which aim to close the circle of the material cycle. The CE takes part in 
the objectives of the green economy, understood as an economy “with low carbon 
content, with efﬁcient use of resources and socially inclusive” [16] and is outlined 
as a subcategory of the same, acting as the main center of interest is the efﬁciency in 
the use of resources and the reduction of waste production [17, 18]. Also managing 
the built environment according to the principles of the CE with the aim of limiting 
energy consumption and environmental pollution is a highly topical issue and digi-
tization can play a crucial role in promoting virtuous scenarios [19]. However, the 
concept of circularity applied to concrete cases has struggled to take hold, due to 
the complexity of the method: [20] study that more than 100 hundred articles were 
published on the topic in 2016, compared to only about 30 articles in 2014. Life 
cycle Assessment analysis (LCA) is a systematic methodology that evaluates the 
environmental footprint of a construction, from the extraction phases of the raw 
materials that constitute it, to its construction, to its use, up to its ﬁnal disposal. By 
quantifying the environmental impacts associated with its life cycle, it has a strategic 
role in identifying critical aspects. Nußholz et al. [21] in their research investigates 
65 real-life circular building cases of new build, renovation, and demolition to the

24
LCA Analysis and Strategic Scenarios for the Reduction of Gas …
505
understanding of the application and decarbonisation potential of circular strategies 
in the building industry. Cai et al. [22] studies the lifecycle GHG and reduction 
p

otential of traditional cast-in-place building and prefabricated light-steel building 
using LCA method. Aye et al. [23] assesses the embodied greenhouse gas emissions 
of circular building and conventional building elements, to demonstrate with envi-
ronmental data as the business model has the potential to reduce gas emissions. Also 
[24, 25], using the LCA methodology, prove as prefabricated constructions provides 
improved environmental performance over traditional in-situ concrete construction. In the Australian context [26] evaluate the environmental impacts of various concrete 
types, including innovative alternatives, using a computational LCA model. Results 
show as recycling 60% of concrete demolition waste further decreases environmental 
impacts by over 20%, aligning with CE principles and supporting resource recovery. The main bibliography previously discussed is shown in Table 24.1. However, in 
cases where the use of traditional concrete construction is essential, it is necessary 
to develop strategies to evaluate the environmental impact of this material, improve 
its performance and adopt construction strategies that help to increasing the life of a 
concrete structure [27]. This study proposes a methodology based on the application of LCA analysis 
divided into four phases, in compliance with the international standards EN ISO 
14040 and ISO 14044, and the subsequent analysis of the most impactful phases, to 
which strategies are applied for reduction of GHG emissions. The study is expressed 
through the evaluation of the environmental impact produced by a speciﬁc case, a 
newly built three-level underground concrete car park with a garden roof, in order to 
direct the design process towards more sustainable choices. The following paragraphs 
illustrate the results of the four phases on which an LCA analysis is based, the 
conclusions aim to summarize the results obtained, to identify mitigation strategies 
and to identify future research developments. 24.2 Materials and Methods 
The circular perspective imposes a design that considers the entire cycle of mate-
rials, from their origin (extraction of the raw material) to their disposal [28]. The LCA 
analysis allows us to consider the effects of the transformation actions of the built 
environment by considering the ﬂows of resources and environmental impacts along 
all phases of the life cycle, from the extraction of raw materials to their transformation, 
from production to assembly and installation, from marketing to use, from mainte-
nance to end-of-life management. The evaluation of the environmental sustainability 
of the project is linked to the deﬁnition of environmental reference thresholds (bench-
marks) which allow measuring the level of sustainability of a material, a system and 
a building designed within the building processes considered [29]. In accordance 
with the international standards UN EN ISO 14040 [30] and UN EN ISO 14044 [29] 
the current LCA study was di

vided into four phases, as shown in the Fig. 24.1:

Fig. 24.1 The four phases of a life cycle impact assessment 
1. Goal and scope deﬁnition of the LCA: it is the phase in which the objective of the 
study is deﬁned, the functional unit (i.e. the reference unit with respect to which 
all energy consumption and resources and the

related environmental impacts will 
be accounted for) the boundaries of the system (i.e. the processes and aspects to 
be included in the study based on its rationale for the study and any constraints 
induced by the data) the categories of data to be collected and analysed. 2. Life Cycle Inventory Analysis (LCI) phase: it is the most delicate and demanding 
phase, fundamental for the subsequent life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) 
phase. It involves the quantiﬁcation of input and output data relating to the system

508
F. Cucchiella et al. under study; in it the data necessary to achieve the objectives of the study are 
collected through archiving in an inventory table. The data collected can have 
different origins: primary source, i.e. data collected directly at the production 
sites; secondary source, literature data whose source must be reported (publi-
cations, manuals, previous studies, etc.); tertiary source, data obtained from 
estimates or technical coefﬁcients, for which the calculation method must be 
indicated. The data collected and processed constitute the basis of calculation 
for the assessment of the impact of the life cycle. 3. Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) phase: the EN ISO 14044 standard deﬁnes 
the LCIA as the “LCA phase aimed at understanding and evaluating the extent 
and signiﬁcance of the potential environmental impact of a product system”. The 
purpose of the impact assessment phase is to use the data relating to energy and 
resource consumption, coming from the LCI phase, to build some environmental 
impact indicators. The purpose of this phase is to determine the system’s potential 
effects on the environment by linking inventory data to speciﬁc impact categories, 
providing additional information to help evaluate a product system’s LCI results 
to better understand their environmental signiﬁcance. 4. Life Cycle Interpretation phase: life cycle interpretation is the ﬁnal stage of the 
LCA procedure, in which all results are summarized and discussed as the basis 
for conclusions and decisions in accordance with the objective. In particular, 
in this phase, in addition to analyzing the results and therefore identifying any 
improvement actions for both the study and the production processes analyzed, a 
series of checks are carried out: completeness check on the data and information 
to verify that they are available and complete; data consistency check, in terms 
of differences in data source, accuracy, technological coverage, age of data and 
geographical coverage; sensitivity check, i.e. sensitivity analyzes are carried out 
in order to evaluate the percentage error of the study and therefore the inﬂuence 
of any estimates, hypotheses or assumptions. The LCA methodology allows to understand where the greatest opportunities are 
located for reducing environmental impacts during the entire life cycle: once the 
phases with the greatest impact have been identiﬁed between pre-use, use and end-
of-life, the researc

h opens up scenarios several linked to greater attention to the 
performance of the material, to its supply, to the identiﬁcation of the most suitable 
materials depending on their origin and production. 24.2.1 
Goal and Scope Deﬁnition 
The object of interest of this paper is the possibility of reducing the environmental 
impacts of traditional reinforced concrete construction. To this end, for the case study 
a project was selected relating to the construction of a three-storey underground car 
park with a garden roof serving the Coppito Campus of the University of L’Aquila, 
which was sized to have a gross ﬂoor area equal to 5155.38 m2, a total height of

24
LCA Analysis and Strategic Scenarios for the Reduction of Gas …
509
9.50 m and a total volume of 48,976.11 m3. The project was evaluated through an 
LCA analysis performed with SimaPro software version 9.3.0.2, which allowed the 
environmental performance of the project to be monitored. The processes that were considered in the case study can be traced back to the 
following phases:
• extraction of raw materials;
• production of the materials making up the car park;
• transport of materials to the construction site;
• construction of parking lot;
• parking use phase;
• demolition of the car park;
• recycling/disposal of materials. The functional unit to which the LCA results were referred is the entire car park. In 
reference to the Technical Standards for Construction (Ministerial Decree 17 January 
2018), a useful life of 50 years has been envisaged. 24.2.2 
Inventory Analysis (LCI) 
In the second phase of the study, the LCI was carried out, quantifying all the materials 
making up the car park and the related processes for their creation. The datasets were 
obtained mainly from the Ecoinvent v3 database, integrated with input data obtained 
from the literature and manufacturing companies. The model was divided into the 
following components: foundation slab, pillars, bulkhead, walls, intermediate ﬂoors 
and ramp, roof slab. The choice of construction materials was conducted with a 
“transformation” approach, i.e. using data inclusive of production inputs and not of 
transport on site, to obtain clear information on the exact origin of the raw materials 
involved in the creation of each product. Pre-use Phase

to the construction site was also considered. An average distance of approximately

explain Table 24.10. Use Phase 
In the use phase, the energy needs linked to the lighting of the underground car park 
were considered. For the modeling in SimaPro, watertight LED lighting ﬁxtures 
with a power of 26 W were chosen. The installation of 50 lighting ﬁxtures for each 
ﬂoor integrated with presence sensors with a daily consumption of 60 kWh was 
hypothesized. All consumption was reported in the model with reference to the 
assumed useful life of 50 years for a total of 1103.76 MWh (Table 24.11). End of Life

Life Cycle Impact Assessment and Interpretation 
(LCIA) 
Following the inventory analysis, the LCIA of the underground car park was 
performed. To construct the life cycle of the artefact, the following phases were 
considered:
• Assembly: contains a list of all the raw materials or semi-ﬁnished products (with 
relative quantities) necessary for the realization of the project and the processes 
that deﬁne the energy consumption for the assembly itself. • Life cycle: contains a link to the assembly deﬁned above and to the processes 
relating to the pre-use, use and end-of-life phases of the product. There are various LCIA methods for conducting an LCA analysis, such as 
CML2001, Eco-indicator 99, and ReCiPe [31]. In any case, this phase is complex 
due to the large number of impact categories to be examined. For the environmental 
impact assessment, the ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint (H) method in version 1.05, created 
by RIVM, Radboud University, Norwegian University of Science and Technology 
and PRé Sustainability [32] and the IPCC 2013 method, developed by Intergovern-
mental Panel on Climate Change, which contains IPCC climate change drivers with 
a 100-year time horizon. ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint (H) v1.05 Method 
In this paper the ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint method [33] was used, chosen for some 
fundamental aspects: as underlined [34]  “it contains the broadest set of midpoint 
impact categories; enables impact categories to implement characterisation factors

at an international scale; and does not include potential impacts from future extrac-
tions in the impact assessment and assumes that they have been included in the 
inventory analysis”. The chosen method includes two levels for evaluation as shown 
in Fig. 24.3: 
Fig. 24.3 The Endpoint method phases: it deﬁnes impact categories disaggregated at midpoint 
level and aggregate impact categories at the endpoint level

24
LCA Analysis and Strategic Scenarios for the Reduction of Gas …
517
• a problem-oriented approach, which deﬁnes impact categories disaggregated at 
midpoint level;
• a harm-oriented approach, which deﬁnes aggregate impact categories at the 
endpoint level. This method is considered the most recent and advanced, combining the approach 
based on the midpoint of the CML-IA and the endpoint of the Eco-indicator 99, 
methods recognized worldwide. At an intermediate level, 22 impact categories are 
considered, such as global warming, particulate formation, ecotoxicity, etc. At the 
endpoint level, these categories are multiplied by damage factors and aggregated into 
three endpoint categories:
• Human Health;
• Ecosystems;
• Resources. The ReCiPe method outlines ﬁve steps in calculating life cycle impact: characteri-
zation, damage assessment, normalization, weighting, and single scoring. According 
to ISO 14040 standards, the only mandatory phase is characterization, while the 
others are optional. For greater clarity, the initial characterization phase and the ﬁnal 
single scoring phase are reported in the results, leaving out the intermediate phases 
of damage assessment, normalization and weighting. The results relating to the characterization phase are listed in Fig. 24.4, divided by 
impact category, expressed in the respective units of measurement and in percentage 
terms. By analyzing the data relating to impacts, it is possible to observe in the Fig. 24.5:
• in the pre-use phase, the most relevant impact categories are “Human carcinogenic 
toxicity” (99.3%) and “Mineral resource scarcity” (99.3%), both ma

inly linked 
to the extraction of raw materials necessary for construction of the parking lot. • in the use phase, the most relevant impact categories are “Stratospheric ozone 
depletion” (12.54%) and “Water consumption, human health” (10.81%), linked 
to the production of electricity. • in the end-of-life phase, the most important category is “Terrestrial acidiﬁcation” 
(18.48%) mainly caused by the transport of materials to waste treatment sites. The results are reported in Fig. 24.6, in the form of tables and graphs, in terms of 
scores. It can be seen that in all damage categories, the most impactful phase is the pre-
use phase with a total damage equal to 452.14 kPt, as show Fig. 24.7. Within the 
pre-use phase, the most impactful damage category is “Human health” with a score 
of 434.6 kPt. The pre-use phase contributes 94.7% to the overall damage, the use 
phase 2.9% and the end-of-life phase 2.34%. Below is the impact assessment relating 
to the pre-use phase only. Analyzing the pre-use phase, the greatest impact is attributed to the foundation 
slab with approximately 172 kPt (38.1%), followed by the bulkhead with 99.1 kPt 
(21.9%) and the intermediate ﬂoors and ramp with 85.1 kPt (18.8%); while the minor

Analysing the pre-use phase, the Fig. 24.11 illustrate as the greatest impact comes 
from the foundation slab for 38.5% (5.52E6 kg CO2-eq), followed by the bulkhead

The concrete production process appears to have the most impact from the results of 
the impacts processed, both with the ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint (H) method and with 
the IPCC 2013 GWP 100° V1.03 method. 24
LCA Analysis and Strategic Scenarios for the Reduction of Gas …
521
Fig. 24.7 Analysis of the pre-use phase—ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint (H) V1.05 method—single score
In fact, analyzing the impacts due to the pre-use phase with the ReCiPe 2016 
Endpoint (H) method, it is clear that concrete production is the predominant one 
with 233 kPt (51.6%). This is due on the one hand to the high environmental load 
associated with the production of the material, and on the other to the quantity of 
concrete used in the car park. While in the use phase the impact is due only to 
the use of electricity with 14 kPt (100%) to power the lighting of the underground 
car park. In the end-of-life phase, however, the greatest impact is attributed to the 
transport (55.4%) of the materials to waste treatment sites, followed by the electricity 
(24.9%) used for the recycling and reuse of the materials after the decommissioning 
of the car park. Even with the examination of the results of the analysis performed 
with the IPCC GWP method, the greatest impact is attributed to the pre-use phase 
with 1.43E7 kg CO2-eq (94.7%). In particular, from the contribution analysis, the 
concrete process is also the most impactful for this method, with 73%, corresponding 
to 1.04E7 kg CO2-eq. In order to reduce impacts and improve the green footprint of the concrete industry, 
the scientiﬁc community is making a contribution through the study of design choices

and alternative solutions to be put in synergy during the processing phase of the main 
material of traditional constructions. There are numerous strategies that can be used 
to decarbonise cement production. To date, those on which researchers agree and 
which are also starting to make headway in the industrial world are the replacement 
of clinker, the use of alternative fuels, the improvement of production kilns, the 
capture and storage of carbon [35]. Mikulčić et al. [36] in their studies, through the 
LCA methodology, demonstrate that in 2050 cement can be produced with signiﬁcant

524
F. Cucchiella et al. reductions in CO2-eq emissions compared to 2020 through the replacement of clinker 
(42%), the use of alternative fuels (25%) or the improvement of kiln efﬁciency (12%); 
if combined, these measures could reduce CO2-eq emissions. of cement production 
by approximately 58%. To reduce the negative impacts of concrete production while maintaining the 
engineering properties, alternating raw materials such as recycled aggregates and 
supplementary cementitious materials are encouraging [37, 38]. For example, some 
studies have evaluated the use of rubberised concretes: [39] and [40] show that there 
is a promising future for the use of waste tire rubber as a partial replacement for 
aggregate in cement concrete for the purpose of environmental sustainability and in 
the production of pervious concrete [41]. Others with the addition of textile materials 
[42], or recycled [43, 44]. For example, [45] design a concrete panel with integrated 
mineral wool produced with recycled material deriving from glass, silica, which 
allows the reduction of environmental impact through the reduction of embodied 
energy and carbon as well as the production of waste. Also [46] developed a method-
ological approach to produce a new panel for use in construction and use the LCA 
methodology to make assessments related to the reduction of environmental impacts. [47] presents the comparison, in environmental and economic terms, of the cradle-
to-gate life cycle of concrete with coarse natural and recycled concrete aggregates. By analyzing the environmental impact of 216 concrete mixes

from 24 references, the 
results show how using recycled coarse aggregates from concrete can signiﬁcantly 
reduce environmental impacts and costs. Furthermore, promoting a greater share of alternative fuel use, leading to 
cleaner cement production, could signiﬁcantly reduce sustainable concrete’s carbon 
emissions and energy consumption. 24.4 Conclusions 
This study aims to provide awareness of the environmental impact deriving from 
a speciﬁc project, the construction of a underground car park with garden roof, to 
highlight how the choice of materials and the construction process inﬂuence envi-
ronmental emissions. The application of the LCA methodology made it possible to 
highlight the greatest impact in the pre-use phase, which includes the processes for 
the extraction of raw materials, for the construction of the product and for the energy 
necessary to power means and equipment of worksite. Although signiﬁcant impacts 
have emerged in the concrete and steel production processes, in the end-of-life phase 
the impacts appear to decrease drastically through recycling treatments and disposal 
of waste in landﬁll for subsequent reuse. The research leads to reﬂections regarding design choices, the use of innovative 
materials (such as concrete with the addition of aggregates) and the reduction of 
consumption in concrete production factories, to reduce consumption and improve 
environmental sustainability. Life cycle analysis has proven to be a reliable tool for 
assessing the real sustainability of the product, allowing environmental critical issues

24
LCA Analysis and Strategic Scenarios for the Reduction of Gas …
525
to be highlighted in the choice of materials, avoiding moving from one phase of the 
cycle to another. Finally, it is underlined how the availability of an accessible and 
updated database of building materials relating to the Italian context would allow to 
increase the reliability and signiﬁcance of the results obtained. Acknowledgements Part of the study is funded by the European Union - NextGenerationEU

Chapter 25 
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime 
Concrete: Analyzing Methodology 
and Environmental Impacts 
Ilija Bošković 
Abstract The construction industry is responsible for signiﬁcant greenhouse gas 
emissions, leading to climate change. Utilising more environmentally sustainable 
building materials is a key strategy to improving the overall sustainability of the 
construction industry. Bio-based building materials have been identiﬁed as poten-
tially more sustainable alternatives to conventional materials as they can make use 
of renewable raw materials and can sequester carbon dioxide during the life cycle 
of the material. Hemp-lime concrete is a composite bio-based material formed by 
combining the chopped woody core (shives/hurds) of the industrial hemp plant with a 
lime-based binder and water. This study analyzes the approaches taken by prominent 
studies on the life cycle assessment of hemp-lime concrete. An overview of the life 
cycle of a typical in situ hemp-lime concrete wall is presented. Key factors including 
functional unit properties, carbon sequestration factors and end of life scenarios are 
discussed. The environmental impacts of the life cycle of the material and the rela-
tive impacts of various phases and processes are analyzed and discussed. Finally, 
key uncertainties in the life cycle of hemp-lime concrete are identiﬁed and sugges-
tions for improving the accuracy of hemp-lime concrete life cycle assessments are 
presented. 25.1 
Introduction 
The construction industry is responsible for signiﬁcant energy use, resource depletion 
and greenhouse gas emissions. Utilising more sustainable building materials is one 
of the strategies, the construction industry can implement to reduce the negative 
environmental impacts associated with its activities. With the pressing need to reduce 
the negative impacts of climate change, utilising building materials with a low life 
cycle global warming potential is crucial. Novel bio-based building materials can be 
a more sustainable alternative to the traditionally used mineral building materials, as
I. Bošković (B) 
Faculty of Architecture, University of Belgrade, Belgrade, Serbia 
e-mail: ilija.boskovic@arh.bg.ac.rs 
© The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025 
D. A. H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series 
in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_25 
529

530
I. Bošković
they utilise renewable raw materials that can sequester carbon dioxide (CO2)  from  
the atmosphere through photosynthesis. However it is also important to consider 
the negative impacts on other environmental phenomena such as eutrophication, 
acidiﬁcation, stratospheric ozone depletion, photochemical ozone formation and the 
depletion of mineral and fossil resources and water . Hemp-lime concrete or hempcrete is a prominent composite bio-based building 
material formed by combini

ng the chopped woody core (shives/hurds) of the indus-
trial hemp plant with a lime-based binder and water. It is a porous and vapour perme-
able non-loadbearing thermal insulation material most often used for the construction 
of exterior walls. One of the advantages of hemp-lime concrete is its versatility, as it 
can be applied in situ by casting it around a timber frame and it can also be prefab-
ricated into blocks or panels. In addition, it can also be used for the construction 
of ﬂoors and roof insulation. It was developed in the 1980s as a vapour permeable 
replacement for deteriorated wattle and daub in medieval timber buildings [1]. Thus, 
it can be used for historical restoration as well as in new construction and the energy 
renovation of existing modern buildings. The industrial hemp plant is a fast growing 
crop that can be used to create a variety of products including textiles, biocomposites, 
cosmetics, building materials, food, paper, biofuels and health supplements. Hemp 
shives are the byproduct of hemp ﬁbre extraction and are a readily available renew-
able biological resource with few competing applications, as the other primary use is 
for animal bedding. The key advantage of the material is that it can proved long-term 
storage of the CO2 sequestered by the industrial hemp plant during a short growth 
cycle of several months. Finally, studies [2–10] have shown that hemp-lime concrete 
exhibits positive hygrothermal performance and can delay the effects of oscillations 
in the exterior environment, thus maintaining interior comfort and reducing the need 
for active climate control. The environmental sustainability of hemp-lime concrete has so far been investi-
gated through published life cycle assessment studies [11–20] and commercial envi-
ronmental product declarations (EPD) [21–25]. As such the environmental impacts 
of various hemp-lime concrete products have been examined in several different 
geographical locations. In general, studies have analysed the life cycle of exterior 
hemp-lime concrete wall constructions. This study aims to analyse the life cycle 
of hemp-lime concrete wall constructions through a comparative analysis of the 
approaches taken by prominent published hemp-lime concrete life cycle assessment 
studies. An overview of the life cycle of a typical in situ hemp-lime wall construc-
tion is presented and key stages and unit processes are discussed in the context of 
the methodologies applied in existing studies. The relative impacts of unit processes 
and uncertainties in the life cycle of the material are identiﬁed and suggestions for 
improving the accuracy of hemp-lime concrete life cycle assessments are presented. 25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
531
25.2 
Method 
The framework for the study is comprised of key published life cycle assessments that 
examine the environmental impacts of functional units deﬁned as a 1m2 hemp-lime 
concrete wall construction. 25.2.1 
Func

tional Units and Key Scenarios 
Table 25.1 shows the characteristics of the primary functional units or key scenarios 
in the chosen studies. For most studies, the primary functional units was chosen for 
the comparison. For the scenarios from Sinka et al. [18] and Arehart et al. [20], 
where a variety of different scenarios were deﬁned with no primary functional unit 
the scenarios with the most similar physical characteristics and binder compositions 
are presented. In Heidari et al. [19] the functional unit is deﬁned as 1 kg of hemp 
shiv, thus the wall represented in the comparison is an additional scenario and not 
the primary functional unit in the study. All of the studies followed the life cycle assessment methodology deﬁned in ISO 
Standards 14040 [26] and 14044 [27]. The life cycle assessments were therefore 
carried out in four major phases: goal and scope, inventory analysis, environmental 
impact assessment and interpretation. The initial step of deﬁning the goal of the life 
cycle assessment entails the deﬁnition of the aim and reasons for carrying out the life 
cycle assessment. The scope primarily entails the deﬁnition of the product system to 
be analysed, the functional unit, system boundary and environmental impact cate-
gories through which the results of the assessment will be represented. The system 
boundary includes all the unit processes that will be analysed, while the functional 
unit is the reference unit connected to all the inputs and outputs of the product system 
[26]. Outside of the studies presented in Table 25.1, most functional units are also 
deﬁned as 1 m2 hemp-lime concrete wall constructions. Several EPDs [22, 24, 25] 
used a functional unit deﬁned as a 1 m2 hemp-lime concrete block wall construc-
tions, while others took a different approach analysing the life cycle of 1 m3 of in situ 
hemp-lime concrete [20] and 1 m3 of prefabricated blocks [23] respectively. Addi-
tionally, Rivas-Aybar, John and Biswas [28], analysed the life cycle of a different type 
of hemp-based material, by deﬁning a functional unit of 1 m2 of novel hemp-based 
prefabricated board. While all of the scenarios presented in Table 25.1 have the same height and length 
their thicknesses vary due to the deﬁnition of different thermal conductivities and U-
values. The density of hemp-lime concrete can vary due to differences in hemp shiv 
bulk density, the ratio of hemp shiv to binder used when preparing the composition 
and the level of compaction applied when applying the material, thus variations in the 
deﬁned densities are to be expected. Variations in density can also be assumed to be 
one of the primary reasons for variations in the deﬁned thermal conductivity. Studies 
have shown that increasing the density of the material negatively impacts its thermal

Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
535
conductivity [29, 30]. Prefabricated blocks [31], also typically have lower thermal 
conductivities, as unlike with in situ applications, the material can be compacted in 
the same direction as thermal loading [31, 32]. In Bošković [11] and Bošković and 
Radivojević [9, 11] the thermal conductivity of the hemp lime concrete was deﬁned 
using the density-dependent formula deﬁned in Cerezo [29], while Arehart et al. [20] 
used the formula deﬁned in Collet and Pretot [30]. The varying thicknesses in the 
wall constructions ultimately therefore result in the scenarios having different inputs. Most of the analysed studies deﬁne the life span of the material as 100 years, 
though a lower value of 60 years is also used. Hemp-lime concrete buildings are still 
relatively young, with the ﬁrst use widely reported to be in 1986 in the renovation 
of an XVI century building (Maison de la Turque) in Nogent sur Seine. However, 
it is assumed that hemp/lime concrete wall construction can have an equivalent life 
span to conventional wall constructions [16]. Leonard and Groslambert [24] deﬁne 
a life cycle of 60 years according to the Belgian standard for EPDs, but they state 
that hemp-lime concrete can have a life span of up to 150 years or the life span of 
the building. Commercial producers also advertise a life span that is as long as the 
service life of the building [33]. As it is a vapour permeable material, hemp-lime 
concrete is able to absorb and desorb moisture preventing the excess build-up of 
moisture in the wall. The high alkalinity of the lime-based binder protects the hemp 
shives from pests and microbial deterioration and increases the ﬁre resistance of the 
material. However, some microorganisms can grow in alkaline conditions and the pH of the 
binder will fall as a result of carbonation [34, 35]. Experimental

research has shown 
that if the material is produced properly, with an adequate binder, microorganisms 
may form only under extreme conditions. Walker et al. [36] kept samples of hemp-
lime concrete in hot and humid conditions (30 °C, RH = 80%) for 7 months and 
directly inoculated them with microorganisms (Aspergillus and Penicillium fungi 
and Bacillus bacterium) on several occasions. The samples experienced no physical 
deterioration and microorganisms didn’t develop. In Marceau et al. [37] microorgan-
isms didn’t develop in samples that were subjected to 8 wetting and drying cycles 
(over 75 days), but did develop in samples that were kept under very hot and humid 
conditions (30 °C, RH = 98%) for several months and the pH dropped under 10. Obuka et al. [38] found similar results. Directly inoculated samples kept for 45 days 
under very humid conditions (20 °C, RH = 99%) showed no sign of microorganism 
growth. However, samples with a lower ratio of binder in the mix with a pH under 9 
showed microbial growth. Delannoy et al. [39] concluded that though viable fungal 
cells may remain on the hemp shiv following retting and carbonation may reduce 
the pH of the binder, the relative humidity inside a coated wall is unlikely to be high 
enough for the period of time necessary for fungal growth to begin, and that no fungal 
growth should occur “without wrong manufacturing or accidental damages [40].” 
Experimental research has therefore shown that microorganisms cannot develop in 
natural conditions in which the material can dry after reaching a higher moisture 
content. 536
I. Bošković
In terms of durability, research has shown that the material can withstand 10 
[41] and 25 [42] freeze–thaw cycles without showing physical damage and signif-
icant negative impacts to the compressive strength of the material. It is important 
to note that all of these studies were undertaken with uncoated hemp-lime concrete. In practice the material will either be coated by a lime-based render or covered by 
a ventilated façade, providing the material with additional protection. Though it is 
important that an adequate coating is used as coatings that allow excess liquid water 
to pass into the material or prevent water vapour diffusion are likely to increase the 
relative humidity of the hemp-concrete wall and increase the risk of fungal growth 
[43, 44]. Applying the coating before the material has sufﬁciently dried can inhibit 
the drying of the wall [1, 9, 39]. The results of existing research and experiences 
from practice do not go against the assumption that the material, if formulated and 
applied correctly can have a life cycle of up to and possibly even over 100 years, 
though further published research testing the long-term durability of the material to 
conﬁrm this further would be welcome. 25.2.2 
System Boundary 
As can be seen in Table 25.1, the studies set varying system boundaries. As a 
minimum, all of the studies took into account production of

the raw materials (cradle 
to gate) and carbonation of the binder in the use stage [20]. Uncertainty regarding 
the end of life of the material is the primary reason behind the exclusion of the end 
of life stage in some of the studies. As a minimum, the studies that considered the 
end of life stage took into account transport of the wall construction materials to the 
disposal site [16]. All of the studies only took into account embodied environmental 
impacts, thus the impact of the material on operational energy and water use in the 
use phase was not considered. Almost all of the studies considered several sensitivity 
analyses within the system boundary. Table 25.2 shows all of the different sensitivity 
analyses undertaken so far. Additional sensitivity analyses aim to analyse the environmental impacts of varia-
tion in the life cycle of hemp-lime wall constructions. In Bošković [12] and Bošković 
and Radivojević [11, 13] an average (A), pessimistic (P) and optimistic scenario (O) 
is deﬁned by varying key variables that include the hemp sequestration factor, trans-
port distance, construction waste formation rate and carbonation factor. In Bošković 
and Radivojević [13] and Pretot et al. [16] the environmental impacts of applying 
various ﬁnishes to the wall construction are examined, as well as the impacts of 
various renewal regimes in the use phase. These variables were also examined in 
Bošković [12], as well as comparisons between in situ and prefabricated block appli-
cations and comparisons with typical construction practices. Among other sensitivity 
analyses, Arrigioni et al. [17] experimentally measured the degree of carbonation in 
a hemp-lime concrete block and examined the impact of varying the binder carbona-
tion factor. Sinka et al. [18] primarily examined the environmental impacts of using 
different types of binder. Arehart, Nelson and Srubar [20] also examined the impacts
