I. Bošković
They provide an insight into general principles that can be applied to improve the 
embodied environmental impacts of hemp-lime concrete constructions. 25.2.3 
Inventory Analysis 
The inventory analysis (LCI) is an iterative process that entails the data collection 
and calculation procedures quantifying the inputs and outputs associated with the 
product system [26, 27]. The four primary stages in the life cycle of the product 
system are the production, construction, use and end-of-life stages. The lifecycle 
inventory of a typical in situ hemp-lime concrete wall from cradle to grave is shown 
in Fig. 25.1. As all of the studies didn’t use the same life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) 
methodology and didn’t uniformly evaluate the same environmental impact cate-
gories, the relative impacts of stages and processes in the life cycle of hemp-lime 
concrete wall constructions will be primarily discussed in the context of scenarios 
A  [11, 13] and A+Flp [13] presented in Table 25.1. The studies by Bošković and 
Radivojević [11, 13] utilised the cut-off by classiﬁcation system model and the 
environmental impact categories were calculated using the Environmental Footprint 
impact assessment method conforming to EN15804:A2+2019 [28]. The ﬁrst study 
[11] evaluated the four global warming potential categories (GWPtotal, GWPbio-
genic, GWPfossil and GWPluluc), while the second study [13] also analysed ozone 
depletion potential (ODP), acidiﬁcation potential (AP), freshwater eutrophication 
potential (EPfresh), marine eutrophication potential (EPmarine), terrestrial eutroph-
ication potential (EPter), photochemical ozone formation potential (POCP), abiotic 
depletion potential (ADP) fossil depletion potential (ADPfossil) and water deple-
tion potential (WDP). Figure 25.2 shows the relative environmental impacts of the 
average uncoated

hemp-lime concrete wall (scenario A) shown in Table 25.1. Figure 25.3 shows the relative environmental impacts of a coated hemp-lime 
concrete wall (scenario A+Flp) shown in Table 25.1. Scenario A+Flp is constructed 
by applying a lime putty and sand ﬁnish (+Flp) to both sides of scenario A [13]. 25.2.3.1
The Production Stage 
The production stage includes the extraction of raw materials (A1), transport of 
the raw materials (A2) and manufacturing processes (A3). The production stage 
primarily includes the production of the hemp shiv and binder, as well as the timber 
frame and coating materials, where they are present. As can be seen in Figs. 25.2 
and 25.3, the production stage is responsible for most of the emissions and negative 
environmental impacts in both a coated and uncoated hemp-lime concrete wall. In 
the case of hemp-lime concrete blocks the production stage would also include the 
prefabrication processes, but production of the mortar and steel ﬁxings is likely to 
be attributed to the construction stage. 25

and sugarbeet required higher inputs of potassium (K). As a result of fertilization, 
ammonia (NH3) and nitrous oxide (N2O) are emitted to the atmosphere, while nitrates 
(NO3) and phosphates (PO4) are leached and discharged into water systems. At 
harvest, the plants are cut and left in the ﬁeld for roughly two to four weeks for the 
dew retting process to take place. Retting is a microbial process by which the chem-
ical bonds holding the stem of the industrial hemp plant together are broken down, 
allowing for the separation of bast ﬁbres from the woody core. During this period 
the straw should be turned several times to allow for even retting. Once the straw 
has been sufﬁciently broken down, it is baled and transported to the ﬁbre processing 
plant. At the processing plant, the ﬁbres are separated from the woody core through 
the decortication process. The primary product of the decortication process is hemp 
ﬁbre, while hemp shiv and dust are the byproducts. Based on the existing studies, 
hemp shiv is assumed to make up 47–75% of hemp straw (Table 25.3). As hemp shiv is a byproduct of industrial hemp ﬁbre production, it is appropriate 
to allocate only a certain percentage of the environmental impacts associated with 
growing and processing industrial hemp, to hemp shiv production. Environmental 
impacts can be allocated by mass, meaning most of the environmental impacts are 
attributed to hemp shiv production. Almost all of the existing studies took into account 
mass allocation, though some also considered economic allocation. As the ﬁbre is 
the primary and more valuable product, applying economic allocation reduces the 
environmental burden of hemp shiv production. In applying economic allocation 27– 
32% of the environmental impacts of hemp straw production and processing were 
ascribed to hemp shiv production [14, 18, 19]. Dust is typically considered waste, 
but it can be used to make fuel briquettes [15]. Existing studies therefore did not 
consider dust to be a waste product and didn’t allocate any of the environmental 
impacts of producing and potentially disposing of the dust to hemp shiv production. As the difference between mass and economic allocation can vary from 20 to 48% 
of the environmental impacts of hemp straw production and processing the choice of 
allocation method can have a signiﬁcant effect on the environmental impacts of hemp 
shiv production. Though it is worth noting that with economic allocation Boutin et al. [14] also assigned a lower CO2 sequestration factor. Whichever allocation method 
is used, the accuracy of the life cycle assessment will depend on using an accura

te 
hemp shiv mass value for the industrial hemp cultivar(s) in question or following 
up-to-date market trends. Though the environmental impacts of hemp shiv production will be context-
speciﬁc it can be argued that it can have a relatively low impact on the overall 
life cycle of a hemp-lime concrete wall. In Bošković and Radivojević [11] hemp shiv 
production was responsible for 6.35% of greenhouse gas emissions (GWPtotal) in 
the uncoated hemp-lime concrete wall (A), which was reduced to 4.15% when the 
same wall was coated with a hemp-lime putty render/plaster (A+Flp) [13]. Though 
in some studies hemp shiv production had a higher global warming potential (GWP), 
the relative impact was still far less when compared to binder production. Hemp shiv 
production had a higher relative impact on most other environmental impact cate-
gories. For scenario A in Bošković & Radivojević [13] hemp shiv production had a

25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
543
higher relative impact on other environmental impact categories such as acidiﬁca-
tion potential (AP = 22.47%), water depletion potential (WDP = 25.84%) abiotic 
depletion potential (ADP = 36.00%) and terrestrial eutrophication potential (Epter 
= 48.67%), primarily due to fertiliser production and use and the processing and 
packaging of hemp shiv. The environmental impacts of hemp shiv production will 
therefore primarily depend on the amount of fertiliser used and the efﬁciency of t he
decortication process. The hemp straw yield varies depending on the cultivar, growing conditions and 
agricultural practices. In Bošković [12] the environmental impacts of varying hemp 
straw yield were examined by varying an average hemp straw yield of 10t per hectare 
(ha) by ± 2.5 t (total yield of 7.5 t to 12.5 t per hectare) with the same fuel and 
fertiliser inputs. The study utilised the CML-IA (v4.8) impact assessment method-
ology. Global warming potential (GWP) had the highest relative variation and the 
5 t/ha yield differential accounted for 1.079kgCO2eq. The remaining environmental 
impact categories were affected to a lesser extent. The 5t/ha yield increase reduced 
acidiﬁcation potential (AP), photochemical ozone formation (POCP), ozone deple-
tion potential (ODP) and fossil resources depletion (ADPf) by around 2%. Eutrophi-
cation potential (EP) was reduced by 4.24%, while abiotic resource depletion (ADP) 
decreased by 13.79%. Though the beneﬁts are less pronounced when comparing 
the pessimistic and optimistic yields to the average scenario, it is clear that using 
higher yielding industrial hemp varieties that don’t require additional fertiliser inputs 
can have a positive environmental impact. However, as climate conditions that can 
affect yield can’t be controlled it’s also important to note that reducing the environ-
mental impacts of hemp shiv production through consistent yields may not always 
be possible. Carbon dioxide (CO2) sequestration is a crucial parameter to

take into account for 
the life cycle assessment of hemp shiv production. The existing studies in Table 25.3 
utilising mass allocation, use a range of values for the hemp shiv CO2 sequestration 
factor. The values range from 1.349 kgCO2/kg of hemp shiv [11–13] to 2.105 kgCO2/ 
kg of hemp shiv [14]. The carbon sequestration factor can be calculated with Formula 
(25.1), where MDM represents the mass of dry matter, CDM the carbon content, MMCO2 
the molar mass of carbon dioxide and MMC the molar mass of carbon [11, 12]: 
SqCO2 = MDM · CDM · MMCO2 / MMC
(25.1)
The CO2 sequestration factor can therefore be determined based on the carbon 
and moisture content in the hemp shiv. The carbon content will vary based on cultivar 
and growing conditions, as Sausserde and Adamovicz [43] measured carbon content 
values ranging from 41.62 to 45.38% and 41.33 to 43.94% for the same selection of 
10 cultivars in two consecutive years. Amziane et al. [44] examined the initial water 
content of hemp shiv and determined an average value of 10.9% with a standard 
deviation of 0.6%. Bošković [12] and Bošković and Radivojević [11, 13] examined 
the environmental impacts of varying the hemp shiv carbon sequestration factor. An average, pessimistic and optimistic scenario were deﬁned based on the lowest

544
I. Bošković
(41.33% [43]) and highest (47.4% [45]) carbon content values reported in the liter-
ature and an average moisture content of 11% based on the results from Amziane 
et al. [44]. With an average hemp shiv carbon content of 44.37% a CO2 sequestration 
factor of 1.448 kgCO2eq/kg hemp shiv at 11% moisture content was deﬁned for the 
functional unit (A). Based on the highest and lowest carbon content values from liter-
ature a hemp shiv a CO2 sequestration factor of 1.349 kgCO2eq/kg hemp shiv at 11% 
moisture content was deﬁned for the pessimistic scenario (P) and 1.547kgCO2eq/kg 
hemp shiv for the optimistic scenario (O). The results from Bošković and Radivo-
jević [11] indicated that variation of the CO2 sequestration factor had a signiﬁcant 
impact on global warming potential (GWP), as the 6.07% carbon content difference 
accounted for the emission of 5.943kgCO2eq. (0.198kgCO2eq. per kg of hemp shiv). Though carbon content is also likely to depend on climate conditions identifying 
and promoting industrial hemp cultivars that can sequester more CO2 (have a higher 
hemp shiv carbon content) can have environmental beneﬁts. The ability of the hemp plant to sequester CO2 is a key environmental beneﬁt of 
using hemp-lime concrete, but the variability of the CO2 sequestration factor, due 
to cultivar speciﬁcity and growing conditions, introduces additional uncertainty into 
the life cycle assessment of hemp-lime concrete that can have a large impact on the 
reported global warming potential value. Care should be taken not to overestimate the 
ability of the plant to sequester CO2. The most typically used CO2 sequestration factor 
of 1.84 kgCO2/kg of hemp shiv use

d in some existing studies [17–20] would have 
to assume a carbon content of around 50% and a moisture content of 0%. Based on 
currently available data for hemp shiv carbon content [43, 45–48] this appears to be an 
overestimation. Additional context-speciﬁc experimental data for the carbon content 
of hemp shiv would be valuable to show which cultivars have can sequester more 
biogenic carbon. However, the most crucial parameter is to accurately determine for 
how long the sequestered CO2 can be stored in the material. This primarily depends 
on the life span of the wall construction, whether the material can be successfully 
recycled at end of life and organic carbon degradation at ﬁnal disposal. Binder Production 
Hemp-lime concrete generally utilises a lime-based binder which is primarily made 
up of hydrated lime (CL90s), (60–80%) that provide a slow set over time through 
carbonation, and hydraulic components that provide a faster initial set through 
hydration (20–40%). The hydraulic component is typically comprised of naturally 
hydraulic lime (NHL3.5 or NHL5) and various pozzolans (metakaolin, GGBS etc.), 
though some scientiﬁc studies have also used Portland cement [17, 20, 34, 49, 50] 
and “natural cement binder is also available commercially [51]. Most of the studies 
used commercial binder Tradical PF70 or a binder based on it in either the base case 
[11–16] or sensitivity analyses [17, 18]. The full composition of Tradical PF70 is 
a commercial secret, but its composition is deﬁned as 75% hydrated lime (CL90s), 
15% natural hydraulic lime and 10% pozzolana. In Bošković [12] and Bošković and 
Radivojević [11, 13] the pozzolan was deﬁned as metakaolin. Sinka et al. [18] and

25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
545
Arehart et al. [20] also examined binders that contained metakaolin. The primary 
purpose of the study by Sinka et al. [18] was to examine the environmental impacts 
of magnesium phosphate cement (case MPC 0.15/0.50) and magnesium oxychloride 
cement binders (case MOC 0.15/0.50). Arrigioni et al. [17] also deﬁned an alterna-
tive binder for the base case, as a binder containing 80% dolomitic lime and 20% 
portland cement was used, while Heidari et al. [19] used an alternative commercial 
binder (Tradical Thermo). Arehart et al. [20] examined the largest number of binder 
variations, by comparing the environmental impacts of 36 different mixes comprised 
of hydrated lime (50–80%) and either natural hydraulic lime (20–50%), portland 
cement (10–25%) or metakaolin (40–50%). Mix 20 deﬁned in Table 25.1 used a 
binder composed of 65% hydrated lime and 35% natural hydraulic lime. Regardless 
of binder composition, all studies found that binder production was the single most 
environmentally damaging process in the the life cycle of hemp-lime concrete. Hydrated lime production starts with the extraction and preparation of limestone. The crushed limestone is then burned in a kiln at temperatures of 900–1200

°C typi-
cally using fossil fuels as the energy source. During calcination calcite (CaCO3)  in  
the limestone is turned to quicklime (CaO) which releases large amounts of CO2. The  
quicklime is then milled and slaked with water resulting in hydrated lime (Ca(OH)2). The process of producing natural hydraulic lime is less energy intensive, as tempera-
tures of 950–1050 °C are required for the calcination of calcareous marl and less CO2 
is released due to the lower calcite content in the stone. However, it still involves the 
burning of fossil fuels and releases CO2 directly into the atmosphere during calci-
nation. Pozzolans are typically favoured over portland cement as they can be natu-
rally occurring and can require no thermal activation (e.g. pumice) and will almost 
certainly require much less energy if they do require calcining (e.g. metakaolin). Pozzolans can also be the byproducts of other industrial processes (e.g. GGBS, ﬂy 
ash etc.), thus allowing for the reuse of waste or less desirable products. The envi-
ronmental impacts of binder production will be dependent on its composition as well 
as the efﬁciency of the lime kiln and the type of fuel used. In Bošković and Radivojević [13], binder production is the single most impactful 
in all of the environmental impact categories other than terrestrial eutrophication 
potential (EPter) and abiotic depletion potential (ADP) in both the coated (A+Flp) 
and uncoated (A) wall constructions. Binder production was responsible for 64.36% 
of all greenhouse gas emissions (GWPtotal) in scenario A and 42.07% in scenario 
A+Flp. However it is important to state that part of the CO2 emitted during calcination 
will be sequestered due to carbonation in the use phase. Therefore the overall impact 
of binder production will be somewhat reduced depending on the rate of carbonation. Carbonation is the process by which the material hardens, as hydrated lime (calcium 
hydroxide, Ca(OH)2) in the binder reacts with CO2 from the atmosphere to form 
calcium carbonate (CaCO3). It is a process that occurs during the use stage, but will 
be discussed here to cover the overall environmental impact of the binder. The degree of carbonation depends on ambient conditions (temperature and rela-
tive humidity) and is deﬁned as the percentage of the CO2 released during calcina-
tion that is reabsorbed. Studies in general assumed that CO2 will be able to dissolve 
through the whole thickness of the wall and the deﬁned degree of carbonation can

546
I. Bošković
be achieved during the relatively long life cycle of the material. In addition, if the 
assumed degree of carbonation is not met during the use stage it is likely to be 
achieved at the end of life, when the material is crushed and the surface area in direct 
contact with air is increased [12]. The amount of CO2 released during calcination 
depends on the composition of the raw material being calcined and how much of it is 
transformed. However, the binder carbonat

ion factors used in the studies primarily 
vary due to differences in binder composition and assumed degrees of carbonation. Studies used a degree of carbonation of 75–100% for hydrated lime and 40–75% 
for natural hydraulic lime. In an extensive literature review, Despotou et al. [49] 
found a complete degree of carbonation of 80–92% for hydrated lime. Thus, using 
a degree of carbonation of 100% may be an overestimation. The degree of carbona-
tion for natural hydraulic lime is generally considered to be lower, as the process is 
more complex. Natural hydraulic lime has a lower amount of “free lime” (Ca(OH)2), 
available for carbonation as the calcium oxide (CaO) present in the material is also 
found in the hydraulic mineral phases (primarily belite). During hydration, the belite 
reacts with water forming calcium-silicate-hydrate (CSH) and additional Ca(OH)2 
available for carbonation. In Bošković and Radivojević [11, 13] carbonation of the binder in the use stage 
reduced the total global warming potential (GWPtotal) of the binder from + 50.699 
kgCO2eq to between + 23.579 and 30.239 kgCO2eq, depending on the binder carbon-
ation factor. Binder carbonation is therefore a key factor in reducing the global 
warming potential of the material. However, just as with the hemp shiv carbon seques-
tration factor, overestimating the carbonation factor can skew the results. The long 
predicted life span of the material and crushing it at the end of life should ensure a high 
degree of carbonation that can offset some of the greenhouse gas emissions of binder 
production. In scenario A, binder production also had a particularly high impact on 
ozone depletion potential (ODP = 69.36%) and the depletion of fossil resources 
(ADPf = 61.39%) [13]. Due to the high overall environmental impacts of binder 
production, developing binders with lower emissions in the production stage would 
be very advantageous and could further improve the environmental sustainability of 
the material. Timber Production 
As hemp-lime concrete is a nonloadbearing material all in situ hemp-lime walls 
must be cast around a timber frame. This is typically a timber stud frame utilising 
untreated softwood studs placed at 40–60 cm centres. Most of the in situ hemp-
lime wall constructions shown in Table 25.1 include two timber studs. Hemp-lime 
concrete blocks are also nonloadbearing, but do not require a timber substructure for 
support and can thus be used independently to form non-loadbearing walls or used as 
inﬁll for concrete, steel and timber frame structures. Arrigioni et al. [17]  as  well  as  all  
of the environmental product declarations analysing hemp-lime block constructions 
therefore don’t include any timber frame elements. Sinka et al. [18] and Heidari et al. [19] also included the steel ﬁxings required for constructing the timber stud frame. As

25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
547
timber is also a bio-based material it sequesters CO2 in

the production stage, which 
can be stored until the end of life. The CO2 sequestration factor will depend on the type 
of timber used and can be calculated using Formula 25.1. Usually untreated softwood 
timber is used. In the life cycle of scenario A [13] timber stud production impacted 
freshwater eutrophication potential (EPfresh = 22.2%), acidiﬁcation potential (AP = 
15.62%) and water depletion potential (WDP= 13.35%) the most, but had a relatively 
low impact on greenhouse gas emissions (GWPfossil = 3.99%). In the case of coated 
wall construction A+Flp, timber production was responsible for 2.61% of greenhouse 
gas emissions [13]. Thus, while the timber frame is a key element of the in situ wall 
construction it typically has a low relative environmental impact. Finishes 
Studies have primarily focused on the material itself, analysing the life cycle of 
uncoated hemp-lime wall constructions. The external surface of a hemp-lime concrete 
wall needs to be covered with a vapour permeable ﬁnish to protect the wall from 
damage, prevent excess moisture ingress and complement the hygrothermal prop-
erties of the material [1]. Lime-based renders and plasters are commonly used as 
they are vapour permeable and allow inﬁltrated water to evaporate [52]. Alterna-
tively ventilated façade systems can also be used and they are typically ﬁnished with 
timber cladding. So far studies have examined the environmental impacts of applying 
lime-based renders and plasters [11, 13, 15, 16] and ventilated façade systems with 
timber cladding [11, 13]. Production of the ﬁnish materials is attributed to the produc-
tion stage. In Bošković and Radivojević [13] production of the lime putty render 
and plaster for the initial application has a much lower relative impact than binder 
production, due to the much lower amount of lime required, but it had a higher rela-
tive impact than hemp shiv production in several environmental impact categories 
(GWPtotal, ODP and ADPfossil). Lime putty render and plaster production for the 
initial exterior and interior contributed to 8.88% of greenhouse gas emissions, but 
the overall impact of the ﬁnish materials depends on the amount of maintenance and 
the number of replacements required in the use stage. The durability of the applied 
ﬁnish materials is therefore also an important parameter to consider, alongside the 
environmental impacts of the initial production. 25.2.3.2
Construction Stage

the installation of the hemp-lime concrete wall construction. Table 25.4 shows the 
inputs of primary raw materials and the transport distance to site in the analysed

Transport to Site 
The transport distances deﬁned in the studies vary as they are dependent on geograph-
ical context. All of the studies assumed the use of lorries to transport the hemp shiv, 
binder, timber and ﬁnish materials to the site. In the life cycle of an uncoated hemp-
lime concrete wall construction (A), transporting the hemp shiv, timber (EURO 5, 
7.5-16t lorry) and binder (EURO 5, 16-32t lorry) 125 km impacted abiotic resource 
depletion potential (ADP = 13.72%), ozone depletion potential (ODP = 8.52%) and 
photochemical ozone depletion potential (POCP = 8.17%) the most [13]. Trans-
port of the raw materials contributed to only 3%/of total greenhouse gas emissions 
(GWPtotal). As transport distances are context-speciﬁc, deﬁning the overall relative 
environmental impact of transport can be difﬁcult as they can vary signiﬁcantly. Increasing the transport distances to 180 km [11, 13] only slightly increased the 
relative impact of transport in the construction stage on greenhouse gas emissions 
(GWPtotal = 4.2%). However, with a more signiﬁcant increase to 500 km for each 
raw material, the relative impact of transport on global warming potential (GWPtotal) 
increases to 10.95%. As the binder is a specialized product currently produced by a 
small number of commercial producers that makes up most of the mass of the wall 
construction, its transport has the highest potential of negatively impacting the life 
cycle of hemp-lime concrete. Using the framework of the same study [13], if the 
transport of the hemp shiv and timber remained 125 km, but the binder is transported 
1600 km (EURO 5, > 32 t lorry) from a producer in France, the relative impact of 
transport on global warming potential increases to 11.59%. Long gate to site transport 
distances can therefore have a notable negative impact on global warming potential 
and offset some of the inherent environmental advantages of the material. However 
if the materials can be sourced locally, transport from gate to site has a relatively low 
effect on most environmental impact categories. Installation on Site 
Installation of the wall entails erecting the timber stud wall and formwork, mixing 
the hemp-lime concrete and casting it in the formwork. The mixing proces

s requires 
inputs of water and electricity. In situ hemp-lime concrete can be cast by hand place-
ment or spraying. Hand application is a more manual process, whereby the hemp 
shiv, binder and water are usually mixed in a pan mixer and transferred in buckets or 
other containers and placed in the formwork by hand. Spray application is a mecha-
nized process in which the hemp shiv and binder slurry are mixed at the nozzle of the 
sprayer and sprayed onto the surface of a permanent shuttering board or wall. The key 
parameter to consider during construction is the amount of material and packaging 
waste that will be produced on site. Most existing studies didn’t consider construc-
tion waste formation. Where construction material waste formation was considered, 
a wastage rate of 0–10% was examined [11–13]. Thus the construction stage also 
includes the production and transport of the hemp shiv, binder and timber neces-
sary to replace the materials lost in transport or on site. Following installation the

550
I. Bošković
waste materials and packaging are transported to a disposal site. The waste hemp 
and timber waste will also contribute to the sequestration of CO2. However, to avoid 
waste formation having a positive impact on global warming potential it would be 
appropriate to not take this into account and where possible reuse the biobased waste 
for another purpose or incinerate it to provide energy. Binder waste will have the 
greatest impact, though some of the waste hydrated lime will inevitably undergo 
carbonation to a certain degree, depending on where it is lost or disposed of. At 
a 5% wastage rate constructing in scenario A impacted freshwater eutrophication 
(EPfresh = 13.95), water depletion potential (WDP = 11.77%), acidiﬁcation poten-
tial (AP = 10.86%/7.95%) and abiotic resource depletion (ADP = 10.48%). The 
global warming potential can vary notably as at 0% waste formation construction 
of the wall had a relative impact of 1.58% on global warming potential (GWPtotal), 
which rose to 8.97% at a 10% waste formation rate. While achieving 0% waste is 
highly unlikely in practice improving construction practices and reducing waste can 
improve the environmental performance of the material. 25.2.3.3
Use Stage 
All of the studies assumed that the hemp-lime concrete would not require any main-
tenance or refurbishment during the life span of the wall construction and that the 
binder would undergo carbonation [12–20]. The environmental impacts of carbona-
tion were discussed in the Binder Production section. Studies that analysed the life 
cycle of uncoated hemp-lime concrete wall constructions therefore didn’t attribute 
any negative environmental impacts to the use stage. The studies that analysed wall 
constructions with applied ﬁnishes [12, 13, 16] took into account the replacement of 
the ﬁnish materials at their end of life. The use stage therefore includes the produc-
tion and transport of the replacement materials and the d

isposal of the old materials 
and packaging. The environmental impacts associated with the use stage therefore 
depend on the life span of the ﬁnish materials and the number of exterior and inte-
rior replacements required during the life cycle of the hemp-lime concrete wall 
construction. In practice, the life span of ﬁnish materials will vary due to varying 
environmental conditions, material quality and user preference. As the life span of 
lime render is typically 25–40 years [53, 54] the studies [12, 13, 16] took into account 
various renewal scenarios. In Bošković and Radivojević [13] varying renewal regimes 
resulted in the use stage having an impact of 18.99–27.81% on global warming poten-
tial (GWPtotal) in scenario A+Flp. When the initial production of the ﬁnish materials 
is also factored in, it was found that the lime coating for the hemp-lime concrete 
wall construction can be responsible for over a third of greenhouse gas emissions 
(GWPtotal = 27.87–36.69%). Ultimately applying a lime putty coating increased 
the global warming potential of the wall construction from 0.135kgCO2 to between 
21.747kgCO2 and 30.273kgCO2. Applying and renewing wall ﬁnishes therefore has 
a pronounced effect on the overall life cycle environmental impacts of a hemp-
lime concrete wall and should not be ignored when evaluating the environmental 
performance of the material. 25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
551
25.2.3.4
End of Life 
As the end of life strategy for the material hasn’t been widely developed in practice, 
the end of life stage is still relatively unknown. The key uncertainty that needs to be 
determined is the carbon storage potential of the material at the end of the life of the 
hemp-lime concrete wall construction. It is important to determine how the material 
can be recycled and reused to extend the period of carbon storage. Ip and Miller 
[15] suggest that at end of life hemp-lime concrete can be reused as a lightweight 
aggregate, an aggregate for construction blocks or to adjust the pH of soil. It is also 
crucial to determine the degradation mechanisms at ﬁnal disposal to determine how 
much, if any, of the initially sequestered carbon can remain stored in the material if 
it is landﬁlled. Pretot et al. [16] assumed that the lime binder would make the hemp 
shives inert, preventing organic carbon degradation and the emission of greenhouse 
gases at end of life. A static or dynamic approach may be taken when accounting for biogenic carbon 
in the life cycle of building materials. In a static approach, the “0/0” or “− 1/ + 1” 
approaches are typically used. The “0/0” or “carbon neutral” approach assumes that 
all sequestered biogenic carbon will be released at end of life, so the movement of 
biogenic carbon is not considered at all. The “− 1/ + 1” approach tracks the ﬂow 
of biogenic carbon through the life cycle of the building material, accounting for 
sequestration in the production stage, but also assum

ing that all of the sequestered 
carbon will be released at the end of life stage. A criticism of the static approaches is 
that they don’t take into account the timing of the carbon emissions and the rotation 
periods as they relate to biomass growth [55]. As a result, dynamic approaches have 
been developed to factor in the impact of time [56–61]. All of the existing studies 
deﬁned in Table 25.1 essentially take a static approach, but by not considering the 
end of life phase or assuming the material remains inert, most studies have essentially 
taken  a  “− 1/0” approach [62]. In this approach, all of the carbon sequestered by the 
hemp and timber remains stored in the material, which can lead to an overestimation 
of the environmental performance of the material and overall wall construction. At end of life the hemp-lime wall construction will be demolished and the mate-
rials will be transported to ﬁnal disposal. Studies typically considered that the 
entire construction will be landﬁlled with no further emissions. Alternatively, in 
Bošković [12]  in  Bošković and Radivojević [11, 13] the hemp-lime concrete and 
timber construction were assumed to be separated on site and treated separately. The 
timber was assumed to be incinerated releasing all of the initially sequestered carbon 
dioxide (“− 1/ + 1” approach) and producing energy to be used in another product 
system. The hemp-lime concrete was assumed to be recyclable and would therefore 
be sent to a recycling centre where it would be crushed, sorted and prepared for reuse. In scenarios A and A+Flp the hemp-lime concrete was also assumed to be inert at the 
end of life (“− 1/0”). In this scenario, 16.57% (A) or 11.64% (A+Flp) of greenhouse 
gas emissions take place at the end of life stage due to the incineration of the timber. Though the greenhouse gas emissions are higher than in the construction stage, most 
of these emissions are neutralised by sequestration in the production stage. 552
I. Bošković
In a sensitivity analysis, the hemp-lime concrete was considered to be landﬁlled 
at end of life and would release greenhouse gases as a result of organic carbon 
degradation [11–13]. Data regarding the degradation of organic carbon in the hemp-
lime concrete in ambient or landﬁll conditions at the end of life is not yet available. Environmental product declarations for hemp-lime block products [22, 24] take into 
account biodegradation after landﬁlling and the release of CO2 and methane (CH4) 
at the end of life. They used a fraction of degradable organic carbon dissimilated 
(DOCF) of 15%, thus 15% of the organic carbon in the hemp shiv was assumed to 
degrade at the end of life. This value was deﬁned based on French conventions for 
timber products in general and therefore may not be reﬂective of the actual behaviour 
of hemp-lime concrete under landﬁll conditions. The default DOCF for wood waste 
prescribed by the IPCC methodology is 50% [63], though experimental investigations 
sug

gest this value is an overestimation. Through laboratory simulations, Wang et al. [64] determined a DOCF of 0.1–1.8% 
for softwood lumber and 7.8% for red oak lumber, while DOCF of values of 0–9.5% 
values were determined for pine branches and 17.1–28.5% for oak branches [65]. Ximenes et al. [66] found similar results when examining samples from various 
landﬁlls in Australia. Factors such as ambient conditions, the ratio of lignin and 
holocellulose in the plant, lignin type and the type and amount of extractives inﬂuence 
the degradation of organic carbon [64, 66]. It can be assumed that the DOCF of 
hemp shives would be higher than in pine or spruce, as hemp shives have a lower 
lignin content and higher holocellulose and lignin ratio. However, without extensive 
experimental data it is difﬁcult to predict how much the lime binder would inhibit 
degradation. As such Bošković [12] and Bošković and Radivojević [11] analysed the 
end of life greenhouse gas emissions of various DOCF values used for wood products 
in literature (1.5% [67], 10% [68], 15% [22, 24], 23% [69] and 50% [63]). It was 
assumed that 50% of the DOCF would be dissimilated as CO2 and 50% as CH4 and 
that the landﬁll site would have a CH4 recovery system in place with a collection 
efﬁciency (CE) of 75% and a destruction efﬁciency of 99.9%. Landﬁll sites need to 
have an efﬁcient CH4 recovery system in place, as CH4 has a much higher global 
warming potential than CO2. Burning the captured methane can produce energy to 
be used in another product system and ensures that the captured CH4 is emitted 
as CO2, reducing the environmental impacts of organic carbon degradation. In the 
Environmental Footprint impact assessment method, the characterisation factor used 
for 1 kg of CH4 is 36.75 kgCO2eq.. It was determined that with a low to moderate 
DOCF and an efﬁcient methane recovery system not all of the CO2 sequestered in 
the production phase would be emitted at the end of life. Assuming a DOCF of 15% 
and CE of 75% it was found that 35.86% of the biogenic carbon sequestered in the 
hemp shiv would be emitted at end of life. This would decrease to 23.91% with a 
DOCF of 10% and increase to 54.98% with a DOCF of 23%. However, with a lower 
CE of 50%, the amount of sequestered biogenic carbon released at end of life would 
vary from 37.81 to 86.7% at the same DOCF values. The degradation of hemp shiv 
can therefore result in high greenhouse gas emissions at end of life and have a large 
impact on the global warming potential (GWPtotal) impact category, as biogenic 
carbon sequestered in the production stage is released. Composting, as suggested

25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
553
by Heidari et al. [19] may be the environmentally preferable option, but would also 
result in greenhouse gas emissions. Due to the lack of experimental data and the 
high variability of potential greenhouse gas emissions, the end of life stage is a large 
factor of uncertainty

in the life cycle of hemp-lime concrete. 25.2.3.5
Environmental Impact Assessment and Interpretation 
Table 25.5 shows the results of the environmental impact assessments carried out by 
the studies. Only the results of the global warming potential indicator are shown as it 
is widely considered to be the most important impact category and is the only impact 
category that is present in all of the studies. In addition, though the studies used 
different impact assessment methodologies that use some different characterisation 
factors, the unit for the impact category is the same (kgCO2eq.). Results highlighted 
as “()*” [11–13] show the results of the scenario with an alternative end of life 
strategy that assumes the hemp-lime concrete is landﬁlled and degrades at the end 
of life with an assumed DOCF of 15% and CE of 75% (+DOCF=15%). Though the 
described differences in the functional units/scenarios, system boundaries and impact 
assessment methods make direct quantitative comparisons of the results non-viable, 
contextualising them can help identify trends and the impact of variations in the 
approaches and system boundaries. It can be seen that the base scenarios and scenarios with similar binder composi-
tions (hydrated lime 65–75%, natural hydraulic lime 15–35% and pozzolans 0–10%) 
in all of the studies have carbon negative or close to carbon neutral global warming 
potential. It was determined that global warming potential could be as low as − 
36.08 kgCO2eq . [15]. However, it is important to note that all of the studies with the 
lowest results analysed uncoated wall constructions and assumed that all of the CO2 
sequestered by the hemp and timber frame would stay stored at the end of life and 
some also used potentially overestimated CO2 uptake factors. This can be seen when 
comparing the results of scenarios P and O [11, 13] that are variations of the same 
uncoated hemp-concrete wall construction. Both scenarios assume that the hemp 
shiv stays inert at end of life, but scenario P uses lower CO2 uptake factors, longer 
transport distances and takes into account the production of construction material 
waste in the construction stage. The results of scenario O are carbon negative and 
in line with many other existing studies, while scenario P emits more greenhouse 
gases than it sequesters. This demonstrates the potentially large variations in global 
warming potential that can be caused by external factors. Other variations in the life cycle of the wall construction can also cause the global 
warming potential to become positive. Sinka et al. [18] demonstrated that by using 
alternative binders such as magnesium phosphate cement or magnesium oxychlo-
ride cement instead of a commercial hemp-lime concrete binder, the global warming 
potential becomes positive even within a cradle to use system boundary. Similarly, 
treating the hemp shiv with sol gel [19] also made the global warming potential 
indicator positive. Applying ﬁnish ma

terials to the wall construction signiﬁcantly 
increases global warming potential. Applying lime putty coatings (+Flp) to scenario

A increases the global warming potential by 25.413 kgCO2eq. and the global warming 
potential of optimistic scenario O became positive (O+Flp). In Pretot et al. global 
warming potential remains negative despite the applied lime ﬁnishes, but thinner 
coatings and less interior renewals were deﬁned compared to scenario O+Flp [13]. Taking into account alternative end of life scenarios that consider the degradation 
of hemp shiv further increases global warming potential. In Bošković and Radi-
vojević [13] the global warming potential of a pessimistic coated wall construc-
tion construction scenario that assumes hemp shiv degradation at the end of life 
is 54.665 kgCO2eq (P+Flp+DOCF=15%.). This is 90.745 kgCO2eq. higher than the 
life cycle global warming potential of the primary functional unit in Ip and Miller 
[15], demonstrating how much differing unit process inventories and system bound-
aries can impact the ﬁnal result and the conclusi

performance of the material. 25.3 
Discussion 
Analysing the relative impacts of the unit processes in the life cycle of a hemp-lime 
concrete wall construction it is apparent that binder production overall has the most 
negative environmental impacts. Binder production also releases the most greenhouse 
gases in both uncoated and coated scenarios, though part of the CO2 released during 
carbonation can be sequestered through carbonation. A high degree of carbonation 
can be assumed due to the long expected life span of the material and end of life 
treatment, carbonation may still be variable in practice. Therefore using mixes with 
a lower binder content where possible and potentially developing binders with lower 
negative emissions in the production stage can notably improve the environmental 
performance of the material. However, Sinka et al. [18], demonstrate that some 
alternative binders can be signiﬁcantly more environmentally damaging than the 
typically used commercial binders. Hemp shiv and timber production have a much 
lower relative impact in most environmental impact categories compared to binder 
production. Heidari et al. [19] showed that applying treatments to hemp shiv can also 
have a very adverse effect on the environmental impacts of hemp shiv production. Deﬁning the relative impact of gate to site transport distance can be difﬁcult as 
transport distances are context dependent, thus the relative impact of transport can 
vary signiﬁcantly. With very high transport distances transport to the site can have 
a more negative impact than hemp shiv and timber production on global warming 
potential, but it will still have a much lower impact than binder production. The 
relative impact of installing the wall construction on site can be relatively low, but 
will primarily depend on the amount of construction material waste formed during 
transport and installation. Comparing the results of pessimistic and optimistic scenarios P and O [11, 13] 
in Table 25.5 it is clear that variations to unit processes in the production, construc-
tion and use stages can signiﬁcantly impact global warming potential. The results 
vary by 19.861 kgCO2eq. and controlling the variables may not be fully possible in

25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
557
practice. Having a low hemp sequestration factor can be caused by cultivar choice or 
unfavourable growing conditions, transport distances will be dictated by geograph-
ical context and the availability of raw materials, high material wastage can be 
caused by poor construction site practices or unforeseen events and a low degree of 
binder carbonation can be caused by unconducive ambient conditions and end of life 
disposal. The ability of the material to sequester CO2 as a result of the photosynthesis 
of the industrial hemp plant and the carbonation of the binder is a signiﬁcant advan-
tage. However it is important to take into account the po

tential impact of suboptimal 
uptake factors. Comparisons with other wall constructions would help contextualize 
the performance of the material in the other environmental impact categories. Most studies analysed the life cycle of uncoated hemp-lime concrete wall 
constructions. As the exterior surface of a hemp-lime concrete wall must be protected 
in practice, thus current studies have primarily focused on the materials and not on 
complete wall constructions. Considering the life cycle of coated or covered wall 
constructions is therefore crucial to fully understanding the environmental impacts 
of using the material. In Bošković and Radivojević [13], though producing the hemp-
lime putty coatings had a relatively low impact, the environmental impacts of the use 
stage were comparable to binder production in many of the environmental impact 
categories (Fig. 25.3). Thus, it is important to not only consider the environmental 
impacts of producing the ﬁnish materials but also their durability, as high maintenance 
and renewals can have a high relative impact on life cycle emissions. In Bošković & 
Radivojević [13] applying lime putty ﬁnishes somewhat changed the environmental 
perception of the material in the average (A) and optimistic (O) scenarios, as with 
the added coatings the global warming potential went from almost carbon neutral or 
carbon negative, to carbon positive. Applying the coatings also had a more negative 
impact on global warming potential than just applying the deﬁned hemp shiv degrada-
tion scenario (+DOCF=15%)  [11, 13]. However, with a higher fraction of dissimilated 
organic carbon or a lower methane collection efﬁciency the degradation of hemp shiv 
at the end of life can have an even greater impact. The sequestration of CO2 by the hemp plant is considered the primary advantage 
of hemp-lime concrete. However, the ﬂow of biogenic carbon in the end of life phase 
is the greatest factor of uncertainty in the life cycle of the material. Typically in 
traditional LCA with a static approach (“0/0” or “− 1/ + 1”) no biogenic carbon can 
be assumed to be stored after end of life. All of the existing studies however have 
taken a “− 1/0” or partial approach, assuming that all or some of the biogenic carbon 
will remain stored in the material after ﬁnal disposal. Taking a “− 1/ + 1” approach
in scenario A+Flp [13] (Table 25.5) and assuming that all of the biogenic carbon 
sequestered in the production stage is emitted at the end of life, the global warming 
potential would increase from 25.548 kgCO2eq. to 68.896 kgCO2eq. For comparison, 
in Bošković [12] the global warming potential of a typical masonry wall construction 
in Serbia (thermo blocks + ETICS façade system), with an equivalent U-value and 
life span, was determinrd to be 108.845 kgCO2eq.. Thus even though the global 
warming potential of the hemp-lime concrete wall may still be signiﬁcantly lower 
than for the conventional masonry wall, the perception of the mater

ial is signiﬁcantly 
different when compared to the carbon negative global warming potentials presented

558
I. Bošković
in existing studies. Applying this static approach somewhat nulliﬁes the advantage 
of sequestering CO2 and ignores the stages of the life cycle when the emissions 
take place. Therefore it may be more appropriate to examine the life cycle of the 
material using a dynamic, rather than static approach. Even if it is assumed that all 
of the biogenic carbon is released at the end of life, hemp-lime concrete still has 
signiﬁcant advantages when the temporal dimension is also considered. Pittau et al. [70] highlighted that fast growing bio-based materials such as hemp and straw can 
provide effective mitigation to global warming due to their short growing cycle. The 
hemp-lime concrete should be able to store the biogenic carbon for 100 years (or 
more if successfully recycled) and as hemp has a short growth cycle the biomass is 
replaced quickly. The “ + 1/− 1” approach typically assumes that the biological material will be 
incinerated at the end of life. This may not be practical in the case of hemp-lime 
concrete, as incinerating the material may calcinate some of the hardened binder 
releasing additional CO2 into the atmosphere. Thus the primary strategy at the end 
of life should be to recycle as much of the material as possible to extend the period 
of biogenic carbon storage. Unless new technologies or strategies are developed, at 
practical end of life composting or disposal in a landﬁll would likely be preferable 
to incineration. It is currently unknown to what extent the lime binder may be able to 
inhibit the degradation of organic carbon in the hemp shiv. To be able to accurately 
model the end of life phase it is therefore important to experimentally examine the 
degradation mechanisms of the material when composted and in landﬁll conditions 
and ﬁnd to what extent the degradable organic carbon can be expected to dissimilate 
and how much biogenic carbon, if any, can be stored beyond the life cycle of the 
material. Once this data is available the uncertainty factor of the end of life phase can 
be mitigated and the overall accuracy of hemp-lime concrete life cycle assessment 
studies can be impro ved. 25.4 
Conclusion 
The life cycle of hemp-lime concrete wall constructions was examined by analysing 
the approaches taken by existing hemp-lime concrete life cycle assessment studies. The studies deﬁned varying system boundaries and only some considered the life 
cycle from cradle to grave. Most studies considered the material to have a life span 
of 60–100 years and the durability of the material against microbial growth, freeze– 
thaw cycles and accelerated aging has been experimentally veriﬁed. The relative 
environmental impacts of the unit processes were examined and it was determined 
that binder production was the primary hotspot and the most negatively impactful 
unit process. As a result, the production stage

is responsible for most of the envi-
ronmental impacts, though the relative impacts of the remaining life cycle stages 
showed potentially high variability. The relative impact on the construction stage 
depends primarily on gate to site transport distances and construction material waste 
formation. 25
Life Cycle Assessment of Hemp-Lime Concrete: Analyzing …
559
Studies primarily focused on the material itself and few analysed the impacts of 
coating or covering the hemp-lime concrete wall construction. As such the use stage 
in most studies only included carbonation of the lime binder. However, when a lime 
putty coating is applied to the wall the environmental impacts of the use stage can 
be comparable to binder production in many environmental impact categories. The 
environmental impacts associated with the use stage depend on the environmental 
impacts of producing and transporting the ﬁnish materials and the number of replace-
ments necessary throughout the life cycle of the material. The ability of the material 
to sequester CO2 through photosynthesis of the hemp plant and carbonation of the 
binder is an important advantage of the material that mitigates greenhouse gas emis-
sions in the production and use stages. As such some studies determined the global 
warming potential of the material to be carbon negative, though care should be taken 
not to overestimate the CO2 uptake of the material, by using overoptimistic sequestra-
tion and carbonation factors. The end of life stage is the primary factor of uncertainty 
in the life cycle of the hemp-lime wall constructions, as disposal strategies have not 
yet been fully developed and data regarding organic carbon degradation of hemp shiv 
is not yet available. As such it is difﬁcult to estimate the expected greenhouse gas 
emissions at the end of life and determine how much, if any, of the biogenic carbon 
can remain stored for a signiﬁcant period of time after the end of life. To accurately 
model the end of life stage of hemp-lime concrete wall constructions, future research 
into the material should include experimental studies examining the degradation of 
organic carbon in hemp-lime concrete in landﬁll conditions and when it is composted. In the absence of this data using a dynamic approach for biogenic carbon accounting 
may be preferable to the static approach utilised in all of the analysed studies. References

Chapter 26 
Lime for the Built Environment 
Marco Simoni, Cecilia Pesce, and Theodore Hanein 
Abstract This chapter offers a general overview of the lime industry in relation to 
the built

environment. The vital role of lime in cement making is also discussed. The production of lime from limestone calcination is carbon and energy-intensive 
requiring more than 3000 kJ per kg of lime and with processing temperatures around 
1000 °C. In cement making, even higher temperatures (1600 °C) are required for 
the formation of the targeted calcium-rich clinker phases. More than half of the 
carbon emissions from lime making arise due to the process chemistry where calcium 
carbonate is thermally decomposed into calcium oxide and carbon dioxide gas. The 
use of lime in construction dates back thousands of years; since then, the fundamental 
principles of lime production, based on thermal calcination, has not changed. This is 
mainly because, until recent breakthroughs, society believed this to be the only way. Although the calcination process is industrially robust and its efﬁciency has been 
improving over the years, process improvements alone are not sufﬁcient to achieve 
Net Zero and our climate goals. A description of novel approaches that avoid thermal 
calcination, and which have the potential of abating the carbon emissions from lime 
and cement manufacturing, are highlighted as they represent genuine solutions for the 
complete decarbonization of these industries that are crucial to our built environment. M.

Simoni (B) · C. Pesce · T. Hanein 
earth4Earth Technology Ltd., London W1W 7LT, UK 
e-mail: marco.simoni@earth4earth.co.uk 
C. Pesce

563

564
M. Simoni et al. 26.1 
Lime 
The term “lime” is usually referred to all those products derived from the calcination 
of different types of limestone distinguished by purity degree or rock source. The 
types of lime obtainable may be classiﬁed as: quicklime CaO, hard-burnt lime CaO, 
slaked/hydrated lime Ca(OH)2, and dolomitic lime (containing signiﬁcant amounts 
of Magnesium [1]). Due to the variability of the chemical and physical properties, 
stemming from the source and processing conditions used for its manufacturing, lime 
is a very versatile inorganic bulk chemical. Its setting properties were already well 
known in ancient civilisations, such as the Romans, Greek, and Egyptians, where 
it was mainly used in construction [2]. It is likely that the fundamental scientiﬁc 
reasons behind its properties were unknown, and arguably not still fully understood 
today, but that did not stop its widespread use contributing to the development of 
those civilizations. CaO is the direct product of the thermal calcination of limestone (mainly CaCO3) 
and is also a precursor for conventional cement manufacturing [3]. The calcination of 
CaCO3 (Eq. 26.1) is a highly endothermic reaction (requiring ~ 1800 kJ per kilogram 
of CaCO3 [4]). In Eq. 26.1,  the  CO2 released upon calcination is differentiated 
between fuel, i.e., arising from the combustion of the mostly fossil fuels used in the 
process, and raw material CO2, arising from the carbonate rock that is decomposed 
[5]. CaCO3(Solid) + heat energy → CaO(Solid) + CO2(Combustion) + CO2(raw material)
(26.1)
The physical and chemical properties of CaO are largely inﬂuenced by the temper-
ature, residence time, impurities content, and type of fuel used during manufacturing, 
leading to higher or lower reactivities and an array of potential applications. That is 
also valid for the derivatives of the quicklime, such as slaked lime, and limes obtained 
from different natural sources, such as dolomite, or even biogenic calcium carbonate 
such as chalk. The reactivity of the CaO can be assessed by exploiting the exothermic 
properties upon contact with water and monitoring the rate of temperature increase 
[6] or by adopting commonly used reactivity tests [7]. 26.2 
Lime and Cement Manufacturing 
Outside the cement industry, in 2023 about 430 Mt of lime were produced globally 
[8], of which 20–22 were in the EU. Such production rate indicates its extraordi-
nary physical and

chemical properties, which make it suitable for a wide variety of 
industrial applications. To truly grasp the vital importance that lime has in modern society, the cement 
industry must be considered. With over 4 Gt globally produced every year (180 Mt/

26
Lime for the Built Environment
565
y in the EU), cement is the most manufactured commodity worldwide [9]; it was 
found that the cement production rate of a given country is an accurate indicator of 
its development degree. In the near future, the global cement production is expected 
to increase in some underdeveloped/developing areas [10]. Global cement demand 
forecasting is essential to understand the past, the present, and the future of lime’s 
role in the global market and wider society. Lime is the main ingredient (50–60%) 
of ordinary Portland cement [11], and a key ingredient of most mineral binders used 
in construction, which still rely on the fundamental role of calcium in the overall 
binder chemistry. Conventional cement and lime manufacturing routes share the same preliminary 
steps prior to ﬁring such as: quarrying and crushing prior to calcination [12]. The 
composition of the limestone fed into the high temperature kiln may vary according 
to the speciﬁcs of the desired product but generally requiring ≥ 80 wt% (CaCO3 
+ MgCO3) [13]. Cement is typically manufactured in the long rotary kiln (LRK) 
conﬁguration, comprising the counter-current ﬂow of solid feed and combustion 
ﬂame, generally obtained by burning natural gas, coke, and waste, such as exhausted 
tires [14]. The solid feed is fed at the top of the 90 m long kiln unit, while both 
the rotation and inclination of the kiln ensure homogeneous mixing between the 
reactants and a sufﬁcient residence time allowing for the formation of the main 
clinker phases, namely: alite (Ca3SiO4), belite (Ca2SiO5), tetracalcium alumino-
ferrite (Ca4Al2Fe2O10), and tricalcium aluminate (Ca3Al2O6)  [15]. Notably, clinker 
refers to the indurated output products of the ﬁring in the rotary kiln, whereas the term 
cement applies once gypsum (5%) and other optional mineral additives or admix-
tures are blended with the clinker at the end of the processing [16]. The Long Rotary 
Kiln with Pre-heaters (PRK) conﬁguration reﬂects the most energy-efﬁcient option 
for clinkering, which was ﬁrstly introduced in the 1960s. Depending on the design 
considered [17], such as air-through or air-separate pre-calciners, up to the 40–95% 
of the raw feed can be decarbonized prior to entering the rotary kiln. It is essential that 
the formation of the clinker does not occur in the pre-calciner unit; for this reason, 
around 70% of the overall amount of fuel can be efﬁciently used in the pre-calciner 
unit [18] as the decarbonisation of limestone is much more energy intensive than 
clinker formation. Lime is the essential component for clinker making and subsequent cementi-
tious properties, since calcium ions are essential for the formation of the clinker

and 
hydration phases, whilst contributing to the adequate environment in the pore solu-
tion of the hardened cement to promote mechanical properties. Upon hydration of 
Portland cement, the gel-like structure of the main hydration phase, calcium silicate 
hydrate (known as C–S–H) readily forms, providing early strength and formation 
of micro-domains within the structure, where Ca(OH)2 precipitates and allows for 
the achievement of those alkaline conditions required for further reaction and hard-
ening [11]. Like lime hydration, the hydration of cement is also exothermic, mainly 
because of the formation of Ca(OH)2 [19]. In fact, like for lime, the reactivity of 
cement upon hydration is also monitored through heat release; the higher and faster 
the heat release, the more reactive the cement is considered. It must be noted that
