Le et al. analyses, and discusses ﬁndings from relevant studies identiﬁed in the existing liter-
ature. The results indicate that most existing studies are dedicated to assessing the 
environmental sustainability of speciﬁc materials, lacking those that evaluate the 
comprehensive sustainability of buildings. The sustainability assessment methods 
used in these studies, mostly life cycle assessments, have methodological issues 
that need to be addressed in future studies. Moreover, although circular bio-based 
building materials outperform their traditional counterparts in mitigating climate 
change and reducing production costs, their use does not always offer better economic 
and environmental sustainability. 28.1 
Introduction 
It is widely accepted that the construction sector has severe impacts on the envi-
ronment, particularly climate change and natural resource scarcity. This is because, 
ﬁrstly, only buildings contribute to nearly 35% of energy consumption, more than 
half (55%) of total electricity use, and nearly two-ﬁfths (38%) of greenhouse gas 
(GHG) emissions globally [1]. Secondly, the construction of only infrastructure and 
buildings in developing economies consumes up to half of the total amount of raw 
natural resources extracted for material purposes worldwide [2]. The construction 
industry is also well-known for the large amount of materials wasted, with around 
15% annually, and this ﬁgure is forecast to continue rising [1]. To address the aforementioned impacts attributed to the construction industry, 
many different solutions have been implemented. For example, to cut off GHG emis-
sions, scholars and practitioners have proposed and applied different technologies to 
improve buildings’ energy efﬁciency, such as air tightness, active shading systems, 
efﬁcient lighting, etc., and transition to renewable energy sources [3]. However, it is 
noted that most of the current solutions are focused only on reducing the operational 
impacts of buildings, even though the contribution of embodied GHG associated 
with the use of materials can be half of the total buildings’ life-cycle emissions [4]. Therefore, to achieve a sustainable built environment, it is necessary to address the 
embodied impacts of building materials. Among the solutions to reduce energy consumption, GHG emissions, and virgin 
natural resource dependence in the construction industry, using circular bio-based 
building materials emerges as a coherent approach. These materials are deﬁned as 
“materials wholly or partly derived from renewable biological origins, or by-products 
and biowaste of plant and/or animal biomass that can be used as raw building mate-
rials and decorating items in construction, in their original forms or after being repro-
cessed”  [5, 6]. There are strong links between these materials and circular economy 
strategies, for instance, “recycle” and “reduce”, according to the 9Rs framework [5, 
7]. The deﬁnition suggests that the input source

s of these materials should be circular 
and be retrieved from secondary sources. Consequently, using these materials can 
alleviate the demand for virgin materials in the construction industry. Furthermore,

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
601
adopting these materials can not only dramatically reduce GHG emissions associ-
ated with the construction industry and bring economic beneﬁts [5, 8], but can also 
offer great opportunities for transitioning the sector from the current linear model to 
a circular one. Although the term “circular bio-based building materials” and the deﬁnition of 
these materials have just been proposed recently [5], many kinds of them have been 
developed in previous practice and discussed in previous scientiﬁc studies. However, 
these studies are fragmented, and the materials themselves are diversiﬁed in size, 
composition, shape, function, and biological origins. This may confuse the adopters 
when considering the use of these materials, highlighting the need to clearly cate-
gorise and also indicate the potential use of these materials. Furthermore, since most 
of these materials are new and emerging, their adoption encounters various barriers 
and challenges. This is because the introduction of new materials to any sector of 
the economy has to face barriers, especially in a highly conservative and cost-driven 
sector like construction [9]. These barriers have to be investigated and addressed 
to promote the successful and widespread adoption of these materials. Alongside 
addressing the barriers, a better understanding of the pros and cons in terms of 
sustainability performance is essential to convincing stakeholders in the construc-
tion industry to uptake these materials. This information should be supported by 
sustainability assessments regarding the use of these materials. Despite this impor-
tance, there is still a lack of studies assessing the sustainability of circular bio-based 
building materials [6], since most studies on this topic have been dedicated to the 
development and performance testing of these materials. This highlights the need to 
ﬁgure out the status of assessing the sustainable performance of circular bio-based 
building materials. Because of these motivations, it is necessary to conduct a study 
to shed light on the categorisation of CBBMs, investigate their adoption barriers 
in both developed and developing economies, and evaluate the state-of-the-art of 
sustainability assessment of these materials. In order to address the aforementioned gaps, this chapter ﬁrst summarises the 
ﬁndings from deep analyses of 97 relevant studies identiﬁed from the existing liter-
ature to provide insights into the categorisation, potential use, and sustainability 
assessments of these materials. In particular, the relevant articles included in the 
literature review are identiﬁed from the Scopus and Web of Science databases and 
screened using inclusion and exclusion crit

eria. Only peer-reviewed articles written 
in English that focus on assessing the sustainability of circular bio-based building 
materials are selected. The literature review is carried out using systematic and critical 
approaches. Secondly, questionnaire surveys are adopted to investigate the adoption 
barriers of these materials in both developed and developing economies. To provide the research ﬁndings, the chapter is organised into ﬁve sections, 
including the introduction. Section 28.2 presents the categorisation and potential 
use of circular bio-based building materials, while Sect. 28.3 discusses the adoption 
barriers of these materials, including a comparative study between developed and 
developing economies. Meanwhile, the sustainability assessment regarding the use 
of these materials is presented in Sect. 28.4, and ﬁnally, Sect. 28.5 concludes the 
chapter. 602
D. L.

Le et al. 28.2 
Categorisation and the Potential Use of Circular 
Bio-Based Building Materials 
28.2.1 
Categorisation 
It is highlighted that circular bio-based building materials are heterogeneous in size, 
composition, shape, function, and biological origins [5]. They can be insulation board 
made from cork waste [10], pineapple by-products [11], or recycled cellulose and 
sheep wool waste [12]; hemp-based materials like hempcrete [13, 14] and hemp 
hurds [15]; and concrete or bricks partly retrieved from bio-wastes like coconut shell 
[16], rice husk [17–21], wheat straw and olive stone ﬂour [22], etc. Despite these 
heterogeneities, these materials can be grouped into three macro categories [5]: (i) 
circular bio-based insulation materials; (ii) circular bio-based cementitious materials; 
and (iii) indoor-used circular bio-based building materials (e.g., ceiling, ﬂooring, and 
furniture). 28.2.1.1
Circular Bio-Based Insulation Materials 
This category is the most dominant of the three aforementioned categories, high-
lighting that circular bio-based building materials are better suited to be used as 
insulation materials than for other purposes [5]. Within this category, materials are 
also diversiﬁed in forms and origins. Among them, hemp-based, cork-based, and 
straw-based materials have emerged as the most popular ones [5, 23]. A list of 
example materials in this category is presented in Table 28.1. Circular bio-based insulation materials emerge as promising alternatives to be 
installed in building envelopes to improve in-use energy efﬁciency [5, 79]. This is 
because of their distinct qualities regarding hygrothermal performance, for example, 
the sorption and desorption of water vapour in their porous structure in dynamic equi-
librium with their surroundings, constantly adjusting their hygric and thermal charac-
teristics while causing energy transfers themselves [79]. Hence, using these materials 
can reduce dramatic operational energy consumption. For example, a building using 
agglomerated cork and hempcrete walls can save up to 75% [62] and 16% [52]  of  
its operational energy consumption compared to a brick-wall building, respectively. Furthermore, as these materials are sourced from renewable biosources and have a 
low carbon footprint compared to petrol-based insulation materials, rock and glass 
wool, etc., their use offers great opportunities to mitigate the climate impacts of the 
construction industry [5]. 28

28.2.1.2
Circular Bio-Based Cementitious Materials 
It is worth noting that materials in this category are rarely discussed in the literature as 
bio-based building materials [5]. However, since these materials fulﬁl the circular bio-
based building material deﬁnition and their environmental beneﬁts are considerable, 
they should be considered an important group of circular bio-based building materials 
[5]. A list of example materials in this category is presented in Table 28.2. Table 28.2 indicates that most of the materials in this group relate to the use 
of biowaste-based inputs to partially replace cement or, in some instances, sand 
in concrete and mortar. This is because the production of cement has detrimental 
impacts on the environment. Indeed, the cement industry consumes around 12%-
15% of the total available industrial energy and contributes about 5% of the total

cement can not only save on disposal costs but also reduce the production cost of 
concrete. 28.2.1.3
Indoor-Used Circular Bio-Based Building Materials 
Indoor-used circular bio-based building materials can be used for ﬁnishing ceilings, 
ﬂooring, and producing furniture. For instance, circular bio-based linoleum can be 
used as a no-joint ﬂooring material, especially in high-trafﬁc areas like sports and 
ofﬁce facilities and also can be used in residential buildings [92]. Meanwhile, circular 
bio-based composites have potential uses in framing, wallboards, window frames, 
doors, ﬂooring, decorative panelling, cubicle walls, and ceiling panels [93]. These 
highlight the varied potential applications of indoor-used circular bio-based building 
materials within the construction industry. A list of example materials in this category 
is presented in Table 28.3. 28

28.2.2 
Potential Use 
Generally, circular bio-based building materials can be used for three main purposes 
associated with their three categories. Particularly, materials within the ﬁrst cate-
gory can mainly be used as thermal [42, 50, 66, 70] and acoustic insulation [42, 52] 
to be installed mostly in walls and also ceilings [5]. In this context, hemp-based, 
cork-based, and straw-based are the most commonly used circular bio-based insula-
tion materials [5, 23]. Meanwhile, materials in the second category can be used for 
diversiﬁed applications, from masonry works [84–87] to constructing load-bearing 
structures [80, 81, 84], and even for insulation purposes [85–87]. This suggests that 
circular bio-based building materials can be used either indoors or outdoors, as well 
as for constructing substructures like foundations, upper structures such as columns, 
beams, slabs, etc., or infrastructure like bridges, sewers, etc. Finally, materials in the 
third category can be used for various purposes, such as ﬂooring, ceilings, wallboards 
to divide space, decoration, windows and doors, and furniture [92, 93]. With the aforementioned potential uses, circular bio-based building materials can 
be adopted at different scales, such as components, bu

ildings, and building stock 
scales [5]. Generally, these materials can be adopted in any building type, including 
residential, commercial, and industrial ones. These buildings can be new [54]  or  
existing ones [34, 112]. In developed countries, especially in Europe, where around 
four-ﬁfths of residential buildings were built before 1990 [113] and 75% of the 
building stock is energy-inefﬁcient [112]. This implies that in these countries, energy 
renovation of existing buildings is the main market for circular bio-based building 
materials, especially for insulation purposes. Meanwhile, in developing countries, 
with a high need for new buildings to meet the requirements of the population and 
economic growth, circular bio-based building materials are more likely to be used not

606
D. L. Le et al. only for insulation and ﬁnishing purposes but also for constructing load-bearing struc-
tures using circular bio-based cementitious materials, for example, RHA concrete 
and mortar [114]. 28.3 
Barriers to Circular Bio-Based Building Material 
Adoption 
28.3.1 
Potential Barriers 
According to Le et al. [115], the adoption of circular bio-based building materials 
in both developed and developing economies is faced with various barriers. These 
barriers are attributed to the cost-driven and conservative nature of the construction 
industry [9]. Indeed, cost-related barriers such as fear of high initial costs and extra 
costs [116–119] and the sector’s culture-related factors like reluctance to change from 
easy-procurement-traditional materials to new materials [116, 117, 120] are identiﬁed 
as the main barriers to the adoption of sustainable materials in the construction 
industry. As most circular bio-based building materials are new and emerging, their 
adoption has to face market-related barriers regarding their availability in the market 
[116, 121], the challenges in supplying [117, 122], etc., as well as barriers linked to 
the lack of data and information [118, 123]. Finally, these materials’ use is affected 
by barriers related to the government’s role, such as the lack of public incentives and 
promotion [116, 121] and the inadequacy of building codes and regulations [116, 
118], for instance. A list of 23 potential barriers to the adoption of these materials is 
identiﬁed from the existing literature and presented in Table 28.4. 28.3.2 
A Comparative Study of Adoption Barriers 
in Developed and Developing Economies 
To conduct a comparative study of the barriers to the adoption of circular bio-based 
building materials in developed and developing economies, Flanders in Belgium 
and Vietnam are chosen as case studies, respectively. First, the criticality of the 23 
identiﬁed barriers is investigated based on the perspectives of building professionals 
in the Flemish and Vietnamese construction industries. To collect data, a question-
naire survey, which is a technique commonly used to gather specialist perceptions 
on factors affecting th

e implementation of sustainable practices in the construc-
tion industry [116, 124, 125], is employed. The questionnaire was developed based 
on the potential barriers identiﬁed in the literature (Table 28.4). Particularly, the 
research aims and contact details are presented in the ﬁrst section, while the second 
section is designed to gather background information about respondents (e.g., posi-
tion, area of expertise, working experience, etc.). Meanwhile, the rank of respondents

610
D. L. Le et al. to barriers to circular bio-based building material utilisation is gathered in another 
section using a ﬁve-point Likert scale (1 = extremely low and 5 = extremely high). This is because using the Likert scale can speed up the data collection and provide 
clear and simple-to-understand results [126]. Before carrying out the mass survey, a 
pilot study involving three experts in the building sector was conducted to evaluate 
and reﬁne the questionnaire. The data collection was conducted simultaneously in the cases of Flanders and 
Vietnam, using a combination of purposive and snowball sampling techniques. Although these two methods are non-probability sampling techniques, their use is 
appropriate. This is because the probability-based random sampling technique is 
rarely feasible in construction management research [116, 127]. In this context, non-
probability sampling techniques, especially purposive and snowball samplings, are 
most commonly used in construction management research [116, 128, 129]. There-
fore, the combination of these two methods is appropriate to be used to gather data 
in this study. The questionnaire was presented in Google Forms and distributed to 
potential respondents via personal emails. Besides, in line with the snowball sampling 
technique, potential respondents were asked to circulate the questionnaire to other 
experts in their network that suited the survey. To analyse the data, different methods are applied. Firstly, Cronbach’s alpha is 
used to evaluate the reliability of the data collected using the ﬁve-point scale. The 
reliability is evaluated via the alpha coefﬁcient (α), which ranges from 0 to 1 [129, 
130]. It is widely accepted that this coefﬁcient must be greater than 0.7 to ensure 
the acceptable reliability of the scale [129, 130]. Next, the mean score technique, a 
typical quantitative analysis technique to rank the criticality or relative importance 
of factors [129], is adopted to rank the

level of importance of barriers based on the 
votes of respondents. The rank of different barriers is decided by relying on their 
mean scores and standard deviation (SD). In cases where more than two barriers have 
equal mean scores, the one with the highest SD is ranked lowest. Finally, to identify 
the critical barriers, the normalised value (NV) of the mean scores is calculated 
according to Eq. (28.1)  [129]. Barriers with a value equal to or higher than 0.5 are 
deemed to be critical [129]. NV =
(Mean score − lowest mean score )
(Highest mean score − lowest mean score) 
(28.1)
28.3.2.1
The Case of the Developed Economy of Flanders 
Flanders is the most populated region in Belgium [131], where the government 
has chosen the construction industry as a key sector for the transition to a circular 
economy [132]. Therefore, solutions and initiatives to transform the current linear 
to circular construction industry are the priority in the region. Besides, within the 
Flemish construction industry, research shows that strategies to address the embodied 
impacts associated with the production of energy and building materials are still

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
611
lacking [133]. To contribute to the transition of the construction industry and provide 
a solution to address the embodied impacts of the sector, promoting the use of circular 
bio-based building materials is one of the most potential approaches [5, 6]. To investigate the impact and interaction of adoption barriers of circular bio-based 
building materials, the data collection targeted specialists, including researchers and 
industry practitioners, as well as ﬁrms that are frontrunners in circular and sustainable 
construction practices in the region. The data collection was taken placed from mid-
April to mid-November 2023. The combination of purposive and snowball sampling 
techniques helped collect 37 valid responses. Among these 37 respondents, 11 are 
architects, accounting for nearly 30% of the sample, followed by eight green building 
development consultants (21.6%), academic staff (n = 4), contractors, and material 
producers (n = 3 for each). The remaining respondents are working as authorities, 
owners, staff of industry associations or civic organisations, engineers, educators, 
advisers for natural building development, and facilitators of the circular economy. Regarding working experience, nearly four-ﬁfths of them (73%) have at least ﬁve 
years of experience in the construction industry, and more than half (54.05%) have 
hands-on experience in using circular bio-based building materials. 28.3.2.2
The Case of the Developing Economy of Vietnam 
Vietnam is one of the most vulnerable countries to climate change, especially sea level 
rise and its consequences [134]. The economy in Vietnam relies on the agricultural 
sector as a backbone, as the country is the sixth-largest and second-largest rice [135] 
and coffee producer [136], respe

ctively. It implies that there are diversiﬁed biowaste 
and byproducts generated in the country, which may pose negative impacts on the 
environment, economics and also society. For example, the majority of rice straw 
in Vietnam currently has been burned or mixed with the soil, and both of these 
methods harm the air, water and soil environment, affecting human health in the 
neighbourhood area [137]. Hence, there is an urgent need for other approaches to 
valorising these waste and by-product streams to mitigate their impacts and turn them 
into valuable materials. In this context, developing and promoting circular bio-based 
building materials in the construction industry is one of the best solutions. The use 
of these materials also offers a coherent solution to mitigate climate change and 
other negative impacts of the construction industry on the environment, economy, 
and society [5, 6]. As a result, the impact of sea level rise and other consequences of 
climate change on the country may be mitigated. Since the development and utilisation of circular bio-based building materials in 
Vietnam are only in a very early stage, the number of experts with hands-on expe-
rience in using these materials is very limited. Hence, the data collection targeted 
building professionals and companies that are working on or have knowledge of 
sustainable construction practices. This is because the adoption of sustainable mate-
rials like circular bio-based materials is one important aspect of sustainable construc-
tion [138]; therefore, these targeted respondents are assumed to be able to provide 
valuable insights into the barriers affecting the adoption of these materials in Vietnam. 612
D.

Le et al. The questionnaire was open from February 2023 to mid-May 2023, which helped to 
gather 51 responses [115]. Of these respondents, 17 are civil engineers (33.33%), followed by 13 academics 
(25.49%), nine employees of housing developers (17.65%), three architects (5.88%), 
three green building consultants (5.88%), and those working for other stakeholders, 
including materials producers, quantity surveyors and authorities (n = 6, accounting 
for 11.77%) [115]. Nearly four-ﬁfths of the respondents have at least 5 years of expe-
rience working in the construction sector. However, it should be highlighted that only 
5.88% of respondents have ﬁrsthand experience in developing and utilising circular 
bio-based building materials [115]. This indicates that the use of these materials is in 
its infancy in Vietnam, highlighting the need to promote their adoption in the country. 28.3.2.3
Comparison Barriers in Developed and Developing Economies 
The collected data is ﬁrst evaluated for the reliability of the ﬁve-point Likert scale 
by using SPSS 29.0 statistical software [139]. The α coefﬁcient for the 23 barriers is 
computed to be 0.83 and 0.95 for the cases of Flanders and Vietnam, respectively. These values are far higher than the threshold of 0.7, indicating the appropriate 
reliability of the ﬁve-point scale used is at the 5% signiﬁcance level, and, therefore, 
the data collected is suitable for further analyses. In the next step, mean scores 
are calculated to rank barriers in both cases. The calculation of normalised values 
helps to identify 15 and 12 critical barriers to the adoption of circular bio-based 
building materials in Flanders and Vietnam, respectively. The mean score, rank, SD, 
normalised value of each barrier, and comparison of the two cases are presented in 
Table 28.4. It can be seen that the rank of almost all barriers is signiﬁcantly different between 
the two cases. In Flanders, cost- and risk-related barriers, such as B21 (concern about 
the high initial cost), B23 (risks and uncertainties involved in adopting new materials), 
and B13 (perception of the extra cost being incurred), arise as the three most critical 
ones hindering the use of circular bio-based building materials. Meanwhile, these 
barriers are ranked only 10th, 3rd, and 19th in the case of Vietnam. Except for the 
similarity in consideration of the risks and uncertainties associated with the use of 
these materials (B23), the perceptions about the cost of building professionals in 
the case of a developed economy are totally different from those in a developing 
economy. It should be highlighted that the results in the developed economy of 
Flanders are indeed in line with the cost-driven nature of the construction industry 
[9]. Otherwise, cost-related barriers are not the most signiﬁcant obstacles in the case 
of the developing economy of Vietnam, suggesting that there may be other factors 
that surpass costs in hindering the adoption of new materials i

n the country. Indeed, 
the government’s role-related barriers are deemed to be the most critical to circular 
bio-based building materials in Vietnam. As presented in Table 28.4, B01 (building 
code restriction and lack of regulation), B04 (lack of information on CBBMs), B02 
(lack of government promotion and incentives), and B03 (lack of design guides and 
tools) are in the top ﬁve most signiﬁcant barriers in Vietnam, while these are ranked

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
613
only 7th, 16th, 9th, and 18th in the case of Flanders. This implies that without the 
involvement of the government, it is hard to achieve sustainability in the construction 
industry in developed economies like Vietnam. This is echoed with the previous 
studies on barriers to the implementation of green building, which is a sustainable 
practice in the Vietnamese construction industry [140]. Furthermore, this ﬁnding 
also highlights that the adoption of CBBMs in Vietnam is hindered by inadequate 
information regarding the environmental impacts, performance, available types, how 
to install these materials, etc. It suggests that the collection and dissemination of these 
data sets can facilitate the wide adoption of CBBMs in the context of Vietnam. The aforementioned differences indicate that developed economies like Flan-
ders may have better preparation for the implementation of sustainable construction 
practices, including the adoption of new materials like circular bio-based building 
materials, than developing economies like Vietnam. Indeed, the Flemish government 
already took action more than a decade ago to promote circular bio-based building 
materials [141], and these materials already have a speciﬁc share in the construc-
tion market. Although less than 5% of the total building projects are partly made 
of circular bio-based building materials [142], these buildings can perfectly repre-
sent either case studies or demonstration projects. Conversely, the development of 
circular bio-based building materials was ofﬁcially mentioned in the Vietnamese 
government’s document less than two years ago [143], and the use of these materials 
is just in its infancy and not yet well documented. That is why Vietnamese building 
professionals highlight the need for guidance from the government through issuing 
building codes and regulations, as well as building demonstration projects, forming 
technical databases, and introducing tools to compare and select circular bio-based 
building materials over their traditional counterparts. Therefore, positive involve-
ment in issuing regulations and taking needed actions by the government can also be 
considered lessons learned from a developed economy to be applied in a developing 
economy for promoting circular bio-based building materials. 28.4 
Sustainability Assessment of Circular Bio-Based 
Building Materials 
28.4.1 
Scales of Case Studies and Assessed Dimensions 
To better analyse the stat

e-of-the-art sustainability assessment studies of circular 
bio-based building materials, Le et al. [5, 6] categorised the research subjects of the 
relevant studies into four scales. These include the material, component, building, 
and building stock scales. Studies on the ﬁrst scale take into account the sustain-
able performance of certain materials, while those on the second scale evaluate the 
sustainability of these materials when they are incorporated into components like 
walls. Meanwhile, the third scale comprises studies that take into account the inﬂu-
ence of circular bio-based building materials on the sustainability of buildings. The

614
D.

Le et al. last scale refers to studies assessing the effects of utilising these materials to build 
houses in a city, region, country, or beyond. This categorisation is vital because the 
research aims and objectives differ at each scale, leading to the differences in methods 
and data needed for carrying out sustainability assessments [5, 6]. Indeed, while the 
operational phase is mostly excluded from sustainability assessments at the mate-
rial scale [10, 95], this phase is essentially included in assessments of buildings [32, 
52]. Since sustainability comprises three dimensions: social, economic, and environ-
mental [160], sustainability assessments can cover either a single dimension, simul-
taneously two dimensions, or all three dimensions (comprehensive sustainability). The share of assessed dimensions at each scale is presented in Fig. 28.1 
It can be seen from Fig. 28.1 that the environment is the most assessed dimension, 
while most of the relevant studies are distributed to the material scale. Indeed, among 
the 97 studies identiﬁed from the existing literature, 74 evaluate only the environ-
mental impacts of circular bio-based building materials, accounting for more than 
76%. Nine papers consider only the economic sustainability of these materials, which 
is equal to the number of studies that simultaneously consider the environmental and
Fig. 28.1 Sustainable dimensions assessed in each case study scale

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
615
economic impacts of these materials. Meanwhile, only ﬁve studies take into account 
all three dimensions of sustainability, despite these pillars interacting and relying 
on each other to survive [161]. Furthermore, no study that either evaluates solely 
the social aspect or simultaneously with another dimension can be found. These 
highlight the need for more studies evaluating the social impacts of these materials 
as well as comprehensive sustainability assessments of circular bio-based building 
materials. Regarding the scales of case studies, more than half of the 97 papers (n = 53) 
consider only the sustainable performance of materials themselves (material scale) 
rather than considering the interaction and inﬂuence of these materials in the sustain-
able performance of a system. Therefore, the results of these studies may not compre-
hensively reﬂect the pros and cons of these materials compared to their traditional 
counterparts regarding sustainability. The building and component scales are the 
second and third most evaluated, with 24 and 18 papers, respectively. Meanwhile, 
only two papers consider the impacts of these materials when they are used in the 
residential building stock of a city . 28.4.2 
Sustainability Assessment Methods 
Generally, the methods used to evaluate the sustainability performance of these 
materials at all four scales are diversiﬁed, which are presented in Fig. 28.2. (Note: BiSA = Bio-Inspired sustainability assessment; CFA

= Carbon footprint 
analysis; EEA = Embodied energy analysis; EFA = Ecological footprint analysis; 
EPA = Energy performance analysis; LCC = Life cycle costing; LCSA = Life 
cycle sustainability assessment; MCDA = Multi-criteria decision analysis; MDM = 
Multi-criteria decision-making; MFA = Material ﬂow analysis; MIVES = Modelo 
Integrado de Valor para Estructuras Sostenibles; SCA = Simple cost analysis; SDSS
= Sustainable Decision Support System) [6]. It can be seen that life cycle assessment (LCA) is the most commonly applied. LCA is an approach that identiﬁes and assesses the potential environmental impacts 
of a product thoroughly at all stages of its life cycle [162, 163]. In the context of 
circular bio-based building materials, LCA is employed to evaluate the environmental 
performance in 58 studies and is either integrated or combined with other methods 
in environmental assessments (n = 2), environmental and economic assessments (n 
= 4), and comprehensive sustainability assessments (n = 2). It means that LCA is 
adopted in 66 out of 97 studies, accounting for nearly 70% [5, 6]. It is highlighted that most LCA-related assessments focus on the material scale 
(n = 37), followed by 20 studies on the component scales [6]. Concerning the func-
tional unit, the most commonly adopted is area-related, followed by mass-related and 
volume-related. This is an unsurprising result since insulation materials are the most 
dominant among the three groups of circular materials discussed in Sect. 28.2, and 
those materials are mostly in the form of panels or boards and are installed on walls, 
which are typically measured by area [6]. The dominance of studies at the material

616
D. L. Le et al.

Fig. 28.2 Sustainability assessment methods used
scale leads to “cradle-to-gate” being the most applied research boundary (n = 28), 
where only production impacts are considered, excluding impacts from other life 
cycle stages. Regarding the end-of-life phase, only 18 studies speciﬁcally propose 
scenarios, yet most of them are unsustainable or have a low level of circularity, such 
as landﬁlling (n = 14), incineration without energy recovery and recycling (n = 9 
for each), and energy recovery (n = 4). These limitations highlight the necessity of 
conducting more holistic assessments such as “cradle-to-grave” or “cradle-to-cradle” 
with more sustainable end-of-life scenarios. Additionally, there are other methodological issues associated with these studies 
that need to be highlighted. Firstly, concerning the life cycle inventory, data quality 
directly determines the reliability and accuracy of LCA results, and, therefore, this 
aspect should be clearly described to avoid biases due to data variations and trans-
parency issues [6]. However, up to 21 studies do not precisely provide detailed

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
617
information about material inventory data. Secondly, to conduct the life cycle impact 
assessment, most studies consider multiple categories [6], primarily climate change, 
i.e., global warming potential, carbon footprint, greenhouse gas emissions, etc., 
followed by acidiﬁcation potential, eutrophication, etc. Conversely, despite GHG 
emissions being attributed to energy consumption, the cumulative energy demand 
indicator is evaluated in only 28 studies. Similarly, carbon sequestration presents 
additional pros of these materials in addressing climate change impacts but is consid-
ered in only 25 studies, with no consensus on assessment methods. This aspect affects 
the comparability of the results, which may not accurately reﬂect the beneﬁts of these 
materials. Lastly, in the life cycle interpretation phase, since most of these materials 
are new and emerging, and many of them are even just under research at a lab scale, 
not all the needed data for LCA studies are available. This limitation necessitates 
making assumptions for LCA analyses, potentially affecting the accuracy of results 
and decision-making. To address this shortcoming, sensitivity analysis is needed 
to assess the impacts of uncertain parameters on the results [6]. Nonetheless, only 
25 studies consider sensitivity analyses. Likewise, only six studies investigate the 
effects of uncertainty data on the environmental sustainability of these materials, 
and most of them do not specify the parameters considered [6]. Future LCA studies 
in the context of circular bio-based building materials should address these issues 
to ensure the appropriateness of decisions and strategies to promote the widespread 
adoption of these materials. The second most employed method is energy performance analysis (EPA, n = 
10), which is a

n approach to evaluating the energy performance of buildings. That 
is why all ten relevant studies are attributed to the building scale. However, since 
this method can only take into account the energy consumption during the operation 
phase, it cannot holistically reﬂect the sustainable performance of a building. It is 
recommended to combine it with other methods, especially life cycle thinking tools, 
to provide accuracy and comprehensive results on the inﬂuence of circular bio-based 
building materials on the sustainability of buildings. The same recommendation is 
made for simple cost analysis, which is used in economic assessment as well as 
economic and environmental assessments, especially at the building scale. As this 
method is only a simple calculation of expenses that occurred during the production 
or construction phase, it cannot cover the whole life cycle of products and the time 
value  of  money [5, 6], affecting the accuracy of results. For the methods applied in ﬁve 
comprehensive sustainability studies, i.e., assessing all three sustainable pillars, it is 
highlighted that most of them are tailored tools rather than commonly used methods, 
affecting the comparability of these studies. For instance, Valtere et al. [89] and Garas 
et al. [18] develop tailored sustainability assessment frameworks based on multi-
criteria decision analysis (MCDA) approaches, yet the speciﬁc MCDA methods used 
are distinctive. Indeed, while the former study employs the TOPSIS method, the latter 
adopts a combination of the AHP, Entropy, and TOPSIS approaches. Besides, the 
criteria used in these studies are also different, challenging the comparison of these 
studies’ results. 618
D. L.

Le et al. 28.4.3 
Economic Sustainability 
The ﬁndings from nine economic sustainability assessments of circular bio-based 
building materials indicate that these materials are generally more economical than 
their traditional counterparts. For instance, with the same use, concrete that uses 
coconut shells to supplement 30% of ﬁne aggregates can reduce the production cost 
by nearly 6% [16]. A similar result can be observed in the case of replacing 5% of 
cement with rice husk ash [17], etc. This is unsurprising as most of these biowaste-
based materials are either free or low-cost, leading to a reduction in the production 
costs of circular bio-based building materials. However, does the use of circular bio-based building materials always provide 
better economic performance compared to traditional materials? To answer this 
question, it is essential to take into account the comprehensiveness of the assess-
ment methods used. Indeed, if using the SCA method, the economic performance 
of these materials surpasses that of conventional materials [5]. Conversely, when it 
comes to more comprehensive methods like life cycle costing, the result shows that 
using circular bio-based building materials is economically weaker than traditional 
materials [69]. However, since the number of economic assessments is limited, this 
result may not be true for all types of circular bio-based building materials that have 
not been assessed yet. To shed light on the economic performance of circular bio-
based building materials, it is needed to conduct more studies and synthesise the 
results to evaluate if the ﬁndings are different from those reported in this chapter. 28.4.4 
Environmental Sustainability 
To provide insights into the environmental sustainability of circular bio-based 
building materials, the ﬁndings from studies that compare their environmental perfor-
mance with traditional materials are collected and discussed. Firstly, results from ten 
studies using the EPA approach at the building scale indicate that these materials 
offer great opportunities to reduce the operational energy consumption of build-
ings compared to conventional materials. For example, using giant reed residues 
and agglomerated cork to replace bricks in walls reduces more than half and three-
fourths of the total amount of energy that a building consumes in the operation phase 
[62]. The same results can also be observed when comparing the energy perfor-
mance of a hemp concrete building to brick and aerated concrete buildings [52]. It 
is noted that the greater the reduction in energy consumption, the lower the climate 
change impact of a building. It means that at the building scale, the use of circular 
bio-based building materials signiﬁcantly reduces the climate change impact, espe-
cially when assessed by the EPA method. These ﬁndings are consistent with those 
from LCA-related studies, which are synthesised and presented in Table 28.5 [5]. It can be seen that circular b

io-based building materials offer better environmental

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
619
performance compared to traditional ones in almost all impact categories, especially 
climate change and, to a lesser extent, abiotic depletion, human toxicity, etc. Nonetheless, does the use of circular bio-based building materials always bring 
better environmental sustainability? Answering this question is crucial to providing 
a concrete basis for scholars, industry practitioners, and, especially, policymakers, to 
take appropriate actions towards the use of these materials. As presented in Table 28.5, 
several impact categories of these materials are worse than those of their traditional 
counterparts. For example, although reducing the climate impact, expanded cork 
agglomerate causes higher ozone depletion, acidiﬁcation potential, and eutrophica-
tion impacts [59] compared to polystyrene, etc. This highlights that, in some cases, 
using circular bio-based building materials to mitigate climate change impacts may 
have to be a tradeoff with other impact indicators. To address this shortcoming, 
research needs to identify and address the hotspots among the life cycle stages of 
these materials to reduce their harm to the environment. Prioritising the develop-
ment of materials based on local biowaste and by-products can reduce the impact of 
logistics and, therefore, reduce their general environmental impacts. Furthermore, 
according to Table 28.5, materials made from straw, recycled papers, or wood waste, 
have excellent environmental sustainability compared to traditional materials [5]; 
therefore, these materials should be prioritised. Finally, the decision-making process 
regarding the use of circular bio-based building materials should also consider the 
beneﬁts of valorising biowaste or residues used to produce these materials. As discussed in Sect. 4.1., only ﬁve studies evaluate the comprehensive sustain-
ability, i.e., all three sustainable dimensions, of circular bio-based building materials. The ﬁndings of these studies indicate that circular bio-based building materials have 
better sustainability than traditional materials. For instance, RHA concrete [18] and 
agro-waste-based bricks [72] have better general sustainability than benchmarking 
materials. However, in these studies, not all environmental impact categories of 
circular bio-based building materials outperform those of conventional materials, 
underscoring again that using these materials does not always imply improved envi-
ronmental sustainability [6]. Furthermore, it should be noted that most of these ﬁve 
studies exclude the impacts of the use, maintenance, and end-of-life phases of these 
materials, so the results may not be comprehensive and accurate [6]. Therefore, 
future studies should evaluate the comprehensive sustainability of circular bio-based 
building materials from a whole-lifecycle perspective to better understand their pros

and cons and assist decision-making processes towards their use. 28.5 
Conclusion 
Although circular bio-based building materials offer promising opportunities to 
combat climate change, their contribution to a sustainable built environment needs 
to be better investigated. Plus, since these materials are mostly new and emerging, 
they have not yet gained enough attention and acknowledgement from scholars, 
practitioners, and policymakers. To convince stakeholders to use circular bio-based

Resources. 4.5. Comprehensive sustainability

624
D. L. Le et al. building materials, this chapter ﬁrst provides a precise categorisation, highlights the 
potential utilisation, and analyses state-of-the-art sustainable assessments of these 
materials to identify their pros and cons regarding sustainability. This chapter also 
sheds light on the barriers to the adoption of these materials in both developed and 
developing economies, with the speciﬁc case of Flanders in Belgium and Vietnam. A 
comparative study is conducted to identify the differences between these two cases 
and collect lessons learned in Flanders that can be applied in Vietnam. Particularly, circular bio-based building materials are grouped into three main 
groups: circular bio-based insulation materials, circular bio-based cementitious mate-
rials, and indoor-used circular bio-based building materials, with various potential 
uses ranging from insulation to masonry, ﬁnishing works, structural purposes, etc. These materials can be used in both new and existing buildings. Regarding the sustain-
ability assessment, the existing studies mostly evaluate the environmental pillar of 
certain materials associated with the production phase, whatever their inﬂuence on 
the sustainability performance of a building in other phases, such as the use, main-
tenance, and end-of-life phases. The results indicate a lack of studies that deeply 
evaluate the social impacts and the comprehensive

sustainability of these materials, 
especially at the building scale. Concerning the assessment methods, LCA is the 
most commonly used in the existing papers, yet most LCA studies consider only the 
cradle-to-gate impacts of materials. Furthermore, assessment methods used, mainly 
LCA, also have methodological issues, such as the lack of a clear description of mate-
rial inventory data, shortcomings in the life cycle interpretation phase, end-of-life 
scenarios having low levels of sustainability and circularity, etc. These limitations 
affect the comparability, accuracy, and comprehensiveness of results and, therefore, 
need to be addressed in future studies. The sustainability assessments indicate that, compared to traditional materials, 
circular bio-based building materials have better sustainable performance because 
they have lower production costs and fewer impacts on climate change, human toxi-
city, etc. The comprehensive sustainability scores of these materials are also higher 
than those of conventional materials. Nonetheless, the utilisation of these materials 
does not always bring better sustainability performance. When assessed by compre-
hensive methods like life cycle costing, the economic performance of these mate-
rials is slightly weaker than that of standard materials. LCA results also reveal that 
various impact categories of these materials are higher than those of benchmark mate-
rials. Therefore, when considering adopting these materials, comprehensive methods 
should be applied to evaluate their sustainability to ensure the appropriateness of the 
selection. Next, barriers to the adoption of these materials exist in both developed and devel-
oping economies. 23 potential barriers identiﬁed from the existing literature are inves-
tigated in the cases of Flanders (a developed economy) and Vietnam (a developing 
economy) to identify the most signiﬁcant barriers in each case and also the differ-
ences between these two cases. The investigation identiﬁes 15 and 12 critical barriers 
to the adoption of these materials in Flanders and Vietnam, respectively. The results 
also show that cost- and risk-related barriers are deemed the most critical in Flan-
ders, while the government’s role-related barriers are the biggest hindrances to the

28
Circular Bio-Based Building Materials for a Sustainable Built …
625
adoption of circular bio-based building materials in Vietnam. These ﬁndings suggest 
that in the developing economy of Vietnam, circular bio-based materials cannot be 
successfully adopted without the involvement of the government in issuing building 
codes, regulations, and design guides. Besides, it seems that a lack of government 
promotion and incentives may also prevent the takeoff of these materials in the Viet-
namese construction industry. To encourage adopters, the government needs to offer 
them appropriate promotions and incentives. This action is also essential to offset the 
additional costs and contribute to a

lleviating the most critical barriers in Flanders. Generally, this chapter may be a good reference for not only scholars but also 
industry practitioners and especially policymakers in the construction industry. This 
is because its ﬁndings may assist these stakeholders in taking appropriate actions 
to promote the adoption of circular bio-based building materials, contributing to 
achieving a sustainable built environment. However, it should be noted that this 
chapter also has limitations. For instance, the sample size in both cases is relatively 
small, which may affect the comprehensiveness of the results on the adoption barriers 
of these materials. Furthermore, the characteristics of the sample in the two case 
studies are quite distinctive, which may affect the appropriateness of the comparative 
study. Future studies should increase the sample size in both cases and include more 
respondents with ﬁrsthand experience in using circular bio-based building materials 
in Vietnam to investigate if the results differ from those reported in this chapter. Acknowledgements This chapter and related research have been conducted during and with the

Chapter 29 
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based 
Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian Dwelling 
Nicolas Bertoldo, Sergio Renato Maria Sabbadini, Gianluca Ruggieri, 
and Giovanni Dotelli 
Abstract The construction industry stands as a primary energy consumer and a 
signiﬁcant emitter of Greenhouse gases, making a substantial environmental impact. The industry’s extensive material usage and inadequate building energy efﬁciency 
compound the detrimental environmental effects. Notably, the energy performance 
of buildings within the Italian built-stock registers particularly low ﬁgures. A closer 
examination of energy certiﬁcations issued in 2021 reveals that 34% of these certiﬁca-
tions fall into the G class. This study explores the potential environmental advantages 
of using bio-based materials for building retroﬁtting. To achieve this, a comparative 
cradle-to-grave Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) will be conducted on a real case retroﬁt 
project. The LCA will compare two design scenarios: one utilizing bio-based mate-
rials and the other using conventional synthetic materials. Results indicate that mate-
rial production has the greatest impact across most categories, but building energy use 
dominates global warming, abiotic depletion, and ozone depletion. Bio-based mate-
rials offer a potential environmental beneﬁt, particularly for global warming poten-
tial where a 96% reduction is observed compared to conventional materials. This 
advantage is attributed to the biogenic carbon sequestration of bio-based materials. 29.1 
Introduction 
The construction industry is a critical sector for global development, providing essen-
tial infrastructure and shaping our built environment. However, this progress comes 
at a signiﬁcant environmental cost. The industry is a major consumer of energy,
N. Bertoldo · G. Dotelli (B)

Milano, Milan, Italy

S. R. M. Sabbadini

G. Ruggieri

H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series 
in Materials Science 352, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-97818-0_29 
633

634
N. Bertoldo et al. accounting for 34% of global energy consumption, and a leading emitter of green-
house gases, contributing 37% of global emissions [1]. This substantial environ-
mental impact arises from two key factors: the extensive use of resource-intensive 
materials and the often inadequate energy efﬁciency of buildings [2]. Material usage throughout the construction lifecycle, from extraction and 
processing to transportation and on-site construction, creates a signiﬁcant environ-
mental burden. Energy-intensive processes like cement production contribute heavily 
to greenhouse gas emissions [3]. Additionally, the construction of buildings often 
fails to prioritize energy efﬁciency, leading to buildings that require excessive energy 
for heating, cooling, and lighting. This is particularly concerning in Italy, where a 
recent analysis of energy certiﬁcations in 2023 revealed a disproportionate share 
(31.3%) of buildings falling into the lowest energy efﬁciency class (G), with only 
a small percentage (11.2%) achieving the highest efﬁciency (A classes) [4]. This 
highlights the urgent need for strategies to improve the energy performance of the 
existing building stock and future constructions. By carefully selecting building materials, we can minimize the environmental 
impact of construction [5, 6]. Bio-based materials, for example, offer a sustainable 
alternative to traditional materials. Their production can even act as a form of carbon 
sequestration, trapping the CO2 absorbed by plants during their growth phase [5, 
7–9]. Additionally, many natural materials offer comparable thermal performance to 
conventional materials. This means that by embracing new construction paradigms 
that prioritize the use of bio-based and other sustainable materials, we can achieve 
good building thermal insulation and enhance indoor air quality and comfort, all 
while signiﬁcantly reducing a building’s energy consumption [10]. Retroﬁtting existing buildings offers a key approach to achieving sustainability 
goals [11]. Compared to constructing new buildings, it presents signiﬁcant envi-
ronmental, social, and economic advantages [11, 12]. Constructing a new building 
necessitates the use of vast quantities of raw materials, each with its environmental 
footprint. Moreover, building new structures consumes land, leading to habitat loss, 
ecosystem disruption, and a strain on natural resources [13–15]. Retroﬁtting breathes 
new life into existing structures, minimizing the need for virgin materials and the asso-
ciated environmental damage from their extraction and processing [12]. Furthermore, 
retroﬁtting an existing building is often cheaper than demolishing and rebuilding 
a new structure [11]. Retroﬁtting can signiﬁcantly reduce a building’s operational 
energy consumption, leading to

a reduction in operational costs. By extending the 
lifespan of buildings, retroﬁts contribute to the preservation of valuable natural 
resources like timber, steel, and concrete [14]. Retroﬁtting can play a vital role in preserving the character and history embodied 
in existing structures [16, 17]. Improving the building envelope and ventilation 
systems within a retroﬁt can signiﬁcantly enhance indoor environmental quality 
for occupants. This translates to healthier and more comfortable living and working 
environments, potentially leading to improved well-being and productivity [16]. The European Union (EU) has established ambitious goals for energy efﬁciency, 
aiming to reduce its overall energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions 
[18–20]. Achieving these targets demands a multi-purpose approach, and building

29
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian …
635
retroﬁtting plays a key role in this strategy. The huge volume of existing buildings in 
the EU necessitates a focus on improving their performance as well. Buildings are 
responsible for a signiﬁcant portion of the overall energy consumption [1, 20], and 
a large percentage of these structures were built before stringent energy efﬁciency 
standards were in place [4, 21]. Retroﬁtting these existing buildings offers a much 
faster and more cost-effective path towards achieving the EU’s energy efﬁciency 
goals compared to solely relying on new construction [11, 22]. While building retroﬁts offer a promising strategy for achieving energy efﬁciency 
goals, a comprehensive assessment of their environmental impact across their entire 
lifecycle is essential to ensure their potential. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) method-
ology provides a robust framework for such an evaluation. LCA studies allow quanti-
fying the environmental burdens associated with a retroﬁt project, encompassing all 
stages from the cradle (e.g., extraction of raw materials for new building components) 
to the grave (e.g., demolition and disposal of the renovated structure), including the 
operational energy use of the retroﬁtted building. A critical challenge in LCA studies lies in acquiring reliable and consistent data on 
the environmental impact of materials and processes. This is where Environmental 
Product Declarations (EPDs) emerge as a valuable resource. Environmental Product 
Declarations (EPD) are type III environmental declarations deﬁned by ISO 14025 
[23]. An EPD is a technical document that provides a standardized and veriﬁed 
assessment of a product’s environmental footprint throughout its life cycle [24]. To 
ensure consistent comparisons between products, EPDs establish common criteria 
for each category. For what concerns construction products, the category rules are 
deﬁned by the standard EN 15,804 [25]. This study investigates the environmental beneﬁts of utilizing bio-based construc-
tion materials in building retroﬁtting. We aim to achieve this by conducting a 
comparati

ve LCA of a real-case retroﬁt project. The LCA will assess two design 
scenarios: one employing bio-based materials and the other using conventional 
synthetic materials. Through this comparative approach, the study seeks to address the following 
research questions: 
Question 1. To what extent does the choice of materials during a building retroﬁtting 
design inﬂuence the overall environmental footprint of the building across its entire 
lifecycle? Question 2. Can bio-based construction materials offer a signiﬁcant reduction in the 
environmental impact of buildings compared to conventional synthetic materials? 29.2 
Materials and Methods 
This study aims to assess the potential environmental impact of a retroﬁtting project 
employing bio-based construction materials in the restoration of a historic Italian 
dwelling. To achieve this goal, we conducted a cradle-to-grave LCA in a practical case

636
N. Bertoldo et al. study involving the retroﬁtting of an old farmstead in Italy, utilizing bio-based mate-
rials, following the attributional approach [26, 27]. Through this methodology, we 
assessed the environmental impact associated with material production, and carbon 
sequestration within bio-based materials, and compared it to a conventional scenario 
employing synthetic materials. In the following sections, ﬁrst, the case study is introduced, followed by a detailed 
description of the LCA phases. 29.2.1 
Case Study 
The object of this study is a retroﬁtting project of an historic Italian farmstead, located 
in the town of Casatenovo (LC). When the renovation concerns, as in this case, a 
historic farmstead, the dialectic comes into play between the preservation of architec-
tural, constructional and material characteristics and the issue of improving energy 
performance required by legislation [28]. In this case, the complexity is accentuated 
by the fact that the intervention concerned only one portion of the entire farm-
stead (Fig. 29.1), a closed courtyard farmstead once used for livestock fairs and 
then agricultural equipment. The dwelling develops over two ﬂoors, with an overall 
useful surface of 104 m2. The original plasterwork of both the interior and exte-
rior façades consisting of lime mortar and sand not left rough as workmanship and 
not completed by ﬁnishing or painting, characterised the portion of the farmstead 
undergoing the intervention, making it possible to justify choices compatible with 
the existing building while differentiating it from the modern cement plasters of the 
neighbouring portions. The energy recovery choices concerned both the envelope 
and the building systems that previously consisted only of the heating of a chimney 
on the ground ﬂoor. Constrained by the façade alignments, the insulation of the 
external walls was carried out for the thickness allowed on the façade and for the 
rest by acting on the internal part of the masonry. The new ventilated crawl space, 
which also involved reinforcing the maso

nry, was insulated with a cork and vitriﬁed 
screed. The plant engineering involved radiant heating systems positioned mainly 
on the ceiling. The heating circuits are powered by the condensing boiler assisted by 
a heat pump, with the possibility of a photovoltaic supply, to produce domestic hot 
water. For the mid-seasons, there is also a thermo-ﬁreplace that replaces the original, 
less-performing one. Summer air conditioning has not been entrusted to the plant 
engineering but to the bioclimatic operation summer operation of the building with 
large wall masses that contribute to thermal inertia, the existing and new openings 
that guarantee excellent air feedback, to the solar protection given by the eaves over-
hangs and the existing south-facing loggia. The parameters of the thermal improve-
ments were always compared with the architectural choices, respecting the historical 
building and the healthiness of the building. The materials and techniques applied had 
to guarantee the breathability of the walls and low environmental impact, favouring 
natural insulators of vegetable origin: panels in mineralised wood wool, mineralised 
wood ﬁbre, hemp ﬁbre and hemp-lime. 29
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian …
637
Fig. 29.1 Picture of the 
farmstead with the portion 
involved in the retroﬁtting 
project highlighted in green 
The environmental impact principle of the materials also involved the use of raw 
earth; the same red earth rich in clay and iron oxides that were excavated on-site 
to create the aerated crawl space. The compounds for plasters and ﬁnishes were 
formulated and ﬁne-tuned on-site differentiated according to the use and function 
they were to perform. An example of the intervention carried out on the ground ﬂoor 
of the farmstead is shown in Fig. 29.2, comparing the situation before and after the 
renovation. This paper explores this retroﬁtting project in detail, focusing on the potential 
of bio-based materials and improved building design to reduce the environmental 
impact of the construction industry, particularly within the context of the Italian built 
environment, strongly vernacular. We explored the environmental beneﬁts of bio-
based materials, and their thermal and indoor air quality properties, comparing them 
to an alternative scenario employing conventional synthetic materials. 29.2.2 
Goal and Scope Deﬁnition 
In this study, the goal is to assess the environmental impact of a retroﬁtting project 
employing bio-based materials and in order to understand the environmental impli-
cations of such design, we compared it with a conventional one using synthetic 
materials. With this study we want to understand the importance of materials selec-
tion in a retroﬁtting project, considering the entire life cycle of the building, from the 
extraction of raw materials to the end of life after the demolition stage. We would 
like ﬁrst to understand the contribution of the production of the materia

ls over the 
whole life cycle. The outcomes of this study could be used to support the materials 
selection in the design stage of a retroﬁtting project, presenting a real case study. 638
N. Bertoldo et al. Fig. 29.2 Details of the renovations carried out on the ground ﬂoor of the farmstead. Situation 
before, a and after, b the intervention
29.2.2.1
System Boundary 
The system boundary of the study covers from the extraction and processing of raw 
materials needed to manufacture the products involved in the retroﬁtting project to 
the end of life of such products after the demolition of the building. The scheme 
follows the one deﬁned in the standard EN 15,804 including the core rules for the 
product category of construction products [25]. The life cycle stages are subdivided 
into modules: the product stage (from A1 to A3) is related to the production of 
the building materials, including the extraction and processing of raw materials, the 
transport of raw materials to the manufacturing site and the production of the building 
materials for both the bio-based and synthetic scenarios. The retroﬁtting stage is 
subdivided into the transportation of building materials to the building site (A4) 
and the installation into the building (A5). The use stage is subdivided into various 
modules and includes the use of the material (B1), maintenance (B2), repair (B3), 
replacement (B4), refurbishment (B5), operational energy use (B6) and operational 
water use (B7). Then, the end-of-life stage includes the demolition of the building 
(C1), the transportation of the waste to the processing facility (C2), the processing 
of the waste for reuse, recovery and recycling (C3) and the disposal of the waste 
(C4) [25]. In this study, not all modules were considered, but only those that were 
relevant with respect to the goal and for which detailed information was available. For example, regarding the use stage, the module B3 and B7 were not considered. 29
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian …
639
Fig. 29.3 The system boundary of the LCA study following the scheme from the EN 15,804:2012 
+ A2:2019 [25] 
The system boundary is reported in Fig. 29.1. The geographical scope considered is 
Italy for the construction and use phase, while Europe is considered for the material 
production phase (Fig. 29.3). 29.2.2.2
Functional Unit 
To effectively compare the two retroﬁtting scenarios, employing bio-based and 
synthetic materials, a proper functional unit must be deﬁned. First, we must deﬁne 
the function to consider. In this case, we are dealing with a retroﬁtting project whose 
main goal is to upgrade the energy performance of the building. Therefore, the func-
tion is related to the insulating properties of the materials. This function is considered 
to properly deﬁne the alternative scenario with synthetic materials with similar char-
acteristics to the bio-based one, in order to have the same thermal resistance and the 
same energy pe

rformance of the bio-based scenario. This means that it is necessary 
to deﬁne the thickness in relation to thermal conductivity and the amount of mate-
rial in terms of weight, in relation to density, that are required to fulﬁl the expected 
performance. Taking an overall balance, accounting for both energy and impacts caused by 
the production and construction of the building, and energy and impacts generated 
during the use phase of the building, it is necessary to deﬁne the building’s lifetime. The choice generally made is to estimate a scenario of 50–100 years, which is the 
expected lifetime for a building [29]. In this study, we considered an expected lifetime

640
N. Bertoldo et al. of 80 years. Given the signiﬁcant inﬂuence of this parameter on the outcome of 
our ﬁrst research question, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to ensure a robust 
understanding. We are aware that performing a sensitivity analysis on this parameter 
changes the functional unit of the whole building, but this is done just to understand 
how the materials selection could be important in the overall assessment, in terms of 
environmental impact. Considering the entire life cycle, the reference ﬂow is related to the whole building. 29.2.3 
Life Cycle Inventory 
In the inventory phase, we collected and quantiﬁed all the environmental inputs and 
outputs associated with the two scenarios: one utilizing bio-based materials and the 
other employing conventional synthetic materials. The complete inventory for the 
whole building life cycle is available in the supplementary material. The software 
used to perform the LCA study is the open-source framework brightway [30]. 29.2.3.1
Materials Production and Retroﬁtting Phase 
The construction materials employed in the retroﬁtting project are mainly of natural 
origin. Natural insulating materials such as hemp-derived building materials, in 
particular hempcrete and hemp ﬁbre mats [31], wood wool and wood ﬁbre panels, 
and cork granules, as well as earthen plasters [32] and bio-based thermal plasters. For such materials is of particular importance the assessment of biogenic carbon [7]. To comprehensively track all biogenic carbon ﬂuxes, we align with the EN 15,804 
standard, and thus also with the results of EPDs, where the −1/ + 1 approach is used, 
considering both CO2 uptake and release [7, 9]. Starting from the bio-based scenario, the inventories to produce the construc-
tion materials were both primary, provided directly by some of the producers, and 
secondary, considering activities from ecoinvent 3.10 cut-off, and from building 
materials EPD. The entire Casatenovo retroﬁtting was modelled by taking all the 
drawings of the various walls and ﬂoors involved in the intervention and their 
materials composition, in order to obtain the materials requirement for the entire 
building. The Synthetic scenario represents a more traditional approach. It utilizes widely 
employed construction materials, primarily of synthetic

origin, commonly encoun-
tered in conventional renovation projects. This scenario was developed by analysing 
the materials employed in the actual renovation of the dwelling in Casatenovo, Italy. Some of these materials were then replaced with alternatives commonly used in tradi-
tional construction methods. The replacement materials were carefully selected to 
ensure they possessed similar thermal conductivity and density to the original mate-
rials. Furthermore, a comprehensive evaluation of their speciﬁc characteristics was 
conducted to conﬁrm their suitability as replacements. This evaluation process began

29

where the required parameters, i.e. density and thermal conductivity, were reported. In Fig. 29.4 it is reported, as an example, the stratigraphy of a structure of the 
dwelling, with the materials employed in the bio-based scenario and the selected 
substitution materials considered for the synthetic scenario. The complete list of 
materials employed in the bio-based scenario and the substitute materials included 
in the synthetic scenario with their data source is shown in Table 29.1. For further 
details refer to the data available in the supplementary materials. Following material selection, the next step involves transporting them to the 
construction site. We aimed to maintain consistency in transportation distances and 
means of transport whenever possible. However, in cases where primary data on 
speciﬁc materials was unavailable, certain assumptions were necessary to ﬁll these 
knowledge gaps. The retroﬁtting phase includes the installation of the materials in the building 
structures. This phase relies on the information reported in the selected EPDs, 
including the possible use of machinery and tools for the laying. Fig. 29.4 Details of the composition of a structure, reporting the materials employed in the bio-
