29.2.3.2
Use Phase 
For the use phase, we considered the modules related to the use of the material (B1), 
maintenance (B2), replacement (B4), refurbishment (B5) and operational energy 
(B6). The modules B1, B2, B4 and B5 rely on the information reported in the EPD of 
the considered materials. Information on the impacts of module B1 is available only 
for the hempcrete materials, namely Blocco Ambiente, Bio Beton jet and Bio Beton 
pronto produced by the company Senini S.r.l. For these materials, in this module

29
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian …
643
is considered the carbon uptake due to carbonation [45]. Carbonation consists of 
the reaction of the calcium hydroxide contained in the lime with the carbon dioxide 
present in the atmosphere, resulting in the formation of calcium carbonate; this 
phenomenon involves the removal of CO2 from the atmosphere and the subsequent 
storage of carbon in the material in the form of calcium carbonate [46]. In the EPD, 
a lifespan of 100 years is assumed for these hemp-derived materials, so the CO2 
absorption is reduced to take into account the lifespan of the building. Module B2 is considered only for multi-layer parquet and Porcelain stoneware. This module includes the cleaning of these materials. However, adjustments were 
necessary to account for the discrepancy between the reference service lives (RSL) 
used in the EPDs and the actual lifespan considered for this study. The EPD for multi-
layer parquet references a service life of 50 years. To align with the 80-year lifespan 
of the building, the cleaning impacts from module B2 were proportionally increased. Similarly, the EPD for porcelain stoneware references a one-year maintenance cycle. In this case, the cleaning impacts from module B2 were multiplied by 80 to reﬂect 
the 80-year lifespan of the building. According to the st

andard EN 15,804, module B4 includes the replacement of a 
whole construction element [25]. Therefore, RSL must be considered for this module 
too, in order to cover the whole building lifespan. RSL of multi-layer parquet is 
50 years, hence, to cover the building lifespan, we must consider that this element 
has to be replaced one time. This also applies to porcelain stoneware, as they have 
an expected RSL of 50–60 years. Replacement includes the impacts of replacing old 
materials and their disposal, and the impacts of new material production, transport 
to site and installation. On the other hand, module B5 includes all the interventions to restore a construc-
tion element to perform its required function. This module is disclosed only for 
multi-layer parquet and involves 2 renovations over its RSL. Therefore, the impacts 
related to this module were properly increased. The operational energy use module (B6) typically involves monitoring energy 
consumption for heating, cooling, and hot water. However, due to the nature of the 
Casatenovo farmstead—a holiday home used seasonally—daily consumption data 
wasn’t obtainable. To address this limitation, we leveraged the issued energy certi-
ﬁcation, which classiﬁed the renovated dwelling in the A1 class. Only the thermal 
energy consumption data for winter heating computed in the certiﬁcation was consid-
ered since the energy consumption due to summer air-conditioning and domestic hot 
water was considered unreliable. The dwelling does not have any air-conditioning 
system and does not use an electric storage tank for domestic hot water heating, but 
a heat pump. Therefore, the primary data of the condensing boiler installed in the 
dwelling were used to make the inventory concerning the use phase of the thermal 
system for winter heating. According to the type of boiler, a proper activity within 
the ecoinvent database was selected and modiﬁed with the speciﬁc boiler efﬁciency. The value relative to the annual thermal energy demand for winter heating (ETh) was 
used for the calculation of the total annual thermal energy consumption, multiplied 
then for the considered building lifespan (80 years). Furthermore, since the synthetic

644
N. Bertoldo et al. scenario was deﬁned to have the same thermal resistance as the bio-based one, the 
same annual energy consumption was considered for both scenarios. 29.2.3.3
End-Of-Life Stage 
This stage analyses the environmental impact associated with dismantling the 
elements added during the building’s retroﬁt (C1). The total amount of these materials 
is considered to assess the impact of demolition. The materials are then transported 
(C2) assuming an average distance of 100 km by truck to a processing facility (C3). Existing data (EPDs) are used to determine the environmental impact of processing 
these materials. In cases where multiple end-of-life scenarios are presented in the 
EPDs, the most conservative option is chosen for C3 and disposal of any rem

aining 
waste (C4). Landﬁll disposal or incineration is assumed for any remaining mate-
rials. For further information about the end-of-life modelling refer to table 19 in the 
supplementary materials. The end-of-life scenario considered is speciﬁcally relevant for the bio-based mate-
rials since it determines whether the stored biogenic carbon is released back into the 
environment [7, 9]. 29.2.4 
Life Cycle Impact Assessment 
To guarantee consistency with the results taken from EPDs, we considered the same 
impact assessment method to compute the overall results for the entire life cycle. The 
method considered is the version of the method Environmental Footprint (EF) 3.1 
[47] associated with the standard EN 15,804, named EF v3.1 EN15804 implemented 
in brightway. For the same reason, the impact categories considered are the ones 
included in the EPDs, following the same standard. Building lifespan is a critical parameter for understanding the environmental 
impact of construction materials throughout the building’s life cycle. To assess 
its signiﬁcance, we conducted a sensitivity analysis evaluating the cradle-to-grave 
effects for lifespans of 50 and 100 years. 29.3 
Results and Discussion 
In this section, the cradle-to-grave results of the two retroﬁtting scenarios are 
presented. In Fig. 29.5 the results for the considered impact categories are presented, 
comparing the bio-based scenario with the synthetic one, highlighted with a diag-
onal pattern in the ﬁgure. These results allow us to investigate our research ques-
tions. The ﬁrst question focuses on the inﬂuence of material selection on the

29
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian …
645
entire building life cycle’s environmental performance. Material production (A1– 
A3) emerges as the primary contributor across most impact categories. However, for 
global warming, abiotic depletion, and ozone depletion, the dominant contributor is 
energy consumption during the building’s use phase (B6). Regarding the second research question, we can see if the retroﬁtting solution 
employing bio-based materials allows for the reduction of the environmental impacts 
when compared to the conventional solution employing synthetic materials. If we 
compare the two scenarios, we can spot that the bio-based one has lower environ-
mental impacts for most of the impact categories, being worse only for acidiﬁcation 
potential, eutrophication potential and abiotic depletion potential related to elements 
and materials. Another module which results to be relevant seems to be A4, related to the trans-
portation of the materials to the construction site. The difference between the two 
scenarios for this module is related to the higher weight of the materials selected for 
the synthetic scenario, due to their higher density. Fig. 29.5 Cradle-to-grave relative results for the retroﬁtting project employing bio-based materials 
compared with the alternative scenario with synthetic mate

rials (highlighted with a pattern). The 
scenario with the highest impact for each category is set to 100%. GWP = Global Warming Potential, 
AP = Acidiﬁcation Potential, EP = Eutrophication Potential, ADPE = Abiotic resource Depletion 
Potential, minerals and element, ADPF = Abiotic resource Depletion Potential, fossils, ODP = 
Ozone Depletion Potential, POCP = Photochemical Ozone formation Potential, WDP = Water 
user Deprivation Potential

646
N. Bertoldo et al. Fig. 29.6 Cradle-to-grave absolute results for the bio-based and synthetic scenarios, focusing on 
global warming potential impact category and its sub-categories. LULUC = Land Use and Land 
Use Change 
Bio-based materials offer a signiﬁcant advantage in terms of global warming 
potential. Compared to the synthetic scenario, bio-based solutions demonstrate a 
potential reduction of the impact of 96% (Fig. 29.5). The absolute impact results 
related to the global warming potential are reported in Fig. 29.6, showing the subdi-
vision between its sub-categories, i.e. biogenic, fossil and land use and land use 
change (LULUC). Here we can see the effect of the biogenic carbon uptake of the 
bio-based materials, being responsible for the great reduction of the total impact. If 
for the synthetic scenario, material production contributes 27.3% of the total impact, 
the contribution of this module is equal to 1.7% for the bio-based scenario. It’s important to acknowledge that the results are particularly sensitive to the 
deﬁned scenarios, especially the alternative scenario utilizing synthetic materials. The speciﬁc selection of materials within this scenario signiﬁcantly inﬂuences our 
ﬁndings. Moreover, this study relies on data from EPDs, which despite being a valu-
able source of life cycle information, have some limitations that should be considered 
when interpreting the results. Different EPDs are based on speciﬁc modelling choices 
and assumptions, which can lead to inconsistencies between EPDs for similar prod-
ucts. Additionally, the quality of an EPD can be inﬂuenced by the chosen system 
boundaries and allocation methods, potentially affecting the comparability of results 
across different products. Therefore, careful consideration should be given to these 
limitations when drawing conclusions from this study. 29
Cradle-To-Grave LCA of a Bio-Based Retroﬁtting of an Old Italian …
647
29.3.1 
Sensitivity Analysis on Building Lifetime 
The use phase signiﬁcantly impacts the overall life cycle of the retroﬁt project. A 
key parameter inﬂuencing this stage is the assumed building lifespan. To assess 
the importance of this parameter and gain a more comprehensive understanding of 
material production’s contribution, we conducted a sensitivity analysis. We evalu-
ated two alternative lifespans: 50 and 100 years, representing the lower and upper 
bounds commonly found in building life cycle studies [29]. The use phase impacts 
are directly tied to the assumed building lifespan. For a 100-

year lifespan, Module B6 
(energy consumption) simply increases by 20 years, reﬂecting additional daily energy 
use. Conversely, a 50-year lifespan reduces impacts in B6. Similarly, modules B1 
(material use—including hempcrete materials CO2 uptake through carbonation), B2 
(maintenance), and B5 (refurbishment) are proportionally adjusted based on lifespan. Module B4 (component replacement) behaves differently. With a 50-year lifespan, 
no replacements are needed since materials like multi-layer parquet and porcelain 
stoneware last as long as the building. However, replacements become necessary for 
a 100-year lifespan, leading to increased impacts in B4. The sensitivity analysis results for the bio-based scenario are reported in Fig. 29.7. From these results, we can notice that the greatest variation is recorded for the global 
warming impact category since the use phase is the main contributor to its overall 
impact. Moreover, as expected, changing the assumed building lifespan affects the 
relative importance of material selection. Indeed, if we consider a building lifespan 
of 50 years, the materials production phase becomes equally relevant concerning 
module B6, also for the ozone depletion impact category. 29.4 
Conclusions 
Through this study, we aimed to understand the importance of materials selection 
on the whole building life cycle and to assess if a retroﬁtting project employing bio-
based materials has the potential to reduce the environmental impacts compared to 
a conventional retroﬁtting solution employing synthetic materials. From our ﬁndings, material production (A1–A3) results are the main contributor 
to most impact categories. However, building use phase energy consumption (B6) 
dominates global warming, abiotic depletion, and ozone depletion. The bio-based 
scenario shows lower environmental impact for most categories, apart from acidiﬁca-
tion, eutrophication, and abiotic depletion related to elements/materials. Bio-based 
materials have the potential to signiﬁcantly reduce global warming compared to 
synthetics (96% reduction). This is mainly attributed to the biogenic carbon uptake 
of bio-based materials. While the study offers valuable insights, the results are inﬂuenced by speciﬁc 
scenario choices and limitations of EPD data. Material selection within the synthetic

Chapter 30 
A New Framework for the Automated 
Generation of Discontinuum-Based 
Structural Analysis Models from Images 
Andrei Farcasiu, Peter Griesbach, Qipei Mei, Sinan Acikgoz, 
and Bora Pulatsu 
Abstract Masonry walls, arches, and other structural elements have been primary 
components of buildings from ancient to modern times; residential and public 
masonry buildings are widespread in North America and Europe. These build-
ings often have distinctive architectural and cultural character which needs to be 
preserved. To assess these buildings’ response to hazards, such as seismic action and 
settlements, high-ﬁdelity computational models are needed. Discontinuum-based 
structural analysis tools, where the contact of each individual unit (e.g., brick or 
stone blocks) to other units is modeled, can be used for this purpose. However, 
manually generating the computational models with thousands of units per building 
is arduous and time-consuming. To this end, this chapter introduces a new data-driven 
framework where AI-assisted object detection and instance segmentation algorithms 
are used to generate discontinuum-based models from images of the building. The 
modeling pipeline is illustrated with an application to a stone masonry building with a 
nonperiodic masonry wall morphology. It is envisioned that the developed tools will 
enable reliable structural analysis of masonry buildings and support future activities 
related to their sustainable rehabilitation and/or reuse. 30.1 
Introduction 
The carbon embodied in new construction materials makes up 11% of the annual 
greenhouse gas emissions. To meet Net Zero targets and ensure a sustainable built 
environment, we need to reduce these emissions. Rehabilitation and/or reuse of our

D. A. H. Hanaor (ed.), Sustainable Materials for the Built Environment, Springer Series

653

654
A. Farcasiu et al. existing building stock can play a signiﬁcant role in this effort. A signiﬁcant part of 
the building stock that makes up urban centers worldwide is constructed of masonry. Structural analysis is used to support the rehabilitation and reuse of existing 
masonry buildings. The ﬁrst objective of these analyses is to assess whether the 
buildings are structurally sound under gravity loads. Furthermore, since masonry 
buildings are constructed of brittle materials that possess limited resistance against 
tensile stresses, the impact of likely hazards that may induce

signiﬁcant deforma-
tion demands on the structure must also be evaluated. In seismically active coun-
tries, earthquakes pose the biggest risk to masonry buildings. Structural analyses are 
required to prevent collapses and ensure life safety. Another important risk faced by 
buildings is ground movements, particularly in congested urban areas. Nearby deep 
excavation activities, e.g., for the construction of other buildings or infrastructure 
such as tunnels, inevitably induce ground movements, which alter the equilibrium 
state of the building. While collapse is unlikely in this case, structural analyses are 
needed to accurately predict crack development. If moderate and above structural 
damage is predicted, preventative measures need to be taken. Structural analysis techniques which can be used to assess masonry buildings 
are introduced in Sect. 30.2. This section highlights that reliable analysis of failures 
requires explicit modeling of units (e.g., bricks or stones) with discontinuum-based 
analyses. However, generating high-ﬁdelity discontinuum models that represent real 
buildings is a labour- and time-intensive task; it requires identifying thousands of 
units one by one and idealizing them for structural analysis purposes. Whilst this is 
possible for research activities, the time and resource commitment required for these 
activities renders discontinuum-based analysis impractical for practical consultancy 
settings, except for special buildings. To this end, Sect. 30.3 explores the automated generation of structural analysis 
models, harnessing the excellent performance of AI algorithms in various image 
processing tasks, such as object detection and instance segmentation. Different from 
several previous studies on segmentation [1] and automated model generation [2]  for  
masonry buildings, object detection and segmentation are carried out with the cutting 
edge convolutional neural network model. This approach allows the use of robust 
image processing algorithms, already pre-trained on large datasets, to be ﬁne-tuned 
with single-shot specialized training to conduct the required processing. The adopted 
strategy is known to reduce the volume of data required for conducting specialized 
image processing tasks and offers the possibility to beneﬁt from state-of-the-art image 
processing algorithms. Another novel aspect of this study relates to the morphological 
analysis of masonry texture (e.g., related to the size and relative arrangement of units) 
following image processing. This information is used to generate artiﬁcial masonry 
textures that are compatible with the observations; these textures are then used to 
complete the model in areas image processing has failed. Section 30.4 demonstrates 
the application of the automated model generation technique to a two-storey masonry 
building from Ontario, Canada. The generated model is subjected to lateral loads and 
settlements to demonstrate how it may be used for assessments

and how the new ‘as-
is’ representation of masonry texture in the model delivers new insight into structural 
response. 30

30.2 
Discontinuum-Based Anaylsis of Urm Structures: 
Concept, Theory and Applications 
Computational structural mechanics of unreinforced masonry (URM) constructions 
can be grouped under the continuum and discontinuum-based analysis depending on 
how stone or brickwork assemblages are represented within the adopted modeling 
framework. Speciﬁcally, continuum-based analysis, also called macro-modeling, 
denotes composite material characteristics of masonry as a homogeneous isotropic or 
orthotropic medium with no distinction between masonry constituents (i.e., masonry 
units, mortar, and unit-mortar interfaces). The continuous ﬁnite element analysis 
(FEA) is the most used macro-modeling strategy to simulate masonry buildings 
where nonlinear material behaviour follows plasticity or other macro-scale constitu-
tive relationships [3–7]. Typically, the damage is smeared out through a continuous 
medium unless special crack-tracking algorithms are utilized (e.g., [8]). Alternatively, 
the discontinuum-based analyses explicitly represent the morphological features of 
masonry structures using discrete elastic or rigid polyhedral blocks that can interact 
with each other along their contact planes [9]. Discontinuous FEA, Discrete element 
method (DEM), Non-Smooth Contact Dynamics (NSCD) and Applied Element 
Method (AEM) are the main discontinuum-based analysis approaches employed 
in the literature, which differ based on their implementation of contact constitutive 
relationships and mechanics as well as numerical solution schemes (e.g.,  [10–17]). To illustrate the outcome of different computational modeling techniques, a typical 
diagonal tension failure mechanism of a URM wall subjected to in-plane loading is 
utilized (Fig. 30.1a), where the associated damage pattern is predicted via continuum 
and discontinuum-based analyses. The result of continuum analysis is shown in 
Fig. 30.1b, where the diagonal tension failure is smeared out through the continuous 
medium without necessarily addressing the interaction between the masonry unit and 
mortar. On the other hand, the explicit representation of local failure mechanisms 
developing at the unit-mortar interfaces and masonry units is obtained from the 
block-based representation of the analyzed URM wall (Fig. 30.1c, d). It is important 
to note that the presented discontinuum-based models follow the simpliﬁed micro-
modeling strategy where the masonry units are expanded up to half-thickness of the 
mortar joint and nonlinear bond behaviour lumped at the contact surfaces among 
the adjacent block ignoring the presence of mortar, suggested by Lourenco and 
Rots [18]. The adopted simpliﬁcation offers an acceptable compromise between 
computational cost and accuracy. However, it should be noted that the masonry units 
can be simula

ted with different levels of detail within the simpliﬁed micro-modeling 
framework, depending on the expected accuracy in fracture resolution. The most 
common technique is representing each masonry unit via two discrete blocks (rigid 
or elastic), considering a single potential crack surface (e.g.,  [19, 20]), which can be 
depicted in Fig. 30.1c. Alternatively, masonry units can be represented via multiple 
randomly generated tetrahedral blocks (or polyhedral blocks, e.g.,  [21, 22]) to better 
represent spatially varying crack patterns in bricks and stone blocks, which offers

A Brief Overview of the Discrete Element Method 
In this chapter, we use discontinuum-based analysis to assess the structural behaviour 
of an old stone masonry building using DEM, developed by Cundall [23]. The adopted 
modeling strategy, following the simpliﬁed micro-modeling approach, represents 
the stone masonry morphology as a system of discrete rigid blocks, as shown in 
Fig. 30.2a. The geometrical properties of the deﬁned block pattern can be obtained 
from vision-based data (i.e., images, points cloud, etc.), or a ﬁctitious representation

existing construction characteristics of masonry. The block interactions are computed 
at the sub-contacts (deﬁned along each contact plane), where three orthogonal springs 
are assigned, and inter-block penetration is controlled by the pre-deﬁned sub-contact 
stiffness in the normal (kn) and shear (ks) directions, illustrated in Fig. 30.2b. The 
cohesive bond behaviour between the discrete blocks is described using elasto-
softening contact constitutive laws, which require bond tensile strength (fT ), cohesion 
(c) and friction angle (θ..) as input parameters. The rigid block movements are computed by solving the governing equations of 
motion using the central difference method to predict translational and rotational 
velocities given in Eqs. 30.1 and 30.2, respectively. The adopted explicit solution 
scheme is formulated by calculated block velocities at the mid-time step (i.e., t+ =

A. Farcasiu et al. constitutive model option.1 Next, the comprehensive application of DEM to analyze 
the in-plane strength and collapse mechanism of URM walls is presented. 30.2.2 
Predicting the In-Plane Failure Mechanism 
and Capacity of URM Walls via DEM 
As witnessed in past and recent seismic actions, URM buildings are severely affected 
by strong ground motions and exhibit highly complex and nonlinear behaviour inﬂu-
enced by various factors. The construction quality of the load-bearing masonry walls 
and the implementation of effective connections between ﬂoor-wall systems are 
the primary factors affecting the local and global structural behaviour of URM 
masonry buildings. Moreover, the geometrical properties, vertical compression 
stress, mechanical properties, and construction quality are the critical factors asso-
ciated with the structural performance of URM walls [26]. In-plane failure mecha-
nisms are usually observed in URM buildings once the local failure mechanisms are 
prevented, frequently noted during post-earthquake site visits [27, 28]. To this end, 
we simulate the two most common in-plane mechanisms, ﬂexural/rocking failure, 
and diagonal shear cracking, using the proposed computational modeling strategy to 
demonstrate its potential and applicability. Furthermore, the results obtained from 
computational models are compare

d against frequently used strength formulas based 
on the fundamental concepts of static and strength of the material, summarized below. Flexural/Rocking Failure: The ﬂexural failure mode is observed once the lateral 
load yields an overturning mechanism due to violating the static moment equi-
librium condition of the URM wall, typically accompanied by toe-crushing. The 
ultimate lateral load associated with the rocking failure can be obtained simply by 
considering moment equilibrium and neglecting the bed-joint tensile strength. There-
fore, the in-plane maximum lateral load for rocking failure (VF) can be calculated 
using the Eq. 30.6. Note that the geometrical characteristics, masonry compres-
sion strength (related to the toe-crushing) and vertical pre-compression stress (σ0) 
strongly inﬂuence the ﬂexural capacity of the URM wall, which can also be depicted 
in Eq. 30.6. VF = σ0L2 t

that masonry is a homogenous and isotropic material and the diagonal shear failure 
occurs when the principal stress at the wall panel center reaches the critical value (i.e., 
reference tensile strength of the masonry). Accordingly, the following expression, 
comprehensively discussed in the reference study [30], is derived that is provided in

the masonry units. The contact stiffness (corresponding to the composite action of

literature for soft and weak mortar joint brickwork assemblages (Em ≈ 200− 350 fm,

AI-Assisted Data Collection for Documentation 
and Structural Analysis 
Performing discontinuum-based analyses of unreinforced stone and/or brickwork 
masonry assemblages begins with generating discrete block-based geometries, which 
may vary in complexity depending on the analyzed assembly and expectation from the 
computational analysis. As URM buildings contain numerous units, generating such 
models by hand results in a tedious, time-consuming effort. Advances in machine 
learning technology have provided tools for streamlining vision-based tasks via 
the available open-source object detection and instance segmentation frameworks. Leveraging such frameworks can be advantageous for documenting masonry struc-
tures and eliminating the need to prepare computational models through manual 
processes [35]. This study primarily employs YOLOv8, an advanced computer 
vision model for object detection and instance segmentation. YOLOv8 performs real-
time bounding box detection and instance segmentation of multiple objects within 
images via trained convolutional neural networks [36]. YOLOv8 used in this study 
allows for the segmentation of high-resolution images by performing detections on 
different sections of the image separated into grids. An additional segmentation 
model, Segment Anything (SAM), is used to streamline training data preparation for 
the

YOLOv8. SAM identiﬁes and delineates objects in images directed to different 
prompting formats, such as text queries and bounding boxes [37]. This model differs 
from YOLOv8, as it only performs instance segmentation tasks and utilizes propri-
etary neural networks trained on vast datasets. While it is possible to ﬁne-tune SAM 
for more speciﬁc segmentation tasks, original network checkpoints must be loaded 
with large ﬁle sizes. The generation of an AI-assisted tool for the present study 
is achieved using Ultralytics, a comprehensive toolset for developing and testing 
convolutional neural networks developed by an organization of the same name [38]. The ability of YOLOv8 to detect and segment objects relevant to this study requires 
the model to be trained on a suitable dataset consisting of numerous annotated images. Therefore, a sizeable dataset of 3768 annotated images is compiled from an existing 
dataset in the literature [39], and numerous royalty-free images are obtained from 
the internet. The original dataset, “MCrack1300”, comprises 1300 images obtained 
from a crack detection dataset, online sources, and numerous photographs of masonry 
buildings near the University of Birmingham Campus [39]. While this dataset was 
initially designed for ﬁne-tuning the SAM model, the annotations are converted into 
the YOLO format using Roboﬂow, a web-based suite of dataset preparation tools 
[39]. A preliminary model is trained using this dataset to provide a checkpoint to 
further expand the dataset. The images, a selection provided in Fig. 30.6, are split for 
training and testing. In this case, 1000 images were used to train the neural network, 
150 were used to validate accuracy and performance, and the remaining 150 were set 
aside for automated testing. This dataset contains numerous annotations for bricks 
of various types and cracks of different sizes and severities. In addition, there is also 
a separate annotation type for damaged bricks, such as those split apart by cracks. YOLOv8 is trained for one hundred epochs, the number of pass-throughs of the

30

entire dataset during the training process. In addition, the boilerplate “yolov8n-seg” 
checkpoint, trained on the COCO dataset from Microsoft [40], is used as the starting 
point for training. The YOLOv8 model trained using just the “MCrack1300” dataset is not meant to 
be the primary detector of masonry units in this study but rather to perform detections 
on additional smaller images to efﬁciently generate additional training and validation 
annotations for a larger, more diverse dataset. These additional images originate from 
royalty-free photographs of masonry façades subdivided and resized into smaller 
640 × 640 images. Ultralytics provides an automatic annotation workﬂow that uses 
YOLOv8 to perform bounding-box detections and SAM to generate segmentation 
masks of the

objects within the bounding boxes. This procedure has resulted in the 
instant preparation of an additional 2468 annotated masonry images of various types, 
colours, and sizes. Figure 30.7 provides a selection of the annotations obtained from 
this workﬂow, showing that it effectively prepares annotations suitable for training 
and validation. As the sole purpose of this dataset is to detect masonry units, the 
crack annotations from the original dataset were discarded, and the broken brick 
annotations were merged with the brick annotations. While datasets can be expanded using new images, they can also be expanded 
signiﬁcantly via data augmentation to increase the model’s performance by creating 
variations of each image with various manipulations such as rotations, colour adjust-
ments, and mirroring [41]. This procedure was completed using Roboﬂow, where 
the combined images from the “MCrack1300” dataset and the new annotated images 
were mirrored horizontally and vertically, rotated 90 degrees in both directions 
and rotated further slightly. Hence, the dataset was enlarged to 16,275 images by 
incorporating these augmentations and removing redundant and poorly annotated 
images. While Roboﬂow provides many different augmentation options, rotations

Fig. 30.7 Annotations generated using Ultralytics’ automatic annotation algorithm
were primarily used to provide sufﬁcient augmentations to accurately detect vertically 
placed masonry units, such as those making up lintels above openings. Upon comple-
tion of the augmentations, the dataset was exported from Roboﬂow for training 
using Ultralytics. Since this dataset already incorporates the photos from the original 
dataset, the new model is trained again using the COCO-based boilerplate checkpoint 
rather than the previously trained model as a starting point. The performance of the newly trained YOLOv8 model is assessed during the 
training process. Multiple validation metrics exist for convolutional neural networks, 
such as precision-recall and the mean average precisions at different intersection-
over-union (IoU) thresholds. The precision metric provides an outlook into the accu-
racy of optimistic predictions compared to the ground truth validation data [42]. On 
the other hand, the recall metric is the ratio of true positives to the sum of both true 
and false positives. Higher recall values signify the neural network’s effectiveness 
in correctly identifying objects. During the validation process, the IoU of a mask is 
determined by the overlap between the predicted mask and the ground truth mask. The mean average precision is quantiﬁed for different IoU thresholds, which provides 
insight into the model performance at different levels of accuracy, either at a speciﬁc 
threshold or a range between two thresholds. As shown in Fig. 30.8a, b. c and d, these 
metrics are obtained across each consecutive epoch during the training process. The above results indicate that after being trained for 100 epochs, the model 
effectively detects the bricks found within the validation with an average precision of 
94%. Similarly, a high recall ratio of around 91% shows that most positive detections 
are true positives, meaning that the detected objects match up with the ground truth 
validation data. Even though the mean annual precision at the 50% IoU threshold is 
at 97%, the model is precise, albeit with predicted masks that weakly overlap with 
the ground truth masks. While observing the same metric within an IoU threshold

Application of the Proposed Data-Driven 
Computational Modeling Approach on a Stone 
Masonry Building 
This section shows a potential application of the proposed data-driven computational 
modeling framework on a historic stone masonry building (The Buchanan Block 
located in Kemptville, Ontario, Canada), with a load-bearing wall system comprising 
stone masonry units visually distinct from the outside. The Buchanan Block is a

commercial space built in the 1860s. It consists of squared ashlar stone masonry 
with ﬂat arched lintels above its openings along its western elevation, as shown in 
Fig. 30.10. The building’s architectural style can be characterized as Georgian, in 
keeping with the large number of buildings built in this style during the late 18th and 
early nineteenth centuries [44]. The simplicity of the building allows for a preliminary 
evaluation of the proposed model generation framework. The Buchanan Block is 
located along a busy intersection, making it arduous to take orthogonally correct 
photographs. The orthophotos of the building’s western elevation are created from 
a photogrammetric 3D model generated using Agisoft Metashape from a collection 
of photographs taken by a DJI Mini 3 Pro drone. The creation of orthophotos via 
photogrammetry aims to provide accurate two-dimensional representations of the 
building despite the lack of space to take orthophotos with a conventional camera. Upon acquiring photographs of the building in multiple positions and angles, they 
are processed through Agisoft Metashape’s algorithms to create point clouds and 3D 
models. 30.4.1 
AI-Assisted Block Generation 
The proposed workﬂow includes a series of Python scripts utilizing different libraries 
necessary to generate a group of discrete blocks replicating the load-bearing stone 
masonry wall based on the collected vision-based data for subsequent discontinuum-
based analysis. The orthophoto in Fig. 30.10 serves as the input image for the 
YOLOv8 object detection script, leveraging the masonry detection neural network 
mentioned in Sect. 30.3. The model processes the orthophoto and then superim-
poses masks with randomly selected colours to ensure they are visually distinct. Figure 30.11a shows the output masks obtained above a conﬁdence ratio threshold

rithm samples the sizes and staggering ratios of the contours to create missing (or 
undetected) masonry units in line with the as-is construction quality and construc-

threshold of 80%, there are still poor-quality masks, such as those that incompletely 
capture the bricks from the input image. Therefore, as shown Fig. 30.11b, many of 
these masks were discarded to eliminate the generation of inaccurate geometry by 
the algorithm. 30.4.2 
From Vision-Based Data to a Discrete Block Model 
Once masonry units of the analyzed stone masonry wall have been detected and 
deﬁned by a set of masks, they are further utilized to create a discrete block system 
for computational modeling based on the simpliﬁed micro-modeling approach. It 
is important to recall that in the adopted modeling approach, the masonry units are 
expanded up to half-thickness of the surrounding mortar joints, and they directly 
contact their neighbours at the zero-thickness interfaces. Throughout this research, 
the expanded units are deﬁned as rigid, and the bond behaviour is simulated at the 
interfaces of adjacent blocks. Therefore, the proposed approach requires converting 
detected masonry units, discussed in the previous section, into “expanded” blocks 
compared to their actual dimensions. It is worth noting that this conversion requires 
both geometric continuity and maintaining existing mechanical interactions without 
introducing new ones. This section will detail the implemented approach regarding 
the conversion process and discuss the implemented steps. The proposed workﬂow begins with analyzing the geometrical features of hori-
zontal bed joints. Typically, the bed joints of well-maintained and periodic (or quasi-
periodic) masonry walls continuously span the entire wall and remain at an approx-
imately constant height. A skeletonization procedure is implemented to determine 
the position vectors of bed joints for each masonry course. The proposed skele-
tonization procedure starts with a high-resolution binary image of the analyzed wall 
where block masks and the regions they encompass are true, and all other regions are 
false. The head joints separating horizontally adjacent blocks in the binary mask are 
removed. This is done by repeatedly applying a kernel to the image, which extends 
blocks horizontally without modifying bed joints. When only the bed joints remain

30

detections
in the image, an inv

erse Boolean operation is applied in which ‘blocks’ correspond 
to false, and the mortar bed joint corresponds to true. Results can be improved by 
setting large empty regions (missed detections or windows) currently considered 
mortar bed joints to be represented instead as blocks (false). This approach reduces 
white (or true) regions into minimum thickness (single pixel) lines that follow the 
centerline of the initially provided mortar bed joint shapes. Then, white pixels in 
the skeletonized image can be used to determine the average vertical location of bed 
joints. If a large portion of the masks has been missing, the bed joint detection tool 
can fail to detect the bed joints. In this case, an additional check is executed to ﬁnd 
spacings between detected bed joints greater than twice that of the average distance 
between bed joints. These regions are then assigned additional bed joints spaced as

672
A. Farcasiu et al. close to the average distance between bed joints as possible. The initial block mask 
data drawn on the binary image is presented in white, and window data is denoted 
using gray, as shown in Fig. 30.12a. Furthermore, the determined bed joints based 
on the described methodology are illustrated explicitly in Fig. 30.12b. Note that the 
proposed algorithm does not consider signiﬁcant irregularities along the bed joints; 
instead, it provides a continuous and approximate bed-joint height for each course 
of masonry (see Fig. 30.12b). The wall geometry is stored in an array where each row represents a single course 
of masonry. Within a row, each entry or ‘cell’ represents a ~ 1 cm length of the 
masonry wall. These cells are assigned integers based on which block they are a 
part of. Each block in the wall has a unique integer associated with it. Therefore,

assigned the same block ID. However, many cells within the discrete block array still

is consistent with the expanded block geometry and zero-thickness interface repre-
sentation of analyzed masonry walls prescribed by the simpliﬁed micro-modeling

A. Farcasiu et al. approach. The array operation to achieve this gap-ﬁlling procedure starts by ﬂag-
ging unassigned cells that are horizontally contiguous with a certain number of other 
unassigned cells. Having less contiguous cells, the smallest block in the wall is the 
threshold for gaps to be ﬂagged. These ﬂagged cells are then assigned the same block 
ID as the nearest block (already assigned cell) in the same row. The result of this 
operation is showcased in Fig. 30.14. Moreover, a novel custom generation tool is 
used to remedy larger sections of the array where no blocks were assigned to cells. It matches the statistical properties of newly generated regions with those of already 
generated sections, discussed in the following section. Once block IDs are assigned to all remaining portions of the discrete block array, 
a deﬁciency remains in the wall’s lintels. Lintel blocks above the openings in the 
wall often do not conform to the assumptions made about blocks represented in the 
discrete block array. They rarely have perfectly vertical head joints and usually have 
different heights from blocks in adjacent layers. Discrete block representations are 
created based on a Voronoi transform utilizing the initial lintel masks to address this 
issue. Initially, lintel blocks are detected and associated with a window by scanning 
which blocks neighbour a given window and have a centroid higher than its top frame. A Voronoi transformation is applied to the block masks, as seen in Fig. 30.15a, b. This transformation expands the masks until the entirety of the wall is encompassed 
by a mask. The space initially outside of a mask becomes part of the nearest adja-
cent mask. Each window is then individually processed by excluding all blocks and 
windows that do not belong to

the window and the associated lintels under consid-

lintel blocks are created wherever three Voronoi cells meet (seen in Fig. 30.15d); 
these Voronoi cells can correspond to blocks, the window, or the rest of the wall. Additional points must be added for the corners furthest from the window centroid 
in the leftmost and rightmost lintels blocks (Fig. 15e). Finally, all the vertical coor-
dinates of the lintel points are normalized to the nearest bed joint height and utilized 
to generate discrete blocks for the lintel, as shown in Fig. 30.15f. It should be noted 
that there is a substantial variance in the character of window lintels frequently seen 
in historic masonry. In this study, we proposed a solution primarily for ﬂat arches 
where it is appropriate to normalize the lintel block’s vertical coordinates to the

Generating the Missing Section of the Masonry Wall 
A portion of a given masonry wall may not be fully detected through AI-assisted 
or manual block segmentation methods. The primary reasons may include material 
degradation, staining, efﬂorescence, or no clear lines of sight. Additionally, an entire 
wall may be undetectable due to the neighbouring building with no clear vision. In these situations, generating a “geometric twin” becomes impossible. Therefore, 
we propose an alternative solution in which undetected or invisible wall sections 
are created utilizing the collected information from the already documented wall 
morphology, including the size distribution of masonry units and the staggering

ratio of vertical head joints. The two parameters adopted for characterizing the wall 
morphology can be directly obtained from the detected wall cross-sections. However,

676
A. Farcasiu et al. it must be highlighted that the present study only considers single-layer walls; hence, 
additional information needs to be collected for multi-layer walls. In Fig. 30.16,  the  
block size distribution corresponding to the case study is presented considering only 
the length of masonry units since no considerable variation is noted in height. The nonperiodic bond pattern used in the analyzed stone masonry wall is repre-
sented in the artiﬁcially generated wall sections by measuring the horizontal offset 
ratio, denoted by Ml, between head joints in adjacent rows in the actual wall geom-
etry. As demonstrated in Fig. 30.17a, the staggering ratio of head joints is computed 
as the distance ratio between the stepped line (shown in red colour), the shortest 
path starting from an arbitrary head joint to the bottom section of the representative 
window, divided by the straight line (blue), the direct distance between the start and 
end of the stepped line. Thus, a representative masonry wall can be generated based 
on these two parameters collected from the actual wall cross-section and realized 
via the proposed AI-assisted discrete block-generation methodology. At the artiﬁ-
cially generated wall sections, blocks are selected at a rate corresponding to their 
frequency in the sample of block sizes. The Ml ratio of the generated wall is modiﬁed 
by considering multiple candidate blocks during placement in an unoccupied space. Each candidate block is selected using the previously described method, following 
the block size frequency. Note that the one creating a new head joint with the greatest 
offset from the nearest head joints in the adjacent row is placed. By modifying the 
number of candidate blocks, the Ml ratio of the wall can be adjusted to match that of 
the already detected region. As shown in Fig. 30.17b, Ml ratio is not a constant value 
for nonperiodic or quasi-periodic masonry walls through its length. The distribution 
of Ml, presented in Fig. 30.17c, can be found by measuring the head joint staggering 
ratio in multiple wall regions. This approach provides a more realistic characteriza-
tion of the construction quality of the analyzed masonry wall. The adopted parameters 
are part of the Masonry Quality Index (MQI) method introduced by Borri et al. [46], 
developed to perform preliminary seismic analysis and predict the material strength 
of existing unreinforced masonry buildings relying solely on visual inspection. Hence, the discrete block model is generated utilizing the detected stone units 
(or blocks) based on the implemented AI-assisted framework, and missing ones are 
created following actual morphological features of the masonry wall by taking into

important to underline that most undetected blocks suffer from signiﬁcant deteriora-
tion and damage observed at the bottom of the building together with the most right 
slender pier, which can be depicted in Fig. 30.18. Overall, the proposed approach 
offers high ﬁdelity in terms of representation of the discontinuous medium by taking 
advantage of AI-assisted tools and the most relevant wall construction features. Next, 
the generated discrete block medium is utilized to perform the structural analysis of 
the stone masonry façade. 30.4.4 
Data-Driven Computational Modeling of a Stone 
Masonry Façade 
In this section, the proposed data-driven computational modeling strategy is applied 
to the reference stone masonry building, where the analyzed section of the building 
(façade) is documented using AI-assisted tools mentioned earlier. The discrete block 
model, consisting of roughly 1600 masonry units, is utilized to predict the structural 
behaviour of the stone masonry façade subjected to lateral loading and differential

678
A. Farcasiu et al. Fig. 30.18 Discrete block model of the analyzed stone masonry façade
support settlement. The primary objective is to demonstrate the potential use of the 
adopted methodology in the structural assessment of the analyzed building. The identical mechanical properties utilized in Sect. 30.2 to predict the in-plane 
capacity and behaviour of masonry walls with different aspect ratios are used for 
the large-scale façade simulation without considering any potential crack surface for 
the masonry units following the assumption of weak mortar joints with strong stone 
blocks. Moderate vertical stress (i.e., 0.1 MPa) is considered to address roof and ﬂoor 
loads, and lateral loads are applied at the ﬂoor slab and roof location as point loads. First, the simulated façade is brought into equilibrium under its self-weight, and then 
lateral loads are uniformly applied along its height. During the analysis, the cantilever 
boundary condition is considered, and the lateral displacement is monitored at the 
top corner of the building. Figure 30.19 shows the predicted damage progression 
and associated force (base shear) versus drift curves under the increasing horizontal 
displacement where the lateral load is appl

ied from right to left (denoted as L1). Note that the drift is calculated by dividing the lateral displacement traced at the 
monitoring point to the height of the building. The result indicates that the in-plane 
load-carrying capacity of the analyzed stone façade is limited by the diagonal shear 
cracking capacity of the large panel at the ﬁrst storey (see Fig. 30.19a). Also, cracks 
at the spandrel walls and rocking mechanism at the slender masonry piers are noted. After the severe diagonal crack, an excessive sliding mechanism is noted along the 
bed joint with further bond failures among the stone units (Fig. 30.19b-c). Overall, 
the macro behaviour of the analyzed stone masonry building can be idealized as a bi-
linear elasto-plastic response (see Fig. 30.19d). The damage progression associated 
to bond failure can be noticed along the bed and head joints indicated using red color 
at the contact planes in Fig. 30.19. Next, the same loading scheme but in the opposite direction (L2) is utilized to 
assess the structural behaviour and capacity of the analyzed stone masonry building. 30

Fig. 30.19 Predicted damage progression in the analyzed stone masonry façade subjected to lateral 
loading scenario L1 and the associated force-drift curve

680
A. Farcasiu et al. The results align with the previous analysis (L1) regarding maximum lateral load 
(Fig. 30.20a), whereas a less ductile response is predicted from the second analysis. The localized cracks at the sides of ﬂat-arch lintels are noticed in both structural anal-
yses. It is important to note that the analyzed building comprises irregular openings 
and has a non-periodic bond pattern, which yields considerable variations regarding 
the macro-mechanics of the discontinuum under different loading directions. As 
shown in Fig. 30.20a, b, the diagonal shear crack and several vertical cracks at 
the ﬂat-arch lintels are the primary failure mechanisms. In line with the previous 
computational investigation, the structure has approximately kept its strength by 
relying mainly on the sliding resistance along the bed joints, which can be depicted 
in Fig. 30.20c, d. Furthermore, localized toe-crushing phenomena are observed once 
the structure has reached the ultimate in-plane load-carrying capacity. Finally, the structural behaviour of the stone masonry façade is predicted under 
different differential soil settlement scenarios. The support blocks, placed under 
the large openings, are displaced 5 mm separately (Fig. 30.21). The computational 
investigations show that even relatively small displacement (Fig. 30.21a) or variations 
of boundary conditions may yield widespread crack distributions in low-bond stone 
masonry buildings (Fig. 30.21b). Similar to the lateral behaviour of the building, the 
vulnerable condition of the ﬂat arches against even minor deformations is explicitly 
noted. 30.5 
Conclusions 
With the advances in computational mode

ling and computer vision technologies, 
data-driven simulations offer more precise inferences regarding structural behaviour 
and performance of masonry buildings and cultural heritage subjected to various 
loading scenarios. Through this study, we demonstrate a novel application of AI-
assisted visual inspection tools employed to document the geometrical characteristics 
of a stone masonry building and nonlinear structural analysis via the discrete element 
method. The primary outcomes are summarized as follows:
• The implementation of AI in discontinuum-based simulations accelerates the 
conversion of vision-based data into discrete block representations of existing 
stone masonry buildings, making the documentation process efﬁcient. • Overall, the employed image segmentation and classiﬁcation tools provide reason-
able accuracy in detecting masonry units and offer a source of information 
regarding the construction quality of the investigated masonry building, which 
may be further utilized to generate the block-based representation of the structure. • The undetected sections of the masonry walls can be later generated by utilizing the 
knowledge obtained through the documented sections of the building, including 
but not limited to block size distribution and staggering ratio of the vertical joints. 30

highlight the complex failure mechanism of unreinforced masonry walls. Since

Chapter 31 
LCA of Self-Compacting Concrete 
Incorporating Waste Plastic Fibre, 
Recycled Aggregate and SCMs 
Zhanggen Guo, Ling Zhou, Qiansen Sun, Zhiwei Gao, Qinglong Miao, 
Conglin Chen, and Yujie Chen 
Abstract This study intends to conduct an in-depth life cycle assessment (LCA) and 
life cost analysis (LCCA) of waste plastic ﬁber (WPF) reinforced sustainable recycled 
aggregate self-compacting concrete (WPF-RSCC). A sustainable self-compacting 
concrete (SCC) was manufactured by recycling of waste concrete, industrial waste, 
and waste plastic. This paper assessed the advantages of the addition of waste 
plastic ﬁber (WPF), recycled coarse aggregate (RCA) and supplementary cemen-
titious materials (SCMs) on the environmental impact and economic cost of SCC. 10 
WPF reinforced recycled aggregate SCC (WPF-RA-SCC) mixtures were prepared 
by substantially replacing natural aggregates and cement with RCAs and SCMs, 
respectively. The environmental and economic impacts of WPF-RA-SCC were quan-
tiﬁed using Life cycle assessment and life cycle cost analysis. The VIKOR MCDA 
method was deployed to identify the optimal concrete mixture by integrating fresh 
and hardened properties, environmental and economic performance. The research 
ﬁndings indicate that combined addition of RCAs, SCMs and WPF offered consid-
erable advantages on environmental and economic performance, by reducing the 
environmental load and economic cost up to 73.9% and 34.7%, respectively. Quater-
nary RA-SCC comprising 1.0% WPF and 75% SCMs using a combination type was 
recognized by the MCDA as the optimized mix, which exhibited superior mechan-
ical properties, low environmental impacts and economic cost. This study proposed 
a novel approach for prioritizing sustainable materials selection, thus facilitating the 
recycling of waste concrete, waste plastic and industrial by-products. Z. Guo (B) · L. Zhou · Q. Sun · Z. Gao · Q. Miao · C. Chen · Y. Chen 
School of Civil Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, China 
e-mail: zhgguo@njtech.edu.cn

Z. Guo et al. 31.1 
Introduction 
In China, Accelerated urbanization and the processes of old city reconstruction have 
led to a signiﬁcant increase in the generation of construction and demolition waste 
(CDW) [1]. According to statistics, China recently generated about 2.4 billion tons of 
CDW annually [2], of which nearly 35% ended up in landﬁlls [3]. Landﬁlling CDW 
not only requires vast land and human resources b

ut also emits pollutant emissions, 
resulting in severe environmental pollution. To address this issue, recycling CDW to 
produce recycled coarse aggregate (RCA) as a substitute for natural aggregate has 
become a feasible option [4, 5]. Over the past two decades, the inclusion of recycled coarse and ﬁne aggregates 
into self-compacting concrete (SCC) has attained extensive research attentions and 
has been systematically studied [6–9]. On the other hand, the substituting cement 
with supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs), such as ﬂy ash (FA), silica fume 
(SF), and ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBFS), for the production of SCC 
is signiﬁcantly promoted recently, which resulted in the beneﬁt of declining CO2 and 
embodied energy of concrete [10–12]. Portland cement, a key component of concrete, 
is one of the most energy-intensive materials, releasing about 0.87 tons of CO2 per 
ton of Portland cement clinker. Cement production is estimated to account for 5–8% 
of annual CO2 emissions [13–15]. Therefore, the inclusion of SCMs into recycled 
aggregate SCC (RA-SCC) can mitigate the negative effect of the recycled aggregates 
on the fresh and hardened properties of SCC, thus improving the mechanical and 
durability properties of RA-SCC. By incorporating of recycled aggregate and SCMs 
into the SCC, a sustainable and cost-effective concrete material cab be developed 
[4, 5]. In recent years, plastics have become an indispensable part of industries such as 
packaging, agriculture, construction, and electronics due to their high durability, low 
density, relative lightweight, and low cost [16, 17]. Over the past several years, 350 
million tons of plastics are produced around the world every year [18]. The extensive 
use of plastics has led to a severe environmental crisis. Majority of waste plas-
tics persist in the environment for decades or even centuries without decomposing, 
contributing to signiﬁcant environment pollution [19]. Among various types of plas-
tics, polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is recognized as a major polymer. Although 
PET is a recyclable material, it is frequently discarded into the environment improp-
erly, leading to microplastic pollution. It is estimated that people ingest 74,000– 
121,000 micro-plastic particles annually through food consumption and inhalation 
[20], which generate various immune disorders and potentially damage the kidneys, 
digestive system, and nervous system in humans [21]. Research implemented by 
Prata et al. [22] suggested that microplastic pollution could elevate the risk of cancer. Addressing this issue necessitates enhancing the efﬁciency of plastic waste collection 
and exploring innovative recycling methods. An effective method involves incorpo-
rating PET waste to concrete production, which not only enhances the properties 
of the concrete but also effectively recycles the PET waste, thereby reducing the 
environmental footprint [23–25]. 31
LCA of Self-Compacting Concrete Incor

porating Waste Plastic Fibre …
689
By recycling PET waste into concrete, an eco-efﬁcient and cost-effective construc-
tion material was developed [23, 26, 27]. In recent years, a considerable amount of 
research has have been conducted on the workability, mechanical properties and dura-
bility of waste plastic ﬁber (WPF) reinforced recycled aggregate concrete (RAC). Ngoc et al. [26] evaluated the effect of WPFs on the mechanical properties and dura-
bility of RAC and found that RAC containing SF and recycled PET ﬁbers showed 
various degrees of improvement in compressive strength, modulus of elasticity, and 
splitting tensile strength compared to RAC without ﬁber. Guo et al. [28] found that 
combined addition of SCMs and WPF improved the fresh and hardened properties 
of RA-SCC. Ahmad et al. [29] also found that the addition of WPFs can signiﬁcantly 
improve the mechanical properties and durability of concrete. Guo et al. [30] studied 
the effect of adding an appropriate content of WPFs on the mechanical properties of 
RAC and found that the addition of WPFs signiﬁcantly improved the compressive 
and ﬂexural tensile strength, with the maximum improvement was observed with 
the addition of WPFs with a length of 30 mm and a volume ratio of 1%. Pereira 
et al. [31] and Al-Hadithi et al. [32] both obtained that the incorporation of waste 
PET ﬁbers effectively improved the hardened properties of concrete. Alani et al. [33] 
concluded that the mechanical properties of concrete with oil fuel ash and silica fume 
were enhanced after being reinforced with recycled PET ﬁbers. Nikbin et al. [23] 
explored the plain high strength concrete properties containing different percentages 
of PET particles which could be accounted as a partial volumetric replacement for 
sand and found that replacing up to 15% of sand with PET reduces tensile strength 
and modulus of elasticity by about 20%. Additionally, concrete with PET particles 
is more environmentally friendly due to lower CO2 emissions. More recently, several studies have explored the environmental and economic 
impacts of WPF reinforced recycled concrete. Tahir, Sbahieh and Al-Ghamdi [25] 
reviewed the application and environmental impact of recycled plastics in concrete 
aggregates and concluded that recycling plastics not only signiﬁcantly reduces envi-
ronmental burdens such as land eutrophication, global warming potential, and acidi-
ﬁcation potential compared to landﬁlling and incineration, but also offers signiﬁcant 
economic beneﬁts. Borhan et al. [34] found that the combined inclusion of waste 
ﬁbers and recycled concrete aggregates can not only conserve huge amounts of non-
renewable natural resources, but also decrease the carbon footprints of concrete. Bahmani and Mostoﬁnejad [24] studied the impact of steel, basalt, and WPF on 
various aspects of high-performance concrete with calcium oxide-activated slag and 
found that replacing steel or basalt ﬁbers with WPFs, obtained from discarded plastic

bottles, in high-performance concrete is a sustainable and cost-effective alterna-
tive. Rispoli et al. [35] conducted a comparative life cycle assessment of traditional 
pavement systems, demonstrating that sustainable road pavement mixes using recy-
cled plastic and carbonated aggregates signiﬁcantly reduced environmental impacts. Javadabadi [36] conducted a cradle-to-gate analysis to evaluate the environmental 
effects of using recycled PET as a ﬁne aggregate in concrete applications and revealed 
that utilizing recycled-based aggregates signiﬁcantly reduces the impact on the envi-
ronment in terms of global warming potential and human toxicity potential. Ersan 
et al. [37] explored the environmental impacts of incorporating FA and WPFs for the

690
Z. Guo et al. production of lightweight concrete and found that lightweight concrete containing 
30% WPFs and 20% FA had a 13% less greenhouse gas emissions when compared 
to conventional concrete. Majority of existing research on WPF reinforced sustainable recycled aggre-
gate SCC (WPF-RA-SCC) focused on speciﬁc individual criteria, such as technical 
performance, environmental or economic aspects. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) 
has been widely used to evaluate environmental issues, such as climate change and 
resource depletion. Life Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA) has been extensively employed 
in economic studies in the ﬁeld of concrete to evaluate the total cost of concrete struc-
tures over their entire lifecycle, including initial construction costs, maintenance and 
repair expenses, and eventual demolition and disposal costs. However, the concept 
of sustainability should involve a comprehensive evaluation of concrete considering 
environmental, technical, and economic aspects. This essentially requires taking 
multiple factors into account, often involving complex trade-offs. Therefore, Multi-
Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) is needed to address conﬂicts arising from 
multiple attributes. MCDA encompasses a set of methods and procedures designed 
to rank various alternative solutions based on different criteria, ultimately comparing 
and selecting the optimal alternative [38]. Nowadays, MCDA methods have become 
a popular tool for selecting the best solution, with main methods including TOPSIS, 
VIseKriterijumska Optimizacija I Kompromisno Resenje (VIKOR), Data Envelop-
ment Analysis, Analytic Hierarchy Process, ELECTRE algorithms, etc. [39–42]. Furthermore, to the best knowledge of the authors, there is limited relevant research 
in the open literatures which focused speciﬁcally on the application of MCDA 
employing VIKOR method to evaluate the environmental and economic advan-
tages of recycling waste concrete, industrial by-products and waste plastic for the 
production of sustainable high-performance SCC. This paper is part of a comprehensive study aimed at proposing a high-
performance and sustainable SCC, which was produced by substantially replacing 
cement with SCMs and natural aggreg

ates with RCA, as well as reinforced with 
WPFs. Previous studies implemented by the authors have investigated the worka-
bility, mechanical properties, and durability of WPF-RA-SCC mixtures. The test 
results indicated that the combined use of SCMs and WPF produced a synergistic 
effect, substantially improving the mechanical properties and durability of RA-SCC 
[10, 28]. This paper intends to conduct an in-depth LCA and LCCA of WPF-RA-
SCC, combining the diverse of the performance keys in terms of engineering, environ-
mental, and economic perspectives. LCA and LCCA were performed to quantify the 
environmental and economic impacts of RA-SCC containing different percentages 
of WPF and SCMs. In addition, MCDA was carried out to compare the production of 
SCC made with WPF, SCMs and RCAs with the production of conventional concrete 
made with natural aggregates and ordinary Portland cement. Considering environ-
mental, economic, and technical criteria, the VIKOR MCDA method was adopted to 
determine the optimal mix proportion. Five key performance indicators were iden-
tiﬁed, and WPF-RA-SCC mixtures with excellent mechanical properties, low envi-
ronmental impact, and economic cost were determined, optimizing the proportions 
of SCMs and WPF. 31

31.2 
Experimental Program and Test Results 
31.2.1 
Experimental Program 
The materials used in this study include water, cement, natural coarse aggregate 
(NCA), RCA, river sand, water-reducing agent, and SCMs such as FA, GGBFS, and 
SF. The compressive strength of cement is 42.5 MPa. The river sand had a maximum 
particle size of 5 mm, a water absorption capacity of 1.1%, a ﬁneness modulus 
of 2.42, and an apparent density of 2610 kg/m3, as determined according to the 
Chinese standard JGJ 52–2006 [43]. The NCA was crushed limestone sourced from 
a local plant in Nanjing. RCA is obtained by crushing concrete components with 
a compressive strength of 35 MPa, as shown in Fig. 31.1. The WPFs utilized were 
derived from waste PET bottles. The bottles were manually cut into small pieces 
with a dimension of 0.3 mm in thickness, 3 mm in width, and 20 mm in length. The 
WPFs exhibited a speciﬁc gravity of 1.12, a density of 1250 kg/m3, and a tensile 
strength of 57 MPa. Table 31.1 illustrates the mix proportions of WPF-RA-SCC utilized in this study, 
with each specimen designated as A (B, C, D)-X-Y-2 (3). In this notation, A, B, C, 
and D represent the volume content of WPFs, namely 0%, 0.5%, 1.0%, and 1.5%, 
respectively; X indicates the substitution rate of RCA, and Y indicates the substitution 
rate of mineral admixture. 2 represents a ternary mix, incorporating FA and GGBS 
to partially replace cement; 3 indicates a quaternary mix, involving FA, GGBS, 
and SF to partially replace cement. For example, specimen B-100-75-3 indicates a 
quaternary mix containing 100% RCA and 75% SCM (25% FA + 25% GGBFS 
+ 25% SF) as well as strengthened

with 0.5% WPFs. The detailed information of 
concrete proportions can be obtained in the Guo et al. [28]. The fresh tests of all WPF-RA-SCC mixes were performed align with SCC Spec-
iﬁcation and Guidelines [44]. The slump-ﬂow value, T500 slump ﬂow time, J-Ring

ability and segregation resistance were evaluated. The detailed fresh properties of 
all WPF-RA-SCC mixes were presented and analyzed in a preceding paper [10]. Some of the fresh properties are presented in Table 31.2. As shown in Table 31.2, 
the addition of WPFs consistently impaired the workability of RA-SCC mixes. The 
slump-ﬂow value decreased and the SF-JF value and T500 slump ﬂow time increased 
as WPF content increased. Nevertheless, the segregation rate was reduced with the 
inclusion of WPFs. Although the inclusion of WPFs had a negative effect on the fresh 
properties, the vast majority of WPF-RA-SCC mixes had excellent fresh properties 
and met the limitation described by EFNARC [44]. This is ascribed to the inclusion 
of various SCMs considerably enhanced the workability of WPF-RA-SCC mixes. 31.2.2 
Test Results 
The cubic and axial compressive strength, splitting tensile and ﬂexural strength, of 
all specimens are summarized in Table 31.2. The performance of WPF-RA-SCC 
mixes with various WPFs and SCM is comparatively analyzed in detail, as well as 
the coupling effect of WPFs and various SCM combinations. As shown in Table 31.2, the addition of RCAs declined the cubic compressive 
strength, which is aligned with the prior test results [34]. This is mainly ascribed 
to inferior performances, such as porosity, high water absorption and residual old 
mortars of RCAs. Finally, it is obvious that the incorporation of SCMs using 
different combinations signiﬁcantly affected the cubic compressive strength. Quater-
nary mixes containing of FA, GGBFS, and SF had the highest compressive strength. The least strength was obtained for binary mixes with FA. This is ascribed to the 
pozzolanic reaction and the ﬁlling effect, as well as the concurrent effect of these 
mineral admixtures [45]. 31

Z. Guo et al. The relationship between WPF content and the compressive strength of WPF-
RA-SCC mixes can be clearly obtained from Table 31.2, which demonstrates that 
the incorporation of WPFs is effective in enhancing the compressive strength of RA-
SCC. The highest increase in compressive strength was achieved with the addition 
of 1% WPFs. The addition of 0.5% WPFs had the least enhancement. According 
to the fresh tests, WPF-RA-SCC mixes with 0.5% and 1.0% WPFs had superior 
workability, resulting in the uniform distribution of WPFs within the concrete matrix, 
which improved the homogeneity and reduced the internal voids. Additionally, the 
incorporation of WPFs hindered the formation and expansion of cracks and reduced 
the stress concentration at crack tips. Similar ﬁndings were obtained in previous 
studies [46, 47]. 31.3 
Research Methodology 
31.3.1 
Life Cycle Assessment 
Recently, the environmental performance of RAC has been given substantial atten-
tion and extensive studies have been conducted by employing the LCA method, 
which systematically investigates the environmental burdens and calculates resource 
inputs and environmental outputs throughout a product’s life cycle, thus assessing 
the environmental performance and proposing the potential improvement options 
[48]. Some research has addressed the positive environmental effects of using recy-
cled materials in concrete [49, 50]. In this paper, LCA was employed to study the 
environmental performance of WPF-RA-SCC mixes. A comprehensive introduction 
of the LCA methodology is given in international standards ISO14040 [51] and 
ISO14044 [52] and the LCA methodology commonly includes four stages (Goal 
and Scope Deﬁnition, Life Cycle Inventory (LCI), Life Cycle Impact Assessment 
(LCIA), and Interpretation). The study aims at quantifying and comparing the environmental performance of 
WPF-RA-SCC with various WPFs, RCAs, and SCMs. The functional unit used is 
1 m3 concrete mix, which was commonly adopted in prior research. Additionally, 
the cradle-to-gate system boundary was employed in this study, which consists of 
material production and transportation and the production of concrete in the plant 
[53]. The total LCI i

nventory for 1 m3 WPF-RA-SCC production is shown in Table 31.3, 
which was obtained from literatures, monitoring analysis, interviews and ﬁeld inves-
tigation with local suppliers and databases, including the Ecoinvent database in 
Simapro 9.0. The inputs include comprise energy (i.e., electricity and fuels) and 
raw materials, and outputs include the emissions and pollutants (i.e., CO2,  NOX, 
CH4,  SO2, CO, etc.). NCA was obtained from a quarry situated in Anhui Province 
and transported to Nanjing by trucks over a distance of 500 km. RCAs were obtained

31
LCA of Self-Compacting Concrete Incorporating Waste Plastic Fibre …
695
from a demolished concrete structural element in Nanjing. The concrete compo-
nents were carried to a nearby recycling plant by truck over a distance of 20 km and 
were crushed to produce RCAs. Natural sand was purchased from a supplier near the 
concrete plant and transported by truck; The distance is 50 km. Barjoveanu et al. [54] 
concluded that the environmental pollution caused by landﬁlling or incinerating WPF 
is signiﬁcantly higher than that of recycling WPF. WPFs used in this test is produced 
by manually cutting PET bottles, which produces much little CO2 during the hand-
cutting process. Therefore, the environmental impact of WPF preprocessing adopted 
in this study was ignored. In addition, Foolmaun and Ramjeeawon [55] investigated 
and compared the environmental and social impacts of four disposal methods of 
waste PET bottles and concluded that the landﬁlling WPF will produce CO2 of 1.19 
per kg, which was adopted as the WPF inventory (a negative values of 1.19 per kg) 
in this study. The cementitious binders such as cement, FA, GGBFS, and SF were 
all obtained from local suppliers and transported to the concrete plant by truck over 
50 km. Electricity and fuel were used to produce concrete in the concrete plant, and 
the energy depletion and related criteria air pollutants were determined from the ﬁeld 
statistic investigation at the concrete factory. In this study, several impact categories described in CML2001, such as global 
warming potential (GWP [kg CO2 eq.]), acidiﬁcation potential (AP [kg SO2 eq.]), 
eutrophication potential (EP [kg PO4 3− eq.]), human toxicity potential (HTP inf. [kg 1,4-DCB eq.]), and photochemical ozone creation potential (POCP [kg Ethylene 
eq.]) were employed and calculated align with CML mid-point method. 31.3.2 
Life Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA) 
This study employs Environmental Life Cycle Cost analysis (E-LCCA) proposed by 
international standard ISO 15686 [56] to evaluate the economic feasibility of WPF-
RA-SCC. According to the framework proposed by the Society of Environmental 
Toxicology and Chemistry, E-LCCA is a common economic tool used in conjunction 
with LCA [57], which translates the environmental impacts into economic costs. Because environmental emissions represent abstract hidden costs, the calculation of 
these hidden costs is both complex and challenging. Since

monetary units serve as 
the sole measure for LCCA, Willingness to Pay (WTP) was adopted in this study to 
convert environmental impacts into monetary units, facilitating the calculation of the 
E-LCCA for WPF-RA-SCC. WTP is now mostly used internationally to capture the 
costs caused by environmental impacts, which has been widely used in environmental 
and economic impact assessment [58–61]. Considering the system boundary of this study is cradle to gate, the effects of 
operating and end-of-life costs are not considered, assuming the entire life cycle is 
completed within a relatively short timeframe. In the E-LCCA analysis, the total 
life cycle costs are divided into ﬁxed costs and variable costs. In this study, variable 
costs were calculated, which include direct production costs, environmental emission 
costs, and so forth. 696

Environmental Costs 
(1) Landﬁll costs for waste concrete 
Currently, waste concrete in China is primarily disposed of through landﬁlling, 
accompanied by a prescribed pollution fee. This study proposes for the reuse of coarse 
aggregates, thereby circumventing the landﬁlling of waste concrete. Consequently, 
the avoidable cost of waste concrete disposal was calculated from Eq. (31.7): 
Cdis = m1 ∗ C s
(31.7)
where Cs is the sewage charges per ton of waste concrete. According to China’s 
pollution fee collection standards [68], a fee of 25 RMB per ton of waste concrete is 
charged. With China’s escalating focus on environmental conservation, it is projected 
that pollution fee rates will increase. (2) Cost of landﬁlling of plastic waste 
According to statistics, the plastic waste recycling rate in China is approxi-
mately 17% [66], while the landﬁlling rate is about 80% on average [25]. Therefore, 
assuming that the WPF recovered in this study was originally intended for landﬁlling, 
the avoidable landﬁlling processing fee was calculated using Eq. (31.8). Clan = m2 ∗ C i
(31.8)

which is 973 RMB/tonne [69]. 31
