Multi-Criteria Decision-Making Analysis 
The feasibility of using RCAs, SCMs and WPF to produce sustainable SCC must 
be analyzed with respect to technical performance, environmental impacts, and 
economic beneﬁt to improve the recycling efﬁciency of these waste materials and 
waste plastic management, which is a multifaceted issue involving a range of consid-
erations. Therefore, the most appropriate approach to address this concern is MCDA, 
which is a set of methods and process that can be applied to the sustainable assessment 
of complex issues involving multiple interests and viewpoints [71]. In this study, the VIKOR MCDA method was employed to assess the environ-
mental, economic, and technical criteria of the production of WPF-RA-SCC as well 
as seek the optimal mixtures. The fresh and hardened properties, environmental 
and economic performance was integrated to evaluated and identify the optimal 
concrete mixture. By considering decision makers preferences and integrating eval-
uation indicators, VIKOR method aims to maximize group beneﬁts and minimize 
individual regrets. Through the assessment, comparison, and ranking of alternative 
solutions based on stability criteria of the optimal solution and the selection of one or 
more compromise solutions, the VIKOR method aims to strike a reasonable balance

700
Z. Guo et al. between different decision variables and possibilities, minimizing subjectivity [72, 
73]. The process begins by determining the relevant weights for each criterion. Then, 
a speciﬁc method must be selected to rank and choose the alternative solutions [39, 
74]. The VIKOR MCDA steps used in this study include deﬁning the problem, 
identifying the criteria, assigning weights, evaluating alternatives, and ranking the 
solutions. [72, 73]. This study employed the VIKOR method to calculate and rank 10 alternatives 
of WPF-RA-SCC based on four standardized functions for workability, mechanical 
properties, environmental impact, and economic impact, namely F1,  F2,  F3, and F4, 
respectively. Index F1 was related to the workability, index F2 was associated with 
compressive strength and ﬂexural strength, index F3 corresponded to ﬁve environ-
mental impact categories (GWP, AP, EP, HTP, ODP), and index F4 was related to 
economic cost. Firstly, F1,  F2,  F3 and F4 were normalized using the aggregation 
function Eq. (31.10):

Results and Discussions 
31.4.1 
LCA Results and Discussions 
Table 31.7 summarizes the calculated impact indicators resulting from the production

of 1 m3 concrete. The calculated GWP (kg of CO2-eq/m3) from the production of 
1  m3 WPF-RA-SCC is shown in Table 31.8, as well as the contributions to the GWP 
from various concrete constituents. It is obvious from Table 31.8, that normal SCC 
A-0-0 had the highest GWP, which is mainly due to cement production emitting the 
highest GHG emissions (66% to 95% of GWP) [11]. It is apparent from Tables 31.7 
and 31.8 that the replacement cement with SCMs signiﬁcantly reduced the GWP. For example, when cement was 50% and 75% replaced with SCMs, GWP decreased 
by 42.8% and 65.0%, respectively. Teixeira et al. [75] had similar results. At last, it 
is clear from Table 31.8 that the inclusion of WPFs consistently reduced the GWP, 
which is ascribed to the negative value of WPF on the GWP emissions. For example, 
the inclusion of 0.5%, 1.0% and 1.5% WPFs reduced the GWP by 7.7%, 15.3% and 
23.0%, respectively. This is primarily attributed to the incineration and landﬁll of 
WPFs generating huge amounts of pollutant gases. The effect of RCAs, various SCM combinations and various WPF content on the 
AP, EP, HTP, and POCP is similar to that on the GWP. It can be seen from Table 31.7 
that the AP, EP, HTP, and POCP reduced when NCAs were 50% and 100% replaced 
by RCAs, which is ascribed to the much shorter transportation of RCAs. Moreover, 
as shown in Table 31.7, replacement cement with SCMs had notable potential to 
decrease AP, EP, HTP, and POCP. To search for an optimum content of RCAs, WPFs, and SCM combinations, 
the CO2-eq intensity, which is the ratio of calculated GWP to the compressive 
strength, was utilized to analyze the relationship between strength and environmental

impacts [76]. The calculated CO2-eq intensity per compressive strength of WPF-RA-
SCC mixtures is given in Table 31.9. The  CO2-eq strength signiﬁcantly decreased 
with SCM content increasing (i.e., cement content decreasing) (Table 31.9). At a 
speciﬁed compressive strength, the CO2-eq intensities can be substantially reduced 
by replacing cement with SCMs, especially at 90 days. For example, at a 90-day 
compressive strength of approximately 48.14 MPa, specimen A-100-0 with 100% 
cement produced 10.6 kg CO2-eq/MPa, while quaternary mixes A-100-75-3 with 
75% SCMs produced 3.63 kg CO2-eq/MPa. A reduction of about 65.7% in CO2-
eq intensity was obtained by replacement cement with SCMs (GGBFS, SF, and 
FA), which demonstrates that substituting cement with mineral admixtures (GGBFS, 
SF, and FA) considerably declined the CO2-eq intensities. It can also be seen from 
Table 31.9 that the inclusion of WPFs effectively reduced the CO2-eq intensity. For 
instance, the CO2-eq intensity of mix D-100-75-3 with 1.0% WPFs is 33.6% less than 
that of mix A-100-75-3 without WPFs at 90 days. Quaternary mixes D-100-75-3 with 
100% RCAs and 75% SCMs (FA + GGBFS + SF) and 1.5% WPFs had the lowest 
90-day CO2-eq intensity (2.41 kg CO2-eq/MPa). Therefore, for a given performance, 
such as strength, the combined incorporation of WPFs and SCM combination (FA, 
GGBFS, and SF) signiﬁcantly reduced the CO2-eq intensity of WPF-RA-SCC mixes. The experimental result reveals that the combined incorporation of WPFs and 
various mineral admixtures produced a signiﬁcant concurrent effect, which resulted 
in an additional enhancement in the net gain of hardened properties and dramat-
ically enhanced the mechanical properties of WPF-RA-SCC. This is commonly 
ascribed to several reasons. Firstly, incorporating different types of SCMs, such 
as FA, and GGBFS, can effectively improve the fresh properties. Therefore, WPFs 
can be uniformly distributed in the concrete matrix. Secondly, the crack-bridge effect 
of WPFs led to a load transfer from cement paste to ﬁbers, thus lowering the stress 
concentration and slowing down the occurrence and propagation of cracks. At last, 
the pozzolanic reaction and hydraulic activity, as well as the remarkable syner-
gistic effect due to the inclusion of various mineral additives such as FA, GGBFS,

LCCA Results and Discussions 
The total cost CE-LCCA of each WPF-RA-SCC mixture is presented in Table 31.10. It is clear from Table 31.10 that Cmat (raw material cost) accounted for the largest 
proportion in E-LCCA, ranging from 67.3% to 91.8%. The second largest proportion 
was Ceni (Environmental loads from raw material production), which accounted for 
13–27% of the total E-LCCA. In addition, Cdis (avoidable cost of waste concrete 
disposal) and Clan (avoidable cost of landﬁlling waste plastics) exhibited a net cost 
beneﬁt with the inclusion of RCA and WPF, stemming from the avoidance of disposal 
costs associated with landﬁlling of waste concrete and incinerating of plastic waste, 
indicating that recycling RCA and WPF to produce RA-SCC not only effectively 
reduces the disposal costs of waste concrete, but also increases the recycling rate 
of waste plastics, contributing to a more sustainable construction practice. This was 
consistent with the ﬁndings of Zhang et al. [58] that the production of recycled 
aggregate concrete can effectively reduce the CDW disposal costs and the associated 
transportation costs. It also can be seen from Table 31.10, the substituting of 75% cement with SCMs 
resulted in a 31.3% reduction in the total cost CE-LCCA. In addition, the total cost 
CE-LCCA was further reduced with the addition of WPF. For example, the total cost

of the quaternary mix D-100-75-3 containing 1.5% WPF was 4.9% lower than that 
of the mix A-100-75-3 without WPF. This is mainly due to the synergistic effect of 
the SCMs and the WPFs that resulted in lower environmental impacts, thus reducing 
the environmental costs of the RA-SCC. Saha et al. [66] obtained similar results that 
the total cost of concrete decreased with increasing of plastic waste content. This is 
mainly due to the reduced environmental and social costs resulted from the recycling

of plastic waste. At last, as shown in Table 31.10, when NCA was fully replaced 
with RCA, Ctra (transportation cost) decreased by 82%, which was attributable to 
transportation distance for RCA being signiﬁcantly shorter compared to NCA, thus 
reducing economic costs and highlighting the logistical and cost efﬁciency of using 
recycled concrete aggregates. Tables 31.7 and 31.10 presents a comparison of LCA and LCCA for the production 
of WPF-RA-SCC. The ﬁve environmental impacts categories and the corresponding 
costs related to WPF-RA-SCC production exhibited similar trends. When cement was 
replaced with SCMs, both environmental impacts and E-LCCA showed an overall 
decreasing trend. In addition, the inclusion of WPF led to a slight reduction in envi-
ronmental impacts and E-LCCA. In addition, it is clear from Tables 31.7 and 31.10 
that quaternary mix D-100-75-3 with 1.5% WPF and 75% SCMs had the lowest 
environmental impacts and economic costs, with E-LCCA that was 35% less than 
that of conventional concrete (0-0) and GWP value that was 73.9% less than that of 
conventional concrete (0-0). 31.4.3 
Multi-Criteria Decision-Making Analysis 
The MCDA using VIKOR was conducted to qualitative and quantitative assess the 
workability, mechanical properties, environmental impact, and economic cost of the

706
Z. Guo et al. production of WPF-RA-SCC, thus ranking the various alternatives and identifying 
the optimal solution. Table 31.11 presents the technical performance (segregation 
rate and compressive strength) of WPF-RA-SCC mixtures, which were integrated 
with environmental impacts and economic cost to evaluate the sustainability aspect 
of WPF-RA-SCC. Detailed information in terms of fresh and hardened properties 
are provided in the study conducted by Dong et al. and Guo et al. [10, 28]. From 
Table 31.11, it is clear that segregation rate consistently reduced as the content of WPF 
increased. It can also be seen from Table 31.11 that quaternary mix C-100-75-3 with 
1.0% WPF achieved the highest compressive strength at both 7 and 28 days, which 
was 30.4% and 12.5% higher than that of conventional concrete 0-0. Meanwhile, 
quaternary mix D-100-75-3 with 1.5% WPF had the highest ﬂexural strength at 
28 days, which was 60% higher than that of conventional concrete 0-0. Table 31.12 presents the results of the VIKOR MCDA method of all WPF-RA-
SCC alternatives. Considering Q refers to a linear interpolation of S and R, the results

the ﬁnal list of hierarchical alternatives in this study. As shown in Table 31.12, when 
all ﬁve criterias are assigned to equal importance, the two compromise solutions 
were D-100-75-3 and C-100-75-3. The advantage of C-100-75-3 over D-100-75-

Conclusions 
A novel methodology for VIKOR MCDA for WPF-RA-SCC was presented in 
this paper, by integrating workability, mechanical properties, environmental and 
economic performance to identify optimal concrete mixture with superior mechan-
ical properties, lower carbon footprint and economic cost. The main research ﬁndings 
were listed as following: 
1. LCA results indicated that the substitution of cement and natural aggregates with 
RCA and SCMs, respectively, and the inclusion of WPFs in SCC production 
resulted in a substantial reduction (up to 73.9%) in the environmental load. In 
addition, the type of concrete with the lowest environmental impacts of all the 
categories evaluated was quaternary mix D-100-75-3 with WPFs and various 
SCM combinations, indicating that the combined addition of WPFs and various 
SCM combinations substantially decreased the carbon footprint, in addition to 
the improvement on the mechanical properties. 2. E-LCCA results indicated that the substitution of cement and natural aggregates 
with RCA and SCMs, respectively, signiﬁcantly reduced the total economic cost 
CE-LCCA. In addition, the total cost CE-LCCA was further reduced with the addition 
of WPF. Higher CE-LCCA were obtained for the conventional SCC than for the 
WPF-RA-SCC. The lowest CE-LCCA were determined for quaternary mix D-100-
75-3 with 100% RCAs, 1.5% WPF and 75% SCM, which is 34.7% less than that 
of normal SCC. 3. MCDA results indicate that the best option, considering technical performance, 
environmental impact, and economic cost, was quaternary mix C-100-75-3 
containing 100% RCA, 75% SCMs, and 1.0% WPF for all ﬁve classiﬁcations 
criteria in the list of Q, indicating that the inclusion of RCA, SCMs and WPF 
offered considerable beneﬁts

for environmental performance and economic cost 
in addition to the improvement on the engineering properties. Conventional SCC 
was identiﬁed as the worst option in all classiﬁcations of the criteria. By conducting LCA, LCCA and MCDA, the technical, economic and environ-
mental beneﬁts of recycling waste plastic, waste concrete and industrial waste into 
SCC production was highlighted in this study. In addition, for the ﬁrst time, this 
study speciﬁcally addressed the application of VIKOR MCDA to identify the optimal 
solution to manufacture sustainable concrete by integrating technical, environmental 
and economic performances of WPF-RA-SCC and proposed a novel approach for 
prospective decision-making as well as presented new method for prioritizing mate-
rials selection, thus promoting the sustainability of recycling of waste plastic and 
waste concrete. Further research is recommended to consider more factors and vari-
ables, such as further evaluation of economic impacts at the societal dimension. 31
LCA of Self-Compacting Concrete Incorporating Waste Plastic Fibre …
709
Moreover, the above conclusions are valid only in the cradle-to-gate system boundary 
adopted in this study, and further studies in different systems and extended bound-
aries are needed to expand the technical, environmental, and economic feasibility of 
WPF-RA-SCC application. Acknowledgements This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China

Chapter 32 
CO2 Sequestration and Storage 
in Recycled Concrete Waste: 
An Emerging Solution for Sustainable 
Construction 
Hamideh Mehdizadeh and Mohammad Hajmohammadian Baghban 
Abstract Our world faces critical environmental challenges, such as greenhouse 
gas emissions, natural resources depletion, and solid waste disposal. Concrete, the 
second most consumed material on Earth after water, is responsible for 8–10% of 
global CO2 emissions, primarily stemming from clinker production. Meanwhile, the 
substantial volume of concrete waste (CW) generated throughout its lifecycle, along 
with the rising global demand for concrete due to rapid urbanization, exacerbates 
these pressing concerns. Accelerated carbonation of CW is an effective method to 
consume CW and sequester long-lasting CO2. Upcycling carbonated CW to substi-
tute aggregate and cement in sustainable construction materials can also beneﬁt in 
reducing CO2 emissions and conserving natural resources. However, bridging the 
gap between research and practice by promoting the effective use of carbonated 
concrete waste in the construction sector is the key for achieving this goal. This 
chapter thus discusses advancements in the carbonation of concrete waste, focusing 
on the carbonation mechanism and its strategic application to various recycled CW 
fractions, namely recycled coarse and ﬁne aggregates, as well as recycled concrete 
powders. 32.1 
Introduction 
The cement and concrete industry is one of the major contributors to CO2 emis-
sions (8–10%) and heavily depends on natural resources. Recycling concrete-based 
components in construction and demolition (C&D) wastes at the end of the life of 
concrete structures is considered an ideal strategy to address the environmental chal-
lenges faced by the industry [1]. Billions of tons of concrete waste enter the global

715

716
H. Mehdizadeh and M. Hajmohammadian Baghban
C&D waste stream annually, with amounts rising due to rapid urbanization. To miti-
gate the environmental impacts of concrete production and save natural resources, 
technologies have recently been developed to recycle concrete waste into aggre-
gates with two size fractions: below 4.75 mm (recycled concrete ﬁnes, RCFs), and 
above 4.75 mm (recycled coarse aggregates, RCAs) [2, 3]. Despite the potential 
to recycle 50–90% of concrete waste as aggregates, the inferior properties of recy-
cled aggregates compared to natural aggregates result in their down cycling or

use 
in applications like road construction, rather than reintroducing them into concrete 
production. For example, the hydrated cement paste residue on the surface of RCAs 
after crushing increases their porosity and water absorption, leading to the formation 
of weak interfacial transition zones (ITZ) among old cement paste, RCA, and new 
cement paste, which lowers the performance of new concrete [4]. In comparison, 
RCFs, with a higher content of residual cement pastes due to their ﬁner particle 
size, have more challenges regarding the workability, drying shrinkage, and trans-
port properties of concrete [5]. Consequently, only very low amounts of untreated 
RCAs and RCFs are used in concrete production. Alongside the interest in recycling concrete waste into aggregates, there has been 
growing attention on re-utilizing the waste powder generated during the recycling 
process, particularly those with size fractions below 0.15 mm [2, 3]. Studies have 
shown that recycled concrete powders (RCPs) contain 80–90% hardened paste, 
which is signiﬁcantly higher than that found in RCFs, making them more like 
hydrated cement [6]. Despite efforts to reuse RCPs as a cement substitute, the 
signiﬁcant amount of gel pores in RCPs and their high-water absorption proper-
ties hinder the direct use of untreated RCPs in cementitious binders. Moreover, 
RCPs have poor pozzolanic activity compared to cement particles, resulting in the 
poor mechanical and durability performance of cementitious materials [7–9]. For 
instance, Mehdizadeh et al. [7] have reported that replacing 10–30% of cement with 
untreated RCPs causes a 12–35% reduction in the 28-day compressive strength of 
cement paste. Hence, it is crucial to enhance the properties of recycled concrete waste 
(i.e. RCA, RCF, and RCP) to facilitate their effective utilization in cement-based 
materials. Accelerated mineral carbonation, also known as CO2 mineralization, involves the 
reaction of oxides such as CaO or MgO in cementitious materials with dissolved 
CO2 to produce Ca/Mg carbonate minerals. These carbonate phases exhibit thermo-
dynamic stability under atmospheric conditions, providing a permanent CO2 storage 
solution [2]. In recent years, extensive research has been conducted on the carbona-
tion of concrete waste, aiming to strengthen the RCAs and RCFs, as well as enhance 
the reactivity of RCPs [2–9]. It has also been reported that the carbonation of concrete 
waste can effectively capture about 7.9 kg CO2/t of RCA, 20 kg CO2/t of RCF, and 
190 kg CO2/t of RCP, which is signiﬁcantly higher than that achieved by chemical 
absorption methods (15–54 kg/t) [10]. To achieve a comprehensive recycling strategy for concrete waste in sustain-
able construction, this chapter discusses the advances in the carbonation of concrete 
waste. It will cover the carbonation strategies and their effects on the properties of

32
CO2 Sequestration and Storage in Recycled Concrete Waste …
717
RCAs, RCFs, and RCPs.

Additionally, the application of carbonated products in new 
cementitious materials will be described. 32.2 
Accelerated Carbonation of Recycled Concrete Waste 
Due to the high content of Ca-based minerals and alkalinity, various fraction sizes of 
concrete waste (CW) exhibit signiﬁcant carbonation reactivity in the order of RCPs 
> RCFs > RCAs. In a CO2-rich environment, the unhydrated calcium silicate phases 
(C2S and C3S) and hydration products (portlandite, calcium silicate hydrate, and 
calcium aluminate hydrates) associated with residual cement paste react with CO2 
to form calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and Si-rich gel. The accelerated carbonation 
of CW commonly takes place at atmospheric pressure and ambient temperatures, 
aiming to enhance feasibility for use in industrial applications. This process involves 
gas–liquid-solid reactions and can occur in either a dry environment with 50–80% 
humidity (semi-dry carbonation route) or a wet environment (aqueous carbonation 
route). In general, under ambient conditions, unhydrated clinker phases, hydrated 
products of residual cement paste in concrete waste fractions, as well as CO2 gas, 
dissolve in the carbonation solution, and resulting products precipitate from the 
solution through two main kinetics steps [11–13]. A schematic of phase evaluation 
and pH changes during the carbonation of residual cement paste in concrete waste 
is shown in Fig. 32.1. (1) In the ﬁrst kinetic step, carbonation is limited by the availability of CO2 and the 
dissolution rate of Ca-based components in concrete waste. The pH of the solu-
tion rises to 9–12 within few minutes due to the rapid dissolution of hydrates in 
cement paste (Ca(OH)2, followed by ettringite, AFm phases and partial decalci-
ﬁcation of the calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) phases) and CO2 consumption. This pH increase is inﬂuenced by particle size and the residual cement paste 
content in concrete waste fractions [14–16]. It is important to note that CaCO3
Fig. 32.1 A schematic of phase evolution and pH changes during the carbonation of residual 
hydrated cement paste in concrete waste [14]

718
H. Mehdizadeh and M. Hajmohammadian Baghban
nucleation and precipitation are critical at this stage, and delays in nucleation 
can cause a pH decrease. (2) In the second kinetic step, the carbonation reaction is controlled by the disso-
lution rate of the hydrates and the transport of dissolved Ca ions to the surface 
of particles. As CO2 concentration increases in the solution over time, the pH 
begins to drop due to the reduction in saturation indices. At this stage, only 
the larger cement-paste grains react since smaller particles have already fully 
carbonated during the ﬁrst reaction step, leading to the precipitation of addi-
tional CaCO3. During this step, the ﬁnal carbonation of calcium silicate hydrate 
phases occurs, and all aluminum-bearing hydrates except hydrotalcite react with 
CO2, forming Si-rich gel and CaCO3. 32.3 
Properties and Appli

cation of Carbonated CW 
Materials 
32.3.1 
Carbonated Recycled Coarse Aggregates 
Physical and microstructural characteristics: Experimental studies have shown 
that carbonation reactions signiﬁcantly reﬁne the pore structure of RCAs, reducing 
the amount of capillary pores and total porosity by up to 30% [17]. This reﬁnement 
leads to a 5–45% decrease in the water absorption capacity of RCAs. Addition-
ally, the electrical conductivity and chloride permeability of RCAs can be reduced 
by up to 70% after 7 days of carbonation [18]. Notably, the reduction of porosity 
and densiﬁcation of RCA microstructure occurs not only in the residual paste on 
the aggregate surface but also in the old ITZ section, which can reduce the likeli-
hood of internal failure [19]. Moreover, the precipitation of CaCO3 on the surface of 
carbonated RCAs contributes to improved mechanical properties, resulting in higher 
microhardness and a lower of crushing value. This phenomenon could be related to 
the higher elastic modulus of carbonated products (CaCO3) compared to hydration 
products (i.e. Ca(OH)2 and C-S-H gel) [20]. Studies on RCAs of different origins, 
strength grades and particle sizes demonstrated that the improved mechanical prop-
erties of RCAs depend on the properties of original concrete. For instance, RCAs 
derived from high-strength concrete structures showed less improvement due to their 
lower initial porosity [21]. Application of carbonated RCAs as coarse aggregate in concrete: Recent research 
on RCAs has focused on improving their properties for use as coarse aggregate in 
concrete production. It has been found that the densiﬁcation of RCA upon carbonation 
enhances the compressive strength and durability of concrete made with carbonated 
RCAs compared to those made with non-carbonated RCAs [22] (see Fig. 32.2). Also, the strength loss of RCA-based is limited to 10% when 100% coarse aggregate

is replaced with carbonated RCAs, while its elastic modulus became closer to that 
normal concrete [22, 23]. The decrease in drying shrinkage by up to 25% and the improvement in water 
penetration and chloride resistance of concrete by up to 36% were other beneﬁts of 
carbonation of RCAs when they were used as coarse aggregates in concrete [22, 24]. Research ﬁndings have demonstrated that carbonated RCAs could serve as internal 
curing agents due to their porous structure, thereby facilitating additional cement 
hydration in the new ITZ (i.e. the region between RCAs and new binder) by gradually 
releasing water [25]. Besides, the CaCO3 precipitated on the surface of RCAs could 
act as nuclei for accelerating cement hydration, resulting in a 12% decrease in the 
porosity of the new ITZ in concrete. As a result, the bond between RCAs and the 
new cement paste was s

trengthened by up to 90% [26]. In summary, the densiﬁcation 
of RCAs upon carbonation treatment and the improved quality of new ITZ are key 
factors enhancing the performance of RCA-based concrete. 32.3.2 
Carbonated Recycled Concrete Fines 
Properties of carbonated RCFs: Compared to RCAs, RCFs exhibited higher water 
absorption and carbonation capacity due to their ﬁner particle size and greater residual 
hydrated cement paste. Water absorption in RCFs ranges from 4% to16%, depending 
on the amount of hydrated cement paste on the aggregate surface. However, it was 
found that carbonation reduces water absorption in RCFs by 3% to 60%, a more 
substantial decrease than in RCAs [3]. Interestingly, pre-soaking RCFs in limewater 
before carbonation further reduced the water absorption by 21% due to the formation 
of more CaCO3 [27]. Additionally, the carbonation of RCFs in a wet environment 
(i.e. aqueous carbonation route) was more efﬁcient than the dry carbonation route; 
so that, the aqueous carbonated RCFs showed similar water absorption and density 
value after 6 h of carbonation compared to 24 h of dry carbonation [28]. 720
H. Mehdizadeh and M. Hajmohammadian Baghban
In addition to the densifying of RCF microstructure caused by the precipitation 
of CaCO3, the formation of Si-rich gel with Q3 and Q4 silicon coordination also 
signiﬁcantly increased the pozzolanic activity of RCFs. Research has shown that 7– 
17% of the silica tetrahedral in the residual hydrated cement paste on the RCF surface 
participated in polymerization, resulting in the formation of Si-rich gel (see Fig. 32.3). As a result, when carbonated RCFs are used as sand in mortar or concrete production, 
the Si-rich gel formed by carbonation reacts with Ca(OH)2 in new cement paste to 
form secondary C-S-H gel, thereby increasing the pozzolanic activity of RCFs [29]. It is worth noting that the ﬁner fractions of RCF (i.e. 0.3–0.6 mm), as well as the 
aqueous carbonation route, enhanced the pozzolanic reactivity due to the formation 
of more Si-rich gel [30]. Another signiﬁcant approach for improving the properties 
of carbonated RCFs was to modify the polymorphs and morphologies of CaCO3. For instance, the formation of whisker-like CaCO3 during carbonation could act as 
micro-ﬁbers, strengthening the bonding between carbonated RCF and new cement 
paste in concrete [19]. Moreover, the formation of spear-like CaCO3 signiﬁcantly 
increased the surface roughness of RCFs, facilitating the effective interlocking of 
carbonated RCFs into new mortar or concrete. Application of carbonated RCFs as sand in construction materials: Recent 
research has shown that carbonating RCFs before use in concrete can mitigate the 
strength loss observed in RCF-based concrete [30]. This strength improvement could
Fig. 32.3 SEM images and microstructural changes of: (a) non-carbonated RCF, (b, d) RCF 
carbonated under pressurized dry route, and (c, e) RCF carbonated under aqueous route [3]

32
CO2 Sequestra

tion and Storage in Recycled Concrete Waste …
721
be attributed to the nucleation effect of CaCO3 and the pozzolanic reaction of the Si-
rich gel precipitated on the surface of carbonated RCFs, which accelerated the cement 
hydration. As a result, concrete containing carbonated RCF achieved a comparable 
strength to that of concrete made with natural sand [3], while also experiencing 
reduced chloride permeability and the risk of steel corrosion [24]. However, due to 
the higher water demand of carbonated RCFs compared to natural sand, it was still 
necessary to add an extra amount of superplasticizer to adjust the workability of the 
concrete. Interestingly, using carbonated RCFs as ﬁne aggregate in concrete was more 
effective in developing the strength of new ITZ than using RCAs as coarse aggregate, 
thus facilitating overall strength improvement [3]. From an economic perspective, 
the treatment of RCFs via accelerated carbonation was found to be a cost-effective 
method to improve the properties of weak recycled concrete aggregates (i.e. RCFs 
and RCAs) compared to the slurry coating modiﬁed method [31]. 32.3.3 
Carbonated Recycled Concrete Powders 
Carbonation mechanisms and carbonated products: The recycled concrete 
powders (RCPs) composed of 80–90 wt.% hydrated cement paste. Hence, most 
research has focused on the carbonation of hydrated cement paste without consid-
ering the inert materials present in real RCPs. Studies have shown that RCPs can 
nearly fully carbonate in a wet environment, whereas the carbonation degree reaches 
only 40–60% with the semi-dry carbonation route [14]. Like RCAs and RCFs, the 
main carbonation products of RCPs are CaCO3 and an amorphous alumina-silica gel, 
regardless of the reaction conditions (Fig. 32.4). At ambient temperature, calcite is 
the dominant carbonate product, whereas the presence of alkalis or sulfate ions in the 
carbonation solution promotes the formation of aragonite and vaterite polymorphs 
of CaCO3 [32, 33]. In the carbonation process, the amorphous alumina-silica gel 
(with a high Si ratio) also forms from the carbonation of ettringite, AFm phases, 
and C-(A)-S-H phases. It is worth noting that the phase assemblage of carbonated 
RCPs can be inﬂuenced by the composition of the carbonation solution. For instance, 
high sulfate concentrations in the carbonation solution may reduce CaCO3 forma-
tion due to gypsum precipitation, while high alkali concentration in the solution can 
counteract gypsum formation, increasing the carbonation potential. Application of carbonated RCPs as cementitious materials: Carbonated RCPs, 
regardless of the carbonation method, could be successfully utilized as supplementary 
cementitious materials (SCMs) up to 20% of binder without compromising compres-
sive strength [7–9]. The hydration mechanism of carbonated RCP-cements resembles 
that of ordinary Portland cement. In the blended cements, CaCO3 in carbonated RCP 
acts as a nucleation site for C3S hydration,

accelerating the cement hydration. In 
addition, the amorphous (Al)-Si gel reacts rapidly with Ca(OH)2 produced during 
the hydration of cement particles, leading to the formation of secondary C-(A)-S-H

722
H. Mehdizadeh and M. Hajmohammadian Baghban
Fig. 32.4 Mass changes of (a) hydrated and (b) non-hydrated components in RCP after carbonation 
[14]
gel [34]. It was also observed that the incorporation of RCPs decreased the ﬂowa-
bility and compressive strength of cement paste, with reduction becoming more 
obvious at higher RCP contents. Interestingly, carbonation treatment reduced the 
negative impact of RCPs on the fresh and mechanical properties of blended cements. Studies suggest that the blended cement containing carbonated RCPs could achieve 
a compressive strength of 43–61 MPa, depending on the water-to-binder ratio (0.3– 
0.4), which was notably 10–40% higher than those made with non-carbonated RCPs 
[7–9, 9, 34]. 32.4 
Challenges for Industrial Upscaling and Future 
Perspective 
Semi-dry carbonation of CW can be conducted in CO2 curing chambers where 
relative humidity and CO2 concentrations are controlled. Studies have shown that 
adjusting relative humidity at 50–80% and CO2 concentration above 20% is neces-
sary to maximize CO2 sequestration. However, the high energy consumption and 
long carbonation duration (28 days) pose signiﬁcant challenges for industrial appli-
cations. Compared to semi-dry carbonation, the aqueous carbonation route offers 
higher efﬁciency, completing the process in 3–6 h. This method enhances CO2 
diffusion and calcium leaching rate for carbonation by homogenizing CW and satu-
rating pores of CW during the reaction. Additionally, temperatures up to 100°C 
enhance the carbonation rate due to an increase in the leaching rate. However, the 
high-temperature requirement for aqueous carbonation complicates scaling up for 
real-world applications. In addition to carbonation efﬁciency, the environmental and economic impacts of 
carbonation routes are pivotal in decision-making for upscaling. The ﬁnancial bene-
ﬁts of re-circulating carbonated CW in the construction industry and reducing the 
costs of traditional recycling processes encourage the applicability of the carbonation 
process. However, the higher cost of producing carbonated products than the natural 
aggregates, attributed to the transportation distances, may reduce the ﬁnancial bene-
ﬁts. Therefore, optimizing the location placement for of units within the construction

32
CO2 Sequestration and Storage in Recycled Concrete Waste …
723
industry is essential. Moreover, most studies have focused on the carbonation of CW 
using pure CO2 gas, while the puriﬁcation of CO2 gas is an expensive process. Hence, 
the use of ﬂue gas for CW carbonation should be considered to further optimize CO2 
sequestration and carbonated products in actual industrial applications. 32.5 
Conclusions 
Accelerated carbonation provides a sustainable approach to achieving total r

ecycling 
of recycled concrete waste (CW) in C&D wastes, while also sequestrating CO2. This  
process involves the carbonation of residual hydrated cement pastes present in three 
distinct fraction sizes sourced from CW: recycled coarse aggregates (RCAs), recy-
cled concrete ﬁnes (RCFs), and recycled concrete powders (RCPs). The carbonation 
reactions typically occur in a moist or wet CO2-rich environment at atmospheric pres-
sure. Carbonating RCAs mainly consumes Ca(OH)2 in the residual cement paste to 
form CaCO3, resulting in a reduction in the porosity of aggregate by up to 30%, thus 
enhancing the mechanical strength and durability properties of RCA-based concrete. Notably, the involvement of C-S-H phases in ﬁner particles of CW in the carbon-
ation reaction not only improves their physical properties through the precipitation 
of CaCO3 but also enhances the pozzolanic reactivity of the RCFs and RCPs by 
forming of Si-rich gel. As a result, the modiﬁcation of RCF surfaces through the 
formation of needle-like and spear-like CaCO3 beneﬁts the bonding of RCF with the 
new cement paste in the mortar or concrete, while Si-rich gel enhances cement hydra-
tion in new ITZ. Moreover, incorporating carbonated RCPs up to 30% of cement or 
traditional SCMs in cementitious products holds promise for reclaiming CW and 
reducing global CO2 emissions. Acknowledgements Financial support from the European Union’s Horizon Europe research and

Analysis of Energy Systems. Recovering 
Nature-Based Solutions for Future 
Sustainable Development 
Anya Montserrat Pulido Cavada, Federico de Isidro Gordejuela, 
and Roberto Alonso González-Lezcano 
Abstract Nowadays, optimising the use of energy resources to reduce energy 
consumption is essential to stop the cycle of environmental pollution. The Anthro-
pocene, the current geological era that is drowning in overconsumption of resources, 
where energy plays a key role in achieving the energy and climate goals that many 
political and legislative agendas have been setting since the ﬁrst oil crisis, is a double-
sided coin. While energy plays a key role in the development of the world economy 
and the growth and development of ecosystems, it has a major impact, posing a chal-
lenge in addressing global warming and the depletion of biodiversity. This chapter 
aims to examine energy systems in the face of increasing global energy needs, antic-
ipating population and economic growth, and analysing resource availability and 
potential bottlenecks. It also proposes the earth as a solution based on nature and 
environmental protection within safe planetary biophysical limits that are already 
being exceeded. 33.1 Introduction 
Efforts to address the environmental impacts that humans have created through pollu-
tion, deforestation and other environmentally damaging actions are just beginning. The last few decades have witnessed the major repercussions of dependence on and 
intensive use of energy systems; the increasing acidiﬁcation of the oceans caused 
by the growing concentration of gases such as carbon dioxide, methane and nitrous

P. Cavada et al. oxide in the atmosphere is a clear reﬂection of the major impact of human activities 
on natural systems on a global scale through greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. At 
the same time, to account for increasing demand for food, agricultural runoff has 
generated large ‘dead zones’ in some areas of the ocean, leading to massive losses 
of marine biodiversity [1]. The construction sector accounts for 40% of global resource consumption and, 
together with the transport sector, represents the main driver of global demand for 
materials, followed by the food and energy sectors; together they account for 90% 
of global demand for natural resources, and this share is increasing steadily. The 
growing demand for minerals demanded by the construction sector dominates the 
global growth of raw material extraction, which has doubled its development in 
the last 30 years [2]. The feared resource scarcity that is already on the horizon 
seems to be less critical than the continuous impacts generated by their extraction, 
transformation, use and disposal of derived waste at the end of their useful life [3]. There is growing international concern about developing strategies to prevent, or at 
least reduce, a supply chain gap and to achieve this goal it is necessary to provide 
tools to monitor the use of materials, energy, waste and greenhouse gas emissions 
from the production-consumption chain [4]. Urgent and large-scale action must be 
taken to curb and reduce the increasing concentrations of gases in the atmosphere 
by considering the multiple pathways linked to changes in energy systems [1]: 
I. More effective management of human activities that interact with natural 
systems on a large scale; to this end, a balance must be achieved in the consump-
tion of energy sources, promoting clean and sustainable energy sources, as well 
as a reduction in the use of other materials such as black soot, a by-product of 
incomplete combustion of organic materials with high carbon content. II. Reduction of energy demand as a response to high energy requirements in 
sectors that are in continuous progress, such as the construction and trans-
port sectors. Any improvement in efﬁciency reduces the amount of energy 
supply needed; good insulation of infrastructures would imply a reduction in 
the consumption of indoor air-conditioning installations and, consequently, of 
water heating and/or cooling. III. Increased effectiveness in terms of energy conservation; low cost and high 
availability have led to imprudent use. According to Renewables, the share of renewable energy included in all other 
power generation systems in 2023 will grow by 50%, with China being the region 
with the highest growth rate, commissioning as much photovoltaic power in 2023 as 
the rest of the world in 2022. Despite the continued growth of renewables in recent 
years, there is an urgent need to respond to the current paradigm: an unprecedented 
increase in global energy consumption

in response to continued population growth 
which, in parallel, requires new infrastructure and higher energy demand. We are 
facing a snake biting its own tail, a cycle that responds to the accelerated demographic 
transition that took place since the ﬁrst Industrial Revolution and that has been made 
possible by the massive use of hydrocarbons. 33
Analysis of Energy Systems. Recovering Nature-Based Solutions …
729
On 25 September 2015, the UN General Assembly took place, where more than 
150 world leaders adopted 17 global goals as part of a sustainable development 
agenda. Within these 17 goals were 169 integrated targets covering economic, social 
and environmental spheres, all of which were to be achieved over the next 15 years. Now, with six years to go until the Agenda for Sustainable Development is achieved, 
are we any closer to meeting the proposed goals? 33.2 Analysis 
The last 50 years have seen signiﬁcant shifts in urban patterns [5], economic output, 
demographic indices [6] and individual income [7]. Such trends imply changes in 
natural resource consumption and environmental transformations at all scales, putting 
pressure on ecosystem health and biodiversity. One of the elements to consider when referring to Energy Sustainability is 
to reduce GHGs from continued energy use, while providing appropriate energy 
supplies to meet economic, food, manufacturing and mobility needs without compro-
mising future sustainable development. Lowering the costs and increasing the reli-
ability and development of clean energy infrastructures will require technological 
advances if much of future demand is to be met from renewable energy rather than 
relying on stored resources, the replenishment rate of which is lower than their utili-
sation rate. To achieve this goal, it is essential to achieve a systematic reduction in the 
intensive demand for material and energy resources in high-consumption contexts. This can be applied to several sectors; ﬁrstly, changes in food diets can reduce the 
land area required by 5% by 2060 compared to the levels required in 2020, due to a 
reduction in high-impact products—such as animal protein—and thus a reduction in 
food waste. Secondly, this change can be applied to the transport sector; encouraging 
the use of shared and active transport can lead to a 50% reduction in the stock needs 
of related materials, a 50% reduction in energy demand and a 60% reduction in GHG 
emissions. Neighborhoods characterised by a compact and balanced urban fabric and 
structure characterised by a continued use of recycled materials in construction and 
other circular economy measures can achieve reductions in building material stocks 
of up to 25% by 2060, leading to a reduction in energy demand and emissions of 
close to 30% compared to current trends [8]. The path to achieving sustainability on a global scale is narrowing with each 
passing day, due to the failure to deliver on most of the political commitments that 
have b

een made since the ﬁrst Earth Summit in June 1972. Ensuring continued 
progress requires integrating economic and environmental policies that enhance 
sustainable production and consumption within the main sectors—food, construc-
tion, transport and energy—and that result in a signiﬁcant reduction in material and 
energy supplies. Three scenarios to be considered that transcend the Sustainable 
Development Goals and the quest for energy efﬁcient infrastructures are analysed 
below, to support the introduction of a building material that was at the heart of much

Cavada et al. of the international vernacular architecture of the century prior to the Common Era: 
earth. 33.2.1 
Anthropogenic Mass Versus Biomass 
Due to the technological and economic transformation process brought about by 
the Third Industrial Revolution in 1970, the global population has progressively 
increased by 1.5% annually, reaching 8 billion people today, as shown in Fig. 33.1. During this period, Asia and the Paciﬁc have been the two regions with the highest 
recorded population growth rates, starting from a population of about 2 billion in 
1970 and reaching about 4.5 billion in 2024; this ﬁgure represents more than 50% of 
the world’s current population. However, population growth rates have shifted from 
an average of 1.9% at the end of the twentieth century to a decline of an estimated 
0.8% per year between 2020 and 2024. Paul Crutzen, a Dutch chemist who won the Nobel Prize for chemistry in 1995, 
popularised the term Anthropocene at the beginning of the twenty-ﬁrst century, refer-
ring to a new geological era that would succeed the Holocene—the period since the 
last ice age—and begin because of the global impact of human activities on terres-
trial ecosystems. Although the term “anthropocene”—from the Greek anthropos, 
meaning human, and kainos, meaning new—has been a contested term in the scien-
tiﬁc community, it has been associated by many with the onset of the Third Industrial 
Revolution. The anthropogenic mass generated since the second half of the twen-
tieth century has been accelerated by technological progress, overpopulation and 
consequently increased production and consumption. The continuous interaction of

these three factors has led to an unrestrained extraction and transformation of the raw 
materials available in nature, as well as to an uncontrolled expansion of agricultural 
land, urban networks and transport infrastructures and networks. Energy Science and Technology must come together to address the challenges of 
ecosystem health and sustainability. It is indisputable that Energy is key to ecosystem 
functioning and key to the global economy; however, it is a double-sided coin. In 
2018, Global Energy Needs were projected to increase by 60% in the face of projected 
population and economic growth by 2030 [9]. During the CAST (China Association for Science and Technology) Frontier Forum 
on Ecology and Energy in Beijing (China) in June 2018, the country of China as the 
world’s largest major emerging economy was taken as an example, looking at the 
main challenges and opportunities it would face in the years to come. Considering 
that the demographic population grew by 58% in 2016, it was estimated for the 
coming years [10]:
• An increase of about 50% in food demand, which would lead to an increase in 
the continued use of nitrogen fertilisers by 200% an

d phosphorus by 80%. • 19.7 billion tonnes of untreated rural wastewater per year. Among them, about 
4128 tonnes of phosphorus and 82,560 tonnes of nitrogen discharged into water-
sheds [11]. This continued discharge results in agricultural pollution and reduced 
runoff, causing major pollution of watersheds in the dry season and consequently 
affecting Food Security. • A production of more than 3.6 million tonnes of solid waste and around 900,000 
tonnes of toxic landﬁll leachate per day. This would bring a great risk to more 
than 16% of agricultural land due to heavy metal contamination [10, 12], among 
which 36.43% are classiﬁed as “unsafe” [13]. The demand for energy from fossil fuels such as oil, natural gas and coal continues 
to increase in proportion to population growth and the consumption demand it 
demands. In contrast, Renewable Energies such as wind, hydro and solar photovoltaic 
represent a small fraction of the world’s energy supply. Even if annual population 
growth rates can be maintained in the coming decades, it may take more than 20 years 
before renewable energy sources supply more than 20% of total electricity demand 
[1]. To achieve a reduction of energy resources, resource availability and poten-
tial bottlenecks must be analysed [10]. The geographically uneven distribution of 
resources is one of the main problems affecting energy production; a clear example 
of this is cobalt [14]. The rise of lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles has 
boosted demand for this material, with the highest concentration of deposits found in 
the Democratic Republic of Congo. If electric car production is to account for 20% 
of total vehicle production by 2025, it will require a doubling of lithium mining, 
which was around 140,000 tonnes in 2020 according to the US Geological Survey. All environmental impacts are increasing; waste and emissions have increased in 
proportion to the increase in demand for materials. The extraction and transformation 
of natural resources is responsible for more than 55% of greenhouse gas emissions 
into the atmosphere and thus for approximately 40% of health impacts from airborne

P. Cavada et al. particulate pollutants—these include heavy metals, volatile chemicals, combustion 
by-products and solvents. If land use change is considered, environmental impacts 
and risks from biodiversity loss increase to 60%, with biomass contributing the most 
at 28%, followed by fossil fuels and metallic and non-metallic minerals. 33.2.2 
Overconsumption by a Minority 
Today, the competencies to deal with anthropogenic impacts and their derivatives 
have not yet been brought together on a global scale; we are still moving towards a 
climate emergency that is reaching catastrophic levels for current and future genera-
tions [15]. The worsening of this situation hinders global cooperation, leading to frag-
mentation in political, economic and food systems, making it possible to aggravate 
other storage systems (such as a nuclear catastrophe) [16]. For the most part, ecological impacts are driven by the luxury consumption of 
most of the northern hemisphere and certain populations in other parts of the globe, 
whose emissions may become more complex to decarbonise compared to the emis-
sions emitted by those living with some level of sufﬁciency [17]; more importantly, 
despite the positive social outcomes that certain countries have achieved - considering 
factors such as average life expectancy and the Human Development Index—none 
have managed to stay below the planetary boundary [18]. Societies characterised by 
inequality, where the basic needs of the vast majority are insufﬁcient, can be a cause 
of socio-economic instability and violent conﬂict, episodes that may be exacerbated 
by the resurgence of climate change [19, 20]. The correlation between inequality and climate collapse is clear; reducing 
inequality in the social, economic and political spheres is key to enabling emissions 
mitigation [21]. Taking as an archetype the work done by Goldemberg et al. [22] and successive 
estimates of the energy required to ensure an optimal standard of living on a regional 
and global scale, the study by Joel Millward-Hopkins [23] updates the aforemen-
tioned model by assessing an inventory of consumption, technological advances and 
knowledge of human needs, thereby providing a series of static estimates of global 
energy needs for the year 2050. According to this analysis, the near-term scenario 
for high-income and lower-middle income individuals is virtually identical, except 
for the 1% of the population represented by the billionaire elite, whose lifestyle-
related ecological impact has a strong inﬂuence on future sustainable development 
because of their political power and investments in destructive industries [24, 25]. As a result, achieving an egalitarian quality of life, but with high material inequality 
close to current levels, could result in a doubling of energy consumption compared 
to an egalitarian world with inequalities based on needs alone. Estimating an 18% 
increase in global energy consumption by 2050 due to population g

rowth, it will 
be necessary to consider ambitious technology deployment accompanied by lower 
population growth or, instead, a deployment of negative emissions technologies and 
practices (NETP) to complement emissions reductions (or both). 33
Analysis of Energy Systems. Recovering Nature-Based Solutions …
733
The problems caused by economic inequality and ecological collapse have had a 
greater impact since the Third Industrial Revolution, leading to the implementation of 
multiple sustainable development goals and the creation of various social movements 
and groups such as Fridays For Future and Occupy Wall Street. If we look at the 
expectations suggested by researchers for future urban development, the evolution is 
more than positive; however, because of this reality, social inequality is increasing, 
a condition that tends to accompany the collapse of civilisations. While resources 
are exploited to meet the needs of the average citizen, the poorer regions experience 
the immediate effects; meanwhile, the elite citizen relies on uninterrupted luxury 
consumption. Due to the intensive exploitation of materials such as carbon, iron, steel and 
cement, and the growing demand and production of energy, global greenhouse gas 
emissions have been increasing considerably since 1970. According to an Emissions 
Database for Global Atmospheric Research (EDGAR) report conducted in 2023, in 
the last ﬁfty years, GHG emissions have doubled from 20 billion tonnes in 1970 to 
about 43 billion tonnes in 2020, without considering their global warming potential. Furthermore, the production of carbon dioxide (CO2) represents the largest propor-
tion of gases released into the atmosphere compared to other gases derived from 
the burning of fuels in various industries (Fig. 33.2), such as ﬂuorinated refrigerant 
gases (F-gases) or oligogases, such as methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O). The 
latter two, together with CO2, make up the main GHGs—Greenhouse Gases-, gases 
that concentrate in the atmosphere, trapping heat from the sun and preventing it from 
escaping into space, thus causing global warming. The behaviour of CO2 is completely different from the behaviour of oligogases; 
according to a study conducted by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change

and labour from 1970 to 2020

734
A. M.

Cavada et al. (ipcc) in 2007, any moderate reduction in CO2 emissions shows increasing concen-
trations due to the exchange processes characteristic of the carbon cycle in the climate 
system, such as the transfer of gases between the ocean and the atmosphere, and the 
chemical and biological processes that are integrated into it [26]. Considering the 
removal processes of various trace gases such as methane (CH4), which is removed 
from the atmosphere by chemical processes, and nitrous oxide (N2O), the largest 
percentage of which can be destroyed in the upper atmosphere by solar radiation, the 
atmospheric permanence of these gases can be relatively accurately determined, oper-
ating on a time scale of no more than 120 years. In contrast, deﬁning the atmospheric 
lifetime of CO2 is difﬁcult. From a study relating the residence time and removal processes of different gases 
in order to observe the evolution of concentrations from certain reductions—10, 30 
and 50compared to the current level—it concludes that a small or moderate reduction 
of CO2 emissions would not lead to stabilisation of its concentration, but would only 
reduce the rate of its growth over the next two decades, i.e. the current rate of carbon 
dioxide emission represents a higher rate than its rate of removal. According to data from joint research between Oxfam and the Stockholm Envi-
ronment Institute (SEI), the extreme inequality of carbon emissions in recent decades 
is closely linked to the average income level of the population. This study reﬂects 
how, during the years between 1990 and 2015 in which humanity doubled its concen-
tration of dioxide in the atmosphere, an enormous population gap occurred between 
individuals with the most and the least income. Consequently, the poorest 50% of 
the population—represented by approximately 3.1 billion people—only generate 
around 7% of global emissions attributed to individual consumption, compared to 
the richest 1% of the world population—represented by 63 approximately millions 
of people—who generate 15% of accumulated emissions; that is, twice as much 
as the poorest half of the population. As Fig. 33.3 shows, the percentage of people 
living in high-income countries has decreased by around 7% between 2000 and 2024; 
This translates into a lower population growth rate in regions with higher per capita 
income. However, low-income regions have recorded faster population growth, with 
an increase rate of 2.6% annually. The global Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita between 1970 and 2024 
multiplied by 2.4; However, substantial disparities between regions and different 
income groups have generated uneven annual GDP growth. Since the beginning 
of the twenty-ﬁrst century, the Asia–Paciﬁc region has overtaken the rest of the 
world as the most dominant resource extractor, being responsible for more than 
half of global greenhouse gas emissions. Returning to the example of China as one 
of the largest emerging markets in r

ecent years, according to a study carried out 
by the International Resource Panel in 2024, most of the materials used worldwide 
were extracted in middle-income countries-high. In 2020, 48.9 billion tons of natural 
resources—that is, 51% of the world total—were extracted in the Asia–Paciﬁc region, 
where, in addition, countries like China with an upper-middle income have replaced 
high-income population as main emitters (Fig. 33.3). This reﬂects a change in the 
global energy system along with a saturation of urbanization and living standards in 
the group of middle-income countries. However, in per capita terms, high-income

33
Analysis of Energy Systems. Recovering Nature-Based Solutions …
735
Fig. 33.3 Total domestic material consumption (DMC) related to greenhouse gases (GHG) 
emissions by seven world regions and income groups in 1970, 2000 and 2020
countries continue to represent the largest group of greenhouse gas emitters, about 
seven times higher than low-income economies since 1970. The population affected by greater economic inequality lives mostly in the 
country’s most vulnerable to climate change. This extreme inequality and the social 
and economic poverty it generates causes a CO2 emissions gap; In other words, the 
problem to be considered is not reduced to the imminent growth of the population, 
but to the level of income. People with daily incomes greater than $23—around 10% 
of the population—contribute 43% of CO2 emissions; On the contrary, those individ-
uals with an income below $1.25 per day—around 18% of the population—produce 
3% of CO2 emissions [27, 28]. According to the data collected in the twelfth Global 
Wealth Report of 2021, prepared by Credit Suisse Research Institute, it is ensured 
that the total wealth of the richest adults in the world has multiplied almost four-
fold, with an increase of 7.4% in world wealth. However, according to the NGO, the 
richest 5% of the world’s population is responsible for a third of the total increase in 
emissions (Fig. 33.4)  [29]. Global materials productivity, deﬁned as the relationship between GDP and 
national consumption of materials, increased by 0.7% on average annually between 
1970 and 2020. This improvement in productivity was favored due to various causes, 
such as the outsourcing of transformation processes to foreign countries and the 
change from the primary sector to tertiary sector activities. The material produc-
tivity gap in each region is more evident if we look at the income groups. In 1970, 
high-income countries had a productivity nine times higher than the productivity 
generated by low-income countries, with low and upper-middle income representing 
20% of the average for high-income countries. By 2024, a proportion thirteen times 
greater than that mentioned is expected. It is a fact: the excessive consumption of 
a wealthy minority that increases over the years is inﬂaming the climate crisis and 
slowing down efforts to address global impacts [8]. 7

Fig. 33.4 Increase in CO2 emissions from fuels and industry in proportion to the increase in urban 
disposable income in China from 1985 to 2025
The limits between stability, inequality and vulnerability to possible climate 
shocks have been little studied by the scientiﬁc community; No study to date has 
brought together the foundations that promote a healthy, resilient quality of life for 
humans and the planet, remaining under the ecological limits required to guarantee 
social security [18]. Without urgent and common action on a global scale to change 
the way resources are used, the extraction and transformation of material resources 
could increase by 60% by 2060 compared to the rates obtained in 2020; This trans-
lates into intensive exploitation of 100,000 to 160,000 million tons, exceeding the 
limits imposed by the Sustainable Development Goals to satisfy human needs. The question that arises then is how to stay in the race towards “zero emissions”? 33.2.3 
The City. Strategic Point of Action 
Information technologies, communication and energy systems continue to increase 
as urbanizations expand; The urban energy network demanded by cities represented 
two-thirds of the world’s energy in mid-2017, and this percentage is not hindered 
by the safe biophysical limits already exceeded in the ﬁrst decade of the twenty-ﬁrst 
century [30]. Among the main supply services that satisfy human needs, the following stand 
out:

2. Built environment

To provide the service to the population, all of them require an infrastructure and a 
series of speciﬁc materials to carry out their construction, maintenance and operation 
of said infrastructure. As can be seen in Fig. 33.5, during 2020, 59,000 million tons 
of materials were required to meet the demand of the construction, transportation 
and infrastructure sectors; This is equivalent to 55% of all material demand. Considering the Material Footprint (MF) index—an indicator based on demand, 
reﬂecting the material consumption and investment needs of a country’s capital 
regardless of its origin—the Asia and Paciﬁc regions recorded an increase of 27% 
in global material footprint between 2000 and 2020, reaching more than half of the 
registered global footprint with a value of 56%. Although North America, along with 
Europe, minimized this index by 17% during the same period, the per capita foot-
print was almost triple the world average. In parallel, looking at groups by income 
index, the ﬁgures between the high-income and upper-middle-income classes in 2000 
represented 51% and 34% respectively. In 2020 the ﬁgures were practically reversed 
(Fig. 33.5).

Based  on  Fi  g. 33.5, the construction sector, together with the transportation sector, 
is one of the sectors with the greatest potential to improve energy performance and 
achieve the energy and climate objectives that have guided numerous political and 
legislative agendas since the ﬁrst crisis of the oil: 
i. Sustainable Cities and Communities 
ii. Production and Responsible Consumption 
iii. Affordable and non-polluting energy 
iv. Health & Wellness

A. M.

Cavada et al. Upper-middle-income countries are responsible for the largest proportion of mate-
rials demand directed at the construction and transportation sector, with 68% of the 
total material footprint. On the contrary, in groups with a low-middle and low income, 
this percentage is represented by the food sector, which represents a total of 54% of 
the demand for materials in the group of individuals with a low income. This perspec-
tive is reiterated at the regional level in countries such as Africa, Latin America, the 
Caucasus and the Caribbean, where material provision systems respond to social and 
cultural practices. The massive growth in demand for natural resources, the paral-
ysis of the productivity of the use of materials and the beneﬁts derived from them, 
are distributed unevenly among nations and populations. This unsustainable scale 
of prolonged and comprehensive use of materials makes it impossible at different 
scales to achieve beneﬁts in the social, economic and environmental spheres. As the 
middle class expands and the industrialization process advances in response to this 
continuous growth, the corresponding increase in materials is evident, which brings 
with it an increase in the use of land and water, waste generation and production 
greenhouse gases; this situation intensiﬁes the pressure on the natural ecosystem 
when it comes to regenerating resources and managing waste. This opens the doors 
to the search for more eco-efﬁcient production systems and the implementation of 
greater green infrastructure [31]. The current problem is no longer focused on questioning the need for a transfor-
mation towards sustainable use of natural resources on a global scale; The concern 
lies in how to materialize this objective by ensuring decent standards of living at all 
scales within safe planetary limits [30]. Each challenge manifests itself at a local, regional and global scale, and must 
complement and support national policies to be able to integrate, ﬁrst of all, sustain-
able goods and services, regulating the marketing practices that cause uncontrolled 
consumption; secondly, deﬁne means for a sustainable implementation of resources 
that complement global agendas to achieve the United Nations SDGs; and thirdly, 
promote the circular economy, applying production and consumption models that 
contribute to prolonging the useful life of materials and achieving more efﬁcient 
production of goods and services through repair, reuse, renewal and recycling. The transition towards a sustainable and efﬁcient use of natural resources that does 
not lead to inequality and inefﬁciency requires the implementation of low-impact, 
high-performance supply systems. The problems are well known, but new approaches 
are required, and for this it is necessary to involve the public in the construction of new 
alternative models that ensure a future ecological civilization, raising awareness about 
climate change and its negative effects: sc

arcity of resources, pollution environmental 
degradation, degradation of ecosystem services and functions linked to the loss of 
habitats and species, loss of soil and agricultural production, and the scarcity of 
resources that hinders the supply of water, soil, energy and food, in a sustainable 
manner [10]. Between energy supply and demand trends, cities demand two-thirds of the 
world’s energy. In 2017, nuclear and coal energy production in Germany fell by 
51% (from 150 TWh to 73.6 TWh) and 22.5% (310 TWH to 240.1 TWh) compared

33
Analysis of Energy Systems. Recovering Nature-Based Solutions …
739
to production in 1990, leading to an increase in renewables by 95% (from 10 TWh to 
218.3 TWh). The great impact that future urbanization will have on energy consump-
tion is indisputable, potentially causing an excess in plans for the introduction of 
renewable energies [32]; However, the high rate of consumption demanded by cities 
makes them become the point of greatest strategic interest in which to implement 
new alternatives that bring us closer to meeting the SDGs. It is essential to curb fossil fuel production and investments in energy-intensive 
infrastructure if renewable solutions are to be promoted and future carbon lock-
ins avoided. To do this, it must be considered that the construction sector represents 
30% of national energy consumption and that, between the production, execution and 
operation of buildings and most of the materials used in architecture. In contemporary 
times, the sector is one of the largest emitters of GHG, as can be seen in Fig. 33.5. The large carbon footprint left by the modern city, added to other more indirect 
consequences such as inequality, crime and the heat island effect, raise alarm bells 
towards new future urban models. According to Marta Olazabal and María Ruíz de 
Gopegui, researchers at the Basque Center for Climate Change (BC3) in the magazine 
Landscape and Urban Planning, the current cities’ plans for adaptation to climate 
change in most cities are large cities in the world will not achieve effective results in 
the medium and long term. We are faced with a double situation: the need to confront the increase in popula-
tion that brings with it an increase in socioeconomic and political inequality, faced 
with the obligation to reduce global warming and the decrease in biodiversity derived 
from human activities, which they respond to the ﬁrst need. Since the end of the twen-
tieth century, there has been a call for the use of new technologies and alternative 
practices that respond to the current need to build with decarbonized materials that 
reduce the embodied carbon of buildings. Increasing investment in solutions based 
on nature and environmental protection favors:
• The adaptation of urgent measures to combat climate change (SDG 13), reducing 
extreme weather events, protecting and restoring eco-systematic services, 
improving the management and treatment of solid waste that degrade

s the soil, 
etc. • Sustainable management of forests and the ﬁght against desertiﬁcation, halting 
and reversing land degradation and loss of biodiversity (SDG 15) 
In retrospect, vernacular architecture has left great clues about how the design of 
the future building stock should be approached. This period, characterized by a close 
relationship between man and the environment, is based on an architectural design 
that responds to the cultural, technological, environmental and economic dimension; 
These four dimensions lay the foundations for a model that is respectful of the envi-
ronment, reﬂecting the identity of a territory and a culture that collects and transforms 
the resources that the environment offers. However, this type of traditional infras-
tructure closely related to human ecology and due to its low environmental impact, 
has not been compatible with the ideology of progress that prevailed throughout 
the twentieth century, which is why its use has been declining, being unique and

740
A. M.

P. Cavada et al. exclusively accessible to the very rich or the very poor. Such is the case of earth, 
a material that has been used worldwide throughout history due to its great acces-
sibility and versatility, its minimal cost and the history of tradition it has [33]; It 
currently constitutes around 30% of the world’s cultural heritage. Currently, there are numerous research groups and architectural studies that are 
dedicated to the research, application and dissemination of knowledge related to 
this type of architecture and its development along with other local materials. Both 
developing countries, where economical, quick and easy-to-build housing models 
are required, and more developed countries—such as the United States, Australia, 
France and Germany—have focused their research on experimental projects for 
improvements in composition, additives, compositional and aesthetic concepts [34]; 
However, there is a continuous struggle regarding the introduction of earth as a 
construction material in large cities, dominated by materials such as concrete and 
steel, which are a reﬂection of modernity and the industrial progress that has been 
developing since the Third Industrial Revolution. Referring to the words of Amer-
ican poet Robert Frost, “Humanity has reached a fork in the road”. It is clear that 
now we have to choose the road less traveled, because that will make the difference. In contrast, many ask: Why and how does the earth naturally incorporate high-tech 
properties for sustainable construction? 33.3 Results 
Adopting a supply systems perspective can help rethink the way human needs are 
met, designing new urban models that meet sustainability objectives. There are more 
and more platforms that integrate and share improvements in resource efﬁciency, 
development of circular economy and how to make use of existing ones. Such coali-
tions help provide resources and knowledge for the design of capacity development 
plans through demonstration facilities; An example of this is the Global Alliance 
on Circular Economy and Resource Efﬁciency (GACERE), the African Circular 
Economy Alliance, the Latin American Circular Economy Alliance or the Platform 
to Accelerate the Circular Economy (PACE). The Intergovernmental Scientiﬁc Policy Platform on Biological Diversity and 
Ecosystem Services (IPBES) is currently underway with the aim of, in cohesion 
with governments, academia and civil society, establishing a series of foundations 
that reinforce policy formulation to maximize the beneﬁts of biodiversity and ecosys-
tems for quality of life and health in an equitable manner. One of the main problems 
when it comes to ‘recycling’ a material after the end of its useful life is found in the 
chemical modiﬁcation to which it has been subjected in its extraction and transfor-
mation process; Most conventional construction materials have been extracted from 
bedrock and geological deposits and, during their manufacturing process, have been

chemically altered, hindering their ability to be recycled at low energy costs and 
making it impossible to return to the natural environment. The earth, on the other 
hand, adjusts to the principles of the circular economy, beneﬁting from its:

33
Analysis of Energy Systems. Recovering Nature-Based Solutions …
741
a. High Thermal Mass 
b. Noise Control 
c. Humidity control 
d. Pest Control 
e. Low Carbon footprint 
f. Good structural support 
g. Ease of Transportation and Automation of on-site manufacturing 
The technical barriers that arise from the use of earth as a construction material 
have been arousing great interest on the part of the scientiﬁc community since 1990 
and publications have multiplied since then. Other types of impediment categories, 
such as economic, organizational, political and social, have not yet been addressed 
in the scientiﬁc literature with such exhaustiveness; This may be one of the reasons 
why earth as a construction material remains a niche market despite beneﬁting from 
most of the attributes to combat the current and future climate and resource crises. Other impediments such as the inaccuracy of its composition in terms of adequate 
material content, the availability of land suitable for construction with earth and the 
impact on the availability of land that its development could cause are aspects to 
consider. However, it is clear the growing interest that has emerged in recent decades 
in relation to a search for energy balance in accordance with the trends of resource 
use in the built environment of the present and the future. 33.4 Conclusions 
The construction industry is responsible for high energy consumption, especially 
associated with the use of heating and cooling in buildings in which interior thermal 
comfort is sought, which has been altered in recent decades due to extreme changes of 
temperature resulting from climate change. For years, power generation has beneﬁted 
from non-renewable energy sources derived from fossil fuels (coal, oil and gas), 
which has caused a call for attention towards the development of new clean and 
renewable energy models and the implementation of new materials that minimize 
energy consumption in buildings thanks to their thermal storage capacity. During 
the last two decades, the various earth construction techniques have garnered great 
interest in research projects and scientiﬁc groups; All of this has optimized the 
modernization of the traditional techniques worked by the great masters who shaped 
the land, authors of constructions still present such as the ancient walled city of 
Shibam in Yemen or the citadel of Arg-é Bam, known for being the largest building in 
molded clay of the world. However, its introduction in large cities involves breaking 
down certain social, economic and political barriers, in addition to improving its 
industrialization and mechanization to optimize processes. Promoting the use of 
biodegradable materials such as soil will not

Abstract Coal ash mitigation and utilisation has been a major challenge in many 
countries with a greater dependence on coal power. The unutilised ash gets dumped 
in open ponds and dykes, wasting large amount of land and adversely impacting 
the local population, ﬂora and fauna. This study presents the current scenario of ash 
dumping in Australian ash ponds and their impact on population and biodiversity. The locations and areas of ash ponds were mapped for all ash ponds in Australia 
using satellite image analysis. Statewide data for pond sizes were compared with 
their population, province area, and power plant capacities. Potential risks to local 
population and biodiversity arising from ash ponds were also indexed and mapped. Possible beneﬁcial applications of ﬂy ash are further discussed to reduce the adverse 
impact of ash dumping. T. Gupta
