Coal has long served as a major global energy source. At present, ~ 27% of the 
world’s total energy demand is met by coal [1]. In 2022 the world produced 8,318 
million tonnes (Mt) of coal [2], out of which the major contributors were China (4237 
Mt), India (893 Mt), the United States (535), Indonesia (622 Mt) and Australia (446 
Mt) (IEA, 2022). While the proportional contribution of coal towards global energy 
needs may decrease due to the development of alternate energy sources, the total 
amount of coal produced and consumed globally is still growing, or is at least stable. Consequently, issues pertaining to coal waste management are also likely to persist. In this study, we explore the current scenario and options for coal ﬂy ash utilisation in 
Australia. Fly ash has been described as one of Australia’s biggest waste challenges 
[3], yet rarely does it enter the public consciousness. Mitigating the large volumes of ﬂy ash produced during the coal burning process 
is a major challenge. Annual amounts of ﬂy ash generated worldwide is in excess of 
900Mt per year [4]. This ﬁgure changes from country to country based on the ash 
content of the local coal. Australian coal has a relatively low ash content (10–15% 
[5]), resulting in the production of 11.4 Mt of ﬂy ash in year 2021 (ADAA, 2022). In 
comparison, other countries like India produce coal with a higher ash content (30– 
50% [6]). However, in comparison with the major coal-producing nations, Australia 
produces the largest amount of ash per capita. In 2021, Australia produced approx-
imately 460 kg of ash for every person, greater than much more populous coun-
tries such as China which produces 416 kg per person. The latest available ﬂy ash 
production statistics for major coal-consuming countries are shown in Table 34.1. There is a large variation in the type of coal burnt in the power plants within 
Australia. For instance, the lignite (brown coal) burnt in the Latrobe valley in Victoria 
exhibit different properties than the traditional black coal burnt in power plants 
located in other provinces such as New South Wales. The nature and amounts of ﬂy 
ash generated also differ in these cases. Therefore, it is important to understand the 
physical and chemical properties of the generated ash. Table 34.1 Ash production and utilisation of major coal producing countries

34.2 
Physical and Chemical Properties of Fly Ash 
Fly ash often appears in the form of a ﬁne grey powder, but its colour may change 
based on its contents, speciﬁcally the amount of iron and free carbon. The chemical 
composition of ﬂy

ash is commonly based on the source and characteristics of the 
coal that is burnt in a power plant. Various chemical characterisation studies have 
shown that a major portion of ﬂy ash is composed of Silica (SiO2), Alumina (Al2O3), 
Ferric oxides (Fe2O3), and Lime (CaO). Some other mineral oxides such as the oxides 
of Magnesium (MgO), Sodium (Na2O), Potassium (K2O), Manganese (MnO), and 
Titanium (TiO2) can be present based on the coal burnt. Fly ash mostly contains 
spherical glass-like particles of silica that are formed under a process of glassiﬁcation 
which occurs during the combustion of coal in thermal power plants (Fig. 34.1). The 
size of these spherical particles can range from < 1 µm  (µm) to > 100 µm, however 
in general, over 90% of the particles are < 75 µm. Chemical compositions of ﬂy ash 
in some of countries are presented in Table 34.2. 34.3 
Pozzolanic Nature of Fly Ash 
One of the unique properties that makes ﬂy ash a highly desirable coal combustion 
by-product is its pozzolanic nature. While mostly non-reactive in its original state, it 
can attain useful cementing abilities when lime is introduced from an external source. This is due to the presence of silica and alumina in ﬂy ash which react with the added 
lime to form cement-like end products, thus giving strength to the material. The 
pozzolanic nature of ﬂy ash (especially of low lime ASTM Class F ﬂy ash) makes it 
a viable partial substitute for cement in the civil and construction industry. 748

Fly Ash Utilisation in Australia 
Despite its abundance and a range of viable industrial uses, the uptake and utilisation 
of ﬂy ash is poor. Though its utilisation has increased over the years, roughly 50% has 
been reported to remain as waste globally [4], with ut

ilization rates ranging between 
3 and 57% between countries. Australia has reported a ﬂy ash utilisation rate of ~ 
46% [5, 6]. A large portion of the ﬂy ash utilised in Australia is used by cement and concrete 
industries as a partial substitute for cement. In 2021, almost 40% of beneﬁcially 
utilised ﬂy ash (ﬂy utilising its pozzolanic nature) was used in cement and concrete

Unutilised Fly Ash 
Although efforts are being made to better utilise ﬂy ash, a major portion of generated 
ﬂy ash is still not utilised but is stored in form of ash ponds and ash dykes. ~ 53% 
of Australian ash was dumped in ash ponds and ash dykes in 2021, a proportion that 
has remained relatively constant in recent years. Dumping ﬂy ash in open ﬁelds requires substantial land use. In addition, ﬂy ash 
dumping is a major source of air, water and land pollution in the locality which can 
be detrimental to health of local human population and biodiversity including the 
ﬂora and fauna. 750

34.5.1 
Land Wastage Due to Fly Ash Dumping 
A satellite image analysis of major ﬂy ash storage areas in Australia was carried 
out in 2022 to get a perspective of the extent of land wastage (Fig. 34.3). A list of 
major coal-ﬁred power plants in Australia was obtained from web resources [54]. These details were cross-referenced and geo-tagged on the map and the associated 
ash ponds/dykes were located for each power plant (Fig. 34.4). The area of these ash ponds was calculated manually using measurement tools 
available in Google Maps. Since the ash ponds are typically made in the vicinity of the 
coal power plants to avoid transportation costs and logistics, the area measurements 
were based on manual searching and demarcation of the ash pond boundaries. These 
demarcations were based on observation of some of the pond design characteristics 
such as stepped dykes, lagoons, water escape structures and ash embankments [55]. Additionally, the reclaimed and revegetated ash ponds are not considered in this 
analysis. A summary of all the power plants and the related ash pond area is provided 
in Table 34.4. The analysis revealed that the amount of generated ash and corresponding area of 
ash ponds varies based on the type of coal being used. Sites using lignite or brown 
coal in power plants, such as Victoria, generate lower amounts of ﬂy ash, which is 
reﬂected in the area of ash ponds. The area increases in other locations using black 
coal, with averages ranging from 0.32 km2 in Victoria to 1.72 km2 in New South 
Wales (Fig. 34.5). Due to remediation South Australia’s ash pond footprint is now 
indicated as zero. 34

Excluding South Australia, Victoria has the lowest ash dumped area in relation 
to the population size, wasting 0.19 km2 of land per million people. In comparison, 
the highest amount of land wasted area for ﬂy ash dumping is in Queensland, which 
uses almost 1.49 km2 of land area per million people (Fig. 34.6). The dumped area per unit capacity of the coal-ﬁred power plants is most signiﬁcant 
in Queensland with almost 1.63 km2 of ash dumped area per GW capacity at their 
coal power plants (Fig. 34.7). New South Wales wastes the largest amount of area compared to the total area of 
the state, with more than 11 m2 of land wasted per km2 of total area. Since recent 
rehabilitation works, South Australia now wastes the least amount of land with 0 m2 
of ash pond area wasted per km2 (Fig. 34.8). 34.5.2 
Environmental Impact of Open Dumping of Fly Ash 
Dumping of ﬂy

ash in these ponds and dykes not only wastes precious land resources, 
but also has serious effects on the local environment (Fig. 34.9). Dumping ﬂy ash

752
T. Gupta et al. Fig. 34.4 Geo-locations of all coal power plants in Australia with ash pond area example
into ash ponds often requires slurry formation and pumping of the ash slurry to the 
ash pond. This water slowly seeps out of the pond but is contaminated with heavy 
metals and other toxic elements present in the ash itself. This contaminated water is highly detrimental to local water bodies and the under-
ground water table, which can make the local water unsuitable for drinking as well 
as being toxic to ﬂora/fauna. This effect has been seen in many studies on local 
water quality near ash ponds. Chakrabarti 2005, and Mudd 1998 have performed 
detailed studies for the coal ash leaching in Latrobe valley ash ponds, Victoria, 
and demonstrated the presence of higher amounts of heavy metals during leaching 
[56, 57]. The water near Parichha Thermal Power Plant Jhansi, India, had excessive concen-
trations of Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) and high turbidity due to the leaching of 
ash-contaminated water [58]. In Nagpur, India, concentrations of elements like Lead, 
Copper, Zinc, Manganese, and Iron, increased near ﬂy ash dumps, polluting local 
river bodies, and making the water unsuitable for drinking. This also resulted in 
bioaccumulation of chemicals in the local ﬁsh population [59]. Similar effects were 
seen in well water near Kolaghat thermal power station, West Bengal, India where

34.5.3 
Impact of Fly Ash Dumping on Population 
and Biodiversity 
As discussed in the previous section, dumping of ﬂy ash in ponds and dykes create 
envir

onmental impacts that can directly or indirectly affect the health of local human 
population and can be detrimental to the ﬂora and fauna of the region. Using GIS 
mapping and open-source mapping data provided by Federal and State Australian 
Government, a study was conducted to observe the potential risks of ash dumping on 
humans and animals alike. Considering a circular area with radius of 1 km around 
the ash ponds as the highest impact zone, analysis was performed to establish the 
proximity of ash ponds to the local population, endangered species, and biodiversity 
centres, which can be interpreted as the impact quantiﬁcation due to ash ponds. Figure 34.10 displays the proximity of local population to the ash ponds in 
Australia. Although legislations and environment clearances necessitate the loca-
tion of power plants to be at regulated distance from local communities, many times 
this is not practical for distribution and logistical purposes [66]. As is clear from the 
Fig. 34.11, some ash ponds in Queensland, like those of Gladstone and Swanbank 
power plants, are in very close proximity to the local population centres as compared 
to other regions with lower population densities [67]. These locations as well as other 
in Western Australia and Victoria have reported increased incidences of particulate 
pollution and general nuisance due to ash in close proximity to the communities [66]. Australian biodiversity is a key component of Australia’s national identity. It is not 
only an essential component of the lives and culture of indigenous Australians, but

34
Current Perspectives, Challenges and Opportunities for Ash Utilisation …

Fig. 34.10 Population proximity map for each ﬂy ash pond
also supports health, environment and human wellbeing [68]. It was thus important 
to understand how the extensive ash dumping affects the biodiversity at various 
locations in Australia. The Department of Climate, Change, Energy, the Environment and Water 
(DCCEEW) maintains records of Species of National Environmental Signiﬁcance 
(SNES) under The Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation (EPBC) 
Act 1999 [69]. The SNES is a database of EPBC listed species which include threat-
ened, migratory, marine and cetacean species that have known and predicted areas 
of occurrence, including areas of potential habitat. The DCCEEW [69] states that 
the data is indicative rather than deﬁnitive with the publicly available data to be 
used as a starting point for further investigation. The number of EPBC species that 
were located within the same area as the ash pond were studied using the available 
database. Figure 34.11 indicates the number of Species of National Environmental Signif-
icance that are within the same area as the ash ponds with a range of between 20 
and 114 species. A ﬁlter was used to overlay only “Critically Endangered” species 
with the range of overlay species with ash ponds being much smaller between 1 and

10 counts. Vales Point had the greatest count of Critically Endangered species with

of water bodies near the ash ponds [66]. Sincere consideration and necessary efforts 
are therefore required to curtail the problems posed by the open dumping of ﬂy ash. 34.6 
Challenges for Fly Ash Utilisation 
Fly ash utilisation has increased in many countries recently, however the problem of 
not utilising ﬂy ash adequately still remains prevalent. There are many challenges 
that hinder the complete utilisation of ﬂy ash today. Some of the major challenges 
are discussed here. 34
Current Perspectives, Challenges and Opportunities for Ash Utilisation …
759
1. Transportation cost: A major hindrance in the use of ﬂy ash is the transportation 
cost involved. The cost of ﬂy ash itself is negligible as compared to other construc-
tion material such as bricks, cement or aggregate. Costs associated with using 
ﬂy ash occur at the place of application because being a material with low bulk 
density, ﬂy ash occupies more space for transportation therefore, increasing trans-
portation and logistical costs. Traditionally, the coal-ﬁred power plants, which 
are the source of ﬂy ash generation, are located at considerable distance from 
major cities and communities for several socio-environmental reasons. However, 
this exacerbates the transportation costs as most of the ﬂy ash utilisation is in 
civil construction industries near the cities and towns. 2. Large quantities of produced ﬂy ash: The quantitative amount of the generated 
ﬂy ash is also a major hindrance in its complete utilisation. As can be seen in 
Table 34.4, only a few coal power plants have complete ash mitigation which 
has resulted in no ash being dumped in the ash ponds. Contrary to this, most 
coal power plants have to dump some ﬂy ash in the open dumps resulting in ash 
ponds that are more than 2.5 km2 in some locations. These large quantities of ﬂy 
ash are currently not ﬁnancially feasible for a single ﬁeld of utilisation such as 
in cement and construction industry, hindering complete utilisation of ﬂy ash. 3. Lack of sufﬁcient research: Although the potential applications for ﬂy ash util-
isation have been researched for more than a century, there is still a lack of 
signiﬁcant research that can utilises the ﬂy ash quantitatively and qualitatively. To ensure ﬂy ash is suitable and safe for reuse research is also required to remove 
undesirable contaminants from the ﬂy ash such as Pb and Cu that may be at 
concentrations above their states EPA regulations. As discussed before, ﬂy ash 
possesses a pozzolanic nature which makes it a viable product in the construction 
industry. However, ﬂy as

h is still being used as landﬁlls, in mine backﬁlling, and 
other similar operations where any other material could easily be used [5, 6]. This 
may be due to mandatory utilisation targets set by the local governments [70], 
however it still is under-utilising a high-potential industrial by-product. Thus, a 
lack of signiﬁcant research exists that qualitatively investigates the utilisation of 
ﬂy ash to improve its application potential. 4. Lack of required regulations: There are some government policies that can be 
changed in order to facilitate improved utilisation of ﬂy ash. For instance, in 
some states ﬂy ash is considered a waste material in Australia, regardless of 
the recycling process and concentration of comingled contaminants. This means 
that it is subject to additional environmental controls resulting in a market pref-
erence for fresh material as compared to the alternates such as ﬂy ash [71]. Another example of the regulative hinderance is the difference in opinion between 
Australian federal and state government laws and regulations. Because each state 
follows its own Environment Protection Acts and operational regulations, ﬂy ash 
can be classiﬁed as ‘inert waste’ in one state and ‘prescribed waste’ in another, 
even when it has the same chemical composition. Further, the management of 
ﬂy ash ponds can differ between states with Victoria classifying ﬂy ash ponds as 
a ‘landﬁll’ under the Victorian Environment Protection Act 2017 in comparison

760
T. Gupta et al. to NSW classifying these as a ‘dam’ under the Dams Safety Act 1978 and the 
Protection of the Environment Operations Act 1997. 34.7 
Potential Opportunities for Fly Ash Utilisation 
in Australia 
In Australia 10.2 M tonnes of cement was produced and imported between 2020 and 
2021 [72]. Although, the composition of cement and concrete can vary depending on 
the manufacturer and end-use requirements, ﬂy ash can potentially replace 15–30% 
of cement in some of these products. This would range between 1.53 and 3.06 M 
tonnes of ﬂy ash shown as a comparison to total cement in Fig. 34.12. Currently, ﬂy ash is most commonly utilised in the cement and concrete industry 
due to its pozzolanic nature. The historic utilisation of ﬂy ash in the cement-concrete 
industry is summarised in Fig. 34.13. In addition to the cement industry, there are various other ﬁelds of application that 
have been and are presently being researched. These not only improve the utilisation 
of ﬂy ash, thus reducing the environmental impact of ﬂy ash dumping, but also 
contribute to the economics of the utilisation. Some of these application areas and

T. Gupta et al. Enhancing the utilisation of ﬂy ash in some of these areas will also generate 
many job opportunities for local populations. Thus, increased utilisation of ﬂy ash will 
further add to the economics and wellbeing of a community. However, overcoming 
the challenges towards complete ﬂy ash utilisation is a necessary step to reach a stage 
of a zero-waste target in the coal power sector. A possible way to deal with these challenges is to ﬁnd applications for ﬂy ash 
that are within close proximity of coal-ﬁred power plant, therefore reducing the 
transportation cost. These sites would also need the capacity to utilise larger amounts 
of ﬂy ash to its fullest potential. One of the likely venues for such utilisation is in 
opencast coal mines. These opencast mines experience heavy material movement 
throughout their working life due to large-scale production with extensive excavation 
of land area. In most cases, coal-ﬁred power plants are strategically located near coal mines to 
reduce the transportation cost of coal from the mines to the power plant. Conversely

, 
transportation cost of ﬂy ash will be reduced considerably if it can be directly utilised 
back in mines, thus addressing the ﬁrst major challenge of ﬂy ash utilisation. An 
opencast coal mine is also large in size and the potential for large scale utilisation of 
ﬂy ash easily exists in such scenarios, thus addressing the second major challenge in 
ﬂy ash utilisation. Some of the research projects involving utilisation of ﬂy ash in opencast coal mines 
have already been initiated successfully under the Resources Engineering division 
of the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Monash University, 
Clayton, along with their national and international collaborators. These and other 
potential ﬂy ash applications in coal mines are discussed in brief hereafter. 34.7.1 
Fly Ash in Haul Road Construction 
Haul roads are the lifelines of surface mining operation and a critical component for 
a smooth mine production cycle. However, due to mine economics and the dynamic 
nature of mining operation, these haul roads are made of blasted waste rock material 
(overburden) (Fig. 34.14). This makes them susceptible to breakdowns and failures, 
especially under heavy dump truck movement and watery conditions. Utilising the pozzolanic nature of ﬂy ash, a construction material was synthesised 
that could not only provide strength to the haul roads but was also economically and 
environmentally safe. The construction material synthesised here used high volumes 
of ﬂy ash with polymeric additives, which made the composite competent enough to 
deal with the water-related structural challenges of the mine haul road. This construc-
tion material was rigorously tested in haul road simulation environments and was 
proved to be capable of sufﬁciently strengthening the haul roads, simultaneously 
improving its hydrological behaviour (Fig. 34.15). This drastically reduced the main-
tenance cost of haul roads and dumpers alike, simultaneously increasing the ﬂy ash 
utilisation [138]. 34
Current Perspectives, Challenges and Opportunities for Ash Utilisation …
763
Fig. 34.14 A typical mine haul road made of overburden material [137]
Fig. 34.15 Synthesis and testing of ﬂy ash composite for application in haul road construction 
[138]

764
T. Gupta et al. The experimental and numerical analysis conducted using the Latrobe Valley ﬂy 
ash revealed that a strong cement-free ﬂy ash based composite can be synthesized by 
categorically optimising the percentages of ﬂy ash and the added lime. For the Latrobe 
Valley ash, this optimisation was achieved at 52.5% Fly ash, 27.5% Lime, 20% OB 
with the addition of 3% Gypsum by weight. Simulation results also revealed that the 
use of this composite in haul roads could safely reduce the surface layer thickness 
by 34% [138]. Further studies to universalize the approach of ﬂy ash composite 
design optimisation, irrespective of the source of ash and other raw materials, are in 
progress. 34.7.2 
Fly Ash in Waste Dump Stabilis

ation 
In a surface mining operation, huge amounts of waste rock (overburden) have to be 
removed in order to reach the mineral. This overburden is usually stored in form of 
dumps, either inside the mine (internal dumps) or outside the mine (external dumps), 
based on the working method. With increasing technological advancement, higher 
amounts of overburden can be removed economically for mining out a unit of mineral, 
resulting in the need of higher and steeper dumps. To make sure these dumps remain 
stable and do not break down it is required to stabilise these dumps artiﬁcially. Using 
the pozzolanic nature of ﬂy ash, these dumps can be stabilised by introducing layers 
of ﬂy ash composite. Several studies have already been performed which prove the 
effectiveness of ﬂy ash in dump stabilisation. Fly ash stabilisation of dumps not only 
helps protect the dump against failure, but also allows for steeper and taller dumps 
leading to improved storage capacities [83, 87, 139, 140]. 34.7.3 
Fly Ash in Artiﬁcial Soil for Mine Reclamation 
A major problem faced in conducting mine rehabilitation of coal mines is the reveg-
etation of overburden dumps and other mine sites. Apart from other factors such as 
a lack of nutrients and chemical wash-off during the long storage of overburden, a 
major reason for the infertility of overburden material is the acidity induced due to 
acid mine drainage (AMD). The addition of ﬂy ash with the overburden material increases its pH due to the 
alkaline nature of ﬂy ash. The missing nutrients in the soil can be supplemented by 
the addition of compost obtained from local sewage plants (Fig. 34.16). The artiﬁcial 
soil thus formed has proven to be substantially effective in promoting vegetation in 
various laboratory-based experiments (Fig. 34.17)  [82, 141]. 34
Current Perspectives, Challenges and Opportunities for Ash Utilisation …
765
Fig. 34.16 Pot experiments conducted for optimising artiﬁcial soil for mine reclamation [82] 
Fig. 34.17 Vegetation studies conducted to test the effectiveness of artiﬁcial soil [141] 
At present stage these artiﬁcial soils are being used for progressive mine reha-
bilitation in Latrobe valley’s coal mines in collaboration with Monash University. These efforts will not only help in effective mine rehabilitation of present sites but 
also become blueprints for the rehabilitation of other mines around Australia and the 
world [142]. 766
T. Gupta et al. 34.8 
Conclusion 
Substantial beneﬁts are achievable if ﬂy ash utilisation is prioritised by policymakers. With a signiﬁcant resource of ﬂy ash, alongside industry and academic expertise, 
Australia has the capacity to establish state-of-the-art research facilities for ﬂy ash 
utilisation and mitigation. This will not only help Australia reach its target of a 
zero-waste power sector but will also act as a framework for multinational research, 
establishing Australia as the leader in this ﬁeld. Focused efforts and research in 
this dir

ection can result in a better environment, a thriving economy, and higher 
employment for the local communities. References

Chapter 35 
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy 
in Construction Materials to Support 
the Diversiﬁcation of Greener Design 
Koorosh Gharehbaghi, Naiyla Goverdhan, Ken Farnes, and Kong Fah Tee 
Abstract The construction sector is a signiﬁcant consumer of natural resources 
and a major contributor to energy consumption, leading to considerable environ-
mental repercussions that have raised serious concerns. This study seeks to enhance 
the process of selecting building materials, given the adverse effects that many 
conventional construction materials impose on the environment. Certain materials 
utilized in building construction are associated with elevated levels of Embodied 
Energy (EE) and Carbon Dioxide (CO2) emissions, necessitating the identiﬁcation 
of more sustainable and environmentally friendly alternatives to foster a healthier 
future. Furthermore, this research includes a comparative analysis of three houses 
constructed with varied materials: a standard burnt brick house, a cement block 
house, and a mudbrick house. A mixed-methods approach was utilized to quantify 
each construction technique’s EE and CO2 emission. After thorough evaluation, three 
houses of similar ﬂoor area and exposed to identical climatic conditions were chosen 
for the study. The ﬁndings revealed that the mudbrick house (House 2) emerged as 
the most sustainable option, primarily since mudbrick generates no EE or CO2 emis-
sions. In contrast, materials such as cement blocks, steel, concrete, and aluminum 
were found to signiﬁcantly contribute to environmental degradation and toxicity. This 
research advocates for a practical framework for the adoption of alternative building 
materials in residential construction, emphasizing the urgent need for decisive actions 
to combat climate change and promote sustainability. K.

Introduction and Background 
The surge in residential construction activities in recent years has highlighted the 
environmental challenges linked to this sector. It is crucial to explore strategies for 
enhancing material selection to mitigate the adverse effects on the environment [1]. This concern is particularly pertinent given that building materials are among the 
largest consumers of energy and resources, thus contributing signiﬁcantly to envi-
ronmental degradation [2]. Research by [3] indicates that building materials account 
for 30–50% of global raw resource consumption, with approximately 40% of land-
ﬁll waste in OECD countries originating from construction materials. This issue is 
increasingly critical in light of rising global temperatures and ozone layer depletion 
[4]. Annually, construction materials generate over a million tons of waste, exac-
erbating environmental challenges. Furthermore, the materials utilized in construc-
tion often possess prominent levels of Embodied Energy (EE), and their production 
processes are responsible for substantial Carbon Dioxide (CO2) emissions. The typical stages encompassed in a building’s lifecycle consist of the extraction 
and processing of raw materials, the construction phase, the operational period of the 
building, and the demolition once the building has reached the end of its useful life. Furthermore, shifts in lifestyle and evolving demands can contribute to a reduction 
in a building’s lifespan, potentially leading to increased landﬁll waste or recycling 
efforts as a result of renovations and demolitions. The substantial consumption of 
construction materials, along with the associated EE, has led to signiﬁcant resource 
depletion on a global scale [5]. The choice of environmentally sustainable building 
materials and products is often shaped by the various stakeholders involved in the 
construction sector [6]. 35.1.1 
Research Objectives 
The construction sector must prioritize environmental preservation and the estab-
lishment of a sustainable future for upcoming generations. The processes, method-
ologies, and materials employed within the industry must be innovatively devel-
oped to facilitate the global adoption of sustainable building practices, making 
this a fundamental consideration at every phase of construction [7]. Data from the 
Austra

lian Bureau of Statistics [8] indicates a signiﬁcant increase in the consump-
tion of construction materials. This research aims to assist in the selection of 
building materials that minimize adverse environmental effects while also shedding 
light on the ecological consequences associated with various construction mate-
rials. Utilizing materials that can be reused or recycled is crucial in decreasing the 
demand for newly produced materials. Edil [9] posits that recycling not only miti-
gates environmental harm but also leads to substantial cost savings by reducing land-
ﬁll expenses, creating economically viable alternatives, and lowering energy usage. 35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
773
Furthermore, the energy expended during material extraction, processing, and trans-
portation contributes signiﬁcantly to pollution levels [10, 11]. Sourcing construction 
materials locally can prove advantageous by diminishing transportation costs and 
CO2 emissions, thereby reducing greenhouse gas output. The selection of materials in construction is inﬂuenced by numerous factors, 
including budget constraints and the availability of resources. However, the assess-
ment of EE and CO2 emissions presents signiﬁcant challenges due to the complexity 
of the data involved, which encompasses aspects such as the geographical origin of 
materials, the technologies employed, and the methodologies utilized in manufac-
turing processes [12]. Consequently, it is crucial to comprehend the environmental 
ramiﬁcations associated with the choice of construction materials and to implement 
strategies aimed at mitigating these impacts. Two effective approaches for signiﬁ-
cantly lowering the environmental footprint of construction materials are recycling 
and reusing existing materials. The careful selection of materials during the design 
phase plays a vital role in minimizing environmental waste, as these practices not 
only decrease the demand for added resources but also lead to cost reductions and 
a decrease in the volume of new materials required for construction. Therefore, 
it is essential to consider factors such as the source of materials, their EE, and the 
CO2 generated throughout the extraction, manufacturing, and construction processes. While these strategies are particularly relevant to the construction sector, they can also 
be adapted for use in other industries. Thoughtful material selection can contribute 
to improved quality of life and overall human well-being. This study explores the beneﬁts associated with the utilization of sustainable mate-
rials in the construction of residential buildings. As previously noted, construction 
materials are often characterized by signiﬁcant levels of EE, and their production 
and disposal contribute to substantial CO2 emissions. Identifying which materials 
exert the least environmental impact in contrast to those that are more detrimental is 
crucial. Furthermore, this research a

ims to provide a comprehensive overview and a 
deeper insight into how selecting construction materials and processes can mitigate 
environmental harm. By comparing three distinct types of houses, the study evaluates 
the materials employed, the associated EE, and the CO2 produced. This analysis will 
reveal which building practices have minimal environmental impact and which are 
more harmful. The construction industry must prioritize the careful selection of sustainable mate-
rials, as this practice can mitigate the adverse effects associated with materials that 
possess high EE. It is essential to evaluate materials throughout the entire construc-
tion process, encompassing stages such as extraction, sourcing, manufacturing, and, 
demolition, while also considering their potential for recycling and reuse. By estab-
lishing material selection strategies during the initial phases of construction, particu-
larly in design and planning, it becomes possible to make environmentally beneﬁcial 
choices that promote the use of sustainable materials. Consequently, this research 
focuses on the following questions:
• (RQ1) How can the environmental impact be reduced through better building 
materials usage? 774
K. Gharehbaghi et al. • (RQ2) How can the control and decrease of waste within the construction industry 
be better addressed? • (RQ3) How can the CO2 footprint be reduced through increased material 
performance? 35.1.2 
Research Gap 
A comprehensive examination of existing literature and extensive research indicates 
that the process of selecting building materials, along with the environmental costs 
tied to these materials, lacks transparency. While sustainable materials offer potential 
environmental advantages, they often come at a higher price compared to conven-
tional standardized options. The elevated costs associated with sustainable materials 
can be attributed to the more intensive processes required for their cultivation and 
production. Additionally, the principle of economies of scale plays a signiﬁcant role in 
pricing; as demand for sustainable materials remains relatively low, their prices tend 
to stay elevated. Consequently, a rise in demand could lead to increased production 
levels, which in turn would result in a reduction of unit costs. 35.2 
Literature Review and Background 
Researchers have observed that signiﬁcant amounts of CO2 are released into the 
atmosphere throughout various stages of the construction life cycle [13–15]. The 
connection between emissions generated during material production and construc-
tion practices has been extensively examined, particularly concerning the choice of 
construction materials. Hu et al. [16], Costa et al. [17], Rossello-Batle [18] propose 
that the environmental footprint can be mitigated by substituting the most energy-
demanding components of a building with more ecologically sustainable alternatives. Furthermore, studies conducted by [12, 19] highlight that construction activit

ies are 
signiﬁcantly contributing to the swift exhaustion of non-renewable resources. The construction of buildings necessitates various resources that undergo both 
physical and chemical transformations throughout their lifecycle. The manufacturing 
processes associated with these products generate waste outputs that contribute to the 
release of harmful emissions into the environment. As urban populations continue to 
grow and cities expand, there is a corresponding rise in resource consumption, leading 
to an increased demand for housing and infrastructure. This heightened demand 
results in greater waste production, as well as elevated levels of discharge and CO2 
affecting land, water, and air quality. Wackernagel et al. [20], Nguyen [21] assert 
that sustainability involves living within the environmental regenerative capacity, 
a threshold that human activities have surpassed. This study investigates the EE 
associated with the production of building materials, encompassing their extraction, 
processing, manufacturing, and transportation. The integration of recycled and reused

35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
775
materials in construction practices is beneﬁcial, as it helps to minimize waste gener-
ation and associated CO2 emissions, thereby reducing the amount of waste sent to 
landﬁlls. Additionally, implementing an effective construction waste management 
strategy is crucial for reducing costs and conserving resources. Finally, materials 
characterized as sustainable building materials are those that are environmentally 
friendly and generate minimal waste, including low-cost options that can be sourced 
locally to further decrease transportation emissions. 35.2.1 
Embodied Energy (EE) 
EE refers to the cumulative energy utilized throughout the various direct and indirect 
processes linked to a building’s lifecycle, adhering to the cradle-to-grave framework 
[22]. This concept encompasses a wide range of activities, including the extraction of 
raw materials, their manufacturing, transportation to the construction site, the actual 
construction process, any refurbishment or replacement efforts, and the disposal of 
the building once it reaches the end of its useful life. Buildings are constructed using a diverse array of materials, each of which requires 
energy at various stages, including production, utilization, and eventual dismantling 
[23]. This energy consumption encompasses several critical processes such as the 
extraction of raw materials, their transportation, manufacturing, assembly, installa-
tion, disassembly, deconstruction, and decomposition [24, 25]. The manufacturing 
and transportation of numerous building materials are particularly energy-intensive. In recent years, there has been a growing focus on the environmental implica-
tions of the building and construction sector. The production processes associated 
with building materials are signiﬁcant contributors to greenhouse gas em

issions, 
particularly CO2 [26, 27]. The construction industry is recognized as one of the largest consumers of 
commercial energy, primarily derived from electricity and fossil fuel combustion. Urge-Vorsatz and Novikova [28] noted that in 2004, buildings accounted for 32% 
of global CO2 emissions, with projections indicating that by 2030, this ﬁgure could 
rise to between 35 and 42%. Bansal et al. [29] suggest that energy consumption can 
be mitigated by utilizing materials with low EE, which are also cost-effective. The 
overall life cycle energy of a building comprises both EE and operational energy, 
with the former arising from the production, on-site construction, demolition, and 
disposal of building materials. In contrast, operational energy pertains to the manage-
ment of the internal environment, which includes heating, cooling, lighting, and the 
operation of appliances [24, 30, 31] provide a detailed examination of the numerous 
beneﬁts associated with green buildings in the context of high-rise construction, as 
illustrated in Fig. 35.1. Figure 35.1 illustrates that the primary characteristics of green buildings encom-
pass structural performance, constructability and maintenance, innovation and design 
processes, and materials and resources. These attributes are crucial for conducting 
Life Cycle Assessments (LCA) and understanding their associated energy dynamics. 776
K. Gharehbaghi et al. Fig. 35.1 The inﬂuences of green buildings for high-rise construction [31]
Conversely, Fig. 35.2 presents an overview of the energy associated with the building 
life cycle. Various scholars have approached the concept of EE and its assessment from 
different perspectives. The analyses conducted by [24, 32, 33] characterize EE as 
the cumulative energy necessary for the construction of a building, which encom-
passes both the direct energy utilized during the construction and assembly phases, 
as well as the indirect energy associated with the production of the building’s mate-
rials and components. Furthermore, [34] articulate EE as the total energy needed to 
deliver a product, encompassing both direct and indirect energy inputs throughout all 
upstream processes. A more comprehensive elucidation is offered by [35–39], who 
collectively describe EE as the sum of energy expended during the extraction and 
processing of raw materials, the transportation of these materials, the manufacturing 
of building components, and the energy consumed in various activities throughout 
the construction and eventual demolition of the structure, as referenced in [40]. Numerous research investigations including [41–43] have focused on the CO2 
emissions associated with the energy demands of producing and processing various 
building materials. Consequently, architects and designers globally are actively 
seeking strategies to mitigate the adverse environmental effects attributed to build-
ings, as highlighted by [44]. Adopting energy-efﬁcient alternative building

materials 
can signiﬁcantly lower the EE within a structure, as noted by [26]. By distinguishing

35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
777
Fig. 35.2 Building life cycle energy [24]
between the EE expended during the construction phase and that used in the oper-
ation and maintenance phase, it becomes possible to pinpoint the most effective 
opportunities for reducing EE and, in turn, lessening harmful environmental impacts. The construction sector is a signiﬁcant consumer of energy and natural resources, 
accounting for approximately 40% of the materials that enter the global economy 
and contributing to 40–50% of worldwide greenhouse gas emissions, as reported by 
[45]. The energy consumption of buildings, inﬂuenced by the types and quantities of 
construction materials utilized, represents about 10–20% of the total energy expended 
throughout their construction and operational lifespan, according to [29, 46]. The 
diversity in building typologies leads to varying levels of EE, as different structures 
require differing amounts of construction materials. Natural resource-derived mate-
rials such as bricks, cement, aggregates, timber, glass, steel, and aluminum undergo 
multiple manufacturing processes that embed energy, as discussed by [47]. Moreover, EE encompasses the total energy invested in a product throughout 
its lifecycle, beginning with the extraction of raw materials and extending through 
transportation, reﬁning, manufacturing, and assembly. The energy consumed during 
these stages can be quantiﬁed and assessed with relative ease; however, the indirect 
energy released, which includes the energy utilized by machinery that facilitates 
these processes, poses a greater challenge in terms of measurement. Direct energy is 
typically associated with on-site and off-site operations such as construction, prefab-
rication, administration, and transportation activities, as noted by [48]. Conversely, 
the energy associated with the production of building materials, as well as activities

778
K. Gharehbaghi et al. related to renovation, refurbishment, and demolition, is classiﬁed as indirect energy. This category encompasses initial EE, recurrent EE, and energy expended during 
demolition. As previously mentioned, EE signiﬁes the cumulative energy necessary 
for the production of a product, spanning from the extraction of raw materials to the 
ﬁnal product’s readiness for use. In the case of bricks, this process typically involves three key stages: ﬁrst, the 
extraction of raw materials, which refers to the energy expended in mining clay 
and other essential materials; second, transportation, which accounts for the energy 
consumed in moving these raw materials to the manufacturing facility and subse-
quently transporting the ﬁnished bricks to the construction site; and third, the manu-
facturing process itself, which includes the energy utilized during various stages of 
brick production, such as crushing, mixin

g, molding, drying, and ﬁring. The EE of 
bricks can vary based on several factors, including the type of brick—such as ﬁred 
clay bricks versus concrete bricks—the speciﬁc production methods employed, and 
the energy sources utilized. For instance, ﬁred clay bricks typically exhibit a higher 
EE compared to concrete bricks, primarily due to the elevated temperatures required 
during the ﬁring process. Further, the average values for the EE of common brick 
types are:
• Fired clay bricks: approximately 2.5–3.5 MJ/kg. • Concrete bricks: approximately 0.8–1.5 MJ/kg. A signiﬁcant approach to minimizing the EE of bricks involves ﬁring them at lower 
temperatures. This method not only contributes to a reduction in CO2 but also helps 
maintain the inherent properties of the raw materials, thereby enhancing produc-
tion efﬁciency by optimizing kiln usage and potentially boosting overall output. The 
process of extracting raw materials, along with their manufacturing and transporta-
tion to construction sites, represents the initial EE invested in material production. In contrast, the energy expended on maintenance and refurbishment throughout the 
building’s lifespan is categorized as recurrent EE. Additionally, the energy required 
for the deconstruction and disposal of materials at the end of a building’s life cycle 
is referred to as demolition energy [24]. Various methodologies have been devel-
oped to accurately assess EE, including process analysis, input–output analysis, and 
hybrid analysis [49]. The careful selection and utilization of building materials in 
the construction sector can signiﬁcantly diminish the overall EE associated with a 
project. Consequently, the reduction of EE has emerged as a critical concern for 
researchers, industry professionals, builders, and material manufacturers alike. 35.2.2 
Recycled and Reusable Materials 
Research consistently highlights recycling as a pivotal strategy for diminishing the 
overall consumption of energy and resources within society [50]. The construction 
sector, as noted by [51, 52], is increasingly incorporating recycled materials into its

35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
779
practices. This trend is further bolstered by selective demolition techniques, the inte-
gration of recycled materials, and the architectural design of structures that promote 
future recycling efforts [53]. Wagner [54] emphasizes that the construction industry is 
progressively utilizing raw materials at a higher rate than other sectors. The processes 
of reusing and recycling present a range of opportunities and challenges, which not 
only yield environmental beneﬁts but also result in economic savings [55]. Conse-
quently, the adoption of recycled materials in construction can signiﬁcantly mitigate 
the demand for new materials, thereby conserving ﬁnancial resources, natural assets, 
and EE [56]. Numerous nations have enacted regulations aimed at promoting the circular 
economy to

enhance sustainable development and improve the recycling of construc-
tion waste. For instance, the European Union’s Waste Framework Directive [57] 
established in 2008, set an ambitious target for member states to ensure that at least 
70% of non-hazardous construction waste is reused or recycled by the year 2020. This directive applies to countries within the EU and the European Economic Area 
(EEA) [57]. In Australia, the transition towards a circular economy began with the 
implementation of the National Waste Policy in 2009 [58, 59]. The construction 
sector has signiﬁcantly reﬁned its operational practices to foster the development of 
sustainable and eco-friendly buildings. Construction waste encompasses a variety 
of materials, including timber, concrete, plasterboard, bricks, vegetation, rock, and 
soil, which are primarily produced during construction, demolition, or renovation 
activities. Notably, waste generated from these processes account for more than one-
third of all landﬁll waste, with over 75% of landﬁll contents consisting of clean, 
excavated materials such as concrete, bricks, and timber that are suitable for recy-
cling [60]. While solid waste-to-energy incinerators are frequently promoted as a 
rapid and effective method for managing increasing waste volumes while simultane-
ously generating energy, they also pose environmental challenges, including water 
pollution, unpleasant odors, noise, and vibrations, which can adversely affect nearby 
residential and commercial areas. Furthermore, the incineration process generates hazardous byproducts, particu-
larly ﬂy ash and bottom ash, which necessitate meticulous management and disposal 
[61]. As a result, waste incinerators signiﬁcantly contribute to the environmental 
challenges faced by the construction sector. These facilities emit a considerable 
volume of CO2, surpassing the emissions produced by power plants that utilize coal, 
oil, or natural gas. In light of these concerns, scientists and scholars have under-
taken extensive research to explore alternative strategies for alleviating the environ-
mental impacts of waste generation and pollution. For instance, [62] proposes that 
the replacement of raw materials with recycled alternatives can conserve natural 
resources. Their investigation, which involved surveying companies regarding the 
advantages of utilizing recycled materials, revealed that the primary beneﬁt was a 
38% reduction in landﬁll waste, followed by improvements in quality at 33%, and 
cost savings at 27%. A variety of construction materials are amenable to recycling, with studies indi-
cating that both aluminum and steel can be entirely recycled, as noted by [63, 64]. This recycling process signiﬁcantly mitigates the EE associated with these materials,

achieving reductions of 72% for steel and an impressive 95% for aluminum. Further-
more, while plasterboard poses environmental hazards by releasing toxic hydrogen 
sulﬁde (H2S) in landﬁlls, it remains a recyclable material. Timber is particularly 
notable for its versatility, as it can be repurposed into particleboard or transformed 
into mulch for horticultural applications. Windows, too, can be reused or recycled 
for similar purposes. The recycling of glass results in a 20% decrease in EE, and the 
resultant recycled glass can serve as aggregate in the production of concrete, bricks, 
and tiles, as highlighted by [65]. Additionally, concrete can incorporate industrial

power generation, steel manufacturing, and silicon metal production, respectively 
[65]. The environmental impacts of commonly utilized materials in the construction 
sector are summarized in Table 35.1. The government of New South Wales adopted the principles of reduce, reuse, 
and recycle (3R) as a foundational waste management strategy at Sydney Olympic 
Park, aiming to optimize outcomes [66]. Munn and Soebarto [65] highlighted that 
various reused and recycled materials were incorporated into concrete for several 
prominent Australian landmarks, including the Sydney Harbour Tunnel, the Sydney 
Parallel Runway, the Melbourne Crown Casino, and the white-water canoe course 
for the Sydney Olympics located in Penrith. Furthermore, they pointed out that the 
Sydney International Shooting Gallery, constructed for the 2000 Sydney Olympics, 
was made from reused timber, with 90% of its materials sourced from this category 
and the remaining 10% derived from plantation timber. 35.2.3 
Construction Waste Management 
Construction and Demolition (C&D) waste represents one of the most signiﬁ-
cant waste streams globally. This type of waste originates from various activities, 
including land excavation, civil and building construction, site clearance, roadwork,

35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
781
and refurbishment, all of which are integral to construction, renovation, and demo-
lition processes [67]. Research conducted by [68–70] indicates that C&D waste 
constitutes 30–40% of total solid waste, a ﬁgure that has escalated due to the 
increasing frequency of C&D activities driven by urbanization and city redevelop-
ment. The rapid urban growth has led to heightened consumption of non-renewable 
resources, raising signiﬁcant environmental concerns [71]. In certain nations, such 
as Bangladesh, challenges persist

, including low public awareness, inadequate law 
enforcement, and reliance on outdated technologies [72, 73]. Furthermore, deﬁcien-
cies in communication, coordination, oversight, and regulatory frameworks, along 
with insufﬁcient contractor awareness and poor project waste budgeting, contribute 
to the excessive generation of C&D waste. In the United States, it is reported that 
over one hundred million tons of C&D waste are produced annually, while the United 
Kingdom accounts for more than 50% of its C&D waste being sent to landﬁlls [74], 
with an annual rejection of 70 million tons of C&D waste. Furthermore, the yearly production of C&D waste in Hong Kong experienced a 
signiﬁcant increase, having doubled between 1993 and 2004, reaching approximately 
20 million tons by 2004 [75]. Numerous studies and industry analysts suggest that the 
volume of C&D waste continues to rise markedly each year, underscoring the critical 
need to comprehend the magnitude of the C&D waste generated by construction 
operations. Effective management of C&D waste can transform it into a signiﬁcant resource 
rather than a source of environmental concern. Various materials, including metals, 
mortar, concrete, bricks, plastics, timber, ceramics, and glass, have the potential 
for reuse, thereby allowing the C&D waste stream to generate revenue instead of 
contributing to pollution. According to [76], the ﬁeld of C&D waste management 
is gaining traction as a vital discipline, with efﬁcient management practices leading 
to decreased energy consumption, conservation of raw materials, and minimized 
transportation requirements. The worldwide C&D waste management industry is 
experiencing growth, creating economic prospects that appeal to entrepreneurs [77]. Although the ongoing growth of C&D waste management companies has not yet 
substantially reduced environmental repercussions, it is paving the way for enhanced 
waste management strategies. C&D waste can be divided into two main categories: 
inert waste, which consists of both soft and hard materials, and non-inert waste, which 
comprises residuals such as metals, wood, plastics, and glass. This categorization is 
depicted in the life cycle ﬂowchart of various C&D wastes, as illustrated in Fig. 35.3. The challenge of material waste in the construction industry is a pressing concern 
that requires immediate attention. To effectively manage waste throughout all stages 
of a construction project, the implementation of a robust waste management system 
is essential [78]. Shifting the construction sector towards a ‘Zero waste’ paradigm 
demands informed decision-making at every phase of the construction life cycle, as 
well as the development and enforcement of appropriate standards [4, 79]. Empha-
sizing the principles of reduce, reuse, and recycle (3R) during construction activities 
aims to decrease the amount of mixed construction waste sent to landﬁlls, thereby 
promoting material waste reduction. Research by [80] u

Fig. 35.3 Lifecycle ﬂowchart of C&D waste [77]
environmental risks and socio-economic impacts linked to C&D waste in landﬁll 
sites. The depletion of natural resources is accelerating, driven by the growing 
demands of infrastructure development and the diminishing availability of alternative 
resources. To address the environmental challenges posed by construction waste, the 
principles of reduce, reuse, recycle (3R) and reduce, reuse, recycle, and recover (4R) 
can be effectively integrated throughout the entire construction process. The concept 
of reduction emphasizes minimizing resource consumption at its origin, thereby 
curtailing unnecessary material usage, which can be applied from the initial planning 
stages to the ﬁnal handover of projects by ensuring meticulous and precise material 
planning. Reuse involves repurposing the same materials in construction without 
altering their original function or composition, such as utilizing formwork multiple 
times or adapting materials for new applications, thereby extending their lifespan 
and reducing waste generation while beneﬁting the environment. This approach is 
particularly advantageous as it requires minimal processing and energy input. Recy-
cling, on the other hand, entails reprocessing materials to create new products once 
they are no longer usable in their original form, which helps decrease the demand 
for virgin materials and mitigates the volume of waste directed to landﬁlls. Finally, 
recovery pertains to salvaging discarded materials for alternative uses, allowing for 
their processing rather than outright disposal [81]. 35.2.4 
Sustainable Building Materials 
A signiﬁcant body of research has explored the challenges associated with mate-
rial selection; however, these studies often neglect to establish a clear deﬁnition of 
‘sustainable building materials’ [82]. Sustainable building materials are character-
ized by their natural origins and offer numerous beneﬁts to users, including reduced 
maintenance requirements, enhanced energy efﬁciency, and improved health and 
comfort for occupants [83]. The selection of materials typically takes place during

35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
783
the design phase, where detailed speciﬁcations and working plans are formulated. Halliday [84] notes that various methodologies and tools have been created to facil-
itate optimal material selection based on their physical and mechanical properties. Research indicates that certain materials and resources are becoming increasingly 
scarce, necessitating careful management of the remaining supplies [84]. Reddy 
[85] argues that the development of sustainable alternative building technologies 
can promote energy conservation, reduce reliance on materials with high energy 
embeddedness, encourage environmentally friendly practices, minimize transporta-
tion needs, maximize the use of local resources and skills, incorporate industrial 
waste into building mater

ials, and utilize recycling and renewable energy sources. By 
adhering to these principles, construction methods can evolve to become sustainable, 
thereby minimizing their negative impact on the environment. Glass is widely recognized as a prevalent material in the construction industry, 
primarily utilized for its ability to facilitate daylight harvesting. However, its effec-
tiveness is hindered by limitations in light management, inadequate thermal insula-
tion, and a signiﬁcant CO2 footprint. The thermal properties of glass allow heat to 
transfer easily, leading to increased energy expenses, particularly as warmth escapes 
during colder months and enters during warmer periods. In contrast, transparent wood 
is emerging as a highly promising alternative to glass, offering substantial advan-
tages for future applications. This innovative material not only demonstrates superior 
thermal insulation—boasting thermal conductivity that is at least three times lower 
than that of glass—but also presents a cost-effective solution for energy savings. Furthermore, transparent wood is derived from sustainable and renewable resources, 
contributing to lower CO2 [86–88]. Research conducted by [88] has illustrated the 
potential of transparent wood composites as building materials that can effectively 
harness sunlight, thereby ensuring consistent and uniform indoor lighting while 
enhancing thermal insulation. The production, utilization, and disposal of carpets raise considerable environ-
mental and health issues. The majority of carpets are constructed from synthetic 
materials that necessitate substantial energy and water consumption, resulting in the 
generation of toxic waste during their production and lacking biodegradability. In 
contrast, carpets made from natural ﬁbers possess the advantage of being biodegrad-
able. For instance, wool carpets, particularly those crafted in India, are steeped in a 
rich Persian-style tradition. According to [89], the Indian carpet industry is hindered 
by a deﬁciency in innovation and reliance on antiquated technologies. This sector is 
highly fragmented and faces numerous challenges that require urgent attention. The 
manufacturing process consumes excessive water and employs chemical dyes, which 
pose signiﬁcant risks to both the environment and human health, contributing to 
substantial waste that exacerbates landﬁll issues. In response to these concerns, a new 
standard for evaluating the environmental sustainability of carpets was established in 
November 2007 and received approval from the American National Standards Insti-
tute (ANSI). While hardwood and laminated ﬂooring are aesthetically pleasing, they 
contribute to the depletion of natural timber resources. Conversely, bamboo ﬂooring, 
derived from grass, offers superior hardness and durability compared to hardwood

784
K. Gharehbaghi et al. and laminated options, and it is also more sustainable due to the rapid regeneration 
of bamboo. 35.2.4.1
Local

ly Sourced Materials 
Transportation plays a crucial role throughout the various phases of construction 
projects, encompassing the movement of goods and materials to and from the 
construction site, as well as site extraction, processing, and actual construction activ-
ities. An increase in transportation distance correlates with a rise in both EE and 
greenhouse gas emissions. By utilizing locally sourced materials, energy expen-
diture is signiﬁcantly reduced, thereby fostering a more sustainable environment. Research indicates that employing locally sourced materials can lead to a reduction 
in energy consumption in buildings by as much as 215% and a decrease in transporta-
tion impacts by 453% [10]. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) studies have demonstrated 
that buildings incorporating a high proportion of bio-based materials exhibit the least 
environmental impact [90]. Furthermore, [91] found that greenhouse gas emissions 
are highly variable based on the geographical origin of the materials. Locally grown 
crops used in construction not only consume less energy but also lower transporta-
tion energy costs [92]. The use of locally sourced materials can also expedite the 
construction timeline [93]. Commonly available low-cost raw materials include soil, 
clay, straw, and sand, whereas many materials prevalent in the construction sector, 
such as steel, cement, glass, aluminum, plastics, and bricks, are energy-intensive and 
typically sourced from greater distances, exacerbating their environmental impact 
through increased CO2 emissions and energy consumption during manufacturing 
[94]. It is essential to reduce the consumption of energy resources and raw materials 
to create simple, energy-efﬁcient, and sustainable alternatives in building construc-
tion. The construction sector faces signiﬁcant challenges in embracing sustainable 
practices, primarily due to insufﬁcient information, technological advancements, and 
evaluation methodologies. The gradual integration of sustainable building techniques 
is hindered by a reluctance to adopt modern technologies, the necessary changes 
in processes, and concerns regarding potential risks and unforeseen expenses, as 
noted by [95]. This resistance complicates the promotion of sustainable construc-
tion methods, as highlighted by [96]. Nevertheless, there is a growing trend towards 
utilizing locally sourced materials, which aligns with the principles of sustainable 
development. The use of heavy construction materials, such as aggregates and bricks, 
presents limitations in transportation capacity due to their weight, while lighter mate-
rials, like insulation and wind cowls, necessitate more delivery loads. Consequently, 
it is crucial to evaluate the average transportation distances based on both weight 
and volume. Research conducted by [97] indicates that sourcing materials locally 
can signiﬁcantly lower CO2 emissions without incurring additional costs, simply by 
opting for local suppliers. Th

erefore, prioritizing sustainability and adopting a 3R 
or 4R approach to materials is vital for achieving energy-efﬁcient designs, although 
this endeavor can prove to be quite challenging. 35
An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
785
35.2.4.2
Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) 
The assessment of EE and CO2 in buildings is typically conducted through a modi-
ﬁed version of LCA, which serves as a framework for evaluating the environmental 
repercussions associated with the material, energy, and pollutant ﬂows throughout a 
product’s entire lifespan. According to the International Standard ISO 14044:2006, 
LCA encompasses four essential stages: ‘Deﬁne goal and scope’, ‘Life cycle inven-
tory analysis’ (LCI), ‘Impact Assessment’, and ‘Interpretation’ [98]. Central to 
the LCA process is the functional unit, which must be quantiﬁable, relevant, and 
consistent, encompassing aspects such as building materials, energy systems, pack-
aging, and transportation. Additionally, the ISO standards related to LCA are inte-
grated within the ISO 14000 series, which outlines environmental management stan-
dards. This series includes (I) ISO 14040: Environmental Management—Life Cycle 
Assessment—Principles and Framework; (II) ISO 14044: Environmental Manage-
ment—Life Cycle Assessment—Requirements and Guidelines. Other supportive 
ISO standards for LCA include ISO 14025: Environmental Labels and Declara-
tions, ISO 14067: Greenhouse Gases, and ISO 14046: Environmental Management, 
among others. These standards are crucial as they provide the necessary guidelines 
and requirements for conducting LCA assessments, ensuring that evaluations are 
performed consistently and credibly. Sustainable building materials are fundamental to the welfare of the environment 
and the LCA enables analysis and helps to select the best sustainable materials. Cole 
[99] reports that the use of the LCA approach enables the comparison of alternative 
materials, components, and services in buildings. In addition, based on [100] it allows 
for measuring the environmental impacts of buildings. In Europe and the United 
States, the LCA is being used for resource comparison, however, it has slowly been 
implemented and included in government policy, strategic planning, and product 
design [101]. The input and output ﬂows of materials, energy, and pollutants to and 
from the environment life cycle perspective enable complex processes to be analyzed 
[40]. The process of a LCA is depicted in Fig. 35.4. In addition to the speciﬁc LCA processes, the main principles of LCA help in 
(a) decision-making in selecting alternatives by measuring and assessing the envi-
ronmental performance of materials or processes; and (b) analyzing the environ-
mental performance of the system for potential improvements, hence modiﬁcation, 
or designing a system to reduce the impact. This can occur for speciﬁc stages in the 
life cycle [40]. 35.2.4.3

mance through energy efﬁciency, building design, system, 
and occupants need to be taken into consideration. It is found that globally buildings 
contribute to 20–30% of primary energy use and CO2 emissions. According to [102],

Research Development and Process 
This study investigates the beneﬁts associated with the utilization of sustainable mate-
rials in the construction of residential buildings, addressing the growing concerns 
regarding the environmental repercussions of construction activities, which have 
escalated alongside the rise in both construction volume and the production and 
consumption of construction materials. Signiﬁcant advancements have been made 
in techniques and practices aimed at mitigating environmental impacts through the 
careful selection of sustainable building materials and methodologies. The environ-
mental consequences of construction encompass issues such as resource depletion, 
greenhouse gas emissions, solid waste generation, and climate change, with the 
construction sector being identiﬁed as one of the most signiﬁcant contributors to 
these challenges. Analyzing the EE and CO2 emissions of various materials will 
facilitate the identiﬁcation of those that are most beneﬁcial for the environment, as 
well as for human health and well-being. The study employs an analytical framework that integrates both quantitative and 
qualitative methodologies, beginning with an extensive literature review from which 
relevant case studies will be identiﬁed and summarized. The quantitative aspect of 
the research will involve examining various scenarios where sustainable construction 
materials are evaluated against traditional building materials, to quantify the data to 
bolster the case for sustainable options. In contrast, the qualitative component will 
focus on an analysis of thre

e houses of comparable size situated in similar climatic 
conditions but constructed using different methods. Furthermore, the research will 
assess the materials utilized in the selected case studies, employing logical reasoning 
and a comprehensive literature review to elucidate the advantages of sustainable 
construction materials over their conventional counterparts. 788
K. Gharehbaghi et al. 35.3.1 
Research Methodology 
The data-gathering process for this research is structured into two distinct segments. Initially, an extensive review of existing literature was performed to assess the envi-
ronmental implications associated with various construction materials. This phase 
involved utilizing multiple search queries that incorporated terms such as ‘compara-
tive material selection’ and ‘buildings constructed with recycled or reused materials’ 
to systematically identify and evaluate relevant scholarly works. The subsequent 
segment of the study comprised three case studies focusing on houses built from 
varied materials, all of which were comparable in size and situated within similar 
environmental contexts. Numerous researchers have published evaluations of indi-
vidual or multiple building case studies, while others have synthesized these ﬁndings 
to provide a comprehensive overview of the embodied environmental impacts. The literature review indicated a substantial body of research focused on the 
environmental implications of building materials. By synthesizing relevant ﬁndings 
from prior studies, this analysis aimed to enhance the understanding of material 
selection processes. Moreover, the research investigates the environmental conse-
quences associated with the choice of construction materials by evaluating the EE 
and CO2 emissions linked to various buildings. The central research question posed 
is, “In what ways can the environmental impact of construction be mitigated through 
improved selection of building materials?” The primary aim of this study was to 
perform a comparative analysis of residential structures constructed from several 
types of materials. Speciﬁcally, this research examined three distinct houses: one 
built with conventional standardized burnt bricks, another utilizing cement blocks, 
and a third constructed from soil cement, or mudbrick. This investigation relied 
on secondary sources, online databases, and web searches to gather comprehensive 
information regarding buildings made from recycled or reusable materials. Addi-
tionally, by employing search criteria such as “comparative material selection” and 
“buildings made with recycled/reused materials,” the case studies yield pertinent 
information and data. Particularly, the selected houses for comparison are of similar 
dimensions and situated in comparable environmental contexts, thereby facilitating 
a reliable comparison of the gathered data. The main data was collected through academic journals that display the release of 
EE and CO2 emissions. Hence

, Tables and Figures (graphs) were created to demon-
strate which house construction method has the most severe impact on the envi-
ronment. The Tables analyzed each house and were divided into different building 
elements such as the ﬂoor, roof, external walls, windows, doors, and ﬁnishes. This 
allowed the depiction of which house construction method has the lowest release of 
EE and CO2 emissions as well as which material has the most consequence. Thus, 
it created a framework that will enable the makeup of sustainable houses in the 
future due to lower detrimental effects on the environment. Figure 35.6 represents 
the research methodology. 35

Through vigorous research conducted through numerous sources of peer-reviewed 
academic literature, initial data was acquired. Further, as previously mentioned, the 
next step was the calculation of EE, and CO2 emissions ﬁgures based on the material 
used for the buildings. The data attained was collected through a mixed-method 
approach of quantitative data and qualitative data with the use of secondary research. A quantitative case study offers ‘emphasis on numbers and ﬁgures in the collection 
and analysis of data’ [104] whereas qualitative research enables us to systemically 
measure variables and explore the concepts and experiences more in-depth. The 
quantitative data will be able to depict the total EE in megajoules (MJ) and the CO2. A substantial body of research has been undertaken through a variety of case 
studies that examine the advantages associated with the incorporation of recycled 
materials in construction. Speciﬁc ﬁndings from these studies reveal the environ-
mental implications of utilizing materials such as concrete, brick, steel, and wood, 
as well as the signiﬁcance of geographical distance. Additionally, the research is 
grounded in empirical evaluations that facilitate comparisons with other case studies 
featuring similar building dimensions, which employed new and standardized mate-
rials, leading to detrimental environmental consequences. The analysis has been 
meticulously conducted, underscoring the research outcomes from these case studies 
that demonstrate the superior environmental beneﬁts of utilizing a considerable 
proportion of reused or recycled materials in the construction process. 35.3.3

Findings and Discussion 
The comparative analysis of the building materials from three houses was based on the 
release of EE and CO2 through the quantity of materials used. This research enables 
the construction industry to identify which building materials are less harmful to the 
environment. This research also helps to ident

ify the most sustainable form of a house 
built within the same climate conditions and of similar dwelling sizes. The principal 
building materials used in each house are listed in Table 35.2 above, which shows the

more popular and have a larger impact, this will encourage the construction industry 
to look for other alternative materials. 35.4.1

considerably more glass for windows in comparison to Houses 2 and 3. The total

records a total EE for windows and doors at 3105 MJ and CO2 emission of 264 kg 
CO2, while House 3 (Cement block) recorded total EE at 3090 MJ and CO2 of 263 kg 
CO2. 35.4.1.5
Finishes

houses use tiles however, House 1 also uses carpet. House 1 in using two types 
of ﬁnishes increases the amount of EE and CO2 emissions. This results in House 
1 (Standard build) recording a total EE of 36,267 MJ and CO2 at 3359 kg CO2. Contrastingly, Houses 2 and 3 only use tiles, hence, releasing similar amounts of EE 
and CO2 emissions. House 2 (Mudbrick) with the lowest amount of EE produced

Findings and Discussion 
Attention has been drawn to the environmental issues associated with residential 
construction. This research aimed to analyze building materials to address potential

798
K. Gharehbaghi et al. ways of improving material selection to reduce the detrimental impacts on the envi-
ronment by the construction industry. Based on the attributes of the materials that 
are used, it is possible to show the amount of EE and CO2 that are released by the 
three houses and which of the materials being used can be beneﬁcial. Comparing 
the houses, House 2 (Mudbrick) has the lowest EE and CO2 being produced (Refer

to Figs. 35.7 and 35.8), when converted to energy and CO2 footprint per square 
meter (using Gross Floor Area (GFA) = 78.66m2 from Table 35.2), the values are 
913.84 MJ/m2 and 93.90 kg CO2/m2. Additionally, for House 1 (using GFA of 111 
m2), the values are obtained: 2555.40 MJ/m2 and 230.47 kg CO2/m2, and for House 
3 (using GFA of 109.98 m2) the values being: 1718.90 MJ/m2 and 221.89 kg CO2/ 
m2. There have been several research studies comparing EE and CO2 of different 
construction materials, results from other studies are discussed as a comparison. A study by [26] considered the EE of a variety of construction materials including 
clay brick masonry walls buildings with reinforced concrete structure (4210 MJ/m2), 
burnt clay brickwork (2920 MJ/m2), and soil–cement block houses (1610 MJ/m2). Another study undertaken in India by [116], compared the EE of reinforced cement 
concrete (3702.3 MJ/m2) and mudbrick houses (2298.8 MJ/m2), see Table 35.7. It is important to acknowledge that the studies in question exhibit limitations in 
compatibility due to the varying dimensions and locations of the houses analyzed (for 
further details, refer to Sect. 3.3). Nevertheless, these studies facilitate a comprehen-
sive comparison. Reddy and Jagadish [26] found that a mudbrick house, constructed 
from compressed soil cement, possesses a lower EE compared to both burnt brick and 
reinforced concrete houses. Similarly, [116] reported that a mudbrick house demon-
strates lower EE than a reinforced concrete house of comparable size. The ﬁndings 
of the current study corroborate the results of both [26, 116], revealing that House 2 
(Mudbrick) has the least EE, while House 1 (Standard build—Burnt brick) exhibits 
the highest, followed by House 3 (Concrete block). As illustrated in Table 35.5,  the  
CO2 from House 1 (Standard build—Burnt brick) and House 3 (Concrete block) are 
at least 3.5 and 3.3 times greater than those from House 2 (Mudbrick), respectively. While it is crucial to evaluate the total EE and CO2 for each house, it is equally
