An Exploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …
799
Signiﬁcant reductions in EE and CO2 within residential buildings can be realized 
by substituting high-energy and high-CO2 construction materials with more sustain-
able alternatives. Research has identiﬁed speciﬁc materials that contribute the most 
to EE, as evidenced by the works of [38, 117, 118]. For instance, an analysis reveals 
that House 1, constructed with burnt brick, incorporates stee

l, while Houses 2 and 
3, built with mud and concrete blocks respectively, do not utilize this material. By 
replacing steel with timber, the overall EE and environmental impact can be dimin-
ished. Additionally, aluminum, known for its substantial EE and CO2 footprint, can 
also be substituted with more eco-friendly materials. Timber stands out as the most 
sustainable option due to its low EE and CO2 emissions, along with its recyclability 
and renewability, as highlighted in Table 35.1. This has led to a renewed interest in 
timber as a preferred building material, particularly as a substitute for concrete and 
steel in modern construction practices, as noted by [119]. The use of natural, replenishable resources can be beneﬁcial to the environment 
as they have fewer negative environmental impacts. Earthen construction materials, 
such as mudbricks, can have cost advantages as reported by [120], where the South 
African Rand (R) cost per unit of mudbrick is between R1.20-R1.50 ($0.11–0.14 
AUD) while conventional industrially burnt bricks or cement blocks vary between 
R2.50-R3 ($0.23–0.28 AUD) per unit. Adegun and Adedji [120] also suggest that 
earthen materials have thermal beneﬁts as they absorb heat slowly hence, during 
hot afternoons the interior remains cool. Moreover, the accumulated heat dissipates 
gradually at night during cold nights helping to maintain an even temperature within 
the house. As previously mentioned, glass is a prevalent material in the construction industry; 
however, its use incurs signiﬁcant economic and environmental costs. The data 
presented in Fig. 35.9 indicates that House 1, constructed with standard burnt brick, 
contains the highest proportion of glass. Transitioning to transparent wood could offer 
substantial cost savings, as it provides superior thermal insulation compared to glass, 
thereby reducing energy expenses. Transparent wood is derived from sustainable, 
renewable resources and is associated with lower CO2 [86–88]. Conversely, the carpet 
utilized in House 1, as illustrated in Fig. 35.9, is not a sustainable choice, despite 
its common application in residential settings. Carpets are composed of synthetic 
materials derived from petroleum, rendering them unsustainable and signiﬁcantly 
contributing to landﬁll waste. In 1999, Anderson, while chairing the President’s 
Council for Sustainable Development, advocated for stricter penalties regarding 
carpet waste in landﬁlls [121]. More sustainable alternatives to traditional carpeting 
include bamboo ﬂooring, recycled timber, and natural linoleum, all of which are 
recyclable and sourced from renewable materials. 800
K. Gharehbaghi et al. 35.5 
Conclusion and Recommendations 
This research presents a comparative evaluation of the building materials used in three 
distinct houses, focusing on the total EE and CO2 associated with each. The ﬁndings 
facilitate an understanding of which materials exert the most signiﬁcant negative 
effects on the env

ironment, thereby guiding the search for more sustainable alterna-
tives in future construction projects. The study employed a quantitative approach to 
assess the three houses by integrating material characteristics with construction quan-
tities. The analysis of EE and CO2 emissions, as detailed in Tables 35.4 and 35.5, 
allows for a comparative evaluation of the environmental impacts of the various 
house designs. The central research inquiry revolved around identifying strategies 
to mitigate environmental impacts through improved selection of building materials. The study conﬁrms that careful material selection can indeed lead to a reduction 
in environmental harm. Speciﬁcally, the incorporation of recyclable materials and 
judicious choices made during the planning and design stages can signiﬁcantly lower 
both EE and CO2 emissions. To ensure the reliability of the results, the three houses 
were selected based on comparable climatic conditions and similar sizes, thereby 
providing a robust framework for analysis. The ﬁndings of the research indicate that House 2, constructed from mudbrick, 
demonstrates the highest level of sustainability when compared to the other housing 
options analyzed. This superior sustainability is attributed to the utilization of 
mudbricks, which are characterized by their zero EE and CO2 emissions. In 
contrast, conventional materials such as burnt bricks and cement blocks signiﬁcantly 
contribute to an environmentally detrimental and toxic atmosphere. Furthermore, 
the case studies reveal that the use of construction materials like concrete, steel, and 
aluminum should be minimized due to their elevated levels of EE and CO2 emissions, 
particularly in comparison to more sustainable alternatives like timber and earthen 
materials. An effective building design should prioritize the incorporation of non-
virgin materials that are sustainably sourced, ideally from local suppliers, to bolster 
local economies, mitigate high energy consumption, and reduce the environmental 
impact associated with the long-distance transport of materials. This research advo-
cates for a more sustainable approach to material selection, emphasizing that the 
adoption of environmentally friendly materials can signiﬁcantly diminish ecological 
footprints and foster healthier living conditions. The key considerations taken from 
this research include: 
I. Increased renewable and recycled material selection, such as recycled Steel and 
timber reducing the need for more production which is energy intensive. II. Increased use of low EE materials that can be sourced naturally such as Straw 
Bale, Hempcrete, etc. III. Boosted use of locally sourced materials such as local Stone and aggregate to 
reduce the transportation impacts and support sustainable forestry practices. IV. Increase the use of high thermal mass materials that can store and release thermal 
energy, reducing heating and cooling needs. More sustainable rooﬁng materials

35

ploration of the Embodied Energy in Construction Materials …

such as green and cool roofs covered with vegetation, provide insulation, absorb 
rainwater, and reduce the urban heat island effect. V. Appropriate lifecycle considerations to increase durability, ease of maintenance, 
and End-of-Life recyclability. This in turn reduces landﬁll waste. VI. The utilization of highly innovative materials such as Cross-Laminated Timber 
(CLT) which provides strong, sustainable properties and good thermal proper-
ties. Along with better insulation techniques such as using aerogel insulation 
which has highly effective insulators with low thickness. The persistent reliance on standardized materials poses a signiﬁcant risk to the 
future of sustainable communities, potentially hindering the attainment of global 
sustainable development objectives. Consequently, the construction sector must 
prioritize efforts aimed at minimizing EE and CO2 emissions. A thorough exam-
ination of current practices is essential to identify and mitigate unnecessary contri-
butions to these environmental impacts. To foster a sustainable future, it is crucial to 
implement rigorous strategies and interventions that address the primary contributors 
to climate change. 35.5.1 
Recommendations 
The ﬁndings of this study suggest several avenues for recommendations that could 
enhance the accuracy and precision of results. Given that the current research relies 
on secondary data, the incorporation of primary data could yield more reliable and 
trustworthy outcomes. In utilizing primary data, the researcher would engage in 
direct collection, thereby mitigating the potential biases associated with secondary 
data sources. Additionally, a comparative analysis of houses with identical ﬂoor 
areas situated within the same vicinity could facilitate a more equitable assessment, 
particularly when examining the diverse materials employed, such as standardized 
materials versus fully recyclable and earthen alternatives. This approach would also 
encompass an evaluation of the EE and CO2 associated with the various material 
phases. Future investigations could beneﬁt from a broader sample size of houses, 
which would enhance the accuracy of mean values, as the current analysis of three 
houses presents a limited margin of error. Furthermore, adopting a more qualitative 
methodology could enrich the research, involving human-centered approaches such 
as surveys and interviews to gather insights from residents regarding their experiences 
in sustainable versus standardized homes, as well as inquiries directed at builders 
concerning the manageability of varied materials. This multifaceted approach would 
provide a more comprehensive perspective, leading to more robust and dependable 
research outcomes. 802
